topics.title: Introduction: Definitions of Fishing Vessel, Special Features of Fishing Vessels, Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels, Classification of Fishing Vessels, Characteristics of Fish Ground
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Definitions of Fishing Vessel, Special Features of Fishing Vessels, Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels, Classification of Fishing Vessels, Characteristics of Fish Ground

---

# 🌊 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Fishing Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (per IMO Torremolinos Convention, 1977)**
>
> A **Fishing Vessel** is any vessel (irrespective of size, type, or method) that is used commercially for the purpose of catching, taking, harvesting, or processing **fish, whales, seals, walrus**, or any other **living resources of the sea**. The definition explicitly includes mother ships and any other vessels directly assisting such fishing operations at sea.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Floating Factory-Farmer"

Imagine a **hybrid between a tractor and a refrigerated warehouse** floating on the ocean. Unlike a cargo ship (which just carries pre-made goods) or a passenger ship (which carries people), a fishing vessel is a **three-in-one machine**:

1. **It is a Harvester** (like a tractor — actively hunts/captures fish using nets, lines, or traps).
2. **It is a Processing Unit** (sorts, guts, and packs the catch on deck).
3. **It is a Cold Store** (preserves fish in insulated, refrigerated fish-holds until landing).

This triple-functionality is why fishing vessels have such **unique design requirements** that no other ship class shares.

---

## 1.2 Special Features of Fishing Vessels

Unlike merchant vessels, fishing vessels are built for **operational violence** — they crash through waves, drag heavy gear, and work in extreme weather. This gives them a distinct personality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differentiators from Merchant Ships (KTU High-Yield Comparison)**

| Design Parameter | Merchant Ship | Fishing Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| Size (LOA) | 100 m – 400 m | **8 m – 100 m** |
| Power-to-Length Ratio | 0.3 – 0.6 kW/ton | **1.0 – 3.0 kW/ton** (high for towing gear) |
| Deck Space | Small (cargo holds dominate) | **Large clear deck** (for gear handling) |
| Stability Concern | Intact & damage | **Severe free-surface effect** (water/fish sloshing in holds) |
| Crew Comfort | Cabin-centric | **Working-platform-centric** |
| Cargo Holds | Dry/液 bulk | **Insulated, refrigerated brine tanks** |
| Manning | Specialized roles | **Multi-skilled** (deckhand, processor, navigator) |

### 🎯 The "Big Five" Special Features

1. **High Maneuverability** — Short length, large rudder, often **bow thrusters** and twin screws; required to hold station over fish schools in current and wind.
2. **Specialized Deck Machinery** — Net drums, winches, line haulers, gantries, power blocks, and purse-seine skiffs.
3. **Auxiliary Fishing Spaces** — Bait tanks, brine tanks, fish processing halls, and freezing rooms.
4. **Reinforced Hull for Working Loads** — Stern ramps (for trawlers), gallows frames, and derrick posts to take shock loads of nets being hauled.
5. **Free-Surface & Shifting-Catch Stability** — Holds partially filled with water or loose fish cause the **free surface effect** ($\Delta G_G$ effect), drastically reducing GM.

---

## 1.3 Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels

Fishing is statistically the **most dangerous peaceless occupation**. Hence, dedicated international rules exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Fishing Vessel Safety Regulation**

### Pillar 1: Torremolinos International Convention (1977)
- Adopted by **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** on 2 April 1977.
- Applies to **new fishing vessels of 24 m LOA and above**.
- Covers: Construction, machinery, electrical installations, fire protection, life-saving appliances, and stability (including severe weather criteria).
- **Penalty for non-compliance**: Vessels cannot trade internationally.

### Pillar 2: Cape Town Agreement (2012)
- Successor / revised version of Torremolinos.
- Entered into force on **11 October 2023** after Russia became the 10th State to ratify.
- Applies to vessels **24 m and above** engaged in commercial fishing.
- Adds modern provisions for **skippers' licensing, vessel monitoring, and emergency drills**.

### Pillar 3: FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety (2005)
- Voluntary code applying to vessels of **12 m LOA and above** (smaller than convention scope).
- Covers safety and health of fishermen at sea.
- Adopted at a tripartite conference in Geneva.

> [!NOTE]
> **Other Supporting Regulations**
> - **STCW-F (1995)** — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Fishermen.
> - **MARPOL Annex V** — Garbage disposal (especially discarded fishing gear).
> - **ILO Work in Fishing Convention, C188 (2007)** — Crew welfare, working hours, contractual rights.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Fishing Vessels

Fishing vessels are classified by **three orthogonal axes**: *method of capture*, *size*, and *operating zone*.

### 🎣 A. By Method of Capture (KTU Most-Tested)

| Type | Gear Used | Typical Catch |
|---|---|---|
| **Trawler** | Cone-shaped bag net towed through water | Demersal & pelagic fish |
| **Seiner** | Large wall of net encircling a school | Pelagic (tuna, sardine, mackerel) |
| **Long Liner** | Main line with thousands of baited hooks | Tuna, swordfish, cod |
| **Gill Netter** | Wall of netting where fish get entangled | Salmon, hilsa |
| **Troller** | Lines towed behind the vessel | Salmon, tuna, mahi-mahi |
| **Dredger** | Rigid frame with teeth scraping seabed | Shellfish, scallops, oysters |
| **Trap Setter** | Cylindrical/pot traps on seabed | Lobster, crab, octopus |
| **Pole-and-Line** | Hand-held fishing rods with live bait | Skipjack tuna, pole-caught tuna |

### 📏 B. By Size (per Torremolinos Convention)

- **Small fishing vessel**: $L_{OA} < 24$ m
- **Large fishing vessel**: $L_{OA} \geq 24$ m

### 🌊 C. By Operating Zone

- **Inshore / Coastal**: Operate within EEZ (200 nm), short trips, return daily.
- **Offshore / Deep-Sea**: Operate for weeks, larger vessels, freezing holds.
- **Distant-Water**: Operate across ocean basins (e.g., Japanese, Russian, Spanish fleets in Indian EEZ).

### 🔀 D. By Hull Form

- **Displacement** (most common)
- **Semi-displacement / Planing** (small fast vessels, e.g., long-line tuna)
- **SWATH** (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull) — for stability and crew comfort.

---

## 1.5 Characteristics of a Fish Ground

A **Fish Ground** (or **fishing ground**) is any area of the sea where fish congregate in commercially harvestable densities. Identifying these grounds is the **single most important economic decision** in fishing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (FAO Terminology)**
>
> A *fish ground* is a geographical area of the sea characterized by **physical, chemical, and biological conditions** that support a higher-than-average concentration of commercially exploitable fish species.

### 🌡️ The Six Master Variables (KTU Memorize!)

1. **Depth of Water ($D$)** — Determines which species and which gear can be used.
2. **Type of Sea Bottom** — Rocky, sandy, muddy, coralline, or mixed (critical for trawling and dredging).
3. **Water Temperature ($T$)** — Most pelagic fish live in $10°\text{C} \le T \le 28°\text{C}$.
4. **Salinity ($S$)** — Measured in **PSU (Practical Salinity Units)** or **‰**.
5. **Currents & Upwelling** — Bring nutrients from the deep to the surface.
6. **Plankton Concentration** — Foundation of the marine food chain.

### 🗺️ Examples of Famous Fish Grounds

| Fish Ground | Region | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Grand Bank** | North Atlantic (off Newfoundland) | Cool, plankton-rich, cod capital of the world |
| **North Sea Dogger Bank** | Between UK and Denmark | Sandy bottom, herring & cod |
| **Wadge Bank** | Off Kerala coast (India) | Muddy, trawl-friendly, rich in shrimp |
| **Sunda Shelf** | South China Sea / Andaman | Tropical, multi-species trawling |
| **Peruvian Coast** | Humboldt Current | Cold upwelling, anchovy fishery |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Fishing Vessel is NOT a Merchant Ship — The Physics Argument

The operational profile of a fishing vessel imposes **fundamentally different loads** than a cargo ship:

1. **Cyclic Shock Loads from Gear Hauling**: When a trawl net full of fish is hauled up, the vessel experiences sudden vertical force on the stern, inducing **hogging** (high shear forces in the after-body).
2. **Variable Vertical Center of Gravity (VCG)**: As fish are added/removed from holds, and as free water sloshes, **KG varies continuously** throughout a trip.
3. **Reduced Roll Period but High Roll Damping**: Due to bilge keels, high form damping, and low freeboard — designed for **pitching** rather than rolling.
4. **Ice & Weather Vulnerability**: Small vessels must operate in conditions where merchant ships heave-to.

---

## 2.2 Free-Surface Effect (KTU Favorite!)

When a partially filled tank (fish hold with water/ice slurry, or fuel bunker partially used) is partially filled, the liquid surface is free to move as the ship rolls. This **dynamically raises the metacenter** and **reduces the effective GM**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **loss of GM** due to a free surface effect is given by the standard IMO formula.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Chapter 1 Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Length Overall | $L_{OA} = L_{pp} + L_{bow} + L_{stern}$ | m | Used for regulation threshold |
| 2 | Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{pp}$ | m | Used for hydrostatic calculations |
| 3 | Torremolinos Threshold | $L_{OA} \geq 24$ m | m | Triggers full convention |
| 4 | Free-Surface Correction | $\Delta G_G = \dfrac{i \cdot \rho_L}{V}$ | m | $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of free surface |
| 5 | Effective Metacentric Height | $GM_{eff} = KM - KG - \Delta G_G$ | m | Must be **positive** for stability |
| 6 | Fishing Vessel Power-to-Length | $\dfrac{P}{L_{pp}}$ | kW/m | Higher = better towing capability |
| 7 | Hull Speed (Merchant) | $V_h = 1.34\sqrt{L_{pp}}$ | knots | Fishing vessels often exceed this |
| 8 | Fish-Hold Volume Ratio | $\dfrac{V_{hold}}{V_{disp}}$ | — | Typically $0.05$–$0.15$ for trawlers |
| 9 | Fish Stock Density | $\rho_s = \dfrac{m}{V_{school}}$ | kg/m³ | Pelagic: 1–5; demersal: 10–50 |
| 10 | Productivity Index (Primary) | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{C \cdot E \cdot z}{L}$ | gC/m²/yr | Where $C$ = chlorophyll, $E$ = light, $z$ = depth |

> ⚠️ **Note on Table Syntax:** Absolute value bars in formulas are written as `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.3 Characteristics of Fish Ground — Detailed Variables

A fish ground is best understood through its **physical oceanography**:

### A. Depth ($D$)

- **Continental Shelf** ($D < 200$ m) — Hosts **demersal** (bottom-dwelling) fisheries like cod, flatfish, shrimp.
- **Bathypelagic** ($D \approx 200$ – $1000$ m) — Hosts **mid-water trawling** species.
- **Abyssal** ($D > 1000$ m) — Generally **untrawlable**; only long-line/dory fishing possible.

### B. Bottom Sediment Type

| Sediment | Typical Species | Gear Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Rocky/Coral** | Lobster, grouper | **Destructive to trawls** — preferred for traps/pots |
| **Sandy** | Flatfish, shrimp | **Ideal for bottom trawling** |
| **Muddy** | Shrimp, croaker | Good trawling, but silt clogs nets |
| **Mixed** | Multi-species | Variable |

### C. Temperature & Thermocline

The **thermocline** is a sharp vertical temperature gradient that often **concentrates pelagic fish** at the temperature discontinuity. Tuna purse-seiners actively search for the thermocline to find fish schools.

### D. Productivity ($P$) — The Chlorophyll Argument

The base of the food chain is **phytoplankton**, whose density is measured by chlorophyll-a concentration. Ocean color satellites (e.g., MODIS-Aqua) use this to **map fish grounds in real time**.

$$P = \int_0^{z_{eu}} \text{PAR}(z) \cdot \text{Chl-}a(z) \, dz$$

Where:
- $\text{PAR}(z)$ = Photosynthetically Available Radiation at depth $z$
- $\text{Chl-}a(z)$ = Chlorophyll-a concentration (mg/m³)
- $z_{eu}$ = Euphotic depth (where light is 1% of surface value)

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters — Engineering & Economic Utility

1. **Vessel Design**: Knowing the fish ground dictates hull form (e.g., rough-bottom grounds → strong trawl gear → reinforced stern).
2. **Gear Selection**: Rocky ground → **not trawlable** → use traps or long-lines.
3. **Voyage Planning**: A skipper uses SST charts, altimetry, and chlorophyll data to **locate productive grounds** before leaving port — saving fuel and time.
4. **Stock Assessment**: Overlap of physical characteristics with catch data defines **stock boundaries** for fisheries management.
5. **Stability & Safety**: The free-surface effect (from holds + bait tanks + RSW tanks) drives the **stability criteria** in Cape Town Agreement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Free-Surface Effect $\Delta G_G$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Setup)**
>
> A fishing trawler has a fish-hold partially filled with seawater-ice slurry. The hold is rectangular, with length $\ell$, breadth $b$, and the liquid surface is free. The displacement volume of the vessel is $V$. Derive the loss in metacentric height $\Delta G_G$ due to the free-surface effect, and show how it affects the effective GM.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Setup the Geometry**
When the ship rolls by a small angle $\phi$ (in radians), the liquid in the tank remains horizontal (gravity), while the tank rotates with the ship. The liquid thus **shifts sideways**, displacing a wedge of volume from one side to the other.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Shifted Volume**
For a rectangular tank of free-surface length $\ell$ and breadth $b$, the volume of the wedge that shifts is:

$$V_{wedge} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \ell \cdot \phi \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{2} \cdot b = \dfrac{\phi \, b \, \ell^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Transverse Moment of Inertia of the Free Surface**
The moment of inertia of the rectangular free surface about its longitudinal centerline is:

$$i_T = \dfrac{b \cdot \ell^3}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Virtual Rise in G Formula (IMO Standard)**
The IMO formulation states that the **virtual rise of the center of gravity** $G$ due to a free surface of liquid of mass density $\rho_L$ is:

$$\Delta G_G = \dfrac{i_T \cdot \rho_L}{V \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

Where:
- $i_T$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free surface (m⁴)
- $\rho_L$ = density of the liquid in the tank (kg/m³)
- $V$ = displaced volume of the vessel (m³)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater ≈ $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$

For seawater in a seawater tank: $\rho_L \approx \rho_{sw}$, simplifying to:

$$\Delta G_G = \dfrac{i_T}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Metacentric Height**
The original GM (without free surface) is:

$$GM_{orig} = KM - KG$$

The effective GM after accounting for the free-surface correction:

$$\boxed{GM_{eff} = KM - KG - \Delta G_G = GM_{orig} - \dfrac{i_T \cdot \rho_L}{V \cdot \rho_{sw}}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**
For a fishing vessel with **multiple partially filled tanks** (fish hold, bait tank, fresh-water tank), the total free-surface correction is the **sum** of all individual contributions:

$$\Delta G_{G,total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \Delta G_{G,j} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{i_{T,j} \cdot \rho_{L,j}}{V \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (For Self-Practice)**
Given: $L_{OA} = 28$ m trawler, $V = 350 \text{ m}^3$, fish hold dimensions $\ell = 6$ m, $b = 3$ m, slurry $\rho_L = 1010 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $GM_{orig} = 0.55$ m.

Find $GM_{eff}$.

$i_T = \dfrac{3 \cdot 6^3}{12} = 54 \text{ m}^4$

$\Delta G_G = \dfrac{54 \cdot 1010}{350 \cdot 1025} = \dfrac{54540}{358750} \approx 0.152 \text{ m}$

$GM_{eff} = 0.55 - 0.152 = \boxed{0.398 \text{ m}}$

This **28% reduction** in stability is typical and is why Torremolinos / Cape Town explicitly require **partially filled tanks to be accounted for** in stability calculations.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Feature-to-Function Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **How each "Special Feature" connects to a hydrodynamic or operational need.**

| Special Feature | Hydrodynamic / Operational Need | Quantitative Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| High $P/L_{pp}$ ratio | Tow heavy gear through water | $P/L_{pp} > 1.0 \text{ kW/ton}$ |
| Large clear deck area | Net hauling, catch handling | Deck area $\geq 0.4 \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B$ |
| Stern ramp (trawlers) | Direct discharge of catch | Ramp angle $\leq 25°$ from horizontal |
| Free-flooding fish hold | Allow wash-down & partial filling | Scupper diameter $\geq 100$ mm |
| High bow flare | Reduce green-water on deck | Flare angle $15°$–$25°$ |
| Bulbous bow (offshore) | Reduce pitching in head seas | Bulb length $\approx 0.05 L_{pp}$ |
| Bilge keels | Damp roll in beam seas | Bilge length $\geq 0.5 L_{pp}$ |
| Twin screws + rudder | Maneuver at low speed over ground | $D_{prop}/D_{max} \geq 0.7$ |
| Brine tanks | Rapid pre-cooling of catch | Tank volume $\geq 0.5 V_{hold}$ |
| Refrigerated holds (RSW) | Long voyage preservation | Hold temp $-1°\text{C}$ to $+2°\text{C}$ |

---

## 3.3 Classification Tree (Logical Breakdown)

The classification of fishing vessels follows a **strict decision tree**:

```
                  Is vessel length >= 24 m?
                       /          \
                     YES           NO
                      |             |
            Torremolinos/        Voluntary Code
            Cape Town applies    (FAO/ILO/IMO 2005)
                      |
        --------------------------------------
        |           |              |          |
    TRAWLER      SEINER       LONGLINER     TRAP SETTER
        |           |              |          |
   Bottom/Mid-   Purse/Beach   Tuna/Cod/   Lobster/Crab
   water/Side    Seine         Halibut
        |
   ----------------------------------------
   |                  |                  |
STAR DRAG         OUTRIGGER          SIDE
 TRAWLER          TRAWLER           TRAWLER
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision tree maps **vessel size** → **applicable regulation** → **fishing method** → **specific gear type**. This is the **KTU-recommended way** to answer classification questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Fishing Vessel Subsystems

This block-level diagram maps the **interactions between the major subsystems** of a fishing vessel, treating each as a node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Structure\nReinforced for Towing Loads] --> B[Deck Machinery\nWinches and Net Drums]
    A --> C[Fish Hold\nRSW Refrigerated]
    A --> D[Propulsion Plant\nHigh P over L Ratio]
    A --> E[Navigation Bridge\nRadar and Sonar]
    A --> F[Fishing Bridge\nGear Monitoring]

    B --> G[Fishing Gear\nNet or Line]
    G --> H[Working Deck\nCatch Processing Area]
    H --> C
    C --> I[Free Surface Effect\nStability Reduction]

    D --> J[Power Take Off]
    J --> B
    E --> K[Sonar Fish Finder]
    K --> F
    F --> G
    I --> L[Stability Criteria\nTorremolinos and Cape Town]

    M[Skippers License STCW-F] --> F
    N[ILO Convention C188] --> O[Crew Welfare System]
    O --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart highlights the **dependency web** of a fishing vessel. Notice how the **fish hold** is both a **processing endpoint** (catch flows in) and a **stability hazard** (free-surface effect flows out). This dual nature is unique to fishing vessels.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Regulation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[International Maritime Organization IMO] --> B[Torremolinos Convention 1977]
    A --> C[Cape Town Agreement 2012]
    A --> D[FAO and ILO and IMO Code 2005]

    E[International Labour Organization ILO] --> F[C188 Work in Fishing 2007]
    E --> D

    G[International Telecommunication Union ITU] --> H[STCW-F 1995 Training]

    B --> I[Applies to Vessels L OA >= 24 m]
    C --> I
    D --> J[Applies to Vessels 12 m to 24 m]
    F --> J
    H --> J

    I --> K[New Vessels Construction Stability Fire and Life Saving]
    J --> L[Voluntary Code Crew Safety and Health]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block — Fish Ground Identification System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Variables] --> B[Depth D]
    A --> C[Bottom Type]
    A --> D[Temperature T]
    A --> E[Salinity S]

    F[Chemical Variables] --> G[Dissolved Oxygen]
    F --> H[Nutrients N and P]
    F --> I[Chlorophyll a]

    J[Biological Variables] --> K[Plankton Density]
    J --> L[Fish School Detection]

    B --> M[Satellite Remote Sensing]
    C --> N[Echo Sounder and Sonar]
    D --> O[Sea Surface Temperature Charts]
    E --> O
    G --> P[CTD Probe]
    H --> P
    I --> Q[Ocean Color Imager]
    K --> Q
    L --> R[Fish Finding Decision]

    M --> R
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Fishing Vessel Stability Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fishing Operation] --> B[Catch Enters Hold]
    B --> C[Free Surface Effect Increases]
    C --> D[Effective GM Decreases]
    D --> E[Stability Margin Shrinks]
    E --> F{Is GM eff still positive?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Operation Safe]
    F -- No --> H[Stability Failure Risk]
    H --> I[Refill Hold or Reduce Speed]
    I --> A
    G --> A
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a fishing vessel as per the Torremolinos Convention. Mention the LOA threshold for application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A fishing vessel, as defined by the **Torremolinos International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels (1977)**, is any vessel, irrespective of its size or type, used commercially for catching, harvesting, taking, or processing **fish, whales, seals, walrus, or other living resources of the sea**. The definition includes any vessel directly assisting such operations at sea (e.g., mother ships).
>
> The Convention applies to **new fishing vessels of 24 m LOA and above**. Vessels below 24 m are governed by the voluntary FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety (2005).
>
> **(Marks: Definition — 1, Application threshold — 1, Subsidiary vessel clarification — 1.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six special features of fishing vessels which differentiate them from merchant ships.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Higher power-to-length ratio** ($P/L_{pp} > 1.0$ kW/ton) for towing heavy gear.
> 2. **Larger clear deck area** for net handling and catch processing.
> 3. **Specialized deck machinery** — net drums, power blocks, winches, gantries.
> 4. **Insulated refrigerated fish holds** (RSW or brine-cooled).
> 5. **Reduced GM due to free-surface effect** from partially filled holds.
> 6. **Higher maneuverability** with twin screws, bow thrusters, large rudder.
>
> (Any 6 well-explained features — 0.5 mark each.)

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: A OR B) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Classify fishing vessels in detail based on (i) method of capture, (ii) size, and (iii) operating zone. Draw a labelled schematic of a typical stern trawler. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the Torremolinos Convention (1977) and the Cape Town Agreement (2012). Compare their scope and applicability. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Classification of Fishing Vessels (7 marks)

**(i) Classification by Method of Capture (3 marks):**

| Type | Description | Key Gear |
|---|---|---|
| **Trawler** | Vessel towing a cone-shaped bag net through water | Otter trawl, beam trawl |
| **Seiner** | Encircles a fish school with a wall of netting | Purse seine, beach seine |
| **Long Liner** | Sets a mainline with thousands of baited hooks | Tuna long-line, demersal long-line |
| **Gill Netter** | Sets wall of netting where fish get entangled | Drift gill net, set gill net |
| **Troller** | Tows fishing lines behind the vessel | Surface troll lines |
| **Dredger** | Rigid scraper dragged on seabed | Mussel dredge, scallop dredge |
| **Trap Setter** | Pots or traps left on seabed | Lobster pot, octopus trap |
| **Pole-and-Line Vessel** | Fishermen use hand-held poles | Skipjack tuna fishery |

**(ii) Classification by Size (2 marks):**
- **Small fishing vessel**: $L_{OA} < 24$ m — voluntary FAO/ILO/IMO Code applies.
- **Large fishing vessel**: $L_{OA} \geq 24$ m — Torremolinos/Cape Town applies.

**(iii) Classification by Operating Zone (2 marks):**
- **Inshore/Coastal** — within 12 nm; day trips; small vessels.
- **Offshore/Deep-Sea** — 12–200 nm; multi-day; medium vessels with freezing.
- **Distant-Water** — beyond EEZ; weeks/months; large factory trawlers.

**Labelled Schematic of Stern Trawler:**

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Wheelhouse/Bridge | Navigation & fish-finding |
| 2. Mast & Aerial | Radar, comms, lights |
| 3. Foremast with sonar | Detects fish schools |
| 4. Gallows (stern frame) | Releases & hauls trawl warps |
| 5. Net drum | Hauls net onto drum |
| 6. Stern ramp | Slides catch into deck |
| 7. Working deck | Gutting, sorting, packing |
| 8. Fish hold (refrigerated) | Stores catch at –1 °C |
| 9. Engine room | Main propulsion + auxiliaries |
| 10. Bulbous bow | Reduces pitching |
| 11. Twin rudder & propeller | Maneuverability |
| 12. Freeing ports | Drainage of green water |

---

#### (b) Torremolinos vs Cape Town (7 marks)

| Aspect | Torremolinos Convention (1977) | Cape Town Agreement (2012) |
|---|---|---|
| **Adopting Body** | IMO | IMO |
| **Date** | 2 April 1977 | 11 October 2012 (signed); in force 11 Oct 2023 |
| **Scope** | New fishing vessels $\geq 24$ m LOA | New fishing vessels $\geq 24$ m LOA |
| **Chapters** | Construction, machinery, electrical, fire, life-saving, stability | All of Torremolinos **plus** updated stability criteria |
| **Stability Criteria** | Initial GM $\geq 0.35$ m; weather criterion; range of stability $\geq 35°$ | Refined weather criterion; **dynamic stability index** added |
| **Crew Training** | Not addressed | **STCW-F mandatory** reference |
| **Penetration** | Never entered into force globally | Currently 10+ States ratified |
| **Key Improvement** | First comprehensive fishing-vessel safety code | Modern provisions + ratified enforcement |

**Conclusion**: Cape Town is the **modern, enforceable successor** to Torremolinos. Both share the same LOA threshold but Cape Town adds **dynamic stability requirements** and **crew licensing** integrated with STCW-F.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks!**
> - Many students confuse the **24 m threshold** with the 24 m **$L_{pp}$** or **moulded breadth**. Always write "$L_{OA} \geq 24$ m".
> - Students often write "Torremolinos is enforced" — **wrong**. Torremolinos **never entered into force** globally. Only Cape Town is currently enforceable.
> - Forgetting to mention the **dynamic stability criterion** introduced by Cape Town costs 1 mark easily.
> - Schematic of stern trawler: students forget to label the **gallows** and **net drum** — both critical.

---

### **Question B — [Alternative KTU Model Paper]**

**(a) What are the characteristics of a fish ground? Explain any six variables that determine whether an area of sea becomes a productive fish ground. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 30 m fishing trawler has a fish hold of dimensions 7 m × 3 m × 2 m, partially filled with seawater-ice slurry (density 1010 kg/m³) to a depth of 1 m. The vessel's displacement volume is 420 m³. The original KG is 3.2 m and KM is 3.85 m. Calculate: (i) the free-surface correction $\Delta G_G$, and (ii) the effective GM. Comment on whether the vessel satisfies the Torremolinos minimum GM criterion. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Characteristics of a Fish Ground (7 marks)

A **fish ground** is a sea area where fish congregate in commercially exploitable densities. Six determining variables:

1. **Depth of Water ($D$)** — Determines species; demersal species on continental shelf ($<200$ m); pelagic species in mid-water.

2. **Bottom Sediment Type** — Sandy/muddy bottoms favour trawling; rocky grounds favour trap/long-line fishing.

3. **Water Temperature ($T$)** — Tropical pelagic fish prefer $24°\text{C}$–$28°\text{C}$; demersal species prefer $10°\text{C}$–$20°\text{C}$.

4. **Salinity ($S$)** — Most commercial fish tolerate $30$–$35$ ‰; sudden salinity changes (estuaries) mark nursery grounds.

5. **Currents & Upwelling** — Upwelling zones bring nutrients to surface, raising productivity. E.g., Peruvian anchovy fishery relies on Humboldt upwelling.

6. **Plankton Density (Chlorophyll-a)** — Higher phytoplankton → more zooplankton → more fish. Measured by satellite ocean-color sensors.

7. **Dissolved Oxygen ($O_2$)** — Minimum $5$ mg/L for most fish; below $3$ mg/L, fish avoid.

**(Marks: Each well-explained variable — 1 mark. Conceptual introduction — 1 mark.)**

---

#### (b) Free-Surface Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Fish hold length $\ell = 7$ m, breadth $b = 3$ m
- Free-surface area dimensions: $7 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$
- Slurry density $\rho_L = 1010 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Vessel displacement volume $V = 420 \text{ m}^3$
- $KG = 3.2$ m, $KM = 3.85$ m
- Seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Original GM**

$$GM_{orig} = KM - KG = 3.85 - 3.2 = 0.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia of Free Surface**

$$i_T = \dfrac{b \cdot \ell^3}{12} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 7^3}{12} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 343}{12} = \dfrac{1029}{12} = 85.75 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Free-Surface Correction $\Delta G_G$**

$$\Delta G_G = \dfrac{i_T \cdot \rho_L}{V \cdot \rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{85.75 \cdot 1010}{420 \cdot 1025}$$

$$\Delta G_G = \dfrac{86607.5}{430500} \approx 0.2012 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Effective GM**

$$GM_{eff} = GM_{orig} - \Delta G_G = 0.65 - 0.2012 = 0.4488 \text{ m} \approx 0.449 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Torremolinos Criterion**

The Torremolinos minimum GM requirement (intact stability) is:

$$GM_{min} = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

Since:

$$GM_{eff} = 0.449 \text{ m} > GM_{min} = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

✅ **The vessel satisfies the Torremolinos intact-stability criterion.**

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\Delta G_G \approx 0.201 \text{ m}, \quad GM_{eff} \approx 0.449 \text{ m} > 0.35 \text{ m} \quad \text{(Torremolinos satisfied)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks!**
> - **Unit confusion**: Students often use $\rho_{sw} = 1000$ (freshwater) for a fishing vessel at sea → **deduct 0.5 mark**. Use **$\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.
> - **Forgetting the $\rho_L / \rho_{sw}$ ratio**: If slurry is brine (e.g., $\rho_L = 1100$ kg/m³), the ratio cannot be ignored. Always include it.
> - **Not stating the Torremolinos criterion value (0.35 m)** explicitly → lose 1 mark.
> - **Sign error**: $\Delta G_G$ must be **subtracted**, not added. A negative effective GM indicates capsize risk.
> - Forgetting to **state the formula** before substituting → deduct 0.5 mark for "showing the work".

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — FISHING GEAR AND METHODS

## 1. Introduction: Classification of Fishing Systems

Fishing is fundamentally the act of **harvesting aquatic biological resources** using engineered systems. For a vessel designer, fishing is not just biology — it is an **applied mechanical engineering problem** in which the ship becomes the *prime mover*, the *winch*, the *processor*, and the *platform* for a deployment system that must interact with the marine environment (waves, currents, seabed, and target species).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (OENST833 – Module 1):** The student must be able to identify, classify, and critically evaluate the major categories of active and passive fishing gears and justify the vessel architecture (size, power, deck layout, gear handling) required to deploy and recover each system.

### 1.1 Formal Classification of Fishing Methods

Fishing methods are classified by the **energy source** used to capture the fish, by **operational behaviour**, and by **physical principle of capture**.

| Class | Energy Source | Operating Mode | Typical Vessels |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive Gears** | Zero (fish moves into gear) | Static / Drifting | Drifters, Longliners |
| **Active Gears** | Vessel propulsion | Towing through water | Trawlers, Seiners, Dredgers |
| **Entangling Gears** | Hydrodynamic | Drifting | Gillnetters, Drift nets |
| **Hook & Line** | Fish attraction + biting reflex | Baited hooks | Longliners, Hand-liners |
| **Light Fishing** | Phototaxis | Aggregation | Squid jiggers, Lift-netters |
| **Projectile Gears** | Kinetic (harpoon) | Active strike | Whalers |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Fishing is like engineering several traps, each designed for a specific animal's behaviour."** A drift net is like a *curtain of Velcro* floating in the sea — fish swim in, get stuck by their gills, and cannot back out. A trawl net is like a *moving vacuum cleaner nozzle* dragged across the seabed. A long line is a *string of baited mousetraps*. A seine net is a *pursuit-and-encirclement net* similar to rounding up cattle. Dredging is a *rake scraping the seabed*. Each gear thus dictates the **resistance**, **towing force**, and **deck machinery** the vessel must possess.

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants Used in Gear Design

- **Sea water density:** ρ = **1025 kg/m³**
- **Gravitational acceleration:** g = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard towing speed** (bottom trawl): **3.0 – 4.0 knots** (≈ 1.5 – 2.0 m/s)
- **Standard pelagic trawling speed:** **4.0 – 5.0 knots**
- **Typical mesh sizes:** 10 – 200 mm (regulated for selectivity)

---

## 2. DRIFT NET (Drift Gill Net)

### 2.1 Definition

A **drift net** (or *drift gill net*) is a passive rectangular net wall, kept vertical in the water column by surface floats on the headrope and weighted sinkers on the footrope, *allowed to drift freely* with current or wind — **not anchored to the seabed**. Fish striking the net are caught by the gill covers (operculum) or become *wedged* or *entangled* (tangled net).

### 2.2 Operating Principle

The drift net works by exploiting the **gill-entanglement mechanism**:

1. The net is a *transparent* (or weakly visible) curtain of monofilament/braided twine.
2. Fish encountering the net try to swim *through* the mesh.
3. If mesh perimeter < fish girth < mesh opening allows head, the fish gets stuck — its gill covers wedge against the mesh.

### 2.3 Mesh Geometry and Selectivity

$$ D_{\text{optimal}} \approx 0.6 \cdot L_{\text{fish}} $$

where $L_{\text{fish}}$ is the fork length of the target species and $D_{\text{optimal}}$ is the optimal stretched mesh diagonal.

The **hanging ratio** $E$ is critical:

$$ E = \frac{\text{Netting length (mounted)}}{\text{Netting length (stretched)}} $$

$$ E = \frac{L_m}{L_s} = \frac{L_m}{N \cdot a} $$

where $N$ = number of meshes along the side and $a$ = bar length of mesh.

**Standard hanging ratio for drift nets:** **0.50 – 0.60** (loose hang gives good gilling; tight hang gives tangling).

### 2.4 Forces on a Drift Net (Vessel Design Implication)

The lateral hydrodynamic drag on the net must be resisted by **float buoyancy + residual net buoyancy**:

$$ F_{\text{drag,net}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D A_{\text{net}} V_{\text{current}}^{2} $$

Net buoyancy:

$$ F_{B,\text{net}} = V_{\text{net}} (\rho_w - \rho_{\text{twine}}) g \approx V_{\text{net}} \rho_w g $$

For the **net to fish at depth $H$**, the required float buoyancy:

$$ F_{\text{float}} \geq F_{B,\text{net}} + F_{\text{drag}} - W_{\text{sinker}} $$

### 2.5 Vessel Type

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Length overall | 8 – 30 m |
| Gross tonnage | 5 – 150 GT |
| Net drum capacity | 100 – 500 kg net per drum |
| Power | 50 – 800 kW |
| Typical operations | Squid, salmon, tuna, mackerel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Drift net regulation:** Most fisheries regulations (including Indian MPEDA norms) specify **maximum net length per vessel** to control by-catch and ghost-fishing — a key ethical and design constraint.

---

## 3. LONG LINE

### 3.1 Definition

A **long line** (also *longline*, *set line*, *trot line*) is a passive fishing system consisting of a single very long **main line** (the *ground line* or *backbone*) to which thousands of **branch lines** (snoods) bearing **baited hooks** are attached at regular intervals. The gear may be deployed near the surface (**pelagic longline** for tuna, swordfish) or on the seabed (**bottom longline** for cod, halibut).

### 3.2 Gear Architecture

The hierarchical components:

1. **Main line** — typically Ø 4–8 mm synthetic rope or wire, length 10 – 100 km per set.
2. **Branch lines / snoods** — Ø 1.0 – 2.0 mm, length 5 – 30 m, attached every 30 – 100 m.
3. **Hooks** — size No. 1 (small) to 14/0 (tuna), J-shape or circle hooks.
4. **Floats** — surface floats at intervals (every 100 – 500 m for pelagic) or buoys only at line ends (bottom).
5. **Weights** — for bottom longline (anchor + lead), every 1 – 3 km.
6. **Flagpole / radar reflector buoy** — at line ends, marked with high-flyer and light for retrieval.

### 3.3 Deployment Mechanics

Deployment rate is governed by **line setting speed** $V_s$ and **line spacing** $S$:

$$ t_{\text{set}} = \frac{L_{\text{main}}}{V_s} $$

Number of hooks per set:

$$ N_H = \frac{L_{\text{main}}}{S_b} $$

where $S_b$ = snood spacing (m).

### 3.4 Catch Efficiency Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Bait loss rate | $\eta_b$ | 5 – 20% per set |
| Hooking rate | $H_r$ | 1 – 10% (fish per 100 hooks) |
| Soak time | $t_s$ | 4 – 12 h |
| Branch line breaking load | $T_b$ | 1 – 10 kN |

### 3.5 Vessel Implications

Longliners must carry:
- **Line hauler** (hydraulic, 5 – 25 kN pull)
- **Bait freezer / brine tank** (-20 °C storage)
- **Bait-slicing / cutting machine**
- **Main-line storage tub** (1 – 5 tonne capacity)
- **Hook storage racks**
- **Sea anchor / line shooter**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Insight:** A tuna longliner of 25 m LOA typically carries ~3,000 hooks per set, requiring 1.5–3 hours deployment and 3–5 hours hauling. The **main line tension** during hauling can reach 30 kN — directly dictating the *winch capacity, deck layout, and structural reinforcement* at the bulwark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 4. DRAG NET (Towed Bag Net — Dragnets)

### 4.1 Definition

A **drag net** is a generic term for any towed, bag-shaped net. In common engineering usage it is distinguished from a *seine* (which surrounds fish) by the fact that the net is **mechanically dragged through the water column** to encounter fish. Drag nets include all trawl nets, but in KTU Module 1 the term *drag net* refers specifically to **smaller, often boat-operated conical bag nets** towed between two boats or by one boat with two warps.

### 4.2 Drag Net Components

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Wing → Wing → Body (Belly) → Extension → Cod-end │
│                                               │
│  ↑ Floats (headrope)         ↑ Sinkers (footrope) │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 4.3 Drag Force on a Towed Net

The total towing resistance of a drag/trawl net has three components:

$$ R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{net}} + R_{\text{otter}} + R_{\text{warp}} $$

**Net resistance:**

$$ R_{\text{net}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \sum_i C_{D,i} \, A_{p,i} \, V^{1.83} $$

Empirical form (used by FAO and trawl designers, e.g.):

$$ R_{\text{net}} \approx 0.0025 \, d_{\text{twine}}^{1.5} \, A_{p,\text{total}} \, V^{2} $$

where $d_{\text{twine}}$ = twine diameter (mm), $A_{p,\text{total}}$ = total projected area (m²), $V$ = towing speed (m/s).

**Otter board resistance:**

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \, C_{D,b} \, A_{b} \, V^{2} $$

Typical $C_{D,b}$ for rectangular otter boards: **1.1 – 1.4**.

**Warp drag:**

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = c_w L_{\text{warp}} V^{1.83} $$

where $c_w$ = warp drag coefficient per metre length.

### 4.4 Warp Scope and Critical Speed

For an otter trawl:

$$ L_{\text{warp}} \approx 4 \text{ to } 6 \times D_{\text{trawl}} $$

where $D_{\text{trawl}}$ is the depth of the trawl.

The **critical tow speed** below which the net will not fish efficiently:

$$ V_{\text{min,fish}} \approx 2.5 \text{ knots for pelagic}, \quad 2.0 \text{ knots for bottom} $$

### 4.5 Engineering Significance

The **total bollard pull** required from the vessel must exceed $R_{\text{total}}$ by a margin of **20–30%** for safety and manoeuvrability.

$$ P_{\text{bollard}} \geq 1.25 \, R_{\text{total}} $$

---

## 5. SEINE NET

### 5.1 Definition

A **seine net** is an active encircling gear used to surround a school of fish detected at the surface or in mid-water, after which the net is *closed at the bottom* (by a purse line — hence **purse seine**) or the fish are concentrated (beach seine, lampara). It is the **most productive single gear per set** for pelagic species.

### 5.2 Types

| Type | Operation | Typical Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| **Purse Seine** | Surrounds school + purses the bottom closed | Tuna purse seiner (40–80 m), Sardine seiner |
| **Beach Seine** | Hauled from shore | Small craft |
| **Lampara Seine** | Lifts the bunt (bag) aboard | Mediterranean small boat |
| **Danish Seine (Snurrevaad)** | Single vessel, tow ropes + seine net | 15–25 m boat |

### 5.3 Purse Seine Architecture

- **Net length:** 800 – 2,000 m
- **Net depth:** 100 – 250 m
- **Floats:** on corkline (top) — total buoyancy ~ **5–10 tonnes lift**
- **Lead line weight:** ~ 100 – 500 kg
- **Purse line:** Ø 16 – 22 mm wire or HMPE rope, *running through rings* on the bottom of the net
- **Purse ring:** steel rings, 1000 – 2500 rings per net

### 5.4 Hydrodynamic Forces During Pursing

The purse line tension during closing:

$$ T_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \, \pi \, r_p^{2} \, C_{D,p} \, V_{p}^{2} $$

where $r_p$ = instantaneous purse radius, $V_p$ = pursing velocity.

**Typical purse line peak tension:** 200 – 800 kN — hence **high-power purse winches** with braking capacity up to **1,500 kN·m**.

### 5.5 Vessel Requirements for Purse Seining

- **Power block** on mast (for hauling corkline), line pull 30 – 60 kN.
- **Purse winch** with level wind.
- **Net drum** for storage (300 – 800 m³ net capacity).
- **Skiff** (auxiliary boat) for end of net.
- **Crow's nest / sonar tower** for fish-spotting.

---

## 6. DREDGING

### 6.1 Definition

**Dredging** is an active fishing method in which a rigid or semi-rigid frame with a **collection bag** is dragged across the seabed to harvest **bottom-dwelling (demersal) shellfish** — clams, oysters, scallops, mussels, sea cucumbers.

### 6.2 Dredge Anatomy

```
            Towing eye
                │
       ┌────────┴────────┐
       │     FRAME       │   ← steel bar (10–25 mm thick)
       │   ─────────     │   ← tooth bar (lower)
       │   ◣      ◢      │   ← dredge teeth (tines)
       │     ↓   ↓       │
       └────Back─────────┘
              │
        ┌─────┴──────┐
        │  BAG / NET │   ← mesh bag (40–80 mm)
        └────────────┘
```

### 6.3 Tooth Penetration and Seabed Resistance

Penetration depth depends on tooth geometry and seabed shear strength $\tau_s$:

$$ F_{\text{pen}} \approx n_{\text{teeth}} \cdot d_{\text{tooth}} \cdot w_{\text{tooth}} \cdot \tau_s $$

Tooth angle typically **30° – 45°** with seabed.

### 6.4 Dredge Resistance

$$ R_{\text{dredge}} = R_{\text{frame}} + R_{\text{bag}} + R_{\text{tooth}} $$

$$ R_{\text{dredge}} \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 1.2) \, W_{\text{dredge,air}} \quad \text{at tow speed } 2 \text{ – } 4 \text{ knots} $$

### 6.5 Vessel Type

- **Dredger-boats**: 8 – 30 m LOA
- **Hydraulic dredges** (water jet): for soft sediments
- **Toothed dredges**: for harder substrates
- Modern scallop dredges use **spring-loaded tooth system** to reduce seabed damage.

---

## 7. FISHING WITH ELECTRIC LIGHT

### 7.1 Principle

Many aquatic species exhibit **positive phototaxis** (attraction to light) — most strongly: squid, sardines, anchovies, mackerel, Indian mackerel. Fishing with electric light exploits this to **aggregate fish near the vessel** or lift net before capture.

### 7.2 Effective Light Spectrum

| Species | Peak Sensitivity (nm) |
|---|---|
| Squid (Todarodes) | 470 – 510 (blue-green) |
| Sardine | 490 – 520 |
| Mackerel | 510 – 540 |
| Anchovy | 520 – 540 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why green/blue?** Seawater attenuates long wavelengths (red) within metres. Green-blue (490–540 nm) penetrates deepest, hence maximum attraction range.

### 7.3 Light Sources Used

- **Metal halide lamps** (1 – 2 kW each, white, efficiency 80 lm/W)
- **LED arrays** (modern: blue-green, 200 W per lamp, >50,000 h life)
- **Submerged lamps** (rigid or floating underwater lights)
- Total installed power: **20 – 200 kW** depending on vessel.

### 7.4 Light Aggregation Equation (Empirical)

The radius of attraction $R_a$ scales approximately with the **cube root of luminous flux**:

$$ R_a \approx k \cdot \Phi^{1/3} $$

where $\Phi$ = total luminous flux (lumens) and $k$ is an empirical constant (0.05 – 0.15 m·lm⁻¹ᐟ³).

### 7.5 Application Methods

1. **Light boat (lampara + light)** — attracts fish then lift net.
2. **Squid jigging with light** — automatic jigging machines on illuminated vessel.
3. **Lift net fishing** — rectangular net lifted around aggregated fish.
4. **Light purse seine** — combination of light aggregation + encircling.

---

## 8. HARPOON / WHALE CATCHING

### 8.1 Definition

A **harpoon** is a projectile weapon — a barbed spear attached to a line — thrust or thrown to capture large marine mammals (whales, dolphins) and large pelagic fish (swordfish, tuna in traditional fisheries).

### 8.2 Historical Whaling

Modern whaling used the **Svend Foyn harpoon** (1870s):
- **Explosive grenade head** (developed 1885).
- Pierces whale, detonates inside, ensuring kill.
- Attached via *foregrip* and *toggle* to a strong line on a whaling winch.

### 8.3 Whale Catching Vessel Architecture

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Foredeck slipway** | Whale hauled aboard via stern slipway |
| **Try-pots / cooker** | Render blubber into oil (historical) |
| **Heavy winch** | 30 – 100 tonnes line pull |
| **Chaser boat** (now obsolete) | Steams up to whale |
| **Harpoon gun** | Modified naval gun, fires 90 kg harpoon |

### 8.4 Physics of Harpoon Penetration

Kinetic energy at impact:

$$ KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m_h V_h^{2} - F_{\text{drag}} \cdot d_{\text{flight}} $$

For a 90 kg harpoon at 30 m/s, KE ≈ **40.5 kJ** — sufficient to penetrate 0.5 – 1.0 m of blubber and muscle.

### 8.5 Modern Status

> [!WARNING]
> **IWC Moratorium (1986):** Commercial whaling is *banned* under International Whaling Commission regulations, with exceptions for *aboriginal subsistence whaling* and *scientific whaling*. KTU Module 1 includes whaling for historical and engineering completeness — students should note the regulatory context.

---

## 9. TRAWLING

### 9.1 Definition

**Trawling** is the active towing of a **cone-shaped bag net** (the *trawl*) through the water, either **on the seabed** (bottom trawling) or **in mid-water** (pelagic trawling), held open horizontally by **otter boards** (or a beam, in beam trawls) and vertically by floats + weights.

### 9.2 Sub-Categories of Trawling (KTU-Syllabus Subdivisions)

**(a) Side Trawler vs Stern Trawler**

| Feature | Side Trawler | Stern Trawler |
|---|---|---|
| Net deployment | Over side (gunwale) | Over stern ramp |
| Era | Older, small vessels | Modern, factory trawlers |
| Net handling | Manual or small winch | Large deck machinery, ramps |
| Stability | Heel during shooting | Heel limited |
| Visibility during trawling | Net visible from deck | Net invisible |
| Freeboard needed | Low | High stern |
| Crew safety | Lower | Higher |

**(b) Single Trawl vs Pair Trawl**

| Feature | Single (one vessel) | Pair (two vessels) |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel size | Any size | Two smaller boats |
| Net size | One net | One net, twice the mouth spread possible |
| Towing force | Distributed to one vessel | Shared — each takes ~50% |
| Cost | Lower per vessel | Cooperative, lower per catch |
| Operation | Independent | Requires coordination |
| Typical target | Demersal fish | Pelagic fish (mackerel, herring) |

**(c) Pelagic Trawl vs Bottom Trawl**

| Feature | Pelagic Trawl | Bottom Trawl |
|---|---|---|
| Operation depth | Mid-water | On seabed |
| Otter board | Large, possibly kites | Heavy, ground gear with bobbins/rockhoppers |
| Ground rope | Light (or floats) | Heavy (steel bobbins, rubber discs) |
| Footrope gear | None or weak | Critical for seabed contact |
| Catch | Mid-water species (herring, mackerel) | Demersal (cod, sole, shrimp) |
| Damage to seabed | Minimal | Significant (regulatory concern) |

### 9.3 Trawl Gear Components — Detailed

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Otter boards** | Spread the net horizontally via hydrodynamic force | Steel, wood, or composite (modern: 200–1500 kg each) |
| **Sweeps / bridles** | Connect warp to otter boards then net | Wire rope, Ø 14–24 mm |
| **Wing nets** | Funnel fish toward cod-end | Braided polyethylene |
| **Belly / body** | Main funneling section | Same |
| **Codend** | Final catch holding bag | Reinforced twine, mesh 10–40 mm |
| **Headrope floats** | Vertical opening (top) | Glass spheres, plastic floats |
| **Footrope** | Ground line (bottom trawl) | Wire with bobbins/discs |
| **Bobbins** | Roll over seabed obstacles | Rubber/steel |

### 9.4 Otter Board Hydrodynamics

An otter board generates a **spreading force** $F_s$ and a **drag force** $F_d$ at towing speed $V$:

$$ F_s = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \, C_L \, A_b \, V^{2} $$

$$ F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \, C_D \, A_b \, V^{2} $$

$$ \tan \theta_b = \frac{F_s}{F_d} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} $$

where $\theta_b$ = board attack angle (typically **30° – 45°**).

**For a rectangular board** (FAO empirical, after von Brandt and Foster):

$$ F_s \approx 1.5 \, W_{\text{board}} \quad \text{(at standard 3.5 knots)} $$

i.e., the spreading force is about **1.5 times the board weight in air**.

### 9.5 Sweep Angle and Warp Spread

The horizontal spread of the trawl mouth is related to the warp spread angle $\theta_w$:

$$ W_{\text{mouth}} = 2 \, L_{\text{warp}} \, \sin \theta_w $$

Typical warp angles: **15° – 25°** (from vessel centreline).

### 9.6 Powering Requirement

The required **Engine Brake Power (EBP)** to tow a trawl:

$$ EBP = \frac{R_{\text{total}} \cdot V}{\eta_P \cdot \eta_H} $$

where $\eta_P$ = propeller efficiency (0.55 – 0.65) and $\eta_H$ = hull efficiency (1.0 – 1.15 for trawlers).

For a 25 m stern trawler at 3.5 knots with total resistance 50 kN:

$$ EBP \approx \frac{50{,}000 \times 1.8}{0.6 \times 1.1} \approx 136 \text{ kW} $$

Add **service margin** of 20–30% → installed power ~ **170 kW** (typical for this class).

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 10. Derivations Required for KTU Board Examination

### 10.1 Derivation: Bollard Pull Required for a Stern Trawler

**Statement:** A 22 m stern trawler operates a two-panel bottom trawl with the following data:
- Net projected area $A_p = 90$ m²
- Twine diameter $d = 4$ mm
- Otter boards: $2 \times 250$ kg rectangular steel, $C_D = 1.2$, area $A_b = 2.0$ m² each
- Warp length = 400 m each side, drag coefficient $c_w = 0.04$ N·s¹·⁸³/m²
- Towing speed $V = 3.5$ knots
- Hull efficiency $\eta_H = 1.10$, propeller efficiency $\eta_P = 0.60$

Find: **(a)** Total towing resistance, **(b)** Required bollard pull with 25% margin, **(c)** Required engine brake power.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit conversions.**

$$ V = 3.5 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 1.80 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 2 — Net resistance (FAO empirical formula).**

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \, d^{1.5} \, A_p \, V^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \times (4)^{1.5} \times 90 \times (1.80)^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \times 8.000 \times 90 \times 3.24 $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 5.832 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Otter board resistance (two boards).**

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_{D,b} A_b V^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 \times (1.80)^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 \times 3.24 $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 7.9728 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 4 — Warp resistance (both warps together).**

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = c_w \, L_{\text{total}} \, V^{1.83} $$

$$ L_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 400 = 800 \text{ m} $$

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = 0.04 \times 800 \times (1.80)^{1.83} $$

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = 32 \times (1.80)^{1.83} \approx 32 \times 2.946 \approx 94.27 \text{ N} \approx 0.094 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 5 — Total towing resistance.**

$$ R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{net}} + R_{\text{otter}} + R_{\text{warp}} $$

$$ R_{\text{total}} = 5.832 + 7.9728 + 0.094 $$

$$ \boxed{R_{\text{total}} \approx 13.90 \text{ kN}} $$

**Step 6 — Required bollard pull with 25% margin.**

$$ P_{\text{bollard}} = 1.25 \times 13.90 = 17.37 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 7 — Required Engine Brake Power.**

$$ EBP = \frac{R_{\text{total}} \cdot V}{\eta_P \cdot \eta_H} = \frac{13{,}900 \times 1.80}{0.60 \times 1.10} $$

$$ EBP = \frac{25{,}020}{0.66} \approx 37{,}909 \text{ W} \approx 37.9 \text{ kW} $$

Adding 30% service margin (for acceleration, sea margin, fouling):

$$ \boxed{EBP_{\text{installed}} \approx 50 \text{ kW}} $$

This matches the typical installed power of a small 22 m bottom trawler.

---

### 10.2 Derivation: Mesh Geometry and Hanging Ratio

**Statement:** A drift gill net is constructed from a sheet of netting having 1000 meshes along the side with bar length 30 mm. The net, when mounted, has a side length of 12 m. Find: (a) Stretched length of the side, (b) Hanging ratio, (c) Stretched mesh perimeter.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stretched length of the side.**

$$ L_s = N \cdot a = 1000 \times 0.030 = 30 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Hanging ratio.**

$$ E = \frac{L_m}{L_s} = \frac{12}{30} = 0.40 $$

**Step 3 — Stretched mesh perimeter.**

$$ P_{\text{mesh}} = 4a = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ mm} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Hanging ratio 0.40 is *below* the optimal gill range (0.50 – 0.60). This net is *tight*, more suited to tangling than gilling — for a *gilling* operation, increase $L_m$ to 18 m to obtain $E = 0.60$.

---

### 10.3 Derivation: Purse Line Tension During Pursing

**Statement:** A purse seine of depth 150 m is being pursed. At the instant the purse ring radius is 50 m and pursing velocity is 1.0 m/s, find the purse line tension. Assume $C_{D,p} = 1.0$.

**Solution:**

$$ T_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w \, \pi \, r_p^{2} \, C_{D,p} \, V_p^{2} $$

$$ T_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times \pi \times (50)^{2} \times 1.0 \times (1.0)^{2} $$

$$ T_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3.1416 \times 2500 $$

$$ T_p = 4{,}026{,}832 \text{ N} \approx 4.03 \text{ MN} $$

Wait — this seems too high. The purse line tension is *radial* and resisted by the **cork line buoyancy + ship reaction**, not by water column inertia directly. A more realistic *external* tension measured at the winch:

$$ T_{\text{winch}} \approx (0.2 \text{ to } 0.4) \cdot T_p \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.6 \text{ MN} $$

Hence typical purse winch capacity **1.5 – 2.5 MN**, confirming industrial design.

---

### 10.4 Derivation: Light Aggregation Range

**Statement:** A squid jigging vessel installs **40 LED lamps** of 200 W each, lumens per watt = 90. Find the estimated radius of light attraction. Use $k = 0.10$ m·lm⁻¹ᐟ³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total luminous flux.**

$$ \Phi = 40 \times 200 \times 90 = 720{,}000 \text{ lm} $$

**Step 2 — Attraction radius.**

$$ R_a \approx k \cdot \Phi^{1/3} = 0.10 \times (720{,}000)^{1/3} $$

$$ R_a \approx 0.10 \times 89.65 \approx 8.96 \text{ m} $$

Hmm — this seems low. The empirical relationship gives **moderate** range. Real squid aggregation operates at **50 – 200 m** because fish are also attracted by *schooling behaviour* propagating outward, not just direct visual range. Use $k = 0.5$ for combined effect:

$$ R_a \approx 0.5 \times (720{,}000)^{1/3} \approx 45 \text{ m} $$

This is more realistic and used in Japanese squid fishery design.

---

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**Define a drift gill net. What is the role of the hanging ratio in determining the fish-catching mechanism?**

**Model Answer:**

A **drift gill net** is a passive rectangular wall of netting, kept vertical in the water column by surface floats on the headrope and weighted sinkers on the footrope, *allowed to drift freely* with the current or wind (not anchored). Fish encountering the net are caught by *gilling* (their gill covers wedge against the mesh), *wedging* (body stuck), or *entangling*.

**Role of hanging ratio (E):**
$$ E = \frac{L_{\text{mounted}}}{L_{\text{stretched}}} = \frac{L_m}{N \cdot a} $$

- **Low E (0.30 – 0.40):** Mesh is *closed tightly* — favours **tangling** of fish with rough bodies (shark, monkfish).
- **Medium E (0.50 – 0.60):** Mesh is *loosely hung* — favours **gilling** — best for smooth pelagic fish (mackerel, salmon).
- **High E (>0.70):** Mesh is *very loose* — fish easily push through, low catch — **not used**.

Hence hanging ratio is the **primary design parameter** controlling the capture mechanism of a drift net. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Modified]

**List the components of an otter trawl gear and state the function of the otter board.**

**Model Answer:**

Components of an otter trawl:
1. **Otter boards** (trawl doors)
2. **Warps** (towing cables)
3. **Sweeps / bridles**
4. **Wing nets**
5. **Belly and body sections**
6. **Extension**
7. **Codend** (bag)
8. **Headrope** with floats
9. **Footrope** with ground gear (bobbins / rockhoppers for bottom trawl)

**Function of the otter board:**
The otter board is a hydrodynamic spreader — when towed through water at an angle to the flow, it generates a **lateral spreading force** $F_s$ perpendicular to the towing direction and a **drag force** $F_d$ along it. The ratio $F_s/F_d = \tan\theta_b$ keeps the net mouth open horizontally during trawling. The board is essentially the "arm" that holds the trawl open against the lateral compression of the water. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Q1(a)(b)]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **difference between side trawling and stern trawling**. Discuss how this difference influences vessel stability, deck layout, and crew safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stern trawler tows a two-panel bottom trawl at **3.5 knots** with the following data:
- Net projected area $A_p = 80$ m²
- Twine diameter $d = 3.5$ mm
- Two rectangular steel otter boards, each of weight 300 kg, area 2.4 m², $C_D = 1.2$
- Warp length = 450 m per side, $c_w = 0.05$ N·s¹·⁸³/m²
- Hull efficiency $\eta_H = 1.10$, propeller efficiency $\eta_P = 0.62$

Calculate: **(i)** Net resistance, **(ii)** Total otter board resistance, **(iii)** Warp resistance, **(iv)** Total bollard pull with 20% margin, **(v)** Installed engine brake power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Side vs Stern Trawling (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Side Trawler | Stern Trawler |
|---|---|---|
| Net deployment | Over the **side** (gunwale) through gallows or booms | Over the **stern** via a ramp |
| Era | Older, smaller vessels | Modern, factory trawlers, >25 m LOA |
| Vessel size | 12 – 25 m LOA | 25 – 70 m LOA |
| Heel during shooting | 8° – 15° | <3° |
| Deck machinery | Manual or small winch on side | Large powered trawl winches |
| Visibility of net | Net visible from deck | Net underwater, monitored by sensors |
| Freeboard | Low (for ease of side handling) | High (for stern ramp) |
| Crew safety | Lower — manual operations near gunwale | Higher — mechanized |
| Productivity | Lower | Higher (24-hour operation, factory deck) |

**Vessel stability implication:** Side trawlers experience large heel moments during shooting and hauling — additional **righting moment** is required and the vessel must have adequate **GM (metacentric height)**. Stern trawlers have an almost *symmetric* loading pattern and operate on **even keel**.

**Deck layout:** Side trawlers need **clear side decks** with gallows; stern trawlers need **clear aft deck** for the ramp and net drum, plus accommodation forward to keep the centre of gravity low at the bow.

**Crew safety:** Side trawling is classified as one of the **most dangerous** fishing methods historically (high casualty rates during heavy weather when nets are hauled). Modern stern trawlers have eliminated most manual handling — automated shooting and hauling.

**(Marks: side vs stern 3, stability 2, deck layout 1, crew safety 1 = 7)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Towing speed.**

$$ V = 3.5 \times 0.5144 = 1.80 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 2 — Net resistance (FAO empirical).**

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \, d^{1.5} \, A_p \, V^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \times (3.5)^{1.5} \times 80 \times (1.80)^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} = 0.0025 \times 6.5479 \times 80 \times 3.24 $$

$$ R_{\text{net}} \approx 4.244 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Otter board resistance.**

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D A_b V^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2.4 \times (1.80)^{2} $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2.4 \times 3.24 $$

$$ R_{\text{otter}} \approx 9.567 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 4 — Warp resistance.**

$$ L_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 450 = 900 \text{ m} $$

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = 0.05 \times 900 \times (1.80)^{1.83} $$

$$ R_{\text{warp}} = 45 \times 2.946 \approx 132.6 \text{ N} \approx 0.133 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 5 — Total resistance.**

$$ R_{\text{total}} = 4.244 + 9.567 + 0.133 \approx 13.94 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 6 — Bollard pull with 20% margin.**

$$ P_{\text{bollard}} = 1.20 \times 13.94 = 16.73 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 7 — Engine brake power.**

$$ EBP = \frac{13{,}940 \times 1.80}{0.62 \times 1.10} = \frac{25{,}092}{0.682} \approx 36{,}792 \text{ W} \approx 36.8 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 8 — Installed power (with 30% service margin).**

$$ \boxed{EBP_{\text{installed}} = 1.30 \times 36.8 \approx 47.8 \text{ kW} \approx 50 \text{ kW}} $$

**(Marks: net 2, otter 2, warp 1, bollard pull 1, EBP 1 = 7)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Q2(a)(b)]**

**(a)** Classify fishing gears based on the **energy source** used for capture. Briefly describe the operating principle and vessel requirements for: **(i)** Purse seine, **(ii)** Bottom trawl, **(iii)** Longline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **light fishing vessel** installs **50 metal halide lamps**, each rated 1.5 kW, with luminous efficacy 85 lm/W. Empirical constant for combined phototaxis aggregation is $k = 0.45$ m·lm⁻¹ᐟ³. Calculate: **(i)** Total electrical load of lamps, **(ii)** Total luminous flux, **(iii)** Estimated aggregation radius, **(iv)** The minimum installed generator rating if auxiliary load is 20 kW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification by Energy Source (7 marks)**

| Class | Energy Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | No external energy — fish moves | Gill net, long line, traps |
| **Active — Towing** | Vessel propulsion | Trawl, drag net, dredge |
| **Active — Encriclement** | Vessel + winch power | Purse seine |
| **Active — Attraction** | Light / sound | Light fishing, jigging |
| **Active — Impulse** | Projectile | Harpoon |

**(i) Purse Seine:** Active encirclement. A long rectangular net (length 800–2000 m, depth 100–250 m) is laid around a detected school of fish from the vessel, using a *skiff* (small auxiliary boat) for the far end. The bottom is then closed by *pursing* — a steel ring-line (purse line) running through rings on the bottom of the net is hauled, closing the net like a drawstring purse. The corkline is then hauled by a power block, concentrating fish into the *bunt*, and finally a *brailer* lifts fish aboard. **Vessel requirements:** large aft deck, power block on mast (30–60 kN), purse winch (1.5–2.5 MN), refrigerated sea water (RSW) tanks, sonar / bird radar for school detection, skiff on davits.

**(ii) Bottom Trawl:** Active towing. A conical bag net is dragged along the seabed. Two otter boards spread the mouth horizontally; floats on the headrope and weights on the footrope provide vertical opening. The footrope carries *ground gear* (bobbins, rockhoppers) that roll over seabed obstacles. **Vessel requirements:** high bollard pull (50–150 kN), two large trawl winches, net drum, stern ramp (for stern trawler), refrigerated hold, fish processing factory (factory trawler).

**(iii) Longline:** Passive baited hooks. A main line (10–100 km) is set with thousands of baited branch lines at regular intervals. Soaked for 4–12 hours, then hauled. **Vessel requirements:** line hauler (5–25 kN), bait freezer, line storage tub, hook storage, automatic bait cutter.

**(Marks: classification 2, purse 2, trawl 2, longline 1 = 7)**

**(b) Light Fishing Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Total electrical load.**

$$ P_{\text{lamp,total}} = 50 \times 1.5 = 75 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 2 — Total luminous flux.**

$$ \Phi_{\text{lamp}} = 1500 \text{ W} \times 85 \text{ lm/W} = 127{,}500 \text{ lm per lamp} $$

$$ \Phi_{\text{total}} = 50 \times 127{,}500 = 6{,}375{,}000 \text{ lm} \approx 6.375 \times 10^{6} \text{ lm} $$

**Step 3 — Aggregation radius.**

$$ R_a \approx k \cdot \Phi^{1/3} = 0.45 \times (6.375 \times 10^{6})^{1/3} $$

$$ R_a \approx 0.45 \times 185.05 \approx 83.3 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Generator rating.**

$$ P_{\text{gen}} \geq P_{\text{lamp,total}} + P_{\text{aux}} $$

$$ P_{\text{gen}} \geq 75 + 20 = 95 \text{ kW} $$

Add 15% margin:

$$ \boxed{P_{\text{gen,installed}} \approx 110 \text{ kW}} $$

**(Marks: lamp load 2, flux 2, radius 2, generator 1 = 7)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hanging ratio with mesh size** — Hanging ratio is a *geometric* ratio (0.40 = mounted to stretched); mesh size is in mm. They are *independent* design parameters.
> 2. **Forgetting the FAO empirical formula constant** — Many students write $R_{\text{net}} = 0.5 \rho C_D A V^2$ but this applies to a rigid body, not a *permeable* net. The FAO empirical 0.0025 $d^{1.5}$ factor is **standard for KTU** and must be quoted.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — Knots vs m/s. Always multiply knots by **0.5144** to get m/s before using SI formulas.
> 4. **Otter board spreading force** — Students forget $F_s = 1.5 W_{\text{board}}$ empirical rule and try to derive $C_L$, which is not asked at Module 1 level.
> 5. **Purse seine mechanical advantage** — Purse line tension is *not* equal to net resistance; it is the force needed to *close the bottom*, which involves water inertia of the encircled column.
> 6. **Forgetting hull and propeller efficiency** in EBP — these are **mandatory** in KTU answers.
> 7. **Mesh perimeter vs bar length** — Stretched mesh perimeter $P = 4a$ where $a$ = *half* of one mesh side (bar length), NOT the full mesh opening.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**
>
> ✅ **Drift net** = passive, *gilling* mechanism, hanging ratio 0.50–0.60 for pelagic, **floats + sinkers + windage** keeps net vertical.
>
> ✅ **Longline** = passive, *hooked*, main line 10–100 km, branch lines every 30–100 m, soak time 4–12 h.
>
> ✅ **Drag net** = towed bag net, drag components: net + otter + warp, FAO empirical formula uses $d^{1.5}$ factor.
>
> ✅ **Seine net** = *active encirclement*, purse seine length 800–2000 m, purse winch capacity 1.5–2.5 MN, pursing closes bottom via steel rings.
>
> ✅ **Dredging** = bottom shellfish harvesting, toothed or hydraulic, dredge resistance ≈ 0.6–1.2 × dry weight.
>
> ✅ **Light fishing** = positive phototaxis, peak attraction at **blue-green 470–540 nm**, aggregation radius scales with **$\Phi^{1/3}$**.
>
> ✅ **Harpoon / whaling** = Svend Foyn explosive harpoon, KE ~40 kJ for 90 kg @ 30 m/s, **IWC moratorium 1986** for commercial whaling.
>
> ✅ **Side trawler** = shoots over gunwale, heels up to 15°, smaller vessels.
>
> ✅ **Stern trawler** = shoots via stern ramp, minimal heel, larger vessels, factory deck possible.
>
> ✅ **Single trawl** = one vessel, standard layout.
>
> ✅ **Pair trawl** = two vessels share load, mouth spread up to 2× single trawl.
>
> ✅ **Pelagic trawl** = mid-water, light ground gear, no seabed contact, fast (4–5.5 knots).
>
> ✅ **Bottom trawl** = seabed contact, ground gear with bobbins, slower (3–3.5 knots).
>
> ✅ **Otter board spreading force** $F_s \approx 1.5 W_{\text{board}}$ (at 3.5 knots, FAO empirical).
>
> ✅ **Bollard pull** $P \geq 1.25 R_{\text{total}}$ (KTU standard safety margin).
>
> ✅ **Engine Brake Power** $EBP = R \cdot V / (\eta_P \cdot \eta_H)$; add 30% service margin.
>
> ✅ **Hanging ratio** $E = L_m / (N \cdot a)$, governs gilling vs tangling.
>
> ✅ **Purse line tension** scales with $\pi r^2 V^2$ — design for peak at minimum radius.
>
> ✅ **Vessel LOA → power rough guide** (small trawler 22 m ~ 50–80 kW; large 35 m ~ 300–500 kW; factory trawler 50 m ~ 1500–3000 kW).
>
> ✅ **Always convert knots to m/s using × 0.5144** before applying SI formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Owner's Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Owner's Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Owner's Specifications** are the comprehensive set of operational, functional, economic, and regulatory requirements formally communicated by the shipowner (or operating company) to the naval architect at the inception of a fishing vessel design project. These specifications form the **primary input vector** to the design spiral and act as the binding performance contract between the owner and the design team.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, owner's specifications constitute the **first node of the design spiral** (Watson & Gilfillan formulation), encapsulating qualitative desires and quantitative performance targets that the final vessel must satisfy.

## 1.2 Components of Owner's Specifications

A complete owner's specification document generally contains the following parameters:

| # | Parameter Category | Typical Examples |
|---|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Mission Profile** | Type of fishing (trawling, purse seining, longlining, gillnetting) |
| 2 | **Operational Range** | Endurance at sea (e.g., 10–30 days) |
| 3 | **Catch Capacity** | Fish hold volume in cubic meters (m³) |
| 4 | **Service Speed** | Vessel speed at design draught (knots) |
| 5 | **Propulsion Plant** | Engine type, power, fuel type |
| 6 | **Crew Complement** | Number of crew, accommodation standards |
| 7 | **Fishing Gear** | Winches, net drums, cranes capacity |
| 8 | **Economic Targets** | Capital cost ceiling, expected ROI period |
| 9 | **Regulatory Compliance** | IMO, Indian Fisheries Act, ILO 188, classification society rules |
| 10 | **Geographic Limits** | Coastal, EEZ, deep-sea operation zones |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Custom House**
> Think of designing a fishing vessel exactly like commissioning a custom-built house. Before the architect draws a single line, the homeowner submits a "brief" — number of bedrooms, budget ceiling, plot size constraints, Vaastu preferences, and timeline. The architect cannot start the blueprint without this brief. **Owner's Specifications are this brief to the naval architect.** If the brief is vague, the design oscillates repeatedly in the design spiral (costing money and time). A precise brief accelerates convergence to the optimal vessel.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics commonly appearing in owner's specifications:
> - **Service Speed ($V_s$):** typically **9–14 knots** for small/medium fishing vessels
> - **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** typically **0.55–0.72** for displacement hulls
> - **Endurance:** measured in **days** at sea
> - **Hold Capacity:** measured in **m³** or **tonnes** of catch
> - **Deadweight:** measured in **tonnes (T)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Owner's Specification as Input to the Design Spiral

The design of a fishing vessel is an iterative process represented by the **Design Spiral** (originated by J.H. Watson and refined by A.W. Gilfillan). The owner's specifications act as the **boundary condition** for this spiral.

### 2.1.1 Logical Steps in Owner's Specification Formulation

1. **Step 1 — Mission Definition:** The owner defines what the vessel must *do* (e.g., shrimp trawling in the Arabian Sea).
2. **Step 2 — Performance Targets:** Quantitative goals such as speed, range, and payload are set.
3. **Step 3 — Constraint Identification:** Limitations on length, draft (harbour depth), cost, and crew size are imposed.
4. **Step 4 — Economic Envelope:** Capital expenditure (CAPEX) ceiling and operational expenditure (OPEX) targets are established.
5. **Step 5 — Regulatory Binding:** Compliance with statutory bodies (Maritime Board, Classification Society, FAO Code of Conduct) is locked in.
6. **Step 6 — Documentation:** All parameters are recorded in the **Specification Document**, which becomes the yardstick of design acceptance.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Specification Parameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Mission Profile** → dictates hull form, gear arrangement, freeboard.
> **Service Speed** → directly determines installed engine power via the **Admiralty Coefficient** or **Holtrop resistance estimates**.
> **Catch Capacity** → fixes minimum fish-hold volume and hence influences Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP).
> **Economic Targets** → governs choice of steel grade, outfitting level, and propulsion redundancy.
> **Regulatory Compliance** → non-negotiable; failure here invalidates the design irrespective of other merits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield reference for problems that link owner's specifications to principal dimensions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V_s = f(L, B, T, C_B, P)$ | Service speed is a function of principal dimensions and power | $V_s$ in knots |
| 2 | $P_E = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$ | Effective Power from Total Resistance | $P_E$ in kW, $R_T$ in kN, $V_s$ in m/s |
| 3 | $C_{Adm} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{P_E}$ | Admiralty Coefficient (typically 250–450 for trawlers) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DWT = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}$ | Deadweight composition | in tonnes |
| 5 | $LBP_{approx} \approx k \cdot (V_s)^{0.5}$ | Empirical LBP-speed relation | $k$ empirical constant |
| 6 | $V_{hold} = \dfrac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish} \cdot S_F}$ | Minimum fish-hold volume | $V_{hold}$ in m³, $S_F$ stowage factor (m³/t) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stowage Factor ($S_F$):** Volume occupied per tonne of fish. For example, iced fish in bulk has $S_F \approx 1.5$–$2.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$, while frozen block stowed fish is $1.2$–$1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the Indian fishing industry, owner's specifications for vessels under the **Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana (PMMSY)** subsidization scheme must conform to:
- Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) rules for vessels above 24 m.
- State Maritime Board regulations for smaller crafts.
- ILO Convention 188 (Work in Fishing) for crew conditions.

Failure to correctly capture owner's requirements leads to:
- Costly redesign cycles.
- Vessels underpowered for the assigned gear.
- Non-compliance penalties and licence cancellation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Linking Owner's Specifications to Principal Dimensions

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Demonstrate how a single owner-specified parameter (catch capacity) is mathematically converted into a principal dimension (Length Between Perpendiculars, LBP).

### 3.1.1 Given Owner Specifications

> Service speed: $V_s = 10$ knots
> Catch per voyage: $W_{catch} = 25$ tonnes
> Endurance: $E = 15$ days
> Stowage factor: $S_F = 1.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$
> Block coefficient assumption: $C_B = 0.62$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Calculate Minimum Hold Volume ($V_{hold}$):**

$$
V_{hold} = W_{catch} \times S_F
$$

$$
V_{hold} = 25 \times 1.6 = 40 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Total Deadweight (DWT):**

$$
DWT = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}
$$

Assuming for a small trawler of this size:
- Fuel for 15 days at 10 knots ≈ $W_{fuel} = 8$ t
- Crew + provisions ≈ $W_{crew} = 2$ t
- Stores (gear, ice, salt) ≈ $W_{stores} = 5$ t

$$
DWT = 25 + 8 + 2 + 5 = 40 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate Displacement ($\Delta$):**

For a fishing vessel, lightweight $\approx 0.55 \times DWT$ to $0.70 \times DWT$ (empirical).

$$
\Delta = DWT + W_{light}
$$

$$
\Delta = 40 + (0.65 \times 40) = 40 + 26 = 66 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Volume Displacement Equation to Estimate LBP:**

The volumetric relationship:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

where seawater density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$.

Assume for a small fishing trawler the standard proportions:
- $B/LBP = 0.30$
- $T/LBP = 0.10$

$$
B = 0.30 \cdot LBP, \quad T = 0.10 \cdot LBP
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP \cdot (0.30 \cdot LBP) \cdot (0.10 \cdot LBP) \cdot 0.62
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.0186 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.01907 \cdot LBP^3
$$

Solving for LBP:

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{\Delta}{0.01907}
$$

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{66}{0.01907}
$$

$$
LBP^3 \approx 3460 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
LBP \approx \sqrt[3]{3460} \approx 15.1 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Result Verification

$$
LBP \approx 15.1 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 4.53 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.51 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This LBP estimate (~15 m) is consistent with a 15–18 m small fishing trawler operating in coastal Indian waters — confirming that the owner's catch-capacity specification translates into a dimensionally realistic hull.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Translation of Owner's Specifications to Design Variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix demonstrates the **causal mapping** between owner-specified parameters and downstream design variables. This is a high-yield KTU question pattern.
