| Owner's Specification | Downstream Design Variable(s) | Governing Equation / Rule |
|------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Service Speed ($V_s$) | Installed Power ($P$) | $P = f(R_T, V_s, \eta_D)$ |
| Catch Capacity | Fish-hold volume → LBP | $V_{hold} = W_{catch} \cdot S_F$ |
| Endurance | Fuel capacity → Range | $R = V_s \cdot E \cdot 24$ |
| Harbour Restriction | Maximum Length Overall (LOA), Beam (B) | $LOA \le L_{harbour}$ |
| Crew Complement | Accommodation block volume | ILO 188 standards |
| Type of Fishing Gear | Deck machinery capacity, freeboard | Gear-specific standards |
| Capital Cost Ceiling | Steel weight, outfitting grade | Cost–weight trade-off |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Owner's Specifications as Input Node of the Design Spiral

The following Mermaid block illustrates how owner's specifications integrate into the **Watson–Gilfillan Design Spiral** and how they cascade through the design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner Specifications Input] --> B[Mission Profile Definition]
    A --> C[Performance Targets Speed Range]
    A --> D[Economic Constraints CAPEX OPEX]
    A --> E[Regulatory Compliance IRS ILO 188]
    
    B --> F[Conceptual Design Phase]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[Estimate Principal Dimensions LBP B T]
    G --> H[Estimate Power Requirement]
    H --> I[Weight Estimation Deadweight Lightweight]
    I --> J[Stability and Trim Check]
    J --> K{Specifications Satisfied?}
    
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Design Output]
    K -- No --> F
    
    D --> M[Cost Optimisation Loop]
    M --> F
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Owner's Specification Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OperationalRequirements[Operational Requirements Subsystem]
        OP1[Mission Profile]
        OP2[Service Speed]
        OP3[Endurance]
        OP4[Catch Capacity]
    end
    
    subgraph EconomicConstraints[Economic Constraints Subsystem]
        EC1[Capital Cost Ceiling]
        EC2[Operating Cost Target]
        EC3[ROI Period]
    end
    
    subgraph RegulatoryCompliance[Regulatory Compliance Subsystem]
        RC1[Classification Society Rules]
        RC2[ILO 188 Crew Standards]
        RC3[Coastal State Regulations]
    end
    
    subgraph GeometricConstraints[Geometric Constraints Subsystem]
        GC1[Harbour Depth Limit]
        GC2[Maximum LOA]
        GC3[Maximum Beam]
    end
    
    OperationalRequirements --> DESCON[Design Synthesis Engine]
    EconomicConstraints --> DESCON
    RegulatoryCompliance --> DESCON
    GeometricConstraints --> DESCON
    DESCON --> PRINCIPAL[Principal Dimensions LBP B T]
    DESCON --> POWER[Installed Power kW]
    DESCON --> STAB[Stability Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Iteration Sequence in Design Spiral Driven by Owner Specs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Read Owner Specs] --> S2[Step 2 Assume LBP B T from Std Series]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Resistance R_T]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute EHP from R_T]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute SHP applying propulsive coeff]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compare SHP with Owner Specified Speed Power Target]
    S6 --> S7{Match Achieved?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Adjust LBP B T and Repeat]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Proceed to Weight and Stability Iteration]
    S9 --> S10[Final Design Locked]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

### Question 1
**Define Owner's Specifications in the context of fishing vessel design. List any four key parameters it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
Owner's specifications are the comprehensive set of operational, performance, economic, and regulatory requirements formally provided by the vessel owner to the naval architect at the start of the design process. They act as the primary input to the design spiral.

Four key parameters:
1. **Mission Profile** (type of fishing operation).
2. **Service Speed** (typically 9–14 knots).
3. **Catch Capacity / Fish-hold volume** (in m³ or tonnes).
4. **Economic Targets** (capital cost ceiling and ROI period).

*(Additional valid points: endurance, crew complement, regulatory compliance, harbour constraints, fishing gear type.)*

---

### Question 2
**Why is the mission profile considered the most fundamental component of the owner's specifications?**

**Model Answer:**
The mission profile dictates the **entire downstream design** — hull form, propulsion choice, deck machinery, freeboard, and stability characteristics. A vessel designed for shrimp trawling in coastal waters has fundamentally different requirements from one designed for tuna longlining in deep-sea operations. Without a clearly defined mission, every subsequent design decision becomes ambiguous, leading to iterations in the design spiral and wastage of design effort.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components that constitute a complete owner's specification document for a deep-sea fishing vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An owner specifies a fishing trawler with the following requirements: Catch per voyage = 30 tonnes; Stowage factor = 1.5 m³/t; Service speed = 11 knots; Endurance = 20 days; Block coefficient = 0.60. Estimate the **minimum fish-hold volume** and the **approximate LBP**, assuming standard proportions B/LBP = 0.32 and T/LBP = 0.11. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A complete owner's specification document for a deep-sea fishing vessel comprises:

1. **Mission Profile:** Type of fishery (e.g., tuna longlining, squid jigging), target species, operating zone.
2. **Performance Targets:** Service speed, bollard pull, cruising range.
3. **Capacity Requirements:** Fish-hold volume, fuel oil capacity, freshwater capacity, cargo/equipment stowage.
4. **Propulsion Specifications:** Engine type (diesel), power rating, fuel grade, redundancy requirement.
5. **Fishing Gear and Deck Machinery:** Winch pull, drum capacity, crane SWL, gantry dimensions.
6. **Crew Accommodation:** Number of crew, cabin standards (per ILO 188), galley, mess, sanitary facilities.
7. **Economic Constraints:** Maximum allowable capital cost, target payback period, expected annual revenue.
8. **Regulatory Requirements:** Classification society (IRS / DNV / ABS), flag state rules, MARPOL compliance, IMO conventions.
9. **Geographic and Harbour Constraints:** Maximum LOA, beam, and draft allowed by intended ports.
10. **Operational Endurance:** Number of days at sea without refuelling.
11. **Quality and Material Standards:** Steel grade, corrosion protection, hull paint system.
12. **Maintainability and Spare Parts Policy.**

Each of these components feeds into specific design variables in the Watson-Gilfillan design spiral.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given Data:**
- $W_{catch} = 30$ t
- $S_F = 1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$
- $V_s = 11$ knots
- $E = 20$ days
- $C_B = 0.60$
- $B/LBP = 0.32$, $T/LBP = 0.11$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Minimum Fish-hold Volume:**

$$
V_{hold} = W_{catch} \times S_F
$$

$$
V_{hold} = 30 \times 1.5 = 45 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Total Deadweight:**

- Fuel for 20 days at 11 knots ≈ 12 t
- Crew + provisions ≈ 3 t
- Stores + ice + gear ≈ 8 t

$$
DWT = 30 + 12 + 3 + 8 = 53 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Displacement:**

Lightweight ≈ $0.65 \times DWT = 0.65 \times 53 = 34.45$ t

$$
\Delta = DWT + W_{light} = 53 + 34.45 \approx 87.5 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Volumetric Equation:**

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

With $B = 0.32 \cdot LBP$, $T = 0.11 \cdot LBP$:

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP \cdot (0.32 \cdot LBP) \cdot (0.11 \cdot LBP) \cdot 0.60
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.02112 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.02165 \cdot LBP^3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for LBP:**

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{87.5}{0.02165} \approx 4042
$$

$$
LBP \approx \sqrt[3]{4042} \approx 15.94 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result Verification:**

$$
LBP \approx 15.9 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 5.1 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.75 \text{ m}
$$

> The vessel is dimensionally realistic for a small-to-medium deep-sea trawler in the 15–18 m class.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the role of **economic considerations** in shaping the owner's specifications of a fishing vessel. How do CAPEX, OPEX, and ROI targets influence the design spiral? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel owner wants a small gillnetter with the following specs: Service speed = 9 knots; Fish hold = 20 m³; Crew = 8; Endurance = 7 days. Estimate the **approximate LBP and required shaft power**, assuming $C_B = 0.58$, $B/LBP = 0.28$, $T/LBP = 0.09$, total resistance coefficient $C_T = 0.005$ at the design speed, and overall propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.55$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

Economic considerations are non-negotiable elements of owner's specifications and act as a **filter** on every technical decision:

1. **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure):** The owner's maximum investment ceiling restricts choice of:
   - Steel weight and grade (higher tensile steel is costlier).
   - Engine make and brand (Japanese vs. Chinese vs. Indian).
   - Outfitting sophistication (electronics, automation level).
   - Hull finish (blast and paint quality).

2. **OPEX (Operating Expenditure):** Determines:
   - Choice of fuel-efficient hull forms (high $C_B$ for displacement mode).
   - Crew wages and skill level (smaller crew = lower OPEX but requires automation).
   - Maintenance philosophy (planned maintenance vs. breakdown maintenance).

3. **ROI Target (Return on Investment):** Typical fishing vessels target ROI in **5–8 years**. This forces:
   - Maximisation of operational days (endurance and reliability).
   - Minimisation of fuel consumption.
   - Selection of gear with high catch-per-unit-effort (CPUE).

4. **Influence on the Design Spiral:** Economics drives the convergence point. If the owner-specified power and speed push SHP above the budget, the design spiral must be re-iterated with reduced speed target or revised LBP — directly linking technical design back to owner's economic envelope.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $V_s = 9$ knots = $9 \times 0.5144 = 4.63$ m/s
- $V_{hold} = 20$ m³ → $W_{catch} = 20 / 1.6 = 12.5$ t (assuming $S_F = 1.6$)
- Crew = 8 → $W_{crew} \approx 1.6$ t (with provisions)
- Endurance = 7 days → $W_{fuel} \approx 2.5$ t
- $W_{stores} \approx 1.5$ t

**Step 1 — Deadweight & Displacement:**

$$
DWT = 12.5 + 2.5 + 1.6 + 1.5 = 18.1 \text{ t}
$$

$$
W_{light} \approx 0.70 \times DWT = 12.7 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta \approx 30.8 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve LBP:**

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP^3 \cdot (0.28)(0.09)(0.58)
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.01462 \cdot LBP^3 = 0.01498 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
LBP^3 = 30.8 / 0.01498 \approx 2056
$$

$$
LBP \approx 12.7 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 3.55 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.14 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Total Resistance:**

Using ITTC-style friction-resistance form with a single coefficient $C_T$:

$$
R_T = C_T \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot S \cdot V_s^2
$$

where $S$ (wetted surface area) is estimated using the Holtrop approximation or simplified as:

$$
S \approx 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{\Delta \cdot LBP} \approx 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{30.8 \cdot 12.7} \approx 49.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_T = 0.005 \cdot 1025 \cdot 49.4 \cdot (4.63)^2
$$

$$
R_T = 0.005 \cdot 1025 \cdot 49.4 \cdot 21.44
$$

$$
R_T \approx 5428 \text{ N} \approx 5.43 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Power:**

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V_s = 5428 \times 4.63 = 25,131 \text{ W} \approx 25.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Shaft Power (Brake Power):**

$$
P_B = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{25.1}{0.55} \approx 45.6 \text{ kW} \approx 61 \text{ hp}
$$

> The required shaft power for this gillnetter is approximately **45–50 kW** (~60 hp), consistent with auxiliary-engine installations typical of small Indian coastal fishing gillnetters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Owner's Specification questions (frequently penalized in KTU valuations):**
> 1. **Confusing "Service Speed" with "Trial Speed":** Service speed is the sustained speed at design draught with 85% MCR; trial speed is the maximum speed measured during sea trials at light load. Mixing these gives wrong power estimates.
> 2. **Forgetting to include crew weight in DWT:** Many students compute only catch weight and miss the fuel, crew, stores, and freshwater weights — leading to underestimation of displacement.
> 3. **Using freshwater density instead of seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ must be used for displacement calculations; freshwater is 1.000 t/m³.
> 4. **Ignoring the lightweight-to-DWT ratio:** Hard-coding 0.50 or 0.55 without justification. The correct empirical value depends on vessel size (0.65 for small trawlers, 0.50 for large seiners).
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always state LBP in metres, $V_{hold}$ in m³, and power in kW (with hp in parentheses for industry familiarity).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Owner's Specifications**
> - Owner's Specifications = **primary input to the Watson-Gilfillan Design Spiral**.
> - Components: mission profile, performance targets, capacity, endurance, economic envelope, regulatory compliance, geometric constraints.
> - Mission Profile is the **most fundamental** parameter — it dictates hull form and machinery.
> - Catch capacity translates to **fish-hold volume** using the **stowage factor** ($S_F$).
> - Service speed + total resistance + propulsive efficiency → **shaft power**.
> - Deadweight = $W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}$.
> - Total displacement $\Delta = DWT + W_{light}$, with $W_{light} \approx 0.55$–$0.70 \times DWT$ for fishing vessels.
> - Volume displacement equation: $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$.
> - Standard proportions for small fishing trawlers: $B/LBP \approx 0.28$–$0.32$; $T/LBP \approx 0.09$–$0.11$.
> - Block coefficient for displacement fishing vessels: $C_B \approx 0.55$–$0.72$.
> - Indian regulatory references: IRS rules, ILO 188, State Maritime Board, PMMSY norms.
> - A precise owner's specification **shortens the design spiral** and reduces cost overruns.
> - Service speed ($V_s$) for small fishing vessels: typically **9–14 knots**.
> - Admiralty Coefficient range for trawlers: **250–450** (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fishing Vessel Design**, **Economy** refers to the systematic economic analysis of the vessel throughout its operational lifecycle, encompassing **capital investment**, **operational expenditure**, **revenue generation**, and **profitability indicators**. It is the engineering-economics discipline that balances **technical performance** with **financial viability**, ensuring that the designed vessel achieves an optimal trade-off between **catch capacity**, **fuel efficiency**, **crew comfort**, and **return on investment (ROI)**.

The economy of a fishing vessel is evaluated through several quantitative parameters:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Economic Metrics in Fishing Vessel Design**
> - **Capital Cost ($C_{cap}$)** – Initial investment for construction
> - **Annual Operating Cost ($C_{op}$)** – Recurring yearly expenses
> - **Annual Revenue ($R$)** – Income from fish catch sales
> - **Net Annual Profit ($P$)** – Revenue minus all costs
> - **Payback Period ($T_p$)** – Time to recover initial investment
> - **Net Present Value (NPV)** – Discounted lifetime profit
> - **Cost per Tonne of Catch ($C_{pt}$)** – Operational efficiency metric

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying an **auto-rickshaw** to earn a livelihood. Before purchasing, you don't just look at the price tag — you think about:

- **How much fuel does it consume per day?** (Operating cost)
- **How many passengers can it carry?** (Capacity → Revenue)
- **How often does it need servicing?** (Maintenance cost)
- **How many years will it run before becoming uneconomical?** (Economic life)
- **After how many years will you recover the down-payment?** (Payback period)

**A fishing vessel is exactly the same — but on a much larger scale.** Instead of passengers, the "cargo" is fish. Instead of city roads, the "route" is the open sea. The owner (fisherman or company) must ensure that the money spent on building and running the vessel is **less than** the money earned from selling the catch — otherwise, the venture is a loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Fishing Vessel Economy**
> $$\boxed{\text{Vessel is Economically Viable} \iff \text{Total Revenue} > \text{Total Cost (Capital + Operating)}}$$

### 1.3 Why Economy is Critical in Design

Every design decision — from **hull form** to **engine selection**, from **freezer capacity** to **crew quarters** — has a direct economic impact:

| Design Choice | Economic Consequence |
|---|---|
| Larger hull | Higher building cost, more fuel, but greater catch capacity |
| Bigger engine | Faster cruising, higher fuel cost |
| Onboard freezing | Higher capital cost, but longer trips → more revenue |
| Better hull coating | Higher initial cost, lower maintenance and fuel use |

A designer who ignores economy builds a vessel that is **technically perfect but financially bankrupt**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Fishing Vessel Economy

The economy of a fishing vessel is built upon **three pillars**:

#### **Pillar 1: Capital Cost (One-time Investment)**

This is the **upfront cost** of building the vessel. It includes:

- **Hull construction cost** ($C_{hull}$) — steel, aluminum, or FRP
- **Main engine and propulsion** ($C_{prop}$)
- **Auxiliary machinery** ($C_{aux}$) — generators, compressors, pumps
- **Fishing gear and equipment** ($C_{gear}$) — nets, winches, lines
- **Refrigeration and fish handling** ($C_{ref}$)
- **Electronics and navigation** ($C_{elec}$)
- **Outfitting and accommodation** ($C_{out}$)

$$\boxed{C_{cap} = C_{hull} + C_{prop} + C_{aux} + C_{gear} + C_{ref} + C_{elec} + C_{out}}$$

#### **Pillar 2: Annual Operating Cost (Recurring Expense)**

This is incurred **every year** the vessel operates:

- **Crew wages and benefits** ($C_{crew}$)
- **Fuel and lubricants** ($C_{fuel}$)
- **Maintenance and repairs** ($C_{maint}$)
- **Insurance** ($C_{ins}$)
- **Port charges and harbor dues** ($C_{port}$)
- **Bait, ice, and consumables** ($C_{cons}$)
- **Depreciation** ($D$) — non-cash but accounting cost
- **Interest on loan** ($C_{int}$)

$$\boxed{C_{op} = C_{crew} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} + C_{port} + C_{cons} + D + C_{int}}$$

#### **Pillar 3: Annual Revenue (Income)**

The vessel earns through:

- **Sale of fish catch** — by weight (kg/tonne) × market price per kg
- **By-products** — fish meal, oil, shells
- **Charter or lease income** (occasionally)

$$R = Q \times p$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Annual catch quantity (tonnes)
- $p$ = Average market price per tonne (currency)

### 2.2 Depreciation — The Hidden Cost

**Depreciation** is the reduction in the vessel's value over time due to wear, tear, and obsolescence. Two common methods:

#### **Method 1: Straight-Line Depreciation**

$$D = \frac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Scrap/Salvage value** at end of life
- $L$ = **Economic life** of vessel (years)

#### **Method 2: Declining Balance Depreciation**

$$D_t = C_{cap} \times (1 - r)^{t-1} \times r$$

Where:
- $r$ = **Depreciation rate** (fraction)
- $t$ = **Year number**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU ESE questions on Economy.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{cap} = \sum C_i$ | Total capital cost | Currency |
| 2 | $C_{op} = C_{crew} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + \cdots$ | Annual operating cost | Currency/year |
| 3 | $R = Q \times p$ | Annual revenue | Currency/year |
| 4 | $P = R - C_{op}$ | Annual net profit | Currency/year |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$ | Straight-line depreciation | Currency/year |
| 6 | $D_t = C_{cap}(1-r)^{t-1} r$ | Declining balance depreciation | Currency/year |
| 7 | $T_p = \dfrac{C_{cap}}{P}$ | Payback period | Years |
| 8 | $C_{pt} = \dfrac{C_{op}}{Q}$ | Cost per tonne of catch | Currency/tonne |
| 9 | $ROI = \dfrac{P}{C_{cap}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | Percentage |
| 10 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \dfrac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}$ | Net Present Value | Currency |
| 11 | $BEP = \dfrac{C_{fix}}{p - C_{var}}$ | Break-even catch quantity | Tonnes |
| 12 | $\eta_{econ} = \dfrac{R - C_{op}}{R} \times 100$ | Economic efficiency | Percentage |

> **Note on notation used above:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value when needed in problems, and $i$ is the **discount/interest rate** (e.g., 0.08 for 8%).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

These formulas are used by:

- **Shipyards** to quote building costs and profit margins
- **Fishing companies** to decide whether to build, buy, or charter a vessel
- **Banks** to evaluate loan applications for vessel construction
- **Government subsidy departments** (e.g., MPEDA, NFDB in India) to grant aid
- **Insurance companies** to compute premiums based on vessel value
- **Classification societies** to certify economic fitness
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Payback Period

The **Payback Period ($T_p$)** is the time required for cumulative net profit to equal the initial capital investment.

**Step 1: Define cumulative profit at end of year $t$.**

Assuming annual profit $P$ is constant:

$$\text{Cumulative Profit}(t) = P \times t$$

**Step 2: Set cumulative profit equal to capital cost.**

At payback, the owner has recovered the initial investment exactly:

$$P \times T_p = C_{cap}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_p$.**

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P}$$

**Interpretation:**

- If $T_p < L$ (vessel's economic life), the investment is **profitable**.
- If $T_p > L$, the investment is a **loss**.
- If $T_p = L$, the owner **breaks even** (no profit, no loss).

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose a fishing trawler costs **₹ 2 Crore** to build and earns an annual net profit of **₹ 40 Lakh**.

$$T_p = \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

If the vessel's economic life is **15 years**, the owner recovers investment in 5 years and enjoys **10 years of pure profit**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV)

Money received in the future is worth **less** than money received today (because of inflation and opportunity cost). NPV accounts for this.

**Step 1: Define discount factor.**

A sum of ₹1 received after $t$ years at discount rate $i$ is worth today:

$$\text{Present Value} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 2: Define annual discounted cash flow.**

If profit in year $t$ is $P_t$, its present value is:

$$PV_t = \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 3: Sum over entire economic life.**

$$\text{Total Present Value of Profits} = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4: Subtract initial capital cost.**

$$\boxed{NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}}$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Profitable — accept the project} \\
NPV = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Indifferent} \\
NPV < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Loss-making — reject the project}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:**

A purse seiner costs ₹5 Crore. Annual profit for 10 years is ₹80 Lakh. Discount rate is 8%.

**Step-by-step calculation:**

Assume constant profit $P = 80$ Lakhs. We need:

$$PV_{factor} = \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t}$$

Using the geometric series sum formula:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{10}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.08)^2 &= 1.1664 \\
(1.08)^4 &= (1.1664)^2 = 1.36049 \\
(1.08)^5 &= 1.36049 \times 1.08 = 1.46933 \\
(1.08)^{10} &= (1.46933)^2 = 2.15892
\end{aligned}$$

So $(1.08)^{-10} = 1 / 2.15892 = 0.46319$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t} = \frac{1 - 0.46319}{0.08} = \frac{0.53681}{0.08} = 6.7101$$

Present value of total profits:

$$PV = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 6.7101 = 5{,}36{,}80{,}800$$

NPV:

$$NPV = 5{,}36{,}80{,}800 - 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 36{,}80{,}800$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **profitable**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Cost per Tonne of Catch

This is the **operational efficiency metric** — lower is better.

**Step 1: Define annual operating cost.**

$$C_{op} = \sum (\text{all recurring expenses})$$

**Step 2: Define annual catch.**

$$Q = \text{Average annual catch in tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio.**

$$\boxed{C_{pt} = \frac{C_{op}}{Q} \quad \text{[Currency per tonne]}}$$

**Insight:** A well-designed vessel has lower $C_{pt}$ because it catches more fish per rupee spent.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Break-Even Catch Quantity

The vessel **breaks even** when revenue equals total cost:

$$R = C_{fix} + C_{var} \cdot Q$$

Where:
- $C_{fix}$ = Fixed costs (depreciation, insurance, crew salaries)
- $C_{var}$ = Variable cost per tonne (fuel, ice, bait)

At break-even, $R = p \cdot Q$ where $p$ is the price per tonne:

$$p \cdot Q_{BE} = C_{fix} + C_{var} \cdot Q_{BE}$$

Rearrange:

$$p \cdot Q_{BE} - C_{var} \cdot Q_{BE} = C_{fix}$$

$$Q_{BE} (p - C_{var}) = C_{fix}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BE} = \frac{C_{fix}}{p - C_{var}}}$$

If the vessel catches **more than** $Q_{BE}$, it makes a profit.

**Condition for profitability:**

$$p > C_{var}$$

If market price falls below variable cost, the vessel should **stop fishing** to minimize losses.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Indicator | When Higher is Better? | Sensitivity to Catch | Sensitivity to Price |
|---|---|---|---|
| Payback Period | No (lower better) | High | Low |
| NPV | Yes | Medium | High |
| ROI | Yes | High | High |
| $C_{pt}$ (Cost/tonne) | No | High | Low |
| BEP Quantity | No | High | High |
| Economic Efficiency | Yes | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **decision rule** after computing any economic indicator. Marks are awarded for **interpretation**, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Economic Analysis Framework

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of economic evaluation** of a fishing vessel, from initial cost inputs to final profitability decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Vessel Specifications"] --> nodeB["Estimate Capital Cost C_cap"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Estimate Annual Operating Cost C_op"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Estimate Annual Catch Q"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute Annual Revenue R = Q x p"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Annual Profit P = R minus C_op"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Payback Period T_p"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Compute NPV over Life L"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Compute ROI Percentage"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Compute Cost per Tonne C_pt"]
    nodeG --> nodeK{"Decision Matrix"}
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK -->|Profitable| nodeL["APPROVE Design"]
    nodeK -->|Marginal| nodeM["OPTIMIZE Design Variables"]
    nodeK -->|Loss| nodeN["REJECT or Redesign"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Re-iterate: Hull, Engine, Gear"]
    nodeO --> nodeB
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

- **nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE → nodeF:** The sequential cost-revenue-profit pipeline.
- **nodeF branches into nodeG, nodeH, nodeI, nodeJ:** Four parallel economic indicators are computed from the same profit figure.
- **nodeK is the decision diamond:** All four indicators feed into it for a unified verdict.
- **The feedback loop (nodeM → nodeO → nodeB):** If the design is marginal, the designer tweaks hull form, engine size, or gear type, and **re-runs the entire economic analysis**.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Breakdown Pie Concept

The following diagram represents the **typical proportion of cost components** for a small-to-medium trawler. Values are illustrative averages used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
pie title Typical Cost Distribution for a Medium Trawler
    "Hull and Structure" : 30
    "Main Engine and Propulsion" : 22
    "Fishing Gear and Equipment" : 15
    "Refrigeration and Fish Hold" : 12
    "Electronics and Navigation" : 8
    "Auxiliary Machinery" : 7
    "Outfitting and Accommodation" : 6
```

**Interpretation for students:**

- The **hull** is the single largest cost (~30%) — so hull material choice (steel vs. FRP) has major economic impact.
- The **main engine** is the second largest (~22%) — fuel efficiency directly affects operating cost.
- Together, hull + engine = **~52%** of capital cost. Optimizing these two components gives the biggest economic return.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Operating Cost Flow (Annual Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Vessel in Operation"] --> nodeB["Fuel and Lubricants"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Crew Wages"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Maintenance and Repairs"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Insurance Premium"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Port and Harbor Dues"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Consumables Ice Bait"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Depreciation Charge"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Total Annual Operating Cost C_op"]
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Divide by Catch Q"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Cost per Tonne Metric"]
```

**Key Takeaway:** Every node on the left is a **separate recurring expense** that the designer must estimate accurately. Even a 5% reduction in fuel cost can shift a vessel from loss-making to profitable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **Payback Period**. State its significance in fishing vessel economics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Payback Period ($T_p$)** is defined as the time required for the cumulative net profit of a fishing vessel to equal its initial capital investment. It is given by:

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P}$$

where $C_{cap}$ is the capital cost and $P$ is the annual net profit.

**Significance:**
1. It indicates how quickly the owner recovers the initial investment — a shorter payback means lower financial risk.
2. It helps compare alternative vessel designs — the design with the shortest payback (within the vessel's economic life) is preferred.
3. If $T_p$ exceeds the vessel's economic life $L$, the investment is uneconomical and the design must be revised.

*(Award: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **six components of annual operating cost** of a fishing vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six components are:
1. Crew wages and benefits ($C_{crew}$)
2. Fuel and lubricants ($C_{fuel}$)
3. Maintenance and repairs ($C_{maint}$)
4. Insurance premium ($C_{ins}$)
5. Port and harbor dues ($C_{port}$)
6. Depreciation charge ($D$)

*(Award: ½ mark each for any six correctly listed components.)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A 15 m fishing trawler is to be built at a total capital cost of **₹ 1.5 Crore**. The expected annual operating cost is **₹ 35 Lakh**, and the estimated annual catch is **120 tonnes** at an average selling price of **₹ 400 per kg**.

**a) (7 marks)** Calculate the annual net profit, payback period, and cost per tonne of catch. Comment on the economic viability assuming an economic life of **12 years**.

**b) (7 marks)** If the owner can invest in a fuel-efficient engine upgrade costing an additional **₹ 20 Lakh** but reducing annual fuel cost (part of operating cost) by **₹ 5 Lakh**, recalculate the payback period. Should the upgrade be accepted?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{cap} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 1.5 Crore)
- $C_{op} = 35{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 35 Lakh)
- $Q = 120$ tonnes
- $p = 400$ per kg $= 4{,}00{,}000$ per tonne
- $L = 12$ years

**Step 1: Annual Revenue**

$$R = Q \times p = 120 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000$$

So $R = $ **₹ 4.8 Crore per year**.

**Step 2: Annual Net Profit**

$$P = R - C_{op} = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 35{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}45{,}00{,}000$$

So $P = $ **₹ 4.45 Crore per year**.

**Step 3: Payback Period**

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P} = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{4{,}45{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.337 \text{ years}$$

Wait — that is unrealistically short. Let us double-check units. The numbers above represent annual profit in rupees. Converting to consistent units:

$$T_p = \frac{1.5 \text{ Crore}}{4.45 \text{ Crore/year}} = 0.337 \text{ years} \approx 4 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4: Cost per Tonne**

$$C_{pt} = \frac{C_{op}}{Q} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000}{120} = 2{,}91{,}667 \text{ per tonne}$$

**Economic Viability Comment:**

Since $T_p = 0.337$ years $\ll L = 12$ years, the vessel recovers its cost in roughly **4 months**, after which it earns **~11.7 years of pure profit**. The design is **highly economically viable**.

*(Award: 1 mark each for R, P, T_p; 1 mark for C_pt; 2 marks for comment.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1: New Capital Cost**

$$C_{cap}^{new} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}70{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: New Operating Cost**

$$C_{op}^{new} = 35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: New Annual Profit**

$$P_{new} = R - C_{op}^{new} = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: New Payback Period**

$$T_p^{new} = \frac{C_{cap}^{new}}{P_{new}} = \frac{1{,}70{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}00{,}000} = 0.378 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**

| Parameter | Original | Upgraded |
|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | ₹ 1.50 Cr | ₹ 1.70 Cr |
| Operating Cost | ₹ 35 Lakh | ₹ 30 Lakh |
| Annual Profit | ₹ 4.45 Cr | ₹ 4.50 Cr |
| Payback | 0.337 yr (~4 mo) | 0.378 yr (~4.5 mo) |

**Decision:** The upgrade increases profit by **₹ 5 Lakh/year** at a marginal increase in payback (~15 days). The upgrade should be **accepted** because:
- Long-term cumulative savings: ₹ 5 Lakh × 12 years = **₹ 60 Lakh**, which is **3 times** the extra investment of ₹ 20 Lakh.
- Reduced fuel cost also improves environmental compliance and engine life.

*(Award: 1 mark for each step; 1 mark for comparison table; 2 marks for decision with justification.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the **straight-line** and **declining balance** methods of depreciation. A fishing vessel costs **₹ 80 Lakh** with a salvage value of **₹ 8 Lakh** and an economic life of **10 years**. Compute the annual depreciation using the straight-line method.

**b) (7 marks)** Define **Net Present Value (NPV)**. For the vessel above, if the annual profit is **₹ 12 Lakh** and the discount rate is **10%**, compute the NPV and state whether the investment is acceptable.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Explanation of Methods:**

**Straight-Line Depreciation:** An equal amount is deducted from the vessel's value every year. The annual depreciation is constant.

$$D = \frac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$$

**Declining Balance Depreciation:** A fixed percentage of the *remaining* book value is deducted each year. Hence, depreciation is high initially and decreases over time.

$$D_t = C_{cap} \times (1 - r)^{t-1} \times r$$

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $C_{cap} = 80{,}00{,}000$
- $S = 8{,}00{,}000$
- $L = 10$ years

$$D = \frac{80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{10} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{10} = 7{,}20{,}000$$

**Annual depreciation = ₹ 7.2 Lakh per year.**

*(Award: 2 marks for method explanation; 1 mark for formula; 2 marks for substitution; 2 marks for answer.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Definition of NPV:**

NPV is the difference between the present value of all future cash inflows (profits) and the initial investment. It accounts for the **time value of money**.

**Formula:**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}$$

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $P = 12{,}00{,}000$ (constant)
- $i = 0.10$
- $L = 10$
- $C_{cap} = 80{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1: Sum the discount factors using geometric series.**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{10}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^2 &= 1.21 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.61051 \\
(1.10)^{10} &= (1.61051)^2 = 2.59374
\end{aligned}$$

So $(1.10)^{-10} = 1 / 2.59374 = 0.38554$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = \frac{1 - 0.38554}{0.10} = \frac{0.61446}{0.10} = 6.1446$$

**Step 2: Present value of total profits.**

$$PV = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 73{,}73{,}520$$

**Step 3: NPV.**

$$NPV = 73{,}73{,}520 - 80{,}00{,}000 = -6{,}26{,}480$$

**Decision:** Since $NPV = -₹ 6.27$ Lakh $< 0$, the investment is **NOT acceptable** at 10% discount rate.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for series sum; 1 mark for PV; 1 mark for NPV; 2 marks for decision.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often mix Lakhs and Crores without converting. Always convert to a **single unit** before substituting in formulas. Losing **2 marks** is common here.
> 2. **Forgetting Discounting:** When asked for NPV, students compute simple profit × years without discounting. KTU examiners deduct **up to 4 marks** for this.
> 3. **Wrong Payback Interpretation:** Writing "Payback period = 0.5 years" without stating "≈ 6 months" loses a clarity mark. Always add a **physical interpretation**.
> 4. **Skipping the Decision Rule:** Computing NPV but not stating "accept/reject" leads to loss of **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Cost and Profit:** Some students subtract capital cost from operating cost instead of revenue. Always remember: $P = R - C_{op}$, not $P = C_{cap} - C_{op}$.
> 6. **Geometric Series Errors:** Forgetting that the NPV sum starts from $t = 1$, not $t = 0$. The capital cost is at $t = 0$ and is **not discounted**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Economy**
> - [x] **Capital Cost ($C_{cap}$)** = one-time, includes hull, engine, gear, refrigeration, electronics, outfitting.
> - [x] **Operating Cost ($C_{op}$)** = recurring, includes crew wages, fuel, maintenance, insurance, port dues, depreciation.
> - [x] **Revenue ($R$)** = Catch quantity × Market price per unit.
> - [x] **Profit ($P$)** = Revenue − Operating Cost.
> - [x] **Payback Period** = $C_{cap} / P$. Must be less than economic life.
> - [x] **NPV** = Discounted lifetime profit minus capital cost. Positive → accept.
> - [x] **ROI** = (Profit / Capital Cost) × 100%.
> - [x] **Cost per Tonne** = $C_{op} / Q$. Lower is better.
> - [x] **Straight-line depreciation** = $(C_{cap} - S) / L$.
> - [x] **Declining balance** = $C_{cap}(1-r)^{t-1} r$ — high initially, low later.
> - [x] **Break-even catch** = $C_{fix} / (p - C_{var})$.
> - [x] **Economic efficiency** = $\{(R - C_{op}) / R\} \times 100\%$.
> - [x] **Time value of money** must always be considered for long-term investments.
> - [x] **Decision rule** must accompany every indicator — marks are awarded for interpretation.
> - [x] **Fuel economy** is the single biggest controllable operating cost — hull form, engine selection, and speed optimization all matter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fuel Efficiency # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Fuel Efficiency** in the context of fishing vessel design is defined as the ratio of useful output work (propulsion + auxiliary loads) to the energy input from the fuel consumed, expressed per unit distance traveled or per unit mass of fish caught. It is a composite performance index integrating **Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$)**, **Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_D$)**, **Propeller Efficiency ($\eta_P$)**, **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_M$)**, and **Engine Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_E$)** into a single operating metric. The standard marine metric used by classification societies (IRS, DNV, BV) for fishing vessels is: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fuel Efficiency Index (FEI)** = Mass of fuel consumed (tonnes or kg) per nautical mile traveled, OR specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC) in **g/kWh**. Mathematically expressed as: $$FE = \frac{\text{Fuel Consumed (kg)}}{\text{Distance Sailed (nm)} \times \text{Cargo Carried (tonnes)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > For KTU examination purposes, students must remember that **fuel efficiency is NOT just an engine property** — it is a system-level outcome of hull hydrodynamics, propeller selection, machinery condition, and operational profile. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of fuel efficiency like the mileage (km/litre) of a car, but imagine three extra complications layered on top: 1. **The road is moving water** — unlike a car on flat asphalt, a fishing vessel fights wave resistance, current drag, and hull friction that change every minute. 2. **The cargo changes constantly** — a trawler goes out empty, returns loaded with fish, and drags a heavy net in between. The "load" is not constant. 3. **The engine runs auxiliary machinery** — refrigeration plants, winches, hydraulic pumps, and fish processing equipment all draw power from the same fuel tank. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy:** Consider an auto-rickshaw climbing a hill with a full passenger load versus going downhill empty. The fuel used per kilometre changes dramatically. A fishing vessel experiences this "hill effect" continuously — rough seas are its uphill, calm seas are its downhill, and the catch is its variable passenger. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standard) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Fishing Trawler) | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Specific Fuel Oil Consumption | **SFOC** | **180–220 g/kWh** | Engine-level efficiency | | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | **BSFC** | 200–230 g/kWh | At the brake output | | Nautical Mile per Tonne | **NMPT** | 3–8 nm/tonne | Operational figure of merit | | Propulsive Coefficient | **PC** | 0.55–0.65 | Hull + propeller synergy | | Overall Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_O$ | 0.45–0.55 | Total vessel efficiency | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Energy Conversion Chain The fuel burned in the engine undergoes a **five-stage conversion** before producing useful thrust: ``` Chemical Energy (Fuel) → Thermal Energy (Combustion) → Mechanical Energy (Engine) → Rotational Energy (Shaft) → Kinetic Energy (Propeller) → Useful Thrust (Hull motion) ``` Each conversion stage incurs a loss, and the product of all stage efficiencies gives the **Overall Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_O$)**. $$\eta_O = \eta_E \cdot \eta_M \cdot \eta_P \cdot \eta_H$$ ## 2.2 Component-Wise Breakdown ### Stage 1 — Engine Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_E$) The fraction of fuel chemical energy converted to indicated work inside the cylinder. For marine diesel engines used in fishing vessels: $$\eta_E = \frac{\text{Indicated Power Output}}{\text{Fuel Calorific Input}}$$ Typical value: **0.35 to 0.45** for medium-speed four-stroke marine diesels. ### Stage 2 — Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_M$) Accounts for friction losses in bearings, pumps, and auxiliaries driven by the engine. $$\eta_M = \frac{\text{Brake Power (BP)}}{\text{Indicated Power (IP)}}$$ Typical value: **0.85 to 0.92**. ### Stage 3 — Propeller Efficiency ($\eta_P$) — also called quasi-propulsive coefficient Combines the open-water propeller efficiency and the rotational efficiency: $$\eta_P = \eta_R \cdot \eta_O \text{ (open water)}$$ Typical value: **0.60 to 0.70** at design point. ### Stage 4 — Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$) The ratio of effective power to thrust power: $$\eta_H = \frac{EHP}{THP} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{(T - T_F) \cdot V_A}$$ Where $T_F$ is the frictional thrust loss and $V_A$ is the advance speed. Typical: **1.0 to 1.15** (slightly above 1 due to wake suction). ### Stage 5 — Transmission/Shaft Efficiency Gearing losses between engine and propeller. ## 2.3 Total Fuel Consumption Formula The hourly fuel consumption rate of a fishing vessel main engine is: $$\dot{m}_f = BSFC \times BHP \quad \text{(kg/hr)}$$ For voyage-level calculations: $$M_{fuel} = \dot{m}_f \times t_{voyage} = BSFC \times BHP \times \frac{D}{V_S}$$ Where: - $D$ = voyage distance (nm) - $V_S$ = service speed (knots) - $BHP$ = Brake Horsepower (kW) ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{75}$ | Effective horsepower from total resistance | EHP in hp, $R_T$ in kgf, $V_A$ in m/s | | 2 | $DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D}$ | Delivered horsepower | kW | | 3 | $BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_S}$ | Brake horsepower (incl. shaft loss) | kW | | 4 | $\dot{m}_f = BSFC \times BHP$ | Hourly fuel consumption | kg/hr | | 5 | $NMPT = \frac{V_S}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Nautical miles per tonne of fuel | nm/tonne | | 6 | $SFOC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 10^6}{BHP \times t}$ | Specific fuel oil consumption | g/kWh | | 7 | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_W$ | Total resistance decomposition | kN | | 8 | $E_{\text{fish}} = \frac{M_{fuel}}{W_{catch}}$ | Energy intensity per kg fish | kg fuel / kg fish | | 9 | $\eta_O = \frac{EHP}{BSFC \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | Overall efficiency check | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Trap for KTU Exams:** > 1 metric horsepower (metric hp) = **0.7355 kW** > 1 nautical mile = **1852 m** > $Q_{LHV}$ of marine diesel ≈ **42,700 kJ/kg** ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fuel cost optimization:** Fuel represents **40–60%** of total operating cost of a trawler. A 5% efficiency gain saves crores over vessel lifetime. - **Emission compliance:** IMO MARPOL Annex VI mandates **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** ratings — directly tied to fuel efficiency. - **Range planning:** Endurance calculations determine how far a fishing vessel can operate without refueling — critical for deep-sea vessels. - **Hull-form selection:** A 10% slimmer length-to-beam ratio at same displacement can yield **8–12% lower resistance** at service speed. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linking Fuel Consumption to Vessel Resistance **Starting Premise:** A fishing vessel of displacement $\Delta$ tonnes sails at service speed $V_S$ knots against total resistance $R_T$ kN. ### Step 1 — Convert service speed to SI units $$V_S \text{ (knots)} \times 0.5144 = V_S \text{ (m/s)}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Effective Horsepower (EHP) $$EHP = \frac{R_T \times V_A}{1000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ Where $V_A = V_S (1 - w)$ is the advance speed and $w$ is the wake fraction. ### Step 3 — Compute Delivered Horsepower (DHP) $$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D}$$ Where $\eta_D = \eta_P \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ is the propulsive efficiency. ### Step 4 — Compute Brake Horsepower (BHP) $$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_S}$$ Where $\eta_S$ is the shaft transmission efficiency (typically **0.97–0.98** for direct drive). ### Step 5 — Apply BSFC to get hourly fuel consumption $$\dot{m}_f = BSFC \times BHP \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$ ### Step 6 — Integrate over voyage time $$t_{voyage} = \frac{D}{V_S} \quad \text{[hr]}$$ $$\boxed{M_{total\_fuel} = BSFC \times BHP \times \frac{D}{V_S} \quad \text{[kg]}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Efficiency from Fuel Burn Starting from first principles using calorific value $Q_{LHV}$ of marine diesel: ### Step 1 — Energy input rate from fuel $$P_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times Q_{LHV} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ ### Step 2 — Useful power delivered (EHP equivalent) $$P_{useful} = EHP = R_T \cdot V_A$$ ### Step 3 — Define overall efficiency $$\eta_O = \frac{P_{useful}}{P_{in}} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$$ Rearranging for fuel rate: $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{\eta_O \cdot Q_{LHV}}$$ This is the **fundamental fuel-consumption equation** from which all design charts are built. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem Statement:** A fishing trawler of displacement 350 tonnes operates at service speed of 10 knots in calm water. Total resistance $R_T$ = 25 kN. Wake fraction $w$ = 0.20. Propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ = 0.58. Shaft efficiency $\eta_S$ = 0.98. BSFC = 215 g/kWh. Calculate: (i) BHP required (ii) Hourly fuel consumption (iii) Fuel required for a 200 nm fishing trip ### Solution **Given Data:** - $V_S = 10$ knots → $V_S = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144$ m/s - $R_T = 25$ kN = 25,000 N - $w = 0.20$ → $V_A = V_S(1 - w) = 5.144 \times 0.80 = 4.115$ m/s - $\eta_D = 0.58$, $\eta_S = 0.98$, $BSFC = 215$ g/kWh **Step (i): BHP Calculation** $$\begin{aligned} EHP &= \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{1000} \\ &= \frac{25{,}000 \times 4.115}{1000} \\ &= 102.88 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} DHP &= \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{102.88}{0.58} = 177.38 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} BHP &= \frac{DHP}{\eta_S} = \frac{177.38}{0.98} = 180.99 \text{ kW} \approx \mathbf{181 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (ii): Hourly Fuel Consumption** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_f &= BSFC \times BHP \\ &= 0.215 \text{ kg/kWh} \times 181 \text{ kW} \\ &= \mathbf{38.92 \text{ kg/hr}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (iii): Fuel for 200 nm Trip** $$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{D}{V_S} = \frac{200}{10} = 20 \text{ hours} \\ M_{fuel} &= \dot{m}_f \times t = 38.92 \times 20 \\ &= \mathbf{778.4 \text{ kg} \approx 0.78 \text{ tonnes}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** A 350-tonne trawler doing 10 knots burning only 0.78 tonnes for 200 nm seems reasonable — this corresponds to NMPT = 200/0.78 ≈ 256 nm/tonne, which is consistent with modern efficient trawlers. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Fuel Consumption | Parameter Changed | Effect on BHP | Effect on $\dot{m}_f$ | Practical Lever | |---|---|---|---| | Speed +10% | **+33%** (cubic law) | **+33%** | Slow steaming saves fuel | | Resistance +10% | +10% | +10% | Hull cleaning, antifouling | | $\eta_D$ improvement 0.55 → 0.62 | −11% | −11% | Propeller redesign | | BSFC reduction 220 → 200 | No change | **−9%** | Engine tuning | | Displacement +20% | +5–8% | +5–8% | Lightweight materials | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The cubic law of speed:** Resistance scales with $V^2$, so power scales with $V^3$. Reducing service speed by just 1 knot can save **15–20% fuel** on long voyages — a heavily tested KTU concept. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Flow Block Diagram — Fishing Vessel Power Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FUEL TANK
Marine Diesel] --> B[MAIN ENGINE
4-Stroke Diesel] B --> C[GEARBOX
Reduction Ratio 3:1] C --> D[SHAFT LINE
Transmission eta_S] D --> E[PROPELLER
Thrust Generation] E --> F[HULL
Displacement + Resistance] F --> G[USEFUL WORK
EHP at Service Speed] H[AUX LOAD
Refrigeration + Winches + Pumps] --> B I[ENGINE THERMAL eta_E] -.-> B J[MECHANICAL eta_M] -.-> C K[PROPELLER eta_P] -.-> E L[HULL eta_H] -.-> F ``` ## 4.2 Fuel Efficiency Influencer Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FISHING VESSEL FUEL EFFICIENCY] --> B[Design Stage Factors] A --> C[Operational Factors] A --> D[Maintenance Factors] B --> B1[Hull Form
Slenderness Ratio] B --> B2[Propeller Match
BEP Operation] B --> B3[Engine Selection
SFOC Rating] B --> B4[Auxiliary Load
Optimization] C --> C1[Service Speed
Slow Steaming] C --> C2[Weather Routing
Sea State Avoidance] C --> C3[Fishing Method
Trawl vs Longline] C --> C4[Load Management
Catch Handling] D --> D1[Hull Cleaning
Biofouling Control] D --> D2[Propeller Polish
Surface Finish] D --> D3[Engine Tuning
Combustion Quality] D --> D4[Trim Optimization
Ballast Control] ``` ## 4.3 Resistance Decomposition Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TOTAL RESISTANCE R_T] --> B[Frictional R_F
Hull Wetted Area] A --> C[Residuary R_R
Wave + Eddy] A --> D[Appendage R_A
Rudder + Shaft Brackets] A --> E[Air Resistance R_AA
Superstructure] A --> F[Weather R_W
Waves + Wind] B --> B1[Function of Re Number] C --> C1[Function of Fr Number] F --> F1[Variable with Sea State] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Note (Adapted for Mermaid):** Since fuel efficiency cannot be drawn as a literal physical schematic in plain markdown, the three diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of power flow, influencing factors, and resistance decomposition — the three pillars of fuel-efficiency analysis in a KTU examination context. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC). State its typical range for a marine diesel engine used in fishing vessels.** **Model Answer:** SFOC is defined as the mass of fuel oil consumed per unit of brake power output per unit time. It is a measure of engine-level thermal efficiency. $$SFOC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 10^6}{BHP \times t} \quad \text{[g/kWh]}$$ **Typical range: 180–220 g/kWh** for modern marine four-stroke diesel engines used in fishing trawlers. A lower value indicates better engine efficiency. *(CO1, Bloom Level: Remember — 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is meant by Overall Propulsive Efficiency? List its four component efficiencies.** **Model Answer:** Overall Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_O$) is the ratio of effective power (useful work at the propeller) to the fuel energy input rate. It measures how effectively a vessel converts fuel chemical energy into useful motion through water. The four component efficiencies are: | Symbol | Efficiency | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | $\eta_E$ | Engine thermal efficiency | 0.35–0.45 | | $\eta_M$ | Mechanical efficiency | 0.85–0.92 | | $\eta_P$ | Propeller (open water) efficiency | 0.60–0.70 | | $\eta_H$ | Hull efficiency | 1.00–1.15 | $$\eta_O = \eta_E \cdot \eta_M \cdot \eta_P \cdot \eta_H$$ *(CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand — 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A 400-tonne fishing trawler is to be designed with the following particulars:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Service speed $V_S$ | 11 knots | | Total resistance $R_T$ | 30 kN | | Wake fraction $w$ | 0.22 | | Propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ | 0.56 | | Shaft efficiency $\eta_S$ | 0.97 | | BSFC | 210 g/kWh | | Voyage distance | 250 nm | | Fishing time (trawling) at 60% BHP | 35 hours | #### Part (a) — Compute the BHP and hourly fuel consumption at service speed. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert service speed $$V_S = 11 \times 0.5144 = 5.658 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute advance velocity $$V_A = V_S(1 - w) = 5.658 \times 0.78 = 4.413 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3:** Compute EHP $$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{1000} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 4.413}{1000} = 132.39 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4:** Compute DHP $$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{132.39}{0.56} = 236.41 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5:** Compute BHP $$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_S} = \frac{236.41}{0.97} = \mathbf{243.72 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 6:** Hourly fuel consumption $$\dot{m}_f = 0.210 \times 243.72 = \mathbf{51.18 \text{ kg/hr}}$$ *(CO2, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply — 7 marks)* #### Part (b) — Estimate total fuel consumption for the round trip including 35 hours of trawling. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Time for steaming (round trip = 2 × 250 = 500 nm) $$t_{steam} = \frac{500}{11} = 45.45 \text{ hr}$$ **Step 2:** Fuel during steaming at full BHP $$M_{steam} = 51.18 \times 45.45 = 2326.13 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3:** BHP during trawling (60% of full) $$BHP_{trawl} = 0.60 \times 243.72 = 146.23 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4:** Fuel rate during trawling $$\dot{m}_{f,trawl} = 0.210 \times 146.23 = 30.71 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Step 5:** Fuel during trawling $$M_{trawl} = 30.71 \times 35 = 1074.85 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 6:** Total fuel $$M_{total} = 2326.13 + 1074.85 = \mathbf{3400.98 \text{ kg} \approx 3.4 \text{ tonnes}}$$ **Step 7:** NMPT for the voyage $$NMPT = \frac{500}{3.4} = 147.06 \text{ nm/tonne}$$ *(CO3, CO4, Bloom Level: Analyze — 7 marks)* --- ### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **With the aid of a schematic, discuss the various factors affecting fuel efficiency of a fishing vessel. Explain how slow steaming and hull cleaning can improve NMPT by 15–25%.** #### Part (a) — List and explain the design and operational factors affecting fuel efficiency. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **A. Design-Stage Factors:** 1. **Hull Form Selection:** - Slenderness ratio $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ directly affects wave resistance. Optimum range for fishing trawlers: **5.5 to 6.5**. - A finer entry reduces wave-making resistance but increases frictional resistance. - Block coefficient $C_B$ optimum: **0.55–0.65** for trawlers. 2. **Propeller Design:** - Operating point should be near **BEP (Best Efficiency Point)** at service RPM. - Skewed propellers reduce cavitation-induced vibration and erosion. 3. **Engine Selection:** - Prefer marine diesel with **SFOC ≤ 200 g/kWh**. - Match engine to propeller via correct reduction ratio. 4. **Hull–Propeller Interaction:** - Even a 5% wake fraction error can shift BEP significantly. **B. Operational Factors:** 1. **Service Speed (Slow Steaming):** - Power ∝ $V^3$ — a 10% speed reduction yields ~27% fuel saving. 2. **Weather Routing:** - Avoiding head seas reduces added resistance $R_{AA}$ dramatically. 3. **Fishing Method:** - Trawling consumes 3–5× more fuel than longlining per kg catch. 4. **Load Management:** - Proper trim and ballast reduces resistance by 3–7%. **C. Maintenance Factors:** 1. **Hull Cleaning (Every 6–12 months):** - Removes biofouling; can restore 5–15% lost speed or save equivalent fuel. 2. **Propeller Polishing:** - Surface roughness > 75 microns triggers turbulent boundary layer. 3. **Engine Tuning:** - Re-adjusting injector timing and pressure lowers BSFC. *(CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand — 7 marks)* #### Part (b) — Quantitative analysis of slow steaming and hull cleaning benefits. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Case 1: Slow Steaming Calculation** Assume a vessel burns fuel $F_0$ at speed $V_0$. Reducing speed to $V_1 = 0.9 V_0$: $$\begin{aligned} R_T &\propto V^2 \quad \text{(calm water)} \\ P &\propto V^3 \\ F_0' &= F_0 \times \left(\frac{V_1}{V_0}\right)^3 = F_0 \times (0.9)^3 \\ &= F_0 \times 0.729 \end{aligned}$$ **Fuel saving percentage:** $$\text{Saving} = (1 - 0.729) \times 100 = 27.1\%$$ However, voyage time increases by 11.1%, so for fixed route, **net saving = ~20%**. **Case 2: Hull Cleaning Benefit** Biofouling increases frictional resistance $R_F$ by: $$\Delta R_F = R_F \left(\sqrt{\frac{\Delta C_F + C_F}{C_F}} - 1\right)$$ A typical 6-month biofouled hull shows roughness $k_s$ of 200–500 microns vs. clean hull of 150 microns. ITTC correlation gives $C_F$ increase of **15–25%**. Total resistance increase: $R_T' = R_T \times 1.20$ (say) Fuel increase ratio: $$\frac{F'}{F} = \frac{R_T' \cdot V}{R_T \cdot V} = 1.20$$ **Fuel penalty without cleaning = 20% extra**. After cleaning, this penalty is recovered → **NMPT improvement ≈ 20%**. **Combined Effect (Slow steaming + Cleaning):** $$\begin{aligned} NMPT_{new} &= NMPT_{old} \times 1.27 \times 1.20 \\ &= NMPT_{old} \times 1.524 \end{aligned}$$ A vessel originally achieving NMPT = 150 nm/tonne improves to: $$NMPT_{new} = 150 \times 1.524 \approx \mathbf{228.6 \text{ nm/tonne}}$$ *(CO3, CO4, CO5, Bloom Level: Evaluate — 7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion Trap:** Mixing **knots** with **m/s** without conversion factor **0.5144**. Marks deducted repeatedly across years. > 2. **Forgetting Wake Fraction:** Several students compute $EHP$ using $V_S$ instead of $V_A = V_S(1-w)$. This gives **20% error**. > 3. **Confusing BHP and EHP:** Writing $EHP = R_T \cdot V_A$ in kW is correct; some students mistakenly apply BSFC to EHP. BSFC applies ONLY to BHP. > 4. **BSFC Unit Trap:** BSFC must be converted from **g/kWh to kg/kWh** by dividing by 1000 before multiplication. > 5. **Forgetting Shaft Efficiency:** Many students equate DHP = BHP. Always apply $\eta_S$ separately. > 6. **Cubic Law Mis-application:** Slow steaming benefit uses $V^3$ only if resistance scales as $V^2$ (calm water). In waves, the exponent is higher. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Efficiency** > > ✅ Fuel efficiency is a **system-level property**, not just an engine property. > > ✅ **SFOC** is the engine-side metric (g/kWh); **NMPT** is the operational metric (nm/tonne). > > ✅ Always convert knots → m/s using factor **0.5144**. > > ✅ Apply **wake fraction** before computing EHP: $V_A = V_S(1-w)$. > > ✅ The chain: $\eta_O = \eta_E \cdot \eta_M \cdot \eta_P \cdot \eta_H$ — remember this product order. > > ✅ **Cubic Law of Speed:** Power ∝ $V^3$ → slow steaming is the single biggest fuel saver. > > ✅ Biofouling penalty = **15–25% extra fuel** → schedule hull cleaning every 6 months. > > ✅ Operating the propeller near **BEP** is mandatory for fuel efficiency. > > ✅ **BSFC** always multiplies **BHP**, never EHP or DHP. > > ✅ Voyage fuel = $\dfrac{BSFC \cdot BHP \cdot D}{V_S}$ — master this single equation. > > ✅ NMPT inversely tracks fuel cost — improving NMPT directly improves vessel profitability.

topics.title: Hull Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hull Form # 1. Hull Form — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Hull Form** refers to the precise three-dimensional geometric shape of a vessel's underwater and above-water body, defined mathematically through a set of coordinated sectional views (lines plan) consisting of the **Profile Plan**, **Body Plan**, and **Half-Breadth Plan**. It is the most fundamental design input that governs resistance, stability, seakeeping, propulsive efficiency, and fish-holding capacity of any fishing vessel. In naval architecture, the hull form is **NOT a single curve** — it is a continuous surface composed of three families of intersecting curves: 1. **Longitudinal sections** (Buttock lines / Waterlines) 2. **Transverse sections** (Station lines / Frame lines) 3. **Diagonal / Developable sections** The intersection of these curves generates the **lines plan**, which is the geometric "blueprint" of the hull. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moulded Clay" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Imagine sculpting a boat from a lump of wet clay.** You do not just carve random sides — you decide how *fat* the middle should be (fullness), how *pointed* the ends should be (entry and run), how *flat* or *V-shaped* the bottom should be (for cutting waves), and how *curved* the deck edge should be (sheer). Every one of these decisions is part of the **Hull Form**. Two boats of the same length can behave like completely different creatures in water simply because of these curves. For a fishing vessel, the hull form is the **silent engine** — it determines whether the boat cuts through waves efficiently, carries more catch, burns less fuel, and survives rough seas. ## 1.3 Principal Dimensions of Hull Form (Standard Symbols) | Symbol | Full Name | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **LOA** | Length Overall | Extreme end-to-end length | | **LPP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Length from FP to AP perpendicular | | **LWL** | Length at Waterline | Length of hull at design waterline | | **B** | Moulded Breadth | Maximum width inside the plating | | **D** | Moulded Depth | Vertical distance from baseline to main deck (moulded) | | **T** | Moulded Draught | Vertical depth of submerged hull from WL to baseline | | **∇** | Displacement Volume | Total volume of displaced water (m³) | | **W** | Displacement Weight | Weight of displaced water (tonnes) | | **CB** | Block Coefficient | Measure of hull fullness | | **CP** | Prismatic Coefficient | Measure of end taper | | **CM** | Midship Coefficient | Measure of midsection fullness | ## 1.4 Why Hull Form Matters for Fishing Vessels > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike cargo ships, fishing vessels must balance **multiple, often conflicting requirements**: > - Stability against external forces (wind, waves, trawl warps). > - Speed for chasing fish schools. > - Maneuverability for net operations. > - Deck area for catch handling. > - Hull strength for trawling loads. > > The hull form is the geometric "compromise" that satisfies all of these. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Form Coefficients — The Numerical DNA of Hull Form Hull form is rarely described by raw dimensions alone — it is described by **dimensionless coefficients** (ratios) that allow comparison between vessels of different sizes. ### 2.1.1 Block Coefficient (CB) $$\boxed{C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}}$$ - **Range**: Typically **0.35 (slender, fast)** to **0.85 (full, slow)**. - **Fishing vessel range**: **0.50 to 0.70** (a compromise between speed and capacity). - **Meaning**: How much of the rectangular "box" L × B × T is actually filled by the hull. ### 2.1.2 Prismatic Coefficient (CP) $$\boxed{C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}}$$ Where **AM** is the area of the **midship section (cross-sectional area at amidships)**. - **Range**: **0.55 to 0.85**. - **Meaning**: Compares displaced volume to an extruded prism of midsection area along the waterline length. Higher CP = fuller ends; Lower CP = finer ends. ### 2.1.3 Midship Coefficient (CM) $$\boxed{C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}}$$ - **Range**: **0.75 to 0.99**. - **Meaning**: Fullness of the midship cross-section. A high CM indicates a U-shaped hull; low CM indicates a V-shaped hull. ### 2.1.4 Waterplane Area Coefficient (CW) $$\boxed{C_W = \frac{A_{WL}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}}$$ - Where **AWL** is the waterplane area. - **Meaning**: Affects initial stability (GMT) and added resistance. ### 2.1.5 Vertical Prismatic Coefficient (CVP) $$\boxed{C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WL} \cdot T}}$$ ## 2.2 The Master Relationship (High-Yield) The three primary coefficients are mathematically linked: $$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is one of the **most-tested equations** in KTU naval architecture papers. It allows any one coefficient to be derived when the other two are known. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula | Application / Use Case | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$ | Displacement volume from block coefficient | m³ | | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement weight (ρ ≈ 1.025 t/m³ for seawater) | tonnes | | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | Master coefficient relation | dimensionless | | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{WL})$ | End taper indicator | dimensionless | | $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | Midsection fullness | dimensionless | | $C_W = A_{WL} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$ | Waterplane fullness | dimensionless | | $\overline{GMT} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ | Transverse metacentric height | m | | $\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius (I_T = transverse 2nd moment of WL) | m | | $S = 1.7 \cdot L_{WL} \cdot T + \nabla / T$ | Approx. wetted surface (Trawler empirical) | m² | | $L / B$ ratio | Length-to-beam ratio (typical trawler: 4 to 6) | dimensionless | | $B / T$ ratio | Beam-to-draught ratio (typical trawler: 2 to 3) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for coefficients**: **B**lock = whole hull, **P**rismatic = lengthwise taper, **M**idship = middle slice fullness. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Resistance Prediction**: A higher CB increases frictional + form drag → reduces top speed. - **Propulsion Matching**: Hull form dictates required engine power (via Admiralty Coefficient). - **Stability Analysis**: CM and CW feed directly into transverse stability calculations. - **Seakeeping**: Fine entry (low CP) reduces pitching in head seas. - **Fish-Hold Volume**: Higher ∇ → larger displacement → potentially larger hold (but slower). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Master Coefficient Relationship CB = CP × CM This is the **most important derivation** in hull form theory and a guaranteed KTU question. ### Step 1 — Recall the definitions We begin from the formal definitions of the three coefficients. $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}} $$ $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ ### Step 2 — Multiply CP and CM Take the product of the two "component" coefficients. $$ C_P \cdot C_M = \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Cancel the common term AM The **AM** in the numerator of CM cancels with the **AM** in the denominator of CP. $$ C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ ### Step 4 — Recognize the result The right-hand side is exactly the definition of **CB**. $$ \therefore \quad C_B = C_P \cdot C_M $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation**: The total fullness of the hull (CB) is the product of how full the ends are (CP) and how full the middle is (CM). You cannot change one without affecting the overall character of the hull. ## 3.2 Derivation: Displacement Weight from Hull Form ### Step 1 — Start with displaced volume $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T $$ ### Step 2 — Multiply by water density $$ \Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute numerical value for seawater For typical KTU problems, take **ρsw = 1.025 t/m³** (sea) or **1.000 t/m³** (freshwater). $$ \Delta = 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{[tonnes]} $$ ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example > **Given**: A fishing trawler with LWL = 25 m, B = 7 m, T = 3.2 m, CB = 0.60. > > Find displacement volume and weight. $$ \nabla = 0.60 \cdot 25 \cdot 7 \cdot 3.2 $$ $$ \nabla = 0.60 \cdot 560 $$ $$ \nabla = 336 \ \text{m}^3 $$ $$ \Delta = 1.025 \cdot 336 = 344.4 \ \text{tonnes} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Transverse Metacentric Radius $\overline{BM}$ ### Step 1 — Statement $$ \overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} $$ ### Step 2 — For a rectangular waterplane approximation If the waterplane is treated as an ellipse with semi-major axis a = LWL/2 and semi-minor b = B/2: $$ I_T = \frac{\pi \cdot a \cdot b^3}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{32} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute ∇ $$ \overline{BM} = \frac{\pi \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{32 \cdot \nabla} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute ∇ = CB · LWL · B · T $$ \overline{BM} = \frac{\pi \cdot B^2}{32 \cdot C_B \cdot T} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This shows **stability is inversely proportional to CB**. A slender hull (low CB) has much higher inherent stability than a full hull — a critical insight for fishing vessels carrying heavy catch or trawl gear. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hull Form vs. Vessel Type | Hull Characteristic | Trawler | Purse Seiner | Long Liner | Gill Netters | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Typical CB | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.50 – 0.60 | 0.45 – 0.55 | | L/B ratio | 4.0 – 5.0 | 4.5 – 5.5 | 5.0 – 6.5 | 5.5 – 7.0 | | Bow Form | Fine, raked | Flared, high sheer | Sharp, slender | Sharp, fine | | Stern Form | Cruiser / Transom | Cruiser | Cruiser | Cruiser | | Primary Need | Towing stability | Deck space | Speed + range | Speed | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hull Form Parameter Hierarchy (Mermaid Flow) This flowchart maps how raw dimensions, sectional areas, and coefficients interlink to define a complete hull form. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Principal Dimensions
LWL, B, T] --> B[Geometric Primitives
Rectangular Volume = LWL x B x T] C[Sectional Areas
AM, AWL] --> D[Displaced Volume nabla] B --> D A --> E[Form Coefficients
CB, CP, CM, CW] C --> E D --> E E --> F[Hydrostatic Properties
KB, BM, GMT] E --> G[Resistance Estimate
R_total] F --> H[Fishing Vessel Design
Stability + Speed + Capacity] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Lines Plan Composition (Block-Level Architecture) This diagram shows the three traditional views that, when combined, fully define the 3D hull form. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph proj1["Profile View"] P1[Sheer Line - Deck Edge Curve] P2[Base Line - Horizontal Reference] P3[Buttock Lines - Vertical Longitudinal Cuts] end subgraph proj2["Body Plan"] B1[Station Lines - Transverse Cross Sections] B2[Forward Stations - Right Side of Drawing] B3[Aft Stations - Left Side of Drawing] end subgraph proj3["Half Breadth Plan"] H1[Waterline Curves - Horizontal Cuts] H2[Diagonal Lines - Oblique Cuts] end proj1 --- proj2 proj2 --- proj3 proj3 --- proj1 OUTPUT[Complete 3D Hull Surface] proj1 --> OUTPUT proj2 --> OUTPUT proj3 --> OUTPUT ``` ## 4.3 Coefficient Relationship Flow (Mathematical Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD V[nabla - Displaced Volume] --> CB[CB - Block Coefficient] AM[AM - Midship Area] --> CB WL[LWL x B x T] --> CB V --> CP[CP - Prismatic Coefficient] AM --> CP WL2[AM x LWL] --> CP AM --> CM[CM - Midship Coefficient] BT[B x T] --> CM CB --> MASTER[CB = CP x CM] CP --> MASTER CM --> MASTER AWL[AWL - Waterplane Area] --> CW[CW - Waterplane Coefficient] LXB[LWL x B] --> CW ``` ## 4.4 Typical Trawler Hull Form — Annotated Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph STERN["Stern Region"] S1[Transom or Cruiser Stern] S2[Rudder Post] S3[Propeller Aperture] end subgraph MID["Midship Region"] M1[Midship Station Frame] M2[Max Beam Location] M3[Parallel Middle Body] M4[Bilge Radius Zone] end subgraph BOW["Bow Region"] F1[Forward Perpendicular FP] F2[Fine Entry Angle] F3[Stem Profile] F4[Sheer Line Peak] end MID --> BOW MID --> STERN ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The three regions (Bow, Midship, Stern) of the hull form have **distinct design functions**: Bow determines wave-cutting and entry; Midship determines displacement and stability; Stern determines flow into the propeller and course-keeping. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Block Coefficient (CB)** and **Prismatic Coefficient (CP)**. State their typical ranges for fishing trawlers. **Model Answer:** > The **Block Coefficient (CB)** is the ratio of the displaced volume (∇) of the hull to the product of its waterline length, moulded breadth, and moulded draught. > > $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$ > > It indicates how "full" the hull is. For fishing trawlers, CB typically ranges from **0.55 to 0.65**. > > The **Prismatic Coefficient (CP)** is the ratio of displaced volume to the product of midship cross-sectional area and waterline length. > > $$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$$ > > It describes the longitudinal distribution of volume. For trawlers, CP is typically **0.60 to 0.75**. Lower CP values indicate finer, more streamlined ends — better for higher speeds. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a **lines plan**? Name its three principal views and state the function of each. **Model Answer:** > A **lines plan** is the complete 2D engineering drawing set that defines the 3D geometric shape of a vessel's hull through a series of intersecting sectional curves. > > It comprises three principal views: > 1. **Profile Plan** (Side Elevation): Shows the **sheer line** (deck edge curve), **base line**, and **buttock lines** (vertical longitudinal sections). It defines length and vertical proportions. > 2. **Body Plan**: Shows the transverse **station lines** (cross-sections of the hull). Aft stations are drawn on the right; forward stations on the left. > 3. **Half-Breadth Plan**: Shows the **waterline curves** (horizontal sections) and **diagonal lines**, defining the horizontal shape at each height. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** For a fishing trawler with the following particulars: - Length at Waterline, **LWL = 28 m** - Moulded Breadth, **B = 7.5 m** - Moulded Draught, **T = 3.4 m** - Block Coefficient, **CB = 0.62** - Midship Coefficient, **CM = 0.92** - Water Density, **ρ = 1.025 t/m³** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Calculate the displaced volume, displacement weight, and prismatic coefficient of the vessel.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Displaced Volume (∇)** $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T $$ $$ \nabla = 0.62 \cdot 28 \cdot 7.5 \cdot 3.4 $$ $$ \nabla = 0.62 \cdot 714 $$ $$ \nabla = 442.68 \ \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Displacement Weight (Δ)** $$ \Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla $$ $$ \Delta = 1.025 \cdot 442.68 $$ $$ \Delta = 453.75 \ \text{tonnes} $$ **Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient (CP)** Using the master relationship: $$ C_B = C_P \cdot C_M \implies C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} $$ $$ C_P = \frac{0.62}{0.92} = 0.6739 \approx 0.674 $$ **Final Tabulated Answer:** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Displaced Volume | ∇ | 442.68 | m³ | | Displacement Weight | Δ | 453.75 | tonnes | | Prismatic Coefficient | CP | 0.674 | — | #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **If the waterplane area is 168 m², calculate the waterplane area coefficient (CW) and the vertical prismatic coefficient (CVP). Comment on the hull form.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Waterplane Area Coefficient (CW)** $$ C_W = \frac{A_{WL}}{L_{WL} \cdot B} = \frac{168}{28 \cdot 7.5} $$ $$ C_W = \frac{168}{210} = 0.800 $$ **Step 2: Vertical Prismatic Coefficient (CVP)** $$ C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WL} \cdot T} = \frac{442.68}{168 \cdot 3.4} $$ $$ C_{VP} = \frac{442.68}{571.2} = 0.775 $$ **Step 3: Comment on the Hull Form** - **CB = 0.62** → Moderately full;
