appropriate for a trawler needing stability and payload capacity. - **CP = 0.674** → Reasonably fine ends → moderate speed capability. - **CM = 0.92** → Very full midsection (U-shaped) → high transverse stability. - **CW = 0.80** → Full waterplane → contributes positively to initial stability (large GMT). > The hull is **a stable, displacement-type trawler** with a good balance between capacity and hydrodynamic efficiency — typical of a 28 m class stern trawler. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** With the aid of a sketch, explain the **principal dimensions and form coefficients** used to describe the hull form of a fishing vessel. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **List and define the principal dimensions of a fishing vessel hull with standard symbols.** **Model Answer:** | Symbol | Dimension | Definition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **LOA** | Length Overall | Maximum length measured between the extreme forward and aft points of the vessel. | | **LPP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Length from the forward perpendicular (FP) to the after perpendicular (AP). | | **LWL** | Length at Waterline | Length of the hull at the design waterline. | | **B** | Moulded Breadth | Maximum inside-plating width of the hull. | | **D** | Moulded Depth | Vertical distance from the baseline to the main deck (moulded line). | | **T** | Moulded Draught | Vertical distance from the waterline down to the baseline. | | **∇** | Displaced Volume | Volume of water displaced by the submerged hull, in m³. | | **Δ** | Displacement | Mass of displaced water (in tonnes), Δ = ρ·∇. | #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Define all major form coefficients and state their typical ranges for fishing vessels.** **Model Answer:** > **1. Block Coefficient (CB)** > $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$ > **Range (Trawlers): 0.55 – 0.68**. Indicates overall hull fullness. > > **2. Prismatic Coefficient (CP)** > $$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$$ > **Range: 0.60 – 0.75**. Indicates longitudinal taper of the ends. > > **3. Midship Coefficient (CM)** > $$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$ > **Range: 0.85 – 0.97**. Indicates fullness of the central cross-section. > > **4. Waterplane Area Coefficient (CW)** > $$C_W = \frac{A_{WL}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$ > **Range: 0.70 – 0.85**. Affects initial stability and added resistance. > > **5. Vertical Prismatic Coefficient (CVP)** > $$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WL} \cdot T}$$ > **Range: 0.65 – 0.85**. Indicates vertical distribution of volume. **Key Master Relation:** $$ \boxed{C_B = C_P \cdot C_M} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units**: Marks are deducted if displacement is given without "tonnes" or "m³" units. > 2. **Using freshwater density (1.000) instead of seawater (1.025)** when the problem states "sea" — read carefully! > 3. **Mixing up LOA, LPP, and LWL** — calculations MUST use LWL for coefficient formulas. > 4. **Not stating ranges of coefficients** in 7-mark theory questions — examiners specifically expect a numerical range. > 5. **In derivation questions**, missing the cancellation step in CB = CP · CM → full 7 marks lost. > 6. **Confusing block coefficient with prismatic coefficient** in answers — they are NOT the same and are easily swapped under exam stress. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Block — Hull Form** - ✅ Hull form = the 3D geometric shape of a vessel, defined by the **Lines Plan** (Profile + Body + Half-Breadth). - ✅ Six principal dimensions: **LOA, LPP, LWL, B, D, T**. - ✅ Five major form coefficients: **CB, CP, CM, CW, CVP**. - ✅ Master relation (most-tested): **CB = CP · CM**. - ✅ Displacement volume: **∇ = CB · LWL · B · T**. - ✅ Displacement weight: **Δ = ρ · ∇**; use **ρ = 1.025 t/m³** for seawater. - ✅ Fishing trawler ranges: **CB ≈ 0.55–0.65**, **CP ≈ 0.60–0.75**, **CM ≈ 0.85–0.97**. - ✅ Typical trawler ratios: **L/B ≈ 4–6**, **B/T ≈ 2–3**. - ✅ **Transom vs. Cruiser Stern**: Transom = simpler construction, common in purse seiners; Cruiser = better for trawlers (smoother propeller flow). - ✅ **Fine bow** → better seakeeping in head seas; **full midship** → higher stability and capacity. - ✅ Wetted surface approx. for trawlers: **S ≈ 1.7·L·T + ∇/T** (m²). - ✅ Higher CB → lower speed but greater stability & capacity — a fundamental design trade-off. - ✅ Lower CB → higher BM (stability radius) but lower displacement & cargo capacity. - ✅ Hull form affects **resistance, propulsion, stability, seakeeping, and fish-hold volume** — it is the central design compromise in fishing vessels.

topics.title: Investment Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Investment Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Investment Cost** (also termed **Initial Capital Cost**, **Capital Outlay**, or **First Cost**) in the context of fishing vessel economics is defined as the *total expenditure incurred from the conceptual design stage through to the commissioning and outfitting of a fishing vessel, making it operationally ready for sea trials and commercial fishing operations.*

Mathematically, the total investment cost $C_T$ is the summation of all major cost blocks attributable to the new-build project:

$$C_T = C_H + C_M + C_E + C_F + C_O + C_D + C_I$$

where:
- $C_H$ = Hull and structural cost
- $C_M$ = Main propulsion and auxiliary machinery cost
- $C_E$ = Electronic, navigation, and communication equipment cost
- $C_F$ = Fishing gear and deck machinery cost
- $C_O$ = Outfitting, accommodation, and furnishing cost
- $C_D$ = Design, engineering, classification, and consultancy cost
- $C_I$ = Insurance, financing, and project management cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The investment cost is a *one-time, sunk cost* incurred at the inception of the project. Unlike operating costs (fuel, crew wages, maintenance), it is **not repeated** during the vessel's economic life, but it directly determines the **fixed cost component**, **depreciation**, and the **capital recovery factor (CRF)** in any discounted cash-flow analysis of a fishing enterprise.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning to **open a small restaurant**:
- Before serving the *first plate of food*, you must pay for the building/space, kitchen equipment, furniture, licenses, and decoration.
- These are *one-time, upfront expenses* — you cannot recover them easily, and they are committed even before any income starts flowing.

Similarly, for a fishing vessel:
- Before the boat catches its **very first kilogram of fish**, the owner must already have paid for the **steel hull, engine, winches, nets, GPS, sonar, life-saving equipment, refrigeration plant**, and the architect/naval architect's fees.
- This entire upfront expenditure is the **Investment Cost**.

Think of it as the **"ticket price"** of entering the fishing business — it includes *everything* required to bring the vessel from a blank drawing board to a seaworthy, fish-ready platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric used by shipbuilders worldwide:** Investment cost is commonly benchmarked against vessel **tonnage (GRT)**, **length overall (LOA)**, or **deadweight (DWT)** to allow comparison between vessels of different sizes. A modern purse-seiner of ~25 m LOA may have an investment cost in the range of **₹ 4 – 8 crore (USD 500,000 – 1,000,000)** depending on fishing systems installed.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Size exponent (cost-vs-size) | $n$ | **0.55 – 0.80** (dimensionless) |
| Steel density | $\rho_{steel}$ | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Hull steel fraction | $f_{steel}$ | **25 – 35 %** of $C_T$ |
| Machinery fraction | $f_{mach}$ | **20 – 30 %** of $C_T$ |
| Electronics fraction | $f_{elec}$ | **5 – 12 %** of $C_T$ |
| Fishing gear fraction | $f_{gear}$ | **8 – 18 %** of $C_T$ |
| Outfitting fraction | $f_{out}$ | **8 – 15 %** of $C_T$ |
| Design/overhead fraction | $f_{des}$ | **5 – 10 %** of $C_T$ |
| Reference cost index | $I_{ref}$ | Year-specific (e.g., base year) |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Investment Cost Components

Investment cost components may be classified along two primary axes used by naval architects:

**Axis A — By Physical Subsystem:**
1. Hull construction (steel plate, fabrication, welding, painting)
2. Propulsion system (main engine, gearbox, propeller, shafting)
3. Auxiliary systems (generators, pumps, compressors, hydraulics)
4. Fishing systems (winches, net haulers, lines, nets, hooks)
5. Fish handling & preservation (refrigeration, ice plant, RSW, freezers)
6. Navigation & electronics (radar, GPS, echo sounder, sonar, VHF, AIS)
7. Safety & life-saving (life rafts, fire-fighting, EPIRBs)
8. Accommodation & galley (cabins, bunks, mess, pantry, HVAC)
9. Documentation & certification (classification, registration, flag fees)

**Axis B — By Cost Type:**
- **Direct Cost:** Material + Labour directly tied to fabrication
- **Indirect Cost:** Design, supervision, insurance, profit margin, overheads
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The estimation of investment cost progresses through five sequential logic layers:

#### Layer 1 — Identify the Reference Vessel
- Select a *similar, already-built vessel* whose cost is known (either historical actuals or quoted price).
- Document its principal particulars: $L_{OA}$, $L_{pp}$, $B$, $D$, $T$, $\Delta$, $GRT$, $DWT$, installed power, fishing system type.

#### Layer 2 — Establish Cost Indices
- Locate the **base-year cost index** $I_1$ at the time the reference vessel was built.
- Obtain the **current-year cost index** $I_2$ (e.g., from *Lloyd's Register Shipbuilding Cost Index*, *Norwegian Skipsteknikk*, or regional indices published by Indian shipyards).
- Compute the **escalation multiplier** $k_I = I_2 / I_1$.

#### Layer 3 — Apply the Size-Scaling Law
- The most fundamental rule of marine cost engineering: *cost does NOT scale linearly with size.*
- Use the **power-law relationship** based on dimensional analysis and empirical shipyard data:

$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \frac{I_2}{I_1}$$

- The exponent $n$ accounts for the **economies of scale** — larger vessels gain cost advantage because hull volume (and hence steel weight) grows faster than length.
- For most fishing vessels, **$n \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$**.

#### Layer 4 — Apply Cost Multipliers for Complexity
- A vessel with sophisticated equipment (RSW plant, acoustic instruments) carries a higher per-tonne cost than a simple gill-netter.
- Introduce a **complexity factor** $k_C$ (typically $1.0$ for reference vessel, $1.05$–$1.30$ for upgraded systems).

#### Layer 5 — Sum All Sub-Blocks
- Apply fractional breakdown to obtain a complete cost-build-up and cross-check against parametric estimates.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_T = \sum C_i$ | Total investment cost = sum of all sub-systems | Currency (₹ / USD) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | Size-and-time scaling law | $n \in [0.55, 0.80]$ |
| 3 | $C = C_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{GT_2}{GT_1}\right)^{0.65} \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | GRT-based scaling | $GT$ = Gross Tonnage |
| 4 | $C = k \cdot L_{OA}^{\,a} \cdot B^{\,b} \cdot D^{\,c}$ | Multi-variable parametric cost | $k$ = empirical constant |
| 5 | $C_{steel} = W_{steel} \cdot P_{steel} \cdot (1 + f_{fab})$ | Hull steel cost | $W$ in tonnes, $P$ in ₹/t |
| 6 | $W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{hull} \cdot f_{waste}$ | Steel weight from hull volume | $f_{waste} \approx 1.10$–$1.20$ |
| 7 | $C_{mach} = P_{inst} \cdot p_{mach}$ | Machinery cost vs installed power | $p_{mach}$ in ₹/kW |
| 8 | $C_R = \dfrac{C_T \cdot i \cdot (1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor (annualized) | $i$ = interest, $N$ = life (yr) |
| 9 | $D_{annual} = \dfrac{C_T - S_V}{N}$ | Straight-line depreciation per year | $S_V$ = scrap value |
| 10 | $\eta_{scale} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{n-1}$ | Scale economy ratio (if $n<1$, big is cheaper per unit length) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `n < 1` is critical:** If $n = 1$, cost scales *linearly* with length — every additional metre of vessel costs the same. If $n < 1$, a doubling of length increases cost by less than double — confirming the famous naval architect's maxim: *"a big ship is cheaper per tonne than a small one."*

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Shipyard Tendering:** Yard owners use these formulas to produce **indicative quotations** to fishing companies within ±15 % accuracy.
- **Government Subsidy Schemes (India):** The Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying uses parametric cost formulas to determine subsidy ceilings under the *PMMSY (Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana)* scheme.
- **Bank Loan Appraisal:** Financial institutions (NABARD, commercial banks) evaluate the loan-to-asset ratio using these cost estimates before sanctioning vessel-purchase loans.
- **Insurance Valuation:** Marine underwriters use investment cost as the basis for *hull insurance premium* calculation.

---

### 2.4 Sources of Cost Variability

1. **Geographic location of shipyard** (Cochin, Colombo, Guangzhou, or Istanbul — each carries different labour rate).
2. **Series production vs. one-off build** (first vessel in a class is 15–25 % costlier).
3. **Flag state and classification society** (IRS, BV, LR, DNV — each adds different fees).
4. **Exchange rate fluctuations** for imported machinery.
5. **Owner-furnished equipment** vs. yard-furnished equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Size-Scaling Law

The classic power-law cost relationship used in marine cost engineering is **empirically derived** from dimensional reasoning combined with statistical regression of historical shipyard data. The logical derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Recognize the dominant physical dependency.**

For a geometrically similar vessel (same form coefficients, same proportions), the *hull volume* $V_{hull}$ scales with the cube of any linear dimension $L$:

$$V_{hull} \propto L^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the hull steel weight.**

Since steel weight $W_{steel}$ equals density $\rho$ times volume times waste factor $f_w$:

$$W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{hull} \cdot f_{waste} \propto L^3$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that cost is not all material.**

Cost has a *material component* (proportional to weight, hence to $L^3$) and a *labour/overhead component* (proportional to surface area $\propto L^2$ or to length $L^1$). A weighted combination gives:

$$C \propto L^{m}, \quad \text{where } 1 \le m \le 3$$

**Step 4 — Empirical regression fixes the exponent.**

Fitting regression curves to thousands of historical vessel quotations shows $m \approx 1.6$–$2.0$ for most vessel types. When cost is normalized per *unit length*, the exponent collapses to:

$$n = m - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final formula.**

Including time escalation via cost index $I$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A 20 m LOA fishing trawler was built in 2015 at a cost of **₹ 1.50 crore**. Estimate the investment cost of a geometrically similar trawler of **25 m LOA** to be built in **2025**, given:

- Size exponent $n = 0.65$
- Cost index in 2015: $I_{2015} = 105$
- Cost index in 2025: $I_{2025} = 168$

**Given Data:**

$$L_1 = 20 \text{ m}, \quad C_1 = ₹ 1.50 \text{ crore}, \quad L_2 = 25 \text{ m}$$
$$n = 0.65, \quad I_1 = 105, \quad I_2 = 168$$

**Step 1 — Compute the length ratio.**

$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute the size-scaling factor.**

$$\left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n = (1.25)^{0.65}$$

Taking logarithm:

$$\ln(1.25)^{0.65} = 0.65 \cdot \ln(1.25) = 0.65 \cdot 0.2231 = 0.1450$$

Therefore:

$$(1.25)^{0.65} = e^{0.1450} \approx 1.1561$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cost-index escalation factor.**

$$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{168}{105} = 1.6000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total cost multiplier.**

$$k_{total} = 1.1561 \times 1.6000 = 1.8498$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new investment cost.**

$$C_2 = C_1 \cdot k_{total} = 1.50 \text{ crore} \times 1.8498$$

$$C_2 \approx 2.775 \text{ crore}$$

**Final Answer:** The estimated investment cost of the 25 m trawler in 2025 is **≈ ₹ 2.77 crore**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost Breakdown for a Modern 22 m Trawler

| Cost Block | Fraction of $C_T$ | Approx. Value (₹ lakh) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull steel + fabrication | **30 %** | 75.0 | Includes plate, welding, blasting, painting |
| Main engine + propulsion | **20 %** | 50.0 | Includes gearbox, propeller, shaft |
| Auxiliaries (gen, hydraulics) | **10 %** | 25.0 | 2× generators, pumps, compressors |
| Electronics + navigation | **10 %** | 25.0 | Radar, GPS, sonar, VHF, AIS |
| Fishing gear + deck machinery | **15 %** | 37.5 | Winches, net hauler, trawl gear |
| Refrigeration / fish hold | **5 %** | 12.5 | Plate freezer or RSW |
| Outfitting + accommodation | **5 %** | 12.5 | Cabins, galley, HVAC |
| Design + classification | **5 %** | 12.5 | Naval architect + IRS fees |
| **Total $C_T$** | **100 %** | **₹ 250 lakh (₹ 2.5 cr)** | Indicative only |

---

### 3.4 Verification Using Alternative Parametric Method

**Cross-check using GRT-based formula:**

For a 22 m vessel with $GT \approx 95$:
$$C_2 = C_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{GT_2}{GT_1}\right)^{0.65}$$

Reference: A 20 m vessel with $GT_1 = 70$ cost ₹ 1.50 cr in 2015. Escalated to 2025 first:
$$C_{1,2025} = 1.50 \times \dfrac{168}{105} = 2.40 \text{ cr}$$

Then scaled to 22 m:
$$C_2 = 2.40 \times \left(\dfrac{95}{70}\right)^{0.65} = 2.40 \times (1.357)^{0.65}$$
$$(1.357)^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \cdot \ln(1.357)} = e^{0.65 \cdot 0.3053} = e^{0.1984} \approx 1.2195$$
$$C_2 = 2.40 \times 1.2195 \approx 2.927 \text{ cr}$$

This is consistent with the size-scaling result (≈ 2.77 cr) within typical estimation accuracy (±15 %), confirming the method's reliability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Investment Cost Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Define Vessel Particulars]
    B --> C[Select Reference Vessel]
    C --> D{Is Cost Data Available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Obtain Reference Cost C1]
    D -- No --> F[Use Parametric Model]
    E --> G[Identify Cost Indices I1 and I2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Size Scaling Law n]
    H --> I[Compute Size Factor L2 over L1 power n]
    I --> J[Compute Time Factor I2 over I1]
    J --> K[Multiply All Factors]
    K --> L[Determine Base Cost C2]
    L --> M[Add Complexity Factor kC]
    M --> N[Add Regional Labour Factor kR]
    N --> O[Subdivide into Cost Blocks]
    O --> P[Hull]
    O --> Q[Machinery]
    O --> R[Electronics]
    O --> S[Fishing Gear]
    O --> T[Outfitting]
    O --> U[Design Overheads]
    P --> V[Total Investment Cost CT]
    Q --> V
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Cross-Check with Parametric Method]
    W --> X[Final Approved Budget]
```

### 4.2 Cost Composition Pie-Chart (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
pie title Investment Cost Distribution for 22 m Trawler
    "Hull and Structure" : 30
    "Main Propulsion" : 20
    "Auxiliary Systems" : 10

```

### 4.3 Cost-Build-Up Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Total Investment Cost CT] --> CH[Hull Cost CH]
    CT --> CM[Machinery Cost CM]
    CT --> CE[Electronics Cost CE]
    CT --> CF[Fishing Gear Cost CF]
    CT --> CO[Outfitting Cost CO]
    CT --> CD[Design Cost CD]
    CH --> CH1[Steel Plate]
    CH --> CH2[Fabrication Labour]
    CH --> CH3[Paint and Corrosion Protection]
    CM --> CM1[Main Engine]
    CM --> CM2[Gearbox and Shaft]
    CM --> CM3[Propeller]
    CM --> CM4[Auxiliary Generators]
    CE --> CE1[Radar and GPS]
    CE --> CE2[Sonar and Echo Sounder]
    CE --> CE3[Communication VHF AIS]
    CF --> CF1[Winches and Net Haulers]
    CF --> CF2[Nets Lines Hooks]
    CO --> CO1[Crew Cabins]
    CO --> CO2[Galley and Provision]
    CD --> CD1[Naval Architect Fee]
    CD --> CD2[Classification Fee]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each terminal node (e.g., CH1, CM4) is a *line item* in the shipyard's quotation. When aggregated, they yield the *total investment cost* the owner must finance before the vessel becomes operational.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Investment Cost" of a fishing vessel. List any four major cost components.*

**Model Answer:**

Investment cost is the **total one-time capital expenditure** required to design, construct, equip, and commission a fishing vessel, making it ready for commercial operation. It is a *sunk cost* incurred at project initiation and forms the basis for calculating depreciation, fixed cost, and loan repayment schedules.

**Four major cost components:**
1. Hull and structural cost ($C_H$) — steel, fabrication, painting.
2. Main propulsion cost ($C_M$) — engine, gearbox, propeller.
3. Fishing gear and deck machinery cost ($C_F$) — winches, nets, lines.
4. Electronics and navigation cost ($C_E$) — radar, GPS, sonar, communication.

*(Any four valid components accepted; award full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the size-scaling law used in marine cost estimation. Explain the significance of the exponent $n$.*

**Model Answer:**

The size-scaling law is given by:

$$\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$$

**Significance of $n$:**
- $n$ is the **size exponent** that quantifies the economies of scale in shipbuilding.
- For fishing vessels, $n$ typically lies in the range **0.55 – 0.80**.
- Since $n < 1$, cost grows *slower* than length — a larger vessel costs less *per metre* than a smaller one.
- This empirical constant is obtained from regression analysis of historical shipyard data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the major cost components that constitute the investment cost of a fishing vessel. Provide a typical percentage breakdown.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 24 m fishing trawler has an estimated build cost of ₹ 3.20 crore in the year 2023. Using the size-scaling law, estimate the cost of a geometrically similar 30 m trawler to be built in 2026. Take size exponent $n = 0.70$ and cost indices as $I_{2023} = 142$ and $I_{2026} = 175$.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Cost Components Breakdown:**

| S.No | Component | Description | Typical % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hull & Structure** | Steel plate, frames, welding, blasting, painting | 25 – 35 % |
| 2 | **Main Propulsion** | Main engine, gearbox, propeller, shafting | 15 – 22 % |
| 3 | **Auxiliary Machinery** | Generators, compressors, pumps, hydraulics | 8 – 12 % |
| 4 | **Fishing Gear & Deck Machinery** | Winches, net haulers, trawl gear, purse seine | 8 – 18 % |
| 5 | **Electronics & Navigation** | Radar, GPS, sonar, VHF, AIS, autopilot | 5 – 12 % |
| 6 | **Refrigeration / Fish Hold** | Ice plant, RSW, plate freezer, insulated hold | 4 – 8 % |
| 7 | **Outfitting & Accommodation** | Cabins, galley, HVAC, sanitation | 8 – 15 % |
| 8 | **Design, Classification & Overheads** | Naval architect, classification society, insurance | 5 – 10 % |

The hull typically dominates because of material weight (≈ 25 % of $C_T$ for steel vessels). Modern trawlers with sophisticated electronics show a higher electronics share (up to 12 %). Owner-furnished items reduce the yard's scope but do not reduce investment cost — only its distribution.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$$L_1 = 24 \text{ m}, \quad C_1 = 3.20 \text{ crore}, \quad L_2 = 30 \text{ m}$$
$$n = 0.70, \quad I_1 = 142, \quad I_2 = 175$$

**Step 1:** Length ratio:
$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{30}{24} = 1.25$$

**Step 2:** Size factor:
$$(1.25)^{0.70} = e^{0.70 \cdot \ln(1.25)} = e^{0.70 \cdot 0.2231} = e^{0.1562} \approx 1.1690$$

**Step 3:** Time escalation factor:
$$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{175}{142} = 1.2324$$

**Step 4:** Combined factor:
$$k_{total} = 1.1690 \times 1.2324 = 1.4406$$

**Step 5:** New investment cost:
$$C_2 = 3.20 \times 1.4406 \approx 4.610 \text{ crore}$$

**Answer:** The estimated cost of the 30 m trawler in 2026 is **₹ 4.61 crore**.

---

**Question B — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various cost estimation methods used in fishing vessel design. Compare their accuracy and applicability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 28 m purse-seiner cost ₹ 5.50 crore to build in 2020. Compute its estimated replacement cost in 2025, given the cost index rose from $I_{2020} = 125$ to $I_{2025} = 162$. If the owner wants to build a larger 35 m vessel geometrically similar, with size exponent $n = 0.72$, what would be the cost (assuming cost index in build year remains 162)?

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Cost Estimation Methods:**

1. **Detailed (Bottom-Up) Estimation** — Itemizes every nut, bolt, plate, and labour hour. Accuracy ±5 %, but extremely time-consuming; used for final tender-stage quotes.
2. **Comparative (Reference Vessel) Method** — Scales cost of a similar existing vessel using size and time indices. Accuracy ±15 %. Used in *concept and contract design phases*. **Most common in KTU problems.**
3. **Parametric Method** — Uses statistical regression equations: $C = k \cdot L^a \cdot B^b \cdot D^c \cdot (P)^{d}$. Accuracy ±20 %. Used for feasibility studies.
4. **Cost-per-Tonne Methods** — Applies $/GRT or $/DWT multipliers from industry databases. Accuracy ±25 %. Used for preliminary budgetary quotes.
5. **Learning Curve / Series Production Adjustment** — Adjusts cost for the $N$-th vessel in a series using Wright's law. Accuracy ±10 % for series production.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Part (i):** Time-escalated cost of same vessel in 2025:
$$C_{2025} = C_{2020} \cdot \dfrac{I_{2025}}{I_{2020}} = 5.50 \times \dfrac{162}{125}$$
$$C_{2025} = 5.50 \times 1.296 = 7.128 \text{ crore}$$

**Part (ii):** Cost of 35 m vessel in 2025:

Length ratio:
$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{35}{28} = 1.25$$

Size factor:
$$(1.25)^{0.72} = e^{0.72 \cdot 0.2231} = e^{0.1606} \approx 1.1742$$

Now applying both size and time scaling from 2020 base:
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times (1.25)^{0.72} \times \dfrac{162}{125}$$
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times 1.1742 \times 1.296$$
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times 1.5218 \approx 8.370 \text{ crore}$$

**Answer:**
- Replacement cost in 2025 (same 28 m): **₹ 7.13 crore**
- New-build cost of 35 m vessel in 2025: **₹ 8.37 crore**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Index mismatch:** Many students mistakenly use the *same year* index for numerator and denominator — always confirm the base year and target year explicitly.
> 2. **Exponent confusion:** $n$ applies to the length ratio **before** multiplication, NOT to the entire expression. Compute $(L_2/L_1)^n$ *first*, then multiply by $I_2/I_1$.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Cost indices must be from the **same source** (don't mix Lloyd's index with regional index). If stated generically, assume consistency.
> 4. **Rounding errors:** Round only the final answer; intermediate steps should retain 4–5 significant figures.
> 5. **Currency unit:** Always state answer with unit (₹ crore / ₹ lakh / USD million).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Investment Cost**

- **Definition:** One-time, upfront capital outlay for new-build fishing vessel — a *sunk cost*.
- **Total cost identity:** $C_T = C_H + C_M + C_E + C_F + C_O + C_D + C_I$.
- **Master formula:** $\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$.
- **Size exponent:** $n \in [0.55, 0.80]$ for fishing vessels; typical value **0.65**.
- **Cost index:** Always use same index series; verify base year and current year.
- **Hull dominates:** ~30 % of total cost in steel fishing vessels.
- **Economies of scale:** Because $n < 1$, *larger vessels are cheaper per metre*.
- **GRT-based alternative:** $C \propto (GT)^{0.65}$ — useful when length data unavailable.
- **Capital recovery:** Use $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ to annualize investment cost.
- **Cross-check:** Always validate the result using a second method (e.g., GRT-based vs length-based).
- **Subsidy relevance:** Indian PMMSY scheme uses these formulas to fix subsidy ceilings.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Don't confuse *investment cost* with *operating cost* — the former is one-time, the latter is recurring.
- **Order of computation:** Length ratio → size factor → index ratio → multiplication → final cost.
- **Significant figures:** Carry 4 digits in intermediate steps; round final to 2 decimal places.
- **Always include units:** ₹ crore / ₹ lakh / USD — never leave the answer unit-less.

---

*End of Notes — Investment Cost | Module 2 | OENST833 — Basics of Fishing Vessel Design*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Revenues and Costs, Design of Main Dimensions and Form: Parent Vessel Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operating Revenues in Fishing Vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Operating Revenue* in a fishing vessel is the total monetary inflow generated over a fiscal year from the sale of the primary catch (target species), secondary catch (by-catch), and any auxiliary income such as charter hire, subsidies, and processing premiums.

The **operating revenue** is a function of three primary variables:
1. **Annual catch capacity** (tonnes/year)
2. **Market price per unit mass** (₹/kg or $/kg)
3. **Operational uptime** (fishing days per year)

Mathematically expressed as:

$$R_{annual} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times P_i) + R_{aux}$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Catch of species *i* (tonnes)
- $P_i$ = Price of species *i* (currency/tonne)
- $R_{aux}$ = Auxiliary revenue (charter, subsidies, etc.)
- *n* = Number of species targeted

### Intuitive Analogy: The Fishing Boat as a Mobile Factory
Think of a fishing vessel as a **floating, mobile factory** that sails out, harvests a raw material (fish), processes it (sorting, icing, gutting, freezing), and delivers a finished product to the market. Just like any factory, the **revenue** depends on:
- How much product you make (catch volume)
- How good your product is (species quality, freshness)
- How often the factory operates (days at sea)
- What the market is willing to pay (price per kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> For fishing vessels, revenue is **highly stochastic** (random) due to weather, fish availability (stock dynamics), and market price fluctuations. This uncertainty is a critical design input.

## 1.2 Operating Costs in Fishing Vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Operating Costs* (also called *Voyage Costs* or *Running Costs*) are the day-to-day expenditures incurred to keep the vessel operational, excluding the initial capital investment (hull, machinery, fishing gear procurement).

The operating costs are conventionally classified into **two categories** as per KTU syllabus:

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Fixed Operating Costs** | Insurance, registration, depreciation, crew salary (off-season) |
| **Variable Operating Costs** | Fuel, oil, lubricants, ice, salt, bait, food, crew share, port dues, maintenance |

## 1.3 Design of Main Dimensions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The *Main Dimensions* of a fishing vessel comprise the four principal geometric parameters that define its size and form: **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**, **Breadth (B)**, **Depth (D)**, and **Draught (T)**. These are derived from displacement, speed, stability, and capacity requirements.

## 1.4 Parent Vessel Data Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Parent Vessel Data Analysis* is a **statistical-based preliminary design methodology** in which the main dimensions and form coefficients of a new fishing vessel are estimated by statistically analyzing the geometric and performance parameters of a fleet of similar, successfully operating vessels (called *parent vessels* or *parent hull forms*).

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Family Resemblance" Approach
Imagine you are designing a new Maruti Swift. Instead of inventing dimensions from scratch, you would look at all existing successful hatchbacks in that class (Hyundai i20, Tata Altroz, etc.), collect their dimensions and performance, and **average them out with appropriate scaling**. The "parents" (existing vessels) pass down their best traits to the "child" (new design). This is exactly the philosophy behind parent vessel data analysis — leveraging **empirical wisdom** of proven designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Significance:**
> The parent vessel approach is the **most widely used preliminary design tool** in small-to-medium fishing vessel design because the statistical database of similar vessels in a region is rich, and the method requires minimal theoretical naval architecture input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Operating Revenues

The annual revenue $R$ of a fishing vessel is structured as:

$$R = C \cdot P \cdot \eta_{util} + R_{subsidy} + R_{charter}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Total catch potential (tonnes/year) — vessel capacity
- $P$ = Average ex-vessel price (₹/kg)
- $\eta_{util}$ = Utilisation factor (0.5–0.85 for trawlers, depends on weather windows)

### Revenue Sensitivity Factors:
- **Species composition** (shrimp vs. finfish — shrimps command 3–5× price)
- **Catch seasonality** (monsoon bans in India, e.g., 47-day trawling ban)
- **Distance to fishing grounds** (longer voyages → more days lost in transit)
- **Onboard preservation** (ice vs. freezer — affects landed quality & price)

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Operating Costs

The total annual operating cost $C_{op}$ is the sum of distinct sub-costs:

$$C_{op} = C_{fuel} + C_{crew} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} + C_{port} + C_{stores} + C_{misc}$$

### Cost Component Analysis:

**1. Fuel Cost (typically 30–45% of variable cost):**
$$C_{fuel} = P_{fuel} \cdot SFOC \cdot P_{engine} \cdot T_{op}$$
Where $SFOC$ = Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (g/kWh), $P_{engine}$ = Engine power (kW), $T_{op}$ = Annual operating hours.

**2. Crew Cost (wages + share of catch revenue):**
$$C_{crew} = N_{crew} \cdot S_{month} \cdot 12 + \alpha \cdot R$$
Where $\alpha$ is the crew share fraction (typically 25–40% of gross revenue in trawlers).

**3. Maintenance Cost:** Generally **2–4% of capital cost per annum** for steel vessels; higher for FRP.

**4. Insurance:** Typically **1.5–2.5% of vessel value** annually.

**5. Port & Harbour Dues:** Vary by port; includes berth hire, pilotage, conservancy.

**6. Stores:** Ice, salt, bait, food, ropes, nets, consumables.

## 2.3 Net Operating Income (NOI) & Profitability

The annual profit (or surplus cash flow) is:

$$P_{annual} = R - C_{op} - C_{capital}$$

Where $C_{capital}$ is the annualised capital cost (loan repayment + interest). The **profitability indices** used in fishing vessel design are:

- **Payback Period (PBP):** $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{P_{annual}}$
- **Return on Investment (ROI):** $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{P_{annual}}{C_{capital}} \times 100\%$
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR):** $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{R}{C_{op} + C_{capital}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most valuation failures in economic analysis questions occur because students forget to subtract the **annualised capital cost** from the operating surplus to obtain the *true* profit.

## 2.4 Main Dimensions — Conceptual Framework

The four main dimensions are not independent. They are linked through:

### (a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)
LBP is governed by **speed requirement** and **hull form** via the Froude Number:

$$Fn = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot LBP}}$$

For displacement-mode fishing vessels, $Fn \approx 0.30 - 0.40$.

### (b) Breadth (B)
Breadth is governed by **stability** (GM requirement) and **deck area** (gear handling):

$$B = f(\Delta, \text{Block Coefficient}, \text{Stability criterion})$$

Typical $L/B$ ratio for fishing trawlers: **4.0 to 5.5**.

### (c) Depth (D)
Depth is governed by **freeboard**, **subdivision**, and **structural strength**:

$$D = T + f_{board}$$

Typical freeboard for trawlers: **0.4 m to 0.8 m**.

### (d) Draught (T)
Draught is governed by **displacement** and **length-displacement ratio**:

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$$

Where $C_b$ is the block coefficient (typical 0.55–0.65 for fishing trawlers).

## 2.5 Form Coefficients — The Shape DNA

The "form" of a vessel is captured by **dimensionless ratios** (form coefficients):

| Coefficient | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (Trawler) |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Block ($C_b$) | $\nabla / (LBP \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Hull fullness | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| Prismatic ($C_p$) | $\nabla / (A_m \cdot LBP)$ | Fineness of ends | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Midship ($C_m$) | $A_m / (B \cdot T)$ | Section fullness | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Waterplane ($C_{wp}$) | $A_w / (LBP \cdot B)$ | Deck plan shape | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Longitudinal Prismatic (L/Cp) | — | End form fullness | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In parent vessel analysis, the form coefficients are typically **inherited from the parents** and only the **scale (length/displacement)** is changed. This drastically reduces the design effort.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $R = C \cdot P \cdot \eta_{util}$ | Annual Revenue | ₹/year |
| 2 | $C_{op} = \sum C_i$ | Annual Operating Cost | ₹/year |
| 3 | $P_{annual} = R - C_{op} - C_{annualised}$ | Annual Profit | ₹/year |
| 4 | $\text{PBP} = C_{cap} / P_{annual}$ | Payback Period | years |
| 5 | $\Delta = C_b \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$ | Displacement | tonnes |
| 6 | $Fn = V / \sqrt{g \cdot LBP}$ | Froude Number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_b = \nabla / (LBP \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Block Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_p = \nabla / (A_m \cdot LBP)$ | Prismatic Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_m = A_m / (B \cdot T)$ | Midship Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{BCR} = R / (C_{op} + C_{cap})$ | Benefit-Cost Ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | $L_{parent}^{new} = L_{parent}^{old} \cdot (\Delta_{new}/\Delta_{old})^{1/3}$ | Cube-root scaling | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use of Cube-Root Scaling:** When scaling a parent vessel to a new displacement, dimensions scale as the cube root of the displacement ratio because volume $\propto L^3$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The cost-revenue framework is the **backbone of techno-economic feasibility studies** for fishing vessels — a mandatory submission for Government of India (Ministry of Fisheries) subsidies under the **PMMSY (Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana)**. Without rigorous cost analysis, no fishing vessel can be financed by a nationalised bank.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annualised Capital Cost

The annualised capital cost converts a one-time capital expenditure $C_{cap}$ into an equivalent yearly cost using a **capital recovery factor (CRF)**.

Starting from the standard present-worth equation for an annuity:

$$C_{cap} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^k}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Annual payment
- $i$ = Annual interest rate (decimal)
- $N$ = Economic life (years)

This geometric series sums to:

$$C_{cap} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^N}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$A = C_{cap} \cdot \dfrac{i \cdot (1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$$

Define the **Capital Recovery Factor**:

$$\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{annualised} = C_{cap} \cdot \text{CRF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic:** This derivation assumes the vessel has a finite economic life $N$ (typically 15–25 years for steel trawlers, 12–18 years for FRP) and a discount rate $i$ equal to the cost of capital.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fuel Cost Component

Starting with the basic relationship of fuel consumed per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{SFOC \cdot P_{engine}}{1000} \quad \text{(kg/hour)}$$

Where $SFOC$ is in g/kWh. The total fuel used annually is:

$$m_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot T_{op} = \dfrac{SFOC \cdot P_{engine} \cdot T_{op}}{1000}$$

The cost is:

$$C_{fuel} = m_{fuel} \cdot P_{fuel} = \dfrac{SFOC \cdot P_{engine} \cdot T_{op} \cdot P_{fuel}}{1000}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Power × time = energy; energy × SFOC (g/kWh) = fuel mass; fuel mass × price = cost.

## 3.3 Derivation: Block Coefficient from Displacement

Starting from the fundamental definition of volumetric displacement:

$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\nabla$ = Volumetric displacement (m³)
- $\Delta$ = Mass displacement (tonnes)
- $\rho$ = Water density (1.025 t/m³ for seawater)

By definition of the block coefficient:

$$C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{LBP \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Substituting $\nabla$:

$$C_b = \dfrac{\Delta / \rho}{LBP \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Rearranging for any unknown dimension (e.g., B):

$$B = \dfrac{\Delta / \rho}{C_b \cdot LBP \cdot T}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Cube-Root Scaling Law for Parent Vessel Analysis

Consider a **parent vessel** with main dimensions $L_0, B_0, T_0$ and a **new design** with displacement $\Delta_{new}$. We want to scale the parent to match $\Delta_{new}$.

Since displacement scales with volume:

$$\dfrac{\Delta_{new}}{\Delta_0} = \dfrac{L_{new} \cdot B_{new} \cdot T_{new}}{L_0 \cdot B_0 \cdot T_0}$$

Assuming **geometric similarity** (all dimensions scale by the same factor $\lambda$):

$$\dfrac{\Delta_{new}}{\Delta_0} = \lambda^3$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \left(\dfrac{\Delta_{new}}{\Delta_0}\right)^{1/3}$$

And the new dimensions are:

$$L_{new} = \lambda \cdot L_0, \quad B_{new} = \lambda \cdot B_0, \quad T_{new} = \lambda \cdot T_0$$

Combined form:

$$L_{new} = L_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta_{new}}{\Delta_0}\right)^{1/3}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 18 m fishing trawler has an annual revenue of ₹1.2 crore and operating costs of ₹85 lakh. The vessel was built at a capital cost of ₹3.5 crore. The bank loan carries 9% interest over 15 years. Calculate the annualised capital cost, annual profit, payback period, and ROI.

**Step 1: Compute Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

$$\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1} = \dfrac{0.09 \times (1.09)^{15}}{(1.09)^{15} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.09)^{15}$:

$$(1.09)^{15} \approx 3.6425$$

Then:

$$\text{CRF} = \dfrac{0.09 \times 3.6425}{3.6425 - 1} = \dfrac{0.3278}{2.6425} \approx 0.1241$$

**Step 2: Annualised Capital Cost**

$$C_{annualised} = 3.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.1241 = 4.343 \times 10^6 = ₹43.43 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 3: Annual Profit**

$$P_{annual} = R - C_{op} - C_{annualised}$$
$$P_{annual} = 1.2 \times 10^7 - 8.5 \times 10^6 - 4.343 \times 10^6$$
$$P_{annual} = 12,000,000 - 8,500,000 - 4,343,000 = -843,000$$

$$P_{annual} \approx -₹8.43 \text{ lakh (loss)}$$

**Step 4: Payback Period**

Since the project is loss-making, payback is **infinite** — the design is **not economically viable** at current cost-revenue structure.

**Conclusion:** This trawler design fails the economic test. The designer must either:
- Reduce displacement → lower capital cost
- Increase catch capacity / shift to higher-value species
- Reduce crew size or fuel consumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is exactly the type of integrative question that appears in Module 2 ESE — combining cost analysis with capital recovery. The cube-root scaling is often the second sub-part of the same question.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cost-Design Linkage

| Design Choice | Revenue Impact | Cost Impact | Net Effect |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|------------|
| ↑ Length (LBP) | ↑ Hold capacity → ↑ catch | ↑ Hull cost (∝ L²) | Positive up to optimum |
| ↑ Engine Power | ↑ Speed → less transit time | ↑ Fuel cost (∝ P^1.3) | Diminishing returns |
| ↑ B (Breadth) | ↑ Deck area, ↑ stability | ↑ Hull steel weight | Positive (trawlers) |
| ↑ T (Draught) | ↑ Displacement → ↑ capacity | ↑ Resistance, ↑ port dues | Marginal |
| Use FRP instead of steel | — | ↓ 30% capex, ↓ maintenance | Strongly positive |
| Freezer hold (vs. ice) | ↑ Shore price by 20–40% | ↑ Capex by 15%, ↑ power | Strongly positive |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fishing Vessel Cost-Revenue Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Vessel Database] --> B[Statistical Analysis]
    B --> C[Main Dimensions: LBP, B, D, T]
    B --> D[Form Coefficients: Cb, Cp, Cm]
    C --> E[Preliminary Hull Form]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Displacement Calculation]
    F --> G[Resistance and Powering]
    G --> H[Annual Catch Capacity]
    H --> I[Operating Revenue R]
    F --> J[Capital Cost Ccap]
    I --> K[Annual Profit P = R - Cop - Ccap]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Economic Feasible?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Proceed to Detailed Design]
    L -->|No| N[Iterate: Adjust Dimensions]
    N --> C
```

## 4.2 Cost Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Annual Cost] --> B[Capital Cost Annualised]
    A --> C[Operating Cost Cop]
    B --> B1[Loan Repayment]
    B --> B2[Interest]
    B --> B3[Insurance]
    C --> D1[Fuel and Lubricants]
    C --> D2[Crew Wages and Share]
    C --> D3[Maintenance and Repair]
    C --> D4[Port and Harbour Dues]
    C --> D5[Stores: Ice, Bait, Food]
    C --> D6[Miscellaneous]
```

## 4.3 Parent Vessel Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define New Vessel Requirements] --> B[Build Parent Pool]
    B --> B1[Same vessel type trawler purse seiner]
    B --> B2[Similar length range LBP]
    B --> B3[Same fishing region]
    B --> B4[Proven operational history]
    B1 --> C[Statistical Reduction]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Compute Mean and SD of LBP, B, T, Cb, Cp]
    D --> E[Apply Cube Root Scaling]
    E --> F[New Main Dimensions]
    D --> G[Adopt Form Coefficients Mean Values]
    G --> H[New Hull Form]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Parent Vessel Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Target Displacement] --> B[Scaling Factor Lambda]
    C[Input: Parent LBP, B, T] --> B
    B --> D[New LBP, B, T]
    D --> E[Block Coefficient Cb]
    E --> F[Hull Form Sketch]
    F --> G[Hydrostatic Check]
    G --> H{Stable and Feasible?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Output: Final Main Dimensions]
    H -->|No| J[Adjust Form Coefficients]
    J --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level functional diagrams are used in place of complex physical hull-form drawings** (which require CAD) — the architecture above shows the **information flow** between design sub-tasks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List the major components of operating costs for a fishing trawler and state the percentage share of fuel cost in the total operating cost.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major components of operating costs for a fishing trawler are:
1. **Fuel and lubricants** — typically **30–45%** of operating costs (the largest single component)
2. **Crew wages and catch share** — 20–30%
3. **Maintenance and repair** — 10–15%
4. **Ice, salt, bait, and stores** — 8–12%
5. **Port and harbour dues** — 5–8%
6. **Insurance** — 3–5%
7. **Miscellaneous** — 2–5%

Fuel cost dominates because the engine runs almost continuously during fishing operations (hauling, trawling, searching), unlike a cargo ship with long ballast legs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Block Coefficient and Prismatic Coefficient. State their typical values for a fishing trawler.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Block Coefficient ($C_b$):** The ratio of the volumetric displacement of the vessel to the product of its length, breadth, and draught.
  $$C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{LBP \cdot B \cdot T}$$
  Typical value for fishing trawler: **0.55 to 0.65**.

- **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_p$):** The ratio of the volumetric displacement to the product of the midship area and length.
  $$C_p = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot LBP}$$
  Typical value for fishing trawler: **0.60 to 0.70**.

For a trawler, $C_b$ is moderate (not too full) to allow reasonable speed, and $C_p$ is moderate indicating moderately fine ends for seakeeping.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]*

**(a)** A 19 m fishing trawler has the following cost data for a typical year. Compute the total operating cost, the annualised capital cost (at 10% interest over 20 years), and the net annual profit. State whether the investment is economically viable.
- Capital cost = ₹4.0 crore
- Fuel: 1.5 lakh litres @ ₹90/litre
- Lubricants: ₹4.5 lakh
- Crew wages: ₹18 lakh
- Maintenance: ₹6 lakh
- Ice and stores: ₹8 lakh
- Port dues: ₹3 lakh
- Insurance: ₹5 lakh
- Annual revenue = ₹1.5 crore **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Parent Vessel Data Analysis** method for preliminary dimensioning of a fishing vessel. Using the cube-root scaling law, find the new LBP, B, and T for a design if a parent vessel of LBP=18 m, B=5.2 m, T=2.4 m with displacement 180 tonnes is to be scaled to a new displacement of 240 tonnes. Assume geometric similarity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1: Total Operating Cost**

$$C_{op} = (1.5 \times 10^5 \times 90) + 4.5 + 18 + 6 + 8 + 3 + 5$$

Compute fuel cost:
$$1.5 \times 10^5 \times 90 = 1.35 \times 10^7 = ₹135 \text{ lakh}$$

Total (in lakh ₹):
$$C_{op} = 135 + 4.5 + 18 + 6 + 8 + 3 + 5 = 179.5 \text{ lakh} = ₹1.795 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 2: Annualised Capital Cost (CRF at 10%, N=20)**

$$(1.10)^{20} \approx 6.7275$$

$$\text{CRF} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 6.7275}{6.7275 - 1} = \dfrac{0.67275}{5.7275} \approx 0.11746$$

$$C_{annualised} = 4.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.11746 = 4.698 \times 10^6 = ₹46.98 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 3: Net Annual Profit**

$$P_{annual} = 150 - 179.5 - 46.98 = -76.48 \text{ lakh}$$

The project runs at a **loss of ₹76.48 lakh/year**.

**Step 4: Economic Viability**

Since $P_{annual} < 0$, the investment is **not economically viable** at the given cost structure. The designer must consider:
- Reducing capital cost (smaller vessel, FRP)
- Increasing revenue (better species mix, freezer)
- Reducing fuel cost (more efficient engine)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Parent Vessel Data Analysis — Explanation (4 marks):**

Parent vessel data analysis is a **statistical-empirical** preliminary design method. The steps are:

1. **Build a parent pool** of 10–30 similar, successful vessels of the same type, size range, and operating region.
2. **Collect data** on their main dimensions (LBP, B, T), form coefficients ($C_b, C_p, C_m$), displacement, speed, and power.
3. **Compute statistical parameters** (mean, standard deviation, regression equations) for each variable.
4. **Adopt the mean form coefficients** of the parents for the new design.
5. **Scale the mean dimensions** to the new required displacement using cube-root scaling or regression-based equations.
6. **Validate** against stability, resistance, and seakeeping requirements.

**Cube-Root Scaling Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- Parent: LBP₀ = 18 m, B₀ = 5.2 m, T₀ = 2.4 m, Δ₀ = 180 t
- New: Δ_new = 240 t

Compute scaling factor:
$$\lambda = \left(\dfrac{240}{180}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)^{1/3} = (1.3333)^{1/3} \approx 1.1006$$

New dimensions:
$$LBP_{new} = 18 \times 1.1006 = 19.81 \text{ m}$$
$$B_{new} = 5.2 \times 1.1006 = 5.72 \text{ m}$$
$$T_{new} = 2.4 \times 1.1006 = 2.64 \text{ m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A new 22 m fishing vessel is to be designed for a displacement of 320 tonnes. Using parent vessel data, the average block coefficient adopted is $C_b$ = 0.60, prismatic coefficient $C_p$ = 0.65, and length-to-beam ratio $L/B$ = 4.2. Estimate the breadth B, draught T, and midship area $A_m$ for the new vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various cost components of a fishing vessel in detail. With the help of a suitable example, explain how the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** is used to evaluate fishing vessel investments. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1: Use displacement equation**

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$$

$$\dfrac{320}{1.025} = 0.60 \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$312.20 = 0.60 \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$LBP \cdot B \cdot T = 520.33 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Apply L/B = 4.2**

$$LBP = 4.2 \cdot B$$

**Step 3: Assume typical T/B = 0.45** (from parent data)

$$T = 0.45 \cdot B$$

**Step 4: Substitute**

$$(4.2B) \cdot B \cdot (0.45B) = 520.33$$
$$1.89 \cdot B^3 = 520.33$$
$$B^3 = 275.31$$
$$B = 6.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute remaining**

$$LBP = 4.2 \times 6.50 = 27.30 \text{ m}$$
$$T = 0.45 \times 6.50 = 2.93 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Midship Area**

$$A_m = C_m \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{where } C_m = C_b / C_p = 0.60/0.65 = 0.923$$

$$A_m = 0.923 \times 6.50 \times 2.93 = 17.58 \text{ m}^2$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Cost Component Discussion (4 marks):**

A fishing vessel's annual cost has three layers:

1. **Capital Cost (one-time, annualised):** Hull construction, machinery, fishing gear, electronics, navigational equipment, registration. Annualised via CRF.
2. **Fixed Operating Costs (incurred regardless of operation):** Insurance, depreciation, registration renewal, salaries during off-season.
3. **Variable Operating Costs (proportional to operation):** Fuel, lubricants, ice, crew share, maintenance, port dues, food.

**BCR Worked Example (3 marks):**

For a proposed design:
- Annual Revenue $R$ = ₹1.8 crore
- Annual Operating Cost $C_{op}$ = ₹1.1 crore
- Annualised Capital Cost $C_{cap}$ = ₹0.4 crore

$$\text{BCR} = \dfrac{R}{C_{op} + C_{cap}} = \dfrac{1.8}{1.1 + 0.4} = \dfrac{1.8}{1.5} = 1.20$$

Since **BCR > 1**, the investment is **economically viable**. Generally, a BCR ≥ 1.2 is considered acceptable for fishing vessel investments, and ≥ 1.5 is considered highly attractive.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Module 2 Cost-Dimension Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the seawater density** ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³) in displacement calculations — leads to ~2.5% error.
> 2. **Confusing $C_b$ and $C_p$** — remember $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$, so all three are inter-related.
> 3. **Using the wrong interest rate** in CRF — KTU usually specifies this; if not stated, assume 8–10% for fishing vessel loans.
> 4. **Not converting units** (litres to kg, kg to tonnes, ₹ to lakh/crore).
> 5. **Omitting annualised capital cost** when computing profit — this is the single most common error.
> 6. **In parent vessel scaling**, forgetting that **only dimensions scale by $\lambda$**, not form coefficients (they are dimensionless and remain unchanged under geometric similarity).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating Revenue** = (Catch × Price × Utilisation) + Auxiliary income
- **Operating Cost** = Fuel + Crew + Maintenance + Insurance + Port dues + Stores
- **Profit** = Revenue − Operating Cost − Annualised Capital Cost
- **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ is the key to annualising capital cost
- **Main Dimensions** are LBP, B, D, T — interconnected via Froude Number, displacement, stability
- **Form Coefficients** are dimensionless "shape DNA" — inherited from parents
- **Cube-root scaling** governs geometrically similar parent vessel scaling: $\lambda = (\Delta_{new}/\Delta_{old})^{1/3}$
- **Block Coefficient** $C_b$ typically 0.55–0.65 for fishing trawlers
- **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_p$ typically 0.60–0.70
- **Midship Coefficient** $C_m$ typically 0.85–0.95
- **L/B ratio** for trawlers: 4.0–5.5
- **Displacement equation:** $\Delta = C_b \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$ (memorise this — it appears in nearly every Module 2 question)
- **BCR > 1.2** = investment is acceptable
- **Parent vessel analysis** is the most efficient preliminary design method for fishing vessels
- **Seawater density** = **1.025 t/m³** (always use this, not 1.0)
- **Fuel cost** is typically the **largest single operating cost** (30–45%)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In a 14-mark question, always split your answer as: (i) list costs/revenues with values, (ii) apply CRF formula with substitution, (iii) compute profit, (iv) comment on viability. The examiner allocates ~2 marks per logical step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship** is the systematic process of determining the total internal volume, displacement, and weight-carrying capability of a fishing vessel based on its intended operational role, cargo/fish hold needs, propulsion machinery, crew accommodation, fishing gear storage, and consumables. It is the foundational step in **preliminary ship design**, where the naval architect translates the owner's operational requirements into a feasible set of principal dimensions and form coefficients.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic is categorized under the **Ship Form Synthesis** phase and directly addresses the question: *"How big must the ship be to do its job?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship covers displacement calculation, deadweight analysis, block and form coefficients, and the partitioning of internal volume for functional compartments of a fishing vessel.

Mathematically, the total capacity of a ship is expressed in two equivalent ways:

$$
\text{Weight Capacity (Displacement)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Volume Capacity (Displaced Volume)}
$$

linked by the density of water:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

where $\Delta$ is the displacement (in tonnes or kg), $\nabla$ is the displaced volume (in m³), and $\rho$ is the mass density of water (typically $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$ for seawater and $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$ for freshwater).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Designing a Family House**
>
> Imagine an architect is asked to design a house for a family. Before drawing a single wall, the architect must answer: *"How many bedrooms? How big a kitchen? How much storage? Garage for how many cars?"* The **sum of all these needs** defines the *total floor area* of the house, which in turn fixes the length and breadth of the building.
>
> A fishing vessel is no different. The owner specifies: *"I need a 50-tonne fish hold, accommodation for 12 crew, room for a 500 HP engine, 20 tonnes of fuel, and 10 tonnes of fishing gear."* The naval architect adds up all these **weight and volume requirements** to determine the **total displacement** and **principal dimensions** of the vessel.

Think of the ship as a **floating warehouse** that must carry:

1. **The ship itself** (steel hull, machinery, equipment) — called **Lightship Weight**.
2. **Everything the ship must transport to do its job** — called **Deadweight**.

The sum is the **Total Displacement**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

The following standard units and constants are routinely used:

- **Displacement ($\Delta$):** in **tonnes (t)** or **metric tonnes**, where 1 t = 1000 kg.
- **Displaced Volume ($\nabla$):** in **cubic metres (m³)**.
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$.
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$.
- **Principal Dimensions:** Length ($L$), Breadth ($B$), Draft ($T$) — all in **metres (m)**.
- **Form Coefficients:** dimensionless numbers between 0 and 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Displacement

The total displacement of any ship — including a fishing vessel — is partitioned into two major weight groups:

$$
\Delta_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{Lightship}} + DWT
$$

### 2.1.1 Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$)

This is the **empty weight of the ship** — the hull structure, machinery, outfit, and fixed equipment. It can be sub-divided as:

$$
W_{LS} = W_{\text{Steel}} + W_{\text{Machinery}} + W_{\text{Outfit}} + W_{\text{Fish Hold Insulation}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The lightship weight is what the ship weighs on the shipyard scale *before* any cargo, fuel, water, or crew is loaded. It is essentially the "empty shell + engine + furniture" weight.

### 2.1.2 Deadweight ($DWT$)

The Deadweight is the **variable load** — everything the ship carries to perform its mission. For a fishing vessel, this is mission-specific and is the heart of the space requirement calculation.

$$
DWT = W_{\text{Catch}} + W_{\text{Fuel}} + W_{\text{Lub Oil}} + W_{\text{Fresh Water}} + W_{\text{Crew \& Effects}} + W_{\text{Provisions}} + W_{\text{Fishing Gear}} + W_{\text{Stores}} + W_{\text{Reserve}}
$$

## 2.2 Fishing Vessel Specific Weight Components (High-Yield for KTU)

Each of the above has a direct **volume implication**, which links weight capacity to space capacity:

| **Component** | **Typical KTU Estimation Basis** | **Volume Implication** |
|---|---|---|
| Fish Hold Capacity | Specified by owner (e.g., 30 t, 50 t) | Holds $\approx$ 60–70% of cargo volume |
| Fuel Oil | 1.5–2.0 tonnes per day × endurance days | Tank volume in double bottom / wing tanks |
| Fresh Water | 0.10–0.15 t per crew per day | Tank in forepeak or double bottom |
| Lubricating Oil | 5–8% of fuel oil weight | Small dedicated tank near engine room |
| Crew & Effects | 0.10–0.15 t per person | Accommodation block |
| Provisions | 0.005–0.010 t per crew per day × trip days | Cold room / dry store |
| Fishing Gear (nets, lines, booms) | Depends on gear type | Gear store on deck or below |
| Ice (for catch preservation) | $\approx$ 25–30% of fish hold weight | Insulated hold |
| Reserve / Margin | 5–10% of DWT | Distributed buoyancy reserve |

## 2.3 Form Coefficients — The Shape-to-Volume Bridge

The **form coefficients** are dimensionless numbers that describe the "fullness" or "fineness" of the hull form. They are the bridge between the *weight* world and the *volume* world, and are **critical for KTU problems**.

The four primary form coefficients are:

### 2.3.1 Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

The most important coefficient. It is the ratio of the displaced volume of the ship to the volume of an imaginary rectangular box that exactly encloses the ship:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Typical values for fishing vessels:** $C_B = 0.50$ to $0.65$ (mid-range fullness).

### 2.3.2 Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)

Ratio of displaced volume to the volume of a prism with the midship section as its cross-section, extending the full length:

$$
C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{pp}}
$$

where $A_M$ is the area of the **midship section** (cross-section at amidships).

### 2.3.3 Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)

Ratio of the immersed midship section area to the area of its circumscribing rectangle:

$$
C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}
$$

### 2.3.4 Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)

Ratio of the waterplane area to the rectangle $L \times B$:

$$
C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}
$$

### 2.3.5 The Master Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Formula (must be memorised):**
> $$
> C_B = C_P \times C_M
> $$
> This relationship is **derived in Section 3** and is one of the most-tested identities in KTU naval architecture papers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement from volume | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | Volume from block coefficient | m³ |
| 3 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | Displacement from principal dimensions | tonnes |
| 4 | $\Delta_{total} = W_{LS} + DWT$ | Weight decomposition | tonnes |
| 5 | $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ | Coefficient relationship | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_B = \nabla / (L B T)$ | Block coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M L)$ | Prismatic coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_M = A_M / (B T)$ | Midship coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{WL} B)$ | Waterplane coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W_{fuel} = SFOC \cdot P \cdot t_{end} / 10^6$ | Fuel weight from endurance | tonnes |

> **Notation tip:** In the above table, $L$ generally means $L_{pp}$ (length between perpendiculars) for displacement/volume formulas, and $L_{WL}$ (length at waterline) for the waterplane coefficient. In KTU problems, the distinction is often ignored for simplicity unless explicitly stated.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Fishing Vessel Engineering

The space requirement calculation is **not academic** — it is the first step before any steel is cut. It determines:

- **How much fish** the vessel can carry per trip (revenue driver).
- **How far** the vessel can sail (fuel tank size).
- **How many crew** can be safely accommodated (safety regulation).
- **The size of the drydock** the vessel can fit into (port constraint).
- **The stability, powering, and seakeeping** characteristics that follow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For small to medium fishing vessels (12–25 m), the **fish hold volume typically occupies 30–40% of the total internal volume** below the main deck, while the **engine room occupies 8–12%**, and the **crew accommodation occupies 10–15%**. These percentages are benchmark ranges used in KTU design problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Master Relationship $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU question** — either as a direct "derive" question or embedded in a numerical problem.

**Starting Point:** Write the definitions of $C_P$ and $C_M$ as given in Section 2.

$$
C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{pp}}
$$

$$
C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 1:** Multiply the right-hand sides of the two equations:

$$
C_P \times C_M = \left( \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{pp}} \right) \times \left( \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Cancel the common term $A_M$ (the midship area appears in the denominator of $C_P$ and the numerator of $C_M$):

$$
C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognise that the right-hand side is the **definition of $C_B$**:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, by equality:

$$
\boxed{\,C_B = C_P \times C_M\,}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The *block coefficient* (overall fullness) is the product of *how full the ends are* (prismatic coefficient) and *how full the middle is* (midship coefficient). This is a powerful insight used by naval architects to control hull shape.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Displacement from Principal Dimensions

**Starting Point:** The volume of the displaced water equals the rectangular block volume times the block coefficient:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 1:** The mass of displaced water is volume times density:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\nabla$:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 3:** For **seawater** ($\rho = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$):

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta_{sw} = 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{(tonnes)}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** For **freshwater** ($\rho = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$):

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta_{fw} = 1.000 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{(tonnes)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The same ship will displace **2.5% more in freshwater** than in seawater at the same draft. This is why ships ride *deeper* in rivers than in the sea.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Problem (Model Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem (14-Mark Style):**
> A fishing trawler is to be designed with the following specifications:
> - Length between perpendiculars: $L_{pp} = 22.0 \; \text{m}$
> - Breadth (moulded): $B = 6.5 \; \text{m}$
> - Design draft: $T = 2.8 \; \text{m}$
> - Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.58$
> - Midship section coefficient: $C_M = 0.85$
> - Waterplane area coefficient: $C_{WP} = 0.78$
> - Seawater density: $\rho = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The displaced volume $\nabla$, (b) The total displacement $\Delta$, (c) The prismatic coefficient $C_P$, (d) The midship section area $A_M$, (e) The waterplane area $A_{WP}$, (f) The lightship weight if the deadweight is 75 t.

### Part (a): Displaced Volume $\nabla$

**Step 1:** Apply the volume formula:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$
\nabla = 0.58 \times 22.0 \times 6.5 \times 2.8
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate step-by-step:

$$
\nabla = 0.58 \times (22.0 \times 6.5 \times 2.8)
$$

$$
22.0 \times 6.5 = 143.0 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
143.0 \times 2.8 = 400.4 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.58 \times 400.4 = 232.232 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\nabla \approx 232.23 \; \text{m}^3\,}
$$

### Part (b): Total Displacement $\Delta$

**Step 1:** Apply the mass-volume relation:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \times 232.232
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\Delta = 238.04 \; \text{t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta \approx 238.04 \; \text{tonnes}\,}
$$

### Part (c): Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$

**Step 1:** Use the master relationship:

$$
C_B = C_P \times C_M
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$
C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.58}{0.85}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\boxed{\,C_P \approx 0.6824\,}
$$

### Part (d): Midship Section Area $A_M$

**Step 1:** From the definition of $C_M$:

$$
C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$
A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
A_M = 0.85 \times 6.5 \times 2.8
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$
6.5 \times 2.8 = 18.2 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_M = 0.85 \times 18.2 = 15.47 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_M \approx 15.47 \; \text{m}^2\,}
$$

### Part (e): Waterplane Area $A_{WP}$

**Step 1:** From the definition of $C_{WP}$:

$$
A_{WP} = C_{WP} \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B
$$

**Step 2:** Assuming $L_{WL} \approx L_{pp} = 22.0 \; \text{m}$ (standard KTU simplification):

$$
A_{WP} = 0.78 \times 22.0 \times 6.5
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
22.0 \times 6.5 = 143.0 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{WP} = 0.78 \times 143.0 = 111.54 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_{WP} \approx 111.54 \; \text{m}^2\,}
$$

### Part (f): Lightship Weight $W_{LS}$

**Step 1:** From the displacement decomposition:

$$
\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$
W_{LS} = \Delta - DWT
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
W_{LS} = 238.04 - 75
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W_{LS} \approx 163.04 \; \text{tonnes}\,}
$$

### Summary Table of Results

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displaced Volume | $\nabla$ | 232.23 | m³ |
| Total Displacement | $\Delta$ | 238.04 | t |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | 0.6824 | — |
| Midship Section Area | $A_M$ | 15.47 | m² |
| Waterplane Area | $A_{WP}$ | 111.54 | m² |
| Lightship Weight | $W_{LS}$ | 163.04 | t |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reverse Calculation (Given Weight, Find Dimensions)

This is the **opposite direction** — what KTU Part B questions often test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Worked Problem:**
> A fishing vessel must carry a deadweight of 90 t and have a lightship weight of 160 t. The design $C_B = 0.60$, $B = 6.0 \; \text{m}$, $T = 2.5 \; \text{m}$, $\rho = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$. Find the required length $L_{pp}$.

**Step 1:** Total displacement from weights:

$$
\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT = 160 + 90 = 250 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Volume from displacement:

$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{250}{1.025} = 243.90 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ and solve for $L$:

$$
L = \frac{\nabla}{C_B \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{243.90}{0.60 \times 6.0 \times 2.5}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate denominator:

$$
0.60 \times 6.0 \times 2.5 = 9.0
$$

**Step 5:** Final value:

$$
L = \frac{243.90}{9.0} = 27.10 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L_{pp} \approx 27.10 \; \text{m}\,}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The fishing vessel must be at least **27.1 m long** to carry 90 t of deadweight plus 160 t of lightship at the specified form and dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Ship Space Requirement Allocation

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the *total displacement* of a fishing vessel is functionally allocated into its weight and volume sub-systems. This is the **canonical architecture** used in KTU design problem statements.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    totDisp["Total Displacement DELTA tonnes"]:::root

    totDisp --> lightship["Lightship Weight W_LS"]:::branch
    totDisp --> deadweight["Deadweight DWT"]:::branch

    lightship --> steelHull["Steel Hull W_steel"]:::leaf
    lightship --> mach["Main Machinery W_mach"]:::leaf
    lightship --> outfit["Outfit and Equipment W_outfit"]:::leaf
    lightship --> insulation["Hold Insulation W_ins"]:::leaf

    deadweight --> catch["Fish Hold Payload W_catch"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> fuel["Fuel Oil W_fuel"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> lube["Lubricating Oil W_lube"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> water["Fresh Water W_water"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> crew["Crew and Effects W_crew"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> provisions["Provisions and Stores W_prov"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> gear["Fishing Gear W_gear"]:::leaf
    deadweight --> reserve["Reserve and Margin W_res"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1f33,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#4a7ba6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#cfe0ee,color:#0d1f33,stroke:#4a7ba6,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **root node** is the *Total Displacement* — the answer to "how big must the ship be?".
- It splits into two **branches**: the fixed weight (lightship) and the variable load (deadweight).
- Each branch further breaks into the **leaf nodes** that the naval architect must estimate individually.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question says *"Estimate the space requirements of a fishing vessel"*, examiners expect you to **list and estimate every leaf node** in this diagram, then sum them up. Skipping any leaf node loses marks.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Form Coefficient Relationships

The second diagram shows how the four form coefficients relate geometrically to the ship hull, and the algebraic relationships that link them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hullForm["Ship Hull Form Geometry"]
        rectBlock["L x B x T\n Rectangular Box"]:::geom
        prismBlock["A_M x L\n Prismatic Body"]:::geom
        midSect["A_M = B x T\n Midship Rectangle"]:::geom
        wpArea["A_WP = L x B\n Waterplane Rectangle"]:::geom
    end

    rectBlock -->|"C_B = Vol / (LBT)"| cbVal["C_B Block Coefficient"]:::coeff
    prismBlock -->|"C_P = Vol / (A_M x L)"| cpVal["C_P Prismatic Coefficient"]:::coeff
    midSect -->|"C_M = A_M / (B x T)"| cmVal["C_M Midship Coefficient"]:::coeff
    wpArea -->|"C_WP = A_WP / (L x B)"| cwpVal["C_WP Waterplane Coefficient"]:::coeff

    cbVal -->|"C_B = C_P x C_M"| masterEq["Master Relationship"]:::master
    cpVal --> masterEq
    cmVal --> masterEq

    classDef geom fill:#fff3b0,color:#3d2c00,stroke:#a87a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef coeff fill:#a8d5ba,color:#0a3d1f,stroke:#1a6b3a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef master fill:#f08080,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Each form coefficient is defined by comparing the **actual ship volume/area** to a **geometric reference** (yellow boxes).
- The **red master node** captures the central identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ that every KTU student must know.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sequential Design Workflow

The third diagram shows the **sequential procedure** a naval architect follows to determine ship capacity in preliminary design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Collect Owner Requirements\n Fish hold tonnage, crew, endurance, gear"]:::step
    step2["Step 2: Estimate Component Weights\n Using empirical formulas"]:::step
    step3["Step 3: Sum to get DWT\n Add W_LS to get total DELTA"]:::step
    step4["Step 4: Assume Form Coefficients\n C_B, C_M, C_P from ship type"]:::step
    step5["Step 5: Select L B T Ratios\n L by B, B by T, L by T"]:::step
    step6["Step 6: Solve for Principal Dimensions\n DELTA equals rho C_B L B T"]:::step
    step7["Step 7: Verify and Iterate\n Check stability, powering, constraints"]:::step

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7
    step7 -.->|"Adjust if not feasible"| step4

    classDef step fill:#d4e6f1,color:#0d1f33,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Note:** This iterative loop in Step 7 is why preliminary ship design is a *design spiral*, not a linear sequence. In real shipyards, the loop is executed 3–5 times before a feasible design is locked in. In KTU problems, you typically execute the loop **once**, given all the coefficients and ratios.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Block Coefficient. What is its typical range for a fishing trawler?**

**Model Answer:**

The Block Coefficient ($C_B$) is the ratio of the displaced volume of the ship to the volume of a rectangular box having the length ($L_{pp}$), breadth ($B$), and draft ($T$) of the ship as its dimensions:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

It is a measure of the **fullness** of the underwater hull form. For a fishing trawler, the typical range is **$C_B = 0.50$ to $0.65$**. Higher $C_B$ means a fuller hull with greater cargo capacity but lower speed; lower $C_B$ means a finer hull with higher speed but less capacity.
