---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between Lightship Weight and Deadweight of a fishing vessel. Give two examples of items under each category.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$)** | **Deadweight ($DWT$)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The weight of the empty ship — hull, machinery, and fixed outfit | The variable load carried by the ship to perform its mission |
| **Nature** | Fixed for a given ship design | Varies from voyage to voyage |
| **Examples** | (i) Steel hull structure, (ii) Main engine and gearbox | (i) Fish catch in hold, (ii) Fuel oil in bunker tanks |

The sum $W_{LS} + DWT = \Delta$ (total displacement).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Q: A fishing trawler has the following principal particulars:**
- $L_{pp} = 20.0 \; \text{m}$, $B = 6.0 \; \text{m}$, $T = 2.5 \; \text{m}$
- $C_B = 0.60$, $C_M = 0.84$, $C_{WP} = 0.75$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$

**(a)** Calculate the total displacement $\Delta$, the displaced volume $\nabla$, and the prismatic coefficient $C_P$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the deadweight is 60 t, find the lightship weight. Also calculate the midship section area $A_M$ and the waterplane area $A_{WP}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Displaced Volume $\nabla$** (2 marks)

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.60 \times 20.0 \times 6.0 \times 2.5
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.60 \times 300.0 = 180.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Total Displacement $\Delta$** (2 marks)

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 180.0 = 184.5 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** (3 marks)

$$
C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.60}{0.84} = 0.7143
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Lightship Weight $W_{LS}$** (2 marks)

$$
W_{LS} = \Delta - DWT = 184.5 - 60.0 = 124.5 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 2: Midship Section Area $A_M$** (2 marks)

$$
A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T = 0.84 \times 6.0 \times 2.5 = 12.60 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Waterplane Area $A_{WP}$** (3 marks)

$$
A_{WP} = C_{WP} \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \approx 0.75 \times 20.0 \times 6.0 = 90.0 \; \text{m}^2
$$

#### Final Answer Summary (Question A)

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| $\nabla$ | 180.0 m³ |
| $\Delta$ | 184.5 t |
| $C_P$ | 0.7143 |
| $W_{LS}$ | 124.5 t |
| $A_M$ | 12.60 m² |
| $A_{WP}$ | 90.0 m² |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q: With the help of neat derivations and sketches, explain the following form coefficients used to determine the space requirement of a fishing vessel:**
- **(a)** Block Coefficient ($C_B$) and Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$). **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$) and Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$). Derive the relationship $C_B = C_P \times C_M$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution Outline for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Block Coefficient $C_B$** (3.5 marks):
   - Definition: $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$
   - Physical meaning: ratio of displaced volume to the bounding box.
   - Typical fishing trawler value: 0.50–0.65.
   - Sketch: side profile of ship inside a rectangle.

2. **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** (3.5 marks):
   - Definition: $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$
   - Physical meaning: measures the fullness of the ends of the hull.
   - Typical fishing trawler value: 0.65–0.75.
   - Note: $C_P = 1.0$ for a perfectly prismatic hull (no taper at ends).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

1. **Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$** (2 marks):
   - Definition: $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$
   - Physical meaning: fullness of the cross-section at amidships.
   - Typical value: 0.80–0.95 for fishing vessels.

2. **Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$** (2 marks):
   - Definition: $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$
   - Physical meaning: fullness of the waterplane — affects transverse stability.

3. **Derivation of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$** (3 marks):

$$
C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} \times \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = C_B
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the water density:** Many students compute $\nabla$ in m³ and stop. You **must** multiply by $\rho$ to get $\Delta$ in tonnes. (Penalty: -2 marks).
> 2. **Using $L_{OA}$ instead of $L_{pp}$:** The block coefficient uses $L_{pp}$ (length between perpendiculars), not the overall length. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks).
> 3. **Inverting the coefficient formula:** A very common error is writing $C_B = L \cdot B \cdot T / \nabla$ instead of the correct form. Always remember: **coefficient = (actual) / (reference)**.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_B$ and $C_P$:** $C_B$ uses the box $L \cdot B \cdot T$; $C_P$ uses the prism $A_M \cdot L$. Confusing these leads to wrong numbers.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Every numerical answer must carry its unit (t, m³, m²). Missing units = -0.5 mark per answer.
> 6. **Forgetting to subtract DWT** when asked for lightship weight: write $\Delta - DWT$, not just $\Delta$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — *Memorise This Before the Exam***

### Core Concepts
- **Space requirement = sizing the ship** before any other design step.
- Total displacement $\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT$.
- Volume $\nabla$ and displacement $\Delta$ are linked by $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$.

### The Four Form Coefficients (Must Memorise)
- $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ — **overall fullness**
- $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$ — **end fullness**
- $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ — **middle fullness**
- $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L \cdot B)$ — **waterplane fullness**

### The Master Identity
$$
\boxed{\,C_B = C_P \times C_M\,}
$$

### Key Numeric Constants
- Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$
- Freshwater density: $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$

### Typical Fishing Vessel Ranges
- $C_B = 0.50$ to $0.65$
- $C_M = 0.80$ to $0.95$
- $C_P = 0.60$ to $0.75$
- $C_{WP} = 0.70$ to $0.85$

### Deadweight Components for Fishing Vessels (List to Recall)
Fish catch, fuel oil, lubricating oil, fresh water, crew and effects, provisions, fishing gear, ice, stores, reserve.

### Volume-to-Weight Conversion
Always multiply volume in m³ by $\rho$ to get tonnes. **Never** give displacement in m³.

### Iterative Design Loop
Collect requirements → estimate weights → assume coefficients → choose L:B:T ratios → solve for dimensions → verify → iterate.

### Frequently Tested Question Patterns
1. *Given dimensions and $C_B$, find $\Delta$.*
2. *Given $C_B$ and $C_M$, find $C_P$.*
3. *Given weights and dimensions, find the missing dimension.*
4. *Derive $C_B = C_P \times C_M$.*
5. *List the deadweight components of a fishing vessel.*

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always show every step with the formula first, then the substitution, then the numerical evaluation. Examiners give **method marks** even if the final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Main Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Main Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Estimation of Main Dimensions** is the foundational preliminary design step in fishing vessel architecture where the principal geometric parameters — **Length (L)**, **Beam (B)**, **Depth (D)**, and **Draft (T)** — are systematically derived from mission requirements such as required deadweight, service speed, fishing gear type, and operational sea conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Ready Phrasing):**
> *"Estimation of main dimensions refers to the iterative analytical process of determining the principal hull form coefficients and ratios (Length Overall, Length Between Perpendiculars, Beam, Depth, and Draft) of a fishing vessel using empirical formulas, statistical data from parent vessels, and standard naval architectural ratios so that the vessel satisfies stability, powering, and tonnage requirements."*

The main dimensions collectively govern:
- **Hydrostatic performance** (resistance, propulsion)
- **Static stability** (GM, GZ curve)
- **Capacity** (fuel, fish hold, crew)
- **Regulatory compliance** (IMO, IRS, fishing vessel rules)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **commercial delivery truck** for a logistics company. Before building it, the engineer must first decide:

| Truck Problem | Fishing Vessel Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Payload weight (in kg) | **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** |
| Top speed required | **Service Speed (V in knots)** |
| Cargo volume (m³) | **Fish hold volume** |
| Fuel tank size | **Endurance × specific fuel consumption** |
| Wheelbase & chassis | **Length, Beam, Depth, Draft** |

You cannot start fabricating the chassis without first knowing *how much* it must carry and *how fast* it must go. The truck's wheelbase, axle width, and chassis height all *emerge* from payload + speed constraints.

> **Similarly**, a fishing vessel's Length, Beam, Depth, and Draft *emerge* from a handful of mission-driven inputs (DWT, V, type of fishing, sea state) combined with empirical coefficients called **Naval Architectural Coefficients** (Block coefficient Cb, Prismatic coefficient Cp, etc.).

This is essentially a **reverse-engineering-from-requirements** process — exactly what KTU Module 2 expects you to master.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants Used Throughout the Module:**
> - **Sea water density:** ρ = **1.025 t/m³** (standard)
> - **Fresh water density:** ρ = **1.000 t/m³**
> - **1 knot** = **0.5144 m/s** = **1.852 km/h**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Displacement (Δ)** is in **metric tonnes (t)** or **m³**
> - **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** is the standard "L" used in resistance/powering formulas
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inputs to the Estimation Process

Before any dimension can be calculated, the designer must fix the following **mission parameters**:

1. **Deadweight (DWT)** — total weight of catch, fuel, freshwater, stores, crew, and gear.
2. **Service Speed (V)** — in **knots**.
3. **Type of fishing** — trawler, purse seiner, long-liner, gill-netter (governs hull form).
4. **Range / Endurance** — nautical miles to be covered or days at sea.
5. **Operating sea state** — coastal (≤ 3 m wave) vs. offshore (> 3 m wave).
6. **Loading condition** — fully loaded departure vs. arrival (light ship).

---

## 2.2 The Displacement Equation — The Core Engine

The single most important relationship in dimension estimation is the **Volume–Displacement Equation**:

$$\Delta = C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$$

Where:
- **Δ** = Displacement (tonnes)
- **Cb** = Block coefficient (dimensionless, typically **0.50 – 0.70** for fishing vessels)
- **L** = Length Between Perpendiculars (m)
- **B** = Moulded Beam (m)
- **T** = Mean Draft (m)
- **ρ** = Water density (**1.025 t/m³**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single equation has FOUR unknowns (L, B, T, plus Cb) and only ONE equation.** To solve it, the designer introduces **empirical ratios** — the **L/B**, **B/T**, and **L/D** ratios — obtained from a *parent vessel* or from statistical data on similar fishing fleets.

---

## 2.3 Standard Naval Architectural Ratios for Fishing Vessels

These are the **"rule-of-thumb" ratios** that KTU examiners repeatedly test:

| Ratio | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **L / B** | 4.5 to 6.5 | Length-to-beam (governs speed vs. stability) |
| **B / T** | 2.0 to 2.8 | Beam-to-draft (governs initial stability) |
| **L / D** | 9.0 to 12.0 | Length-to-depth (governs hull strength) |
| **D / T** | 1.1 to 1.3 | Depth-to-draft (freeboard margin) |
| **LBP / LOA** | 0.85 to 0.92 | Length between perpendiculars to overall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these ratios?**
> - **High L/B** → slender, fast, less stable → suited for offshore trawlers.
> - **Low L/B** → beamy, stable, slower → suited for coastal seiners.
> - **High B/T** → greater initial GM → safer rolling in beam seas.

---

## 2.4 The Powering Equation (Auxiliary Dimension Driver)

Service speed sets the required effective horsepower (EHP), which then sets displacement via **Holtrop's or Admiralty Coefficient** approach:

$$P_E = \frac{R_T \times V}{75} \quad (\text{in HP when } R_T \text{ in kgf and } V \text{ in m/s})$$

A simpler and more commonly used empirical powering relation is:

$$\Delta = \left( \frac{C \times P_E}{V^3} \right)^{3/2}$$

Where **C** is the **Admiralty Coefficient** ≈ **150 – 250** for fishing vessels (this bundles wave resistance, form factor, propulsive efficiency).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. It appears in nearly every Module-2 numerical problem.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$ | Volume-displacement equation |
| 2 | $\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{loaded}} - \Delta_{\text{lightship}}$ | Defines payload capacity |
| 3 | $\Delta_{\text{lightship}} = \text{Steel weight} + \text{Outfit} + \text{Machinery}$ | Light ship breakdown |
| 4 | $C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient definition |
| 5 | $C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Prismatic coefficient |
| 6 | $C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship area coefficient |
| 7 | $C_b = C_p \times C_M$ | Coefficient chain |
| 8 | $\text{L/B}, \text{ B/T}, \text{ L/D}$ | Empirical hull form ratios |
| 9 | $P_E = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective horsepower |
| 10 | $C_{\text{Adm}} = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P_E}$ | Admiralty coefficient |
| 11 | $V = 0.5144 \cdot V_{\text{knots}}$ | Knots → m/s conversion |
| 12 | $\rho_{\text{sea}} = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3$ | Salt water density |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

In professional shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, ABG Shipyard), the **preliminary dimension estimation** is performed during the **Contract Design Phase** before any lines plan is drawn. The estimates are then refined iteratively using:
- Model basin testing (towing tank)
- Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)
- Stability software (e.g., **Maxsurf, AutoHydro, NAPA**)

A flawed estimation here propagates downstream — under-sized displacement leads to inadequate stability, over-sized beam leads to costly steel and port-handling problems. Hence, KTU places heavy weight on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Algorithm — Derivation of Main Dimensions

This is the **canonical KTU examiner algorithm**. Every Part-B numerical is solved by this exact sequence.

---

### **STEP 1 — Determine the Light Ship Weight (LSW)**

The light ship weight is the sum of three major groups:

$$\text{LSW} = W_{\text{steel}} + W_{\text{outfit}} + W_{\text{machinery}}$$

Each is estimated using empirical percentage formulas (Watson & Gilfillan style):

$$W_{\text{steel}} = k_1 \cdot \Delta \quad \text{(steel is typically 25–35\% of Δ)}$$

$$W_{\text{outfit}} = k_2 \cdot \Delta \quad \text{(outfit is typically 10–15\% of Δ)}$$

$$W_{\text{machinery}} = k_3 \cdot \Delta \quad \text{(machinery is typically 8–12\% of Δ)}$$

So:

$$\text{LSW} \approx (0.45 \text{ to } 0.55) \times \Delta$$

---

### **STEP 2 — Determine Loaded Displacement**

$$\Delta_{\text{loaded}} = \text{LSW} + \text{DWT}$$

> **Reading this in plain English:** "Take the deadweight you must carry (catch, fuel, crew, stores) and ADD the weight of the empty ship itself. The sum is the *total weight* the ship must float with — i.e., its displacement."

---

### **STEP 3 — Select Block Coefficient (Cb)**

From a **parent vessel** database or from the empirical chart for fishing vessels:

$$C_b = f(\text{Froude number } F_n, \text{ hull form})$$

For typical fishing trawlers: $C_b \in [0.55, 0.65]$.

> **Useful empirical relation:**

$$C_b \approx 1.05 - 1.6 \cdot F_n \quad \text{(for moderate-speed vessels)}$$

Where Froude number:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

---

### **STEP 4 — Choose Empirical Ratios**

From the design brief and parent vessel:

$$\frac{L}{B} = R_1 \quad ; \quad \frac{B}{T} = R_2 \quad ; \quad \frac{L}{D} = R_3$$

---

### **STEP 5 — Solve the Displacement Equation Algebraically**

Starting from:

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$$

Substitute $B = L/R_1$ and $T = B/R_2 = L/(R_1 R_2)$:

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{L}{R_1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{R_1 R_2}\right) \cdot \rho$$

$$\Delta = \frac{C_b \cdot \rho}{R_1^2 \cdot R_2} \cdot L^3$$

Solve for L:

$$L = \left[ \frac{\Delta \cdot R_1^2 \cdot R_2}{C_b \cdot \rho} \right]^{1/3}$$

Then back-substitute:

$$B = \frac{L}{R_1} \quad ; \quad T = \frac{B}{R_2} \quad ; \quad D = \frac{L}{R_3}$$

---

### **STEP 6 — Apply Service-Speed Powering Check**

The estimated displacement must satisfy the powering requirement:

$$P_E = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{(where } R_T = f(\Delta, V, C_b)\text{)}$$

A common empirical resistance formula for fishing vessels:

$$R_T = k \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^2 \quad (\text{kgf})$$

Setting $V$ in m/s, $k \approx 6$ to $10$ depending on hull form.

Therefore:

$$P_E = \frac{k \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{75}$$

If the required $P_E$ is too high or too low compared to the design brief, the loop returns to Step 3 and $C_b$ (or $R_1, R_2$) is adjusted.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Solved Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A fishing trawler is to carry a DWT of **120 tonnes** at a service speed of **10 knots**. Light ship weight is **45% of displacement**. Assume $C_b = 0.60$, $L/B = 5.5$, $B/T = 2.4$, $\rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3$. Estimate L, B, T.

### **Solution:**

**Step A — Find Loaded Displacement**

$$\text{LSW} = 0.45 \cdot \Delta$$

$$\Delta = \text{LSW} + \text{DWT} = 0.45\Delta + 120$$

$$0.55 \Delta = 120 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta = \frac{120}{0.55} = 218.18 \ \text{t}$$

**Step B — Solve the Displacement Equation for L**

$$L = \left[ \frac{\Delta \cdot R_1^2 \cdot R_2}{C_b \cdot \rho} \right]^{1/3}$$

Substituting:

$$L = \left[ \frac{218.18 \times (5.5)^2 \times 2.4}{0.60 \times 1.025} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = \left[ \frac{218.18 \times 30.25 \times 2.4}{0.615} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = \left[ \frac{15{,}839.86}{0.615} \right]^{1/3} = [25{,}755.87]^{1/3}$$

$$L \approx 29.56 \ \text{m}$$

**Step C — Back-substitute**

$$B = \frac{L}{R_1} = \frac{29.56}{5.5} \approx 5.37 \ \text{m}$$

$$T = \frac{B}{R_2} = \frac{5.37}{2.4} \approx 2.24 \ \text{m}$$

> **Result:** $L \approx 29.6 \ \text{m}$, $B \approx 5.4 \ \text{m}$, $T \approx 2.2 \ \text{m}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Mistakes vs. Correct Method

| Step | Common Student Error (❌) | Correct Method (✓) |
|---|---|---|
| Using displacement | Confusing Δ with DWT directly | Δ = LSW + DWT |
| Selecting Cb | Picking an arbitrary value | Use parent vessel or Froude-based formula |
| Solving L | Mixing LOA with LBP | Use **LBP** consistently |
| Solving T | Forgetting ρ in Δ equation | Always multiply by ρ = 1.025 |
| Back-substitution | Using wrong inverse ratio | T = B/R₂, **not** R₂ × B |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Dimension Estimation Pipeline

This Mermaid block diagram captures the **iterative engineering workflow** from mission requirements down to validated principal dimensions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Requirements] --> B[Deadweight DWT]
    A --> C[Service Speed V knots]
    A --> D[Fishing Type Selection]
    A --> E[Range and Endurance]
    B --> F[Compute Loaded Displacement Delta]
    G[Light Ship Weight LSW] --> F
    F --> H[Select Block Coefficient Cb]
    H --> I[Choose Empirical Ratios]
    I --> J[R1 equals L by B]
    I --> K[R2 equals B by T]
    I --> L[R3 equals L by D]
    J --> M[Solve Displacement Equation for L]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    H --> M
    M --> N[Back Substitute B T D]
    N --> O[Powering Check using EHP]
    O --> P{Displacement and Speed Satisfied}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Finalized Main Dimensions]
    P -- No --> R[Iterate Cb and Ratios]
    R --> H
    Q --> S[Lines Plan and Stability Analysis]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Coefficient Hierarchy and Data Flow

This shows how the **Block coefficient, Prismatic coefficient, and Midship coefficient** interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement Volume] --> B[Cb Definition]
    C[Midship Area AM] --> D[CM Definition]
    E[Length LBP] --> F[Cp Definition]
    B --> G[Cb equals Cp times CM]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hull Form Classification]
    H --> I[Fishing Vessel Type Decision]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Ratio Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operational Profile] --> B{Offshore or Coastal}
    B -- Offshore Trawler --> C[L by B equals 5.5 to 6.5]
    B -- Coastal Seiner --> D[L by B equals 4.5 to 5.5]
    C --> E[B by T equals 2.2 to 2.5]
    D --> F[B by T equals 2.4 to 2.8]
    E --> G[L by D equals 9 to 12]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Dimension Estimation Solver]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU 2024:**
> Examiners often award **2 marks** for a clean schematic that traces inputs → equations → outputs. Use these as a mental template when drawing freehand in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Main Dimensions" of a fishing vessel and list any four principal dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**
The Main Dimensions of a fishing vessel are the principal geometric parameters that fully characterize the size and form of the hull. The four primary dimensions are:
1. **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** – measured at the design waterline between the forward and aft perpendiculars.
2. **Beam (B)** – the moulded breadth measured at the midship section.
3. **Depth (D)** – the moulded depth measured from the base line to the design waterline.
4. **Draft (T)** – the vertical distance from the keel to the design waterline.

These dimensions govern hydrostatic performance, stability, and resistance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Block Coefficient (Cb)? Why is it critical in dimension estimation?**

**Model Answer:**
The Block Coefficient (Cb) is the ratio of the displaced volume of the hull to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length, beam, and draft as the vessel:

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Importance:** Cb directly governs the volume-displacement equation $\Delta = C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$. Selecting an appropriate Cb (typically 0.55–0.65 for fishing vessels) ensures that the calculated dimensions produce the required displacement without over- or under-sizing the hull.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

A coastal fishing trawler is to be designed with the following data:

- **Deadweight (DWT)** = 150 tonnes
- **Light Ship Weight** = 50% of loaded displacement
- **Service Speed** = 9 knots
- **Block Coefficient** $C_b$ = 0.62
- **L/B ratio** = 5.0
- **B/T ratio** = 2.5
- **Sea water density** = 1.025 t/m³

#### **(a) Estimate the principal main dimensions L, B, and T of the trawler. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loaded Displacement**

$$\text{LSW} = 0.50 \cdot \Delta \quad ; \quad \Delta = 0.50\Delta + 150$$

$$0.50\Delta = 150 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta = 300 \ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for L**

$$L = \left[ \frac{\Delta \cdot R_1^2 \cdot R_2}{C_b \cdot \rho} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = \left[ \frac{300 \times (5.0)^2 \times 2.5}{0.62 \times 1.025} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = \left[ \frac{300 \times 25 \times 2.5}{0.6355} \right]^{1/3} = \left[ \frac{18{,}750}{0.6355} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = [29{,}504.33]^{1/3} \approx 30.94 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Back-substitute**

$$B = \frac{L}{5.0} = \frac{30.94}{5.0} \approx 6.19 \ \text{m}$$

$$T = \frac{B}{2.5} = \frac{6.19}{2.5} \approx 2.48 \ \text{m}$$

> **Answer:** L ≈ **30.94 m**, B ≈ **6.19 m**, T ≈ **2.48 m**.

#### **(b) Compute the required Effective Horsepower (EHP) using the empirical resistance formula $R_T = 8 \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^2$, where V is in m/s and R_T is in kgf. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Speed**

$$V = 9 \ \text{knots} \times 0.5144 = 4.63 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Resistance**

$$R_T = 8 \times (300)^{2/3} \times (4.63)^2$$

First, $(300)^{2/3}$: $300^{1/3} \approx 6.694$, so $300^{2/3} \approx 44.81$.

$$R_T = 8 \times 44.81 \times 21.44 \approx 7{,}684.5 \ \text{kgf}$$

**Step 3 — Compute EHP**

$$P_E = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} = \frac{7{,}684.5 \times 4.63}{75} \approx 474.4 \ \text{HP}$$

$$\boxed{P_E \approx 474 \ \text{HP}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

A purse seiner fishing vessel has the following specifications:

- **Deadweight** = 200 tonnes
- **Light Ship Weight** = 40% of loaded displacement
- **Service Speed** = 12 knots
- **L/B** = 5.5
- **B/T** = 2.3
- **L/D** = 10
- **Block Coefficient** $C_b$ = 0.58

#### **(a) Estimate the Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), Beam (B), Draft (T), and Depth (D). (8 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loaded Displacement**

$$\Delta = 0.40\Delta + 200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.60\Delta = 200$$

$$\Delta = 333.33 \ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — LBP Calculation**

$$L = \left[ \frac{333.33 \times (5.5)^2 \times 2.3}{0.58 \times 1.025} \right]^{1/3} = \left[ \frac{333.33 \times 30.25 \times 2.3}{0.5945} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$L = \left[ \frac{23{,}187.6}{0.5945} \right]^{1/3} = [39{,}003]^{1/3} \approx 33.97 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Beam**

$$B = \frac{L}{5.5} = \frac{33.97}{5.5} \approx 6.18 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Draft**

$$T = \frac{B}{2.3} = \frac{6.18}{2.3} \approx 2.69 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Depth**

$$D = \frac{L}{10} = \frac{33.97}{10} \approx 3.40 \ \text{m}$$

> **Answer:** L ≈ **33.97 m**, B ≈ **6.18 m**, T ≈ **2.69 m**, D ≈ **3.40 m**.

#### **(b) Determine the Admiralty Coefficient if the installed shaft horsepower is 850 HP and Brake Power equals 0.85 × EHP. (6 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective Power (EHP)**

Speed: $V = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.17 \ \text{m/s}$

Resistance (assume empirical $R_T = 7 \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^2$):

$$R_T = 7 \times (333.33)^{2/3} \times (6.17)^2$$

$$333.33^{2/3} \approx 48.77 \quad ; \quad R_T = 7 \times 48.77 \times 38.07 \approx 12{,}996 \ \text{kgf}$$

$$P_E = \frac{12{,}996 \times 6.17}{75} \approx 1069 \ \text{HP}$$

**Step 2 — Admiralty Coefficient**

$$C_{\text{Adm}} = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P_E} = \frac{(333.33)^{2/3} \times (6.17)^3}{1069}$$

$$= \frac{48.77 \times 234.9}{1069} \approx 10.72$$

> **Answer:** $C_{\text{Adm}} \approx$ **10.7** (dimensionless × knot³).

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up Δ and DWT** — Many students directly substitute DWT into the displacement equation. **Correct:** Always solve $\Delta = \text{LSW} + \text{DWT}$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting ρ = 1.025** — When Δ is in tonnes and L, B, T are in metres, ρ must appear. Omitting it produces an error of nearly 60% in L.
> 3. **Using LOA instead of LBP** — Formulas are standardized to **LBP**, not overall length.
> 4. **Inverting ratios incorrectly** — T = B/R₂, **not** R₂/B.
> 5. **Not iterating the powering check** — Examiners expect at least a one-pass EHP verification after estimating dimensions.
> 6. **Unit inconsistencies in EHP formula** — V must be in m/s and R_T in kgf for the constant 75 to be valid.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - Main Dimensions = **L (LBP), B, D, T** — fixed in this exact order.
> - Displacement equation: $\Delta = C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho$ with $\rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3$.
> - Always solve Δ first from DWT + LSW.
> - Empirical ratios: **L/B ∈ [4.5, 6.5]**, **B/T ∈ [2.0, 2.8]**, **L/D ∈ [9, 12]**.
> - Master formula: $L = \left[ \frac{\Delta \cdot R_1^2 \cdot R_2}{C_b \cdot \rho} \right]^{1/3}$.
> - Cb for fishing vessels: **0.55 – 0.65**; higher for slower coastal vessels.
> - Knot → m/s conversion: **multiply by 0.5144**.
> - EHP formula: $P_E = R_T V / 75$ (with V in m/s and R_T in kgf).
> - Coefficient chain: $C_b = C_p \times C_M$.
> - Always perform a **powering check** after dimension estimation.
> - **Parent vessel data** is the gold standard for selecting Cb and ratios — never pick them at random.
> - For KTU exams, present the **algorithm as numbered steps** with equations boxed or aligned for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Form Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Form Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Form Coefficients?

**Form Coefficients** (also called **Hull Form Coefficients** or **Fineness Coefficients**) are a set of dimensionless geometric ratios that mathematically describe the **fullness, slenderness, and shape characteristics of a ship's underwater hull**. In the context of fishing vessel design, these coefficients act as the fundamental "fingerprint" of the hull, allowing naval architects to predict resistance, stability, propulsion efficiency, cargo capacity, and seakeeping behavior *before* the vessel is ever built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Style):**
> *"Form coefficients are non-dimensional ratios comparing the actual displaced volume or area of a hull to the volume or area of a circumscribing rectangular prism (or related geometric primitive). They quantify how 'fat' or 'thin' a ship is relative to its principal dimensions."*

The four **principal form coefficients** used universally in fishing vessel design are:

1. **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** — overall fullness
2. **Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)** — fullness at amidships
3. **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)** — longitudinal taper of the hull
4. **Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)** — fullness at the design waterline

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clay Model" Intuition

Imagine you are a sculptor who has been given a rectangular block of clay measuring $L \times B \times T$ (Length × Breadth × Depth). Your job is to carve out a ship-shaped object.

- A **full ship** (like a tugboat or a fishing trawler) is created by carving away very little clay from the block — almost the entire rectangular volume remains filled with material. This ship has a **HIGH Block Coefficient ($C_B \approx 0.75$–$0.85$)**.
- A **slim ship** (like a racing yacht or a fast patrol boat) requires you to carve away most of the clay, leaving only a thin, needle-like shape. This ship has a **LOW Block Coefficient ($C_B \approx 0.30$–$0.45$)**.

The **Block Coefficient is essentially the "survival ratio" of the clay** — what fraction of the original block remains after sculpting.

Now think about the **Midship Coefficient**: at the very middle of the ship (amidships), if you look at the cross-section, it forms a roughly elliptical or U-shape. The Midship Coefficient tells you how much of the rectangular $B \times T$ "slice" at amidships is actually filled with hull. A trawler with a fat U-shaped belly has $C_M \approx 0.95$, while a sleek motorboat has $C_M \approx 0.70$.

The **Prismatic Coefficient** tells you how the fullness at the middle compares to the average fullness along the entire length. A ship that is fat at the middle and tapers sharply toward the ends has a **LOW** $C_P$ (like a submarine). A ship with a parallel middle body (typical of fishing vessels with large cargo holds) has a **HIGH** $C_P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reference Constants for Fishing Vessels:**
> - **Typical Fishing Trawler:** $C_B = 0.60$ to $0.72$, $C_P = 0.65$ to $0.75$, $C_M = 0.90$ to $0.98$
> - **Typical Fishing Troller:** $C_B = 0.50$ to $0.60$, $C_P = 0.60$ to $0.68$
> - **Displacement Limit (Max):** $C_B \leq 1.0$ (theoretically cannot exceed 1)
> - **Speed-Length Ratio ($V/\sqrt{L}$):** A major driver of coefficient selection
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Form Coefficients — Stepwise Logic

### 2.1.1 Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

**Definition:** Ratio of the displaced volume ($\nabla$) of the ship to the volume of a rectangular box having the same length ($L_{PP}$), beam ($B$), and draft ($T$).

**Physical Meaning:** Represents the overall volumetric efficiency or fullness of the hull. Higher $C_B$ = more displacement for a given principal dimension = lower resistance to motion at low speeds but higher at high speeds.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### 2.1.2 Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)

**Definition:** Ratio of the transverse sectional area at amidships ($A_M$) to the area of the circumscribing rectangle of beam $B$ and draft $T$.

**Physical Meaning:** Indicates how "bluff" or "fine" the midship cross-section is. Influences wave-making resistance and stability.

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

### 2.1.3 Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)

**Definition:** Ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of a prism with the same length and midship sectional area. Alternatively expressed as the ratio $C_B/C_M$.

**Physical Meaning:** Describes the longitudinal distribution of volume. A high $C_P$ indicates a hull with a long parallel middle body (good for cargo capacity), while a low $C_P$ indicates a hull that tapers significantly toward the ends (good for high-speed vessels).

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

### 2.1.4 Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)

**Definition:** Ratio of the waterplane area ($A_{WP}$) at the design waterline to the area of the rectangle $L_{WL} \times B$.

**Physical Meaning:** A measure of the fullness of the hull at the waterline. Directly affects the ship's added resistance in waves, roll damping, and course-keeping ability.

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

## 2.2 Secondary / Derived Form Coefficients

### 2.2.1 Longitudinal Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{LP}$ or $C_{PR}$)

Used mainly in planing and high-speed craft. Represents the ratio of the actual immersed volume to the volume of a cylinder of length $L$ and diameter equal to the maximum transverse section beam.

### 2.2.2 Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$)

Represents how the volume is distributed vertically between the keel and the waterline.

$$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WP} \cdot T}$$

### 2.2.3 Relationship between Coefficients

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} \quad \text{and} \quad C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

## 2.3 Why Form Coefficients Matter in Fishing Vessel Design

Fishing vessels (trawlers, purse seiners, longliners, trollers) operate across diverse profiles:
- **Trawlers** operate at moderate speeds (10–14 knots) but require large displacement for catch hold volume → favor higher $C_B$.
- **Tuna purse seiners** have high block coefficients due to massive bow flare and tank capacity.
- **High-speed fishing craft** (like squid jigging boats) require lower $C_B$ to minimize resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Resistance Estimation:** Uses Admiralty Coefficient or Holtrop-Mennen method where $C_B$ is a primary input.
> - **Stability & GM Calculation:** $C_B$ and $C_{WP}$ directly feed into Bonjean and Simpson's rule integrations.
> - **Powering Estimation:** $C_B$ determines the displacement-tonnage, which combined with speed gives EHP.
> - **Standard Series Selection:** Taylor's Standard Series and Series 60 are organized *by* $C_B$ and $B/T$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Coefficient | Formula | Typical Range (Fishing Vessels) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.50 – 0.75 | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | 0.85 – 0.98 | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | 0.60 – 0.78 | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$ | 0.70 – 0.90 | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Vertical Prismatic | $C_{VP} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_{WP} \cdot T}$ | 0.55 – 0.75 | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Coefficient Relation | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | — | Identity |

**Principal Dimension Symbols (KTU Notation):**
- $\nabla$ — Displaced volume in **m³**
- $L_{PP}$ — Length between perpendiculars in **m**
- $L_{WL}$ — Length at waterline in **m**
- $B$ — Maximum molded beam in **m**
- $T$ — Design draft in **m**
- $A_M$ — Midship sectional area in **m²**
- $A_{WP}$ — Waterplane area in **m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board-Exam Trap:** Many students confuse $L_{PP}$ and $L_{WL}$ when computing $C_B$ vs $C_{WP}$. Strictly: $C_B$ uses $L_{PP}$, while $C_{WP}$ uses $L_{WL}$. For fishing vessels, $L_{WL} > L_{PP}$ typically by 1–3% due to stern overhang and bow flare.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Relationship $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$

This is the **single most tested identity** on KTU examinations for form coefficients. The derivation must be shown rigorously.

**Starting Definitions:**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \qquad \text{(Definition of Block Coefficient)}$$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \qquad \text{(Definition of Midship Coefficient)}$$

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \qquad \text{(Definition of Prismatic Coefficient)}$$

**Algebraic Multiplication of $C_P$ and $C_M$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M &= \left( \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\nabla \cdot A_M}{A_M \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \quad \text{(since } A_M \text{ cancels)} \\[6pt]
&= C_B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\therefore C_B = C_P \cdot C_M}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The overall volumetric fullness ($C_B$) is the **product** of (i) how full the cross-section is at amidships ($C_M$) and (ii) how the volume is distributed longitudinally ($C_P$). This identity is exact and holds for any hull form.

## 3.2 Derivation of $\nabla$ from Given Coefficients

A common KTU board problem: Given $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$ and principal dimensions, find the displaced volume.

**Starting from $C_B$:**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Rearranging for $\nabla$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using $C_P$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L_{PP}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both forms must give the **same numerical $\nabla$**. If they do not, either the coefficients or the dimensions are inconsistent.

## 3.3 Empirical Estimation — Mumford's Formula for $C_B$

For preliminary design of fishing vessels and merchant ships, **Mumford (1940s)** developed a regression formula linking $C_B$ to the **Speed-Length Ratio (SLR)** and **Beam-Draft Ratio ($B/T$)**:

$$C_B = K - \left( \frac{V}{3.05 \cdot \sqrt{L_{PP}}} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{10 \cdot C_V}{C_P}\right)$$

where $K$ is a function of $B/T$ and $C_V$ is the volumetric coefficient (often equal to $C_B$ for conventional hulls). For a simpler KTU-applicable form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B \approx 1.10 - 0.275 \cdot \frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{PP}}} \quad &\text{(Very rough preliminary estimate)} \\
\text{where } V & \text{ is in knots and } L_{PP} \text{ is in metres}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The speed-length ratio $V/\sqrt{L}$ (with $V$ in knots, $L$ in feet) is the classical form. For SI units, the conversion is:
> $$V/\sqrt{L_{ft}} = V_{knots} \cdot \sqrt{0.3048} \cdot \sqrt{L_m^{-1}} \approx 0.554 \cdot \frac{V_{knots}}{\sqrt{L_m}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (Full KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP} = 28$ m
- Beam, $B = 7.5$ m
- Draft, $T = 3.2$ m
- Midship section area, $A_M = 21.6$ m²
- Displaced volume, $\nabla = 380$ m³

**Required:** Calculate $C_B$, $C_M$, and $C_P$, and verify the master identity.

### Step 1 — Calculate Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{380}{28 \times 7.5 \times 3.2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{380}{672.0} \\[4pt]
\therefore C_B &= 0.5655
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Midship Coefficient ($C_M$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{21.6}{7.5 \times 3.2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{21.6}{24.0} \\[4pt]
\therefore C_M &= 0.9000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)

**Method A — Direct Definition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{380}{21.6 \times 28} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{380}{604.8} \\[4pt]
\therefore C_P &= 0.6283
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method B — Using Master Identity $C_P = C_B / C_M$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.5655}{0.9000} = 0.6283
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification of $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M &= 0.6283 \times 0.9000 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5655 = C_B \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

- $C_B = 0.566$ → moderate fullness (typical for a fishing trawler operating at moderate speed).
- $C_M = 0.90$ → fat U-shaped midship (efficient for cargo hold volume and stability).
- $C_P = 0.628$ → moderate longitudinal taper (between parallel-middle-body and sharply-tapered forms).

**Comparison with Typical Fishing Trawler Ranges:** All values lie within the expected range. The vessel is correctly proportioned.

## 3.5 Estimation via $B/T$ Ratio — Ayre's Method (Simplified)

For preliminary design, the prismatic coefficient can be estimated from the **Beam-to-Draft ratio** and **displacement**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &\approx 0.55 + 0.20 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{T}\right) \quad &\text{(for displacement hulls)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For our trawler:** $B/T = 7.5/3.2 = 2.344$
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &\approx 0.55 + 0.20 \times 2.344 \\
&\approx 0.55 + 0.4688 \\
&\approx 1.019 \quad \text{(INVALID — exceeds 1.0!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Empirical Coefficient Traps:** Simple linear formulas are valid only within restricted ranges. Always cross-check the estimate against Taylor's Standard Series or Series 60 charts for the given $B/T$ and $C_B$ before accepting. For the trawler example, Ayre's formula fails because $B/T$ is too low; an alternative regression must be used.

## 3.6 Estimation of $C_{WP}$ — Simpson's Rule Approximation

For a fishing vessel with a known length-to-beam ratio ($L/B$) and assuming a parabolic waterplane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{WP} \approx \frac{2}{3} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.3 \cdot C_P}{L/B}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a useful preliminary-design rule of thumb.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull-Form Coefficient Inter-Relationship Block Diagram

The following **functional block diagram** illustrates how the four primary form coefficients interact and how they are derived from the underlying geometric quantities of a fishing vessel's hull. This conceptual map is essential for KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Principal Dimensions: L_PP, B, T] --> A1[Displaced Volume: nabla]
    A0 --> A2[Midship Area: A_M]
    A0 --> A3[Waterplane Area: A_WP]
    A1 --> B1["C_B = nabla / (L_PP x B x T)"]
    A2 --> B2["C_M = A_M / (B x T)"]
    A3 --> B3["C_WP = A_WP / (L_WL x B)"]
    A1 --> C1["C_P = nabla / (A_M x L_PP)"]
    A2 --> C1
    B1 --> D1["Identity: C_B = C_P x C_M"]
    C1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B1 --> E1["Resistance & Powering Estimation"]
    C1 --> E1
    B2 --> E2["Stability & GM Calculations"]
    B3 --> E2
    B3 --> E3["Seakeeping & Course Keeping"]
    E1 --> F1["Final Hull Form Selection"]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Standard Series Check: Taylor / Series 60]
    G1 --> H1[Validated Form Coefficient Set]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Origin of Each Coefficient (Visual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Block Coefficient C_B"]
        A1[Displaced Volume nabla] --> A2["C_B = nabla / (L x B x T)"]
        A3[Prism L x B x T] --> A2
    end
    subgraph B["Midship Coefficient C_M"]
        B1[Midship Area A_M] --> B2["C_M = A_M / (B x T)"]
        B3[Midship Rectangle B x T] --> B2
    end
    subgraph C["Prismatic Coefficient C_P"]
        C1[Displaced Volume nabla] --> C2["C_P = nabla / (A_M x L)"]
        C3[Longitudinal Prism A_M x L] --> C2
    end
    subgraph D["Waterplane Coefficient C_WP"]
        D1[Waterplane Area A_WP] --> D2["C_WP = A_WP / (L_WL x B)"]
        D3[Waterline Rectangle L_WL x B] --> D2
    end
```

## 4.3 Form Coefficient Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Fishing Vessel Type] --> S1{Target Speed?}
    S1 -->|Low: 8-10 knots| S2["High C_B: 0.65-0.75"]
    S1 -->|Medium: 10-14 knots| S3["Medium C_B: 0.55-0.65"]
    S1 -->|High: 15+ knots| S4["Low C_B: 0.40-0.55"]
    S2 --> S5{Cargo Hold Required?}
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 -->|Yes - Trawler Purse Seiner| S6["High C_P and C_M for Volume"]
    S5 -->|No - Troller Squid Jigger| S7["Lower C_P for Speed"]
    S6 --> S8[Finalize C_B, C_P, C_M, C_WP]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Validate via Taylor Series or Series 60]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Coefficient Zones on a Hull Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z1["Forward Region"]
        Z1A[Narrow Bow Section]
    end
    subgraph Z2["Parallel Middle Body"]
        Z2A[Constant Cross-Section]
        Z2B[C_P Approaches 1.0 Here]
    end
    subgraph Z3["After Region"]
        Z3A[Tapered Stern]
    end
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z1 -.C_P Low.-> Z1
    Z2 -.C_P High.-> Z2
    Z3 -.C_P Moderate.-> Z3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Block Coefficient and Prismatic Coefficient. State their typical ranges for a fishing trawler.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** It is defined as the ratio of the displaced volume ($\nabla$) of the vessel to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length ($L_{PP}$), beam ($B$), and draft ($T$).

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$):** It is defined as the ratio of the displaced volume ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism whose cross-section is the midship section area ($A_M$) and length is $L_{PP}$.

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$$

**Typical ranges for fishing trawlers:** $C_B = 0.60$ to $0.75$, $C_P = 0.65$ to $0.75$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Derive the relationship $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M &= \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \cdot \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\nabla \cdot A_M}{A_M \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} = C_B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore \boxed{C_B = C_P \cdot C_M}$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**A fishing trawler has the following principal particulars:**
- $L_{PP} = 30$ m, $L_{WL} = 31.5$ m
- $B = 7.8$ m, $T = 3.4$ m
- $\nabla = 410$ m³
- $A_M = 24.5$ m²
- $A_{WP} = 198$ m²
- Design speed $V = 11.5$ knots

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The Block Coefficient ($C_B$), Midship Coefficient ($C_M$), and Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$). Verify the master identity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{WP}$) and Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$). Comment on the suitability of the hull for trawling operations. **(7 Marks)**

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-A**

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Block Coefficient $C_B$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{410}{30 \times 7.8 \times 3.4} \\
&= \frac{410}{795.6} \\
\therefore C_B &= 0.5153
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Midship Coefficient $C_M$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{24.5}{7.8 \times 3.4} \\
&= \frac{24.5}{26.52} \\
\therefore C_M &= 0.9238
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}} \\
&= \frac{410}{24.5 \times 30} \\
&= \frac{410}{735.0} \\
\therefore C_P &= 0.5578
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification of $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M &= 0.5578 \times 0.9238 \\
&= 0.5153 \approx C_B \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{WP} &= \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B} \\
&= \frac{198}{31.5 \times 7.8} \\
&= \frac{198}{245.7} \\
\therefore C_{WP} &= 0.8059
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Vertical Prismatic Coefficient $C_{VP}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{VP} &= \frac{\nabla}{A_{WP} \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{410}{198 \times 3.4} \\
&= \frac{410}{673.2} \\
\therefore C_{VP} &= 0.6091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Engineering Commentary**

- The Block Coefficient $C_B = 0.515$ indicates a **moderately full hull** with good cargo capacity but not overly fat.
- The high Midship Coefficient $C_M = 0.924$ suggests a **bluff, U-shaped midship section** typical of trawlers, providing large internal volume for fish hold.
- The Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP} = 0.806$ ensures **good course-keeping** and **roll damping**, both critical for safe trawling operations in adverse weather.
- The Vertical Prismatic Coefficient $C_{VP} = 0.609$ shows that the displacement is concentrated slightly below the waterline, beneficial for **stability**.
- **Conclusion:** The hull is well-suited for trawling operations — providing adequate hold volume, stable platform, and reasonable speed capability.

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**During preliminary design of a small fishing vessel, the following data are estimated:**
- Required displacement: $\nabla = 220$ m³
- Selected Block Coefficient: $C_B = 0.58$
- Selected Prismatic Coefficient: $C_P = 0.65$
- Assumed $L_{PP}/B = 4.0$ and $B/T = 2.5$

**(a)** Estimate the principal dimensions ($L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$) and verify the consistency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Midship Coefficient $C_M$, the Midship Section Area $A_M$, and comment on whether the hull form is suitable for a purse-seiner. **(7 Marks)**

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-B**

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Express $L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$ in terms of one variable using ratios.**

Given $L_{PP}/B = 4.0 \Rightarrow L_{PP} = 4.0 B$
Given $B/T = 2.5 \Rightarrow T = B/2.5 = 0.4 B$

**Step 2: Apply Block Coefficient Definition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
0.58 &= \frac{220}{4.0 B \cdot B \cdot 0.4 B} \\
0.58 &= \frac{220}{1.6 \cdot B^3} \\
B^3 &= \frac{220}{0.58 \times 1.6} \\
B^3 &= \frac{220}{0.928} \\
B^3 &= 237.07 \\
B &= \sqrt[3]{237.07} \\
\therefore B &\approx 6.18 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate $L_{PP}$ and $T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{PP} &= 4.0 \times 6.18 = 24.72 \text{ m} \\
T &= 0.4 \times 6.18 = 2.47 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{calc} &= C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 0.58 \times 24.72 \times 6.18 \times 2.47 \\
&= 0.58 \times 377.4 \\
&= 218.9 \approx 220 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Calculate Midship Coefficient $C_M$.**

Using $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.58}{0.65} = 0.8923
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Midship Section Area $A_M$.**

Using $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{PP})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_M &= \frac{\nabla}{C_P \cdot L_{PP}} \\
&= \frac{220}{0.65 \times 24.72} \\
&= \frac{220}{16.07} \\
\therefore A_M &= 13.69 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Comment on Suitability for Purse-Seiner.**

A purse-seiner requires:
- **Large displacement** for fish hold and net storage: $\nabla = 220$ m³ is moderate.
- **High $C_B$ for buoyancy during net-pursing operations**: $C_B = 0.58$ is on the **lower side**; purse seiners typically need $C_B \geq 0.65$.
- **High $C_P$** for parallel middle body to maximize tank volume: $C_P = 0.65$ is just adequate.
- **High $C_M$** for stability when the vessel is "heeling to net": $C_M = 0.89$ is acceptable.

**Conclusion:** The hull is **marginally suitable**. For a purse-seiner, the Block Coefficient should be increased to $C_B \approx 0.65$–$0.70$ by either increasing the beam or reducing the length. The current proportions are more suited for a **small trawler or longliner**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert $L$ from metres to feet when using the classical speed-length ratio formula.
> 2. **Wrong Length Used:** Using $L_{WL}$ instead of $L_{PP}$ in $C_B$ calculation, or vice versa for $C_{WP}$.
> 3. **Identity Verification Skip:** Failing to show the cross-check $C_P \cdot C_M = C_B$, which costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Decimal Rounding Errors:** Rounding intermediate values too early; always carry 4–5 significant figures until the final answer.
> 5. **Empirical Formula Misuse:** Applying Mumford/Ayre formulas outside their valid range and accepting impossible results like $C_B > 1.0$.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

✅ **Block Coefficient $C_B = \nabla / (L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T)$** — overall hull fullness.

✅ **Midship Coefficient $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$** — fullness at amidships only.

✅ **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{PP}) = C_B / C_M$** — longitudinal taper.

✅ **Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$** — waterline fullness.

✅ **Vertical Prismatic $C_{VP} = \nabla / (A_{WP} \cdot T)$** — vertical volume distribution.

✅ **Master Identity:** $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ — must always verify after calculation.

✅ **Fishing Vessel Typical Ranges:** $C_B$ = 0.50–0.75, $C_M$ = 0.85–0.98, $C_P$ = 0.60–0.78, $C_{WP}$ = 0.70–0.90.

✅ **Length Convention:** $C_B$ uses $L_{PP}$; $C_{WP}$ uses $L_{WL}$.

✅ **Estimation Methods:** Empirical formulas (Mumford, Ayre), Statistical regression, Standard Series (Taylor, Series 60).

✅ **Design Drivers:** Speed-Length Ratio ($V/\sqrt{L}$), $B/T$ ratio, $L/B$ ratio, displacement requirement.

✅ **Engineering Use:** Resistance estimation, powering, stability (GM), seakeeping, cargo capacity prediction.

✅ **Validity Boundaries:** $0 < C_B \leq 1.0$; $0 < C_P \leq 1.0$; $0 < C_M \leq 1.0$; $0 < C_{WP} \leq 1.0$.

✅ **Always** cross-check empirical estimates against Taylor's Standard Series or Series 60 before final adoption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Light Ship Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Light Ship Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Light Ship Weight (LSW)**, also denoted as $W_{LS}$ or $W_L$, is defined as the complete weight of the vessel in its as-built condition, including the hull structure, permanent machinery, all fixed equipment, and outfit, but **excluding** all variable loads such as cargo (fish), fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, ballast water, crew, provisions, and catch-handling gear loads.

In naval architecture, the Light Ship Weight is a critical design constant because it represents the irreducible mass of the vessel. It is the foundation upon which the **Deadweight (DWT)** and the **Displacement ($\Delta$)** are calculated:

$$\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT$$

The Displacement ($\Delta$) is the total weight of water displaced by the vessel, which by **Archimedes' principle** equals the total weight of the vessel at any given loading condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For fishing vessels, the Light Ship Weight is typically expressed as a fraction of the loaded displacement using empirical coefficients (e.g., Watson's coefficient), since statistical estimation is the most practical approach during the preliminary design stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Empty Backpack" Principle

Imagine you are going on a long trip. Before adding your clothes, food, water bottle, and souvenirs (the **Deadweight**), you must first carry the **backpack itself** along with the empty water bottle and frame — this base mass is the **Light Ship Weight**.

In a fishing vessel:
- The **backpack** = hull steel, engine, propeller shaft, deckhouse, navigation equipment
- The **clothes + water bottle + souvenirs** = fish catch, fuel, ice, crew, fresh water

Just as a heavier backpack reduces how much cargo you can carry, a heavier Light Ship Weight reduces the **payload capacity** of the fishing vessel. Ship designers therefore strive to minimize $W_{LS}$ while maintaining structural integrity and safety.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| Light Ship Weight | $W_{LS}$ | Tonnes (t) or Long Tons (LT) |
| Displacement | $\Delta$ | Tonnes (t) or Long Tons (LT) |
| Deadweight | $DWT$ | Tonnes (t) |
| Watson's Coefficient | $C$ | Dimensionless |
| Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{PP}$ | Metres (m) |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** In KTU 2024 Scheme problems, $1 \text{ Long Ton (LT)} = 1.016 \text{ Metric Tonnes (t)}$. Always verify the unit convention specified in the question paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Estimation, Not Direct Calculation?

At the preliminary design stage of a fishing vessel, the exact weight of every plate, stiffener, and pipe cannot be computed because the detailed structural drawings do not yet exist. Naval architects therefore rely on **statistical regression formulas** derived from analyzing the light ship weights of similar, already-built vessels. The fundamental assumption is:

> *"Geometrically and functionally similar vessels of different sizes will have light ship weights that scale predictably with size."*

The most widely used scaling parameter is the **Displacement ($\Delta$)**, because it is a three-dimensional quantity (mass), and weight scales with volume. For weight varying as a function of volume, dimensional analysis suggests:

$$W_{LS} \propto \Delta^{2/3}$$

This is because $\Delta$ has units of mass (∝ Length³), and the wetted surface area — which governs hull plating weight — scales as Length², hence $\Delta^{2/3}$.

### 2.2 Components of Light Ship Weight

The Light Ship Weight is composed of four major weight groups:

1. **Hull Steel Weight ($W_H$):** Plating, frames, girders, bulkheads, stern frame, rudder, and all primary structural members.
2. **Machinery Weight ($W_M$):** Main engine, gear box, propeller shaft, propeller, auxiliary engines, generators, pumps.
3. **Outfit Weight ($W_O$):** Electrical wiring, piping systems, ventilation ducts, insulation, joiner work (cabins, flooring), paint, anchors, chains, life-saving equipment, navigation electronics.
4. **Superstructure & Deckhouse Weight ($W_{SS}$):** Wheelhouse, accommodation block, mast, funnel, and any raised structures.

Therefore:

$$W_{LS} = W_H + W_M + W_O + W_{SS}$$

### 2.3 Methods of Estimation

There are three principal methods used in KTU-aligned fishing vessel design:

#### **Method A: Watson's Formula (Statistical / Empirical)**

This is the most universally applied method in preliminary fishing vessel design:

$$W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$$

Where:
- $W_{LS}$ = Light Ship Weight in **Long Tons (LT)**
- $\Delta$ = Loaded Displacement in **Long Tons (LT)**
- $C$ = Watson's Coefficient (dimensionless, ship-type dependent)

**Typical Watson's Coefficient ($C$) for Fishing Vessels:**

| Vessel Type | Watson's Coefficient ($C$) |
|---|---|
| Small Outboard Fishing Boats | 0.15 – 0.20 |
| Stern Trawlers (small, < 25 m) | 0.20 – 0.25 |
| Side Trawlers (medium, 25–40 m) | 0.22 – 0.28 |
| Stern Trawlers (large, > 40 m) | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Tuna Purse Seiners | 0.28 – 0.35 |
| Long Liners | 0.25 – 0.30 |

#### **Method B: Weight Group Method (Detailed / Item-by-Item)**

Used in **contract design** and **detailed design** stages. Each major group is calculated using material densities and volumes, then summed.

#### **Method C: Percentage Breakdown Method**

The LSW is distributed as fixed percentages derived from similar vessels. Typical breakdown for a medium trawler:

| Weight Group | % of $W_{LS}$ |
|---|---|
| Hull Steel ($W_H$) | **55% – 65%** |
| Machinery ($W_M$) | **15% – 22%** |
| Outfit ($W_O$) | **18% – 25%** |
| Superstructure ($W_{SS}$) | **3% – 8%** |
| Fixed Ballast | **0% – 5%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When solving numerical problems, the weight group percentages and Watson's coefficient must be cross-checked against the vessel type. Using $C = 0.20$ for a tuna seiner (which requires $C \approx 0.32$) is a common error that leads to underestimation of LSW by ~15-20%.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT$ | Displacement identity | tonnes |
| 2 | $W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$ | **Watson's Formula** (most used) | LT |
| 3 | $W_H = (0.55 \text{ to } 0.65) \cdot W_{LS}$ | Hull steel weight estimate | tonnes |
| 4 | $W_M = (0.15 \text{ to } 0.22) \cdot W_{LS}$ | Machinery weight estimate | tonnes |
| 5 | $W_O = (0.18 \text{ to } 0.25) \cdot W_{LS}$ | Outfit weight estimate | tonnes |
| 6 | $DWT = \Delta - W_{LS}$ | Deadweight computation | tonnes |
| 7 | $\dfrac{W_{LS}}{\Delta} = C \cdot \Delta^{-1/3}$ | LSW-to-Displacement ratio | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the table above, the vertical bar symbol has been intentionally replaced with descriptive text ("to", "dimensionless") to preserve markdown table integrity. In your written answer sheets, the proper mathematical notation $0.55 \text{ to } 0.65$ should be used.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Estimation of Light Ship Weight is the **first quantitative step** in ship design. It directly determines:
- **Payload Capacity:** Higher LSW = Lower fish-carrying capacity.
- **Propulsion Sizing:** Engine power is partly chosen based on the LSW (especially during transit at light load).
- **Stability Analysis:** The vertical centre of gravity ($KG$) is computed only after LSW and its longitudinal distribution are known.
- **Cost Estimation:** Hull steel is the most expensive material; its weight estimate drives material cost projections.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Classification societies (e.g., IRS, BV, DNV) require accurate LSW declarations for stability booklets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Watson's Formula — The Dimensional Logic

The derivation is based on **dimensional homogeneity** and the **principle of geometric similarity**.

**Step 1: State the assumption**
For a family of geometrically similar vessels, all linear dimensions scale with a single characteristic length $L$. Therefore, the displacement volume $∇$ scales as:

$$\nabla \propto L^3$$

**Step 2: Convert volume to mass (displacement)**
Using the density of seawater $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$:

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla \propto L^3$$

**Step 3: Relate weight to surface area (since structure depends on surface)**
The wetted surface area $S$ of the hull scales as:

$$S \propto L^2$$

**Step 4: Express the structural weight**
The hull plating, frames, and stiffeners all depend on the surface area, with a constant material density $\rho_{st}$ and an average plate thickness $t$. Assuming similar stress levels (so $t/L$ is approximately constant):

$$W_{H} \propto \rho_{st} \cdot t \cdot S \propto L^2 \cdot L \propto L^3 \propto \Delta$$

But this gives $W_H \propto \Delta^1$, which is over-simplified. A more accurate empirical fit from real data shows:

$$W_{LS} \propto \Delta^{2/3}$$

This exponent of $2/3$ arises from the empirical fact that **as vessels get larger, the structural efficiency improves** (thinner plating relative to length is possible), so weight grows slower than displacement.

**Step 5: Introduce the proportionality constant**

$$W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$$

This is **Watson's Formula**, verified empirically over a wide range of ship sizes.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A 30-metre stern trawler is to be designed with a loaded displacement of $\Delta = 450 \text{ LT}$. Using Watson's formula with $C = 0.23$, calculate:
1. The Light Ship Weight ($W_{LS}$)
2. The Deadweight (DWT)
3. The hull steel weight, assuming it constitutes 60% of $W_{LS}$

**Complete Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 30$ m
- Displacement $\Delta = 450$ LT
- Watson's Coefficient $C = 0.23$
- Hull steel fraction = 60% of $W_{LS}$

**Step 1: Apply Watson's Formula**

$$W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$$

Substituting values:

$$W_{LS} = 0.23 \cdot (450)^{2/3}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate $\Delta^{2/3}$**

$$(450)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{450} \approx 7.6631$$

$$(450)^{2/3} = (450)^{1/3} \cdot (450)^{1/3} = 7.6631 \cdot 7.6631$$

$$(450)^{2/3} \approx 58.7334$$

**Step 3: Compute $W_{LS}$**

$$W_{LS} = 0.23 \cdot 58.7334$$

$$\boxed{W_{LS} \approx 13.51 \text{ LT}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Deadweight (DWT)**

$$DWT = \Delta - W_{LS}$$

$$DWT = 450 - 13.51$$

$$\boxed{DWT \approx 436.49 \text{ LT}}$$

**Step 5: Compute Hull Steel Weight**

$$W_H = 0.60 \cdot W_{LS}$$

$$W_H = 0.60 \cdot 13.51$$

$$\boxed{W_H \approx 8.11 \text{ LT}}$$

**Step 6: Verification and Sanity Check**

The LSW-to-Displacement ratio:

$$\frac{W_{LS}}{\Delta} = \frac{13.51}{450} \approx 0.030 \text{ or } 3.0\%$$

This is within the expected range (typically 3%–8% for small to medium fishing vessels). ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Convention:** In the worked example, intermediate results are rounded to 4 decimal places only at the final step. This prevents compounding rounding errors — a common pitfall in KTU board examinations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Best Application Stage | Accuracy | Computational Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Watson's Formula** | Preliminary / Concept Design | ±10–15% | Very Low (one equation) |
| **Weight Group Method** | Contract Design | ±5–8% | High (item-by-item) |
| **Percentage Breakdown** | Concept / Feasibility | ±12–18% | Low |
| **CFD / FEA-Based Estimation** | Detailed Design | ±3–5% | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Light Ship Weight Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vessel Concept] --> B[Define Principal Dimensions: L, B, T, D]
    B --> C[Compute Displacement: Delta = rho x Volume]
    C --> D{Select Vessel Type?}
    D -->|Stern Trawler| E[Use C = 0.20 to 0.25]
    D -->|Side Trawler| F[Use C = 0.22 to 0.28]
    D -->|Tuna Purse Seiner| G[Use C = 0.28 to 0.35]
    D -->|Long Liner| H[Use C = 0.25 to 0.30]
    E --> I[Apply Watson Formula: WLS = C x Delta to the 2/3]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute DWT = Delta minus WLS]
    J --> K[Apply Percentage Breakdown Method]
    K --> L[Hull Steel: 55 to 65 percent]
    K --> M[Machinery: 15 to 22 percent]
    K --> N[Outfit: 18 to 25 percent]
    K --> O[Superstructure: 3 to 8 percent]
    L --> P[Sum Verification: Total approx WLS]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Cross-check with Similar Vessel Database]
    Q --> R[Final LSW Approved]
    R --> S[Proceed to Stability and Powering Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hierarchical Decomposition of Light Ship Weight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Light Ship Weight WLS] --> B[Hull Steel Weight WH]
    A --> C[Machinery Weight WM]
    A --> D[Outfit Weight WO]
    A --> E[Superstructure Weight WSS]
    B --> B1[Shell Plating]
    B --> B2[Deck Plating]
    B --> B3[Frames and Girders]
    B --> B4[Bulkheads]
    C --> C1[Main Engine]
    C --> C2[Auxiliary Generators]
    C --> C3[Propeller and Shaft]
    C --> C4[Pumps and Compressors]
    D --> D1[Electrical System]
    D --> D2[Piping and Ventilation]
    D --> D3[Joiner Work and Insulation]
    D --> D4[Anchors Chains and Mooring]
    E --> E1[Wheelhouse]
    E --> E2[Accommodation Block]
    E --> E3[Mast and Funnel]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Iterative Design Refinement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial LSW Estimate] --> B[Stability Analysis]
    B --> C{GM meets Criterion?}
    C -->|No| D[Adjust Hull Form or Ballast]
    D --> E[Recompute Displacement]
    E --> F[Recompute LSW via Watson]
    F --> B
    C -->|Yes| G[Powering Estimation]
    G --> H[Final Design Frozen]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The hierarchical diagram above uses purely alphanumeric node identifiers (A, B, C, D1, etc.) and clean uppercase text inside quoted labels, in compliance with Mermaid compilation safeguards. These flowcharts represent the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the LSW estimation process — a strategy recommended by the KTU 2024 Scheme for representing complex ship design workflows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
Define the term **Light Ship Weight** of a fishing vessel. List the four major weight groups that constitute the LSW.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (1.5 Marks):* The Light Ship Weight ($W_{LS}$) is the weight of the fishing vessel in its as-built condition, including the hull, machinery, outfit, and superstructure, but excluding all variable loads such as fish catch, fuel, fresh water, lubricating oil, crew, and provisions.

*Four Major Weight Groups (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):*
1. Hull Steel Weight ($W_H$)
2. Machinery Weight ($W_M$)
3. Outfit Weight ($W_O$)
4. Superstructure Weight ($W_{SS}$)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
State **Watson's formula** for estimating Light Ship Weight. Explain the significance of the coefficient $C$ and give its typical range for a stern trawler.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Formula (1 Mark):*

$$W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$$

Where $W_{LS}$ and $\Delta$ are in long tons, and $C$ is Watson's dimensionless coefficient.

*Significance of $C$ (1 Mark):* The coefficient $C$ accounts for vessel type, structural material, machinery density, outfit complexity, and construction efficiency. It is empirically derived from the statistical analysis of similar existing vessels.

*Typical Range for Stern Trawler (1 Mark):* For a stern trawler, $C$ typically lies in the range **0.20 to 0.25**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive Watson's formula for estimating the light ship weight of a fishing vessel, clearly stating the underlying assumptions. Mention the typical range of Watson's coefficient for:
- (i) Small outboard fishing boats
- (ii) Side trawlers
- (iii) Tuna purse seiners
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 28 m side trawler has a loaded displacement of 380 LT. Using Watson's formula with $C = 0.25$, calculate:
- (i) The Light Ship Weight
- (ii) The Deadweight capacity
- (iii) The weight of hull steel, machinery, and outfit, using the percentage breakdown method (60%, 18%, 20% respectively)
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Assumption of Geometric Similarity (2 Marks):** Vessels of the same type but different sizes are assumed to be geometrically similar. All linear dimensions scale with a single characteristic length $L$, so displacement volume $∇ \propto L^3$, and displacement $\Delta \propto L^3$. Wetted surface area $S \propto L^2$.

2. **Empirical Scaling (2 Marks):** Hull structural weight is dominated by plating and stiffeners distributed over the wetted surface. Empirically, the average plate thickness $t$ scales such that the structural weight grows with displacement to the power $2/3$ rather than $1$. This is captured by $W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$.

3. **Coefficient Ranges (3 Marks — 1 each):**
   - (i) Small outboard fishing boats: $C = 0.15$ to $0.20$
   - (ii) Side trawlers: $C = 0.22$ to $0.28$
   - (iii) Tuna purse seiners: $C = 0.28$ to $0.35$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $\Delta = 380$ LT, $C = 0.25$, $L = 28$ m

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta^{2/3}$ (1 Mark)**

$$\Delta^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{380} \approx 7.2432$$

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 7.2432^2 \approx 52.464$$

**Step 2: Compute $W_{LS}$ (1 Mark)**

$$W_{LS} = 0.25 \cdot 52.464 \approx 13.12 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 3: Compute DWT (1 Mark)**

$$DWT = \Delta - W_{LS} = 380 - 13.12 = 366.88 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 4: Weight Breakdown (4 Marks — 1.33 each)**

$$W_H = 0.60 \cdot 13.12 = 7.87 \text{ LT}$$

$$W_M = 0.18 \cdot 13.12 = 2.36 \text{ LT}$$

$$W_O = 0.20 \cdot 13.12 = 2.62 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 5: Verification (0 Mark — implicit)**

$$W_H + W_M + W_O = 7.87 + 2.36 + 2.62 = 12.85 \approx W_{LS} \text{ (minor rounding)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **weight group method** of estimating light ship weight. Discuss its advantages and limitations over Watson's statistical method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The preliminary design of a 35 m tuna purse seiner indicates a loaded displacement of 720 LT. Estimate the LSW using Watson's formula with $C = 0.32$. If the vessel must carry 250 LT of fish catch, 80 LT of fuel, 25 LT of fresh water, and 5 LT of crew and provisions, verify whether the design has sufficient deadweight capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Method Description (3 Marks):** The weight group method divides the LSW into discrete, calculable groups: hull steel, machinery, outfit, and superstructure. Each group is computed by summing the weights of individual components (e.g., plates, stiffeners, frames for hull; main engine, auxiliaries, shaft, propeller for machinery). Component weights are obtained from material densities, geometric volumes, and standard mass-per-unit-length tables.

2. **Advantages over Watson's Method (2 Marks):**
   - Higher accuracy (typically ±5–8%) compared to Watson's (±10–15%).
   - Provides detailed distribution of weight, useful for centre of gravity ($KG$) calculation.
   - Allows identification of weight-optimization opportunities.

3. **Limitations (2 Marks):**
   - Requires detailed knowledge of every component, which is unavailable at the preliminary stage.
   - Time-consuming and labour-intensive.
   - Requires access to material databases and standards.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $\Delta = 720$ LT, $C = 0.32$, vessel length $L = 35$ m

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta^{2/3}$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$\Delta^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{720} \approx 8.9628$$

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 8.9628^2 \approx 80.33$$

**Step 2: Compute $W_{LS}$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$W_{LS} = 0.32 \cdot 80.33 \approx 25.71 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 3: Compute DWT (1 Mark)**

$$DWT = \Delta - W_{LS} = 720 - 25.71 = 694.29 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Variable Load (1.5 Marks)**

$$W_{var} = 250 + 80 + 25 + 5 = 360 \text{ LT}$$

**Step 5: Verification (1.5 Marks)**

Since $DWT = 694.29 \text{ LT} \gg W_{var} = 360 \text{ LT}$, the design has **more than sufficient deadweight capacity**. The excess capacity is $(694.29 - 360) = 334.29 \text{ LT}$, which could be utilized for additional ice, refrigerated seawater (RSW) tanks, or extended endurance fuel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing Long Tons (LT) and Metric Tonnes (t) within the same numerical problem leads to a **2-mark deduction** under the strict KTU 2024 marking scheme. Always declare the unit before substitution.
> 2. **Watson's Coefficient Mismatch:** Using the wrong $C$ value for the vessel type (e.g., $C = 0.20$ for a tuna seiner) is treated as a **conceptual error** and forfeits **3 marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Exponent Confusion:** Writing $\Delta^{3/2}$ instead of $\Delta^{2/3}$ — this is the **single most common arithmetic error** observed in KTU board evaluations. The exponent $2/3$ reflects the surface-area-to-volume scaling, and a reversed exponent changes the answer by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Forgetting Fixed Ballast:** Some fishing vessels carry permanent ballast ($W_{ballast}$) that must be included in $W_{LS}$. Omitting this is a **1-mark deduction** if relevant to the question.
> 5. **Decimal Rounding:** Rounding intermediate values to 2 decimal places (e.g., truncating 7.6631 to 7.66) before the final answer introduces a **systematic 0.5–1% error** that KTU examiners explicitly penalize.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Estimation of Light Ship Weight**

- ✅ **Definition:** $W_{LS}$ = vessel weight in as-built condition, **excluding** cargo, fuel, water, crew, and provisions.
- ✅ **Identity:** $\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT$ (always holds for any loading condition).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $W_{LS} = C \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$ (Watson's — most used in KTU papers).
- ✅ **Watson's $C$ Range:** $0.15$ (small boats) to $0.35$ (large purse seiners).
- ✅ **Default $C$:** If vessel type is unspecified in the question, assume $C = 0.25$ (general fishing vessel).
- ✅ **Weight Breakdown (Memorize):** Hull 60% | Machinery 20% | Outfit 20% (approximate).
- ✅ **Unit Convention:** KTU prefers **Long Tons (LT)**; $1$ LT $= 1.016$ tonnes.
- ✅ **Cube Root Calculation:** $\Delta^{2/3} = (\Delta^{1/3})^2$; always compute the cube root first, then square.
- ✅ **LSW-to-$\Delta$ Ratio:** Typically 3%–8% for fishing vessels.
- ✅ **Method Selection:** Use Watson's for preliminary; use weight group method for contract design.
- ✅ **Cross-Verification:** Always verify LSW against a similar vessel's data (regression check).
