- ✅ **Iterative Nature:** LSW estimate feeds back into displacement calculation — the design is iterative, not linear.
- ✅ **Exponent Direction:** $\Delta^{2/3}$, **NEVER** $\Delta^{3/2}$ (most common mistake in KTU exams).
- ✅ **Output Use:** $W_{LS}$ is consumed by stability, powering, resistance, and cost analyses.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Dead Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Dead Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dead Weight (DW) / Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** of a fishing vessel is the **total variable weight** that a vessel must carry when operating at its design load condition. It is mathematically defined as the difference between the **Full-Load Displacement (Δ)** and the **Lightship Weight (LWT)**.

$$\text{Dead Weight (DW)} = \Delta_{\text{full load}} - W_{\text{lightship}}$$

In the specific context of a **fishing vessel**, Dead Weight includes the weight of: fish catch, fuel oil, lubricating oil, fresh water, provisions & stores, crew & their effects, and—uniquely to fishing vessels—the **fishing gear and equipment** (nets, longlines, winches, traps, pots, refrigerated sea water (RSW) systems, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lightship Weight (LWT)**: The weight of the completely empty vessel including hull, machinery, outfit, and permanent ballast—but **NO** consumables, cargo, crew, or fishing gear.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a fishing vessel like a **delivery truck** that has to carry many different "loads" for a single trip:

- A truck has an **empty weight** (the truck itself).
- It then loads **fuel** (to run), **water** (for the driver), **food/snacks** (provisions), and the **actual cargo** (the delivery).
- The **dead weight** of the truck is the sum total of everything it carries *except* its own empty weight.

A fishing vessel is identical, except its "cargo" is the **fish catch** + **fishing gear** (nets, lines, traps). The vessel must also carry enough **fuel** for the voyage, **ice or RSW water** to preserve the catch, **drinking water** for the crew, and **food** for the days at sea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sea Water Density:** $\rho_{sw}$ = **1.025 t/m³** (salt water)
> **Standard Fresh Water Density:** $\rho_{fw}$ = **1.000 t/m³**

### 1.3 Significance in KTU Examinations

The estimation of Dead Weight is a **high-yield topic** because:
1. It is foundational to every subsequent design calculation (stability, powering, freeboard, etc.).
2. It directly determines the **economic viability** of the fishing operation.
3. It is part of the **Weight Estimation Phase** of the design spiral.
4. Numerical problems (computing DW component-by-component) are common in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Dead Weight Components

The Dead Weight of a fishing vessel can be systematically classified into **seven (7) major categories**:

**(a) Weight of Fish Catch (W_catch)**
- Determined by the **fish hold capacity** and the **preservation method**.
- For trawlers: $W_{catch} = V_{hold} \times \rho_{fish}$ where $\rho_{fish}$ ≈ **0.55 to 0.65 t/m³** (whole fish) or **0.95 t/m³** (block-frozen fish).
- Typical hold volume: 3 to 6 m³ per tonne of fish capacity.

**(b) Weight of Fuel Oil (W_fuel)**
- $W_{fuel} = C_{fc} \times R \times t_v \times \rho_{fuel}$
- Where: $C_{fc}$ = specific fuel consumption (**0.18 to 0.22 kg/kWh** for marine diesel), $R$ = engine power used, $t_v$ = voyage duration (hours), $\rho_{fuel}$ ≈ **0.86 t/m³** for marine diesel.

**(c) Weight of Lubricating Oil (W_lube)**
- Usually 3% to 5% of $W_{fuel}$.
- $W_{lube} = 0.04 \times W_{fuel}$ (approximation)

**(d) Weight of Fresh Water (W_water)**
- $W_{water} = N_{crew} \times q_w \times t_v$
- Daily consumption $q_w$ ≈ **0.10 to 0.15 tonnes/crew/day**.
- $\rho_{water}$ = **1.00 t/m³**.

**(e) Weight of Provisions & Stores (W_stores)**
- $W_{stores} = N_{crew} \times q_s \times t_v$
- Daily provision $q_s$ ≈ **0.005 to 0.008 tonnes/crew/day** (food, consumables).

**(f) Weight of Crew & Effects (W_crew)**
- $W_{crew} = N_{crew} \times m_{crew}$
- Average mass per person ≈ **0.075 tonnes** (75 kg) including personal effects.

**(g) Weight of Fishing Gear (W_gear)** — **UNIQUE TO FISHING VESSELS**
- $W_{gear} = W_{nets} + W_{lines} + W_{winches} + W_{traps/pots}$
- This is the most vessel-specific component:
  - **Trawler:** Net + otter boards + warps ≈ 5–15% of DW
  - **Purse-seiner:** Net + purse lines + rings ≈ 8–15% of DW
  - **Longliner:** Main line + branch lines + hooks ≈ 2–5% of DW

### 2.2 Empirical Dead Weight Coefficient Method

A quick first-cut estimation uses the **Deadweight Coefficient ($C_W$)**:

$$C_W = \frac{DW}{\Delta_{\text{full load}}}$$

For fishing vessels, typical $C_W$ values are:

| Vessel Type | Typical $C_W$ Range |
|:---|:---:|
| Small coastal trawlers (10–18 m) | **0.45 – 0.55** |
| Medium stern trawlers (18–30 m) | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| Tuna purse seiners (30–60 m) | **0.55 – 0.65** |
| Longliners (15–35 m) | **0.40 – 0.50** |
| Squid jiggers (40–70 m) | **0.55 – 0.65** |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $DW = \Delta - LWT$ | Fundamental definition | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $C_W = \dfrac{DW}{\Delta}$ | Deadweight coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Delta = L \times B \times T \times C_B \times \rho$ | Volume displacement formula | tonnes |
| 4 | $W_{fuel} = C_{fc} \cdot P \cdot t_v \cdot \rho_f$ | Fuel weight | tonnes |
| 5 | $W_{water} = N_c \cdot q_w \cdot t_v$ | Fresh water weight | tonnes |
| 6 | $W_{stores} = N_c \cdot q_s \cdot t_v$ | Provisions weight | tonnes |
| 7 | $W_{crew} = N_c \cdot m_c$ | Crew + effects weight | tonnes |
| 8 | $W_{catch} = V_h \cdot \rho_{fish}$ | Fish catch weight | tonnes |
| 9 | $DW = a \cdot L^b$ | Length-based empirical estimate | tonnes |
| 10 | $LWT = 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95 \times DW_{design}$ | Quick LWT estimate (small trawlers) | tonnes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, the total DW is often expressed as a *percentage breakdown* — memorize the typical ranges for fuel (~20%), water (~8%), stores (~4%), crew (~3%), gear (~12%), catch (~45%), and misc (~8%).

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

Dead Weight estimation is the **starting point** for:
- **Stability analysis** (KG, GM, GZ curves).
- **Powering** (BHP, EHP estimates).
- **Freeboard & tonnage** (regulatory compliance).
- **Hull strength** (still-water bending moments).
- **Economic studies** (cargo capacity, voyage cost).

In the fishing industry, an **under-estimation of DW** leads to a vessel sitting too low in the water (loss of freeboard, safety hazard), while an **over-estimation** results in a heavy, under-utilized, fuel-inefficient vessel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Fundamental Displacement Principle

We start from Archimedes' principle: a floating vessel displaces a volume of water whose mass equals the vessel's total mass.

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = displacement mass (tonnes)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of sea water = **1.025 t/m³**
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)

The displaced volume for a vessel with principal dimensions $L_{pp}$, $B$, $T$ is:

$$\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$

Where $C_B$ is the **block coefficient**. Therefore:

$$\Delta = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

By definition, the full-load displacement equals the sum of lightship weight and dead weight:

$$\Delta_{\text{full load}} = LWT + DW$$

Solving for Dead Weight:

$$DW = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \cdot \rho_{sw} - LWT$$

This is the **master equation** for DW estimation in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Component-by-Component Build-Up

This is the **most common KTU exam approach**. The Dead Weight is summed over all variable loads:

$$\begin{aligned}
DW &= W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{lube} + W_{water} \\
&\quad + W_{stores} + W_{crew} + W_{gear} + W_{misc}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting each component formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
DW &= (V_h \cdot \rho_{fish}) + (C_{fc} \cdot P \cdot t_v \cdot \rho_f) + (0.04 \cdot W_{fuel}) \\
&\quad + (N_c \cdot q_w \cdot t_v) + (N_c \cdot q_s \cdot t_v) + (N_c \cdot m_c) + W_{gear} + W_{misc}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Empirical Length-Based Estimate (for preliminary design)

Statistical analysis of existing fishing vessels gives a power-law relationship:

$$DW = a \cdot L^b \quad (\text{in tonnes, with } L \text{ in metres})$$

For **small trawlers (10–25 m)**: $a \approx 0.012$ to $0.025$, $b \approx 2.7$ to $2.9$.

**Example numerical check** — For $L = 20$ m, using $a = 0.018$, $b = 2.8$:

$$\begin{aligned}
DW &= 0.018 \times (20)^{2.8} \\
&= 0.018 \times 4061.2 \\
&\approx 73.1 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a reasonable dead weight for a 20 m trawler.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A 22 m fishing trawler has the following particulars:
> - $L_{pp} = 22$ m, $B = 6.5$ m, $T = 2.8$ m, $C_B = 0.55$
> - Engine power $P = 350$ kW, voyage duration $t_v = 7$ days
> - Crew $N_c = 12$, fuel consumption $C_{fc} = 0.20$ kg/kWh
> - Fish hold volume $V_h = 80$ m³, fish density $\rho_{fish} = 0.60$ t/m³
> - Sea water density = 1.025 t/m³
>
> **Estimate the Dead Weight component-by-component.**

**Step 1: Fish catch weight**

$$W_{catch} = V_h \cdot \rho_{fish} = 80 \times 0.60 = 48.0 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Fuel oil weight**

Voyage hours: $t_v = 7 \times 24 = 168$ h
Assume 80% of max power used at sea: $P_{used} = 0.8 \times 350 = 280$ kW

$$W_{fuel} = 0.20 \times 280 \times 168 \times 0.86 / 1000$$

Wait — the units need to be reconciled carefully. The $0.20$ kg/kWh is mass per energy. Multiplying by power × time gives total fuel mass in kg, which must be divided by 1000 to get tonnes.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{fuel} &= \frac{0.20 \times 280 \times 168}{1000} \text{ tonnes (ignoring density factor as it is a mass-based formula)} \\
&= \frac{9408}{1000} = 9.41 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Lubricating oil weight**

$$W_{lube} = 0.04 \times 9.41 = 0.376 \approx 0.38 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4: Fresh water weight**

Using $q_w = 0.12$ t/crew/day:

$$W_{water} = 12 \times 0.12 \times 7 = 10.08 \text{ t}$$

**Step 5: Provisions & stores weight**

Using $q_s = 0.006$ t/crew/day:

$$W_{stores} = 12 \times 0.006 \times 7 = 0.504 \approx 0.50 \text{ t}$$

**Step 6: Crew & effects weight**

Using $m_c = 0.075$ t/person:

$$W_{crew} = 12 \times 0.075 = 0.90 \text{ t}$$

**Step 7: Fishing gear weight** (estimated as 10% of total non-catch DW)

Non-catch items so far: $9.41 + 0.38 + 10.08 + 0.50 + 0.90 = 21.27$ t

$$W_{gear} = 0.10 \times 21.27 = 2.13 \text{ t}$$

*(Alternatively, gear weight can be specified directly in the problem.)*

**Step 8: Miscellaneous weight**

$$W_{misc} \approx 0.05 \times DW_{\text{rest}} \text{ (iterate if needed)} \approx 1.5 \text{ t}$$

**Step 9: Total Dead Weight**

$$\begin{aligned}
DW &= 48.0 + 9.41 + 0.38 + 10.08 + 0.50 + 0.90 + 2.13 + 1.5 \\
&\approx 72.9 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10: Verification using displacement formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \cdot \rho_{sw} \\
&= 22 \times 6.5 \times 2.8 \times 0.55 \times 1.025 \\
&= 225.2 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_W = \frac{DW}{\Delta} = \frac{72.9}{225.2} = 0.324$$

> [!WARNING]
> The $C_W$ value of 0.324 is **lower than the typical range (0.45–0.55)** for small trawlers. This indicates either the vessel is under-loaded at this design condition, or the assumed LWT is too heavy. KTU examiners expect you to **comment on the reasonableness** of the result.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DW Component Ranges for Fishing Vessels

| Component | % of Total DW (Trawler) | % of Total DW (Purse Seiner) | % of Total DW (Longliner) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Fish catch | **40 – 50%** | 35 – 45% | 30 – 40% |
| Fuel oil | 15 – 22% | 18 – 25% | 20 – 28% |
| Lube oil | 1 – 2% | 1 – 2% | 1 – 2% |
| Fresh water | 6 – 10% | 5 – 8% | 8 – 12% |
| Stores & provisions | 2 – 4% | 2 – 3% | 3 – 5% |
| Crew & effects | 1 – 3% | 1 – 2% | 2 – 3% |
| Fishing gear | 8 – 15% | 10 – 18% | 5 – 10% |
| Miscellaneous | 3 – 6% | 3 – 5% | 3 – 6% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Dead Weight Components

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **hierarchical breakdown** of Dead Weight and the inter-relationship of its components in a fishing vessel.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FULL LOAD DISPLACEMENT Delta_full] --> B[Lightship Weight LWT]
    A --> C[Dead Weight DW]
    C --> D[Variable Loads]
    C --> E[Consumables]
    C --> F[Cargo and Gear]
    D --> D1[Fishing Gear W_gear]
    D --> D2[Miscellaneous Stores W_misc]
    E --> E1[Fuel Oil W_fuel]
    E --> E2[Lubricating Oil W_lube]
    E --> E3[Fresh Water W_water]
    E --> E4[Provisions and Stores W_stores]
    E --> E5[Crew and Effects W_crew]
    F --> F1[Fish Catch W_catch]
    F1 --> F11[Whole Fish rho = 0.55 to 0.65 t per m3]
    F1 --> F12[Block Frozen Fish rho = 0.95 t per m3]
    D1 --> D1a[Nets and Warps]
    D1 --> D1b[Otter Boards]
    D1 --> D1c[Winches and Cranes]
    E1 --> E1a[Diesel Oil rho = 0.86 t per m3]
    E1 --> E1b[Consumption = Cfc times P times tv]
    E3 --> E3a[Drinking Water rho = 1.00 t per m3]
    E3 --> E3b[Daily Quota qw = 0.10 to 0.15 t per day]
    E4 --> E4a[Food Provisions]
    E4 --> E4b[Daily Quota qs = 0.005 to 0.008 t per day]
    E5 --> E5a[Number of Crew Nc]
    E5 --> E5b[Mass per Crew mc = 0.075 t]
```

### 4.2 Design Process Flow — Estimating Dead Weight

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1: Define Vessel Mission] --> S2[Step 2: Estimate Voyage Duration tv]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Determine Crew Number Nc]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Fuel and Water]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Determine Fish Hold Capacity]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Sum All DW Components]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compute Lightship Weight LWT]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Total Displacement Delta = LWT + DW]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Verify Using Empirical CW]
    S9 --> S10{Is CW Reasonable?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Accept Design]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Iterate Principal Dimensions]
    S12 --> S2
```

### 4.3 Visual Schematic of a Fishing Vessel Weight Distribution

```mermaid
graph TD
    Hull[Hull and Superstructure LWT 60 to 70 percent] --> Top[Topside Weight]
    Hull --> Bot[Bottom Weight]
    Mach[Machinery and Propulsion 20 to 25 percent of LWT] --> MainE[Main Engine]
    Mach --> AuxE[Auxiliary Engines]
    Mach --> ShaftProp[Shaft and Propeller]
    Out[Outfit and Equipment 10 to 15 percent of LWT] --> Elect[Electrical]
    Out --> Piping[Piping Systems]
    Out --> Safety[Life Saving and Safety]
    Out --> FishG[Fishing Gear Winches and Cranes]
    FGDW[Dead Weight DW] --> Catch[Fish Catch 40 to 50 percent]
    FGDW --> FuelE[Fuel and Oil 17 to 25 percent]
    FGDW --> WaterDW[Fresh Water 6 to 10 percent]
    FGDW --> StoresDW[Stores Crew Gear 15 to 25 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams map the **functional architecture** of the Dead Weight estimation process and the relative weight distribution. They are designed to substitute for a physical ship-section drawing, which cannot be easily rendered in Mermaid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Dead Weight of a fishing vessel. List any four major components of DW with their typical percentage share for a stern trawler.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Dead Weight (DW)** of a fishing vessel is the total weight of all variable loads carried by the vessel at its design load condition, exclusive of the lightship weight. It is given by $DW = \Delta - LWT$.

**Four major components for a stern trawler (with typical % share):**

| Component | % of DW |
|:---|:---:|
| Fish catch | **40 – 50%** |
| Fuel oil | **15 – 22%** |
| Fresh water | **6 – 10%** |
| Fishing gear (nets, otter boards, warps) | **8 – 15%** |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Lightship Weight (LWT) and Dead Weight (DW) for a fishing vessel. Why is the distinction important in design?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lightship Weight (LWT) | Dead Weight (DW) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Weight of empty vessel (hull + machinery + permanent outfit) | Weight of all variable loads (cargo, fuel, water, crew, gear) |
| Variability | **Constant** for a given vessel | **Varies** with voyage and catch |
| Includes | Hull plating, engines (empty), masts, fixed tanks | Fish, fuel, water, ice, crew, fishing gear |
| Time dependency | Fixed once built | Changes trip-to-trip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distinction is **critical** because LWT determines the **minimum displacement** (empty voyage), while DW determines the **maximum displacement** (full-load voyage). Both extremes are used for **stability analysis** at departure and arrival conditions, and the **range** between them defines the vessel's operating envelope.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions follow a **choice pattern** — students answer **ONE** out of two alternatives. Both questions below are calibrated to 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **component-by-component method** of estimating the Dead Weight of a fishing trawler. Mention the relevant formulas and standard constants used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 m fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Fish hold volume: $V_h = 110$ m³, $\rho_{fish} = 0.60$ t/m³
- Engine power: $P = 450$ kW, $C_{fc} = 0.21$ kg/kWh, voyage = 10 days
- Fuel density: 0.86 t/m³, lube oil = 4% of fuel
- Crew = 14, water quota = 0.12 t/crew/day, stores = 0.006 t/crew/day
- Crew mass = 0.075 t/crew, fishing gear = 5.0 t, miscellaneous = 2.0 t
- Sea water density = 1.025 t/m³

**Calculate the total Dead Weight and the Deadweight Coefficient** if the vessel has $L_{pp} = 25$ m, $B = 7.2$ m, $T = 3.0$ m, $C_B = 0.58$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Component-by-component method:**

The DW is built up by summing the weights of seven categories:
1. **Fish catch:** $W_{catch} = V_h \times \rho_{fish}$
2. **Fuel oil:** $W_{fuel} = C_{fc} \times P \times t_v \times \rho_f$ (or equivalently mass-based: $C_{fc} \times P \times t_v / 1000$)
3. **Lubricating oil:** $W_{lube} \approx 0.04 \times W_{fuel}$
4. **Fresh water:** $W_{water} = N_c \times q_w \times t_v$
5. **Provisions:** $W_{stores} = N_c \times q_s \times t_v$
6. **Crew:** $W_{crew} = N_c \times m_c$
7. **Fishing gear & misc:** specified directly or estimated as % of DW.

Sum all: $DW = \sum W_i$

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Step 1: Fish catch**

$$W_{catch} = 110 \times 0.60 = 66.0 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Fuel oil (assume 80% MCR at sea)**

$P_{used} = 0.8 \times 450 = 360$ kW, $t_v = 10 \times 24 = 240$ h

$$W_{fuel} = \frac{0.21 \times 360 \times 240}{1000} = 18.14 \text{ t}$$

(Using mass-based formula, density not needed.)

**Step 3: Lube oil**

$$W_{lube} = 0.04 \times 18.14 = 0.73 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4: Fresh water**

$$W_{water} = 14 \times 0.12 \times 10 = 16.80 \text{ t}$$

**Step 5: Provisions**

$$W_{stores} = 14 \times 0.006 \times 10 = 0.84 \text{ t}$$

**Step 6: Crew**

$$W_{crew} = 14 \times 0.075 = 1.05 \text{ t}$$

**Step 7: Fishing gear** = **5.0 t** (given)

**Step 8: Miscellaneous** = **2.0 t** (given)

**Step 9: Total Dead Weight**

$$\begin{aligned}
DW &= 66.0 + 18.14 + 0.73 + 16.80 + 0.84 + 1.05 + 5.0 + 2.0 \\
&= 110.56 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10: Displacement**

$$\Delta = 25 \times 7.2 \times 3.0 \times 0.58 \times 1.025 = 321.16 \text{ t}$$

**Step 11: Deadweight Coefficient**

$$C_W = \frac{110.56}{321.16} = 0.344$$

**Step 12: Lightship Weight**

$$LWT = \Delta - DW = 321.16 - 110.56 = 210.60 \text{ t}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to convert the voyage duration from days to **hours** when computing fuel weight, leading to gross numerical errors (fuel weight appearing 24× too small). Always write: "$t_v (\text{hours}) = t_v (\text{days}) \times 24$".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students confuse the fuel formula — the $0.86$ t/m³ density is for **volume-based** fuel tank sizing. The standard **mass-based** formula is: $W_{fuel} = C_{fc} \cdot P \cdot t_v / 1000$ (in tonnes when $C_{fc}$ is in kg/kWh). Choose ONE convention and stick to it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** The calculated $C_W = 0.344$ is **below** the typical trawler range (0.45–0.55). This means either (i) the vessel is over-designed for the assumed catch, or (ii) the LWT is overestimated. **Always comment on the reasonableness** of $C_W$ — this is a 1-mark "design judgment" point in KTU papers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is the **Deadweight Coefficient ($C_W$)?** How is it used as a **preliminary design check** for fishing vessels? List the typical $C_W$ ranges for (i) stern trawlers, (ii) purse seiners, and (iii) longliners. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **empirical length-based formula** $DW = a \cdot L^b$ with $a = 0.015$ and $b = 2.8$, estimate the dead weight of a **28 m tuna longliner**. If the vessel has a block coefficient of 0.52 and operates in sea water of density 1.025 t/m³ with $B = 7.0$ m and $T = 3.2$ m, **find the deadweight coefficient** and comment on its reasonableness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Deadweight Coefficient:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_W = \dfrac{DW}{\Delta_{\text{full load}}}$, a dimensionless number representing the **fraction of total displacement** that is variable load (DW).

**Usage in preliminary design check:**
- $C_W$ provides a **sanity check** on the calculated DW.
- If computed $C_W$ falls **outside** the typical range for the vessel type, the designer must revisit the principal dimensions, tank capacities, or catch assumptions.
- $C_W$ also helps in **rapid scaling** of one design to a similar vessel of different size.

**Typical $C_W$ ranges:**

| Vessel Type | $C_W$ Range |
|:---|:---:|
| Stern trawler | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| Purse seiner | **0.55 – 0.65** |
| Longliner | **0.40 – 0.50** |

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Step 1: Empirical DW estimate**

$$DW = 0.015 \times (28)^{2.8}$$

Computing $(28)^{2.8}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(28) &= 3.332 \\
2.8 \times 3.332 &= 9.330 \\
e^{9.330} &\approx 11,318
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$DW = 0.015 \times 11{,}318 = 169.77 \text{ t} \approx 170 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Displacement**

$$\Delta = L \times B \times T \times C_B \times \rho = 28 \times 7.0 \times 3.2 \times 0.52 \times 1.025$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 28 \times 7.0 \times 3.2 \times 0.52 \times 1.025 \\
&= 28 \times 7.0 \times 3.2 \times 0.533 \\
&= 334.2 \text{ t (approximately)}
\end{aligned}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 28 \times 7.0 \times 3.2 \times 0.52 \times 1.025 \\
&= 28 \times 7.0 = 196 \\
&= 196 \times 3.2 = 627.2 \\
&= 627.2 \times 0.52 = 326.14 \\
&= 326.14 \times 1.025 = 334.30 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Deadweight Coefficient**

$$C_W = \frac{169.77}{334.30} = 0.508$$

**Step 4: Design judgment**

> [!NOTE]
> The calculated $C_W = 0.508$ is **slightly above** the typical longliner range (0.40–0.50). This suggests the vessel is carrying a **heavy load** relative to its size, which is reasonable for a tuna longliner with full fuel tanks for an extended voyage and significant catch capacity. The result is on the **upper edge of acceptability**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Dead Weight Estimation**

- **Core Definition:** $DW = \Delta - LWT$; $C_W = DW / \Delta$.
- **7 Components of DW:** Catch, Fuel, Lube, Water, Stores, Crew, Gear (+ Misc).
- **Fish density:** Whole fish ≈ 0.60 t/m³; Block-frozen ≈ 0.95 t/m³.
- **Fuel density:** 0.86 t/m³; $C_{fc} \approx 0.20$ kg/kWh.
- **Water density:** 1.00 t/m³; per-crew quota 0.10–0.15 t/day.
- **Stores quota:** 0.005–0.008 t/crew/day.
- **Crew mass:** 0.075 t/person (including effects).
- **Lube oil:** ~4% of fuel oil weight.
- **Voyage hours:** Always convert days to hours: $t_v(h) = t_v(d) \times 24$.
- **Sea water density:** 1.025 t/m³ (always unless fresh water specified).
- **$C_W$ Ranges:** Trawler 0.50–0.60; Seiner 0.55–0.65; Longliner 0.40–0.50.
- **Displacement Formula:** $\Delta = L \times B \times T \times C_B \times \rho_{sw}$.
- **Empirical DW:** $DW = a \cdot L^b$ with $a \approx 0.015$, $b \approx 2.8$ (preliminary).
- **Fishing gear weight** is **unique to fishing vessels** — never omit it.
- **Always verify** the reasonableness of $C_W$ — this earns design-judgment marks.
- **LWT estimation:** Use empirical formula or manufacturer data; do not include any variable load.
- **Typical DW share for trawlers:** Catch ~45%, Fuel ~18%, Water ~8%, Gear ~12%, Misc ~17%.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Mixing mass-based and volume-based fuel formulas — pick one and stay consistent.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Forgetting the LWT component in the displacement equation.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Reporting $C_W$ without commenting on whether it is within the expected range.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Using fresh water density (1.000) when the vessel operates in the sea (use 1.025).
- **Units check:** All weights in **tonnes (t)**, lengths in **metres (m)**, time in **hours (h)**.
- **Block coefficient** $C_B$ typically 0.45–0.65 for fishing vessels.
- **Lightship weight** is usually 50–60% of full-load displacement for small trawlers.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Estimation of Dead Weight Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (OENST833) syllabus and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Lines, General Arrangement: Engine Room
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Lines, General Arrangement: Engine Room ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Design of Lines (Hull Form Design) **Formal Definition:** The *Design of Lines* in fishing vessel naval architecture refers to the systematic mathematical and graphical definition of the hull's external geometry through a **Lines Plan** — a coordinated set of three orthogonal projections (Profile / Sheer Plan, Half-Breadth / Plan View, and Body Plan) generated from a tabular set of **offsets** (station coordinates) that fully describe the three-dimensional hull surface of a fishing vessel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For fishing vessels, the lines plan is the foundation document from which hydrostatic properties, stability, resistance, powering, propeller design, and general arrangement are derived. Every downstream calculation depends on accurate fairing of the lines. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Clay Sculpture Blueprint":** Imagine sculpting a fish (or a wooden canoe) by hand. Before cutting wood or shaping fibreglass, you would draw the silhouette from the side, the view from above, and the cross-section at every "slice" along its length. These three views together fully define the 3D shape. The *Design of Lines* is exactly this — but expressed as numerical tables of *breadths* (half-beam) and *heights* at every station, frame spacing, and waterline. The lines plan is the *birth certificate* of the hull: every later drawing (GA, construction plan, lines plan for classification) is a child of it. ### 1.2 General Arrangement (GA) of the Engine Room **Formal Definition:** The *General Arrangement (GA) of the Engine Room* is the top-down (and sometimes longitudinal section) plan drawing that shows the **location, size, and spatial relationship of the main propulsion engine, auxiliary machinery, tanks, pumps, piping trunks, control stations, and access/escape routes** within the machinery space of a fishing vessel, compliant with classification society rules (e.g., IRS, BV, DNV, Lloyd's Register) and the IMO/Indian Fishing Vessel rules. > [!NOTE] > **Why Engine Room GA is distinct from the rest of the vessel GA:** The engine room is the *most regulation-dense* space on a fishing vessel because it contains (a) fire and explosion hazards (fuel oil, lube oil), (b) high-temperature/pressure systems, (c) rotating machinery hazards, and (d) single-compartment flooding risk that affects survivability. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen of a Ship":** Think of a ship's engine room as the *galley (kitchen) of a restaurant on water*. The chef (main engine) needs: - Easy access to fuel (fuel oil tanks) placed near the burner - Auxiliaries (mixers, grinders = pumps, compressors) placed within arm's reach - Exhaust ventilation (mushroom + supply fans) over the stove - A separate fire-suppression blanket (CO₂ / foam fixed system) - An escape door (emergency exit) away from the main entrance - The waiter (shaft) leaves through a passage (shaft tunnel) to the stern **Key Constants / Standard Metrics (must memorize):** - **Frame spacing** in small fishing vessels: typically **500 mm to 600 mm** (Rule of thumb: L/40 to L/50 between frames). - **Station numbering**: Station 0 at AP (Aft Perpendicular) or stern post; Station 20 (or last station) at FP (Forward Perpendicular). - **Number of stations**: usually **21 stations** (even, including 0 and 20) — i.e., 20 equal intervals along LBP. - **Engine room location**: predominantly **aft** in modern fishing vessels (≥ 85 % of contemporary designs) for propeller efficiency, crew comfort, and cargo space utilization. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of a Lines Plan A complete fishing vessel **Lines Plan** consists of: 1. **Sheer Plan (Profile View)** — view from the side, showing waterlines, buttock lines, and the intersection of the centreline plane with the hull. Shows **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**, **Length Overall (LOA)**, **Design Waterline (DWL)**, **Sheer line**, **Stem profile**, **Stern profile**, and **Transom**. 2. **Half-Breadth Plan (Top View)** — view from above (one side only), showing the **buttock lines** as curves and the **waterlines** intersecting the hull at each station. Shows **Beam (B)**, **Moulded breadth**. 3. **Body Plan (Front & Side Cross Sections)** — view looking forward at each station. Shows station lines as **waterlines on the right** (forward of midships) and **waterlines on the left** (aft of midships) — the standard naval-architect convention. > [!NOTE] > The **Body Plan** is the *master* drawing because every hull line is plotted and faired in this view first, then transferred to the Sheer and Half-Breadth plans using *ordinate measurement* (walking the offsets with dividers). ### 2.2 Hull Form Coefficients — The "Shape Numbers" These dimensionless ratios describe how *blocky* or *fine* the hull is. **Crucial for KTU numericals.** | Symbol | Coefficient | Formula | Range (Fishing Vessel) | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | $C_B$ | **Block Coefficient** | $\nabla \big/ (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | **0.45 – 0.65** | Volume efficiency; ratio of displaced volume to enclosing rectangular block | | $C_P$ | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $\nabla \big/ (A_M \cdot L_{BP})$ | **0.55 – 0.72** | Longitudinal fullness; how "cigar-like" the hull is | | $C_M$ | **Midship Section Coefficient** | $A_M \big/ (B \cdot T)$ | **0.75 – 0.92** | Fullness at midships section | | $C_WP$ | **Waterplane Area Coefficient** | $A_{WP} \big/ (L_{BP} \cdot B)$ | **0.70 – 0.85** | Fullness of waterplane; affects stability & added resistance | | $C_V$ | **Vertical Prismatic** | $\nabla \big/ (A_{WP} \cdot T)$ | **0.60 – 0.80** | Vertical distribution of volume | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorization Hook (K-C-P-M):** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ — the **Three Pillars Identity** that examiners love to test. ### 2.3 Lines Design Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Establish Principal Dimensions** (LBP, B, T, D) from mission profile (catch/day, fishing grounds, endurance). 2. **Choose Parent Form** — select a similar existing vessel or use a standard series (e.g., **Series 64**, **NPL series**, **BSRA**). 3. **Generate Offset Table** at every station for: baseline-to-deck heights (vertical ordinates) and half-breadths (horizontal ordinates) at multiple waterlines (typically 5–7). 4. **Plot on Body Plan** — the cross-sections form the **station lines**; these are faired (smoothed) using **splines** or **NACA-type fairing**. 5. **Project to Sheer & Half-Breadth Plans** — fair the waterlines and buttock lines by transferring dimensions. 6. **Compute Hydrostatics** — areas, volumes, centroids via **Simpson's 1st Rule** or **Simpson's 2nd Rule** (for even number of ordinates, 1st rule; for odd, 2nd rule). 7. **Iterate** until the form meets: stability criteria, resistance target, seakeeping, and GA constraints (e.g., engine room volume). ### 2.4 Engine Room GA — Design Philosophy **Key Design Drivers (in order of priority):** - **Safety & Regulation** (highest priority): SOLAS, IMO, Indian Fishing Vessel Rules, Classification Society (IRS) — minimum escape routes (≥ 2), cofferdams between fuel tanks and heated surfaces, fire-detection zones. - **Functional Efficiency**: minimum pipe runs, gravity feed where possible, short shaft line, accessible auxiliaries for maintenance. - **Survivability**: engine room flooding (single-compartment standard) → position critical equipment high, locate sea inlets low but with **collision chocks**. - **Crew Workflow**: main passage ≥ **700 mm wide**; access to every item ≤ 3 m reach; clear sightline from control station. - **Vibration & Noise**: main engine on **flexible chocks**; resilient mountings; engine room below accommodation. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Hull fullness check | | 2 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$ | dimensionless | Hull shape indicator | | 3 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Section fullness | | 4 | Three Pillars | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | dimensionless | Identity check | | 5 | Waterplane Coeff. | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{BP} \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Stability input | | 6 | Displacement (vol.) | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | Volume of hull below WL | | 7 | Displacement (mass) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \cdot g$ | tonnes (or kN) | $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ (seawater) | | 8 | LCB Position | $\overline{LCB} = \dfrac{\int x \cdot A(x) \, dx}{\int A(x) \, dx}$ | m from AP | Trim & resistance | | 9 | Simpson's 1st Rule (n even) | $\int \approx \dfrac{h}{3} \left[ y_0 + y_n + 4 \sum y_{odd} + 2 \sum y_{even} \right]$ | — | Area/Volume integration | | 10 | Simpson's 2nd Rule (n odd) | $\int \approx \dfrac{h}{3} \left[ y_0 + y_n + 4 \sum y_{odd} + 2 \sum y_{even} \right] - \dfrac{3h}{8}(y_0 - y_n)$ | — | End-corrected integration | | 11 | Engine Room Volume (Rule of Thumb) | $V_{ER} \approx (0.025 \text{ to } 0.040) \cdot \nabla$ | m³ | Initial sizing for GA | | 12 | Frame Spacing | $s \approx L_{BP} \big/ 40$ to $L_{BP} \big/ 50$ | m | Transverse frame rule | | 13 | Min. Headroom in ER | $H_{min} = 1.9$ to $2.1$ m | m | Crew ergonomics | | 14 | Main Engine Foundation Length | $L_{ME} \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot \sqrt[3]{P_{ME}}$ | m (P in kW) | Initial estimate | > [!WARNING] > Do **not** use vertical pipe symbols (`|x|`) in your answer sheets if writing in markdown tables — examiners use OCR, and pipes break table parsing. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility The lines plan and engine room GA together feed: - **Stability booklet** (righting arm curves, GZ) → required by Flag State for fishing vessel certification. - **Powering calculation** (EHP, BHP via Holtrop or Series 64) → determines main engine rating. - **Classification drawings** (midship section, profile, decks) → required for IRS/IACS class approval. - **Cost estimation** (steel weight, outfit weight) → used by shipyard bid. - **Crew safety case** → emergency escape, fire zones, SOLAS compliance. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Three Pillars Identity $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ **Step 1** — Start from the definition of Block Coefficient (volume efficiency): $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Step 2** — Multiply numerator and denominator by the midship section area $A_M$ (a strategic insertion that will cancel terms later): $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \cdot \frac{A_M}{A_M} = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} \cdot \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ **Step 3** — Recognize the two ratios as known coefficients: - The first factor $\dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$ is the definition of the **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P$. - The second factor $\dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ is the definition of the **Midship Section Coefficient** $C_M$. **Step 4** — Substitute: $$ C_B = C_P \cdot C_M \quad \blacksquare $$ **Physical Interpretation:** - $C_P$ captures **longitudinal** fullness (length-wise). - $C_M$ captures **transverse** fullness (cross-section at midships). - $C_B$ is the **product** of both — total volumetric fullness. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** This identity is the single most-tested formula in fishing vessel design modules. If you remember nothing else, remember $C_B = C_P \times C_M$. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Hull Coefficient Calculation **Problem:** A fishing trawler has the following particulars: - Length Between Perpendiculars $L_{BP} = 24.0$ m - Moulded Breadth $B = 7.20$ m - Design Draft $T = 2.80$ m - Midship Section Area $A_M = 14.50$ m² - Displacement Volume $\nabla = 285$ m³ **Find:** $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, and verify the Three Pillars Identity. Comment on hull form. **Solution:** **Step 1** — Calculate Block Coefficient $C_B$: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ $$ C_B = \frac{285}{24.0 \times 7.20 \times 2.80} $$ $$ C_B = \frac{285}{483.84} = 0.589 $$ **Step 2** — Calculate Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$: $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ $$ C_M = \frac{14.50}{7.20 \times 2.80} = \frac{14.50}{20.16} = 0.719 $$ **Step 3** — Calculate Prismatic Coefficient using the identity (back-calculated): $$ C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.589}{0.719} = 0.819 $$ **Step 4** — Verify by direct calculation: $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{285}{14.50 \times 24.0} = \frac{285}{348.0} = 0.819 $$ **Step 5** — Identity Check: $$ C_P \cdot C_M = 0.819 \times 0.719 = 0.589 = C_B \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6** — Comment on hull form: - $C_B = 0.589$ → typical of a **moderate-to-full fishing trawler** (range 0.45–0.65 for fishing vessels ✓). - $C_M = 0.719$ → indicates a **moderately full midship** with significant bilge radius, good for stability with low freeboard. - $C_P = 0.819$ → on the **higher end** — means the hull has parallel midbody (good for cargo volume) and runs fine at the ends. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** This hull is well-suited for a trawler that needs deck working space and large displacement for stability in beam seas, but with a moderately fine entry for the towing resistance of trawl gear. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Engine Room Location | Location | Pros | Cons | When Chosen | |---|---|---|---| | **Aft (most common)** | Short shaft → less power loss; propeller in clean water; protects crew from noise; more useful forward space for fish handling | Vulnerable to stern flooding; noise near accommodation must be controlled | Modern fishing vessels ≥ 18 m | | **Midships** | Good for very long vessels; balanced trim | Splits cargo & working spaces; long shafting | Older designs, factory trawlers | | **Forward** | Good for towing (tension on warp) — used on **trawlers with forward gantry** | Dangerous — floodable bow; noise in bow; long shaft | Rare, special-purpose | | **Combined** (split ER) | Specialized machinery placement | Complex piping, high cost | Naval auxiliaries only | ### 3.4 Engine Room Volume Sizing Derivation **Rule of Thumb Method:** $$ V_{ER} = k \cdot \nabla $$ where the empirical constant $k$ depends on machinery density: - $k = 0.025$ → for vessels with simple, small auxiliary systems (e.g., small trawlers). - $k = 0.040$ → for vessels with complex machinery (factory trawlers, reefer plants, processing equipment). **For our example trawler** ($\nabla = 285$ m³, k = 0.030 average): $$ V_{ER} = 0.030 \times 285 = 8.55 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Cross-check by major component sizing (additive method):** $$ V_{ER} \approx L_{ME} \cdot B_{ME} \cdot H_{ME} + \sum V_{aux} + V_{tanks, ER} + V_{passage} $$ For a 250 kW main engine: $L_{ME} \approx 1.8 \times \sqrt[3]{250} \approx 1.8 \times 6.30 \approx 3.0$ m; assume $B_{ME} = 1.2$ m, $H_{ME} = 1.8$ m → main engine footprint volume ≈ $6.5$ m³. Add auxiliaries, tanks, passage → totals 8–10 m³. **Consistent with rule of thumb ✓.** ### 3.5 Simpson's Rule Application for Waterplane Area For a 21-station (even, n = 20 intervals) fishing vessel, the waterplane area at the design waterline is: $$ A_{WP} \approx \frac{h}{3} \left[ y_0 + y_{20} + 4 \sum_{i=1,3,5,...,19} y_i + 2 \sum_{i=2,4,6,...,18} y_i \right] $$ where: - $h$ = station spacing = $L_{BP} / 20$ (m) - $y_i$ = half-breadth at station $i$ (m) — so the result is $2 \times$ the integrated half-breadth (since hull is symmetric about the centreline). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** Always integrate the **half-breadth** $y_i$, then multiply by 2 to obtain the full waterplane area. This avoids double-counting. ### 3.6 LCB Calculation (Analytical) The longitudinal centre of buoyancy relative to midships is: $$ \overline{LCB}_{\text{from mid}} = \frac{L_{BP}}{2} - \frac{\int_0^{L_{BP}} x \cdot A(x) \, dx}{\int_0^{L_{BP}} A(x) \, dx} $$ where $A(x)$ is the section area at longitudinal position $x$. In practice, this is integrated using **Simpson's rule on the section area curve** (ordinates = $A_i$ at each station, abscissa = $x_i$). > [!WARNING] > **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Some textbooks measure LCB *forward of AP* (positive forward), others *forward of midships* (positive forward, negative aft). Always state your convention in the answer. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Lines Plan — Three-View Schematic (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TB nodeA["LINES PLAN OF FISHING VESSEL
Three Orthogonal Views"] nodeB["SHEER PLAN
Profile / Side View
Shows: DWL, Sheer, Stem, Stern, LBP"] nodeC["HALF-BREADTH PLAN
Top View
Shows: Waterlines, Buttock lines, Beam B"] nodeD["BODY PLAN
Front and Side Cross-Sections
Master drawing: Station lines faired first"] nodeE["Station 0 to Station 20
21 stations, 20 equal intervals"] nodeF["Waterlines WL0 to WL5
Baseline to Sheer"] nodeG["Offsets Table
yi at each station and waterline"] nodeH["HYDROSTATIC
Calculations"] nodeI["STABILITY
Booklet"] nodeJ["POWERING
and GA Layout"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeJ ``` ### 4.2 Engine Room General Arrangement — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB nodeER["ENGINE ROOM GENERAL ARRANGEMENT
Fishing Vessel - Aft Location"] nodeA["MAIN ENGINE
on flexible chocks

LBP, B, T, D"] step2["Step 2: Choose Hull Form

Key Geometric Features"] hullB["STEM PROFILE
