KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Lines Plan" of a fishing vessel. Name its three principal views.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Lines Plan** is a graphical and mathematical representation of the external hull form of a fishing vessel, prepared from a tabulated set of offsets (half-breadths and heights at each station and waterline). It is the master drawing from which all hydrostatic, stability, resistance, and general arrangement calculations are derived. The three principal views are: 1. **Sheer Plan** — longitudinal profile view from the side. 2. **Half-Breadth Plan** — top view showing one side. 3. **Body Plan** — view of the transverse sections at every station (front and side). *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the sheer plan, 0.5 marks each for half-breadth and body plan.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any six items normally shown on the General Arrangement drawing of an engine room of a fishing vessel.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 marks each, any 6):** 1. Main propulsion engine (with foundations / chocks). 2. Marine gearbox and shaft tunnel leading to the stern tube. 3. Auxiliary generator sets (typically 2 nos. for redundancy). 4. Fuel oil purifiers, lube oil purifiers, and oil storage tanks. 5. Compressors with air receiver. 6. Pumps (bilge, fire, cooling sea-water, fresh-water). 7. Engine room control station (forward, with good visibility). 8. Cofferdams (between fuel tanks and heated surfaces). 9. Fire-fighting fixed installation (CO₂ / foam cylinders). 10. Escape trunk / main access door (watertight). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > KTU 2024 ESE Format: Internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. Each option typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with sketches, the **three views of a Lines Plan** used in fishing vessel design. State the function of each view. **(b) [7 marks]** A fishing trawler has the following particulars: - $L_{BP} = 28.0$ m, $B = 8.0$ m, $T = 3.0$ m - Displacement volume $\nabla = 360$ m³ - Midship section area $A_M = 17.5$ m² Calculate the **block coefficient ($C_B$)**, **midship coefficient ($C_M$)**, and **prismatic coefficient ($C_P$)**. Comment on the suitability of the hull form for a trawler. --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Sheer Plan (2 marks):** The sheer plan is a side elevation of the hull, drawn on the centreline plane. It shows: - The **design waterline (DWL)**, **baseline**, **sheer line** (deck edge), and any chine. - The **stem profile** (forward) and **stern profile** (aft — transom or cruiser). - The **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** and overall length. - The intersections of all **buttock lines** (vertical longitudinal planes) with the hull. - Function: defines the longitudinal silhouette, the position of the design waterline, and the fore-and-aft trim baseline. **Half-Breadth Plan (2 marks):** The half-breadth plan is a top-down view, showing only one side (typically the port side is omitted for clarity, only the starboard half is drawn). It shows: - The **waterlines** as a family of curves (WL1, WL2, …) running fore-and-aft. - The intersections of the **buttock lines** with each waterline. - The **moulded breadth B** at the design waterline. - Function: defines the planform shape (how beam varies along the length), important for resistance and waterplane area calculation. **Body Plan (2 marks):** The body plan is the view obtained by looking forward at each station, projected onto the transverse plane. Conventionally: - The right side of the body plan shows **forward stations** (e.g., stations 0–10). - The left side shows **aft stations** (e.g., stations 10–20). - All **waterlines** are drawn as curves, with the **centreline** as a vertical reference. - Function: this is the **master drawing** where hull lines are faired first; offsets are then transferred to the sheer and half-breadth plans using dividers. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The three views are mutually consistent — a point on the hull has the same offset in all three views. The body plan is faired first, then projected. **Part (b) — 7 marks — Step-by-step calculation:** **Step 1: Block Coefficient $C_B$ (2 marks):** $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{360}{28.0 \times 8.0 \times 3.0} = \frac{360}{672.0} = 0.536 $$ **Step 2: Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$ (2 marks):** $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{17.5}{8.0 \times 3.0} = \frac{17.5}{24.0} = 0.729 $$ **Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$ (2 marks):** $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{360}{17.5 \times 28.0} = \frac{360}{490.0} = 0.735 $$ **Step 4: Verification using identity (0.5 marks):** $$ C_P \times C_M = 0.735 \times 0.729 = 0.536 = C_B \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 5: Comment on hull form (0.5 marks):** - $C_B = 0.536$: typical for a **fine trawler form** — good for towing trawl gear against resistance. - $C_M = 0.729$: moderate fullness at midships, indicating good stability without excessive drag. - $C_P = 0.735$: well within the 0.55–0.72 expected range; hull has fine ends with adequate midbody length. - **Verdict:** Suitable for an ocean-going trawler with good seakeeping and towing efficiency. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **location of the engine room in a fishing vessel**, comparing the **aft**, **midships**, and **forward** arrangements. Justify which is preferred for modern fishing vessels. **(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **General Arrangement of an engine room** of a 24 m fishing trawler. List the safety features included. --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **Part (a) — 7 marks — Comparison of ER locations:** **Aft Location (3 marks):** - **Advantages:** - Short shaft line → less friction loss, higher transmission efficiency. - Propeller operates in **clean, undisturbed water** (no wake from the hull), improving propulsive efficiency by 5–10%. - Engine room noise and vibration kept away from the **accommodation and bridge**, improving crew comfort on long fishing voyages. - **Maximizes forward volume** for fish holds, processing spaces, and working deck. - Centrally located in the vessel, giving good longitudinal weight distribution. - **Disadvantages:** - Vulnerability to **stern collision damage** (especially in fishing grounds with heavy traffic). - Shaft angle and bearings require careful alignment. - Heat dissipation near the rudder may affect steering mechanisms. - **Adoption:** Used in **> 85% of modern fishing vessels** ≥ 18 m length. **Midships Location (2 marks):** - **Advantages:** Symmetric weight distribution, central machinery. - **Disadvantages:** Splits useful volume; longer shafting; vibration passes through both bow and stern. - **Adoption:** Older designs, factory trawlers with mid-body processing. **Forward Location (1 mark):** - **Advantages:** Used in some **side trawlers** with forward gantry. - **Disadvantages:** Hazardous (bow flooding); noise and heat in bow; long shaft; regulatory resistance. - **Adoption:** Rare, declining. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The **aft engine room** is the preferred modern arrangement due to propeller efficiency, crew comfort, and cargo volume — supported by classification society rules and Indian Fishing Vessel Regulations. **Part (b) — 7 marks — Engine Room GA Description:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. The student should draw (or describe) a top-view schematic of the ER showing: - Main engine **centrally fore-aft**, mounted on flexible chocks. - Gearbox **aft of main engine** with shaft tunnel to stern tube. - Two generator sets (port & starboard) **forward** for redundancy. - Pumps, purifiers, and compressor distributed on port and starboard sides. - **Control station** at the forward end of the ER, with good sightline to the engine and accessible to the bridge via a watertight door. - **Cofferdams** between fuel tanks and the ER structure. - **Sewage/bilge tanks** at the lowest level. **Safety Features (4 marks — list with brief explanation):** 1. **Cofferdams** (0.5): Air or inert space between fuel oil tanks and machinery spaces — prevents oil leakage onto hot surfaces. 2. **CO₂ / Foam fixed fire-fighting system** (0.5): Bottles in a separate compartment outside the ER; nozzles flood the ER on activation. 3. **Two independent escape routes** (0.5): Main access door (forward) and escape trunk (aft, leading to weather deck). 4. **Watertight door at main access** (0.5): Closes automatically on flooding alarm; rated for submergence pressure. 5. **Oil-tight drip trays** (0.5): Under all engines, purifiers, pumps — prevent oil spill into bilge. 6. **Bilge level alarms + oily-water separator** (0.5): Detect water accumulation; prevent pollution. 7. **Fire detection & alarm system** (0.5): Smoke and heat detectors in all zones. 8. **Emergency fire pump** (0.5): Located outside the ER (e.g., forward of collision bulkhead) so it survives ER fire. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Engine Room GA & Lines Design:** > > 1. **Identity Reversal (3 marks lost often):** Students write $C_B = C_M / C_P$ instead of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$. Remember: **$C_P$ is longitudinal, $C_M$ is transverse** — they multiply to give total volume efficiency. > > 2. **Sign Convention for LCB (1–2 marks):** Always state whether your LCB is measured from AP, FP, or midships. Vague answers lose marks. > > 3. **Cofferdam Omission (2 marks in GA):** Examiners specifically look for cofferdams around fuel tanks — a *very* common omission. Write "cofferdam, min 600 mm wide, between fuel tank bulkhead and ER shell" to score full marks. > > 4. **Shaft Tunnel vs Shaft Pipe Confusion (1 mark):** Small vessels use a **pipe tunnel** (pipe with glands); larger vessels use a **walkable shaft tunnel** ≥ 1.6 m clear height. State the type. > > 5. **Forgetting to multiply half-breadth by 2 in Simpson's Rule (2 marks):** You integrate *half* the hull by symmetry — remember the factor of 2 at the end. > > 6. **Not drawing the Escape Route (3 marks):** Engine room GA must show *two* escape routes. Forgetting one = marks lost in safety/regulation questions. > > 7. **Wrong units (1 mark):** Displacement volume is in **m³**; displacement *mass* is in **tonnes**. Don't mix them. Use $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ for seawater. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Design of Lines & Engine Room GA** **Lines Plan Essentials:** - ☐ Three views: **Sheer Plan, Half-Breadth Plan, Body Plan** (Body Plan is the master). - ☐ **21 stations** (stations 0 to 20), spacing = $L_{BP} / 20$. - ☐ Offsets = (half-breadth, height) at each (station, waterline). - ☐ Fairing done with splines; lines must be smooth and free of kinks. **Hull Form Coefficients:** - ☐ $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ — volume efficiency. - ☐ $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{BP})$ — longitudinal fullness. - ☐ $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ — transverse fullness. - ☐ **The Three Pillars: $C_B = C_P \times C_M$.** - ☐ Fishing vessel typical ranges: $C_B$ 0.45–0.65; $C_P$ 0.55–0.72; $C_M$ 0.75–0.92. **Integration Tools:** - ☐ Simpson's 1st Rule for even ordinate count. - ☐ Always integrate **half-breadth** (× 2 at end for full value). - ☐ Frame spacing rule: $s = L_{BP}/40$ to $L_{BP}/50$. **Engine Room GA Essentials:** - ☐ **Aft location** is the modern standard (> 85 % of fishing vessels). - ☐ ER volume rule of thumb: $V_{ER} = 0.025$ to $0.040 \times \nabla$. - ☐ **Minimum headroom** in ER: 1.9–2.1 m. - ☐ **Two independent escape routes** (mandatory). - ☐ **Cofferdams** between fuel tanks and heated surfaces (mandatory). - ☐ Main engine on **flexible chocks** (vibration isolation). - ☐ **Two generator sets** for redundancy. - ☐ **Fixed fire-fighting system** (CO₂ or foam) in a *separate* compartment. - ☐ **Watertight access door** with auto-closure. - ☐ **Shaft tunnel** (or pipe tunnel) leads to stern tube. - ☐ **Emergency fire pump** located *outside* the ER (survives ER fire). - ☐ **Bilge alarms + OWS** (Oily Water Separator) — pollution compliance. - ☐ **Control station** at forward end of ER, with sightline to engine and bridge. **High-Yield Quick Formulas to Memorize:** $$ C_B = C_P \times C_M $$ $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T $$ $$ V_{ER} \approx 0.030 \cdot \nabla $$ $$ L_{ME} \approx 1.8 \cdot \sqrt[3]{P_{ME \, (\text{kW})}} $$ $$ A_{WP} = 2 \cdot \frac{h}{3} \left[ y_0 + y_{20} + 4 \sum y_{odd} + 2 \sum y_{even} \right] $$ > **End of Notes — Module 2: Design of Lines, General Arrangement: Engine Room**

topics.title: Fish Holds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fish Holds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fish Hold** is a watertight, thermally insulated, refrigerated (or ice-cooled) compartment constructed within the hull of a fishing vessel, specifically engineered for the **bulk storage, preservation, and hygienic transport of the fish catch** between the fishing grounds and the discharge port. The fish hold forms the central cargo-handling system of any fishing trawler, long-liner, or purse seiner, and its design directly governs the **Quality Index (QI)** of the landed catch, the **energy efficiency** of the vessel, and ultimately the **economic profitability** of the fishing voyage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Fish holds are NOT merely storage spaces — they are **integrated preservation systems** combining hull structural design, thermal insulation, refrigeration plant, drainage, and air circulation subsystems. KTU examiners expect students to treat the hold as a *system*, not just a void.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a fish hold as the **"refrigerator on a ship"** — but with three major upgrades over your household fridge:

1. **Size:** It can be enormous, often consuming 25–40% of the vessel's internal volume below deck.
2. **Motion Tolerance:** It must keep fish intact while the ship rolls, pitches, and heaves in 6-meter ocean swells.
3. **Hygiene & Drainage:** It must constantly drain away meltwater, blood, slime, and slime-water (the liquid oozing out of fish as they age), without allowing this contaminated fluid to touch the marketable catch.

A useful everyday analogy is the **walk-in cold room at a fish market** combined with the **sloshing dynamics of a swimming pool** — every design decision must satisfy *both* thermal and hydrodynamic requirements simultaneously.

### 1.3 Classification of Fish Holds (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the student's ability to **distinguish between hold types** and select the appropriate one for a given vessel.

| Hold Type | Preservation Medium | Typical Vessel | Temperature Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ice Hold (Conventional)** | Crushed ice in contact with fish | Small trawlers, traditional boats | **0 °C to +2 °C** |
| **Chilled Sea Water (CSW)** | Pre-cooled seawater with ice | Tuna purse seiners, seiners | **−1 °C to +1 °C** |
| **Refrigerated Sea Water (RSW)** | Mechanically chilled seawater | Mid-water trawlers, purse seiners | **−2 °C to 0 °C** |
| **Blast Freeze Hold (Air)** | Forced cold air (NH₃ or freon coils) | Trawlers, factory ships | **−25 °C to −35 °C** |
| **Brine Spray/Flood** | Sodium chloride brine solution | Tuna long-liners | **−5 °C to −10 °C** |

### 1.4 Design Requirements (KTU Board-Weighted Content)

- **Hygienic surfaces:** Stainless steel (SS 316) or food-grade GRP lining — never plain steel.
- **Watertight integrity:** Must withstand slamming loads and survive a 30° heel angle without spillage of fish or brine.
- **Drainage gradient:** Floor must slope ≥ **1 in 60** toward drain wells (scuppers).
- **Access hatches:** Watertight, insulated, with quick-release dogs.
- **Insulation:** Continuous, vapour-barrier sealed, no thermal bridges.
- **Refrigeration uniformity:** ΔT across hold should not exceed **±1 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Fish Hold — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The fish hold must perform **four simultaneous functions**:

**Step 1 — Structural Containment**
The hold walls form part of the vessel's transverse and longitudinal strength members. They must resist:
- Hydrostatic pressure from surrounding seawater
- Sloshing dynamic loads of the liquid cargo (in RSW systems)
- Point loads from stowed fish crates (in conventional ice holds)

**Step 2 — Thermal Insulation**
Heat ingress from the outside must be minimized. The thermal circuit is:
- Sea (warm) → Hull plate → Insulation → Inner lining → Hold air → Fish

The aim is to keep the heat leakage rate below the refrigeration plant's capacity.

**Step 3 — Refrigeration / Cooling**
A refrigeration cycle (vapour compression, typically using R-404A, R-717 ammonia, or R-22 historically) extracts heat at the rate:

$$ Q_{load} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{product} + Q_{respiration} + Q_{misc} $$

Where:
- $Q_{transmission}$ = heat through walls, floor, and deckhead
- $Q_{product}$ = sensible heat to cool fish from catch temperature to storage temperature
- $Q_{respiration}$ = heat from any live cargo (negligible for dead fish, important for live crustaceans)
- $Q_{misc}$ = heat from lights, hatches opened, personnel, and fan motors

**Step 4 — Hygiene & Drainage**
Melting ice, blood, and slime-water must be continuously removed. Drain wells lead to **scuppers** with **non-return valves** (to prevent seawater ingress when the vessel rolls).

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (Engineering Rationale)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thermal insulation is non-negotiable:** Every 1 °C rise in fish storage temperature halves the remaining shelf-life. Tuna stored at 0 °C lasts ~14 days; at 4 °C it lasts only ~6 days. The economic loss from poor insulation far exceeds the cost of the insulation itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why drainage slope matters:** Standing slime-water harbours bacteria like *Pseudomonas* and *Shewanella*, accelerating spoilage. The **1 in 60** slope (≈ 0.95°) is the KTU-approved minimum standard from FAO Fishing Vessel Design manuals.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains every high-yield formula you need for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units as rigorously as the equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hold volume required | $V_h = W_c \times S_f$ | m³ | $W_c$ = catch weight (tonnes), $S_f$ = stowage factor |
| 2 | Stowage factor (ice-stowed fish) | $S_f \approx 1.8$ to $2.2$ | m³/tonne | KTU examiners use **2.0 m³/t** as default |
| 3 | Stowage factor (RSW fish) | $S_f \approx 1.3$ to $1.5$ | m³/tonne | Liquid medium allows tighter packing |
| 4 | Thermal resistance of insulation | $R = d / k$ | m²·K/W | $d$ = thickness (m), $k$ = thermal conductivity |
| 5 | Heat transmission through hold | $Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | $U = 1 / R_{total}$ (overall heat transfer coeff.) |
| 6 | Sensible heat to cool fish | $Q_{prod} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | $c_p$ (fish) ≈ **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| 7 | Latent heat of fish freezing | $Q_{lat} = m \cdot L_f$ | W | $L_f$ (fish) ≈ **230 kJ/kg** water content |
| 8 | Refrigeration plant capacity | $Q_{plant} = \eta \cdot COP \cdot W_{comp}$ | W | $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency, $COP$ = coefficient of performance |
| 9 | Insulation thickness (target U) | $d = k \cdot \left( \frac{1}{U} - \frac{1}{h_i} - \frac{1}{h_o} \right)$ | m | Outer surface coeff. $h_o$ includes wind/sea |
| 10 | Time to pre-cool hold | $t = \frac{m_{hold} \cdot c_{p,steel} \cdot \Delta T}{Q_{plant}}$ | s | Pre-cooling consumes ~6 h of plant operation |
| 11 | Drainage flow rate | $Q_{drain} = A_{scupper} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m³/s | $h$ = head of liquid above scupper |
| 12 | Sloshing natural period | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{L / g}$ | s | Used to size baffles in RSW tanks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical pipe symbol `\|` has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` in all formula displays above to preserve markdown table integrity. When writing on the answer sheet, use plain `\|` or `abs(...)`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fish hold design sits at the intersection of **naval architecture**, **food technology**, and **refrigeration engineering**. Mastery of this topic enables a graduate to work as:
- A naval architect in fishing-vessel design yards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard's fishing vessel division)
- A refrigeration engineer for marine HVAC/R systems
- A quality control engineer at seafood processing plants
- A classification society surveyor (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register — all inspect fish holds for hygiene and thermal performance)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hold Volume Required for a Given Catch

We begin with the **fundamental design equation** linking catch weight to required hold volume.

**Step 1 — State the design problem**
Given:
- Catch weight per voyage: $W_c = 50$ tonnes
- Preservation method: Conventional crushed-ice stowage
- Assumed stowage factor: $S_f = 2.0$ m³/tonne

**Step 2 — Apply the volumetric relationship**
The hold volume $V_h$ must physically accommodate the catch plus the ice, with allowances for packaging and voids.

$$ V_h = W_c \times S_f $$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

$$ V_h = 50 \, \text{t} \times 2.0 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{t}} = 100 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Add packaging/void allowance**
Typically a 15% allowance is added for dunnage, crates, and freeboard above the fish:

$$ V_{h,\text{gross}} = 1.15 \times V_h = 1.15 \times 100 = 115 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Result**

$$ \boxed{V_{h,\text{gross}} \approx 115 \, \text{m}^3} $$

This is the **minimum** design hold volume; designers usually round up to **120 m³** for a 50-tonne ice-stowed catch.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Insulation Thickness

This is a **canonical KTU 14-mark derivation question**. We derive the insulation thickness needed to limit transmission heat load.

**Step 1 — Define the thermal circuit**
Heat flows from sea (≈ +28 °C in tropical waters) through hull, insulation, and inner lining into the hold (≈ 0 °C).

The thermal resistances in series are:
- $R_o = 1/h_o$ (outside convective resistance, seawater side)
- $R_{steel} = d_s / k_s$ (hull plate, ≈ 10 mm)
- $R_{ins} = d / k$ (the insulation thickness we seek)
- $R_{liner} = d_l / k_l$ (inner SS316 or GRP lining)
- $R_i = 1/h_i$ (inside air-side convective resistance)

**Step 2 — Define the overall heat transfer coefficient**
The overall U-value combines all resistances:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{d_s}{k_s} + \frac{d}{k} + \frac{d_l}{k_l} + \frac{1}{h_i} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute known standard values**

Assume:
- $h_o = 200 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ (seawater forced convection)
- $h_i = 10 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ (still air inside hold)
- $d_s = 0.010 \, \text{m}, \; k_s = 50 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$ (steel hull)
- $d_l = 0.002 \, \text{m}, \; k_l = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$ (SS316 lining)
- $k = 0.024 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$ (polyurethane foam insulation — best marine choice)
- Target $U = 0.30 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ (industry benchmark for fish holds)

**Step 4 — Solve for the fixed (non-insulation) resistance sum**

$$ \frac{1}{U_{\text{fixed}}} = \frac{1}{200} + \frac{0.010}{50} + \frac{0.002}{16} + \frac{1}{10} $$

Evaluating each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_{\text{fixed}}} &= 0.00500 + 0.00020 + 0.000125 + 0.10000 \\
&= 0.105325 \, \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the required total resistance**

$$ \frac{1}{U_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.3333 \, \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

**Step 6 — Isolate the insulation resistance**

$$ \frac{d}{k} = \frac{1}{U_{\text{total}}} - \frac{1}{U_{\text{fixed}}} = 3.3333 - 0.105325 = 3.228 \, \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

**Step 7 — Solve for insulation thickness**

$$ d = k \times 3.228 = 0.024 \times 3.228 = 0.0775 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 8 — Result and engineering rounding**

$$ \boxed{d \approx 80 \, \text{mm of polyurethane foam insulation}} $$

This is the **standard 3-inch insulation** widely used in modern fishing trawler design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Daily Refrigeration Load

**Step 1 — Catch mass and cooling**
We must cool 50 tonnes of fish from catch temperature ($T_c = +20 \, \text{°C}$) to storage temperature ($T_s = 0 \, \text{°C}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{prod}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 20 \\
&= 3{,}500{,}000 \, \text{kJ} \\
&= 3.5 \, \text{GJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily transmission heat load**
For a 120 m³ hold with surface area $A \approx 240$ m² and $U = 0.30 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$, with $\Delta T = 28 \, \text{K}$:

$$ Q_{\text{trans}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.30 \times 240 \times 28 = 2016 \, \text{W} $$

Convert to daily energy (×86400 s):

$$ Q_{\text{trans,daily}} = 2016 \times 86400 = 174.2 \, \text{MJ/day} $$

**Step 3 — Total daily load**

$$ Q_{\text{daily}} = 3500 + 174.2 \approx 3674 \, \text{MJ/day} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to refrigeration tonnes**

$$ Q_{\text{ref}} = \frac{3674 \times 10^6}{86400 \times 3.517} \approx 120.9 \, \text{kW} \approx 34.4 \, \text{TR} $$

where $1 \, \text{TR} = 3.517 \, \text{kW}$ (heat to melt 1 tonne of ice in 24 h).

**Step 5 — Result**

$$ \boxed{Q_{\text{plant}} \approx 35 \, \text{TR (tonnes of refrigeration)}} $$

A 50-tonne-capacity fish hold typically requires a **30–40 TR** refrigeration plant — this is the design benchmark used in KTU problems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fish Hold Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed high-yield section** for KTU 14-mark questions asking you to "compare and contrast" fish hold types.

| Parameter | Ice Hold | CSW | RSW | Blast Freeze |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Pre-cooling time | 12–18 h | 8 h | 4–6 h | 6–8 h |
| Fish quality retention | Good | Very Good | Excellent | Premium (sashimi grade) |
| Energy consumption (kWh/tonne fish) | Low (ice melts) | Medium | High | Very High |
| Vessel stability impact | High (free surface liquid) | High | Very High (baffles needed) | Low (solid stowage) |
| Suitable voyage duration | 1–3 days | 3–7 days | 5–14 days | 10–30 days |
| Drainage complexity | Moderate (melting ice) | High (seawater) | Very High | Low (frozen blocks) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fish Hold Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **integrated functional architecture** of a modern RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) fish hold system, showing how structural, thermal, refrigeration, and hygiene subsystems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STRUCT["Structural Envelope"]
        nodeH[Hull Plate 10mm Steel]
        nodeF[Hold Floor Sloped 1 in 60]
        nodeC[Deckhead Watertight]
        nodeH --> nodeF
        nodeF --> nodeC
    end

    subgraph THERMAL["Thermal Insulation Layer"]
        nodeI[Polyurethane Foam 80mm k equals 0.024]
        nodeV[Vapour Barrier Aluminium Foil]
        nodeL[SS316 Lining 2mm Food Grade]
        nodeI --> nodeV
        nodeV --> nodeL
    end

    subgraph REFRIG["Refrigeration Subsystem"]
        nodeCOMP[Compressor R404A or NH3]
        nodeCOND[Sea Water Condenser]
        nodeEVAP[Plate Evaporator Coils]
        nodeFAN[Internal Circulation Fan]
        nodeCOMP --> nodeCOND
        nodeCOND --> nodeEVAP
        nodeEVAP --> nodeFAN
    end

    subgraph CARGO["Cargo and Liquid"]
        nodeFISH[Stowed Fish Boxes or RSW Brine]
        nodeBRINE[Chilled Sea Water minus 1 C]
        nodeFISH --> nodeBRINE
    end

    subgraph DRAIN["Drainage and Hygiene"]
        nodeSCUPP[Drain Scupper 50mm]
        nodeNRV[Non Return Valve]
        nodePUMP[Bilge Pump]
        nodeSLIME[Slime Collection Tank]
        nodeSCUPP --> nodeNRV
        nodeNRV --> nodePUMP
        nodePUMP --> nodeSLIME
    end

    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeSCUPP
    nodeEVAP --> nodeFISH
    nodeFAN --> nodeFISH
    nodeBRINE --> nodeSCUPP
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Refrigeration Cycle Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Catch at 20 C] --> B[Pre Cool to 10 C in 6 h]
    B --> C[Cool to 0 C in 12 h]
    C --> D[Hold at minus 1 to plus 1 C]
    D --> E{QI Test at Discharge}
    E -->|QI greater than 80| F[Premium Grade]
    E -->|QI 60 to 80| G[Standard Grade]
    E -->|QI less than 60| H[Reject or Downgrade]
```

### 4.3 Key Visual Notes (Textual Diagram)

**Cross-section of a typical RSW fish hold (side view):**

```
    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │            DECKHEAD (Insulated)              │
    ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← 80mm PU foam
    │ ░░░░░░░░░░ VAPOUR BARRIER ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
    ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐   │
    │  │  EVAPORATOR COILS (NH3)              │   │
    │  └───────────────────────────────────────┘   │
    │                                              │
    │   ╔════════════════════════════════════╗     │  ← Hold interior
    │   ║  FISH + CHILLED SEA WATER (RSW)   ║     │     (RSW type)
    │   ╚════════════════════════════════════╝     │
    │           ↓ slope 1:60                      │
    │   ┌──────────────────────┐                  │
    │   │  DRAIN SCUPPER + NRV │ ──→ to bilge    │
    │   └──────────────────────┘                  │
    ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← 80mm PU foam
    │            HULL PLATE                       │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ↕  SEA WATER (≈ 28 °C)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a fish hold and list any THREE essential design requirements.**

**Model Answer:**
A fish hold is a thermally insulated, refrigerated compartment within the hull of a fishing vessel used for storing and preserving the fish catch during the voyage.

Three essential design requirements:
1. **Thermal insulation** — to maintain 0 °C storage temperature with minimum heat ingress.
2. **Watertight integrity** — must prevent ingress of seawater even at 30° heel angle.
3. **Proper drainage** — floor must slope at least **1 in 60** toward scuppers with non-return valves.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between CSW and RSW fish hold systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CSW | RSW |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling source | Ice added to seawater | Mechanical refrigeration |
| Temperature | 0 °C to +1 °C | −1 °C to −2 °C |
| Plant cost | Lower | Higher |
| Trip duration | Short (3–7 days) | Long (5–14 days) |
| Control precision | Poor | Excellent |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features** of a modern insulated and refrigerated fish hold. List the materials used for insulation and inner lining. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Constructional features:**
- **Hull-integrated compartment** formed between the inner shell and the deckhead.
- **Floor** slopes toward drain well at 1 in 60.
- **Side walls** stiffened with transverse webs to resist sloshing loads.
- **Drainage system** with 50 mm scuppers and non-return valves.
- **Refrigeration coils** mounted on side walls and overhead.
- **Hatches** on deck, watertight, insulated, with quick-release dogs.

**Insulation materials** (in order of marine preference):
1. **Polyurethane (PU) foam** — k = 0.024 W/(m·K), best thermal performance.
2. **Polystyrene** — k = 0.035 W/(m·K), cheaper but absorbs water.
3. **Cork board** — k = 0.040 W/(m·K), traditional but biodegradable.

**Inner lining materials:**
- **Stainless Steel SS316** — corrosion-resistant, hygienic, easy to clean.
- **Food-grade GRP** — lightweight alternative, smooth surface.

---

**(b)** Derive the **insulation thickness required** for the side shell of a fish hold, given:
- Sea water temperature $T_o = +28$ °C
- Hold air temperature $T_i = 0$ °C
- Outer surface coefficient $h_o = 200 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$
- Inner surface coefficient $h_i = 10 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$
- Hull plate thickness = 10 mm, $k_{steel} = 50$ W/(m·K)
- Lining thickness = 2 mm SS316, $k_{SS} = 16$ W/(m·K)
- Insulation: PU foam, $k = 0.024$ W/(m·K)
- Target overall U-value = 0.30 W/(m²·K)

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:** *(Step-by-step, as derived in Section 3.2)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{d_s}{k_s} + \frac{d}{k} + \frac{d_l}{k_l} + \frac{1}{h_i} \\
\frac{1}{0.30} &= \frac{1}{200} + \frac{0.010}{50} + \frac{d}{0.024} + \frac{0.002}{16} + \frac{1}{10} \\
3.3333 &= 0.00500 + 0.00020 + \frac{d}{0.024} + 0.000125 + 0.10000 \\
3.3333 &= 0.105325 + \frac{d}{0.024} \\
\frac{d}{0.024} &= 3.3333 - 0.105325 = 3.228 \\
d &= 0.024 \times 3.228 = 0.0775 \, \text{m} \\
\boxed{d} &\approx \mathbf{80 \, mm \, of \, PU \, foam}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** A fishing trawler expects a catch of **40 tonnes** to be stored in a conventional ice-stowed fish hold. The stowage factor for ice-stowed fish is **2.0 m³/tonne**. Allowing 15% for voids and packaging, calculate the **gross hold volume required**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= W_c \times S_f \\
V_h &= 40 \times 2.0 = 80 \, \text{m}^3 \\
V_{gross} &= 1.15 \times 80 = 92 \, \text{m}^3 \\
\boxed{V_{gross}} &\approx \mathbf{92 \, m^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

If, instead, RSW system is used with $S_f = 1.4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{t}$:

$$ V_{RSW} = 1.15 \times 40 \times 1.4 = 64.4 \, \text{m}^3 $$

So switching from ice to RSW **saves ~30%** hold volume — a major design advantage.

---

**(b)** List and explain **FIVE different types of fish holds** based on the preservation medium. State one advantage and one limitation for each. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Medium | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ice Hold** | Crushed ice (0 °C) | Low cost, simple | Short trip duration, meltwater |
| **CSW** | Ice + seawater (0 °C) | Faster cooling, fish floats | Seawater corrodes holds |
| **RSW** | Mechanically chilled SW (−1 °C) | Long trip, fast cooling | High power, baffles needed |
| **Blast Freeze** | Cold air (−30 °C) | Premium quality, sashimi-grade | Highest energy cost |
| **Brine Tank** | NaCl brine (−5 °C) | Very fast cooling | Salt contamination of fish |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Fish Hold questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 15% void allowance** in volume calculations — costs 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Wrong units** — using $W_c$ in kg instead of tonnes while $S_f$ is in m³/t. The unit mismatch is the #1 silent killer of marks.
> 3. **Confusing CSW with RSW** — examiners deliberately set traps here. Remember: CSW = *Cooled* Sea Water (ice is the cooler), RSW = *Refrigerated* Sea Water (mechanical plant is the cooler).
> 4. **Omitting the 1-in-60 floor slope** in sketches — it is a compulsory drawing annotation per KTU valuation key.
> 5. **Forgetting non-return valves on scuppers** — this is the single most-tested drainage detail.
> 6. **Wrong $c_p$ for fish** — KTU expects **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**. Using water's value of 4.18 will be marked wrong.
> 7. **Missing thermal resistance terms** — students often skip the $1/h_o$ and $1/h_i$ surface resistances, losing 1.5 marks per omission.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fish Holds (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ A fish hold is a **thermal + structural + refrigeration + hygiene system**, not just a void.
>
> ✅ The **5 hold types** to memorize: Ice, CSW, RSW, Blast Freeze, Brine.
>
> ✅ **Stowage factor**: ice = **2.0 m³/t**, RSW = **1.4 m³/t**.
>
> ✅ Always add **15%** void/packaging allowance to hold volume.
>
> ✅ Floor slope is **1 in 60** toward drain scuppers with **non-return valves**.
>
> ✅ **PU foam insulation** is industry-standard, k = **0.024 W/(m·K)**, typical thickness **80 mm**.
>
> ✅ Overall U-value target: **0.30 W/(m²·K)** or less.
>
> ✅ $c_p$ of fish = **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**.
>
> ✅ 1 TR (tonne of refrigeration) = **3.517 kW** = heat to melt 1 tonne ice in 24 h.
>
> ✅ Inner lining material of choice: **SS316** stainless steel or food-grade **GRP**.
>
> ✅ For RSW: **baffles** are required to control sloshing.
>
> ✅ Heat leakage equation: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$.
>
> ✅ Pre-cooling time ≈ **6 hours** before loading.
>
> ✅ Hygiene rule: **drainage + insulation + smoothness** = quality fish.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Erection** in fishing vessel (and ship) construction refers to the systematic assembly and joining of pre-fabricated structural components — such as plates, stiffeners, frames, bulkheads, and sub-assemblies — into the complete three-dimensional hull form of the vessel, performed at the building berth or in a dry dock according to a predetermined sequence defined in the **Erection Plan (E-Plan)** and **Erection Sequence Diagram (ESD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *Erection is the phase of shipbuilding in which the disassembled hull units (blocks/sub-assemblies), produced in the fabrication shop, are transported, positioned, aligned, and welded together on the building berth to form the integrated hull structure of the vessel.*

**Sub-definitions to memorize:**
- **Block**: A three-dimensional pre-fabricated hull section of manageable size and weight.
- **Erection Unit**: The largest assembly lifted and positioned in a single crane cycle.
- **E-Plan**: The 2D/3D engineering document defining the order, position, and joining logic of all hull units.
- **Fairing**: The geometric alignment of the hull form to its designed curvature without steps, kinks, or unfair surfaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Lego Shipyard" Analogy:**
> Imagine building a giant 3D puzzle (Lego set) of a fishing boat. Instead of attaching each tiny Lego brick one-by-one at the final assembly spot, the shipyard first builds large pre-made sections ("Lego chunks") in separate workshops. **Erection** is the stage where these huge pre-made chunks are carried by giant cranes, lowered onto the shipbuilding slipway in a carefully planned order (bottom first, then sides, then decks, then wheelhouse), and welded together to assemble the complete boat — like snapping together the largest Lego pieces to create the full model.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Tolerance for hull plate alignment**: typically **±3 mm** over 1 m length for primary hull structures.
- **Block weight limits (modern yards)**: Up to **600–1500 tonnes** for mega-blocks.
- **Crane capacity for erection**: from **50 tonnes** (small vessels) to **2000+ tonnes** (gantry cranes).
- **Welding heat input limit**: Generally kept below **3.5 kJ/mm** for high-tensile fishing vessel steel to avoid HAZ softening.
- **Block length on berth**: Typically **15–24 m** per block in conventional erection.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of the Erection Process

The erection workflow follows a strict bottom-up, structural hierarchy. Each step has a specific purpose and directly contributes to the vessel's structural integrity and dimensional accuracy.

### Step 1: Keel Laying & Base Block Placement
- The keel plate or base block (bottom shell) is positioned on the **building berth** or **slipway**.
- **Why first?** It defines the baseline (datum) for all subsequent hull geometry.
- **How?** Using wooden or steel keel blocks; aligned using laser/optical plummets and theodolites.

### Step 2: Bottom Shell Erection
- Bottom plating panels (garboards, bottom shell plates, centerline strake) are erected adjacent to the keel.
- Joined using **butt welds** (plate-to-plate) and **seam welds** (edge-to-edge).
- **Why?** Establishes the vessel's lower watertight envelope.

### Step 3: Side Shell Erection
- Side shell plates and frames are erected onto the bottom.
- Two erection philosophies exist here (explained below).
- **Why?** Forms the vertical hull envelope.

### Step 4: Deck & Bulkhead Erection
- Transverse watertight bulkheads and deck plates are lifted into position.
- **Why?** Provides transverse strength and creates watertight compartments (critical for fishing vessels — flooding safety).

### Step 5: Superstructure Erection
- Wheelhouse, mast, funnel, and deckhouse are erected last.
- **Why?** Reduces center-of-gravity issues during construction and minimizes overhead crane work early on.

### Step 6: Alignment, Welding & Fairing
- Final dimensional checks (line, level, squareness).
- All block joints are welded (typically with **submerged arc welding — SAW**).
- **Fairing** ensures the hull surface matches the design lines.

## 2.2 Methods of Erection (High-Yield — Frequently Asked)

| Method | Direction of Assembly | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal (Isometric / Longitudinal Section)** | Forward ↔ Aft | Hull is divided into 2–4 large longitudinal sections, built side-by-side and joined along the ship's length. | Medium/large vessels, modern shipyards. |
| **Transverse (Cross-Section)** | Bottom → Up | Hull is built ring-by-ring using transverse sections. | Smaller vessels, repair yards. |
| **Isometric (Three-Dimensional Block)** | Anywhere → Final Position | Independent 3D blocks built anywhere in yard, then assembled. | Modern modular shipbuilding. |
| **Combined / Hybrid** | Mixed | Uses both longitudinal and transverse block methods. | Most contemporary fishing vessel yards. |
| **Unit / Zonal Erection** | Pre-outfitted zones | Entire pre-outfitted units (engine room, accommodation block) lifted as a single piece. | Highly productive yards. |

## 2.3 Modern Trends in Fishing Vessel Erection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Practice (Board Tip):**
> 1. **Pre-Erection Outfitting (PEO)** — Pipes, cables, and equipment are fitted into blocks BEFORE erection, reducing work at height.
> 2. **Pre-Erection Painting (PEP)** — Blocks are painted in the shop rather than after erection, reducing cost & improving quality.
> 3. **GPS-based Block Tracking** — RFID and GPS tags follow each block through the erection sequence.
> 4. **Moving Platforms / Goliath Cranes** — Allow simultaneous erection along the berth.

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Quantity Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity / Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Formula or Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Lift Weight | $W_b$ (tonnes) | $W_b = \sum \rho_i \cdot V_i$ | Crane sizing check |
| Block Center of Gravity offset | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ (m) | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Sling/cable balancing |
| Hull plate alignment tolerance | $\delta_p$ | $\delta_p \le 3 \text{ mm per m}$ | Quality acceptance |
| Welding heat input | $E$ (kJ/mm) | $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | Weld procedure qualification |
| Sling tension (4-point lift) | $T_s$ (kN) | $T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4 \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Lift planning |
| Block stacking limit on berth | $n_{max}$ | Determined by berth ground-bearing pressure | Yard logistics |
| Steel density (general) | $\rho_{steel}$ | **7850 kg/m³** | Weight estimation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX / Markdown Table Safety:**
> In the formulas above, $\vert$ and absolute values have been replaced by `\\vert` or plain phrasing to **prevent breaking the markdown table pipe-delimiter syntax**. This is a KTU study-note standard.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Erection planning** reduces construction time by **30–50%** in modern yards.
- In **fishing vessel design**, erection methodology directly affects the **hatchery layout**, **gear stowage**, and **stability reserve** since larger blocks allow better outfitting density.
- Poor erection planning leads to **residual stresses**, **hull deflection**, and **squat deformation** of the hull girder.
- Forms the basis of **Lean Shipbuilding** and **Integrated Production Planning (IPP)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension for a 4-Point Block Lift

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation?** This is the most commonly asked analytical problem in Module 2 of OENST833 — examiners love to test whether a student can balance a block being lifted by a crane.

### Problem Setup
A fishing vessel hull block of weight $W_b$ is lifted by a 4-legged sling. Each sling leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical. Determine the tension in each sling leg.

### Given
- Block weight: $W_b$ (in tonnes or kg)
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Number of sling legs: $n = 4$
- Angle of sling with vertical: $\theta$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium equation.**
For a block in static equilibrium while suspended, the sum of vertical forces must equal zero.

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express the vertical force from each sling.**
Each sling exerts a tension $T_s$ along its length. The vertical component of each sling's pull is $T_s \cdot \cos\theta$ (since $\theta$ is measured from the vertical).

$$
n \cdot T_s \cdot \cos\theta - W_b \cdot g = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_s$.**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $n = 4$ for a 4-legged sling.**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4 \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- As $\theta \to 0$ (vertical slings), $\cos\theta \to 1$, and tension is minimum: $T_s \to \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4}$.
- As $\theta \to 60°$, $\cos\theta = 0.5$, tension doubles.
- **Therefore, longer sling length reduces tension, but increases the sling's footprint (headroom requirement).**

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)
A block weighing **80 tonnes** is lifted by a **4-legged sling** with each sling at **30°** to the vertical. Find the tension per leg.

**Given:**
- $W_b = 80 \text{ t} = 80{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $n = 4$
- $\theta = 30°$, so $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$

**Calculation:**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{80{,}000 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.866}
$$

$$
T_s = \dfrac{784{,}800}{3.464}
$$

$$
T_s \approx 226{,}558 \text{ N} \approx 226.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer:** Each sling leg carries approximately **226.6 kN**, well within the safe working load of a 30-tonne-class sling (rated ~300 kN).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Welding Heat Input (Frequently Tested)

### Given
- Arc voltage $V$ (V)
- Welding current $I$ (A)
- Travel speed $v$ (mm/min)

### Derivation

Heat input per unit length is the electrical power delivered divided by the travel speed.

$$
E = \dfrac{P}{v}
$$

Where electrical power $P = V \cdot I$.

$$
E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v} \;\; \text{(J/mm)}
$$

Converting to kJ/mm (the standard shipyard unit) by multiplying by $\dfrac{60}{1000}$ (since $v$ is in mm/min):

$$
E = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

### Example
A SAW weld uses $V = 28$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 400$ mm/min.

$$
E = \dfrac{28 \times 600 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{1{,}008{,}000}{400{,}000}
$$

$$
E = 2.52 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

This lies safely below the typical limit of **3.5 kJ/mm** for AH36 fishing vessel steel.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Erection Method vs. Vessel Size

| Vessel Size (LOA) | Recommended Erection Method | Block Weight Range | Yard Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 15 m | Transverse ring | 5–20 t | Small boatyard |
| 15–35 m | Combined / Isometric | 20–80 t | Mid-size yard |
| 35–60 m | Isometric block | 80–300 t | Modern yard |
| 60–100 m | Unit / Zonal erection | 300–1500 t | Mega-yard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Erection Sequence Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Keel Laying on Berth] --> B[Position Base Block / Keel Plate]
    B --> C[Erect Bottom Shell Plates]
    C --> D[Erect Side Shell Plates Bottom Up]
    D --> E[Install Transverse Bulkheads]
    E --> F[Erect Deck Plates and Tonnage Deck]
    F --> G[Erect Superstructure Wheelhouse]
    G --> H[Final Alignment and Fairing Check]
    H --> I[Block Joint Welding SAW]
    I --> J[End: Hull Ready for Launch]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangular node represents a sequential erection stage. The arrows denote the unidirectional flow of work — you cannot proceed to the next stage until dimensional checks pass.

---

## 4.2 Hull Block Division Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BerthArea[Shipbuilding Berth Top View]
        B1[Block 1 Bow Section] --> B2[Block 2 Forward Cargo Hold]
        B2 --> B3[Block 3 Engine Room Block]
        B3 --> B4[Block 4 Aft Fish Hold]
        B4 --> B5[Block 5 Stern Section]
    end
    C[Shop Fabrication Yard] -->|Crane Lift 1| B1
    C -->|Crane Lift 2| B2
    C -->|Crane Lift 3| B3
    C -->|Crane Lift 4| B4
    C -->|Crane Lift 5| B5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each block is fabricated in the shop, lifted by a gantry crane, and placed in sequence on the berth. This is the **Isometric Block Erection** approach — high productivity, but requires large crane capacity.

---

## 4.3 Erection Method Comparison (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Erection Method Selection] --> Q1{Vessel LOA Size?}
    Q1 -->|Under 15 m| M1[Transverse Ring Method]
    Q1 -->|15 to 35 m| M2[Combined Method]
    Q1 -->|Over 35 m| M3[Isometric Block Method]
    M1 --> X1[Berth based sequential]
    M2 --> X2[Berth plus shop hybrid]
    M3 --> X3[Gantry crane assembly]
    X1 --> End[Completed Hull]
    X2 --> End
    X3 --> End
```

---

## 4.4 Sling Geometry for Block Lift (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crane Hook Top] -->|Sling Leg 1 angle theta| B[Block Corner 1]
    A -->|Sling Leg 2 angle theta| C[Block Corner 2]
    A -->|Sling Leg 3 angle theta| D[Block Corner 3]
    A -->|Sling Leg 4 angle theta| E[Block Corner 4]
    B --> F[Block Body Weight Wb]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Lift Equilibrium Check]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Each sling's tension $T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4 \cdot \cos\theta}$ — longer slings (smaller $\theta$) reduce tension but raise the required crane hook height.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Erection" in the context of fishing vessel construction. List any two modern trends in ship erection.**

**Model Answer (Board Standard):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Erection is the shipbuilding phase in which pre-fabricated hull components (plates, stiffeners, blocks, units) are transported to the building berth, positioned in a planned sequence, aligned, and welded together to form the complete hull structure of a fishing vessel as per the Erection Plan.
>
> **Modern Trends (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. Pre-Erection Outfitting (PEO) — outfitting done in the shop before erection.
> 2. Pre-Erection Painting (PEP) — blocks painted before joining.
> 3. Use of Goliath/gantry cranes for simultaneous multi-block erection.
> 4. GPS/RFID-based block tracking.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is an "Erection Plan" (E-Plan)? Why is it essential before commencing hull erection?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (2 marks):** An Erection Plan (E-Plan) is the engineering document (typically a 2D layout plus 3D CAD model) that defines the size, weight, position, joining sequence, lifting points, and welding sequence of every hull block during the assembly of the vessel.
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** The E-Plan ensures correct dimensional accuracy, structural integrity, optimal crane utilization, controlled distortion/warpage, and adherence to the vessel's design lines — preventing costly rework after launch.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the different **methods of hull erection** in fishing vessel construction. Compare their relative merits. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A hull block weighing **120 tonnes** is to be lifted by a **4-legged sling** with each sling at an angle of **45°** to the vertical. Determine the tension in each sling leg. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major methods of hull erection used in fishing vessel construction are:

**1. Longitudinal Method (Sectional / Isometric-Longitudinal):**
The hull is divided into 2–4 large longitudinal sections (fore, mid, aft). Each section is built independently and joined along the ship's length.
- *Merit:* Faster cycle time, fewer large lifts.
- *Demerit:* Requires large assembly area and heavy transporters.

**2. Transverse Method (Ring Method):**
Hull is built ring-by-ring, with each "ring" being a transverse section lifted and welded successively.
- *Merit:* Suitable for smaller vessels, lower crane capacity needed.
- *Demerit:* Slow, many small lifts, high labor cost.

**3. Isometric (3D Block) Method:**
Hull is divided into independent 3D blocks built anywhere in the yard and assembled on the berth.
- *Merit:* Maximum parallel work, PEO/PEP possible.
- *Demerit:* Requires goliath/gantry cranes.

**4. Combined / Hybrid Method:**
Combines longitudinal and transverse techniques — used by most modern fishing vessel yards.
- *Merit:* Best of both worlds.
- *Demerit:* Complex planning.

**5. Unit / Zonal Method:**
Entire pre-outfitted units (engine room, accommodation block) are erected as one piece.
- *Merit:* Highest productivity.
- *Demerit:* Requires mega-cranes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $W_b = 120 \text{ t} = 120{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $n = 4$ sling legs
- $\theta = 45°$ → $\cos 45° = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Write equilibrium equation.**

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \implies n \cdot T_s \cdot \cos\theta = W_b \cdot g
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $T_s$.**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{120{,}000 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.7071}
$$

$$
T_s = \dfrac{1{,}177{,}200}{2.8284}
$$

$$
T_s \approx 416{,}212 \text{ N} \approx 416.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.**
Each sling leg carries approximately **416.2 kN**, requiring slings rated for at least **50 tonnes Safe Working Load (SWL)** per leg with a safety factor of 1.5–2.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe the **stages of hull erection** in a fishing vessel from keel laying to launch readiness. Include the role of welding and fairing. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **4-legged sling geometry** used during block lifting. Derive the expression for sling tension. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The stages of hull erection in a fishing vessel are:

**Stage 1 — Keel Laying & Base Block Setup:** The keel block and bottom centerline strake are positioned and leveled using laser/optical plummets.

**Stage 2 — Bottom Shell Erection:** Garboard plates and bottom shell panels are erected and tack-welded.

**Stage 3 — Side Shell Erection:** Side plating, frames, and web frames are erected. Stringent dimensional checks (line, level) are maintained.

**Stage 4 — Bulkhead and Deck Erection:** Transverse watertight bulkheads and main/tonnage deck plates are placed — critical for compartmental subdivision on fishing vessels.

**Stage 5 — Superstructure Erection:** Wheelhouse, mast, funnel, and fish hold insulation are added.

**Stage 6 — Final Alignment and Welding:** All block joints are fully welded using **SAW** for thick plates and **FCAW/GMAW** for thinner sections. Heat input must be controlled to **≤ 3.5 kJ/mm**.

**Stage 7 — Fairing:** Hull surface is checked for unfairness (steps, kinks). Grinding and fairing bring the surface to design lines.

**Stage 8 — Launch Readiness Check:** Inclining test, line-check, and weight audit before transfer to the launch cradle.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

(See Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.4 for visual representation.)

**Sling Geometry:**
A 4-legged sling connects the crane hook to the four corners of the rectangular block. Each sling makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical.

**Derivation (already detailed in Section 3.1):**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4 \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Sketch (Text-Based Representation):**

```
              [Crane Hook]
                 /|\ /|\
                / |X | \
               /  |/ \|  \    ← 4 sling legs at angle θ
              / θ |   | θ \
             /    |   |    \
            B-----+---+-----C
            |     BLOCK    |
            D-----+---+-----E
```

Where B, C, D, E are the four lifting points on the block. The vertical equilibrium gives the formula above.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in sling tension** — Many students write "416" instead of "416.2 kN" and lose the unit mark.
> 2. **Wrong angle reference** — Always clarify whether $\theta$ is from vertical or horizontal. Board exams accept either, but state it explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping equilibrium equation** — Even if your numerical answer is right, examiners want the equilibrium expression shown.
> 4. **Confusing "Erection" with "Launching"** — Erection is the assembly phase; launching is the water-entry phase. Don't mix the two.
> 5. **Ignoring welding heat input limits** — Always quote the threshold (**3.5 kJ/mm** for AH36) in any welding-related question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Erections**
> - **Definition**: Erection = assembly of pre-fabricated hull units at the berth.
> - **Five methods**: Longitudinal, Transverse, Isometric, Combined, Unit/Zonal.
> - **Modern trends**: PEO (Pre-Erection Outfitting), PEP (Pre-Erection Painting), Goliath cranes.
> - **E-Plan**: Defines sequence, weight, position, and joining of all blocks.
> - **Fairing**: Alignment of hull surface to design lines without unfair steps.
> - **Sling tension formula**: $T_s = \dfrac{W_b \cdot g}{4 \cdot \cos\theta}$.
> - **Welding heat input**: $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm (limit: 3.5 kJ/mm).
> - **Steel density (memorize)**: 7850 kg/m³.
> - **Block weight**: Small vessel = 5–80 t; large vessel = 300–1500 t.
> - **Hull tolerance**: ±3 mm per meter (general acceptance criterion).
> - **Bottom-up sequence**: Keel → Bottom → Side → Bulkhead → Deck → Superstructure.
> - **Pre-erection outfitting** is the hallmark of modern productive fishing-vessel yards.
> - **Fairing is a quality check**, not a structural operation.
> - **Unit method** = highest productivity, but requires mega-cranes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Machinery Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deck Machinery Arrangement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Deck Machinery Arrangement** refers to the systematic, ergonomic, and structurally integrated positioning of all mechanical equipment mounted on or above the weather deck(s) of a fishing vessel, intended to handle fishing gear (nets, lines, longlines, pots, trawls), mooring lines, anchors, cargo, and crew-safety operations throughout the vessel's seagoing service profile. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, deck machinery arrangement is the discipline that reconciles **three competing design drivers**: 1. **Functional Accessibility** – every machine must be operable safely under worst-case sea conditions (e.g., **Beaufort Scale 6–8**, **Significant Wave Height 2.5–4.0 m** for a typical coastal trawler). 2. **Hydrodynamic & Stability Integrity** – the mass, height of centroid, and induced heeling moments of every winch/crane must respect the vessel's intact and damaged **GM (metacentric height)** and free-surface limits. 3. **Class & Statutory Compliance** – the layout must satisfy **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) Fishing Vessel Rules 2015**, **ILO-188 Work in Fishing Convention**, and **FAO/IMR Code of Safety for Fishermen**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note – Module 2 (Deck Machinery Arrangement)** > The course outcome assessed is **CO2**: *“Illustrate the layout principles and operational characteristics of deck machinery on a fishing vessel.”* Expect derivations, layout sketches, and selection-justification questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Fishing Vessel as a Mobile Factory Floor"** > Imagine a small textile mill on wheels. The mill receives raw cotton (fishing gear), spins it into bales (catches fish), and ships out the product. The deck machinery is the **array of overhead cranes, conveyors, and pulleys** that make this mill mobile. > - The **windlass** is the gatekeeper at the front door that lets the anchors in/out. > - The **trawl winches** are the main cranes that lift the heavy nets. > - The **capstans** are the helper winches that tug small lines. > - The **bollards and fairleads** are the nails and eyelets that direct the ropes. > - The **hydraulic power pack** is the heart of the whole factory, pumping "muscle" (hydraulic fluid) to every moving part. > Just as a poorly arranged factory has workers tripping over each other, a poorly arranged deck leads to line-snarls, lost catch, and even fatalities. ## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Figures - **Wire Rope Drum Barrel Diameter** ≥ **16 × rope diameter (for 6×36 IWRC)** and ≥ **20 × rope diameter (for 6×24)** as per **ISO 4309-2017**. - **Fleet Angle** (angle between rope and drum axis at the first wrap) ≤ **4° (1 in 12)** for smooth winding, ≤ **2°** for long-lay or multi-layer operation. - **Standard Drum Length-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D)** between **1.0 and 3.0**. - **Hydraulic Working Pressure** on modern trawlers: **160–210 bar** (typical); high-pressure systems up to **350 bar**. - **Bollard Pull** of a trawler winch: typically **2.0–3.0 ×** the total drag of the trawl gear at towing speed. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Classification of Deck Machinery The deck machinery of a fishing vessel can be grouped into **five functional families**: ### A. Mooring & Anchoring Family (Forward) - **Windlass** – combined anchor lifting and chain/wire storage. - **Chain Stopper** – secures the anchor chain when the windlass is disengaged. - **Hawse Pipe** – guides the chain from the side shell to the chain locker. - **Bollards & Bitts** – mooring line termination points. ### B. Fishing-Operation Family (Main & Aft Deck) - **Trawl Winch** – dual-drum or single-drum, used for trawling, seiner/liner operations. - **Net Hauler / Line Hauler** – mechanical recovery of longlines, purse lines, gillnets. - **Net Drum** – stores the trawl net in wound form; sometimes replaces separate winches. - **Power Block / Fishing Master** – a large sheave suspended from a derrick/boom to redirect warps. ### C. Auxiliary Handling Family - **Capstan** (vertical drum) – provides continuous, low-tension pulling for small lines. - **Warping Winch** (horizontal drum) – assists docking and minor warping. - **Line Thrower / Heaving Line Reel** – for transferring light lines to shore or mother ship. ### D. Lifting Family - **Derrick / Mast & Boom** – fixed-angle or slewing lifting. - **Deck Crane** (electro-hydraulic) – typically **0.5 T to 5 T SWL** for catch handling. - **Gallows** – A-frame structure for lifting nets in purse seiners. ### E. Safety & Directional Family - **Fairleads** (closed/roller type) – guide ropes/wires at deck edges. - **Sheaves & Snatch Blocks** – redirect pulling force. - **Panama Chock / Bow Chock** – large fairlead at the bow. ## 2.2 Design Philosophy – "Centralized vs Distributed" A **centralized hydraulic drive** uses one prime mover (diesel) coupled to one or two hydraulic pumps that feed **HPU (Hydraulic Power Unit)** lines (HP and LP) routed to all winches via **selenoid valves and hydraulic motors**. Conversely, **distributed electric drive** uses individual **frequency-controlled (VFD) electric motors** at each winch. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trade-off Matrix** – Centralized Hydraulic (preferred for trawlers < 35 m) vs Distributed Electric (preferred for tuna purse seiners and long-liners > 35 m). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|--------|----------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Length of rope wound on drum | $L$ | $L = K \, n \, (D + n \, d) \, B$ | m | $K = 0.85$ (crosby/winding) for wire; $n$ = number of layers; $D$ = drum barrel diameter; $d$ = rope diameter; $B$ = drum width | | 2 | Drum capacity (alternative, "1st-layer-plus-overhang") | $L$ | $L = \pi \, B \, (D + d) \, N_{\text{turns}} \, n$ | m | Useful for first-layer estimation | | 3 | Line pull (theoretical) | $F$ | $F = \dfrac{P \, \eta}{v}$ | kN | $P$ = hydraulic power; $\eta$ = overall efficiency (0.6–0.8) | | 4 | Drum line speed | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{60}$ | m/s | $N$ = drum RPM | | 5 | Hydraulic motor torque | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{\Delta p \, D_m \, \eta_m}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta p$ = pressure drop; $D_m$ = motor displacement | | 6 | Brake holding load | $F_b$ | $F_b = 1.5 \, F_{\text{max}} \geq 0.5 \, \text{SWL}_{\text{rope}}$ | kN | Class rule minimum | | 7 | Heeling moment due to off-centre pull | $M_h$ | $M_h = F_{\text{net}} \cdot y$ | kN·m | $y$ = transverse lever arm from CL | | 8 | Minimum barrel diameter for wire rope | $D_{\text{min}}$ | $D_{\text{min}} \geq 16\,d$ (6×36) | mm | ISO 4309 | | 9 | Maximum fleet angle | $\alpha$ | $\alpha \leq 4^\circ$ (1 in 12) | deg | for smooth multi-layer spooling | | 10 | Specific pull (per cm of drum width) | $q$ | $q = 100 \text{–} 150 \text{ N/cm}$ | N/cm | Empirical limit to prevent rope crushing | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Trawl Winch Sizing**: Determines bollard pull which in turn determines vessel's towing capability and hence the size of trawl that can be deployed. - **Stability Verification**: Off-centre pull of a longline hauler at the bow is a major heeling moment; an uncompensated layout can capsize the vessel (this was the cause of the **BFAR-reported 2019 capsize off Sakthikulangara**). - **Ergonomics & ILO-188**: Proper height and reach of winch controls reduce **whole-body vibration (WBV)** exposure of crew. - **Class Approval**: IRS requires GA drawings of deck machinery for every new-build or major refit ≥ 24 m LOA. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Length of Rope Wound on a Drum (Full Step-by-Step) Consider a cylindrical drum with **barrel diameter** $D$, **rope diameter** $d$, **drum width** $B$, wound with **$n$ complete layers** of rope. **Step 1 – First Layer Geometry**: The first layer has a pitch-circle diameter equal to $D + d$ (since the rope sits on the drum surface and adds its own radius). The circumference of the first layer is: $$ C_1 = \pi (D + d) $$ **Step 2 – Number of Turns per Layer**: The total number of turns that fit in the drum width $B$ (assuming no gap between adjacent turns): $$ N_{\text{turns}} = \frac{B}{d} $$ **Step 3 – Length Wound in the First Layer**: $$ L_1 = C_1 \cdot N_{\text{turns}} = \frac{\pi B (D + d)}{d} $$ **Step 4 – Second Layer Geometry**: The second layer sits on top of the first. The pitch-circle diameter now becomes $D + 3d$ (the rope is centred $2d$ above the drum surface). In general, for the $k$-th layer: $$ C_k = \pi \bigl[ D + (2k - 1)\, d \bigr] $$ **Step 5 – Sum of Lengths of All $n$ Layers**: Summing the geometric series: $$ L = \sum_{k=1}^{n} L_k = \frac{\pi B}{d} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl[ D + (2k - 1)\, d \bigr] $$ **Step 6 – Expand the Sum**: $$ \sum_{k=1}^{n} D = nD $$ $$ \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k - 1) = 2 \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - n = n^2 $$ **Step 7 – Final Closed-Form Expression**: $$ L = \frac{\pi B}{d} \left( nD + n^2 d \right) = \pi B n \left( \frac{D}{d} + n \right) $$ Replacing the geometric $\pi$ factor with the empirical winding constant $K \approx 0.85 \text{–} 0.95$ to account for rope compaction and non-ideal lay: $$ \boxed{\, L = K \, B \, n \, \left( \frac{D}{d} + n \right) \,} $$ This is the form most commonly tabulated in **machinery handbooks (Khurmi, 22nd Ed.)** and is the formula the KTU examiner expects on the answer script. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiners' Tip**: The factor $K$ is **NOT** $\pi$ in the standard textbook form. Use $K = 0.85$ for wire rope and $K = 0.90$ for fibre rope unless the question specifies otherwise. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drum-Line Speed vs Drum RPM **Given**: A trawl winch drum of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ rpm. **Step 1**: One revolution of the drum pulls in one drum circumference of rope: $$ \text{Rope pulled per rev} = \pi D $$ **Step 2**: Number of revolutions per second: $$ f = \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{rev/s}] $$ **Step 3**: Drum-line speed: $$ v = f \cdot \pi D = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > As the drum fills with rope, the effective winding diameter increases, so the **line speed varies** with $D_{\text{eff}} = D + (2n-1)d$. The first wrap is the slowest, the top layer is the fastest for a constant drum RPM. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Line Pull from Hydraulic Power **Given**: A hydraulic motor receives oil at flow rate $Q$ [m³/s] and pressure drop $\Delta p$ [Pa], driving a winch drum of effective diameter $D_{\text{eff}}$ [m] at angular speed $\omega$ [rad/s]. **Step 1** – Hydraulic power delivered to motor: $$ P_{\text{in}} = \Delta p \cdot Q $$ **Step 2** – Mechanical power output (after motor efficiency $\eta_m$ and gear efficiency $\eta_g$): $$ P_{\text{out}} = \Delta p \, Q \, \eta_m \, \eta_g $$ **Step 3** – Power at the drum (which equals the product of line pull $F$ and line speed $v$): $$ F \cdot v = \Delta p \, Q \, \eta_m \, \eta_g $$ **Step 4** – Solve for the line pull: $$ \boxed{\, F = \frac{\Delta p \, Q \, \eta_m \, \eta_g}{v} \,} $$ This is the design equation used to **size the hydraulic pump displacement** of a trawl winch. For example, with $\Delta p = 200$ bar $= 20 \times 10^6$ Pa, $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $\eta_m \eta_g = 0.7$, and $v = 1$ m/s: $$ F = \frac{20 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7}{1} = 21\,000 \text{ N} = 21 \text{ kN} $$ A trawl winch with **21 kN line pull at 1 m/s** is suitable for an **18–20 m shrimp trawler** with a single-rig trawl of nominal spread ~12 m. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Selection Justification | Design Parameter | Trawl Winch | Net Hauler (Longline) | Windlass | |------------------|-------------|------------------------|----------| | SWL (typical) | 30–80 kN | 5–15 kN | 50–150 kN | | Mean line speed | 0.8–1.2 m/s | 0.5–0.8 m/s | 0.1–0.3 m/s (anchor lift) | | Drum storage | 600–1500 m of 22–28 mm wire | 4000–8000 m of 8–12 mm line | 200–400 m of chain + wire | | Drive type | Hydraulic (HPU) | Hydraulic or electric | Hydraulic or electric | | Position | Aft deck, amidships | Aft deck, aft of pilot house | Foredeck, CL | | Special feature | Dual-drum with level-wind | Friction drum (non-spooling) | Cable lifter + chain wheel | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Typical 18 m Trawler Deck Machinery The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physical layout relationships and signal/power flow** of all major deck machinery on a typical 18-m shrimp trawler. ```mermaid graph TD A[HPU Hydraulic Power Unit
Aft Engine Room] -->|HP line 210 bar| B[Trawl Winch
Aft Deck] A -->|HP line 210 bar| C[Net Hauler
Aft Deck] A -->|HP line 210 bar| D[Windlass
Foredeck] A -->|HP line 210 bar| E[Capstan
Main Deck] A -->|LP line 50 bar| F[Deck Crane
0.8 T SWL] B -->|Warp 1| G[Port Power Block
Boom Tip] B -->|Warp 2| H[Stbd Power Block
Boom Tip] G -->|via Otter Board| I[Trawl Net] H -->|via Otter Board| I C -->|via Stern Roller| I D -->|via Hawse Pipe| J[Anchor Chain Locker] K[Wheelhouse Controls] -->|Selenoid Valves| A L[Emergency Stop
Deck Mounted] -->|Hardwired| A ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Flow – Power & Control Topology ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PrimeMover M1[Diesel Engine] end subgraph HPU M1 --> P1[Main Pump
210 bar] M1 --> P2[Aux Pump
50 bar] end subgraph Deck P1 --> W1[Trawl Winch Motor] P1 --> W2[Net Hauler Motor] P1 --> W3[Windlass Motor] P2 --> C1[Deck Crane Motor] end subgraph Controls CON1[Console A - Aft] CON2[Console B - Wheelhouse] CON3[Console C - Foredeck] end CON1 --> W1 CON2 --> W1 CON2 --> W2 CON3 --> W3 CON2 --> C1 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of Deck Machinery ```mermaid graph TD Root[Deck Machinery System] --> L1A[Mooring and Anchoring] Root --> L1B[Fishing Gear Handling] Root --> L1C[Auxiliary Handling] Root --> L1D[Lifting and Support] Root --> L1E[Safety and Guidance] L1A --> L2A1[Windlass] L1A --> L2A2[Chain Stopper] L1A --> L2A3[Bollard] L1A --> L2A4[Hawse Pipe] L1B --> L2B1[Trawl Winch] L1B --> L2B2[Net Hauler] L1B --> L2B3[Net Drum] L1B --> L2B4[Power Block] L1C --> L2C1[Capstan] L1C --> L2C2[Warping Winch] L1C --> L2C3[Line Thrower] L1D --> L2D1[Deck Crane] L1D --> L2D2[Derrick Boom] L1D --> L2D3[Gallows] L1E --> L2E1[Fairlead] L1E --> L2E2[Sheave] L1E --> L2E3[Snatch Block] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define **fleet angle**. State the recommended value of fleet angle for proper multi-layer winding of a wire rope on a drum and explain what happens if it is exceeded.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The fleet angle is the angle subtended between the rope approaching a drum and a line perpendicular to the drum axis, measured at the first wrap. - **Recommended Value (1 mark):** ≤ **4° (1 in 12)** for general service; ≤ **2°** for long lay, multi-layer wire ropes. - **Consequence of Excess (1 mark):** If exceeded, the rope climbs the flange, causing cross-winding, flange wear, kinking, and eventual premature failure of the rope. A wider drum or a movable fairlead (level-wind) is required. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any **six** major items of deck machinery fitted on a fishing trawler, with one-line primary function for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks – ½ mark each):** | # | Item | Function | |---|------|----------| | 1 | Windlass | Lifts and stows the anchor and chain | | 2 | Trawl winch | Stores and pays out/hews in trawl warps | | 3 | Capstan | Provides continuous low-tension line pull for warping | | 4 | Power block | Redirects the trawl warp vertically/horizontally via the boom | | 5 | Net drum | Stores the trawl net in spooled form | | 6 | Deck crane | Lifts catch, gear, and stores into holds | --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) – ESE Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Do not confuse SWL and breaking load.** SWL = ⅙ × minimum breaking load for wire rope. > 2. **Do not write "the bollard pull = weight of vessel."** Bollard pull is the **line pull at the winch drum**, not the static weight. > 3. **Sketch must be dimensioned and labelled** – an unlabelled trawl winch sketch gets 0 marks for the diagram even if the description is perfect. > 4. **In derivations, never skip the "why" of each step.** KTU examiners award 1 mark for each well-justified transition. > 5. **Units are mandatory** in numerical answers – missing units ⇒ 0.5 mark penalty per quantity. --- ### Question A – [14 Marks] *(Choose either A or B)* **(a) Explain with the aid of a neat layout sketch the general arrangement of deck machinery on a typical 18–20 m stern trawler. Highlight the position of HPU, winches, fairleads, and the path of the trawl warps. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** 1. **Foredeck Layout (1.5 marks):** - Windlass is positioned on the **centreline (CL)** just aft of the bow, with the **hawse pipe** leading to the **chain locker** below. - Two **bow fairleads** (port and starboard) for spring lines and towing. - One **anchor-bow roller** for working the anchor in heavy weather. 2. **Main/Aft Deck Layout (3 marks):** - **HPU** is located in the **aft engine room** (or a dedicated compartment) with **HP and LP hydraulic lines** running fore and aft through a **cable & pipe tunnel** on the port side of the vessel. - **Twin trawl winches** are mounted on the **aft deck, port and starboard of CL**, each with its own drum for the warps. - **Aft gantry** with port & starboard **booms** carrying **power blocks** – these redirect the warps over the stern. - **Net drum** amidships on the aft deck, hydraulically driven. - **Capstan** on the CL, just forward of the winches, for warping duties. - **Deck crane** (electro-hydraulic, 0.5–1 T SWL) on the **starboard quarter** for catch handling. 3. **Warp Path (1.5 marks):** - Warp leaves the trawl winch drum → **level-wind sheave** → **deck-level fairlead** → **stern roller fairlead** → **outboard power block** suspended from the boom → down to the **otter board** and net. 4. **Wheelhouse Controls (1 mark):** - All winch control levers, RPM gauges, line-tension displays and emergency stops are duplicated in the **wheelhouse** and at a **local control stand on the aft deck**. **(b) With the help of a sketch, describe the **construction and working of a hydraulic trawl winch**. Mention the functions of the level-wind, the brake, and the over-pressure relief valve.
