[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** 1. **Main Components (2 marks):** - **Drum shaft** with two **wire-rope drums** (port and starboard) keyed onto it. - **Hydraulic motor** (typically axial-piston, swash-plate type) coupled through a **planetary reduction gearbox** to the drum shaft. - **Band brake / disc brake** (spring-applied, hydraulically released) for holding the load when the motor is off. - **Level-wind carriage** that traverses the rope across the drum width during winding to maintain the recommended fleet angle. - **Over-pressure relief valve** set at ~1.25 × working pressure, to protect the hydraulic circuit from shock loads (e.g., net snagging). - **Hydraulic control block** with selenoid-operated directional valves for heave-in / pay-out / stop. 2. **Working Principle (3 marks):** - Pressurized oil (210 bar) from the HPU enters the **hydraulic motor**, which rotates the **drum shaft** through the gearbox. - The **drum** spools the wire rope; rope passes through the **level-wind** for orderly winding. - On **pay-out**, the motor acts as a **hydraulic dynamometer** – oil is throttled back to the tank through a **counter-balance valve**, providing controlled tension. - The **band brake** holds the load when the motor is stopped; it is **fail-safe** (spring-applied). 3. **Functional Roles (2 marks):** - **Level-wind:** Ensures the rope winds uniformly across the drum, prevents cross-winding, reduces fleet angle to within limits, prolongs rope life. - **Brake:** Holds the warp tension against sea loads when the winch is unattended; serves as the **emergency stopping** device. - **Over-pressure relief valve:** Protects the motor and piping from shock loads; redirects excess oil to tank when pressure exceeds set value. --- ### Question B – [14 Marks] *(Alternative choice)* **(a) Discuss the **selection criteria** for deck machinery on a fishing vessel with respect to (i) Safe Working Load, (ii) line speed, (iii) drum storage capacity, and (iv) drive type. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** 1. **(i) Safe Working Load – SWL (2 marks):** - Determined from the **maximum expected static and dynamic load** on the gear. - **Rule of thumb**: $F_{\text{winch}} \geq 2.0 \text{–} 3.0 \times$ peak trawl drag for trawl winches; $F_{\text{windlass}} \geq 1.5 \times$ anchor + chain weight for the windlass. - The **rope/chain breaking load** must be at least **5 × SWL** for wire rope (factor of safety 5) and **3 × SWL** for fibre rope. 2. **(ii) Line Speed (2 marks):** - Trawl winch: **0.8–1.2 m/s** (hauling), **1.5–2.0 m/s** (shooting). - Net hauler (longline): **0.5–0.8 m/s** to prevent crew injury from snap-back. - Windlass: **0.1–0.3 m/s** for anchor lift; higher speed is dangerous due to snap loads. - Selection must be consistent with the **vessel's hydraulic flow capacity** $Q = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot v \cdot 2$ for dual-drum winches. 3. **(iii) Drum Storage Capacity (2 marks):** - Calculated from $L = K \, B \, n \, (D/d + n)$ – the drum must store the **total length of warp/longline** required for the deepest fishing ground plus 20% reserve. - For an 18 m trawler operating in 200 m depth: warp length $\approx 2 \times 1.5 \times 200 = 600$ m minimum; choose drum for **800 m** to allow for oblique scope. 4. **(iv) Drive Type (1 mark):** - **Hydraulic drive** is preferred for heavy, intermittent, high-torque applications (trawl winches, windlasses). - **Electric drive** is preferred for long-duration, low-power, high-precision applications (line haulers, capstans, deck cranes). - Selection also considers **noise & vibration** (electric is quieter), **fire safety** (hydraulic fluid is flammable), and **maintenance skill availability at the fishing harbour**. **(b) Derive an expression for the **length of wire rope wound on a drum of barrel diameter D, drum width B, rope diameter d, wound in n complete layers**. State the empirical form of the formula used in design. [7 marks]** **Model Solution (Step-by-step, the same derivation as Section 3.1 – reproduce the eight-step derivation above):** 1. State assumptions: rope wound tightly, layer-by-layer, no slack between turns. 2. First-layer circumference $C_1 = \pi (D + d)$. 3. Number of turns per layer $N_{\text{turns}} = B/d$. 4. Length of first layer $L_1 = \pi B (D + d)/d$. 5. k-th layer circumference $C_k = \pi [D + (2k-1)d]$. 6. Sum over k = 1 to n: $L = (\pi B/d)[nD + n^2 d]$. 7. Simplify to $L = \pi B n (D/d + n)$. 8. Introduce empirical winding constant $K = 0.85$ for wire rope: $$ \boxed{\, L = K \, B \, n \left( \frac{D}{d} + n \right) \,} $$ **Numerical example (1 bonus mark if computed):** For a drum with $D = 300$ mm, $B = 500$ mm, $d = 22$ mm, $n = 5$ layers, $K = 0.85$: $$ L = 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times (300/22 + 5) = 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times (13.64 + 5) = 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times 18.64 \approx 39.6 \text{ m} $$ This shows the importance of increasing the drum size – a small drum quickly saturates with rope. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist – Deck Machinery Arrangement** - ☐ **Deck machinery** = any equipment on/above the weather deck for fishing, mooring, or cargo handling. - ☐ Five functional families: Mooring/Anchoring, Fishing-Operation, Auxiliary Handling, Lifting, Safety/Directional. - ☐ **Centralized hydraulic drive** = one HPU serves all winches; preferred on trawlers < 35 m. - ☐ **Distributed electric drive** = individual VFD motors at each winch; preferred for long-liners/purse seiners. - ☐ **Fleet angle** ≤ 4° (1 in 12); ≤ 2° for multi-layer wire rope; controlled by **level-wind**. - ☐ **Drum barrel diameter** ≥ 16 d for 6×36 wire rope (ISO 4309). - ☐ **Winding length formula**: $L = K \, B \, n (D/d + n)$; K = 0.85 (wire) or 0.90 (fibre). - ☐ **Line speed** $v = \pi D N / 60$ varies with effective drum diameter as rope is wound. - ☐ **Line pull** $F = \Delta p \cdot Q \cdot \eta / v$ – basis of hydraulic pump sizing. - ☐ **SWL** = ⅕ to ⅙ of minimum breaking load of rope (depends on application and class rules). - ☐ **Bollard pull** ≈ 2.0–3.0 × peak trawl drag. - ☐ **Trawl winch** components: drum, hydraulic motor, planetary gearbox, band/disc brake, level-wind, over-pressure relief valve, control block. - ☐ **Layout principles**: CL symmetry of mooring gear; HPU in protected compartment; control stations duplicated at local & remote; warp path must avoid crew traffic. - ☐ **Stability check**: off-centre line pull produces heeling moment $M_h = F \cdot y$; verify intact & damage GM. - ☐ **Statutory** – ILO-188 (work in fishing), FAO/IMR Code of Safety, IRS Fishing Vessel Rules 2015. - ☐ **Ergonomics** – control stand height 0.9–1.1 m above deck; emergency stop reachable within 0.5 m of operator. - ☐ **Always label sketches** with names, dimensions, and operating fluid/pressure ratings. ---

topics.title: Crew Accommodation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crew Accommodation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crew Accommodation** on a fishing vessel refers to the integrated network of habitable spaces — sleeping cabins (berths), mess rooms, galley (kitchen), sanitary blocks (toilets, wash basins, showers), recreation rooms, hospital/sick bay, offices (for skipper, mates, engineer), and passageways — that are structurally arranged within the hull or superstructure to provide safe, hygienic, and comfortable living conditions for the crew during voyages that may extend from several days (coastal) to months (deep-sea).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> "Crew accommodation on a fishing vessel encompasses the design and arrangement of all living, sleeping, sanitary, catering and recreational spaces provided for personnel on board, in compliance with international conventions (ILO C-188, 2012 Cape Town Agreement) and national regulations."

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, this topic falls under the design-ergonomics and regulatory-compliance leg of the fishing-vessel design syllabus. The two governing regulatory pillars are:

1. **ILO Work in Fishing Convention, 2007 (No. 188)** — the principal international instrument on living and working conditions.
2. **2012 Cape Town Agreement** (International Convention on the Safety of Fishing Vessels) — the successor to the 1977 Torremolinos Convention; covers structural, machinery, and **accommodation** aspects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for a first-time reader:**
> Imagine you are packing for a **two-month houseboat cruise with 25 strangers**. The "boat" is your fishing vessel. Someone must decide:
> — How big each private bedroom is (berth).
> — Where the shared kitchen is (galley).
> — How many toilets and showers are needed for 25 people (sanitary block).
> — Where everyone eats together (mess room).
> — Where a sick fisherman can be isolated (hospital).
> — How cold/hot, bright/dark, and fresh-smelling the air must be (ventilation, heating, lighting).
> — How you escape if water floods in (watertight subdivision of accommodation).
>
> Now, the catch: the "houseboat" is **rolling in 4-metre waves**, smells of diesel and fish, and **cannot pull over to a hardware shop**. Every fitting, locker, shelf, and door must be screwed, hinged, or latched so it cannot fly across the room. That is the essence of **crew accommodation design** — ergonomics + hygiene + safety, all inside a steel box that pitches and rolls.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following key figures are **the numbers an examiner expects you to remember**:

- **Minimum headroom (clear):** **2.00 m** (in accommodation spaces and passageways). Reduced to **1.90 m** in small spaces under ILO 188.
- **Minimum floor area per person in sleeping room:**
  - **3.00 m²** in vessels of **Length (L) ≥ 24 m**.
  - **2.25 m²** in vessels of **L = 18–24 m**.
  - **1.50 m²** in vessels of **L < 18 m** (subject to practical limits).
- **Standard air-change rate (ventilation):** **6 air changes per hour** for accommodation; **12 air changes/hour** for sanitary spaces; **15–20 air changes/hour** for galley.
- **Standard illumination level:**
  - **50 lux** — general living spaces.
  - **100 lux** — mess rooms, galley.
  - **200 lux** — reading lamps at berths, hospital.
- **Minimum sanitary provision (ILO 188, Annex III):**
  - **1 water closet (WC)** per **6 persons** (or fraction thereof).
  - **1 wash basin** per **4 persons**.
  - **1 shower (or bath)** per **8 persons**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "HMS 6-4-8":**
> **H**ealthy crew needs **6** WCs / **4** basins / **8** showers per crew population unit of 24. Memorise "**6-4-8**" — this single triad appears in almost every past-year answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Crew Accommodation (Operational Logic)

The design logic of crew spaces on a fishing vessel follows a **six-step pyramid**:

1. **Step 1 — Determine Crew Complement (N).**
   Driver: vessel LOA, GT, fishing method (trawl, purse-seine, longline, gillnet), endurance (days at sea), and watch-keeping policy.
   Engineer's formula target: derive the **total number of personnel on board (POB)** = crew + officers + supernumeraries (e.g., observers, trainees).

2. **Step 2 — Functional Zoning.**
   Divide the ship into four zones:
   - **Zone A — Sleeping / Private quarters** (most protected, fore or amidships, low noise).
   - **Zone B — Day / Communal spaces** (mess room, recreation, lounge).
   - **Zone C — Service spaces** (galley, pantry, food stores, laundry).
   - **Zone D — Sanitary / Medical** (toilets, showers, hospital).

3. **Step 3 — Space Budget Calculation.**
   Apply minimum area per person to each functional zone to obtain the **total area demand**.

4. **Step 4 — Layout / GA Drafting.**
   Allocate the space budget into the available hull envelope (accommodation block, often a deckhouse aft or a dedicated superstructure).

5. **Step 5 — Systems Integration.**
   Integrate **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning)**, **lighting**, **fresh-water**, **sewage**, **fire-detection**, and **escape routes** into the layout.

6. **Step 6 — Regulatory Verification.**
   Cross-check every dimension against **ILO C-188 Annex III**, **2012 Cape Town Agreement Chapter V**, and the **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules** (for KTU-relevant India-flag vessels).

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why")

- **Step 1** is non-negotiable: every later number depends on $N$ (crew complement).
- **Step 2** keeps noise, odours, and traffic separated — a chef and a sleeping fisherman cannot share a wall.
- **Step 3** is what the examiner tests numerically (see Section 3).
- **Step 4** is the *general arrangement (GA)* exercise — a 14-mark KTU favourite.
- **Step 5** without systems integration produces a beautifully drafted but uninhabitable space.
- **Step 6** without regulatory verification produces a non-registrable vessel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Source / Convention |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Total crew complement | $N = N_{crew} + N_{off} + N_{sup}$ | persons | Flag-state rule |
| 2 | Min. sleeping area per person (L $\geq$ 24 m) | $A_{sleep} = 3.00$ | $\mathrm{m^2/pers}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 3 | Min. sleeping area per person (18 $\leq$ L $<$ 24 m) | $A_{sleep} = 2.25$ | $\mathrm{m^2/pers}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 4 | Min. sleeping area per person (L $<$ 18 m) | $A_{sleep} = 1.50$ | $\mathrm{m^2/pers}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 5 | Total sleeping deck area | $A_{tot,sleep} = N \times A_{sleep}$ | $\mathrm{m^2}$ | Derived |
| 6 | Mess room area | $A_{mess} = 1.0 \times N$ | $\mathrm{m^2/pers}$ | Industry standard |
| 7 | Galley floor area | $A_{galley} \approx 0.6 \times N$ | $\mathrm{m^2/pers}$ | Industry standard |
| 8 | Number of WCs | $n_{WC} = \lceil N / 6 \rceil$ | units | ILO C-188 Annex III |
| 9 | Number of wash basins | $n_{WB} = \lceil N / 4 \rceil$ | units | ILO C-188 Annex III |
| 10 | Number of showers/baths | $n_{SH} = \lceil N / 8 \rceil$ | units | ILO C-188 Annex III |
| 11 | Hospital required when | $N \geq 15$ | persons | Cape Town Agt. Ch. V |
| 12 | Min. hospital area | $A_{hosp} \geq 4.0$ | $\mathrm{m^2}$ | Cape Town Agt. |
| 13 | Ventilation — accommodation | $Q_{acc} = 6 \times V_{room}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 14 | Ventilation — sanitary | $Q_{san} = 12 \times V_{room}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 15 | Min. clear headroom | $h_{min} = 2.00$ | $\mathrm{m}$ | ILO C-188 / IMO |
| 16 | General lighting level | $E_{gen} = 50$ | $\mathrm{lux}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 17 | Reading lighting | $E_{read} = 200$ | $\mathrm{lux}$ | ILO C-188 |
| 18 | Min. temperature in habitable space | $T_{min} = 18$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | ILO C-188 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This matrix is the *single page* you reproduce in the last 2 minutes before the exam hall closes. Every numerical answer in Section 3 originates from these rows.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crew accommodation design affects:
- **Crew retention and welfare** — poorly designed spaces drive fishermen ashore, worsening the manpower crisis.
- **Survey cost and registration delay** — non-compliant layouts cost weeks of class-surveyor rework.
- **Vessel resale value** — documented compliance with ILO 188 materially raises market value.
- **Litigation risk** — flag states (including India under the Merchant Shipping Act) can detain non-compliant vessels.
- **Insurance premiums** — P&I clubs reduce premiums for compliant accommodation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section converts the regulations of Section 2 into **worked numerical examples** — exactly the type KTU examiners set under the "compute the total accommodation deck area" sub-question.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Total Sleeping Deck Area

**Problem statement:**
A purse-seine fishing vessel of **LOA = 28 m** carries a crew complement of $N = 24$ (including officers). Compute the minimum total sleeping-room floor area.

**Step 1 — Identify applicable regulation row.**
For $L = 28\ \mathrm{m}$, since $L \geq 24\ \mathrm{m}$, the applicable sleeping area per person is $A_{sleep} = 3.00\ \mathrm{m^2/pers}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the area formula.**

$$
A_{tot,sleep} \;=\; N \;\times\; A_{sleep}
$$

$$
A_{tot,sleep} \;=\; 24 \;\times\; 3.00
$$

$$
A_{tot,sleep} \;=\; 72.0\ \mathrm{m^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a practical subdivision.**
Typically, sleeping rooms are subdivided into **2-person cabins** for ratings and **single cabins** for officers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{cabin,2p} &= \frac{N_{ratings}}{2} \\
n_{cabin,1p} &= N_{officers}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume $N_{ratings} = 20$, $N_{officers} = 4$:

$$
n_{cabin,2p} = \frac{20}{2} = 10\ \text{cabins}, \qquad n_{cabin,1p} = 4\ \text{cabins}
$$

$$
\text{Total cabins} = 14
$$

**Step 4 — Check headroom.**
Each cabin must have a clear headroom $h \geq 2.00\ \mathrm{m}$.

**Answer:** Minimum total sleeping-room area = **72.0 m²**, distributed over **14 cabins**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Sanitary Block Sizing

**Problem statement:**
For the same vessel ($N = 24$), compute the minimum number of WCs, wash basins, and showers required by ILO C-188.

**Step 1 — Apply the 6-4-8 rule.**

$$
n_{WC} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{6} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{6} \right\rceil = 4
$$

$$
n_{WB} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{4} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{4} \right\rceil = 6
$$

$$
n_{SH} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{8} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{8} \right\rceil = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for "fraction thereof" language.**
Because the ceiling function already accounts for any fractional remainder, no further adjustment is needed.

**Step 3 — Provide separate facilities by sex (where applicable).**
On long-trip vessels, separate male and female facilities are required; in such cases, the counts are split proportionally.

**Answer:** **4 WCs, 6 wash basins, 3 showers** — minimum.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Ventilation Air Quantity

**Problem statement:**
A mess room measures $L_r = 6\ \mathrm{m}$, $B_r = 4\ \mathrm{m}$, $H_r = 2.4\ \mathrm{m}$. Compute the required ventilation air-flow in $\mathrm{m^3/h}$.

**Step 1 — Compute room volume.**

$$
V_{room} = L_r \times B_r \times H_r = 6 \times 4 \times 2.4
$$

$$
V_{room} = 57.6\ \mathrm{m^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the air-change rule (6 ACH for accommodation).**

$$
Q_{acc} = 6 \times V_{room}
$$

$$
Q_{acc} = 6 \times 57.6
$$

$$
Q_{acc} = 345.6\ \mathrm{m^3/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up and select a fan.**
Standard fans come in $50\ \mathrm{m^3/h}$ increments. Round up to $Q = 400\ \mathrm{m^3/h}$.

**Answer:** Required ventilation = **345.6 m³/h** (round up to a standard fan of **400 m³/h**).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Hospital Requirement Decision

**Problem statement:**
A deep-sea long-liner with $N = 12$ is being designed. Determine whether a hospital is mandatory.

**Step 1 — Apply the threshold rule.**

$$
N = 12 < 15
$$

**Step 2 — Conclusion.**
Since $N < 15$, the Cape Town Agreement **does not mandate** a hospital. A dedicated first-aid locker and a sick-berth (folding berth in a cabin) is sufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's trap:** Even when a hospital is *not* mandatory, **a permanently designated first-aid space with a single berth** is still required by ILO C-188 for any vessel operating beyond 24 hours from port.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Regulation Comparison Table

| Provision | ILO C-188 (2007) | Cape Town Agt. (2012) | Indian Fishing Vessels Rules | KTU Exam Emphasis |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Min. sleeping area | 1.5 – 3.0 m² (L-dependent) | 1.5 – 2.5 m² | 1.5 – 2.0 m² | **High** |
| Headroom | 2.00 m | 2.00 m | 1.90 m (small vessels) | **High** |
| Sanitary 6-4-8 | Yes | Referenced | Yes | **Highest** |
| Hospital required | $\geq 15$ crew | $\geq 15$ crew | $\geq 15$ crew | **High** |
| Ventilation ACH | 6 (accom.), 12 (sanitary) | Referenced | 6 | **Medium** |
| Lighting levels | 50–200 lux | — | 50 lux minimum | **Medium** |
| Mess room per person | 1.0 m² | 1.0 m² | 0.8 m² | **Medium** |
| Noise limit (sleeping) | $\leq 60\ \mathrm{dB(A)}$ | — | — | **Low** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters in a 14-mark answer:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme values **comparative analysis**. A direct side-by-side comparison table typically scores 2–3 marks on its own, even before the descriptive answer begins.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Zoning Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vessel Hull Envelope] --> B[Crew Accommodation Block]
    B --> C[Zone A - Private Sleeping]
    B --> D[Zone B - Communal Day]
    B --> E[Zone C - Service]
    B --> F[Zone D - Sanitary Medical]
    C --> C1[Crew Cabins 2-Person]
    C --> C2[Officer Cabins Single]
    C --> C3[Skipper Cabin]
    D --> D1[Mess Room]
    D --> D2[Recreation Lounge]
    D --> D3[Wheelhouse Office]
    E --> E1[Galley]
    E --> E2[Pantry]
    E --> E3[Food Store]
    E --> E4[Laundry]
    F --> F1[WC Block]
    F --> F2[Wash Basin Block]
    F --> F3[Shower Block]
    F --> F4[Hospital if N greater than 15]
    E1 -->|fresh air 15 to 20 ACH| G[HVAC Plant Room]
    D1 -->|fresh air 6 ACH| G
    F1 -->|extract 12 ACH| G
    C1 -->|return air| G
    G -->|regulated supply| H[External Atmosphere via Cowls]
```

### 4.2 Crew Accommodation Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 - Define Crew Complement N] --> S2[Step 2 - Apply ILO and Cape Town Thresholds]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Compute Area Budget per Space]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - Draft General Arrangement GA]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 - Integrate HVAC and Lighting]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 - Verify Headroom and Escape Routes]
    S6 --> S7{Compliant?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Revise Layout]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Approved Accommodation GA]
```

### 4.3 Space Allocation Pyramid (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Deckhouse Top - Wheelhouse and Skipper Cabin] --> P2[Accommodation Deck - Cabins and Mess Room]
    P2 --> P3[Below Accommodation - Galley and Sanitary Block]
    P3 --> P4[Lowest - Food Stores and Laundry]
    P2 -.fire escape.-> P5[External Embarkation Ladder]
    P3 -.ventilation cowl.-> P6[Deckhead Cowl Vent]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- Every node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) or in the form `P1`, `S1`, `F1`, `C1`. **No reserved keywords** such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are used as node names.
- All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric strings inside double quotes — **no bold**, italics, or HTML tables embedded inside node text.
- Arrows use only standard `-->` and `-.->` connectors to maintain renderer compatibility.

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:**
> When a KTU question says *"Draw a block diagram of crew accommodation zones"*, you can reproduce Flowchart 4.1 verbatim on A4 paper. It covers all four zones and earns full marks when each block is **labelled with the relevant ILO clause**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define the term *crew accommodation* as applied to a fishing vessel. List any four spaces that constitute crew accommodation."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Crew accommodation on a fishing vessel refers to the totality of habitable and service spaces provided on board for the crew to live, sleep, eat, and maintain personal hygiene during a fishing voyage, designed in compliance with international conventions such as **ILO Work in Fishing Convention (No. 188)** and the **2012 Cape Town Agreement**.

**Four spaces (any four):**
1. Sleeping cabins / berths.
2. Mess room.
3. Galley (kitchen).
4. Sanitary block (WC, wash basin, shower).
5. Hospital / sick bay (when $N \geq 15$).
6. Recreation room / lounge.
7. Wheelhouse office.

*(Any four — ½ mark each, 1 mark for the definition.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"State the minimum sleeping-room floor area per person as prescribed by ILO C-188 for (i) vessels of length $\geq$ 24 m, and (ii) vessels of length $<$ 18 m."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- (i) For vessels of $L \geq 24\ \mathrm{m}$: $A_{sleep} = 3.00\ \mathrm{m^2/pers}$.
- (ii) For vessels of $L < 18\ \mathrm{m}$: $A_{sleep} = 1.50\ \mathrm{m^2/pers}$.

*(1.5 marks each — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *"Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice given. Each full question carries 14 marks and contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each."*

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functional zoning** of crew accommodation on a fishing vessel with the aid of a neat block diagram. List the international conventions governing the design. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A stern trawler of LOA **32 m** has a crew complement of **30 persons**. Determine:
  (i) The minimum total sleeping-room floor area.
  (ii) The minimum number of WCs, wash basins, and showers required.
  (iii) Whether a dedicated hospital is mandatory. (7 Marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Functional Zoning (7 Marks):**

Crew accommodation is divided into **four functional zones**, each isolated by location, noise level, and traffic pattern:

**Zone A — Private Sleeping Quarters (1.5 m² – 3.0 m² per person):**
Includes individual cabins for officers and 2-person cabins for ratings. Located in the **quietest** part of the accommodation block — usually forward or amidships, away from engine-room noise.

**Zone B — Communal Day Spaces (≈ 1.0 m² per person for mess):**
Comprises the mess room, recreation lounge, and (if size permits) a library or TV room. Centrally located for easy access from cabins.

**Zone C — Service Spaces (galley ≈ 0.6 m² per person):**
Galley, pantry, dry and cold food stores, and laundry. Galley is placed adjacent to the mess room and isolated from sleeping cabins to prevent cooking odours and grease migration.

**Zone D — Sanitary and Medical (per 6-4-8 rule):**
Toilets, wash basins, showers, and (if $N \geq 15$) a hospital with a single berth, medicine cabinet, and wash basin.

**Governing Conventions (1 mark each, total 3 marks):**
1. **ILO Work in Fishing Convention, 2007 (No. 188)** — comprehensive living and working conditions.
2. **2012 Cape Town Agreement** — safety of fishing vessels, Chapter V covers accommodation.
3. **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules / Merchant Shipping Act** — national flag-state requirement.
4. **ISO 8468 / ISO 12217** — referenced for noise and stability.

*(Block diagram as in Section 4.1 should accompany — 2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Sleeping-room floor area:**
Since $L = 32\ \mathrm{m} \geq 24\ \mathrm{m}$, applicable area per person is $A_{sleep} = 3.00\ \mathrm{m^2/pers}$.

$$
A_{tot,sleep} = N \times A_{sleep} = 30 \times 3.00 = 90.0\ \mathrm{m^2}
$$

**(ii) Sanitary provision (6-4-8 rule):**

$$
n_{WC} = \left\lceil \frac{30}{6} \right\rceil = 5
$$

$$
n_{WB} = \left\lceil \frac{30}{4} \right\rceil = 8
$$

$$
n_{SH} = \left\lceil \frac{30}{8} \right\rceil = 4
$$

**(iii) Hospital requirement:**
Since $N = 30 \geq 15$, a **dedicated hospital is mandatory**, with minimum floor area $A_{hosp} \geq 4.0\ \mathrm{m^2}$.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **ventilation, lighting, and headroom requirements** for crew accommodation on a fishing vessel as per ILO C-188. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A coastal gillnetter of LOA **20 m** carries **18 crew**. Calculate:
  (i) The minimum sleeping-room area.
  (ii) The required air-change rate and volumetric flow rate for a mess room of dimensions 5 m × 3.5 m × 2.2 m.
  (iii) Briefly justify the minimum headroom value. (7 Marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Ventilation, Lighting, and Headroom (7 Marks):**

**Ventilation (3 marks):**
- Accommodation spaces: minimum **6 air changes per hour (ACH)**.
- Sanitary spaces: minimum **12 ACH** (to control odour and humidity).
- Galley: **15 to 20 ACH** (to extract cooking fumes and heat).
- Mechanical supply must be supplemented by **natural ventilation** through cowled deckhead vents; dampers must close in fire.
- Air inlets must be located **upwind** of any exhaust outlet to prevent re-circulation.

**Lighting (2 marks):**
- General illumination: **50 lux** minimum in cabins and passageways.
- Mess room and galley: **100 lux**.
- Reading lamps at each berth: **200 lux**.
- Natural light (portholes or windows) is mandatory in all permanent cabins.

**Headroom (2 marks):**
- Minimum clear headroom in all habitable spaces and passageways: **2.00 m**.
- A reduction to **1.90 m** is permitted only in small, non-habitable recesses.
- Headroom is measured from the **finished floor** to the **lowest overhead obstruction** (beams, valances, hatch coamings).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Sleeping-room area:**
Since $18 \leq L = 20\ \mathrm{m} < 24\ \mathrm{m}$, applicable area per person is $A_{sleep} = 2.25\ \mathrm{m^2/pers}$.

$$
A_{tot,sleep} = 18 \times 2.25 = 40.5\ \mathrm{m^2}
$$

**(ii) Air-change rate and volumetric flow:**

Mess room air-change rate (accommodation rule): $\mathrm{ACH} = 6$.

Room volume:

$$
V_{room} = 5 \times 3.5 \times 2.2 = 38.5\ \mathrm{m^3}
$$

Volumetric flow:

$$
Q = \mathrm{ACH} \times V_{room} = 6 \times 38.5 = 231.0\ \mathrm{m^3/h}
$$

**(iii) Headroom justification:**
The minimum headroom of 2.00 m ensures that an **average adult male of stature 1.75 m** can stand upright without stooping, walk safely through passageways during a roll, and permits standard door heights and overhead locker dimensions. Below this value, **long-term ergonomic injury** and **panic during emergency evacuation** become unacceptable risks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** — sanitary provision counts use the **ceiling function** ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$), not floor. $N=24$ gives **4 WCs**, not 3.
> 2. **Wrong L-bracket for sleeping area** — many students apply $3.00\ \mathrm{m^2}$ universally. The correct rule is **L-dependent** (1.5 / 2.25 / 3.00).
> 3. **Missing the hospital threshold** — when $N \geq 15$, the hospital is **mandatory**, not optional.
> 4. **Confusing ACH with $\mathrm{m^3/h}$** — ACH is a *rate*; multiply by room *volume* to get air quantity.
> 5. **Ignoring the flag-state rule** — international conventions are minima; **national rules may be stricter**. In KTU's Kerala context, the **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules** apply on top of ILO C-188.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision flashcards — pin this list to your study wall.**

- **Crew accommodation = four zones:** Private (sleeping), Communal (mess/recreation), Service (galley), Sanitary/Medical.
- **Two governing conventions:** **ILO C-188 (2007)** + **2012 Cape Town Agreement**.
- **Sleeping area per person:** **1.5 / 2.25 / 3.00 m²** depending on $L$ ($<$18, 18–24, $\geq$24 m).
- **Minimum headroom:** **2.00 m** (1.90 m in small spaces).
- **Sanitary 6-4-8 rule:** **1 WC / 6 persons**, **1 wash basin / 4 persons**, **1 shower / 8 persons**.
- **Hospital threshold:** mandatory when $N \geq 15$; minimum area **4 m²**.
- **Ventilation ACH:** **6 (accommodation), 12 (sanitary), 15–20 (galley)**.
- **Lighting lux levels:** **50 (general), 100 (mess/galley), 200 (reading & hospital)**.
- **Minimum habitable temperature:** **18 °C**.
- **Design workflow:** **N → zone → area budget → GA → systems → regulatory check.**
- **Always round UP** when computing sanitary fixture counts.
- **Kerala-specific national rule:** *Indian Fishing Vessels Rules* apply on top of ILO C-188.
- **Ergonomic add-ons** (often missed in answers): heavy items stowed **low**, doors latch **against roll**, furniture **secured**, no sharp corners in mess/galley.
- **Fire safety linkage:** accommodation must have **two means of escape**, **self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** lockers at every deck, and **fire-detection** in galley and laundry.
- **Examiner's mnemonic:** **"HMS 6-4-8"** = WC-per-6, basin-per-4, shower-per-8.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of marine engineering and fishing vessel design, **fuel** refers to any combustible energy-storage material that, when burned in the presence of an oxidizer (typically air), releases thermal energy which is subsequently converted into mechanical work by the vessel's main propulsion engine and auxiliary machinery. For fishing vessels operating under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, fuel engineering encompasses the classification, physicochemical characterization, storage architecture, handling protocols, and consumption analytics of marine fuels used onboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 — Fuel):**
> Marine fuel is a specialized hydrocarbon-based energy carrier formulated to meet the unique operational demands of watercraft propulsion, including long-term storage stability, resistance to microbial degradation, controlled viscosity across varying sea temperatures, and compliance with International Maritime Organization (IMO) emission regulations such as **MARPOL Annex VI**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the diesel engine of a fishing vessel as a **human body performing hard labor at sea**. Just as a fisherman needs a high-calorie, storable meal before a long voyage, a vessel's engine requires fuel to sustain it across days or weeks of operation. The **fuel tank** is the "pantry," the **fuel pipes** are the "veins," the **fuel pump** is the "heart," and the **combustion chamber** is the "stomach" where energy digestion occurs. Just as food quality affects a fisherman's endurance, the grade and properties of marine fuel directly determine the vessel's operational range, engine health, and the fish catch preservation cycle.

### 1.3 Standard Marine Fuel Metrics

- **Calorific Value (CV):** Approximately **42,000 kJ/kg** for marine diesel oils and **40,000 kJ/kg** for heavy fuel oils.
- **Standard Density at 15°C:** Ranges from **860 kg/m³ (Marine Gas Oil)** to **1010 kg/m³ (Heavy Fuel Oil)**.
- **Flash Point Requirement:** Minimum **60°C** for marine residual fuels and **43°C** minimum for distillate fuels.
- **Sulfur Content Cap (IMO 2020):** Maximum **0.50% m/m** globally; **0.10% m/m** in Emission Control Areas (ECAs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fuel Selection Matters in Fishing Vessel Design:**
> Unlike large commercial cargo ships, fishing vessels are size-constrained, have limited bunker space, operate close to coastal waters (often within ECAs), and require rapid maneuvering. Hence, fuel selection balances **energy density**, **cost**, **emission compliance**, and **storage footprint**.

### 1.4 Categories of Marine Fuel

Marine fuels are broadly classified based on their refining process, viscosity, and application:

| Category | Refining Type | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Marine Gas Oil (MGO)** | Distillate | High-speed auxiliary engines, small fishing boats |
| **Marine Diesel Oil (MDO)** | Blend of distillate and residual | Main propulsion of small-to-medium fishing vessels |
| **Intermediate Fuel Oil (IFO 180 / IFO 380)** | Residual blend | Medium fishing trawlers, long-range vessels |
| **Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO)** | Residual | Large ocean-going fishing vessels and factory ships |
| **LNG / Marine LPG** | Cryogenic gas | Emerging green-fuel applications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Marine Fuels

A fishing vessel designer must understand the following physical and chemical properties of marine fuels because they directly influence the design of fuel storage, piping, treatment systems, and engine performance.

#### 2.1.1 Viscosity
Viscosity is the measure of a fuel's resistance to flow. It is critical because marine fuel must be atomized properly in the engine's injection system.

- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Measured in **centistokes (cSt)** or **mm²/s** at a standard reference temperature of **50°C** for residual fuels and **40°C** for distillate fuels.
- **Why it matters:** If viscosity is too high, the fuel cannot be properly atomized, leading to incomplete combustion, carbon deposits, and engine wear. If too low, lubrication of fuel injection pumps is compromised.

#### 2.1.2 Calorific Value (Net & Gross)
- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** Total heat released assuming all water vapor in the exhaust condenses.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV):** Practical heat available, accounting for latent heat lost in water vapor.
- **Formula relationship:** $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - h_{fg} \times m_{H_2O}$

Where $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (**≈ 2,442 kJ/kg**).

#### 2.1.3 Flash Point and Fire Point
- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which fuel vapor forms an ignitable mixture with air.
- **Fire Point:** Temperature at which sustained combustion occurs once ignited.
- For marine safety regulations, fuels with flash point **below 60°C** are prohibited for use as main propulsion fuel on most commercial fishing vessels.

#### 2.1.4 Pour Point and Cloud Point
- **Pour Point:** The lowest temperature at which fuel will still flow under standard test conditions. Critical for fishing vessels operating in cold seas (e.g., North Atlantic, Arctic fisheries).
- **Cloud Point:** The temperature at which paraffin wax crystals first become visible, indicating the start of fuel solidification.

#### 2.1.5 Sulfur Content
Governed by **MARPOL Annex VI**, sulfur content determines whether the vessel can operate in ECAs. Typical values:

- **HFO:** 2.0% to 3.5% (pre-2020) → **0.50% m/m** (global cap post-2020)
- **VLSFO (Very Low Sulfur Fuel Oil):** ≤ **0.50% m/m**
- **ULSFO (Ultra Low Sulfur Fuel Oil):** ≤ **0.10% m/m**
- **MGO / MDO:** Typically **< 0.10% m/m**

#### 2.1.6 Density
Density affects fuel mass-to-volume conversion and tank capacity design. Measured at **15°C** per ISO 3675 standards.

- $\rho_{fuel}$ typically ranges from **860 kg/m³ to 1010 kg/m³** depending on grade.

#### 2.1.7 Water and Sediment Content
- Water content must be **< 1.0% V/V** in distillate fuels and **< 0.5% V/V** in residual fuels.
- Centrifugal purifiers are used onboard to remove water and sediment.

#### 2.1.8 Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom)
Indicates the tendency of fuel to form carbon deposits in combustion chambers. Critical for **IFO 380** and **HFO** used in older marine diesel engines.

#### 2.1.9 Specific Energy
The energy released per unit mass of fuel:
- $\text{Specific Energy} = \text{NCV (kJ/kg)}$

#### 2.1.10 Cetane Number
A measure of ignition quality — the time delay between fuel injection and combustion initiation. Higher cetane number = better cold-start performance.
- Distillate marine fuels: **40 to 55**
- Residual marine fuels: **20 to 35** (often require pilot injection of diesel)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of fuel consumed | $M_f = V_f \times \rho_{fuel}$ | kg | $V_f$ = volume in m³ |
| 2 | Total heat energy from fuel | $Q = M_f \times \text{NCV}$ | kJ | NCV in kJ/kg |
| 3 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{M_f}{P_b \times t}$ | kg/kWh | $P_b$ = brake power |
| 4 | Fuel consumption rate | $R_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b$ | kg/h | Used for endurance |
| 5 | Endurance (range in time) | $T_e = \frac{M_{tank}}{R_f}$ | hours | $M_{tank}$ = total fuel mass |
| 6 | Endurance (range in distance) | $D_e = V_{ship} \times T_e$ | nautical miles | $V_{ship}$ = vessel speed |
| 7 | Tank volume | $V_{tank} = \frac{M_{tank}}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | m³ | For storage design |
| 8 | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_b = \frac{P_b \times 3600}{M_f \times \text{NCV}}$ | dimensionless | Standard ISO 3046 |
| 9 | Propeller-shaft efficiency | $\eta_D = \eta_b \times \eta_g \times \eta_s$ | dimensionless | $D$ = delivered, $g$ = gearbox, $s$ = shaft |
| 10 | Vessel range (mission profile) | $R = \frac{E_{fuel}}{\text{SFC} \times P_{cruise}}$ | nautical miles | $E_{fuel}$ = total energy stored |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fuel properties and formulas above are essential in the following practical scenarios encountered in fishing vessel design:

- **Endurance Estimation:** Determining how many days a fishing trawler can stay at sea before refueling. A typical 25 m trawler with 30 tonnes of fuel and BSFC of 0.22 kg/kWh at 400 kW can operate for roughly $30 / (0.22 \times 400) \approx 341$ hours ≈ **14 days** continuously at full power.
- **Tank Sizing:** Ensuring the bunker tanks fit within the vessel's hull form while leaving volume for catch, freshwater, and ballast.
- **Emission Compliance:** Verifying that the selected fuel grade allows the vessel to fish in regulated ECAs (e.g., North Sea, Baltic Sea, US coasts).
- **Engine Matching:** Selecting the right fuel grade for a given engine — older medium-speed engines handle HFO, modern high-speed engines prefer MDO/MGO.
- **Economic Optimization:** Balancing the lower cost of residual fuels against higher maintenance and treatment system costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fuel Mass from Tank Volume

**Problem Context:** A fishing vessel has a fuel tank of volume **4.5 m³** filled with Marine Diesel Oil (MDO) of density **900 kg/m³** at 15°C. Determine the mass of fuel stored.

**Step 1 — Identify the fundamental relationship between mass, volume, and density.**

The mass of any substance is the product of its volume and its density:

$$M_f = V_f \times \rho_{fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values into the equation.**

Given:
- $V_f = 4.5$ m³
- $\rho_{fuel} = 900$ kg/m³

$$M_f = 4.5 \times 900$$

**Step 3 — Perform the numerical calculation.**

$$M_f = 4050 \text{ kg} = 4.05 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Conclusion:** The fuel tank contains **4,050 kg (4.05 tonnes)** of MDO. This is the standard mass calculation used for bunker surveys and endurance planning.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Heat Energy Released from Fuel

**Problem Context:** Using the previous result, determine the total chemical energy stored in the fuel if the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** of MDO is **42,500 kJ/kg**.

**Step 1 — Apply the energy-mass relationship.**

The total thermal energy available from complete combustion is:

$$Q_{total} = M_f \times \text{NCV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

Given:
- $M_f = 4050$ kg
- $\text{NCV} = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg

$$Q_{total} = 4050 \times 42{,}500$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$Q_{total} = 172{,}125{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 1.721 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}$$

**Conclusion:** The fuel stores approximately **1.721 × 10⁸ kJ** of chemical energy. This value is the input to the propulsion system efficiency chain.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

**Problem Context:** A marine diesel engine develops **500 kW** of brake power while consuming **110 kg of fuel per hour**. Compute the BSFC.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of BSFC.**

BSFC is the fuel mass consumed per unit of brake energy produced:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{M_f}{P_b \times t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known quantities.**

Given:
- $M_f = 110$ kg
- $P_b = 500$ kW
- $t = 1$ hour = 1 h

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{110}{500 \times 1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result.**

$$\text{BSFC} = 0.220 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Conclusion:** The engine has a BSFC of **0.220 kg/kWh**, which is typical for a well-tuned medium-speed marine diesel. For reference, the IMO 2015 baseline is **0.213 g/kWh** for Tier II engines.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Vessel Endurance in Hours

**Problem Context:** A 28 m fishing trawler carries **25 tonnes** of IFO 380 fuel. The main engine has a BSFC of **0.235 kg/kWh** at a continuous service rating of **450 kW**. Calculate the endurance.

**Step 1 — Determine the hourly fuel consumption rate.**

$$R_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$R_f = 0.235 \times 450 = 105.75 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the endurance formula.**

$$T_e = \frac{M_{tank}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values (convert 25 tonnes to kg).**

$$T_e = \frac{25{,}000}{105.75}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the result.**

$$T_e \approx 236.4 \text{ hours} \approx 9.85 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** The trawler can operate continuously at full power for approximately **9.85 days**. In practice, fishing vessels rarely operate at 100% MCR for the entire trip; the typical service profile reduces this to **14–18 days** at cruising loads of 60–75% MCR.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Vessel Range in Nautical Miles

**Problem Context:** Continuing from the previous example, the vessel's cruising speed is **9.5 knots**. Determine the total range in nautical miles.

**Step 1 — Apply the range formula.**

$$D_e = V_{ship} \times T_e$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$D_e = 9.5 \times 236.4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result.**

$$D_e \approx 2245.8 \text{ nautical miles}$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel's full-power range is approximately **2,246 nautical miles**, suitable for transiting to and operating within most regional fishing grounds such as the Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, or extended Laccadive shelf operations.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Problem Context:** Verify the brake thermal efficiency of the engine from Derivation 3.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**

Brake thermal efficiency is the ratio of useful mechanical work output to the chemical energy input:

$$\eta_b = \frac{P_b \times t \times 3600}{M_f \times \text{NCV}}$$

The factor **3600** converts kWh to kJ.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

Given:
- $P_b = 500$ kW
- $t = 1$ h
- $M_f = 110$ kg
- $\text{NCV} = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg

$$\eta_b = \frac{500 \times 1 \times 3600}{110 \times 42{,}500}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator separately.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 4{,}675{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the efficiency.**

$$\eta_b = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{4{,}675{,}000} = 0.3850 = 38.50\%$$

**Conclusion:** The engine's brake thermal efficiency is **38.5%**, which is realistic for a 4-stroke medium-speed marine diesel. The remaining **61.5%** is lost as exhaust heat, cooling water heat, and friction.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Trade-Offs in Fuel Selection for Fishing Vessels

| Selection Criterion | MGO / MDO | IFO 380 | HFO | LNG / Methanol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value (kJ/kg) | High (42,500) | Medium (40,500) | Medium (40,000) | Low (50,000 for LNG) |
| Purchase Cost | High | Medium | Low | Medium (declining) |
| Pre-heating Required | No | Yes (moderate) | Yes (heavy) | Yes (cryogenic) |
| Sulfur Content | < 0.1% | 0.5–1.5% | 2.0–3.5% | < 0.001% |
| Storage Volume Needed | High | Medium | Medium | Very High (gas) |
| Suitability for Small Fishing Vessels | Excellent | Good | Marginal | Poor (infrastructure) |
| IMO 2020 Compliance | Yes | Blending required | Scrubber required | Yes (future fuel) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Marine Fuel System Block Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end fuel flow architecture** of a typical fishing vessel, from bunkering storage to final combustion in the main propulsion engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bunker Storage Tank] --> B[Transfer Pump]
    B --> C[Settling Tank]
    C --> D[Purifier Centrifuge]
    D --> E[Service Tank]
    E --> F[Pre-heater Unit]
    F --> G[Viscosity Control Unit]
    G --> H[Daily Service Tank]
    H --> I[Booster Pump]
    I --> J[Fine Filter]
    J --> K[Main Engine Fuel Pump]
    K --> L[Main Diesel Engine]
    L --> M[Propeller Shaft]
    M --> N[Propulsion Thrust]
    L --> O[Auxiliary Generator]
    E --> P[Auxiliary Service Tank]
    P --> Q[Auxiliary Engine]
    C --> R[Sludge Tank]
    D --> R
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L fill:#FF8888,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N fill:#88FF88,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

- **Bunker Storage Tank:** Primary fuel reservoir, designed to absorb thermal expansion and reduce sloshing. Sizing typically **30–60%** of total fuel capacity.
- **Transfer Pump:** Moves fuel from bunker to settling tank at low pressure (**2–4 bar**).
- **Settling Tank:** Allows water and sediment to settle by gravity over **12–24 hours**.
- **Purifier Centrifuge:** Spins at **6,000–8,000 RPM** to separate water, sediment, and sludge from fuel using density differences.
- **Service Tank:** Intermediate clean-fuel storage, sized for **8–12 hours** of engine operation.
- **Pre-heater Unit:** Heats heavy fuel to **120–150°C** to reduce viscosity to **12–15 cSt** for proper injection.
- **Viscosity Control Unit (VCU):** Continuously measures outlet viscosity and regulates steam heating to maintain **15 cSt** at the injector.
- **Daily Service Tank:** Located above engine level for gravity feed in emergency scenarios.
- **Booster Pump:** Pressurizes fuel to **4–8 bar** for delivery to the engine.
- **Fine Filter:** Final purification, removing particles **> 25 microns**.
- **Main Engine Fuel Pump:** High-pressure pump (**400–1000 bar**) supplying the fuel injectors.
- **Main Diesel Engine:** Converts chemical energy to mechanical work.
- **Auxiliary Service Tank:** Dedicated fuel supply for generator, compressor, and refrigeration units used for fish hold cooling.
- **Sludge Tank:** Collects waste oil, water, and sediment from purifiers for shore-side disposal.

### 4.3 Fuel-Energy Conversion Chain (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> A2[Combustion in Engine]
    A2 --> A3[Thermal Energy at 1800 C]
    A3 --> A4[Mechanical Energy on Crankshaft]
    A4 --> A5[Gearbox Reduction]
    A5 --> A6[Shaft Rotation]
    A6 --> A7[Propeller Thrust]
    A7 --> A8[Hydrodynamic Force]
    A8 --> A9[Vessel Forward Motion]
    A1 -.38 percent .-> A4
    A4 -.97 percent .-> A6
    A6 -.65 percent .-> A7
    A7 -.50 percent .-> A9
    style A1 fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Note:** The dotted edges represent the **cumulative efficiency** at each stage. For a typical fishing trawler, the overall propulsive coefficient is approximately:

$$\eta_{D} = \eta_b \times \eta_g \times \eta_s \times \eta_p \approx 0.38 \times 0.98 \times 0.99 \times 0.65 \approx 0.24$$

This means only **24%** of the chemical energy in the fuel translates to actual vessel motion, with the rest lost as heat, exhaust, and hydrodynamic inefficiencies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]:**
*List any three essential properties of marine fuel oil used in fishing vessels and explain why each is important.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flash Point:** Must be at least **60°C** for residual marine fuels. Importance: Ensures safe storage and handling aboard fishing vessels, minimizing fire and explosion risks in the engine room.

2. **Viscosity:** Measured in centistokes at 50°C; must be reduced to **15 cSt** before injection via pre-heaters. Importance: Proper atomization of fuel in the combustion chamber requires controlled viscosity; incorrect viscosity leads to incomplete combustion and engine deposits.

3. **Calorific Value (NCV):** Approximately **40,000–42,500 kJ/kg** for marine fuels. Importance: Determines the energy density of the fuel and directly affects the vessel's endurance and bunker capacity requirements.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**
*Distinguish between Marine Gas Oil (MGO) and Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) in three points.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MGO | HFO |
|---|---|---|
| Refining Type | Pure distillate | Residual fraction from refinery |
| Viscosity | Low (2–6 cSt at 40°C) | Very high (up to 700 cSt at 50°C) |
| Sulfur Content | < 0.1% (clean) | 2.0–3.5% (high) |
| Pre-heating Required | No | Yes (120–150°C) |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Typical Use | Auxiliary engines, high-speed boats | Large main propulsion engines with fuel treatment systems |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the marine fuel treatment system onboard a fishing vessel with the help of a block diagram. List the components and describe the function of the **purifier centrifuge** and the **viscosity control unit**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 30 m fishing trawler has a bunker tank of volume **12 m³** filled with IFO 380 fuel of density **985 kg/m³** and NCV **40,500 kJ/kg**. The main engine develops **600 kW** of brake power with a BSFC of **0.240 kg/kWh**. Calculate:
- (i) The total mass of fuel stored.
- (ii) The total chemical energy available.
- (iii) The vessel's endurance in hours.
- (iv) The range in nautical miles at a cruising speed of **10 knots**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The marine fuel treatment system consists of the following sequence:

1. **Bunker Storage Tank → Transfer Pump → Settling Tank → Purifier Centrifuge → Service Tank → Pre-heater → Viscosity Control Unit → Daily Service Tank → Booster Pump → Fine Filter → Engine Injector**

**Purifier Centrifuge Function:**
The purifier operates at **6,000–8,000 RPM** and uses the density difference between fuel and contaminants (water, sediment) to separate them. Heavy contaminants are thrown outward to the bowl wall and discharged as sludge. Water is diverted to a water-collecting chamber. Clean fuel exits through a calibrated interface ring. The purifier typically removes **95% of solid contaminants and 99% of free water**.

**Viscosity Control Unit (VCU) Function:**
The VCU measures the kinematic viscosity of fuel leaving the pre-heater using a capillary viscometer. If viscosity exceeds the target **15 cSt**, the controller increases the steam flow to the pre-heater. If too low, it reduces heat input. This feedback loop ensures the engine always receives fuel of optimal atomization quality.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Mass of Fuel Stored:**

$$M_f = V_f \times \rho_{fuel} = 12 \times 985 = 11{,}820 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Total Chemical Energy:**

$$Q_{total} = M_f \times \text{NCV} = 11{,}820 \times 40{,}500 = 4.787 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Hourly Fuel Consumption Rate:**

$$R_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b = 0.240 \times 600 = 144 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Endurance:**

$$T_e = \frac{M_{tank}}{R_f} = \frac{11{,}820}{144} \approx 82.1 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Range in Nautical Miles:**

$$D_e = V_{ship} \times T_e = 10 \times 82.1 = 821 \text{ nautical miles}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **MARPOL Annex VI** regulations on sulfur content of marine fuels. Explain the difference between **global sulfur cap**, **ECA sulfur cap**, and the impact of these regulations on the design and operation of fishing vessels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine diesel engine consumes **85 kg of MDO** in **30 minutes** while developing **320 kW**. The NCV of MDO is **42,500 kJ/kg**. Determine:
- (i) The Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC).
- (ii) The brake thermal efficiency of the engine.
- (iii) Comment on the result and compare it with the IMO Tier II baseline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**MARPOL Annex VI — Air Pollution Prevention:**
The International Maritime Organization (IMO) introduced **MARPOL Annex VI** in 1997, with the sulfur oxide (SOx) emission regulations progressively tightened:

- **2005 (Initial Cap):** Global sulfur cap of **4.50% m/m** in marine fuel.
- **2012 (Tightened):** Reduced to **3.50% m/m**.
- **2015 (ECA Strengthening):** In ECAs, sulfur cap reduced to **0.10% m/m**.
- **2020 (Global Cap):** Reduced globally to **0.50% m/m**.

**Global vs ECA Sulfur Cap:**
- **Global Sulfur Cap:** Applies to all vessels operating outside designated ECAs; current limit is **0.50% m/m**.
- **ECA Sulfur Cap:** Applies in Emission Control Areas (Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA); current limit is **0.10% m/m**.

**Impact on Fishing Vessel Design and Operation:**
1. **Fuel Selection:** Vessels operating in ECAs (e.g., North Sea fishing fleets) must use **MGO, ULSFO, or install scrubbers** to comply with the 0.10% limit.
2. **Tank Segregation:** Bunker tanks may need separation for ECA-grade and non-ECA fuel during multi-zone voyages.
3. **Cost Implications:** Low-sulfur fuel costs **30–80% more** per tonne, affecting the operational economics of small-scale fishing enterprises.
4. **Dual-Fuel Engines:** Modern fishing vessels may adopt dual-fuel engines (diesel + LNG) for compliance flexibility.
5. **Emission Control Technology:** The use of **Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / scrubbers)** is an alternative fuel-switching for continued HFO use in some regions.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $M_f = 85$ kg (in 30 minutes)
- $P_b = 320$ kW
- $t = 0.5$ h
- $\text{NCV} = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg

**(i) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{M_f}{P_b \times t} = \frac{85}{320 \times 0.5} = \frac{85}{160}$$

$$\text{BSFC} = 0.53125 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**(ii) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_b = \frac{P_b \times t \times 3600}{M_f \times \text{NCV}}$$

$$\eta_b = \frac{320 \times 0.5 \times 3600}{85 \times 42{,}500}$$

$$\eta_b = \frac{576{,}000}{3{,}612{,}500} = 0.1594 = 15.94\%$$

**(iii) Comments:**

The brake thermal efficiency of **15.94%** is significantly **lower** than the IMO Tier II baseline for marine diesel engines, which typically achieves **38–42%** for modern 4-stroke medium-speed engines. The low value suggests either:
- The engine is heavily loaded beyond its optimal efficiency point,
- The engine is a small 2-stroke model with inherently lower efficiency,
- The fuel has poor atomization (cold operation), or
- There are significant mechanical losses or fuel leaks.

For a properly designed fishing vessel main engine, the expected $\eta_b$ should be in the range of **35–40%**. The computed value of **15.94%** indicates this engine is operating far from its design point, which a KTU examiner would mark as a clear design or operational deficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Fuel Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert tonnes to kg, hours to seconds (for the 3600 factor), or knots to m/s. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing GCV and NCV:** Using Gross Calorific Value (GCV ≈ 45,000 kJ/kg) instead of NCV (≈ 42,500 kJ/kg) overestimates the efficiency.
> 3. **Ignoring Density Variations:** Marine fuel density changes with temperature; failing to specify reference temperature (15°C) is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting the 3600 Multiplier:** In efficiency calculations, $\eta_b = \frac{P_b \times t \times 3600}{M_f \times \text{NCV}}$ — the 3600 converts kWh to kJ. Skipping it gives a 3600-times-wrong answer.
> 5. **Confusing BSFC and Brake Efficiency:** BSFC is a fuel-rate metric (kg/kWh), while $\eta_b$ is dimensionless. They are inversely related but not interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Fuel**

- [ ] **Fuel is the energy carrier** of a fishing vessel; selection balances cost, energy density, sulfur content, and engine compatibility.
- [ ] **MGO, MDO, IFO 180, IFO 380, HFO, VLSFO, ULSFO** are the main marine fuel grades; arranged by increasing viscosity and decreasing cost.
- [ ] **Flash Point** must be **≥ 60°C** for safety in residual marine fuels.
- [ ] **Viscosity target at injector:** **15 cSt** (controlled by VCU).
- [ ] **Pour Point** is critical for cold-sea operations; use winter-grade fuels below 0°C ambient.
- [ ] **Sulfur Cap (2020):** **0.50% m/m** globally, **0.10% m/m** in ECAs.
- [ ] **Calorific Value:** NCV ≈ **40,000 kJ/kg (HFO)** to **42,500 kJ/kg (MDO/MGO)**.
- [ ] **Density at 15°C:** **860 kg/m³ (MGO)** to **1010 kg/m³ (HFO)**.
- [ ] **BSFC formula:** $\text{BSFC} = \frac{M_f}{P_b \times t}$ in **kg/kWh**.
- [ ] **Endurance formula:** $T_e = \frac{M_{tank}}{R_f}$ in **hours**.
- [ ] **Range formula:** $D_e = V_{ship} \times T_e$ in **nautical miles**.
- [ ] **Brake Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_b = \frac{P_b \times t \times 3600}{M_f \times \text{NCV}}$ (remember the **3600** factor).
- [ ] **Fuel treatment system order:** Bunker → Transfer Pump → Settling Tank → Purifier → Service Tank → Pre-heater → VCU → Daily Tank → Booster → Filter → Engine.
- [ ] **Purifier removes** water and sediment; **VCU regulates** viscosity by adjusting steam flow.
- [ ] **Overall propulsive efficiency** ≈ **24%** for typical fishing trawlers; most energy lost as exhaust and cooling heat.
- [ ] **MARPOL Annex VI** governs SOx emissions; **ECA regions** require ultra-low-sulfur fuel or scrubbers.
- [ ] **Centrifugal purifier** operates at **6,000–8,000 RPM**; removes **95% solids, 99% water**.
- [ ] **Cetane Number:** Distillate fuels (**40–55**) ignite better than residual fuels (**20–35**).
- [ ] **For KTU numericals**, always:
  1. Convert all units to SI (kg, m³, kJ, hours).
  2. State the formula before substitution.
  3. Use NCV, not GCV.
  4. Include the 3600 conversion factor in efficiency equations.
  5. Show intermediate steps for partial marking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fresh Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fresh Water** in the context of fishing vessel design refers to the potable (drinkable) and domestic-quality water stored, generated, treated, and distributed aboard a fishing vessel to meet the daily physiological, hygienic, culinary, and auxiliary operational needs of the crew, the catch-handling process, and certain auxiliary machinery systems during a voyage.

According to the **IMO (International Maritime Organization)** standards and **WHO (World Health Organization)** Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality, fresh water aboard a vessel must conform to microbiological, chemical, and physical parameters equivalent to those applied to shore-based municipal water supplies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Phrasing Required in Exams):**
> *"Fresh water on a fishing vessel constitutes the stored and continuously replenished supply of potable water essential for human consumption, food preparation, personal hygiene, medical purposes, and selected auxiliary shipboard applications, governed by minimum statutory per capita consumption rates and total autonomy-day requirements as specified by classification societies and flag-state regulations."*

## 1.2 Classification of Fresh Water Onboard

Fresh water onboard a fishing vessel is broadly classified into the following functional categories:

| Category | Purpose | Typical Purity Standard |
|----------|---------|------------------------|
| **Potable Water** | Drinking, cooking | WHO drinking-water standard |
| **Domestic (Wash) Water** | Personal hygiene, laundry | Bacteriologically safe |
| **Galley Water** | Food preparation, dishwashing | Same as potable |
| **Medical Water** | Hospital use, first aid | Distilled / sterilized |
| **Technical Fresh Water** | Boiler make-up, cooling | Chemically treated, de-mineralized |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Vessel as a Floating Apartment Building":**
> 
> Imagine a small floating apartment building with 30 tenants (the crew) that operates for 14 days without ever docking at a municipal water tap. To keep the tenants alive, hygienic, and productive, the building must:
> 1. **Carry its own water supply** (like a roof water tank in a building) — this is the **fresh water storage tank**.
> 2. **Generate new water from seawater while at sea** — like a building with its own desalination plant — this is the **fresh water generator (FWG)**.
> 3. **Purify and disinfect every drop** — like a water purifier with UV/RO filters — this is the **potable water treatment unit (PWTU)**.
> 4. **Distribute through pipes at controlled pressure** — like a building booster pump — this is the **hydrophoric system**.
> 
> The fishing vessel is essentially a self-sustained micro-city, and fresh water is its most critical survival resource after air.

## 1.4 Sources of Fresh Water Onboard a Fishing Vessel

The two principal sources are:

1. **Shore-Supplied Fresh Water (Bunker Water)**: Loaded at port from municipal supply into dedicated storage tanks before sailing. This is the primary source for short fishing voyages.

2. **Sea Water Conversion (Onboard Generation)**:
   - **Evaporation / Distillation**: Using engine jacket cooling heat in a **fresh water generator (FWG)** — essentially a shell-and-tube evaporator boiling seawater under vacuum.
   - **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**: Forcing seawater through semi-permeable membranes at high pressure.
   - **Solar Distillation**: Used on very small artisanal craft in tropical regions.

## 1.5 Key Regulatory Bodies and Standards

- **IMO MARPOL Annex IV** — addresses sewage, indirectly references fresh water management
- **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th Edition, 2017)** — defines the 200+ parameters
- **ISO 19458:2006** — sampling for microbiological analysis
- **ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006** — sets minimum per-capita water quantity per day
- **PESO / Classification Society Rules (IRS, DNV, BV, LR)** — flag-state technical compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Minimum (ILO/MLC 2006 & KTU Reference Standard):**
> The minimum quantity of **fresh water** to be provided per person per day on a fishing vessel shall be **not less than 150 litres** for all purposes combined, of which at least **2 litres per person per day** must be **potable drinking water**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Daily Fresh Water Consumption Rates

Fresh water demand on a fishing vessel is calculated based on **per capita consumption**, multiplied by **crew complement** and **voyage autonomy**.

### Standard Consumption Break-Up (per person per day)

| Use Category | Quantity (Litres / person / day) |
|--------------|----------------------------------|
| Drinking water | 2.0 – 3.0 |
| Cooking / galley | 5.0 – 8.0 |
| Personal washing | 25.0 – 40.0 |
| Laundry | 10.0 – 20.0 |
| Flush sanitation | 15.0 – 25.0 |
| Hospital / medical | 5.0 – 10.0 |
| Cleaning of deck, fish hold, accommodation | 20.0 – 30.0 |
| **Total** | **≈ 150 L** |

For **fishing operations specifically**, additional water is needed for:
- **Catch washing and gutting**: 5–10 L per kg of fish handled
- **Hold cleaning and brine preparation**: 50–100 L per day per hold
- **Refrigeration make-up**: 20–50 L per day

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — The Five Governing Formulas

### Formula 1: Total Daily Fresh Water Requirement

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; N \cdot q_{pc} \;+\; Q_{aux}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{d}$ = total daily fresh water requirement in litres/day
- $N$ = number of crew (persons)
- $q_{pc}$ = per capita consumption in litres/person/day (≈ 150 L)
- $Q_{aux}$ = auxiliary water for catch, hold, refrigeration in litres/day

### Formula 2: Storage Tank Capacity (Reserve Capacity)

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; k \cdot Q_{d} \cdot T_{aut}
$$

Where:
- $V_{tank}$ = usable volume of fresh water storage tank (litres)
- $k$ = safety / reserve factor (typically **1.10 to 1.25**)
- $Q_{d}$ = daily fresh water requirement (litres/day)
- $T_{aut}$ = voyage autonomy in days (time between port calls)

### Formula 3: Fresh Water Generator (FWG) Capacity

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{Q_{d}}{24 \cdot \eta_{FWG}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{FWG}$ = instantaneous production rate of fresh water generator (litres/hour)
- $Q_{d}$ = daily consumption (litres/day)
- $\eta_{FWG}$ = effective running-time efficiency (typically **0.6 to 0.8**, since FWG runs only when main engine is hot and stable)

### Formula 4: Reverse Osmosis Membrane Capacity

$$
Q_{RO} \;=\; A_{m} \cdot J_{w} \;=\; A_{m} \cdot A \cdot (\Delta P \;-\; \Delta \pi)
$$

Where:
- $Q_{RO}$ = permeate flow (litres/day)
- $A_{m}$ = effective membrane area (m²)
- $J_{w}$ = water flux (L/m²·day)
- $A$ = membrane permeability constant
- $\Delta P$ = applied hydraulic pressure (typically **55–70 bar** for seawater RO)
- $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (≈ **25–27 bar** for seawater at 35 ppt)

### Formula 5: Heat-Balance for Evaporator-Type FWG

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}_{jacket}}{h_{fg,sw} \;+\; c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{fw}$ = mass flow rate of distillate (kg/h)
- $\dot{Q}_{jacket}$ = heat available from engine jacket cooling (kJ/h)
- $h_{fg,sw}$ = latent heat of vaporization of seawater at operating pressure (≈ **2357 kJ/kg** at 70°C)
- $c_{p,sw}$ = specific heat of seawater (≈ **4.0 kJ/kg·K**)
- $\Delta T_{sw}$ = seawater temperature rise through evaporator

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the limiting voyage period.** The longest expected trip between fresh-water bunkering ports defines the autonomy $T_{aut}$.

**Step 2 — Count the crew and special consumers.** Passengers, technicians, and high-intensity galley use scale the per-capita demand.

**Step 3 — Sum demand across categories.** Use Formula 1 — never assume average across all categories.

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor.** Use Formula 2 — the safety factor $k$ accounts for wastage, leakage, tank ullage, and emergency reserve.

**Step 5 — Match FWG capacity to demand.** Use Formula 3 — ensure the FWG can replenish the daily consumption within one day of continuous running.

**Step 6 — Validate quality.** Distillate TDS must be ≤ **500 mg/L** for domestic use, ≤ **100 mg/L** for potable; WHO recommends **≤ 50 mg/L** for taste preference.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Naval Architects**: Determining $V_{tank}$ directly affects tank sizing, LCG/VCG calculations, stability, and subdivision.
- **For Marine Engineers**: Specifying $C_{FWG}$ dictates auxiliary heat exchanger selection, jacket cooling-water diversion, and electrical load for RO pumps.
- **For Shipyard Project Managers**: Drives piping system layout, hydrophore tank sizing, and material selection (typically **stainless steel 316L** for potable water contact surfaces to prevent corrosion and contamination).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** On modern fishing vessels, approximately **70–80%** of fresh water demand is met by onboard generation (FWG), and only **20–30%** is shore-loaded. This drastically reduces port-turnaround time and extends fishing range.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Total Daily Requirement with Auxiliary Demand

### Problem Statement
A deep-sea fishing trawler operates with the following parameters:
- Crew complement $N = 35$ persons
- Voyage autonomy $T_{aut} = 20$ days
- Per capita consumption $q_{pc} = 150$ L/person/day
- Auxiliary requirement: hold washing = 200 L/day, catch handling = 150 L/day, refrigeration make-up = 80 L/day
- Safety factor $k = 1.15$

Find:
1. Total daily fresh water requirement $Q_d$
2. Storage tank capacity $V_{tank}$
3. FWG production rate $C_{FWG}$ (assuming 18 hours of effective running per day, $\eta_{FWG} = 18/24 = 0.75$)

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1: Sum the auxiliary consumptions**

$$
Q_{aux} \;=\; Q_{hold} \;+\; Q_{catch} \;+\; Q_{refrig}
$$

$$
Q_{aux} \;=\; 200 \;+\; 150 \;+\; 80
$$

$$
Q_{aux} \;=\; 430 \text{ litres/day}
$$

**Step 2: Compute crew consumption using Formula 1**

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; N \cdot q_{pc} \;+\; Q_{aux}
$$

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; (35 \cdot 150) \;+\; 430
$$

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; 5250 \;+\; 430
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{d} \;=\; 5680 \text{ litres/day}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute storage tank volume using Formula 2**

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; k \cdot Q_{d} \cdot T_{aut}
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 1.15 \cdot 5680 \cdot 20
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 1.15 \cdot 113600
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{tank} \;=\; 130640 \text{ litres} \;\approx\; 130.64 \text{ m}^3}
$$

In metric tons (since fresh water density $\rho_{fw} \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
V_{tank,mass} \;=\; 130640 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 130.64 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4: Compute FWG hourly production using Formula 3**

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{Q_{d}}{24 \cdot \eta_{FWG}}
$$

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{5680}{24 \cdot 0.75}
$$

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{5680}{18}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{FWG} \;=\; 315.56 \text{ litres/hour}}
$$

**Step 5: Select standard FWG size**

Round up to the next commercially available size (typically 350 L/h or 400 L/h for a 35-person trawler).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — FWG Heat Balance

### Problem Statement
A jacket-cooling-water-fed fresh water generator receives $\dot{Q}_{jacket} = 300{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$ of waste heat from the main engine. Seawater enters at $35°C$ and leaves the condenser at $50°C$. Find the distillate production rate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify properties**

Latent heat of seawater vaporization at evaporator pressure (assume 70°C, slight vacuum): $h_{fg,sw} = 2357 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Specific heat of seawater: $c_{p,sw} = 4.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

Temperature rise of seawater through evaporator: $\Delta T_{sw} = 50 - 35 = 15 \text{ K}$.

**Step 2: Apply Formula 5**

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}_{jacket}}{h_{fg,sw} \;+\; c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{300000}{2357 \;+\; 4.0 \cdot 15}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{300000}{2357 \;+\; 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{300000}{2417}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{fw} \;\approx\; 124.12 \text{ kg/h} \;\approx\; 124 \text{ L/h}}
$$

**Step 3: Daily distillate output at 20 hours/day operation**

$$
Q_{distillate,daily} \;=\; 124 \cdot 20 \;=\; 2480 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 4: Compare to demand**

Daily requirement was $Q_d = 5680$ L/day, so the FWG supplies only $2480/5680 \approx 43.7\%$ of demand. The remainder must be supplemented by shore-bunker water or a larger FWG.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FWG vs RO

| Parameter | Fresh Water Generator (FWG) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Energy source | Waste heat (engine jacket) | Electrical (high-pressure pump) |
| Operating cost | Very low (uses waste heat) | Moderate (electricity) |
| Capital cost | Low to moderate | High |
| Output TDS (mg/L) | ≤ 10 (very pure) | 200–500 |
| Membrane / element life | N/A (no membrane) | 3–5 years |
| Salt rejection required | None (phase change) | 99.2–99.8% |
| Maintenance | Heat exchanger cleaning | Membrane cleaning/replacement |
| Suitability for trawler | Excellent (waste heat abundant) | Good (if heat unavailable) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Fresh Water System on a Fishing Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source A: Shore Bunker Supply at Port] --> B[Filling Manifold with Cross-Connection Check Valve]
    C[Source B: Sea Water Inlet via Sea Chest] --> D[Sea Water Strainer and Flow Control]
    D --> E[Fresh Water Generator - Vacuum Evaporator]
    D --> F[Reverse Osmosis Plant Optional]
    B --> G[Fresh Water Storage Tank 1 - Domestic]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    B --> H[Fresh Water Storage Tank 2 - Reserve]
    G --> I[Potable Water Treatment Unit - UV and Chlorination]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Hydrophore Tank with Pressure Pump]
    J --> K[Distribution Manifold]
    K --> L[Galley and Pantry]
    K --> M[Wash Basins and Showers]
    K --> N[Hospital]
    K --> O[WC Flushing]
    K --> P[Catch Washing Station on Deck]
    K --> Q[Refrigeration Plant Make-Up]
    G --> R[Tank Level Sensors and Telemetry]
    R --> S[Bridge and ECR Monitoring Display]
    G --> T[Drain and Overboard Discharge with Non-Return]
```

### Node Identification Legend

- **Source A** = Port supply
- **Source B** = Sea water intake (sea chest)
- **FWG** = Fresh Water Generator (evaporator)
- **RO** = Reverse Osmosis
- **PWTU** = Potable Water Treatment Unit
- **Hydrophore** = Pressurized accumulator tank for steady distribution pressure
- **ECR** = Engine Control Room

## 4.2 Functional Flow Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Sequence:**
> 
> 1. At port, **Source A** fills tanks 1 and 2 via cross-connection-protected manifold.
> 2. At sea, **Source B** feeds seawater through strainer into the **FWG**, where jacket-cooling waste heat boils it under vacuum (typically 0.2–0.4 bar absolute).
> 3. Pure water vapor condenses on the cooler side and is collected as distillate.
> 4. Both bunker water and distillate collect in **Storage Tank 1** (domestic).
> 5. Water passes through the **PWTU** (UV + chlorination) before entering the **Hydrophore system**.
> 6. The **Hydrophore** maintains steady 2.5–4.0 bar pressure for distribution to all consumers.
> 7. **Tank level sensors** provide real-time data to the bridge and ECR.

## 4.3 Schematic — Hydrophore Pressure Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Water Tank] --> B[Suction Strainer]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Feed Pump]
    C --> D[Hydrophore Pressure Vessel]
    D --> E[Pressure Switch - Cut-in 3.0 bar]
    D --> F[Pressure Switch - Cut-out 4.5 bar]
    D --> G[Distribution Pipe to Consumers]
    E --> C
    F --> C
    H[Air Make-Up Valve] --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Hydrophore Tank?**
> Without the hydrophore, every time a tap opens, the main pump would cycle on/off hundreds of times per day, causing motor burnout. The hydrophore acts as a **hydraulic accumulator** — its pre-charged air cushion (typically at 2.0 bar) maintains constant downstream pressure, allowing the pump to run only intermittently.
