# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: Define "fresh water" as applied to a fishing vessel. State any TWO statutory requirements for fresh water quality aboard ship.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fresh water on a fishing vessel** is the potable-quality water stored, generated and distributed on board to meet the physiological, hygienic, culinary and auxiliary needs of the crew and operations during a voyage, conforming to WHO drinking-water quality guidelines.
>
> **Two statutory requirements:**
> 1. **Microbiological safety** — Total coliform count must be **0 CFU per 100 mL** in any 100 mL sample; E. coli must be absent.
> 2. **Chemical quality** — Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) should not exceed **500 mg/L** (WHO guideline value); pH should lie between **6.5 and 8.5**.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two valid requirements. Naming two parameters without linking to the standard will fetch only 0.5 marks each.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**Q: List FOUR sources of fresh water generation on board a fishing vessel. Which is the most energy-efficient?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Four sources:**
> 1. Shore-bunker water (loaded at port).
> 2. Evaporation / distillation via fresh water generator (FWG) using jacket-cooling heat.
> 3. Reverse osmosis (RO) using high-pressure electric pump.
> 4. Solar distillation (artisanal vessels in tropical waters).
>
> **Most energy-efficient:** The **fresh water generator (FWG)** — because it utilizes **waste heat** from the main engine jacket cooling, which would otherwise be dumped overboard. Its effective energy input (other than pumping) is essentially zero.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 2 Choice 1]**

**A deep-sea fishing trawler has the following particulars:**
- Crew complement $N = 40$
- Voyage autonomy $T_{aut} = 25$ days
- Per capita consumption $q_{pc} = 160$ L/person/day
- Hold washing: 250 L/day
- Catch washing: 200 L/day
- Refrigeration make-up: 100 L/day
- Safety factor $k = 1.20$

**(a)** Calculate the **total daily fresh water requirement** and the **storage tank capacity** in litres. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the **fresh water generator (FWG) hourly production rate** assuming 20 hours of effective running per day. If standard FWG modules come in sizes of 250, 300, 350, 400 and 500 L/h, select the appropriate size and justify. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Step 1: Compute total auxiliary demand

$$
Q_{aux} \;=\; Q_{hold} \;+\; Q_{catch} \;+\; Q_{refrig}
$$

$$
Q_{aux} \;=\; 250 \;+\; 200 \;+\; 100 \;=\; 550 \text{ L/day}
$$

Step 2: Compute daily fresh water requirement (1 Mark)

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; N \cdot q_{pc} \;+\; Q_{aux}
$$

$$
Q_{d} \;=\; 40 \cdot 160 \;+\; 550 \;=\; 6400 \;+\; 550
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{d} \;=\; 6950 \text{ L/day}}
$$

Step 3: Compute storage tank capacity (1 Mark)

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; k \cdot Q_{d} \cdot T_{aut}
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 1.20 \cdot 6950 \cdot 25
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 1.20 \cdot 173750
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{tank} \;=\; 208500 \text{ L} \;\approx\; 208.5 \text{ m}^3}
$$

This is approximately **208.5 tonnes** of fresh water (since density of fresh water ≈ 1000 kg/m³).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1: Calculate effective efficiency (1 Mark)

$$
\eta_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{20}{24} \;=\; 0.833
$$

Step 2: Apply Formula 3 (1 Mark)

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{Q_{d}}{24 \cdot \eta_{FWG}}
$$

$$
C_{FWG} \;=\; \frac{6950}{24 \cdot 0.833} \;=\; \frac{6950}{20} \;=\; 347.5 \text{ L/h}
$$

Step 3: Select FWG size (2 Marks)

Since the computed demand is **347.5 L/h** and the nearest commercial standard sizes above this value are **350 L/h** and **400 L/h**:

- **350 L/h** is the closest match (only 0.7% excess capacity).
- **400 L/h** would provide 15% excess — useful for future crew increase or auxiliary expansion.

**Recommended selection: 350 L/h (or 400 L/h for design margin).**

Step 4: Justification (3 Marks)

The selected FWG of **350 L/h**, when operated for 20 hours daily, will produce:

$$
\text{Daily production} \;=\; 350 \cdot 20 \;=\; 7000 \text{ L/day}
$$

This satisfies the daily demand of **6950 L/day** with a margin of **50 L/day (~0.7%)**, while allowing for periodic maintenance downtime without affecting fresh water availability. Hence the **350 L/h** unit is the optimal economic and operational choice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Paper, Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a vacuum-type Fresh Water Generator (FWG)** commonly fitted on fishing vessels. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A freshwater generator uses **engine jacket cooling water** at 75°C supplying $\dot{Q}_{jacket} = 250{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$ of waste heat. Seawater enters the evaporator at 40°C and leaves at 55°C. Given $h_{fg,sw} = 2357 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and $c_{p,sw} = 4.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, calculate the **distillate production rate** in kg/h and L/h. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A **vacuum-type Fresh Water Generator (FWG)** is a shell-and-tube heat exchanger that boils seawater under reduced pressure to recover pure water vapor. Its working principle:

1. **Hot jacket cooling water** from the main engine enters the **tubes** of the evaporator.
2. **Sea water** is sprayed onto the **shell side**, falling as a thin film over the tube bundle.
3. A **vacuum pump** (or ejector) maintains the shell side at low absolute pressure (typically 0.2–0.4 bar), causing seawater to boil at **40–60°C** instead of 100°C.
4. The **pure water vapor** rises past a **demister** (to remove entrained brine droplets) and enters the **condenser** section, where it meets the **incoming cold seawater feed**.
5. The vapor condenses on the cold tubes and the liquid distillate drips into the **distillate tray**, from which it is pumped via a **salinity monitor** to the storage tank. If salinity exceeds **5 ppm**, the distillate is diverted back to the sea.
6. The concentrated **brine** is discharged overboard via an eductor using the vacuum.

**Advantages:**
- Uses **waste heat** — virtually free energy.
- Low operating temperature prevents scaling.
- Compact, lightweight, and reliable.

(Examiner may award marks for neat sketch showing: hot water inlet, vapor space, condenser, distillate outlet, brine overboard, vacuum connection.)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1: Identify parameters (1 Mark)

$\dot{Q}_{jacket} = 250{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$
$h_{fg,sw} = 2357 \text{ kJ/kg}$
$c_{p,sw} = 4.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
$\Delta T_{sw} = 55 - 40 = 15 \text{ K}$

Step 2: Compute denominator (1 Mark)

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; h_{fg,sw} \;+\; c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 2357 \;+\; 4.0 \cdot 15 \;=\; 2357 \;+\; 60 \;=\; 2417 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Step 3: Compute distillate flow rate (2 Marks)

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}_{jacket}}{h_{fg,sw} \;+\; c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{250000}{2417} \;\approx\; 103.43 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Step 4: Convert to volumetric flow (3 Marks)

Since fresh water density $\rho_{fw} \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1 \text{ kg/L}$:

$$
\dot{V}_{fw} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{fw}}{\rho_{fw}} \;=\; \frac{103.43}{1} \;\approx\; 103.43 \text{ L/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{fw} \;\approx\; 103.4 \text{ kg/h} \;\equiv\; 103.4 \text{ L/h}}
$$

Step 5: Daily production at 20 h/day operation

$$
Q_{distillate,daily} \;=\; 103.4 \cdot 20 \;\approx\; 2068 \text{ L/day}
$$

This is sufficient to support approximately **12–13 crew members** at the standard 150 L/person/day rate, making this FWG size appropriate for a small to medium fishing trawler.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the auxiliary demand** $Q_{aux}$ — Many students only calculate $N \cdot q_{pc}$ and miss hold washing, catch handling, and refrigeration make-up. This leads to a **15–25% under-design** and **immediate 2–3 mark deduction**.
> 
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor $k$** — The storage tank must always include $k = 1.10$ to $1.25$. Direct multiplication of $Q_d \cdot T_{aut}$ without $k$ results in a non-compliant design per ILO/MLC 2006 and **costs 1–2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $Q_d$ (daily demand) with $C_{FWG}$ (hourly production)** — $C_{FWG}$ must include the duty factor $\eta_{FWG}$. Writing $C_{FWG} = Q_d / 24$ without $\eta$ is a common pitfall.
> 
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** — Students often mix $m^3$, litres, and tonnes. Remember: $1 \text{ m}^3$ of fresh water = **1000 L = 1 tonne**.
> 
> 5. **Drawing the FWG sketch without labels** — Unlabeled sketches fetch 0 marks; always label at least: hot water inlet, vapor space, condenser, distillate outlet, brine outlet, vacuum connection.

---

> - [x] **FWG Production Formula**: $C_{FWG} = Q_d / (24 \cdot \eta_{FWG})$.
> - [x] **Heat Balance Formula**: $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{Q}_{jacket} / (h_{fg} + c_p \cdot \Delta T)$.
> - [x] **WHO TDS Limit**: ≤ **500 mg/L** (general), ≤ **50 mg/L** (preferred).
> - [x] **FWH Vacuum Operating Pressure**: 0.2–0.4 bar absolute.
> - [x] **Material**: Stainless steel **316L** for potable water tanks and pipes.
> - [x] **Hydrophore Pressure**: Cut-in 3.0 bar / Cut-out 4.5 bar.
> - [x] **Two principal sources**: Shore bunker water + onboard generation (FWG/RO).
> - [x] **Most energy-efficient source**: FWG (uses waste heat from main engine jacket).
> - [x] **Distillate salinity alarm**: Divert back to sea if **TDS > 5 ppm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ballast Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ballast Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ Ballast Tanks — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ballast Tank (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A **ballast tank** is a partially or fully enclosed compartment within a vessel's hull that can be deliberately flooded with seawater (or other liquid) or pumped dry to adjust the vessel's **draft, trim, list, and transverse stability**. In fishing vessels, ballast tanks are strategically located in regions that do not compromise the fish hold volume — typically in the **forepeak, afterpeak, double bottom, and wing (side) tanks**.

According to the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and classification societies (IRS — Indian Register of Shipping, Class NK, Lloyd's Register), ballast systems must ensure:
- Adequate **propeller immersion** for safe navigation in light/ballast condition.
- Sufficient **GM (metacentric height)** after applying **free surface correction (GG')**.
- Quick filling/emptying via dedicated ballast pumps.

---

## 🌊 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suitcase Packing" Intuition

Imagine you are carrying an **empty suitcase** (the fishing vessel returning from a fishing trip with a partially empty fish hold). The suitcase is light, tips backward, and the wheels (propeller) lift off the ground.

To make it stable, you place **heavy stones at the bottom and ends** (water inside ballast tanks). Now the suitcase:
- Sits flat (corrected trim).
- Wheels touch the ground (proper propeller immersion).
- Does not wobble when you push sideways (improved stability).

That is exactly what ballast tanks do — they act as **adjustable weights** placed at strategic positions in the hull.

---

## 🎯 Primary Functions of Ballast Tanks in Fishing Vessels

| Function | Real-World Reason |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Draft Control** | Prevents hull from riding too high in light condition |
| **Trim Correction** | Keeps vessel level fore-and-aft for fuel efficiency |
| **List Correction** | Counteracts side pull from trawl warps, nets, or wind |
| **Stability Enhancement** | Lowers centre of gravity (KG), increases GM |
| **Propeller/Rudder Immersion** | Ensures safe manoeuvring in shallow or loaded conditions |
| **Counter Fishing Loads** | Balances heeling moment from single-side trawling |

---

## ⚙️ Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater:** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
- **Density of freshwater:** **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific weight of seawater:** **γ_sw = 10.05 kN/m³**
- **Typical ballast pump capacity:** **50–200 m³/hr** for small fishing vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 2, ballast tank design is studied alongside **Intake of Sea Water, General Arrangement of Piping, and Bilge System** — these are interconnected systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚓ Ballast Tanks — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Types of Ballast Tanks in Fishing Vessels

### 1️⃣ Forepeak Tank (FPT)
- Located at the **bow**, forward of collision bulkhead.
- Used to **counter bow rise** when fish hold is empty.
- Helps maintain propeller immersion when vessel runs **light from stern** (returning from fishing grounds).

### 2️⃣ Afterpeak Tank (APT)
- Located **aft**, near the propeller shaft tunnel.
- Prevents excessive **stern squat** and keeps propeller submerged.
- Often doubles as **lubricating oil tank** in small trawlers.

### 3️⃣ Double Bottom Tanks (DBT)
- Span the **entire length of machinery + fish hold region**.
- Most commonly used for ballast due to **low position** (lowers VCG effectively).
- Height typically **0.6 m to 1.2 m** in small fishing vessels.

### 4️⃣ Wing / Side Ballast Tanks (SBT)
- Located on **port and starboard sides** of fish hold.
- Effective for **list correction**.
- **Disadvantage:** Reduces fish hold capacity, hence used only in vessels > 24 m.

---

## 🧮 The Core Physics Behind Ballasting

### Why Water as Ballast?

$$W_{ballast} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{tank} \cdot g$$

Because seawater is **dense, freely available, and removable**, it provides a controllable weight with zero logistics cost.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU 2024 ESE — these formulas appear repeatedly in Module 2 problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Weight of ballast water | $W_b = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$ | N (or tonnes-force) | ρ = 1025 kg/m³ for SW |
| 2 | Mass of ballast water | $m_b = \rho \cdot V$ | tonnes (if ρ in t/m³) | ρ_sw ≈ **1.025 t/m³** |
| 3 | Moment to Change Trim 1 cm (MCT) | $MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{100 \cdot GM_L}$ | t·cm | L = LBP, W = displacement |
| 4 | Trim change by transfer | $\Delta Trim = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{MCT}$ | cm | w = weight transferred, x = distance LCG shift |
| 5 | Free Surface Correction | $GG' = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{W}$ | m | i = transverse moment of inertia of free surface |
| 6 | Effective GM (corrected) | $GM_{eff} = GM - GG'$ | m | Must be **> 0.15 m** positive |
| 7 | Draft change due to ballast | $\Delta T = \dfrac{W_b}{A_W \cdot 100}$ | m | A_W = waterplane area |
| 8 | List angle from off-centre weight | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{GM \cdot W}$ | rad | y = transverse distance |
| 9 | Volume from weight | $V = \dfrac{W_b}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}$ | m³ | Direct tank sizing |
| 10 | Specific weight seawater | $\gamma_{sw} = 10.05$ | kN/m³ | Use for quick problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion:** 1 tonne of seawater occupies **0.9756 m³**. Memorize: $V = \dfrac{m}{1.025}$ for seawater ballast.

---

## 🧠 Step-by-Step Logic Flow — How to Correct Trim Using Ballast

**Step 1 → Identify Problem**
Vessel has **stern trim** (draft aft > draft forward) due to light fish hold forward.

**Step 2 → Choose Tank**
Fill **forepeak tank** to add weight at bow.

**Step 3 → Calculate Weight Needed**
Use trim correction equation:
$$\Delta Trim\ (cm) = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{MCT\ (per\ cm)}$$

**Step 4 → Verify Stability**
After filling, ensure $GM_{eff} = GM - GG' > 0.15\ m$.

**Step 5 → Verify Pump Capacity**
Time to fill = $\dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{pump}}$ where $Q_{pump}$ is in m³/hr.

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Efficiency:** Properly trimmed vessel reduces resistance by 5–15%.
- **Safety:** IMO Stability Code (IS Code) requires positive GM in all ballast conditions.
- **Operational Speed:** Adequate propeller immersion prevents **cavitation** at high RPM.
- **Catch Preservation:** Maintains vessel motion smoothness, preventing damage to fish in hold.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Module 2 also covers **Sea Water Intakes, Bilge System, and Sanitary System** — the ballast piping shares sea suction lines with these systems through cross-over valves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ⚓ Ballast Tanks — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Derivation 1 — Volume of Ballast Tank for Given Weight

### Problem Setup
A fishing trawler requires **15 tonnes of ballast water** for trim correction. Find the volume needed, considering both freshwater and seawater.

### Mathematical Derivation

We start from the fundamental mass-density relationship:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

Solving for volume:

$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

---

**Case 1 — Using Seawater (ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³ = 1.025 t/m³):**

$$V_{sw} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{15\ t}{1.025\ t/m^3}$$

$$V_{sw} = \dfrac{15}{1.025}$$

$$V_{sw} = 14.634\ m^3$$

---

**Case 2 — Using Freshwater (ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³ = 1.000 t/m³):**

$$V_{fw} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{fw}} = \dfrac{15\ t}{1.000\ t/m^3}$$

$$V_{fw} = 15.000\ m^3$$

---

### 🧠 Analytical Logic Table

| Step | Reasoning | Result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Identify parameter | Ballast mass m = 15 t | Known |
| Choose fluid | Vessel in coastal waters → seawater | ρ = 1.025 t/m³ |
| Apply formula | $V = m / \rho$ | Substitution |
| Calculate | $15 \div 1.025$ | 14.634 m³ |
| Convert for freshwater comparison | If accidentally filled with freshwater | 15.000 m³ (more vol, less mass) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Freshwater provides **less mass per cubic meter** than seawater. If a tank is designed for 14.634 m³ of seawater but filled with freshwater, the vessel will be **under-ballasted by ~2.4%** — critical for stability calculations.

---

## 📐 Derivation 2 — Trim Correction Using Ballast Transfer

### Problem Setup
A 28 m fishing vessel with displacement **W = 180 tonnes**, length **LBP = 26 m**, longitudinal GM **GM_L = 60 m**, has an unwanted **stern trim of 30 cm**. The crew proposes to **transfer ballast from aft peak tank to forepeak tank**. The LCG of these tanks is **x = 18 m** apart.

Find the **mass of ballast water to be transferred**.

### Step 1 — Compute MCT (Moment to Change Trim per cm)

$$MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L}$$

$$MCT = \dfrac{180 \cdot 26}{100 \cdot 60}$$

$$MCT = \dfrac{4680}{6000}$$

$$MCT = 0.78\ t \cdot m / cm$$

---

### Step 2 — Use Trim Transfer Equation

$$\Delta Trim\ (cm) = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{MCT\ (per\ cm)}$$

We need $\Delta Trim = +30\ cm$ (correcting stern trim → even keel or bow trim).

$$30 = \dfrac{w \cdot 18}{0.78}$$

Solving for w:

$$w = \dfrac{30 \cdot 0.78}{18}$$

$$w = \dfrac{23.4}{18}$$

$$w = 1.30\ tonnes$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert to Volume (Seawater)

$$V = \dfrac{w}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{1.30}{1.025}$$

$$V = 1.268\ m^3$$

---

### Step 4 — Verify Pump Time

If ballast pump capacity = 30 m³/hr:

$$t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1.268}{30}\ hr = 0.0423\ hr = 2.54\ minutes$$

---

### 🧠 Analytical Logic Table

| Step | Reasoning | Calculation | Result |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Compute MCT | Resistance to trim change | $180 \times 26 / (100 \times 60)$ | 0.78 t·m/cm |
| Apply trim eq. | Force × distance = restoring moment | $w = 30 \times 0.78 / 18$ | w = 1.30 t |
| Convert to volume | Density-based volume | $1.30 / 1.025$ | V = 1.268 m³ |
| Pump time | Practical feasibility | $1.268 / 30 \times 60$ | ~2.54 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always state **units clearly** in each step — losing marks on unit mismatch is the #1 cause of deduction in numerical problems.

---

## 📐 Derivation 3 — Free Surface Effect (FSE) Reduction in GM

### Problem Setup
A fishing vessel has **GM = 0.65 m**. A double-bottom ballast tank with **length = 8 m**, **breadth = 4 m** is partially filled with seawater. Find the effective GM.

### Step 1 — Transverse Moment of Inertia of Free Surface

For a rectangular surface:

$$i_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

$$i_T = \dfrac{8 \cdot 4^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 64}{12} = \dfrac{512}{12}$$

$$i_T = 42.667\ m^4$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute GG' (Free Surface Correction)

Assuming displacement W = 180 t:

$$GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot i_T}{W} = \dfrac{1.025 \cdot 42.667}{180}$$

$$GG' = \dfrac{43.733}{180}$$

$$GG' = 0.243\ m$$

---

### Step 3 — Effective GM

$$GM_{eff} = GM - GG' = 0.65 - 0.243$$

$$GM_{eff} = 0.407\ m$$

---

### Step 4 — Stability Check

$$\text{IS Code Requirement:}\ GM_{eff} > 0.15\ m$$

$$0.407\ m > 0.15\ m \quad ✓\ \text{PASS}$$

---

### 🧠 Analytical Logic Table

| Step | Action | Formula Used | Output |
|------|--------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | Compute waterplane inertia | $i_T = LB^3 / 12$ | 42.667 m⁴ |
| 2 | Apply FSE correction | $GG' = \rho i / W$ | 0.243 m |
| 3 | Subtract from GM | $GM_{eff} = GM - GG'$ | 0.407 m |
| 4 | Check IS Code compliance | $GM_{eff} > 0.15\ m$ | Compliant |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Mistake to Avoid:** Forgetting that **partially filled** tanks cause free surface effect. A **fully filled (100%)** or **completely empty (0%)** tank has **zero FSE**. Only partial filling (typically 20%–80%) is dangerous.

---

## 📊 Master Calculation Matrix — Ballast Tank Design Flow

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  KTU 2024 BALLAST TANK DESIGN THOUGHT-PROCESS MATRIX     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  INPUTS:  LBP, B, T, W, GM, desired Draft, Fluid type   │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 1 → Compute required W_ballast for target draft   │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 2 → Convert W_ballast to V_ballast (use ρ_sw)      │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 3 → Distribute V among tanks (FPT/APT/DBT/WBT)    │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 4 → Compute trim & list corrections                │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 5 → Apply Free Surface Correction (FSE)            │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 6 → Verify GM_eff > 0.15 m (IS Code)              │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  STEP 7 → Verify pump time and pipe diameters            │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  OUTPUT → Tank dimensions, capacities, pump specs        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ⚓ Ballast Tanks — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1 — Ballast Tank Layout in a Typical Fishing Vessel

```mermaid

    A[Fishing Vessel Hull Profile] --> B[Forepeak Tank - FPT]

    B1 --> H[Ballast Pump Network]
```

---

## 📊 Diagram 2 — Ballast System Process Flow


---

## 📊 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ballast System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INTAKE_MODULE
        S1[Sea Chest]

    end

    end

    end

```

---

## 📊 Diagram 4 — Trim Correction Logic Flow

```mermaid

    ST1[Start: Vessel in Ballast Condition] --> ST2{Trim Check}
    ST2 -->|Even Keel| ST3[No Action - Hold]
    ST2 -->|Stern Trim| ST4[Fill FPT or Empty APT]
    ST2 -->|Bow Trim| ST5[Empty FPT or Fill APT]
    ST2 -->|Port List| ST6[Fill Starboard Wing Tank]
    ST2 -->|Stbd List| ST7[Fill Port Wing Tank]

    ST4 --> ST8[Verify GM_eff]
    ST5 --> ST8
    ST6 --> ST8
    ST7 --> ST8

    ST8 -->|GM_eff below 0.15 m| ST9[Re-distribute or Reduce Fill]
    ST8 -->|GM_eff above 0.15 m| ST10[Action Approved]

    ST9 --> ST2
    ST10 --> ST11[End: Stability Confirmed]
```

---

## 📊 Diagram 5 — Block-Level Vessel Stability Integration

```mermaid
    BLT[Ballast Tanks] --> WGT[Total Weight W]

    DSPL --> DPR[Hydrostatic Draft]

    STAB -->|Pass| OK[IS Code Compliant]
    STAB -->|Fail| RT[Re-engineer Tank Layout]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These flow diagrams illustrate the **functional interconnections** of ballast subsystems rather than physical drawings, in compliance with KTU schematic standards for marine engineering courses.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ⚓ Ballast Tanks — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Ballast Tank" as applied to fishing vessels. List any FOUR functions of ballast tanks.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A ballast tank is a hull compartment that can be filled with or emptied of seawater to adjust the vessel's draft, trim, list, and stability.

**Four Functions (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Adjusts **draft** for safe navigation in light/ballast conditions.
2. Corrects **trim** (fore-and-aft level).
3. Corrects **list** caused by off-centre loads (e.g., trawl warps).
4. Maintains **propeller immersion** and improves **GM** for stability.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the phenomenon of "Free Surface Effect" in a partially filled ballast tank. State one method to minimize it.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Explanation (2 marks):**
When a ballast tank is **partially filled**, the free water surface shifts as the vessel rolls. This shift creates a **righting moment opposing the restoring moment**, effectively lowering the metacentric height (GM) by an amount $GG'$.

**Mathematical Relation:**
$$GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot i_T}{W}$$

**Method to Minimize (1 mark):**
- **Fill the tank completely** or **empty it fully** — partial filling should be avoided during critical operations.
- Use **swash bulkheads / wash plates** to reduce free surface moment of inertia $i_T$.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the various types of ballast tanks used in a fishing vessel. State their relative merits.**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Types of Ballast Tanks (5 marks):**

1. **Forepeak Tank (FPT)** — At the bow, forward of collision bulkhead. Used for trim control and bow rise correction.
2. **Afterpeak Tank (APT)** — Aft of engine room, near propeller. Used for propeller immersion and stern trim correction.
3. **Double Bottom Tank (DBT)** — Between inner bottom shell and bottom plating. Lowest point in vessel — best for lowering VCG.
4. **Wing/Side Ballast Tank (SBT)** — Port and starboard of fish hold. Used for list correction.

**Relative Merits Table (2 marks):**

| Tank Type | Best Use | Limitation |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| FPT | Bow trim | Small volume in small vessels |
| APT | Stern trim | May interfere with shaft |
| DBT | Stability (lowers VCG) | Height-limited (0.6-1.2 m) |
| SBT | List correction | Reduces fish hold capacity |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A fishing trawler of length LBP = 24 m, displacement W = 150 tonnes, has an unwanted stern trim of 25 cm. Calculate the weight of ballast to be transferred from APT to FPT (distance between LCG = 16 m) if $GM_L = 50\ m$. Also find the volume of seawater required.**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Step 1: Compute MCT (Moment to Change Trim per cm)** (2 marks)
$$MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L} = \dfrac{150 \cdot 24}{100 \cdot 50}$$
$$MCT = \dfrac{3600}{5000} = 0.72\ t \cdot m / cm$$

**Step 2: Apply Trim Correction Equation** (2 marks)
$$\Delta Trim = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{MCT}$$
$$25 = \dfrac{w \cdot 16}{0.72}$$
$$w = \dfrac{25 \cdot 0.72}{16} = \dfrac{18.0}{16}$$
$$w = 1.125\ tonnes$$

**Step 3: Convert Weight to Volume** (2 marks)
$$V = \dfrac{w}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{1.125}{1.025}$$
$$V = 1.098\ m^3$$

**Step 4: Result Statement** (1 mark)
Hence, **1.125 tonnes** of seawater (occupying **1.098 m³**) must be transferred from APT to FPT to correct the 25 cm stern trim.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the term "Free Surface Effect" with reference to partially filled ballast tanks. A double bottom ballast tank of length 10 m and breadth 5 m is 50% filled with seawater in a vessel of displacement 200 t and GM = 0.80 m. Find the effective GM and comment on stability.**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Free Surface Effect Concept (2 marks):**
FSE is the **loss of GM** caused by movement of partially-filled liquid. The free water surface acts as a "shifting pendulum," creating a heeling moment opposite to the restoring moment.

**Step 1: Compute Transverse Moment of Inertia** (2 marks)
$$i_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 5^3}{12} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 125}{12} = 104.167\ m^4$$

**Step 2: Compute GG'** (1 mark)
$$GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot i_T}{W} = \dfrac{1.025 \cdot 104.167}{200}$$
$$GG' = \dfrac{106.771}{200} = 0.534\ m$$

**Step 3: Effective GM** (1 mark)
$$GM_{eff} = GM - GG' = 0.80 - 0.534 = 0.266\ m$$

**Step 4: Stability Comment** (1 mark)
Since $GM_{eff} = 0.266\ m > 0.15\ m$, the vessel **passes** the IMO IS Code stability criterion, but the margin is small. Recommended to **fill completely** before sailing.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the piping arrangement for ballast system in a fishing vessel. Discuss the role of sea chest, ballast pump, valves, and level gauges.**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Piping Arrangement Components (each 1.75 marks × 4 = 7 marks):**

1. **Sea Chest** — Hull-penetrating inlet fitted with a **sea valve** to control flow and isolate in emergencies. Always located **forward** of the propeller for clean water intake.

2. **Ballast Pump** — Centrifugal, electrically driven, capacity typically **50–200 m³/hr** for fishing vessels. Connected to **suction and discharge manifolds** with valves directing flow to specific tanks.

3. **Valves (Cross-over, Sounding, Vent)** —
   - **Cross-over valves** allow interconnection with bilge system.
   - **Sounding valves** enable manual level measurement.
   - **Air vent valves** prevent vacuum during filling and overflow during discharge.

4. **Level Gauges / Sensors** —
   - **Pneumatic** or **electronic float-type** level sensors.
   - Provide **high-level alarms** to prevent overflow.
   - Linked to **bridge display** for real-time monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Confusing tonnes-force (1000 kgf) with tonnes-mass (1000 kg) — clarify by stating ρ × g separately.
> 2. **Forgetting FSE:** Students often calculate GM without subtracting GG' — this is the **single most common mark-loss** in KTU ESE.
> 3. **Wrong Density:** Using 1000 kg/m³ for seawater instead of **1025 kg/m³** — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 4. **No Mention of IS Code:** Always cite that $GM_{eff} > 0.15\ m$ is the IS Code requirement for vessels in unrestricted service.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams:** In Part B questions, **neat sketches** carry at least 1–2 marks — never omit them.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Ballast tank** = adjustable water weight compartment to control draft, trim, list, and stability.
- ✅ **Standard seawater density:** ρ_sw = **1025 kg/m³** (1.025 t/m³).
- ✅ **Key tanks:** Forepeak, Afterpeak, Double Bottom, Wing Tanks.
- ✅ **Most important formulas:**
  - $W_b = \rho V g$
  - $MCT = WL / (100 \cdot GM_L)$
  - $\Delta Trim = wx / MCT$
  - $GG' = \rho i / W$
  - $GM_{eff} = GM - GG'$
- ✅ **IS Code requirement:** $GM_{eff} > 0.15\ m$ at all times.
- ✅ **Free surface effect** applies only to **partially filled** tanks (20–80% filling).
- ✅ **Fully filled** or **fully empty** tanks → **zero FSE**.
- ✅ **Swash bulkheads / wash plates** reduce transverse $i_T$ by up to **70%**.
- ✅ **Typical pump capacity** for fishing vessels: **50–200 m³/hr**.
- ✅ **Sea chest** always placed **forward** of propeller for clean water.
- ✅ **Vessel trim correction:** Transfer ballast weight over long distance → larger moment → less mass needed.
- ✅ **Hull design rule:** Lower tank positions lower VCG → improve GM.
- ✅ **Connection to other Module 2 topics:** Ballast system shares sea suction with **bilge**, **fire**, and **sanitary systems** via cross-over valves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulkhead Positions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulkhead Positions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **bulkhead** in a fishing vessel is a vertical, structural partition wall running transversely (ship-shape or corrugated) between the shell plating and often up to the bulkhead deck. It is a key element of the **ship's subdivision** system, providing transverse strength, limiting flooding in case of hull damage, and separating functional zones such as fish holds, engine rooms, tanks, and accommodation spaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *watertight bulkhead* is a bulkhead constructed and tested to prevent the passage of water under a head corresponding to the worst operational condition. The *positions* of bulkheads are governed by statutory regulations (IMO Torremolinos Convention / IRS Rules) and by the principles of **damage stability and subdivision**.

## 1.2 Types of Bulkheads in Fishing Vessels

| Type | Location | Function |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Collision Bulkhead (CB)** | Forward-most watertight bulkhead | Prevents flooding in bow collision damage |
| **After Peak Bulkhead (APB)** | Aft-most watertight bulkhead | Protects rudder/shaft; seals after peak |
| **Engine Room (ER) Bulkhead(s)** | At machinery space boundaries | Isolates machinery; fire/flood barrier |
| **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** | At forward end of shaft tunnel | Prevents flooding from stern gland |
| **Bulkhead between Fish Holds** | Between adjacent fish compartments | Preserves catch quality, limits flooding |
| **Partial / Non-Watertight Bulkheads** | Various | Provide structural stiffening only |

> [!NOTE]
> Only **watertight bulkheads** count toward the vessel's **subdivision index**. Non-watertight bulkheads contribute only to local strength.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a passenger bus travelling through heavy rain. If the floor is divided into several sealed compartments (like sealed boxes), a single puncture or leak will flood only that box, while the rest of the bus continues to function safely. **Bulkheads act as these sealed compartments inside a ship's hull.**

In a fishing vessel:
- The **collision bulkhead** is like the front wall of the driver's cabin — protects the entire vessel if the bow is damaged.
- The **engine room bulkheads** are like firewalls around the engine bay — they contain oil, fire, or water leaks.
- The **fish hold bulkheads** are like separate cold-storage rooms — different species, temperatures, or brine treatments can be kept apart without cross-contamination.

## 1.4 Why Bulkhead Positions Matter in Fishing Vessels

Fishing vessels operate in **harsh sea conditions**, handle large **dynamic loads** (shooting/d hauling of nets, catch weight shifting), and rely heavily on the **fish hold** for cargo value. Correct bulkhead positioning ensures:

- **Damage survivability** (only part of vessel floods on collision)
- **Structural stiffness** (supports hull girder under bending)
- **Operational efficiency** (clean separation of chilled/frozen catch from engine heat)
- **Regulatory compliance** with **classification society** rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, students must know both the **names, positions, and functional significance** of bulkheads and the **subdivision rules** that govern their *placement along the length*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statutory Rules Governing Bulkhead Positions

### 2.1.1 Collision Bulkhead (Forward-most Watertight Bulkhead)
- **Position**: Located between **0.05 L to 0.08 L** from the Forward Perpendicular (FP), where **L = Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**.
- **Minimum absolute distance**: Generally **not less than 2.0 m** from FP for vessels above 30 m.
- **No openings allowed**: No doors, manholes, ventilation ducts, or any passage through the collision bulkhead below the bulkhead deck.
- The **stem** (bow structure) and any forward chain locker must lie ahead of it.

### 2.1.2 After Peak Bulkhead
- Positioned at the forward end of the **after peak tank**, just forward of the rudder trunk.
- Must be watertight and tested to a head of water up to the bulkhead deck.
- Shaft gland or stern tube must be forward of this bulkhead.

### 2.1.3 Engine Room Bulkhead(s)
- For **aft-engine arrangements** (most common in fishing vessels): the forward engine room bulkhead is usually at **0.55 L to 0.65 L** from FP.
- For **midship engine arrangement**: ER lies centrally with bulkheads forward and aft.
- For **forward engine arrangement** (rare in fishing vessels): ER bulkhead is near **0.3 L** from FP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fishing vessels almost universally use **aft-engine arrangements** because they leave the forward region free for the fish hold — the primary revenue-generating space.

### 2.1.4 Minimum Number of Watertight Bulkheads
| Vessel Length (LBP) | Minimum Watertight Bulkheads |
|---------------------|------------------------------|
| L < 30 m | 2 (CB + APB) |
| 30 m ≤ L < 60 m | 3 (CB + APB + 1 ER bulkhead) |
| 60 m ≤ L < 100 m | 4 (CB + APB + 2 ER bulkheads) |
| L ≥ 100 m | 5+ (as per Rule) |

## 2.2 Concept of Floodable Length

The **floodable length (L_f)** is the maximum length of a compartment which, when assumed completely flooded, will not submerge the **margin line** of the vessel.

$$\boxed{L_{f} = \frac{\text{Volume of buoyancy lost at margin line}}{\text{Average transverse area at margin line}}}$$

**Margin line** is drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the **bulkhead deck** at the side. It acts as a safety reference — water must not reach this line when any one compartment is fully flooded.

## 2.3 Factor of Subdivision (F)

The **factor of subdivision (F)** accounts for:
- Length of the vessel (longer vessels require more subdivision)
- Type of service (passenger < cargo in subdivision demand)
- Number of passengers or crew at risk

$$\boxed{F = \frac{P}{L_{f}}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Permissible length of compartment**
- $L_{f}$ = **Floodable length**
- Typical range: **F = 0.33 to 1.00**

## 2.4 Permissible Length Formula (KTU Master Equation)

The core equation governing bulkhead positions is:

$$\boxed{P = L_{f} \times F}$$

**Interpretation**: The actual length of any watertight compartment (between two consecutive watertight bulkheads) **must not exceed** this permissible length.

$$\text{Actual Length } l_{a} \;\leq\; P \;=\; L_{f} \cdot F$$

If $l_{a} > P$, the bulkhead positions are **NOT acceptable**, and additional bulkheads must be inserted.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $0.05\,L \leq x_{CB} \leq 0.08\,L$ | Position of collision bulkhead from FP | metres |
| 2 | $x_{ER} \approx (0.55 \text{ to } 0.65)\,L$ | Position of ER bulkhead (aft-engine) | metres |
| 3 | $L_{f} = V / A_{m}$ | Floodable length (volume / area at margin line) | metres |
| 4 | $P = L_{f} \cdot F$ | Permissible length of compartment | metres |
| 5 | $F \in [0.33,\; 1.00]$ | Factor of subdivision (typical range) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N_{BH} \geq \lceil L / P \rceil + 1$ | Minimum number of bulkheads required | count |
| 7 | $\text{Margin Line} = \text{Bulkhead Deck} - 76\,\text{mm}$ | Vertical offset of safety reference | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> For numerical KTU problems, students are usually given $L$, $L_f$, and $F$. They are expected to compute $P$ and compare with given actual compartment lengths.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The same subdivision philosophy is applied in:
- **Submarines** (every 8–12 m, with multiple pressure bulkheads)
- **Aircraft** (bulkheads between fuselage sections)
- **Offshore platforms** (compartment walls, blast walls)
- **Land-building basements** (water-stop walls)

In a fishing vessel, subdivision is doubly important because of:
1. **High risk of hull breach** from floating debris, ice, or grounding.
2. **Concentrated mass in fish hold** — failure to subdivide may shift weight and capsize vessel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example Problem (Worked Out for KTU Examination Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam - Type Problem]**
> A fishing trawler has the following particulars:
> - Length between perpendiculars, $L = 60\;\text{m}$
> - Floodable length (assumed constant), $L_f = 15\;\text{m}$
> - Factor of subdivision, $F = 0.55$
> - Proposed watertight bulkheads: 3, dividing the vessel into 4 compartments of lengths:
> **12 m, 25 m, 15 m, 8 m** (from FP to AP).
>
> Check whether the proposed bulkhead positions are satisfactory. If not, determine the minimum number of watertight bulkheads required.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Calculate the Permissible Length P

Using the master equation:

$$P = L_f \times F$$

Substituting the given values:

$$P = 15\;\text{m} \times 0.55$$

$$\boxed{P = 8.25\;\text{m}}$$

**Meaning:** No watertight compartment may have an actual length greater than **8.25 m**; otherwise the margin line will be submerged if that compartment is fully flooded.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Compare Actual Compartment Lengths with Permissible Length

| Compartment | Actual Length $l_a$ (m) | Permissible $P$ (m) | Verdict |
|-------------|------------------------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 (Forward) | 12.00 | 8.25 | **FAIL** ($l_a > P$) |
| 2 | 25.00 | 8.25 | **FAIL** |
| 3 | 25.00 | 8.25 | **FAIL** |
| 4 (Aft) | 8.00 | 8.25 | **PASS** ($l_a < P$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only the **after-most compartment** (8 m) satisfies the subdivision criterion. The forward compartments are unacceptably long.

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Determine the Minimum Number of Bulkheads Required

The total length to be subdivided (excluding the already fixed CB and APB) is:

$$L_{\text{useful}} = 60 - 2 \times 3 = 60 - 6 = 54\;\text{m}$$

(Taking CB ≈ 3 m from FP and APB ≈ 3 m from AP as standard allowances.)

Alternatively, treating the entire $L$ as subdivisible:

$$N_{\text{compartments, min}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60}{8.25} \right\rceil = \lceil 7.27 \rceil = 8$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\text{bulkheads, min}} = N_{\text{compartments}} - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{bulkheads, min}} = 7 \;\text{bulkheads}}$$

Out of these 7, **2 are fixed** (Collision Bulkhead and After Peak Bulkhead), and **5 are intermediate bulkheads** (typically 2–3 in the fish hold region and 2 at the engine room boundaries).

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Re-layout the Bulkheads (Revised Positions)

| Bulkhead # | Distance from FP (m) | Compartment Lengths (m) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| FP | 0 | — |
| CB (Collision) | 3.0 (= 0.05 L) | 3.0 |
| BH-2 | 11.0 | 8.0 |
| BH-3 | 19.0 | 8.0 |
| BH-4 (Fwd ER) | 27.0 | 8.0 |
| BH-5 (Aft ER) | 35.0 | 8.0 |
| BH-6 | 43.0 | 8.0 |
| APB (After Peak) | 57.0 | 14.0 *(wait — recheck)* |

**Recheck:** $60 - 8 \times 7 = 4\;\text{m}$. Recalibrate compartments so each ≤ 8.25 m.

| Bulkhead | Position from FP (m) | Compartment Length (m) | Check |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|-------|
| CB | 3.00 | 3.00 | OK |
| BH-2 | 11.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| BH-3 | 19.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| BH-4 (Fwd ER bulkhead) | 27.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| BH-5 (Aft ER bulkhead) | 35.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| BH-6 | 43.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| BH-7 | 51.00 | 8.00 | OK |
| APB | 57.00 | 6.00 | OK |

Each compartment length is now ≤ 8.25 m, satisfying the subdivision rule.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Step | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Value | Purpose |
|------|----------|--------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Permissible length | $P$ | $L_f \cdot F$ | Maximum allowed compartment length |
| 2 | Actual length | $l_a$ | Given | Length between two adjacent bulkheads |
| 3 | Check | $l_a \leq P$? | Inequality | Decides acceptance |
| 4 | Min bulkheads | $N_{BH}$ | $\lceil L / P \rceil - 1$ | Redesigns layout |
| 5 | Position of CB | $x_{CB}$ | $0.05\,L$ to $0.08\,L$ | Forward protection |
| 6 | Position of ER bulkhead | $x_{ER}$ | $0.55\,L$ to $0.65\,L$ (aft-engine) | Machinery isolation |

## 3.3 General Derivation of Floodable Length (Conceptual)

Starting from the principle of **Archimedes**, when a compartment floods, the vessel sinks until lost buoyancy equals added water weight. The margin line provides the upper limit. Equating volumes:

$$\rho \, g \, V_{\text{displaced, lost}} = \rho \, g \, A_{\text{transverse, margin}} \cdot L_f$$

Hence the average floodable length of a long compartment is:

$$L_f = \frac{V_{\text{flooded}}}{A_{\text{transverse at margin line}}}$$

In practice, $L_f$ is determined from **Admiralty/Class society curves** that plot $L_f$ as a function of **draft** and **block coefficient** of the vessel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bulkhead Arrangement Flow (Profile View of Fishing Vessel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FP - Forward Perpendicular"]
    B["Forward Peak Tank"]
    C["CB - Collision Bulkhead"]
    D["Chain Locker and Bow Thruster Room"]
    E["Bulkhead 2"]
    F["Fish Hold Compartment 1"]
    G["Bulkhead 3"]
    H["Fish Hold Compartment 2"]
    I["Forward ER Bulkhead"]
    J["Engine Room with Main Engine"]
    K["Shaft Tunnel"]
    L["After ER Bulkhead"]
    M["Aft Peak Tank"]
    N["AP - Aft Perpendicular"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L --> M --> N

    C -. "0.05 L from FP" .-> D
    I -. "0.55 L to 0.65 L from FP" .-> J
    L -. "Stern Tube Gland Ahead" .-> M
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Bulkhead Position Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START: Define Vessel LBP and Service Type"]
    s2["Determine Floodable Length Lf using Class Curves"]
    s3["Determine Factor of Subdivision F from Length and Service"]
    s4["Calculate Permissible Length P = Lf times F"]
    s5["Identify Functional Zones: Fish Hold, ER, Peak Tanks"]
    s6["Place Collision Bulkhead at 0.05 L to 0.08 L from FP"]
    s7["Place Engine Room Bulkheads at 0.55 L to 0.65 L from FP"]
    s8["Place After Peak Bulkhead at Stern Tube"]
    s9{"Check: Is every compartment length la less than or equal to P?"}
    s10["Layout Acceptable - Lock Design"]
    s11["Insert Additional Intermediate Bulkheads"]
    s12["Recompute Compartment Lengths"]
    s13["Recheck Inequality la less than or equal to P"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9
    s9 -- YES --> s10
    s9 -- NO --> s11 --> s12 --> s13 --> s9
```

## 4.3 Typical Cross-Section Showing Bulkhead Stiffening

> For full visual reference, a fishing vessel's transverse bulkhead is typically built with:
> - **Corrugated plating** (triangular or trapezoidal) for greater stiffness with less weight.
> - **Vertical stiffeners** at every corrugation trough.
> - **Horizontal girders** (stringers) at mid-height and at top/bottom.
> - **Gusset plates** at shell connections to transfer loads to the hull.

> [!NOTE]
> Bulkheads in fishing vessels often use **intercostal construction** (plate between frames) rather than **flush construction**, because of their modular nature and ease of pre-fabrication in the shipyard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q:** Define the term *floodable length* of a compartment and explain its significance in the positioning of bulkheads.

**Model Answer:**
Floodable length is defined as the maximum length of a watertight compartment which, when assumed completely flooded, will not submerge the margin line of the vessel.

$$L_f = \frac{V_{\text{flooded}}}{A_{\text{transverse at margin line}}}$$

**Significance in bulkhead positioning:**
1. It determines the **permissible length (P)** of any compartment via $P = L_f \cdot F$.
2. If the actual length between two bulkheads exceeds $P$, the vessel will sink beyond the margin line in damaged condition — **a hazard to survival**.
3. It is therefore the **primary regulatory input** for placing intermediate bulkheads.
4. Helps designers decide **how many bulkheads** are needed for a vessel of given LBP and service.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q:** State the rule for the position of the collision bulkhead in a fishing vessel. Why are no openings permitted below the bulkhead deck?

**Model Answer:**
**Rule:** The collision bulkhead shall be located at a distance of **not less than 0.05 L** and **not more than 0.08 L** from the Forward Perpendicular (FP), where $L$ is the length between perpendiculars. In any case, the distance must not be less than **2.0 m** for vessels above 30 m LBP.

**No openings permitted below bulkhead deck because:**
1. **Safety**: A bow collision could tear open any forward opening, allowing uncontrolled flooding.
2. **Watertight integrity**: The CB must remain a **continuous watertight barrier** from keel to bulkhead deck.
3. **Regulation**: IMO and IRS rules strictly prohibit doors, manholes, or pipes through the CB below bulkhead deck.
4. **Damage stability**: The CB is the **first line of defence** in collision; any breach defeats its purpose.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain with sketches the different types of watertight bulkheads used in a fishing vessel and their functional roles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel of LBP = 75 m has a floodable length of 18 m and a factor of subdivision F = 0.50. Determine (i) the permissible length of a compartment, and (ii) the minimum number of watertight bulkheads required. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Part (a) – Types of Watertight Bulkheads:**

1. **Collision Bulkhead** – Forward-most bulkhead; prevents flooding from bow damage; no openings allowed.
2. **After Peak Bulkhead** – Aft-most; protects rudder/shaft glands; seals after peak tank.
3. **Engine Room Bulkheads** – Forward and aft boundaries of machinery space; isolate fire/flooding.
4. **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** – At forward end of shaft tunnel; prevents water ingress from stern gland.
5. **Intermediate Bulkheads** – Subdivide fish holds; maintain catch quality and reduce damage stability risk.
6. **Cofferdam Bulkheads** – Between fuel tanks and fish hold/accommodation to prevent contamination.

(Sketch: Profile view showing all bulkhead positions from bow to stern — refer to SECTION 4 mermaid diagram.)

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 75\;\text{m}$, $L_f = 18\;\text{m}$, $F = 0.50$.

**(i) Permissible length:**
$$P = L_f \cdot F = 18 \times 0.50 = \boxed{9.00\;\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Minimum number of bulkheads:**
$$N_{\text{compartments, min}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{75}{9.00} \right\rceil = \lceil 8.33 \rceil = 9$$

$$N_{\text{bulkheads, min}} = 9 - 1 = \boxed{8 \;\text{bulkheads}}$$

Out of these 8, **2 are fixed** (CB and APB) and **6 are intermediate** (typically 3 in fish hold and 2 in engine room + 1 auxiliary).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]

**Q: (a)** What is meant by *factor of subdivision*? Discuss the variables that affect its value. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the typical arrangement of bulkheads in a 60 m fishing trawler with aft-engine layout. Clearly mark the position of each bulkhead from the FP. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Part (a) – Factor of Subdivision (F):**

The **factor of subdivision (F)** is a dimensionless number (typically 0.33 to 1.00) that, when multiplied by the floodable length, gives the permissible length of a compartment.

$$\boxed{P = L_f \cdot F}$$

**Variables affecting F:**
1. **Length of vessel (LBP):** Longer vessels have lower F because more subdivision is required.
2. **Service type:** Passenger vessels have lower F (0.33–0.50); cargo/fishing vessels have higher F (0.50–1.00).
3. **Margin line safety:** Defines submergence limit when compartment is flooded.
4. **Standard of subdivision:** Higher class notation → lower F → more bulkheads.
5. **Crew size and life-saving equipment availability.**

**Typical values of F for fishing vessels:** F = 0.45 to 0.65.

**Part (b) – Typical 60 m Trawler Arrangement (Aft-Engine Layout):**

| Bulkhead | Position from FP | Distance | Functional Role |
|----------|------------------|----------|-----------------|
| FP | 0 m | — | Reference |
| Collision Bulkhead (CB) | 3.0 m | 0.05 L | Bow protection |
| Bulkhead 2 | 11.0 m | — | Fish hold 1 |
| Bulkhead 3 | 19.0 m | — | Fish hold 2 |
| Bulkhead 4 | 27.0 m | — | Fish hold 3 |
| Fwd ER Bulkhead | 36.0 m | 0.60 L | Engine room forward |
| Aft ER Bulkhead | 44.0 m | — | Engine room aft |
| Bulkhead 6 | 52.0 m | — | Lazarette/Store |
| APB | 57.0 m | — | Stern protection |
| AP | 60.0 m | — | Reference |

**Sketch:** Profile view from bow (FP) to stern (AP) showing fish holds forward and engine room aft — exactly as depicted in the SECTION 4 mermaid diagram.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU bulkhead-position problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_f$ with $L$** – $L_f$ is floodable length, NOT vessel length. Many students substitute vessel length directly into the formula and lose full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** – When finding minimum bulkheads, use $\lceil L / P \rceil$, NOT plain division. Fractional compartments are not physically possible.
> 3. **Including non-watertight bulkheads** in the subdivision count – Only watertight bulkheads count for $F$.
> 4. **Placing the ER bulkhead forward of midship** for aft-engine vessels – This wastes prime fish hold space.
> 5. **Ignoring the unit of length** – Always state answers in metres with units.
> 6. **Skipping the inequality check** – Writing "P = 8.25 m" without comparing with actual compartment lengths loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Not stating "NO OPENINGS"** in collision bulkhead answer – Examiners look for this regulation keyword explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision Checklist for Bulkhead Positions:**

- ☐ **Bulkhead** = vertical transverse wall dividing the hull into compartments.
- ☐ **Collision bulkhead** = forward-most; position **0.05 L to 0.08 L from FP**; **NO openings** below bulkhead deck.
- ☐ **After peak bulkhead** = aft-most; **ahead of stern tube gland**.
- ☐ **Engine room bulkhead (aft-engine)** = at **0.55 L to 0.65 L from FP**.
- ☐ **Margin line** = **76 mm (3 inches)** below bulkhead deck at side.
- ☐ **Floodable length** $L_f$ = length whose flooding will not submerge margin line.
- ☐ **Factor of subdivision** $F$ = dimensionless; typically **0.33 to 1.00**.
- ☐ **Permissible length** $P = L_f \cdot F$.
- ☐ **Acceptance criterion**: actual compartment length $l_a \leq P$.
- ☐ **Minimum number of bulkheads**: $N = \lceil L / P \rceil - 1$.
- ☐ Fishing vessels use **aft-engine arrangement** to maximize forward fish hold space.
- ☐ **Watertight integrity tested** by hydrostatic test to the head of water up to bulkhead deck.
- ☐ Subdivision philosophy = **damage stability** + **structural stiffness** + **operational separation**.
- ☐ **Typical $F$ for fishing vessel** ≈ **0.50 to 0.65**.
- ☐ Always remember: **3 things** govern positions → **statutory rules, floodable length, functional zoning**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Resistance

In the context of fishing vessel design, **Resistance (R_T)** is defined as the force component acting opposite to the direction of motion of a vessel, generated by the fluid (water and air) through which the hull moves. It is the hydrodynamic drag that the engine must overcome to maintain a given service speed.

Mathematically, the **Total Resistance** is the sum of all the individual resistive force components:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A$$

where each term is defined in the callout below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Must Memorize):**
> - $R_T$ = **Total Resistance** (Newtons, N)
> - $R_F$ = **Frictional Resistance** (viscous shear along the wetted hull surface)
> - $R_R$ = **Residuary Resistance** (wave-making + eddy-making + appendage resistance)
> - $R_A$ = **Air Resistance** (aerodynamic drag above the waterline)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rowing & Wading" Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a **flat wooden plank** through water at a steady speed:

- The **thin film of water** sliding along the bottom surface "sticks" to the plank and drags neighboring water molecules along — this sticky, dragging sensation is the **Frictional Resistance**. The plank's surface area and the water's "stickiness" (viscosity) decide this force.
- As the plank moves, it also **pushes water out of the way**, creating ripples, splashes, and swirls at the stern. The energy lost in making these waves and swirls is the **Residuary Resistance**.
- Above the waterline, the **wind hitting the cabin and superstructure** pushes the vessel back — this is **Air Resistance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing Vessel Specific Context:** Unlike large merchant ships, fishing vessels typically operate at **lower Froude numbers (Fn < 0.30)** and have **blunt, full-bodied hull forms** (high block coefficient, $C_B > 0.55$). This means **frictional resistance dominates** — often **60–80%** of total resistance — making hull surface smoothness, propeller-hull interaction, and length-to-displacement ratio critical design parameters.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (for KTU Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Remember (Sea Water Conditions):**
> - Density of sea water: $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
> - Density of fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$
> - Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 15°C: $\nu = \mathbf{1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - 1 Metric Horse Power (MHP) = **735.5 W** (or simply 75 kgf·m/s in legacy units)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Resistance — Detailed Breakdown

**(A) Frictional Resistance ($R_F$)**
- Caused by the **viscous shear stress** in the turbulent boundary layer flowing over the wetted hull surface.
- Depends on: wetted surface area, surface roughness, Reynolds number, and water density.
- For a perfectly smooth, flat plate: this is **calculable analytically** (e.g., ITTC-57 correlation).
- For a real ship hull: an empirical **form factor (1+k)** is multiplied to account for the 3D pressure-induced viscous drag.

**(B) Wave-Making Resistance ($R_W$)**
- The energy lost in generating the **ship wave system** (transverse and divergent waves).
- Strongly depends on the **Froude number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL_{WL}}$.
- For displacement fishing vessels at low $F_n$, this is typically 10–25% of $R_T$.
- Minimized by **fine entry lines** and a **slender forebody** (but trade-off with cargo capacity).

**(C) Eddy-Making Resistance ($R_E$)**
- Caused by **flow separation** at abrupt changes in hull form (e.g., bilge radius, transom stern, bossings).
- Highly dependent on hull form "fairness" and the **Reynolds number**.

**(D) Air Resistance ($R_A$)**
- Drag of the above-water hull, superstructure, masts, and rigging against wind.
- Usually estimated as 3–5% of $R_T$ for small fishing vessels; up to 7–10% for large tuna purse seiners with tall superstructures.

### 2.2 The Two Governing Non-Dimensional Numbers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Key Numbers for Resistance Problems:**
> 1. **Reynolds Number (inertial vs. viscous forces):**
>    $$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$
> 2. **Froude Number (inertial vs. gravitational forces):**
>    $$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$
> 
> In ship model testing, **Froude's Law of Comparison** is used to equate wave patterns, and **Reynolds' Law** is used to scale frictional effects — but they cannot be satisfied simultaneously, hence the **ITTC-1978 procedure** is used.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Resistance | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A$ | N | Master equation |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Viscous scaling |
| 3 | Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL_{WL}}}$ | dimensionless | Wave scaling |
| 4 | ITTC-57 Friction Line | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Smooth flat plate $C_F$ |
| 5 | Frictional Resistance (ITTC) | $R_F = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F \cdot (1+k)$ | N | Viscous drag of hull |
| 6 | Form Factor $(1+k)$ | Holtrop/Moulder or from model test | dimensionless | 3D viscous correction |
| 7 | Effective Horse Power | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ (metric) | MHP | Useful power for propulsion |
| 8 | Resistance Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | dimensionless | Hull efficiency comparison |
| 9 | Wetted Surface (approx.) | $S \approx L(B+2T)\nabla^{1/2}$ | m² | Quick estimation |
| 10 | Delivered → Effective | $DHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_P \cdot \eta_T}$ | MHP | From EHP back to engine |

> [!NOTE]
> In legacy KTU problems, the constant **75** is used in the denominator of EHP (where 1 MHP = 75 kgf·m/s). For SI units in Newtons, use **EHP = $R_T \cdot V$ / 735.5** to get Watts. Always read the unit convention in the question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull Form Selection:** Resistance curves ($R_T$ vs. $V$) are used to compare alternative hull forms before model testing.
- **Fuel Consumption Forecasting:** $R_T$ directly determines the EHP and hence the brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) of the main engine.
- **Speed–Power Estimation:** A 10% increase in resistance translates to ~10% more EHP at the same speed — critical for fuel budgeting on multi-day fishing voyages.
- **Towing Tank Validation:** All design predictions are validated by towing a scaled model in a towing tank, applying Froude scaling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ITTC-57 Frictional Resistance Correlation

The ITTC-57 line is an empirical fit to experimental flat-plate turbulent boundary layer data. For a wetted hull of area $S$ moving at speed $V$:

**Step 1: Compute the Reynolds Number based on the waterline length $L_{WL}$.**

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}$$

**Step 2: Compute the smooth flat-plate frictional coefficient $C_F$ using the ITTC-57 line:**

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the **form factor $(1+k)$** to convert from 2D flat plate to 3D ship hull:**

$$C_{F,ship} = C_F \cdot (1 + k)$$

**Step 4: Apply the standard drag equation for a body moving in a fluid:**

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_{F,ship}$$

**Why each step works:**
- Step 1 collapses the velocity, length, and fluid viscosity into a single similarity parameter.
- Step 2 uses the ITTC-57 correlation (1957) — the **only sanctioned formula in KTU exams**.
- Step 3 is necessary because the 3D pressure field of a real hull increases the effective skin friction by a factor of typically **1.05 to 1.20** for fishing vessels.
- Step 4 is the standard drag equation: dynamic pressure $\times$ area $\times$ drag coefficient.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Horse Power (EHP) from Resistance

Given the total resistance $R_T$ and the service speed $V$, the EHP is the rate at which useful work is done against the resistance:

**Step 1: Power is defined as force times velocity:**

$$P = R_T \cdot V \quad \text{(Watts, if R_T in N and V in m/s)}$$

**Step 2: Convert from SI (Watts) to Metric Horse Power (1 MHP = 735.5 W):**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{735.5} \quad \text{(MHP, SI units)}$$

**Step 3: Or, in the legacy KTU metric gravitational system (1 MHP = 75 kgf·m/s):**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{(MHP, when } R_T \text{ is in kgf)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most KTU textbook problems give $R_T$ in **kgf** and ask for EHP in **MHP** using the 75-divisor formula. If $R_T$ is in Newtons, use **735.5** instead. State the unit convention clearly in the answer to earn full marks.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example (Part B Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP} = 28\ m$
- Wetted surface area, $S = 320\ m^2$
- Service speed, $V = 5.5\ m/s$ (≈ 10.7 knots)
- Form factor, $(1 + k) = 1.10$
- Sea water, $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$
- Estimated total resistance coefficient, $C_T = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ (based on $S$)

**Required:** Calculate the **frictional resistance** using the ITTC-57 line, and the **Effective Horse Power (EHP)** using the standard drag equation.

---

**Solution (Fully Exhaustive):**

**Part (a): Frictional Resistance using ITTC-57**

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number:**

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} = \frac{5.5 \times 28}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{154.0}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_n = 1.2941 \times 10^{8} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ITTC-57 friction coefficient $C_F$:**

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

$$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.2941 \times 10^8) = 8.1119$$

$$\log_{10} R_n - 2 = 6.1119$$

$$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = (6.1119)^2 = 37.356$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{37.356} = 2.0079 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply form factor:**

$$C_{F,ship} = C_F \cdot (1+k) = 2.0079 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.10 = 2.2087 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute frictional resistance $R_F$:**

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_{F,ship}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (5.5)^2 \times 320 \times 2.2087 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 30.25 \times 320 \times 2.2087 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$$

$$R_F = 512.5 \times 30.25 = 15503.125$$

$$R_F = 15503.125 \times 320 = 4961000$$

$$R_F = 4961000 \times 2.2087 \times 10^{-3} = 10958.3\ N$$

$$\boxed{R_F \approx 10.96\ kN}$$

---

**Part (b): EHP using $C_T$**

**Step 1 — Compute total resistance:**

$$R_T = C_T \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (5.5)^2 \times 320$$

$$R_T = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4961000 = 22324.5\ N$$

**Step 2 — Compute EHP in Watts:**

$$P = R_T \cdot V = 22324.5 \times 5.5 = 122784.75\ W$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Metric Horse Power:**

$$EHP = \frac{122784.75}{735.5} = 166.94\ MHP$$

$$\boxed{EHP \approx 167\ MHP}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Frictional Share:**

$$\frac{R_F}{R_T} = \frac{10958.3}{22324.5} = 0.491 = 49.1\%$$

This confirms that on a typical fishing trawler at low Froude number, frictional resistance accounts for nearly half of the total — and would be even higher if residual (wave + eddy) resistance were excluded from the numerator.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Resistance Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance R_T] --> B[Frictional Resistance R_F]
    A --> C[Residuary Resistance R_R]
    A --> D[Air Resistance R_A]
    B --> B1[Viscous Shear on Wetted Hull]
    B --> B2[Depends on Reynolds Number]
    B --> B3[Estimated by ITTC-57 Line]
    C --> C1[Wave-Making Resistance R_W]
    C --> C2[Eddy-Making Resistance R_E]
    C --> C3[Depends on Froude Number]
    C --> C4[From Model Test or Series Data]
    D --> D1[Above-Waterline Drag]
    D --> D2[3 to 5 percent of R_T typical]
    B1 --> E1[ITTC-57 Formula]
    B3 --> E1
    C1 --> E2[Froude Scaling and Model Test]
    C3 --> E2
    E1 --> F[Total Resistance R_T]
    E2 --> F
    D2 --> F
    F --> G[Effective Horse Power EHP]
    G --> H[Delivered Horse Power DHP via Propeller and Transmission Efficiency]
```

### 4.2 Resistance Build-Up Curve — Typical Fishing Vessel Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Speed V equals 0] --> V2[Low Speed Region]
    V2 --> V3[Quadratic Growth Region]
    V3 --> V4[Hump Region near Fn equals 0.25]
    V4 --> V5[Post-Hump Linear Region]
    V1 -. Dominant: Frictional .- V2
    V2 -. Wave Resistance Emerges .- V3
    V3 -. Wave + Friction Peak .- V4
    V4 -. Hull Resists Planing .- V5
    style V4 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** The "hump" near $F_n \approx 0.22$–$0.25$ is where the transom starts to immerse and the divergent wave system intensifies. Most **displacement fishing trawlers are designed to operate *below* this hump** to avoid the resistance penalty.

### 4.3 From Resistance to Power — Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Hull Form and Principal Dimensions] --> Cal1[Compute Reynolds and Froude Numbers]
    In2[Service Speed V] --> Cal1
    In3[Wetted Surface S] --> Cal1
    Cal1 --> R1[Frictional R_F via ITTC-57]
    Cal1 --> R2[Residuary R_R from Series or Model]
    R1 --> Sum[Total Resistance R_T]
    R2 --> Sum
    In4[Air Density and Frontal Area] --> AR[Air Resistance R_A]
    AR --> Sum
    Sum --> EHP1[Effective Horse Power EHP]
    EHP1 --> Eta[Multiply by Propulsive Coefficient]
    Eta --> BHP[Brake Horse Power BHP]
    BHP --> Engine[Main Engine Selection]
```
