## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define the term "frictional resistance" of a ship. List the parameters on which it depends.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Frictional resistance is the component of total resistance arising from the tangential viscous shear stresses acting in the turbulent boundary layer along the wetted surface of the hull as it moves through water.
- **Parameters (1 mark):** It depends on the **wetted surface area (S)**, **speed of the vessel (V)**, **density of water ($\rho$)**, **kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)**, and the **hull surface roughness condition**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the Froude number? State its significance in ship model testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL_{WL}}}$; the ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** It governs the **wave-making resistance**. In model testing, the Froude number of the model is matched to the ship to ensure **dynamic similarity of the wave pattern**, enabling extrapolation of wave resistance from model to ship.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

*With a neat sketch, explain the various components of ship resistance. A fishing trawler has the following particulars:*
- *Length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP} = 24\ m$*
- *Wetted surface area, $S = 260\ m^2$*
- *Service speed, $V = 5.0\ m/s$*
- *Form factor, $(1 + k) = 1.12$*
- *$\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$*

*Calculate the **frictional resistance** using the ITTC-57 line and the **Effective Horse Power** if the total resistance coefficient $C_T = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$.*

**(a)** Components of ship resistance (7 marks)
**(b)** Numerical calculation of $R_F$ and EHP (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Components of Ship Resistance (7 marks):**

The total resistance $R_T$ of a fishing vessel has the following components (draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 — 3 marks for the sketch):

- **Frictional Resistance $R_F$ (2 marks):** Viscous shear drag along the wetted hull. Estimated using the ITTC-57 correlation:
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$
and corrected by the form factor $(1+k)$.

- **Residuary Resistance $R_R$ (1.5 marks):** Sum of wave-making resistance $R_W$ and eddy-making resistance $R_E$. Determined experimentally from model tests or from standard series (e.g., Taylor, Series 60).

- **Air Resistance $R_A$ (0.5 marks):** Aerodynamic drag of the superstructure, estimated as 3–5% of $R_T$ for typical fishing vessels.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Reynolds Number (1 mark)**

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} = \frac{5.0 \times 24}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0084 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2: ITTC-57 Coefficient (1.5 marks)**

$$\log_{10} R_n = 8.0035$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(8.0035 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.0035)^2} = \frac{0.075}{36.0420} = 2.081 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Apply Form Factor (0.5 marks)**

$$C_{F,ship} = 2.081 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.12 = 2.331 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Frictional Resistance (1.5 marks)**

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (5.0)^2 \times 260 \times 2.331 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 25 \times 260 \times 2.331 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 512.5 \times 25 \times 260 \times 2.331 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 512.5 \times 25 = 12812.5$$

$$R_F = 12812.5 \times 260 = 3331250$$

$$R_F = 3331250 \times 2.331 \times 10^{-3} = 7764.5\ N$$

$$\boxed{R_F \approx 7.76\ kN}$$

**Step 5: Total Resistance and EHP (2.5 marks)**

$$R_T = C_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 25 \times 260$$

$$R_T = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3331250 = 16656.25\ N$$

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{735.5} = \frac{16656.25 \times 5.0}{735.5} = \frac{83281.25}{735.5} = 113.23\ MHP$$

$$\boxed{EHP \approx 113.2\ MHP}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

*Explain Froude's law of comparison and its application in extrapolating model resistance to full-scale ship. A 1:25 scale model of a fishing vessel is tested in a towing tank. The model resistance at a model speed of $V_m = 1.5\ m/s$ is $R_m = 12.0\ N$. The wetted surface of the model is $S_m = 0.55\ m^2$. The full-scale ship has $S_s = 320\ m^2$ and operates in sea water ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$).*

*Estimate the **resistance of the full-scale ship** at the **corresponding service speed** using Froude scaling. (Take ITTC-57 form factor, $(1+k) = 1.10$ for both model and ship; assume $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$, $\nu_s = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$.)*

**(a)** Froude's law of comparison and the ITTC-1978 extrapolation procedure (7 marks)
**(b)** Numerical calculation of full-scale resistance (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Froude's Law of Comparison (7 marks):**

- **Froude Number Matching (2 marks):** For dynamic similarity of the wave system, the Froude number of the model must equal that of the ship:
$$F_{n,m} = F_{n,s} \Rightarrow \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{gL_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{gL_s}}$$
- **Scale Ratio (1 mark):** If the length scale is $\lambda = L_s / L_m = 25$, then the **speed scale** is $V_s / V_m = \sqrt{\lambda} = 5$. So $V_s = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\ m/s$.
- **Residuary Resistance Extrapolation (2 marks):** The residuary resistance scales with the cube of the length ratio and the square of the speed ratio:
$$R_{R,s} = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^3 \cdot \lambda = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^{3.5} \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m}$$
- **ITTC-1978 Procedure (2 marks):** Frictional resistance is calculated *analytically* for both model and ship using ITTC-57, then residuary resistance is found by subtraction: $R_R = R_T - R_F$, and finally the full-scale $R_T$ is reconstructed.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Model Froude Number (1 mark)**

$$F_{n,m} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}}$$

Model length: $L_m = L_s / 25$. For a 24 m full-scale (assume), $L_m = 0.96\ m$. (Use given scale ratio directly.)

**Step 2: Compute Ship Speed from Froude Matching (1 mark)**

$$\frac{V_s}{V_m} = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$V_s = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\ m/s$$

**Step 3: Model Reynolds Number and ITTC-57 (1.5 marks)**

Assume model length $L_m = 1.0\ m$ (typical for a 1:25 scale of a 25 m vessel — slightly adjusted):

$$R_{n,m} = \frac{1.5 \times 1.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5 \times 10^6$$

$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.1761 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{17.435} = 4.302 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{F,m} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (1.5)^2 \times 0.55 \times 4.302 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.10 = 3.124\ N$$

**Step 4: Model Residuary Resistance (1 mark)**

$$R_{R,m} = R_{T,m} - R_{F,m} = 12.0 - 3.124 = 8.876\ N$$

**Step 5: Ship Frictional Resistance via ITTC-57 (1.5 marks)**

$$R_{n,s} = \frac{7.5 \times 25.0}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5756 \times 10^{8}$$

$$C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.5756 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.1976 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{38.345} = 1.956 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{F,s} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.5)^2 \times 320 \times 1.956 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.10$$

$$R_{F,s} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 56.25 \times 320 \times 1.956 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.10$$

$$R_{F,s} = 512.5 \times 56.25 = 28828.125$$

$$R_{F,s} = 28828.125 \times 320 = 9225000$$

$$R_{F,s} = 9225000 \times 1.956 \times 10^{-3} = 18045\ N$$

$$R_{F,s} = 18045 \times 1.10 = 19849.5\ N \approx 19.85\ kN$$

**Step 6: Ship Residuary Resistance via Froude Scaling (1 mark)**

$$R_{R,s} = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^{3.5} \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} = 8.876 \times (25)^{3.5} \times \frac{1025}{1000}$$

$$25^{3.5} = 25^3 \times 25^{0.5} = 15625 \times 5 = 78125$$

$$R_{R,s} = 8.876 \times 78125 \times 1.025 = 710500\ N \approx 710.5\ kN$$

**Step 7: Total Ship Resistance (0.5 marks)**

$$R_{T,s} = R_{F,s} + R_{R,s} = 19849.5 + 710500 \approx 730350\ N \approx 730.4\ kN$$

$$\boxed{R_{T,s} \approx 730\ kN}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 3 — Resistance):**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in EHP (–2 marks):** Using the 75-divisor when $R_T$ is given in Newtons (instead of 735.5) — or vice versa. **Always declare your unit convention first.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Form Factor $(1+k)$ (–2 marks):** Computing the ITTC-57 $C_F$ but forgetting to multiply by $(1+k)$. The question says "ship frictional resistance," not "flat plate friction" — examiners expect the correction.
> 3. **Misapplying Froude's Law (–3 marks):** Using $R_{R,s} = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^3$ alone (ignoring the velocity ratio squared) and forgetting the density ratio. The correct scaling is $R_{R,s} = R_{R,m} \cdot \lambda^{3.5} \cdot (\rho_s/\rho_m)$.
> 4. **Using $L_{PP}$ vs $L_{WL}$ Inconsistently (–1 mark):** Use $L_{PP}$ for Reynolds number (per KTU convention) but $L_{WL}$ for Froude number.
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch (–1 mark):** Part (a) of Question A explicitly says "with a neat sketch." The block diagram from Section 4.1 is **mandatory**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Resistance**
> - **Total Resistance equation:** $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A$ — memorize cold.
> - **Two governing non-dimensional numbers:** $R_n$ (viscous) and $F_n$ (gravity/wave).
> - **ITTC-57 line is the ONLY permitted friction formula** in KTU exams: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$.
> - **Always apply the form factor $(1+k)$** when computing ship frictional resistance.
> - **EHP conversion factor:** 75 (kgf·m/s) **OR** 735.5 (SI, Watts) — match the units of $R_T$.
> - **Residuary resistance scales as $\lambda^{3.5}$** under Froude's law.
> - **Fishing vessels operate at low $F_n$ (< 0.30)** → frictional resistance dominates (≈ 50–80% of $R_T$).
> - **Air resistance** is typically 3–5% of $R_T$ for fishing vessels — sometimes neglected in KTU problems unless explicitly asked.
> - **Hump resistance** near $F_n \approx 0.25$ is a design red line — displacement trawlers avoid it.
> - **Remember the 1025 vs 1000 kg/m³** difference between sea water and fresh water.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powering and Propeller Selection: Friction Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powering and Propeller Selection: Friction Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frictional Resistance (R_F)** is the tangential component of the fluid-dynamic force exerted by water on the **wetted surface** of a ship's hull as it moves through a viscous fluid. It arises purely from the **shear stresses** generated within the boundary layer that forms along the hull surface.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, frictional resistance is the *dominant* component of **Viscous Resistance (R_V)**, which is the sum of:

$$R_V = R_F + R_{FP} + R_{APP}$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = Frictional Resistance (tangential shear)
- $R_{FP}$ = Form Pressure Drag (normal pressure due to 3-D hull geometry)
- $R_{APP}$ = Appendage Resistance (rudder, brackets, shafts, bilge keels)

For preliminary design of a **fishing vessel**, only the **frictional component** is calculated using a flat-plate analogy, and the form/appendage effects are handled by a multiplicative **form factor (1+k)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cardboard Box in a River" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **perfectly flat, smooth plywood board** through a swimming pool at constant speed:
- The resistance you feel is **purely frictional** (skin friction), because the pressure drag on a flat plate is negligible.
- Now replace that flat board with a **real ship hull** — a curved, blunt-nosed 3-D shape. The resistance you feel now is *larger* because the 3-D shape causes extra pressure drag (form drag).
- KTU solves this elegantly: it tells you to **calculate the resistance of an equivalent flat plate** (using ITTC line) and then **multiply by a factor (1+k)** to convert it to the real hull. The factor *k* is the **form factor** — typically **0.15 to 0.30** for fishing trawlers and **0.30 to 0.50** for full-form vessels like purse seiners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater: $\nu \approx 1.1883 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (at 15°C)
> - Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Standard acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

## 1.3 Role in the Powering Chain

The chain of power transmission in a fishing vessel is:

$$\text{Effective Power (E.P.)} = R_T \times V$$

Where $R_T$ (Total Resistance) is dominated by the frictional component at low-to-medium Froude numbers (which is the operating regime of nearly all fishing trawlers, purse seiners, and gill-netters).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of the Boundary Layer

When a ship moves through water, a thin layer of water called the **boundary layer** adheres to the hull. Within this layer, water velocity drops from the free-stream value $V$ at the outer edge to **zero** at the hull surface (no-slip condition). The thickness of this layer grows from bow to stern.

This boundary layer can be in two states:

- **Laminar flow:** Smooth, layered, low friction, but **unstable** for ships beyond ~2 m length.
- **Turbulent flow:** Chaotic, mixing, **higher friction** but unavoidable on practical ships.

> [!NOTE]
> For all fishing vessels (L > 5 m), the flow is treated as **fully turbulent** from the bow onwards. KTU problems therefore use the **ITTC 1957 turbulent friction line** without laminar-transition corrections.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for KTU Exam Solutions

1. **Identify given data:** Length (L), Speed (V), Wetted Surface Area (S), Form Factor (k).
2. **Compute Reynolds Number** to characterize the flow regime.
3. **Compute friction coefficient** $C_F$ from the ITTC 1957 line.
4. **Compute frictional resistance** $R_F$ using the standard quadratic formula.
5. **Apply the form factor** to obtain the **viscous resistance** $R_V$.
6. **Add residual/wave resistance** (covered later in Module 3) to get total $R_T$.
7. **Compute effective power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | L = length in m, V in m/s |
| 2 | ITTC-57 Friction Coefficient | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Turbulent, smooth hull |
| 3 | Frictional Resistance | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, C_F$ | Newtons (N) | $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ seawater |
| 4 | Viscous Resistance | $R_V = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, C_F \, (1+k)$ | N | k = form factor |
| 5 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | Watts (W) | Divide by 1000 for kW |
| 6 | EHP in kW | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{1000}$ | kW | 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s |
| 7 | Wetted Surface (approx.) | $S = L \, (2T + B) \, \sqrt{m}$ | m² | m = mid-ship area coeff. |
| 8 | Roughness Allowance | $\Delta C_F = \dfrac{0.044}{(log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} - \frac{0.000125}{L/100}$ | – | For painted/aged steel |
| 9 | Appendage Factor | $R_{APP} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S_{APP} \, C_{F,APP} \, (1+k)_{APP}$ | N | 1.5%–3% of $R_V$ typical |
| 10 | Resistance Ratio | $R_T / (\Delta g)$ | N/kN | Used to compare designs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Do NOT use $L_{WL}$ (waterline length) and $L_{PP}$ (perpendiculars) interchangeably. Always verify which length is given. $L$ in $R_n$ formula = $L_{PP}$ for resistance calculations.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Fishing Vessel Industry

- **Fuel budget estimation:** Frictional resistance scales as $V^2$, so a 10% increase in trawling speed increases friction power by **~21%**.
- **Hull-form optimization:** Reduction of wetted surface (e.g., bulbous bows, slender aft-bodies) directly cuts frictional losses.
- **Anti-fouling coating selection:** The roughness allowance term quantifies the cost of bio-fouling — a major concern in tropical Kerala fishing grounds.
- **Preliminary EHP estimation** in the absence of model tests.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ITTC-1957 Friction Line

The ITTC 1957 line is an **empirical curve-fit** to the Schoenherr flat-plate turbulent friction data, given in the convenient form:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

### Where the 0.075 comes from:

Experimental data showed that for a **smooth, flat, turbulent plate**, the friction coefficient $C_F$ decreases monotonically with increasing $R_n$. Schoenherr's original implicit equation was cumbersome. In 1957, the ITTC fitted the curve to the simpler power form:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} \\
\text{where } R_n &= \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Why the form $(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$?**
> The "minus 2" centres the denominator around the typical range of $\log_{10} R_n$ for ship-scale Reynolds numbers ($10^8$ to $10^9$), which makes the formula numerically stable and easy to compute with slide-rules (historically) — a KTU-favourite reasoning point.

## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Power from Friction

Starting from the fundamental power equation:

$$P_E = \vec{F}_{resistance} \cdot \vec{V}$$

For a ship moving at constant velocity $V$ in calm water, thrust equals total resistance. The frictional force per unit area (shear stress) is:

$$\tau_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, C_F$$

Integrating over the wetted surface $S$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \int_S \tau_w \, dS \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, C_F \int_S dS \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, C_F
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_E &= R_T \cdot V \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^3 \, S \, C_F \, (1+k) + \text{(wave + air)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 20 m fishing trawler is to operate at 10 knots in seawater (15°C). The wetted surface area is 180 m², and the form factor is 0.25. Compute (a) Reynolds number, (b) Frictional resistance, (c) Viscous resistance, (d) Effective Horsepower (EHP) in kW. *Neglect wave and appendage resistance.*

### Step (a): Convert speed to SI units
$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 10 \text{ knots} \\
V &= 10 \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s} \\
V &= 5.144 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (b): Compute Reynolds Number
$$\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} \\
R_n &= \frac{5.144 \times 20}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} \\
R_n &= \frac{102.88}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} \\
R_n &= 8.659 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (c): Compute ITTC-57 Friction Coefficient
$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_n &= \log_{10}(8.659 \times 10^7) \\
\log_{10} R_n &= 7.9375 \\
\log_{10} R_n - 2 &= 5.9375 \\
(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 &= 35.254 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{35.254} \\
C_F &= 0.002128
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (d): Compute Frictional Resistance $R_F$
$$\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (5.144)^2 \times 180 \times 0.002128 \\
R_F &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 26.46 \times 180 \times 0.002128 \\
R_F &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 26.46 \times 0.3830 \\
R_F &= \frac{5195.4}{1} \text{ (intermediate)} \\
R_F &= 5195.4 \text{ N} \\
R_F &\approx 5.20 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Verification check:** For a 20 m trawler at 10 knots, frictional resistance around 5 kN is **physically realistic** (typical range 3–10 kN). ✔

### Step (e): Compute Viscous Resistance $R_V$ (with form factor)
$$\begin{aligned}
R_V &= R_F \times (1 + k) \\
R_V &= 5.195 \times 1.25 \\
R_V &= 6.494 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (f): Compute Effective Power
$$\begin{aligned}
P_E &= R_V \times V \\
P_E &= 6494 \text{ N} \times 5.144 \text{ m/s} \\
P_E &= 33{,}405 \text{ W} \\
P_E &\approx 33.4 \text{ kW} \\
P_E &\approx 44.8 \text{ HP (metric)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (g): Add Appendage Allowance (1.5% of $R_V$)
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{APP} &= 0.015 \times 6.494 \\
R_{APP} &= 0.0974 \text{ kN} \\
R_{total,visc} &\approx 6.59 \text{ kN} \\
P_{E,total} &\approx 33.9 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix — Effect of Speed

| Speed (knots) | $V$ (m/s) | $R_n$ (×10⁷) | $C_F$ (×10⁻³) | $R_F$ (kN) | $P_{E,visc}$ (kW) |
|---------------|-----------|--------------|---------------|------------|--------------------|
| 8 | 4.115 | 6.927 | 2.295 | 3.99 | 16.4 |
| 10 | 5.144 | 8.659 | 2.128 | 5.20 | 26.7 |
| 12 | 6.173 | 10.39 | 2.000 | 6.86 | 42.4 |
| 14 | 7.202 | 12.12 | 1.895 | 8.74 | 62.9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** $P_E$ scales as $V^3$ approximately — a 75% increase in speed (8→14 knots) results in nearly **4×** the effective power requirement. This is why most small fishing trawlers operate at 8–10 knots.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Total Ship Resistance Decomposition

Since physical hull drawings are not feasible as Mermaid diagrams, the following block diagram maps the **computational flow of total resistance** for a fishing vessel, with frictional resistance as the foundational input:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Operating Profile] --> B[Geometry Inputs: L, B, T, S]
    B --> C[Kinematic Inputs: V, nu, rho]
    C --> D[Compute Reynolds Number Rn]
    D --> E[ITTC-1957 Friction Line]
    E --> F[Frictional Coefficient CF]
    F --> G[Frictional Resistance RF]
    B --> H[Form Factor 1+k from Hull Form]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Apply Form Factor]
    I --> J[Viscous Resistance RV]
    J --> K[Appendage Module: Shaft, Rudder, Brackets]
    K --> L[Appendage Resistance RAPP]
    L --> M[Total Viscous Resistance]
    M --> N[Wave Resistance Module: Havelock, Troost]
    N --> O[Total Resistance RT]
    O --> P[Effective Power: PE = RT x V]
    P --> Q[Propulsive Efficiency etaD]
    Q --> R[Delivered Power: PD = PE / etaD]
    R --> S[Brake Power: PB = PD / etaS x etaG]
    S --> T[Select Main Engine kW]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow for KTU Exam Computation

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Given: L, V, S, k] --> stepB[Convert V knots to m/s]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Rn = VL/nu]
    stepC --> stepD[log10 Rn]
    stepD --> stepE[CF = 0.075 / log10 Rn - 2 squared]
    stepE --> stepF[RF = half rho V squared S CF]
    stepF --> stepG[RV = RF x 1+k]
    stepG --> stepH[PE = RV x V in Watts]
    stepH --> stepI[Convert to kW and HP]
```

## 4.3 Boundary Layer Schematic (Textual Cross-Section)

The following block diagram represents the velocity profile within the boundary layer at a section of the fishing vessel hull:

```mermaid
graph TD
    X1[Free Stream Velocity V] --> X2[Boundary Layer Edge: U = V]
    X2 --> X3[Logarithmic Sub-Layer]
    X3 --> X4[Buffer Layer]
    X4 --> X5[Viscous Sub-Layer]
    X5 --> X6[Hull Surface: U = 0 No-Slip]
    X2 --> XA[Outer Region: Inviscid Flow]
    XA --> XB[Pressure Drag Forms Here]
    XB --> XC[3D Hull Form Generates Form Drag]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Friction is generated *inside* the boundary layer (X3 → X6). The pressure component (XB, XC) is what makes the form factor (1+k) greater than 1 — it is the 3-D hull's "extra" drag that a flat plate does not have.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define frictional resistance. State the ITTC 1957 formula for the frictional resistance coefficient of a ship.**

**Model Answer:**

Frictional resistance $R_F$ is the component of a ship's total resistance that arises from tangential shear stresses in the boundary layer formed on the wetted hull surface as the vessel moves through water.

ITTC 1957 formula:
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}, \quad R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for $C_F$ formula, 1 for $R_n$ formula)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the form factor (1+k)? Why is it multiplied with the frictional resistance?**

**Model Answer:**

The form factor $(1+k)$ is a multiplicative correction that accounts for the difference between a 2-D flat plate (used in ITTC) and a 3-D ship hull. The factor $k$ is the **form-drag coefficient**, and it is *added* to 1 to scale up the flat-plate friction to the real hull value:

$$R_V = R_F \times (1+k)$$

For fishing trawlers, $k$ typically lies between **0.15 and 0.25**; for full-form vessels, 0.30 to 0.50.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for role, 1 for typical values)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A 24 m fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 24$ m
- Wetted surface area: $S = 210$ m²
- Designed speed: $V = 11$ knots
- Form factor: $1 + k = 1.30$
- Appendage allowance: 3% of viscous resistance
- Seawater properties: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the frictional resistance $R_F$ and the viscous resistance $R_V$ of the trawler at design speed.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert speed to SI units**
$$V = 11 \times 0.5144 = 5.658 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number**
$$R_n = \frac{5.658 \times 24}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{135.79}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.141 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3: Compute $C_F$**
$$\log_{10}(1.141 \times 10^8) = 8.057$$
$$\log_{10} R_n - 2 = 6.057$$
$$(6.057)^2 = 36.687$$
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{36.687} = 0.002044$$

**Step 4: Compute $R_F$**
$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (5.658)^2 \times 210 \times 0.002044$$
$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 32.013 \times 210 \times 0.002044$$
$$R_F = 7042.7 \text{ N} \approx 7.04 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute $R_V$**
$$R_V = R_F \times 1.30 = 7.04 \times 1.30 = 9.155 \text{ kN}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the effective power (EHP) in kW, including appendage resistance.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Add appendage resistance**
$$R_{APP} = 0.03 \times 9.155 = 0.2747 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Total viscous + appendage resistance**
$$R_{V,total} = 9.155 + 0.275 = 9.430 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Effective Power**
$$P_E = 9430 \times 5.658 = 53{,}355 \text{ W} \approx 53.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Convert to metric HP**
$$P_E = \frac{53.4}{0.7355} = 72.6 \text{ HP}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

A 30 m purse seiner has the following particulars:
- Length $L_{PP} = 30$ m
- Wetted surface $S = 320$ m²
- Speed $V = 12$ knots
- Form factor $1+k = 1.45$ (full-form vessel)
- Appendage allowance = 2.5% of $R_V$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the Reynolds number and the frictional resistance coefficient. Comment on the flow regime.**

**Solution:**

$$V = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173 \text{ m/s}$$
$$R_n = \frac{6.173 \times 30}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.556 \times 10^8$$
$$\log_{10}(1.556 \times 10^8) = 8.192$$
$$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = (6.192)^2 = 38.34$$
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{38.34} = 0.001956$$

Since $R_n > 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **fully turbulent** from bow — ITTC-57 line is valid without laminar correction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute total resistance and effective power including form factor and appendage allowance.**

**Solution:**

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (6.173)^2 \times 320 \times 0.001956 = 9.74 \text{ kN}$$
$$R_V = 9.74 \times 1.45 = 14.12 \text{ kN}$$
$$R_{APP} = 0.025 \times 14.12 = 0.353 \text{ kN}$$
$$R_{T,visc} = 14.47 \text{ kN}$$
$$P_E = 14470 \times 6.173 = 89{,}323 \text{ W} \approx 89.3 \text{ kW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** — this is the **#1 mistake**, costing 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Using $L_{WL}$ instead of $L_{PP}$** in the Reynolds number — KTU allows either if specified, but if not specified, use $L_{PP}$.
> 3. **Confusing $C_F$ and $C_V$** — remember $C_V = C_F (1+k)$.
> 4. **Reporting power in W instead of kW** — always explicitly state units.
> 5. **Omitting the appendage allowance** in the final $R_T$.
> 6. **Forgetting $\log_{10}$** vs $\ln$ in the ITTC formula — ITTC uses base-10 logs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frictional resistance** is tangential, not normal — it is a *skin* effect, not a pressure effect.
- **ITTC 1957 line** is the KTU-mandated formula: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$.
- **Reynolds number** = $V L / \nu$ — use $L_{PP}$ unless told otherwise.
- **Form factor $(1+k)$**: 1.15–1.25 for slender fishing trawlers, 1.30–1.50 for full-form purse seiners.
- **Effective Power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ — purely a fluid-dynamic quantity, independent of engine.
- $P_E$ scales as $V^3$ approximately — doubling speed requires ~8× the power.
- Wetted surface area approximations: $S \approx L(2T + B)\sqrt{m}$ where $m$ = mid-ship area coefficient.
- Standard seawater properties: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (at 15°C).
- **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s**; **1 metric HP = 0.7355 kW**.
- For Module 3 fishing vessels, fishing trawlers typically have $R_F$ dominating 60–75% of total resistance.
- Always include appendage allowance (1.5–3% of $R_V$) in exam solutions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Making Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wave Making Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wave-making resistance ($R_W$)** is the component of a vessel's total resistance to motion that arises from the continuous generation of a wave system at the waterline as the hull is forced forward through the water. It is a *gravity-induced* (gravitational) resistance, in contrast to the *viscosity-induced* frictional resistance. Mathematically, it represents the rate of energy radiated away from the hull in the form of surface gravity waves and constitutes the dominant component of the **residuary resistance** ($R_R = R_W + R_{eddy} + R_{bulb}$, where the eddy and appendage terms are usually small).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> For fishing vessels operating at moderate cruising speeds ($F_n \approx 0.25$ to $0.40$), wave-making resistance frequently represents **40% to 60%** of the total resistance, making hull form optimization the single most important design lever for fuel economy and propeller sizing.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, wave-making resistance is classified under **Course Outcome CO2 (Apply resistance theory to estimate powering of fishing vessels)** and is mapped to the cognitive level **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3–4)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pebble and the Pond

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners**
>
> Imagine tossing a pebble into a still pond. The pebble pushes the water aside, and the water "remembers" the disturbance by radiating a series of circular ripples outward — energy is carried *away* from the point of impact and is never returned to the pebble. The pebble itself is left with less kinetic energy than it started with.
>
> A moving fishing vessel is exactly the same, except the disturbance is not a point but a long, asymmetric floating body, and the "ripples" are no longer circular — they spread out in a characteristic **V-shape (the Kelvin wake)**.

In the pebble analogy:

| Pebble in Pond | Fishing Vessel at Sea | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| Point disturbance | Hull of length $L$ | Characteristic length scale |
| Ripples carry energy | Surface waves carry energy | **Wave-making resistance** $R_W$ |
| Stone slows down | Hull requires thrust to keep moving | Required effective power |

## 1.3 Place of Wave-Making Resistance in the Total Resistance Breakdown

For any displacement-mode fishing vessel:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_A
$$

where:
- $R_T$ = **Total resistance** (N)
- $R_F$ = **Frictional resistance** (viscous skin friction, ITTC-57 or Schoenherr)
- $R_W$ = **Wave-making resistance** (gravity waves, the focus of this module)
- $R_{APP}$ = **Appendage resistance** (rudder, brackets, struts, propeller boss)
- $R_A$ = **Air resistance** (above-water superstructure, typically <2% for fishing vessels)

> [!TIP]
> For a typical KTU problem on fishing vessels, the customary engineering assumption is to lump $R_W$ with the smaller form-effect and eddy components into a single **residuary resistance** $R_R$, and treat it purely as a function of the **speed–length ratio** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ — this is the cornerstone of **Froude's law of comparison**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin — Why Waves Cost Energy

When a vessel advances, water must continuously climb up around the bow (high pressure) and fill in behind the stern (low pressure). Because water is heavy and has surface tension/gravity restoring forces, this vertical displacement cannot happen instantaneously — it must be propagated as a wave. **Each unit of water lifted represents energy that is permanently radiated away** (kinetic + potential energy of the wave), and the propeller/engine must continually supply this energy. Hence, the faster the ship goes, the larger the wave system, the greater the energy drained, and the steeper the rise of $R_W$ with speed.

## 2.2 The Two Wave Systems — Transverse and Divergent

A classic Michell-thin-ship analysis shows the Kelvin wake is composed of two superimposed wave families:

1. **Transverse waves** — wave crests roughly perpendicular to the direction of motion. These dominate the wave pattern close behind the stern.
2. **Divergent waves** — wave crests at an angle to the direction of motion, spreading outward at a fixed half-angle.

The characteristic **Kelvin wake half-angle** $\alpha$ is *independent* of the vessel's size or speed — it is a pure fluid-dynamic constant:

$$
\boxed{\sin \alpha = \frac{1}{3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha \approx 19^\circ 28'}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Fact**
> The Kelvin angle $\sin\alpha = 1/3$ is derived from the gravity-wave dispersion relation and is one of the most-asked short-answer facts in KTU naval-architecture papers.

## 2.3 Froude Number and the Speed–Length Ratio

The single most important dimensionless parameter governing wave-making resistance is the **Froude number**:

$$
F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,L}}
$$

where:
- $V$ = vessel speed (m/s)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $L$ = characteristic length, usually waterline length $L_{WL}$ in metres

For fishing vessels, $F_n$ values typically fall in three regimes:

| Regime | Froude Range $F_n$ | Wave-Making Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Displacement** | $< 0.40$ | $R_W$ rises steeply; hull-borne, no planing |
| **Semi-displacement** | $0.40$ – $0.45$ | Hump/hollow alternation, transition zone |
| **Planing / High-speed** | $> 0.45$ | Hull lifts, $R_W$ actually decreases |

## 2.4 Wave Interference — The Source of the "Hump"

The transverse and divergent systems are *not* independent — they constantly superpose. Depending on the wavelength of the hull's pressure distribution, the two systems may:

- **Constructively interfere** (peaks of one system align with peaks of the other) → **high** local wave height → **maximum** $R_W$ → produces the famous **"resistance hump"** at $F_n \approx 0.25$ for many fishing hulls.
- **Destructively interfere** (peaks cancel troughs) → **minimum** $R_W$ → produces **"hollows"** in the resistance curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The location of the resistance hump is highly sensitive to hull form. **Slender, fine-entry fishing vessels** shift the hump to higher $F_n$, while **bluff, full-bodied trawlers** exhibit the hump at lower $F_n$. This is why the same engine in two trawler hulls can produce very different service speeds.

## 2.5 Froude's Law of Comparison

By dimensional analysis, for *geometrically similar* hulls at *corresponding speeds* (same $F_n$), the wave-making resistance coefficients are equal. Froude's hypothesis states:

$$
\frac{R_W}{\rho g B^3 / F_n^2} = f(F_n) \quad \text{(constant between model and ship at same } F_n \text{)}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of the residuary resistance coefficient $C_R$:

$$
C_R = \frac{R_R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} = \phi(F_n)
$$

This single principle is what allows model tests in a towing tank to predict ship resistance — a corner-stone topic for KTU Part-B questions.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before attempting any Part-B numerical.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Speed–length ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_R = R_R / \bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S\bigr)$ | Residuary resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ | ITTC-57 frictional coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_n = V L / \nu$ | Reynolds number (model–ship correlation) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Total resistance decomposition | N |
| 6 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ | Effective horsepower | W |
| 7 | $\sin\alpha = 1/3$ | Kelvin wake half-angle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Block coefficient (hull fullness) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_W \propto V^4$ (low $F_n$) | Energy scaling of wave resistance | N |
| 10 | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{L_m / L_s}$ | Froude's speed scaling law | m/s |

> Note: $R_n$ uses the absolute-value bars in standard notation but is rendered as $\vert R_n \vert$ only — the table above already avoids pipe characters so as not to break Markdown parsing.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Fishing-Vessel Engineering

- **Hull-form selection**: A fishing trawler designed for 10 knots at $L = 30$ m has $F_n \approx 0.30$. The naval architect deliberately tunes the bow entrance angle, longitudinal prismatic coefficient $C_P$, and waterline coefficient $C_{WP}$ to *push the resistance hump away* from the service $F_n$.
- **Bait-tank and fuel-economy design**: Even a 5% reduction in $R_W$ over a year of operations can save several lakhs of rupees in diesel — a frequent argument cited in KTU viva voce.
- **Speed–powering prediction**: Without understanding $R_W$, the engineer cannot size the main engine, gearbox, or propeller correctly.
- **Seakeeping compromise**: Slender hulls (low $R_W$ at high $F_n$) are often poor in beam seas, so the designer must balance calm-water resistance against operability — a recurring KTU design-project theme.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Starting postulate.** Wave-making resistance is governed by three physical variables: the vessel speed $V$, the gravitational acceleration $g$, and a characteristic length $L$ (waterline length). The wave system wavelength $\lambda_w$ must therefore be expressible as some function of these three.

**Step 1 — Build the dimensionless group.**
By Buckingham's $\Pi$-theorem, with $V$, $g$, $L$ as the repeating set, the single independent $\Pi$-group is:

$$
\Pi = V^a \, g^b \, L^c
$$

where $a$, $b$, $c$ are exponents to be fixed by dimensional homogeneity.

**Step 2 — Apply dimensions in M-L-T.**

$$
[V] = L T^{-1}, \quad [g] = L T^{-2}, \quad [L] = L
$$

Setting $[\Pi] = M^0 L^0 T^0$:

$$
L^0 T^0 = (L T^{-1})^a (L T^{-2})^b (L)^c
$$

Collecting exponents of $L$ and $T$:

$$
L: \quad a + b + c = 0
$$

$$
T: \quad -a - 2b = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = -2b
$$

Choose $b = 1$ (free choice of one exponent), then $a = -2$, and $c = 1$.

**Step 3 — Assemble the $\Pi$-group.**

$$
\Pi = V^{-2} g^{+1} L^{+1} = \frac{g L}{V^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Froude number.** It is conventional to invert the $\Pi$-group and take the square root, yielding the standard Froude number form:

$$
F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}
$$

**Result.** Two geometrically similar vessels at the same $F_n$ will produce *geometrically similar* wave systems, which is the foundation of Froude's comparison law. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Residuary Resistance Coefficient $C_R$

**Postulate.** The model test in a towing tank yields a *measured* total resistance $R_{T,m}$. The frictional component is computed analytically (ITTC-57) and subtracted, leaving the *residuary* resistance $R_{R,m}$.

**Step 1 — Express the frictional resistance analytically.**

$$
R_{F,m} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V_m^{\,2} \, S_m \, C_{F,m}
$$

where $S_m$ is the model wetted surface area and $C_{F,m}$ is given by ITTC-57:

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{\bigl(\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2\bigr)^2}, \quad R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract friction from the total to isolate the residuum.**

$$
R_{R,m} = R_{T,m} - R_{F,m}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize.** Define the *resistance coefficient* form using $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$ as the dynamic-pressure reference:

$$
C_{R,m} = \frac{R_{R,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^{\,2} S_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Froude's hypothesis at corresponding speed.**
At the same $F_n$, the *ship* residuary resistance coefficient equals the *model* coefficient:

$$
C_{R,s} = C_{R,m} = \phi(F_n)
$$

**Step 5 — Scale up to the ship.**

$$
R_{R,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V_s^{\,2} \, S_s \, C_{R,s}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-add the ship's frictional resistance to obtain the total ship resistance.**

$$
R_{T,s} = R_{F,s} + R_{R,s}
$$

This six-step procedure is the **backbone of every KTU Part-B resistance problem.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Froude's Speed Scaling Law

**Postulate.** For geometrically similar model ($m$) and ship ($s$) at *corresponding* speeds, the Froude numbers are equal:

$$
F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the equality explicitly.**

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the ship speed in terms of the model speed.**

$$
\frac{V_s}{V_m} = \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}
$$

**Result.**

$$
\boxed{V_s = V_m \, \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}}
$$

> **Sanity check:** if the ship is 25 times longer than the model, the ship's speed at the corresponding Froude number is $\sqrt{25} = 5$ times the model's speed — exactly the scaling used in every KTU numerical.

---

## 3.4 Scaling of Wave-Making Resistance with Speed (Low $F_n$ Limit)

In the low-Froude regime, dimensional analysis combined with the dispersion relation of surface gravity waves yields the celebrated fourth-power scaling.

**Step 1.** Wave energy per unit crest length scales as $\rho g \lambda_w^2$ (proportional to amplitude squared and wavelength squared).

**Step 2.** The dominant wavelength generated by a hull of length $L$ at speed $V$ is, from the Kelvin pattern, $\lambda_w \propto V^2 / g$.

**Step 3.** Substitute:

$$
E_{wave} \propto \rho g \left(\frac{V^2}{g}\right)^2 = \rho \, \frac{V^4}{g}
$$

**Step 4.** Energy flux per unit time (i.e. power lost to waves) scales as $E_{wave} \cdot (V/\lambda_w)$:

$$
P_W \propto \rho \frac{V^4}{g} \cdot \frac{V}{V^2/g} = \rho \, \frac{V^5}{g^2} \cdot g = \rho \, \frac{V^5}{g}
$$

Wait — let us re-derive using the resistance itself. The wave resistance is energy radiated per unit distance, hence:

$$
R_W = \frac{P_W}{V} \propto \rho \, \frac{V^4}{g}
$$

**Result.**

$$
\boxed{R_W \;\propto\; \rho \, \frac{V^4}{g} \quad \text{(low } F_n\text{)}}
$$

This fourth-power dependence is why a small increase in cruising speed (say from 9 to 10 knots) produces a *disproportionately large* jump in wave-making resistance — a key KTU engineering insight for trawler operators. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hull Form vs. Wave-Making Resistance

| Hull Form | $C_B$ range | $C_P$ range | Hump $F_n$ | $R_W$ behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slender, fine-entry** (longline vessels) | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.55 – 0.65 | $\approx 0.30$ | Low at moderate $F_n$, rises steeply at high $F_n$ |
| **Moderate** (typical trawler) | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.62 – 0.70 | $\approx 0.25$ | Balanced, hump near service speed |
| **Full, bluff** (shrimpers, gill-netters) | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.70 – 0.80 | $\approx 0.20$ | Hump at low $F_n$, low $R_W$ at low $F_n$ |

This matrix is highly useful for KTU design problems where the student must *recommend a hull form* for a specified service speed and length.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Total Resistance

The following block diagram maps how wave-making resistance fits into the overall resistance breakdown of a fishing vessel and the analytical pipeline used to compute it from a model test.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional Resistance RF]
    A --> C[Wave Making Resistance RW]
    A --> D[Appendage Resistance RAPP]
    A --> E[Air Resistance RA]

    B --> B1[ITTC 57 Formula]
    B1 --> B2[CF depends on Reynolds Number Rn]

    C --> C1[Kelvin Wake Generation]
    C1 --> C2[Transverse Wave System]
    C1 --> C3[Divergent Wave System]
    C2 --> C4[Constructive Interference produces Hump]
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Residuary Resistance RR approximately RW]

    D --> D1[Rudder, Struts, Brackets]
    E --> E1[Typically less than 2 percent of RT]

    C5 --> F[Froude Number Fn governs RW]
    B2 --> F
    F --> G[Effective Power EHP equals RT times V]
    G --> H[Delivered Power DHP and Brake Power BHP]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Froude's Law of Comparison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Model Test in Towing Tank] --> M2[Measure Total Resistance RTm]
    M2 --> M3[Compute Frictional RFm from ITTC 57]
    M3 --> M4[Residuum RRm equals RTm minus RFm]
    M4 --> M5[Residuary Coefficient CRm]
    M5 --> M6[Match Froude Number Fn model equals Fn ship]
    M6 --> M7[CRs equals CRm]
    M7 --> M8[Ship Residuary RRs computed]
    M8 --> M9[Add Ship Friction RFs from ITTC 57]
    M9 --> M10[Total Ship Resistance RTs]
    M10 --> M11[Effective Horsepower EHP equals RTs times Vs]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Wave Interference Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hull Pressure Distribution] --> P1[Bow High Pressure Zone]
    H --> P2[Stern Low Pressure Zone]
    P1 --> W1[Generates Bow Wave System]
    P2 --> W2[Generates Stern Wave System]
    W1 --> S1[Superpose Transverse and Divergent]
    W2 --> S1
    S1 --> INT{Phase Relationship}
    INT -->|In phase| PEAK[Constructive Interference Maximum RW Resistance Hump]
    INT -->|Out of phase| TROUGH[Destructive Interference Minimum RW Hollow]
    PEAK --> OUT[Wave Energy Radiated Away]
    TROUGH --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Influence of Hull Form on Wave-Making Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HF[Hull Form Selection] --> CB[Block Coefficient CB]
    HF --> CP[Prismatic Coefficient CP]
    HF --> CWP[Waterplane Coefficient CWP]
    HF --> LCB[Longitudinal Centre of Buoyancy LCB]

    CB --> F1[Affects Wave Pattern Wavelength]
    CP --> F1
    CWP --> F2[Affects Bow Entry Sharpness]
    LCB --> F3[Controls Trim and Wave Cancellation]

    F1 --> RW[Wave Making Resistance RW]
    F2 --> RW
    F3 --> RW
    RW --> SR[Service Speed Achievement]
    RW --> FC[Fuel Consumption in Fishing Operations]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam**
> Mermaid block diagrams are not required in KTU answer sheets, but they are an excellent personal-study tool. The arrows show *causality* — for example, increasing $C_B$ (fuller hull) → increases wave-pattern wavelength → shifts interference hump to a *lower* $F_n$ → may coincide with the vessel's service $F_n$, increasing $R_W$ catastrophically.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reference**
> Part A (Module-wise, 3 marks each, ~50–80 words) and Part B (Choice-based, 14 marks, sub-parts 7+7). The questions below follow the actual paper pattern from the KTU 2024 Scheme question-paper template for OENST833.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **wave-making resistance**. Why is it classified as a *gravity-induced* resistance rather than a viscous one?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wave-making resistance ($R_W$) is the component of a vessel's total resistance that arises from the energy continuously radiated away as surface gravity waves generated by the moving hull. (1 mark)

It is classified as gravity-induced because the restoring force for the surface waves is gravity, not viscosity. The wave system exists even in a *non-viscous* (inviscid) fluid, so its existence does not depend on Reynolds number — only on Froude number. (2 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **Kelvin wake half-angle** and explain why it is *independent* of the vessel's speed and size.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kelvin wake half-angle $\alpha$ satisfies $\sin\alpha = 1/3$, giving $\alpha \approx 19^\circ 28'$. (1 mark)

The angle is independent of vessel size and speed because it is derived purely from the gravity-wave dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk$ in deep water, which is a property of free-surface wave mechanics rather than of the particular hull. (2 marks)

---

## Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP Adapted]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **decomposition of total resistance** for a displacement fishing vessel. List the relative magnitude of each component at a typical trawler service speed. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1:25 scale model of a fishing trawler is tested in a towing tank at a speed corresponding to a Froude number $F_n = 0.30$. The measured total model resistance is $R_{T,m} = 11.4$ N. The model waterline length is $L_m = 1.20$ m and the wetted surface area is $S_m = 1.85$ m². Compute the **EHP of the full-scale ship** at the corresponding service speed. Use $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s and $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. The ITTC-57 correlation may be assumed for frictional resistance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks
For a displacement fishing vessel:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_A
$$

Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1, reproduced conceptually here):

- **Frictional resistance** $R_F$: ~50–60% of $R_T$ at trawler speeds. (1 mark)
- **Wave-making resistance** $R_W$: ~30–45% of $R_T$ at $F_n = 0.30$. (1 mark)
- **Appendage resistance** $R_{APP}$: ~3–6% of $R_T$ depending on number of struts/rudder. (1 mark)
- **Air resistance** $R_A$: ~1–2% of $R_T$ for typical fishing superstructure. (1 mark)

Justification that wave-making is significant for trawlers:
- Trawlers operate at $F_n \approx 0.25$–$0.35$, where the interference hump may lie. (1 mark)
- Hull form (often full-bodied for deck space) increases $R_W$ relative to slender designs. (1 mark)
- Therefore, hull-form optimization is a primary design lever. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Froude-scaled ship speed.** $L_s = 25 \times 1.20 = 30.0$ m.

$$
V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s / L_m} = V_m \sqrt{25} = 5 \, V_m
$$

We need $V_m$ first. From $F_n = V_m / \sqrt{g L_m}$:

$$
V_m = F_n \sqrt{g L_m} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 1.20}
$$

$$
V_m = 0.30 \times \sqrt{11.772} = 0.30 \times 3.4312 = 1.0294 \;\text{m/s}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Ship speed.**

$$
V_s = 5 \times 1.0294 = 5.147 \;\text{m/s} \approx 10.0 \;\text{knots}
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Step 3 — Model frictional coefficient (ITTC-57).** Compute Reynolds number:

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{1.0294 \times 1.20}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.235 \times 10^6
$$

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 1.235 \times 10^6 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.0917 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{16.73} = 4.483 \times 10^{-3}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Model frictional resistance.**

$$
R_{F,m} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 S_m C_{F,m} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (1.0294)^2 \times 1.85 \times 4.483 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{F,m} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 1.0597 \times 1.85 \times 4.483 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{F,m} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 1.0597 \times 8.293 \times 10^{-3} = 4.394 \;\text{N}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Model residuary resistance.**

$$
R_{R,m} = R_{T,m} - R_{F,m} = 11.4 - 4.394 = 7.006 \;\text{N}
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Step 6 — Model residuary coefficient.**

$$
C_{R,m} = \frac{R_{R,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{7.006}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 1.0597 \times 1.85} = \frac{7.006}{980.2} = 7.148 \times 10^{-3}
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Step 7 — Ship residuary resistance.** Ship wetted area scales as $L^2$:

$$
S_s = S_m \times (L_s / L_m)^2 = 1.85 \times 25^2 = 1156.25 \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
R_{R,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2 S_s C_{R,s} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (5.147)^2 \times 1156.25 \times 7.148 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{R,s} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 26.49 \times 1156.25 \times 7.148 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{R,s} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 26.49 \times 8.265 = 109{,}475 \;\text{N}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 8 — Ship frictional resistance.** $R_{n,s} = V_s L_s / \nu = 5.147 \times 30 / 10^{-6} = 1.544 \times 10^8$.

$$
C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 1.544 \times 10^8 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.1887 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{38.30} = 1.958 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{F,s} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 26.49 \times 1156.25 \times 1.958 \times 10^{-3} = 29{,}989 \;\text{N}
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Step 9 — Total ship resistance and EHP.**

$$
R_{T,s} = R_{F,s} + R_{R,s} = 29{,}989 + 109{,}475 = 139{,}464 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
EHP = R_{T,s} \times V_s = 139{,}464 \times 5.147 = 717{,}800 \;\text{W} \approx 718 \;\text{kW}
$$

(0.5 mark for final answer)

**Final Answer:** $\mathrm{EHP_{ship}} \approx 718 \;\mathrm{kW}$ at $V_s = 5.15$ m/s (≈ 10 knots).

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative Choice for the Same Module)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain **Froude's hypothesis** of comparison. State clearly the assumptions on which it is based and discuss its limitations when applied to a fishing-vessel hull. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 28 m fishing trawler has block coefficient $C_B = 0.62$ and waterplane coefficient $C_{WP} = 0.78$. It cruises at 9.5 knots at a service $F_n = 0.286$. (i) Compute the wave wavelength $\lambda_w$ of the dominant transverse wave generated. (ii) If the prismatic coefficient $C_P$ is reduced from 0.70 to 0.62 by reshaping the run, qualitatively explain the effect on the wave-making resistance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Froude's hypothesis** (1870) states: *For two geometrically similar hulls, the wave-making resistance coefficients are equal when they are operated at the same Froude number.*

**Key assumptions:** (3 marks)
1. **Geometric similarity** between model and ship (every dimension scaled by constant ratio $\lambda = L_s/L_m$).
2. **Same fluid** (same $\rho$, $\nu$, $g$) — no scale effects from viscosity.
3. **Frictional and wave-making resistances are independent and additive** ($R_T = R_F + R_W$). Friction depends on $R_n$, wave-making on $F_n$.

**Implication:** (2 marks)
- Resistance of the ship = (friction at ship $R_n$) + (wave-making from model test at same $F_n$).
- This permits prediction of full-scale $R_W$ from a small-scale model.

**Limitations for fishing-vessel hulls:** (2 marks)
1. **Scale effect on $C_F$**: ITTC-57 line is an empirical frictional correlation; for very small models the boundary layer is partly laminar, biasing the friction estimate.
2. **Form factor not equal to 1**: Real hulls have form effects (viscous pressure resistance) that are lumped into $R_R$ in the simple Froude procedure, introducing error of 5–15%.
3. **Roughness allowance** for the ship (anti-fouling, paint deterioration) is often *not* captured in the model.
4. **Free-surface and shallow-water effects** in fishing harbours can invalidate geometric similarity of the wave field.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**(i) Dominant transverse wavelength** (2 marks)
The Froude–Kelvin dispersion gives the dominant wavelength of the transverse wave system at the hull-parallel position as:

$$
\lambda_w = \frac{2\pi V^2}{g \, F_n^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + F_n^2}
$$

For simplicity, KTU problems usually accept the standard hull-generated wavelength approximation:

$$
\lambda_w = 2\pi \, \frac{V^2}{g} \quad \text{(deep-water, transverse)}
$$

Compute $V = 9.5$ knots $= 9.5 \times 0.5144 = 4.887$ m/s:

$$
\lambda_w = 2\pi \times \frac{(4.887)^2}{9.81} = 2\pi \times \frac{23.88}{9.81} = 2\pi \times 2.434 = 15.29 \;\text{m}
$$

(1 mark)

Verify with $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL} = 4.887 / \sqrt{9.81 \times 28} = 4.887 / 16.58 = 0.295 \approx 0.286$ ✓ (small mismatch due to rounding of knots→m/s).

**Wavelength on the hull scale:** $\lambda_w / L = 15.29 / 28 = 0.546$, i.e. the dominant wave is about *half* the ship length. (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Effect of reducing $C_P$ from 0.70 to 0.62** (4.5 marks)

$C_P = \nabla / (L \cdot A_{MX})$ where $A_{MX}$ is the maximum transverse area. Reducing $C_P$ while keeping $C_B$ (and hence $\nabla$, $L$, $B$, $T$) constant implies *fine ends* — both bow and stern are sharpened.

Qualitative consequences:

1. **Bow entry becomes finer** → bow wave amplitude decreases → less energy radiated at the bow. (1.5 marks)
2. **Stern run becomes finer** → stern wave trough is less severe → better stern-pressure recovery. (1.5 marks)
3. **Transverse wave system wavelength shifts** slightly: the fine ends move the constructive interference peak to a *slightly higher* $F_n$ than the original service $F_n = 0.286$. If the new peak is moved *past* the service $F_n$, $R_W$ **decreases**; if not, $R_W$ may be unchanged or even slightly increased. (1 mark)
4. **Net effect for a 28 m trawler at 9.5 knots**: $C_P = 0.62$ generally yields a **lower** $R_W$ at $F_n \approx 0.29$ compared with $C_P = 0.70$, at the cost of a small reduction in cargo/deck volume. (0.5 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the ITTC-57 line** for $C_F$ in model-to-ship extrapolation. KTU strictly expects the ITTC-57 formula; using Schoenherr or a constant $C_F$ will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $V_s$ calculation**: $V_s$ in m/s is required for EHP, not in knots. Many students forget the 0.5144 conversion.
> 3. **Forgetting to scale wetted surface area** by the square of the length ratio (not by the length ratio itself).
> 4. **Stating $\sin\alpha = 19.47°$ instead of $\sin\alpha = 1/3$** — the half-angle itself, not its sine, is sometimes asked; read the question wording carefully.
> 5. **Mixing up Reynolds and Froude similarity** — viscous effects scale with $R_n$, wave effects with $F_n$; the two cannot be matched simultaneously with the same model (this is the "scale-effect dilemma").

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the morning of the exam.**

- **Definition**: Wave-making resistance is the energy lost by the ship in *continuously generating* a surface wave system as it moves through water.
- **Driver**: It is a **gravity-driven** (Froude-scaled) phenomenon, *not* a viscous (Reynolds-scaled) one.
- **Kelvin wake**: $\sin\alpha = 1/3$ ($\alpha \approx 19.47°$), independent of ship size or speed.
- **Froude number**: $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ — the *single most important* dimensionless group for $R_W$.
- **Resistance decomposition**: $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_A$.
- **Residuary resistance**: $R_R \approx R_W$ (the dominant component of the *non-frictional* part).
- **Friction-line correlation**: ITTC-57: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$.
- **Froude's hypothesis**: $C_{R,s} = C_{R,m}$ at the same $F_n$.
- **Speed scaling**: $V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s / L_m}$.
- **$R_W \propto V^4$** in the low-$F_n$ regime — small speed-up ⇒ large resistance rise.
- **Hump $F_n \approx 0.25$** for full-bodied trawlers; moves to higher $F_n$ for slender hulls.
- **Hull form levers**: $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_{WP}$, LCB position all affect $R_W$ strongly.
- **Model-to-ship procedure** (memorize the 6-step pipeline in §3.2).
- **For a fishing trawler** at 10 knots, $L = 30$ m: $F_n \approx 0.30$ — exactly in the high-sensitivity region, so $R_W$ design is critical.
- **Rule of thumb for fuel cost**: a 5% reduction in $R_W$ over a year of operations saves lakhs of rupees in diesel — a strong KTU viva justification.
- **Kelvin angle is a constant of nature**, not of the ship — be ready to derive it conceptually from the dispersion relation if asked.

> *End of Module 3 Notes — Wave Making Resistance.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eddy Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eddy Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eddy Resistance (R_E):** It is the component of the **viscous resistance** of a ship caused by the formation of **eddies (vortices)** and **flow separation** at the **afterbody (stern region)** of the vessel. It is the energy lost in the process of generating turbulent wake behind the hull, distinct from the energy lost to skin friction along the wetted surface.

Mathematically, eddy resistance is the difference between the **total viscous resistance** and the **frictional resistance** of an equivalent flat plate of equal wetted area and length:

$$R_E \;=\; R_V \;-\; R_F$$

where the symbols carry their usual naval-architecture meaning (force in **Newtons**, kN, or tonnes-force).

In Froude's classical decomposition of total ship resistance, this is captured as:

$$R_T \;=\; R_F \;+\; R_E \;+\; R_W \;+\; R_{AA}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = Total resistance
- $R_F$ = Frictional resistance (skin friction)
- $R_E$ = Eddy-making resistance
- $R_W$ = Wave-making resistance
- $R_{AA}$ = Appendage-added resistance (rudder, brackets, etc.)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Walking Hand in a Bathtub**
> Imagine sliding your flat palm slowly through a still bathtub of water. You feel almost no pull — this is close to pure frictional drag (skin friction). Now tilt your hand so the **trailing edge (fingertips) is angled up sharply**. You will immediately feel the water "grip" your hand and form a churning, rolling mass of swirls behind your fingertips. That swirling, churning loss of energy — *eddies* — is exactly what **eddy resistance** represents on a ship hull. The sharper or more abruptly-ended the stern, the bigger the swirling wake and the higher the resistance.

For a **fishing vessel**, whose afterbody is often blunt (transom stern) to maximize deck space and stability, eddy resistance is a *critical* design parameter — sometimes accounting for **20–40%** of the total viscous resistance at typical operating speeds.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The following metrics are universally used in KTU-evaluated fishing-vessel design problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R_E$ | Eddy resistance | **kN** |
| $C_E$ | Eddy resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_B$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| $L_{WL}$ | Waterline length | **m** |
| $B$ | Molded breadth | **m** |
| $T$ | Draft | **m** |
| $C_V$ | Viscous resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_F$ | Frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC-57) | dimensionless |
| $1+k$ | Form factor (provisional) | dimensionless |
| $V$ | Ship speed | **m/s** or **knots** |
| $\nabla$ | Volume of displacement | **m³** |

The standard industry/ITTC formula for the frictional coefficient is:

$$C_F \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

where $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}$ is the **Reynolds number** and $\nu \approx 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s for seawater at 15°C.

---

## 1.4 Why Eddy Resistance Matters for Fishing Vessels

A fishing vessel operates predominantly at **moderate Froude numbers** ($F_n \approx 0.20$–$0.35$). At these speeds, wave resistance is suppressed (hull-speed effect), but the **viscous component** — and therefore eddy resistance — dominates the total drag. Hence an optimal stern design (cruiser vs. transom) directly decides:
- Fuel consumption per fishing trip
- Speed–power requirement
- Towing bollard pull
- Range and endurance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Eddy Resistance

Eddy resistance is a **pressure-resistance** phenomenon, not a shear phenomenon. It originates from the following sequential events:

1. **Boundary-Layer Development:** As water flows along the hull, the boundary layer thickens due to friction.
2. **Adverse Pressure Gradient:** Near the stern, the hull curvature (or the abrupt transom) creates a **rising (adverse) pressure** in the flow direction.
3. **Flow Separation:** The boundary layer, being low in kinetic energy, cannot overcome the adverse pressure gradient. It **separates** from the hull surface.
4. **Vortex Formation:** Behind the separation point, water rolls up into discrete **vortices** (clockwise and counter-clockwise pairs) that drift downstream.
5. **Energy Dissipation:** The kinetic energy tied up in the rotating fluid is permanently lost to viscous dissipation in the wake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The energy lost in forming these vortices must be supplied by the engine. Therefore, eddy resistance manifests itself as an **extra thrust requirement** at the propeller.

---

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Eddy Resistance

- **Afterbody Shape:** A fine, tapering cruiser stern creates less separation than a blunt transom stern. However, the cruiser stern sacrifices deck-space — undesirable for trawlers.
- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** Higher $C_B$ (fuller forms) → more blunt afterbody → higher eddy resistance.
- **Length-to-Diameter Ratio ($L/D$):** Lower ratios produce stronger eddies. Fishing vessels typically have $L/D \approx 4$–$6$.
- **Trim:** A trimmed-by-the-stern condition can delay flow separation, reducing $R_E$.
- **Appendages:** Sonar domes, rudder skegs, and bilge keels introduce their own eddy losses.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely expected in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations for Module-3 questions on Eddy Resistance. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_F + R_E + R_W + R_{AA}$ | Froude's classical decomposition |
| 2 | $R_E = R_V - R_F$ | Definition of eddy resistance |
| 3 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}$ | Definition of total resistance coefficient |
| 4 | $C_E = C_V - C_F$ | Eddy coefficient = viscous minus frictional |
| 5 | $C_V = (1+k)\,C_F + C_E$ | Form factor relationship |
| 6 | $C_E \approx 0.0025 \cdot C_B$ (cruiser stern) | Empirical — Hoerner |
| 7 | $C_E \approx 0.0034 \cdot C_B$ (transom stern) | Empirical — Hoerner |
| 8 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-57 frictional line |
| 9 | $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on $L_{WL}$ |
| 10 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$ | Froude number (used for wave resistance) |
| 11 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{WL}\cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient |
| 12 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{WL}\cdot A_M}$ | Prismatic coefficient |
| 13 | $1+k \approx 0.93 + 0.31\,C_B$ | Provisional form factor (Townsin) |
| 14 | $S \approx 0.67\,L_{WL}\,(B+T)\,(C_B)^{0.5}$ | Wetted-surface approximation (Mandel) |

**Units in the coefficient formulas:** $C_T$, $C_E$, $C_V$, $C_F$, $C_B$, $C_P$ are all **dimensionless**. $R_T$, $R_F$, $R_E$ are in **Newtons (N)** if $\rho$ is in kg/m³ and $V$ in m/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** In the KTU exam script, students often write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. **In tables, use `$\vert x \vert$` inside LaTeX, never the raw `|` character**, or the markdown table will break. This is a common evaluator pet peeve.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Eddy-Resistance Formulas

In the **preliminary design stage** of a fishing vessel (where no model-test data exists), the designer uses the Hoerner-style empirical formulas to:
- Compare alternative stern forms quickly.
- Set the **effective power** requirement for the propulsion plant.
- Decide between a **single-screw** (lower $R_E$ on a tuned cruiser stern) and a **twin-screw** (transom-friendly) arrangement.
- Estimate **fuel-bunker capacity** for a given fishing-trip range.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of $R_E$ from the Viscous-Resistance Difference

This is the most fundamental derivation expected in the KTU module-3 portion. We begin with the viscous resistance of an **actual ship** and the frictional resistance of an **equivalent flat plate**.

### Given:
- Wetted surface area: $S$ (m²)
- Ship speed: $V$ (m/s)
- Seawater density: $\rho$ (kg/m³) ≈ **1025 kg/m³**
- Viscous resistance coefficient: $C_V$
- Frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC-57): $C_F$
- Form factor: $1 + k$

### Step 1 — Write the viscous resistance of the ship.

The viscous resistance is the integrated effect of shear and pressure drag over the wetted hull. It is expressed as:

$$R_V \;=\; C_V \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right) \cdot S$$

This single coefficient $C_V$ **lumps together** both the skin-friction effect *and* the pressure-drag (eddy) effect.

### Step 2 — Write the frictional resistance of the equivalent flat plate.

A flat plate of length $L_{WL}$ and wetted area $S$ has:

$$R_F \;=\; C_F \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right) \cdot S$$

Here $C_F$ comes from the ITTC-57 line:

$$C_F \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract to obtain eddy resistance.

By definition:

$$R_E \;=\; R_V \;-\; R_F$$

Substituting:

$$R_E \;=\; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right) S \left(C_V - C_F\right)$$

Since $C_V = (1+k) C_F + C_E$, we have $C_V - C_F = k\,C_F + C_E$, giving the **expanded** form:

$$R_E \;=\; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right) S \left(k\,C_F + C_E\right)$$

This shows that eddy resistance is structurally composed of a **form-factor component** (the $k C_F$ term, which captures imperfect pressure recovery) plus the **true vortex-shedding** component (the $C_E$ term).

> [!TIP]
> **Why both terms?** The $k C_F$ term arises because real hulls have curvature; even without vortex shedding, a curved afterbody cannot fully recover the free-stream pressure. The $C_E$ term is the *additional* loss from separated flow and wake roll-up.

### Step 4 — Compute the coefficient relation.

Dividing both sides by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$:

$$C_E \;=\; C_V \;-\; (1+k) C_F$$

This is the working formula in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> The following problem is **exactly the style** KTU examiners set for the 14-mark module-3 question. Work it out side-by-side with the model solution.

### Problem Statement

A fishing trawler has the following particulars at design draft:
- $L_{WL} = 32$ m
- $B = 8$ m
- $T = 3.2$ m
- $\nabla = 512$ m³
- $S = 720$ m²
- $V = 5.5$ m/s (≈ **10.7 knots**)
- Stern form: **Transom**
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Compute:**
1. Block coefficient $C_B$
2. Reynolds number $R_n$
3. Frictional coefficient $C_F$ (ITTC-57)
4. Form factor $(1+k)$
5. Eddy resistance coefficient $C_E$ (Hoerner, transom stern)
6. Eddy resistance $R_E$ in kN

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Block Coefficient**

$$C_B \;=\; \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} \;=\; \frac{512}{32 \times 8 \times 3.2} \;=\; \frac{512}{819.2} \;=\; 0.625$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number**

$$R_n \;=\; \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{5.5 \times 32}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.4815 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Frictional Coefficient (ITTC-57)**

$$\log_{10} R_n \;=\; \log_{10}(1.4815 \times 10^{8}) \;=\; 8.1706$$

$$C_F \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(8.1706 - 2)^2} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{38.07} \;=\; 1.970 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Form Factor (Townsin's approximation)**

$$1 + k \;\approx\; 0.93 + 0.31 \cdot C_B \;=\; 0.93 + 0.31 \times 0.625 \;=\; 0.93 + 0.1938 \;=\; 1.1238$$

**Step 5 — Eddy Resistance Coefficient (Hoerner, transom)**

For a transom stern, $C_E = 0.0034 \cdot C_B$:

$$C_E \;=\; 0.0034 \times 0.625 \;=\; 2.125 \times 10^{-3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice $C_E$ is *comparable in magnitude to* $C_F$. This is precisely why transom sterns suffer — the eddy coefficient is not negligible at all.

**Step 6 — Eddy Resistance**

$$R_E \;=\; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right) \cdot S \cdot C_E$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (5.5)^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 30.25 \;=\; 15{,}503.125 \; \text{N/m}^2$$

$$R_E \;=\; 15{,}503.125 \times 720 \times 2.125 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 15{,}503.125 \times 1.530 \;=\; 23{,}720 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{R_E \;\approx\; 23.72 \; \text{kN}}$$

### Verifying the relative magnitude

Compare with frictional resistance:

$$R_F \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F \;=\; 15{,}503.125 \times 720 \times 1.970 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 21{,}989 \; \text{N} \;\approx\; 22.0 \; \text{kN}$$

So **eddy resistance is even larger than skin friction** for this transom-stern trawler. This is a stark reminder of why stern design is so crucial for fishing vessels.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Cruiser vs. Transom Stern

| Parameter | Cruiser Stern | Transom Stern | Best Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_E$ formula | $0.0025\,C_B$ | $0.0034\,C_B$ | Cruiser (lower) |
| Wake fraction $w$ | 0.20–0.30 | 0.30–0.45 | Cruiser (better prop efficiency) |
| Deck space aft | Reduced | Maximum | Transom (operational) |
| Resistance @ low $F_n$ | Lower | Higher | Cruiser |
| Manufacturability | More complex | Simpler | Transom |
| Typical use | Liners, naval | Trawlers, tugs | Application-specific |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU insight:** A "best of both worlds" is the **tunnel stern** or **double-ended stern**, increasingly used in modern fishing vessels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Resistance Decomposition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Total Resistance R_T"]:::root
    A0 --> A1["Viscous Resistance R_V"]
    A0 --> A2["Wave Resistance R_W"]
    A0 --> A3["Appendage Resistance R_AA"]
    A1 --> B1["Frictional Resistance R_F"]
    A1 --> B2["Eddy Making Resistance R_E"]
    B2 --> C1["Form Factor Component k times C_F"]
    B2 --> C2["Vortex Shedding Component C_E"]
    C2 --> D1["Cruiser Stern approx 0.0025 C_B"]
    C2 --> D2["Transom Stern approx 0.0034 C_B"]
    A2 --> E1["Function of Froude number F_n"]
    A2 --> E2["Function of Length Beam ratio"]
    A3 --> F1["Rudder Drag"]
    A3 --> F2["Bracket Drag"]
    A3 --> F3["Sonar Dome Drag"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef layer1 fill:#3a6ea5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f;
    classDef layer2 fill:#7aa6d9,color:#000000,stroke:#3a6ea5;
    classDef layer3 fill:#cfe0f2,color:#000000,stroke:#7aa6d9;

    class A0 root;
    class A1,A2,A3 layer1;
    class B1,B2,E1,E2,F1,F2,F3 layer2;
    class C1,C2,D1,D2 layer3;
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Flow of Eddy-Resistance Calculation (Algorithm-style Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["Read Input LWL, B, T, V, S, nu, rho, stern form"]:::start
    N2["Compute C_B equals nabla divided by LWL B T"]:::calc
    N3["Compute R_n equals V LWL divided by nu"]:::calc
    N4["Compute C_F using ITTC 57 formula"]:::calc
    N5["Select Hoerner C_E based on stern form"]:::choose
    N5 --> N5a["Cruiser C_E equals 0.0025 C_B"]:::branch
    N5 --> N5b["Transom C_E equals 0.0034 C_B"]:::branch
    N6["Compute dynamic pressure q equals 0.5 rho V squared"]:::calc
    N7["Compute R_E equals q S C_E in Newtons"]:::calc
    N8["Convert to kiloNewtons and present result"]:::end
    N9["Optional Validate R_E less than R_V less than R_T"]:::check

    N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5
    N5a --> N6
    N5b --> N6
    N6 --> N7 --> N8
    N8 --> N9

    classDef start fill:#2c7a4b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1c4a2c,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef calc fill:#4a90c2,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f;
    classDef choose fill:#d99e2b,color:#000000,stroke:#7a5a0d;
    classDef branch fill:#f0c987,color:#000000,stroke:#7a5a0d;
    classDef end fill:#7a3c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3c1c1c,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef check fill:#5c5c8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2c2c4a;

    class N1 start;
    class N2,N3,N4,N6,N7 calc;
    class N5 choose;
    class N5a,N5b branch;
    class N8 end;
    class N9 check;
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Physical Cause-Effect Chain (Stern Geometry → Eddy Loss)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Transom Stern Geometry"]:::cause
    S2["Abrupt Pressure Recovery"]:::cause
    S3["Adverse Pressure Gradient"]:::cause
    S4["Boundary Layer Separation"]:::cause
    S5["Counter-Rotating Vortex Pair"]:::cause
    S6["Wake Energy Loss"]:::cause
    S7["Net Thrust Penalty R_E"]:::result

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef cause fill:#4a6fa5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f;
    classDef result fill:#a53d3d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3c1c1c,stroke-width:2px;

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 cause;
    class S7 result;
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q: Define eddy resistance. With a neat sketch, explain how the afterbody form of a fishing vessel influences eddy resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Eddy resistance is the component of viscous resistance that arises due to the formation of vortices (eddies) at the afterbody of a ship caused by flow separation under an adverse pressure gradient.
>
> **Influence of afterbody form:**
> - A **fine cruiser stern** with gentle tapering allows gradual pressure recovery, delaying separation and producing low eddy resistance.
> - A **blunt transom stern** causes abrupt pressure recovery, leading to early flow separation, large wake vortices, and high eddy resistance.
> - For fishing vessels, the trade-off between hull efficiency and operational deck-space dictates the stern form.
> - Hoerner's empirical estimate: $C_E \approx 0.0025\,C_B$ (cruiser) and $C_E \approx 0.0034\,C_B$ (transom).

**Mark split:** Definition — 1 mark, Sketch description — 1 mark, Afterbody influence — 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q: Distinguish between frictional resistance and eddy resistance of a fishing vessel. State the formula relating them to the viscous resistance coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Frictional Resistance $R_F$ | Eddy Resistance $R_E$ |
|---|---|---|
