<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# PROPELLER SELECTION — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Propeller Selection** is the systematic engineering procedure of determining the most suitable marine propeller geometry (diameter *D*, pitch *P*, expanded blade area ratio *EAR / BAR*, number of blades *Z*, rpm *n*, and material) for a given fishing vessel so that the required bollard-pull / free-running thrust is delivered at the highest attainable quasi-propulsive efficiency, subject to the boundary conditions of cavitation, vibration, strength, and engine-rpm compatibility.

The selection process rests on three classical naval-architecture pillars:

| Pillar | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Thrust Loading Coefficient | $C_T$ | Thrust generated per unit dynamic pressure and disc area |
| Power Loading Coefficient | $C_P$ | Power absorbed per unit dynamic pressure and disc area |
| Cavitation Number | $\sigma$ | Local pressure margin against vapor formation on the blade back |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3):** Students are expected to demonstrate **CO3 (Apply propeller theory to fishing vessels)** by selecting a propeller using the **Wageningen B-Series / Bp–δ chart methodology**, evaluating cavitation, and justifying the choice of blade material and blade number for typical fishing operating profiles (trawling, purse-seining, long-lining, gill-netting).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Screw-Fan"

Think of a ship’s propeller as the **paddle wheel of a flour mill**, but spinning in water instead of air, and *screwed* instead of flat. The blades are pitched (twisted) so that as they rotate they **bite water and fling it backwards**. By Newton’s third law, the reaction pushes the vessel forward.

- A **steeper blade pitch** is like a *longer stride* — it tries to cover more distance per turn. Good for fast vessels.
- A **flatter pitch** is like a *shorter, more forceful kick* — good for slow, heavily loaded trawlers hauling a full net.
- A **larger diameter** is like a *bigger fan blade* — moves more water per turn, more thrust, but risks hitting the seabed (critical for trawlers!).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing Vessel Nuance:** Unlike a cargo ship that sails a steady route, a fishing vessel swings between **free-running (light ship)** and **trawling at heavy drag (towing 5–25 tonnes of net + catch)**. A propeller selected only for free-run will be grossly over-pitched during towing, causing engine overload, slip drop and excessive fuel burn. The selection *must* account for both regimes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of sea-water:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (**standard KTU assumption**, sometimes rounded to 1026 kg/m³)
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea-water:** $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Vapor pressure of sea-water at 20 °C:** $p_v \approx 2.34 \text{ kPa}$
- **Atmospheric pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Typical BAR (Blade Area Ratio) for fishing trawlers:** $\mathbf{0.50 \text{ to } 0.80}$
- **Typical Pitch Ratio $P/D$ for trawlers:** $\mathbf{0.7 \text{ to } 1.0}$
- **Typical blade number $Z$:** **3, 4 or 5** (4-blade most common for trawlers for smoother torque)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# PROPELLER SELECTION — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Sequential Phases of Selection

### Phase A — Power & RPM Determination

1. From resistance curves, obtain **Effective Power (EHP)** at the design and service speeds.
2. Estimate **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient ($\eta_D$)** — typical fishing vessels: **0.55 to 0.65**.
3. Compute **Delivered Power (DHP):**

$$DHP \;=\; \frac{EHP}{\eta_D}$$

4. Select a **shaft rpm** compatible with the main engine (commonly **rpm = 200 to 400** for medium trawlers using reduction gearing; high-speed diesel direct drive may push to 1000 rpm but is rare in fishing fleet).

### Phase B — Geometry via B-Series (Wageningen) Bp–δ Chart

5. Compute the **Power Coefficient (Bp)** using the chosen rpm:

$$B_P \;=\; \frac{DHP^{0.5}}{(V_a^{2.5} \cdot D_P^{0.5} \cdot \rho^{0.5})}$$

Equivalently, in SI form:

$$B_P \;=\; \frac{P^{0.5}}{V_a^{2.5}}$$

where $P$ is in **kW** and $V_a$ in **knots**.

6. Enter the **Wageningen Bp–δ chart** (δ = BAR) at the chosen rpm contour → read off the **optimum $P/D$ ratio** for maximum efficiency.

### Phase C — Cavitation & Strength Check

7. Verify the **minimum pressure coefficient $C_{pmin}$** at the blade tip from the B-Series regression polynomials.
8. Compute the **cavitation number $\sigma$** at the deepest expected immersion:

$$\sigma \;=\; \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_a^{2}}$$

9. The propeller is cavitation-safe only if $\sigma \geq C_{pmin}$.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Why Bp–δ chart?** Because it *condenses* thousands of model-basin test points (Wageningen 1930s series) into a single graphical tool that any naval architect can use without a CFD license.
- **Why rpm selection first?** Because diameter, pitch and efficiency are all *highly sensitive* to rpm. A 10% rpm drop can demand a 20% larger propeller, which may not fit between the hull and the seabed for a trawler!
- **Why cavitation check?** Fishing vessels operate at **shallow draft** with **uneven wake**, making tip-vortex cavitation almost a default. Unchecked cavitation → erosion, vibration, lost thrust.
- **Why BAR matters?** A *high BAR* = thicker blade = stronger but adds drag and weight; a *low BAR* = thin blade, fast but cavitation-prone. Trawlers need a **mid-to-high BAR (0.55–0.70)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advance Coefficient | $J = \dfrac{V_a}{n D}$ | dimensionless | $V_a$ in m/s, $n$ in rps, $D$ in m |
| 2 | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho\, n^{2}\, D^{4}}$ | dimensionless | Wageningen polynomial in $J$, $P/D$, $Z$, BAR |
| 3 | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho\, n^{2}\, D^{5}}$ | dimensionless | Same polynomial set |
| 4 | Open-Water Efficiency | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | dimensionless | Always $\leq 0.72$ in practice |
| 5 | Power Coefficient | $B_P = \dfrac{n\,D^{2.5}}{V_a^{2.5}}\sqrt{K_T / J^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Bp–δ chart input |
| 6 | Diameter Coefficient | $\delta_D = \dfrac{n\,D}{V_a}$ | dimensionless | Alternative chart input |
| 7 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_a^{2}}$ | dimensionless | $p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h$ |
| 8 | Delivered Power | $DHP = 2\pi\, n\, Q$ | W | From engine to shaft |
| 9 | Effective Power | $EHP = R_T \cdot V_s$ | W | $R_T$ = total resistance |
| 10 | Quasi-Propulsive Coeff. | $\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{DHP}$ | dimensionless | Includes $\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0$ |
| 11 | Slip Ratio | $S = 1 - \dfrac{V_a}{n\,P}$ | dimensionless | Negative slip = over-pitched |
| 12 | Reynolds Number (blade) | $Re = \dfrac{0.75\,\pi D n\,\cos\beta_{0.75}}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | $\beta_{0.75}$ at $r/R = 0.75$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** In the table above, the absolute-value-like vertical bars in $V_a \mid_{...}$ have been replaced with $\vert$ to preserve markdown table syntax. Use the same convention in your KTU answer scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Trawl-Vessel Design Studios (Cochin, Mumbai, Visakhapatnam):** Every new fishing vessel design office uses Bp–δ selection before any model testing is commissioned.
- **Classification Societies (IRS, DNV, BV):** All require cavitation-screening charts to be submitted during plan approval.
- **Operational Use:** Skippers of purse-seiners often carry a *spare propeller* — a correctly selected OEM propeller reduces fuel by 8–12% over a "rule-of-thumb" propeller of the same diameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# PROPELLER SELECTION — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Thrust and Torque Coefficients (Dimensional Analysis)

We begin by recognising that the **thrust $T$** generated by a geometrically similar family of propellers operating at the same advance coefficient depends on four physical quantities:

$$T \;=\; f(\rho,\; n,\; D,\; V_a)$$

There are **5 variables** ($T, \rho, n, D, V_a$) and **3 fundamental dimensions** ($M, L, T$). By the **Buckingham $\Pi$ Theorem**, the number of independent dimensionless groups is $5 - 3 = 2$.

### Step 1 — Choose repeating variables
Select $\rho, n, D$ as repeating variables.

### Step 2 — Form $\Pi_1$ (Thrust group)

$$\Pi_1 \;=\; T\,\rho^{a}\,n^{b}\,D^{c}$$

Imposing dimensional homogeneity:

$$\begin{aligned}
[T] &= M L T^{-2} \\
[\rho^{a} n^{b} D^{c}] &= (M L^{-3})^{a}\,(T^{-1})^{b}\,(L)^{c} \\
&= M^{a}\,L^{-3a + c}\,T^{-b}
\end{aligned}$$

Equating exponents of $M, L, T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= 1 \\
-3a + c &= 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 4 \\
-b &= -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = 2
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_1 \;=\; \frac{T}{\rho\, n^{2}\, D^{4}} \;=\; K_T$$

### Step 3 — Form $\Pi_2$ (Velocity group)

$$\Pi_2 \;=\; V_a\,\rho^{d}\,n^{e}\,D^{f}$$

Imposing dimensional homogeneity yields $d = 0,\; e = -1,\; f = -1$:

$$\Pi_2 \;=\; \frac{V_a}{n D} \;=\; J$$

### Step 4 — Functional Relationship

$$\boxed{K_T \;=\; f(J,\, P/D,\, Z,\, BAR)}$$

By identical reasoning on torque $Q$ (dimensions $M L^{2} T^{-2}$):

$$\boxed{K_Q \;=\; g(J,\, P/D,\, Z,\, BAR)}$$

### Step 5 — Open-Water Efficiency Derivation

The **useful power** (thrust × advance velocity) is $T \cdot V_a$. The **absorbed power** is $2\pi n Q$.

$$\eta_0 \;=\; \frac{T V_a}{2\pi n Q} \;=\; \frac{J\,K_T}{2\pi\,K_Q}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Full Propeller Selection (14-Mark Style)

### Given (typical KTU numerical):
A coastal trawler has the following particulars:
- Service speed $V_s = 10$ knots → $V_a = 4.8$ m/s (assuming wake fraction $w = 0.20$)
- Total resistance $R_T = 78$ kN
- Quasi-propulsive coefficient $\eta_D = 0.58$
- Density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Selected rpm $n = 250$ rpm $= 4.167$ rps

**Step 1 — Effective Power**

$$\begin{aligned}
EHP &= R_T \cdot V_s \\
&= 78\,000 \text{ N} \times 4.8 \text{ m/s} \\
&= 374\,400 \text{ W} = 374.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Delivered Power**

$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{374.4}{0.58} = 645.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Enter Bp–δ Chart**

The Wageningen Bp–δ chart gives, at $n = 250$ rpm and $DHP \approx 645$ kW, the optimum:

- $D \approx 2.10$ m
- $P/D \approx 0.85$
- $BAR \approx 0.60$
- $\eta_0 \approx 0.62$

**Step 4 — Verify $J$**

$$J = \frac{V_a}{nD} = \frac{4.8}{4.167 \times 2.10} = 0.549$$

**Step 5 — Compute Thrust from Wageningen polynomial at $J = 0.55,\; P/D = 0.85,\; Z = 4,\; BAR = 0.60$:**

The polynomial form (simplified, valid range $J \in [0.5, 0.8]$):

$$K_T \approx 0.55 - 0.30\,J \approx 0.55 - 0.30 \times 0.55 = 0.385$$

**Step 6 — Compute Actual Thrust**

$$T = K_T \cdot \rho\, n^{2}\, D^{4} = 0.385 \times 1025 \times (4.167)^{2} \times (2.10)^{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 0.385 \times 1025 \times 17.36 \times 19.45 \\
&= 0.385 \times 1025 \times 337.7 \\
&\approx 133\,200 \text{ N} = 133.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verify against resistance**
$T (133.2 \text{ kN}) > R_T (78 \text{ kN})$ ✓ (provides 70% margin for trawling drag — typical).

**Step 8 — Slip check**

$$S = 1 - \frac{V_a}{nP} = 1 - \frac{4.8}{4.167 \times (0.85 \times 2.10)} = 1 - \frac{4.8}{7.44} = 1 - 0.645 = 0.355$$

Slip of ~35% is **healthy for a trawler** (cargo ships prefer 20–25%).

## 3.3 Analytical Selection Matrix (Decision Table)

| Vessel Type | Free-run Speed (knots) | Trawl Speed (knots) | Typical $\mathbf{P/D}$ | Typical $\mathbf{BAR}$ | Typical $\mathbf{Z}$ | Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small outboard (< 9 m) | 18–22 | n/a | 0.9–1.1 | 0.40–0.50 | 3 | Aluminium |
| Day-boat trawler | 8–10 | 3–4 | 0.75–0.90 | 0.55–0.70 | 4 | Mn-bronze |
| Deep-sea stern trawler | 11–13 | 4–5 | 0.70–0.85 | 0.60–0.75 | 4 or 5 | Ni-Al bronze |
| Purse-seiner | 10–12 | n/a | 0.85–1.00 | 0.50–0.65 | 4 | Ni-Al bronze |
| Long-liner | 9–11 | n/a | 0.80–0.95 | 0.50–0.60 | 3 or 4 | Mn-bronze |
| Tuna long-line | 12–14 | n/a | 0.95–1.10 | 0.45–0.55 | 4 | Ni-Al bronze |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for KTU Numerical Answers:** Always end your derivation with a **dimensional-consistency check** and a **sanity comparison** against the table above. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for "justification of selection" beyond the bare arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# PROPELLER SELECTION — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Selection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT VESSEL PARTICULARS] --> B[CALCULATE EHP AT DESIGN SPEED]
    B --> C[ESTIMATE ETA_D 0.55 TO 0.65]
    C --> D[CALCULATE DHP AT SHAFT]
    D --> E[CHOOSE SHAFT RPM FROM ENGINE CURVE]
    E --> F[ENTER WAGENINGEN BP DELTA CHART]
    F --> G[READ OPTIMUM D AND P BY D]
    G --> H[SELECT BLADE NUMBER Z]
    H --> I[COMPUTE KT KQ VIA POLYNOMIALS]
    I --> J{CAVITATION CHECK PASSES?}
    J -- YES --> K[VERIFY OPEN WATER EFFICIENCY]
    J -- NO --> L[INCREASE BAR OR REDUCE RPM]
    L --> F
    K --> M{STRESS CHECK PASSES?}
    M -- YES --> N[SELECT MATERIAL M BR NI AL CU]
    M -- NO --> O[INCREASE BLADE THICKNESS]
    O --> I
    N --> P[FINAL PROPELLER SPECIFICATION]
```

## 4.2 Propeller Anatomy — Labelled Cross-Section (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HUB] --> B[BLADE ROOT]
    B --> C[BLADE FACE PRESSURE SIDE]
    B --> D[BLADE BACK SUCTION SIDE]
    B --> E[BLADE TIP]
    B --> F[LEADING EDGE]
    B --> G[TRAILING EDGE]
    A --> H[KEYWAY AND TAPER]
    A --> I[PROPELLER NUT]
```

**Mapping the labels to engineering meaning:**

- **Hub:** The central boss; the *larger* the hub, the *less* blade area, the *worse* efficiency but the *simpler* the casting.
- **Blade Root:** Junction with hub — high stress concentration; minimum thickness governed by ISO 484/2.
- **Blade Face (Pressure Side):** Concave, high-pressure side — directly responsible for thrust.
- **Blade Back (Suction Side):** Convex, low-pressure side — first to cavitate.
- **Blade Tip:** Highest velocity, lowest local pressure — site of tip-vortex cavitation.
- **Leading Edge:** Must be smooth to avoid leading-edge erosion in dirty harbours.
- **Trailing Edge:** Defines pitch; **sharp edges improve efficiency** but **rounded edges resist erosion** — a deliberate trade-off.
- **Keyway & Taper:** Mechanical fit to shaft (ISO taper 1:15 for marine propellers).
- **Propeller Nut:** Retained by split pin or locking tab.

## 4.3 Cavitation Regime Map (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAVITATION REGIME] --> B[NO CAVITATION ETA HIGHEST]
    A --> C[SHEET CAVITATION ACCEPTABLE]
    A --> D[BUBBLE CAVITATION PERFORMANCE LOSS]
    A --> E[TIP VORTEX CAVITATION NOISE AND VIBRATION]
    A --> F[SUPER CAVITATION VERY HIGH LOAD]
    B --> B1[SIGMA GREATER THAN 0.6]
    C --> C1[SIGMA 0.3 TO 0.6]
    D --> D1[SIGMA 0.15 TO 0.3]
    E --> E1[SIGMA 0.05 TO 0.15]
    F --> F1[SIGMA LESS THAN 0.05]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing-Vessel Reality:** Stern trawlers with high $\delta$ (block coefficient) often show *incipient tip-vortex cavitation* at free-run speeds. This is acceptable if **not accompanied by erosion** — but warrants a **5-blade upgrade** if vibration exceeds ISO 2021 limits.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# PROPELLER SELECTION — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** 
**Define "Pitch Ratio (P/D)" of a marine propeller. What is its typical range for a fishing trawler, and why are trawler propellers designed with relatively low pitch ratios?**

**Model Answer:**
Pitch Ratio is defined as the ratio of propeller pitch $P$ (the theoretical advance of the propeller per revolution, in metres) to its diameter $D$:

$$\frac{P}{D} = \frac{\text{Pitch (m)}}{\text{Diameter (m)}}$$

For fishing trawlers, **P/D lies between 0.7 and 0.9**, whereas high-speed container ships use 1.0–1.2. The lower ratio is chosen because:
- Trawlers operate at **low service speeds (3–5 knots during towing)**.
- They require **high bollard pull** to drag a heavy net through water.
- A *coarse* (low P/D) propeller is mechanically *stronger* and less prone to over-pitching under sudden load surges (net snagging the seabed).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between "Fixed Pitch Propeller (FPP)" and "Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP)" in the context of fishing vessels. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FPP | CPP |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch | Set at manufacture; cannot be changed at sea | Hydraulically varied between **+15° and −15°** about design pitch |
| Efficiency at design point | Highest | Slightly lower (~3–5% loss) due to hub mechanism |
| Cost & maintenance | Low | High |
| Suitability for fishing | Good for single-mode trawlers | Excellent for vessels with *widely varying* load (trawling + free-run + dynamic positioning) |

- **Advantage FPP:** Simpler, cheaper, more efficient at the design point.
- **Disadvantage FPP:** Cannot absorb engine overload during trawl snatches; engine must be de-rated.
- **Advantage CPP:** Full engine power is always deliverable to the water at any speed → no engine de-rating.
- **Disadvantage CPP:** Complex hub oil-seal system; expensive spare parts.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain, with the aid of a clear schematic, the **Wageningen B-Series Bp–δ chart method** for selecting an optimum propeller for a fishing vessel. List the input data required and the outputs obtained. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bp–δ chart** (also called the **Power–Diameter coefficient chart**) is a graphical device derived empirically from the Wageningen B-Series open-water test data covering 4-, 5-, 6- and 7-bladed propellers in the range:
- $P/D = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.4$
- $BAR = 0.30 \text{ to } 1.00$
- $J = 0 \text{ to } 1.4$

**Inputs Required:**
1. Delivered Power $DHP$ (kW)
2. Shaft rotational speed $n$ (rpm)
3. Advance velocity $V_a$ (knots)
4. Estimated wake fraction $w$ and thrust-deduction fraction $t$ (for $V_a$ derivation)

**Steps:**
1. Compute the **Power Coefficient:**

$$B_P = \frac{n \sqrt{DHP}}{V_a^{2.5}}$$

2. Enter the chart horizontally with $B_P$, intersect the chosen **rpm contour line**, drop vertically to read the **optimum $P/D$** and simultaneously **the diameter $D$** via the auxiliary scale.
3. Read the associated **optimum $\eta_0$** from the efficiency contours.
4. Cross-check the **required $BAR$** from the auxiliary $\delta$ axis for cavitation safety.

**Outputs Obtained:**
- Optimum diameter $D$ (m)
- Optimum pitch ratio $P/D$, hence pitch $P$
- Estimated open-water efficiency $\eta_0$
- Recommended blade area ratio $BAR$

**Schematic (text representation):**

```
   η₀ contours  ─────────────
                \           /
                 \   B_P   /  ←── input power
                  \       /
                   \  •  /  ←── intersection point
                    \  /
                     ▼
   rpm contour ───► [P/D = 0.85, D = 2.10 m, η₀ = 0.62]
```

---

**(b)** A fishing trawler operates at $V_s = 9$ knots with $R_T = 65$ kN and $\eta_D = 0.60$. The engine delivers power at 280 rpm via reduction gear. Seawater density is 1025 kg/m³. Using the Wageningen Bp–δ chart, determine the optimum propeller diameter and pitch ratio. Assume $w = 0.22$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute advance velocity**

$$V_a = V_s (1 - w) = 9 \times 0.78 = 7.02 \text{ knots} = 3.61 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective power**

$$EHP = R_T \cdot V_s = 65\,000 \times 9 \times 0.5144 = 300\,924 \text{ W} = 300.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Delivered power**

$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{300.9}{0.60} = 501.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Power coefficient**

$$B_P = \frac{n \sqrt{DHP}}{V_a^{2.5}} = \frac{280 \times \sqrt{501.6}}{(7.02)^{2.5}}$$

$$\sqrt{501.6} = 22.40$$

$$(7.02)^{2.5} = 7.02^{2} \cdot \sqrt{7.02} = 49.28 \times 2.650 = 130.6$$

$$B_P = \frac{280 \times 22.40}{130.6} = \frac{6272}{130.6} = 48.0$$

**Step 5 — Chart reading**

Entering the Wageningen Bp–δ chart at $B_P = 48$, with $n = 280$ rpm contour:

- Optimum $D \approx 1.95$ m
- Optimum $P/D \approx 0.82$
- Optimum $\eta_0 \approx 0.60$
- Recommended $BAR \approx 0.55$

**Step 6 — Final pitch**

$$P = 0.82 \times 1.95 = 1.60 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Optimum propeller has **D = 1.95 m, P = 1.60 m, P/D = 0.82**, suitable for a 4-bladed Mn-bronze propeller.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is **cavitation**? Derive the expression for the **cavitation number** and explain its significance in selecting a propeller for a trawler. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Cavitation is the formation of vapour-filled cavities (bubbles) on the low-pressure (suction) side of a propeller blade when local static pressure falls below the vapour pressure of seawater.

**Derivation of Cavitation Number:**

Consider a blade element at depth $h$ below the free surface. The local static pressure at the blade section is:

$$p_{local} = p_0 - \Delta p$$

where $p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h$ is the ambient hydrostatic pressure and $\Delta p$ is the local pressure drop due to blade suction.

Cavitation begins when $p_{local} \leq p_v$. Defining a dimensionless ratio:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_a^{2}}$$

For a fully-submerged propeller at depth $h$:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_{atm} + \rho g h - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_a^{2}}$$

**Significance for Trawler Selection:**
1. Trawlers often operate at **shallow draft (3–5 m)** → low $h$ → low $\sigma$.
2. They have **bluff sterns** → high wake peaks → locally low $\sigma$ on blade tip.
3. The **minimum pressure coefficient** $C_{pmin}$ (from Wageningen polynomials) at the chosen $J$, $P/D$, and $BAR$ must satisfy:

$$C_{pmin} \;\leq\; \sigma$$

If not, increase $BAR$ (thicker blade) or reduce rpm to escape cavitation.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **material selection** for marine propellers used in fishing vessels, comparing **Manganese Bronze (Mn-bronze)**, **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (Ni-Al bronze)**, and **Stainless Steel**. State the application boundary of each. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Mn-Bronze | Ni-Al Bronze | Stainless Steel (17-4 PH / 254 SMO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Composition | Cu + ~1% Mn + Zn | Cu + 9–12% Al + 4–6% Ni + Fe | Fe-Cr-Ni-Mo |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 450–500 | 600–750 | 900–1100 |
| Corrosion Resistance in seawater | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cavitation-Erosion Resistance | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 2.5× | 4× |
| Casting Difficulty | Easy | Moderate | Difficult (forging/welding) |
| Weight | Heavy | Heavy (similar) | Lighter (~15% less) |
| Repairability (in-situ) | Easy (weldable) | Difficult | Very Difficult |

**Application Boundaries:**

- **Mn-Bronze** → Coastal and small fishing boats; engines up to **~750 kW per shaft**; service where extreme cavitation is not expected.
- **Ni-Al Bronze** → Deep-sea trawlers, purse-seiners, vessels above **750 kW**; preferred for ice-class and Arctic fishing; mandatory for many classification society notations.
- **Stainless Steel** → Modern high-power trawlers, naval auxiliary vessels, fast ferries; preferred where weight saving or extreme cavitation-erosion resistance is essential.

**Justification:** A KTU answer should explicitly tie material choice to *operating profile*: a stern trawler operating in trawl-mode 8 hours/day with significant wake-velocity variation **must use Ni-Al bronze** to survive 5–8 year docking intervals without blade replacement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wake fraction** when computing $V_a$ — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $V_a$ is taken equal to $V_s$.
> 2. **Mixing units**: knots vs m/s in $B_P$ — use **knots throughout** when reading the Wageningen chart.
> 3. **Skipping the cavitation check** after computing $D$ and $P/D$ — examiners in 2024 Scheme specifically test **CO3 application** which mandates cavitation screening.
> 4. **Recommending "any 4-blade" without justifying $Z$** — always state the reason: e.g., "4-blade selected over 3-blade to reduce torque fluctuation and vibration in trawl-mode."
> 5. **Forgetting the "selection" word in the question** — KTU wants a *recommended propeller*, not just a *calculated propeller*. End every answer with a one-line recommendation.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Propeller Selection = Power & RPM determination → Bp–δ chart → Cavitation check → Material selection.**
- **Standard seawater density** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — always state this value.
- **Wageningen B-Series** is the *universal* propeller series used in KTU numericals; remember polynomial range $J \in [0, 1.4]$, $P/D \in [0.5, 1.4]$.
- **$B_P = \dfrac{n \sqrt{DHP}}{V_a^{2.5}}$** with **$V_a$ in knots**, **$DHP$ in kW** — unit-mismatch is the #1 reason students lose marks.
- **Trawler P/D range: 0.7 – 0.9**; **Trawler BAR range: 0.55 – 0.75**; **Trawler blade number: 4 (preferred) or 5.**
- **Cavitation-safe condition:** $C_{pmin} \leq \sigma = (p_{atm} + \rho g h - p_v)/\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_a^2$.
- **Slip of 25–40%** is normal for trawlers; < 15% indicates *over-pitched*; > 50% indicates *under-pitched*.
- **Mn-bronze for small vessels, Ni-Al bronze for deep-sea trawlers, Stainless steel for premium high-power applications.**
- **FPP vs CPP**: choose FPP for single-mode trawlers; CPP for multi-mode (trawling + free-run + DP).
- Always end with a **justified recommendation**, not just numbers.
- **CO3 mapping:** Every Part B answer should explicitly link back to *Apply propeller theory to fishing vessels* — examiners award 1 mark for CO3 demonstration.
- **Bloom's Taxonomy levels** typically tested: *Apply* (numerical), *Analyse* (Bp–δ chart reading), *Evaluate* (cavitation + material trade-off), *Create* (final propeller specification).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propulsion Systems and Other Machinery/Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **propulsion system** in a fishing vessel is the integrated assembly of prime mover (engine), power transmission devices (gearbox, shafting), thrust-generating units (propeller or jet), and supporting control systems that convert the chemical energy of fuel into useful kinetic energy of the vessel through water. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Ocean Engineering / Naval Architecture & Ship Building*, this is formally defined under the discipline of **Marine Hydrodynamics and Marine Engineering**.

Other machinery and equipment on a fishing vessel include all auxiliary systems required for safe operation, fish capture, processing, navigation, and crew welfare — collectively called the **Auxiliary Machinery and Deck Machinery** complex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:**
> The syllabus emphasizes (a) types of propulsion, (b) propeller geometry and selection, (c) propulsive coefficient chain, (d) deck machinery, (e) fish-handling equipment, and (f) safety/navigation aids.

## 1.2 Components of a Marine Propulsion System

A standard fishing vessel propulsion chain consists of the following elements in series:

1. **Prime Mover** — Diesel engine (most common), Outboard Motor, Gas Turbine (rare).
2. **Reduction Gearbox** — Matches engine RPM to propeller RPM.
3. **Shaft Line** — Includes intermediate shaft, propeller shaft, and stern tube.
4. **Thrust Bearing** — Transmits propeller thrust to the hull.
5. **Propeller** — Converts rotational power into axial thrust.
6. **Appendages** — Rudder, Kort nozzle (optional), shaft brackets.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Fishing Boat as a Maruti Van**
> Imagine a Maruti Omni van that you want to use for fishing. The **engine** is the heart, the **gearbox** is the transmission that adjusts speed, the **propeller** is like the rear wheel — but instead of gripping tarmac, it "grips" water by throwing it backward (Newton's Third Law). The **rudder** is your steering wheel for the rear, and the **deck machinery** (winches, net haulers) are like the loading cranes on a truck. Every component must be sized for the harsh, salty, vibrating environment of the sea — just like how a tractor's components differ from a car's, despite both being road vehicles.

## 1.4 Classification of Fishing Vessel Propulsion

### A) Based on Power Source Location

| Type | Description | Typical Vessel Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Outboard Motor** | Engine + propeller external, mounted on transom | < 12 m (canoes, small craft) |
| **Inboard Engine** | Engine inside hull, propeller external via shaft | 12–35 m (trawlers, purse seiners) |
| **Stern Drive (Inboard/Outboard)** | Hybrid — engine inboard, drive unit outside | 6–15 m (sport, small commercial) |
| **Water Jet** | Pumps water through stern nozzle | 8–25 m (high-speed craft) |
| **Sail-Assisted** | Wind + auxiliary engine (hybrid) | Traditional/eco fishing |

### B) Based on Propeller Type

- **Fixed Pitch Propeller (FPP)** — Blades fixed, simple, robust. Most common on fishing vessels.
- **Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP)** — Pitch adjustable at standstill. Used on trawlers requiring frequent astern maneuvering.

### C) Prime Mover Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** is the key performance metric. For marine diesel engines, BSFC ≈ **190–220 g/kWh**. Lower is better.

- **High-Speed Diesel Engine** — RPM 1000–2500, used in small/medium craft.
- **Medium-Speed Diesel Engine** — RPM 400–1000, used in large trawlers.
- **Low-Speed Diesel Engine** — RPM 80–250, used in ocean-going vessels (rare for fishing).

## 1.5 Other Machinery & Equipment — Overview

Fishing vessels require a dense array of auxiliary systems:

> [!NOTE]
> **Categories of Auxiliary Equipment (for KTU):**
> 1. **Deck Machinery** — Winches (net, trawl, drum), capstans, cranes, derricks.
> 2. **Fish Handling** — Conveyors, gutting machines, ice machines, RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) plants.
> 3. **Hydraulic Systems** — Power pack for winches, steering, hatch covers.
> 4. **Steering Gear** — Hydraulic ram, tiller, autopilot.
> 5. **Bilge & Ballast Pumps** — Centrifugal pumps.
> 6. **Electrical** — Generator, alternator, batteries, switchboard.
> 7. **Navigation & Communication** — GPS, Radar, Echo sounder, VHF, GMDSS.
> 8. **Safety Equipment** — Life rafts, EPIRBs, fire extinguishers, life jackets.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Propulsive Power Chain

The propulsion system can be analyzed as an **energy transformation chain**, where each stage has an associated efficiency. The total propulsive efficiency is the product of all individual efficiencies.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Fuel Energy Input (F)** — Chemical energy released in engine combustion.
2. **Indicated Power (IP)** — Power developed inside engine cylinders.
3. **Brake Power (BP)** — Power available at engine flywheel after mechanical losses.
   - **Mechanical Efficiency** $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$
4. **Shaft Power (SP)** — Power reaching the propeller after gearbox and shaft losses.
   - **Transmission Efficiency** $\eta_t = \dfrac{SP}{BP}$
5. **Thrust Power (TP)** — Useful power delivered to water as kinetic energy.
   - **Propulsive Efficiency** $\eta_p = \dfrac{TP}{SP}$
6. **Effective Horsepower (EHP)** — Power required to tow the vessel at service speed.
   - **Quasi-Propulsive Efficiency** $\eta_d = \dfrac{EHP}{TP}$
7. **Overall Propulsive Efficiency** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_p \cdot \eta_d$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> The 14-mark question typically asks to compute EHP from vessel resistance and then back-calculate the required BP by accounting for every efficiency in the chain. Forgetting one stage loses **3 marks** automatically.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | "Why" it exists | "How" it works |
|---|---|---|
| Gearbox | Engine RPM is too high for efficient propeller operation | Steps down RPM via gear train, increases torque |
| Shaft | Must transmit torque over distance to submerged propeller | Uses torsional steel shaft with bearings |
| Thrust bearing | Propeller pushes water backward → reaction pushes vessel forward | Transfers thrust to hull structure |
| Propeller | Converts rotational motion to axial thrust | Rotating blades accelerate water mass astern |
| Rudder | Enables directional control | Deflects water flow laterally, creating side force |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and vertical bars in formulas below use the `\vert` notation (e.g., $\vert v \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is reserved as a column separator by markdown parsers.

### A) Resistance and Power Formulas

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP}$ | Total resistance = Frictional + Residuary + Appendage | kN |
| $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective horsepower (metric) | HP (metric) |
| $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{0.7355}$ | Effective horsepower (SI conversion) | kW |
| $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{550}$ | Effective horsepower (imperial) | HP (imperial) |
| $BP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Brake power required | kW or HP |
| $\eta_{overall} = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$ | Hull + Rotary + Transmission efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{DHP}$ | Quasi-propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |

### B) Propeller Geometry Formulas

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P = \pi \cdot D \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | Pitch (geometric advance per revolution) | m |
| $\dfrac{P}{D}$ | Pitch ratio (typically 0.5 to 1.4) | dimensionless |
| $s = \dfrac{V - n \cdot P}{n \cdot P}$ | Slip ratio (apparent) | dimensionless |
| $s_{real} = 1 - \dfrac{V_a}{n \cdot P}$ | Real slip | dimensionless |
| $V_a = V (1 - w)$ | Axial inflow velocity (with wake fraction $w$) | m/s |
| $T = \rho \cdot K_T \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ | Thrust from propeller (thrust coefficient form) | N |
| $Q = \rho \cdot K_Q \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5$ | Torque from propeller | N·m |
| $\eta_0 = \dfrac{T \cdot V_a}{2 \pi n Q}$ | Propeller open-water efficiency | dimensionless |

### C) Propeller Coefficients (KTU-Standard)

| Coefficient | Formula | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $K_T$ (Thrust) | $\dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | 0.15 to 0.55 |
| $K_Q$ (Torque) | $\dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | 0.02 to 0.08 |
| $J$ (Advance) | $\dfrac{V_a}{n D}$ | 0.0 to 1.5 |

### D) Hull-Propeller Interaction

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\eta_R = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | Relative rotative efficiency |
| $t$ | Thrust deduction fraction (typically 0.10–0.20) |
| $w$ | Wake fraction (typically 0.20–0.40 for single screw) |
| $\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | Hull efficiency (same form for single screw) |

### E) Auxiliary Machinery Sizing

| Equipment | Typical Sizing Rule |
|---|---|
| Net winch | Line pull = 15–25× vessel displacement (in tonnes) |
| Anchor windlass | Holding force = 40–50× anchor weight |
| Steering gear | Torque = $K \cdot \Delta \cdot L_{pp}$ (empirical) |
| Generator | $0.5$ to $1.0$ kW per GRT |
| RSW plant | $0.5$ kW per tonne fish capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** For a 20 m trawler operating in Indian/Kerala waters, the typical installed BP ranges between **200–500 kW** with a service speed of **9–11 knots**. The propeller is usually 3- or 4-bladed FPP of diameter 1.0–1.5 m with P/D ≈ 0.7.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Power Estimation:** Used by shipyards during contract design to specify main engine.
- **Propeller Selection:** B-Series or Wageningen B-Series charts use $K_T$, $K_Q$, $J$.
- **Fuel Planning:** Knowing EHP and BP, with BSFC, gives fuel consumption per nautical mile.
- **Regulatory:** Classification societies (IRS, BV, DNV) require validated $\eta_{overall}$ for sea trials.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thrust Equation from Blade Element Theory

### Objective:
Derive the **propeller thrust equation** $T = \rho \cdot K_T \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ starting from a single blade element.

### Step 1: Define Blade Element Geometry
Consider a small radial strip at radius $r$ from the propeller axis, with chord $c$ and thickness $dr$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Blade area at radius } r &= 2\pi r \\
\text{Projected blade element area} &= c \cdot dr \\
\text{Number of blades} &= Z \\
\text{Total blade area} &= A_E = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{A_E}{A_O} \quad \text{(Expanded area ratio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Define Velocity Triangle
At the blade element, water enters with:
- **Axial velocity** = $V_a = V(1-w)$
- **Rotational velocity** = $2 \pi n r$

The resultant relative velocity has components:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{axial}' &= V_a + \dfrac{1}{2} \Delta V_a \\
V_{tan}' &= 2 \pi n r - \dfrac{1}{2} \Delta V_t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta V_a$ and $\Delta V_t$ are induced velocity components (from lifting-line theory).

### Step 3: Force on Blade Element
The lift and drag forces on the element produce thrust and tangential force components:
$$
\begin{aligned}
dT &= \dfrac{1}{2} \rho W^2 c \cdot (C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi) \cdot dr \\
dQ &= \dfrac{1}{2} \rho W^2 c \cdot (C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi) \cdot r \cdot dr
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate Over the Blade
Integrating from $r = r_h$ (hub) to $r = R$ (tip):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{r_h}^{R} dT \\
Q &= \int_{r_h}^{R} dQ
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Non-Dimensionalize Using Dimensional Analysis
The only physically meaningful non-dimensional groupings are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_T &= \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} \\
K_Q &= \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} \\
J &= \dfrac{V_a}{nD}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Final Thrust Equation
Solving for thrust:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \rho \cdot K_T \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the fourth power of D?**
> This is critical for KTU exams. Because $K_T$ is dimensionless and the only other physical dimension involved is $\rho$ (kg/m³), $n$ (1/s), and $D$ (m), dimensional analysis **forces** the form $T = f(\rho, n^2, D^4)$. This is why a 10% increase in propeller diameter increases thrust by **46%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slip Ratio

### Objective:
Derive the apparent slip ratio $s$ for a fixed-pitch propeller.

### Step 1: Geometric Advance
In one revolution, a propeller theoretically advances by:
$$
P_{\text{geometric}} = \pi D \tan\theta \quad \text{[m/rev]}
$$

### Step 2: Actual Advance
In reality, due to wake and slip, the vessel moves:
$$
V_a = V(1-w) \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

Distance covered in one revolution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{actual}} &= V_a \cdot \dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{V(1-w)}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Slip Definition
Slip is the relative difference between geometric and actual advance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \dfrac{n \cdot P - V_a}{n \cdot P} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{V_a}{n P}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Substituting $V_a = V(1-w)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 1 - \dfrac{V(1-w)}{n P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *apparent slip* (using $V$) with *real slip* (using $V_a$). The exam expects **real slip** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Propulsive Efficiency Chain

### Objective:
Show that $\eta_{overall} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_p \cdot \eta_d$.

### Step 1: Define Each Power Level
$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \text{Indicated Power (in cylinder)} \\
BP &= \text{Brake Power (flywheel)} \\
SP &= \text{Shaft Power (at propeller)} \\
DHP &= \text{Delivered HP (= SP)} \\
TP &= \text{Thrust Power} \\
EHP &= \text{Effective HP (tow rope)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Define Each Efficiency
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_m &= \dfrac{BP}{IP} \quad \text{(mechanical)} \\
\eta_t &= \dfrac{SP}{BP} \quad \text{(transmission)} \\
\eta_d &= \dfrac{DHP}{TP} \quad \text{(propulsive — sometimes combined with } \eta_R\text{)} \\
\eta_R &= \dfrac{\eta_d}{\eta_H} = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w} \quad \text{(relative rotative)} \\
\eta_H &= \dfrac{1-t}{1-w} \quad \text{(hull efficiency)} \\
\eta_0 &= \dfrac{TP}{SP} \quad \text{(propeller open-water)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply the Chain
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= \dfrac{EHP}{BP} = \dfrac{EHP}{TP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{SP} \cdot \dfrac{SP}{BP} \\
&= \eta_d \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A 25 m trawler has $R_T = 35$ kN at $V = 10$ knots. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$, transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.97$, wake fraction $w = 0.25$, thrust deduction $t = 0.15$, open-water propeller efficiency $\eta_0 = 0.62$. Calculate the required Brake Power in kW.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert speed
$$
V = 10 \text{ knots} = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 2: Calculate EHP
$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{0.7355} = \dfrac{35 \times 5.144}{0.7355} \\
&= \dfrac{180.04}{0.7355} \\
&\approx 244.8 \text{ HP (metric)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Convert EHP to kW
$$
EHP_{kW} = 244.8 \times 0.7355 \approx 180.0 \text{ kW}
$$

Step 4: Calculate Hull Efficiency
$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w} = \dfrac{1-0.15}{1-0.25} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.75} = 1.133
$$

Step 5: Quasi-Propulsive Efficiency
$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 = 1.133 \times 0.62 = 0.703
$$

Step 6: Brake Power Required
$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \dfrac{EHP_{kW}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_D} \\
&= \dfrac{180.0}{0.90 \times 0.97 \times 0.703} \\
&= \dfrac{180.0}{0.6138} \\
&\approx 293.3 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result: BP ≈ 293 kW ≈ 400 HP** — typical for a 25 m coastal trawler.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Combinations

| Given | Find | Formula Path |
|---|---|---|
| $R_T$, $V$ | EHP | $EHP = R_T V / 0.7355$ |
| EHP, all $\eta$'s | BP | $BP = EHP / (\eta_m \eta_t \eta_D)$ |
| $V$, $n$, $P$, $w$ | Real slip | $s = 1 - V(1-w)/(nP)$ |
| $T$, $n$, $D$, $\rho$ | $K_T$ | $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ |
| $K_T$, $J$ | Open-water efficiency | $\eta_0 = J K_T / (2\pi K_Q)$ |
| BSFC, BP | Fuel rate | $m_f = BSFC \times BP$ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Wake Fraction" in a single-screw fishing vessel and state its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**
Wake fraction $w$ is the ratio of the velocity difference between the propeller disc and the free stream to the free-stream velocity. It represents the reduction in water velocity at the propeller due to the hull boundary layer.
$$
w = \dfrac{V - V_a}{V}
$$
Typical range for single-screw fishing vessels: **0.20 to 0.40**. For full-form trawlers, $w \approx 0.30$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for typical range)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a "Kort Nozzle" on a fishing vessel propeller?**

**Model Answer:**
A Kort nozzle is a streamlined annular ring fitted around the propeller, used primarily on **trawlers and tugboats**. Its functions are:
1. Increases thrust at low speeds by recovering energy from the slipstream.
2. Protects the propeller from trawl warp entanglement.
3. Improves bollard pull by 20–40%.
4. Reduces propeller tip cavitation.

It is most effective at **low advance coefficients ($J < 0.6$)**, typical of trawling operations.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** A 22 m fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Total resistance $R_T = 28$ kN at service speed $V = 9.5$ knots
- Propulsive coefficient $\eta_D = 0.65$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.97$
- Wake fraction $w = 0.28$, thrust deduction $t = 0.14$

**(a)** Calculate the **Effective Horsepower (EHP)** and the **Brake Power (BP)** required.
**(b)** Explain with a sketch the components of the propulsive power chain from fuel to vessel motion.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1: Convert speed**
$$
V = 9.5 \times 0.5144 = 4.887 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate EHP in kW**
$$
EHP = \dfrac{R_T \times V}{0.7355} = \dfrac{28 \times 4.887}{0.7355} = \dfrac{136.84}{0.7355} \approx 186.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Hull Efficiency**
$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w} = \dfrac{1-0.14}{1-0.28} = \dfrac{0.86}{0.72} \approx 1.194
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Brake Power**
$$
BP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_D} = \dfrac{186.0}{0.90 \times 0.97 \times 0.65}
$$
$$
BP = \dfrac{186.0}{0.5675} \approx 327.7 \text{ kW} \approx 445 \text{ HP}
$$

**Final Answer:** EHP ≈ **186 kW**, BP ≈ **328 kW (445 HP)**

*(7 marks: 2 for EHP, 3 for hull efficiency + BP, 2 for final answer with units)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

The propulsive power chain consists of the following sequential stages:

$$
\text{Fuel Energy} \rightarrow \text{IP} \xrightarrow{\eta_m} \text{BP} \xrightarrow{\eta_t} \text{SP} \xrightarrow{\eta_0} \text{TP} \xrightarrow{\eta_H} \text{EHP}
$$

| Stage | Quantity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fuel Energy | Chemical energy from marine diesel (BSFC ≈ 200 g/kWh) |
| 2 | IP | Power developed in engine cylinder |
| 3 | BP | Power at flywheel after mechanical losses |
| 4 | SP (DHP) | Power at propeller after shaft losses |
| 5 | TP | Thrust power delivered to water |
| 6 | EHP | Power to tow vessel at $V$ |

**Sketch Description** (would be drawn in exam):

```
Fuel → Engine → Gearbox → Shaft → Propeller → Water → Hull Motion
 ↓       ↓         ↓         ↓        ↓          ↓         ↓
Chem    IP      Mech Loss  Trans   Slip       Wake     Vessel
Energy                         Loss   Loss      Loss    Resistance
```

The product of efficiencies gives:
$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for chain description, 3 for sketch, 2 for formula)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** 
**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the **types of propellers** used in fishing vessels and explain **Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP)** operation.
**(b)** A 4-bladed propeller of diameter 1.4 m rotates at 240 RPM in a wake of $w = 0.30$. The vessel speed is 9 knots, pitch ratio $P/D = 0.75$. Calculate the **apparent slip** and **real slip**.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Classification of Propellers:**

1. **By Pitch Type:**
   - Fixed Pitch Propeller (FPP) — blades fixed to hub.
   - Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP) — pitch varied by hydraulic mechanism.

2. **By Number of Blades:**
   - 3-bladed, 4-bladed, 5-bladed, 6-bladed.

3. **By Blade Form:**
   - Wide-blade, narrow-blade, highly-skewed.

4. **By Application:**
   - Surface-piercing (small craft).
   - Submerged (large vessels).

**CPP Operation:**
In a CPP, the hub contains a hydraulic mechanism that rotates each blade about its own axis while the hub itself rotates with the shaft. This allows pitch to be changed from full ahead to full astern without changing engine direction — ideal for trawlers requiring frequent maneuvering during net hauling.

*(7 marks: 3 for classification, 4 for CPP explanation)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.4$ m
- $n = 240$ RPM = $240/60 = 4$ rev/s
- $V = 9$ knots = $9 \times 0.5144 = 4.630$ m/s
- $w = 0.30$
- $P/D = 0.75 \Rightarrow P = 0.75 \times 1.4 = 1.05$ m

**Step 1: Calculate Geometric Advance per Revolution**
$$
n \cdot P = 4 \times 1.05 = 4.20 \text{ m/rev}
$$

**Step 2: Apparent Slip (using V)**
$$
s_{app} = \dfrac{nP - V}{nP} = \dfrac{4.20 - 4.630}{4.20}
$$

Since the result is negative, the propeller is **over-pitched**. Take absolute value:
$$
s_{app} = \dfrac{4.630 - 4.20}{4.20} = \dfrac{0.430}{4.20} \approx 0.102 \text{ or } 10.2\%
$$

**Step 3: Real Slip (using V_a)**
$$
V_a = V(1-w) = 4.630 \times 0.70 = 3.241 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
s_{real} = \dfrac{nP - V_a}{nP} = \dfrac{4.20 - 3.241}{4.20} = \dfrac{0.959}{4.20} \approx 0.228 \text{ or } 22.8\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Apparent slip ≈ 10.2%** (over-pitched condition)
- **Real slip ≈ 22.8%**

*(7 marks: 2 for converting data, 2 for apparent slip, 3 for real slip)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for KTU Students:**
>
> 1. **Confusing apparent vs real slip (–2 marks)** — Always check whether to use $V$ or $V_a$ in the slip formula. Default to **real slip** unless told otherwise.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in EHP (–1 mark)** — Mixing SI (kW), metric HP, and imperial HP causes dimensional chaos. KTU expects **kW** in all modern papers.
>
> 3. **Omitting the hull efficiency term (–3 marks)** — Many students write $BP = EHP / (\eta_m \eta_t)$ and forget $\eta_D$ or $\eta_H$. This is a **guaranteed partial loss**.
>
> 4. **Sketch marks for diagrams (–2 marks)** — Even rough block diagrams showing the chain **fuel → vessel motion** earn marks. Skipping the sketch = lost marks.
>
> 5. **Sign convention for slip (–1 mark)** — Negative slip just means "over-pitched"; don't write impossible negative slips without explanation.
>
> 6. **Using $V_a$ instead of $V$ in $EHP$ calculation (–2 marks)** — $EHP$ is calculated with **vessel speed $V$**, not inflow velocity $V_a$. $V_a$ is only for propeller-related formulas.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3: Propulsion Systems and Other Machinery)**
>
> ✅ **Propulsion chain:** Fuel → IP → BP → SP → TP → EHP. Memorize every $\eta$.
>
> ✅ **EHP formula:** $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 0.7355$ (for kW) is the most-tested formula.
>
> ✅ **Slip:** Always present as a percentage. Real slip uses $V_a$.
>
> ✅ **Wake $w$:** 0.20–0.40 (single screw); **increases** with hull fullness.
>
> ✅ **Thrust deduction $t$:** 0.10–0.20; higher for full-block trawlers.
>
> ✅ **Hull efficiency** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w) > 1$ is **normal** — don't panic.
>
> ✅ **FPP vs CPP:** FPP is robust and cheap; CPP is for frequent maneuvering.
>
> ✅ **Kort Nozzle:** Used on trawlers for bollard pull; ineffective at high $J$.
>
> ✅ **Deck Machinery:** Driven hydraulically from main engine's PTO (Power Take-Off).
>
> ✅ **BSFC** of marine diesel ≈ 200 g/kWh; lower is better.
>
> ✅ **Propeller $K_T$, $K_Q$:** Read from Wageningen B-Series charts for given $J$, $P/D$, $EAR$.
>
> ✅ **Auxiliary systems to remember:** Bilge pump, fire pump, ballast pump, generator, RSW plant, steering gear, winches, navigation.
>
> ✅ **Steering gear torque** empirical formula: $T_q = K \cdot \Delta \cdot L_{pp}$ (K = constant).
>
> ✅ **Typical small trawler BP:** 100–500 kW; large ocean trawler: 1000–3000 kW.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Propulsion Systems and Other Machinery/Equipment**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OBE guidelines | Course Code: OENST833*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Propulsion Systems and Selection of Main Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Propulsion Systems and Selection of Main Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of fishing vessel engineering, **Propulsion System** refers to the integrated assembly of components — comprising the **Main Engine**, **Shaft Line / Transmission**, **Propulsive Device (Propeller / Waterjet / Cycloidal Drive)**, and supporting auxiliaries — that converts the chemical energy of fuel into useful **Thrust Force** to overcome the vessel's resistance and impart forward motion through water. The **selection of the main engine** is a multi-criteria decision-making process that involves matching the engine's **Brake Power (BHP / kW)**, **rotational speed (rpm)**, **fuel type**, **weight-to-power ratio (kg/kW)**, **physical dimensions**, and **specific fuel consumption (SFC, g/kWh)** with the vessel's **Effective Power (EHP) demand**, operational profile, regulatory constraints, and lifecycle economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For fishing vessels operating in coastal and deep-sea zones, the propulsion system must satisfy three simultaneous conditions: **(i)** adequate bollard pull for trawling operations, **(ii)** high manoeuvrability for station-keeping near fishing grounds, and **(iii)** fuel efficiency for extended voyages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are propelling a small dinghy across a pond. You have several options:
- **Paddle / Oar (analogy: Low-power outboard or oar-propelled craft)** — simple, but slow and tiring.
- **Fixed-pitch propeller driven by a small petrol engine (analogy: Conventional inboard + FPP)** — like a ceiling fan permanently set at one speed; you control forward motion by engine throttle, not pitch.
- **Controllable-pitch propeller (CPP) (analogy: Variable-speed fan blades)** — like a ceiling fan whose blade angle you can change while running, giving instant thrust reversal and fine control — perfect for manoeuvring a trawler near nets.
- **Waterjet (analogy: Garden hose nozzle)** — you suck water in and shoot it out the back; great for shallow waters, but loses efficiency at low speeds.
- **Cycloidal / Voith-Schneider (analogy: Spinning disc throwing water sideways)** — like a rotating turntable with vertical blades that "scoop" water; unbeatable for tug-class manoeuvrability.

The "main engine" is essentially the heart of this system — analogous to choosing between a diesel truck engine, a hybrid car engine, or a gas turbine jet engine for different missions. **Selection of main engine = choosing the right "heart"** for the vessel's mission profile.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants & Standards)

- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** **190–220 g/kWh** (typical for marine medium-speed diesel).
- **Service Speed Factor (K):** typically **1.10 to 1.20** (Margin added to EHP to obtain BHP).
- **Power-to-Weight Ratio:** **2.0 – 5.0 kg/kW** for medium-speed marine diesels.
- **Propulsive Efficiency (η_D):** typically **0.55 – 0.70** in well-designed installations.
- **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (η_P):** typically **0.50 – 0.65**.

> [!NOTE]
> These ranges are **KTU 2024 reference values** for fishing vessels of 12 m to 35 m LOA and are the standards examiners expect in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Marine Propulsion Systems

Marine propulsion systems are classified along three primary axes: **energy source**, **thrust-generating mechanism**, and **transmission architecture**.

#### 2.1.1 By Thrust-Generating Device

1. **Conventional Fixed-Pitch Propeller (FPP) + Shaft Line**
   - Most widely used for fishing vessels above 15 m.
   - Thrust generated by hydrodynamic lift on helical blades.
   - Direction reversal achieved by **clutch + reverse-reduction gear** or by **engine stop-and-reverse** (trolling mode).

2. **Controllable-Pitch Propeller (CPP)**
   - Blade pitch is hydraulically varied in operation (even at full rpm).
   - Enables **full thrust at zero ship speed** (essential for trawling).
   - Allows rapid reverse without stopping the engine.

3. **Waterjet Propulsion**
   - Water is drawn in through an intake duct, accelerated by an impeller, and discharged through a steering nozzle.
   - **No exposed propeller** → ideal for trawlers working with bottom nets.
   - Efficiency drops sharply below **8 knots**.

4. **Voith-Schneider Propeller (VSP) / Cycloidal Drive**
   - Vertical blades on a rotating disc generate thrust in **any direction** instantaneously.
   - Used in harbour tugs; **rare in commercial fishing** due to high capital cost.

5. **Outboard & Stern-Drive Units (OBM / I/O)**
   - For small craft < 12 m (artisanal fishing boats, gill netters).
   - Combines engine, gearbox, and propeller in a self-contained unit.

6. **Sail-Assisted / Hybrid / Diesel-Electric**
   - Auxiliary sail rigs, batteries, or generator-motor configurations for fuel saving on long-line tuna vessels.

#### 2.1.2 By Energy Source

| Type | Typical Use in Fishing |
|------|------------------------|
| **Medium-speed diesel (500–1500 rpm)** | Trawlers, purse seiners (most common) |
| **High-speed diesel (>1500 rpm)** | Small gill netters, fast craft |
| **Gas turbine** | Rare — only in high-speed patrol/research vessels |
| **Hybrid diesel-electric** | Modern fuel-efficient long-liners |

### 2.2 Logic Steps for Selection of Main Engine

1. **Compute Effective Horse Power (EHP)** at service speed from resistance data.
2. **Add sea margin (15–20%)** to obtain **Shaft Horse Power (SHP)**.
3. **Divide by propulsive efficiency (η_D ≈ 0.60)** to obtain **Brake Horse Power (BHP)**.
4. **Add service margin (10–25%)** based on operating profile.
5. **Match BHP to an engine from manufacturer's continuous-service rating** (typically 80–85% of MCR).
6. **Verify physical integration**: engine room length, height, weight, fuel tank capacity, exhaust routing, and shaft alignment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | EHP from resistance | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_S}{75}$ | HP (metric) | Towing tank / series resistance data |
| 2 | SHP | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S}$ | HP | Hull + Rotary + Shaft efficiencies |
| 3 | DHP (Delivered HP) | $DHP = SHP$ | HP | Power reaching propeller |
| 4 | BHP (Brake HP) | $BHP = \dfrac{DHP}{\eta_D}$ | HP | Power at engine flywheel |
| 5 | Service BHP | $BHP_{svc} = BHP \cdot (1+M)$ | HP | M = margin (0.10–0.25) |
| 6 | Propulsive Coefficient | $\eta_P = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_D$ | — | Overall efficiency |
| 7 | Fuel consumption | $F = BSFC \cdot BHP$ | g/h or kg/h | Endurance / tank capacity |
| 8 | Endurance | $E = \dfrac{Fuel_{cap}}{F \cdot 0.85}$ | hours | Range / mission planning |
| 9 | Bollard Pull Power | $P_{BP} = \dfrac{T_{BP} \cdot V_T}{75 \cdot \eta_D}$ | HP | Trawling duty |
| 10 | Power-to-weight | $P/W = \dfrac{BHP \cdot 0.746}{Mass_{eng}}$ | kW/kg | Engine selection filter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mnemonic (KTU):** "**E → D → B → S**" — Effective → Delivered → Brake → Service. Always march in this direction when working power chains.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- For a **15 m trawler** operating off Kerala coast: BHP typically **180–280 kW**, medium-speed diesel, FPP + reverse-reduction gear.
- For a **30 m deep-sea tuna long-liner**: BHP **600–1100 kW**, CPP preferred for station-keeping, BSFC target **≤200 g/kWh**.
- For a **9 m gill netter**: 30–60 kW outboard diesel, no reduction gear.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Resistance to Main Engine Selection

We begin with the **fundamental towing power equation** and walk through every algebraic transition.

**Given:** A fishing trawler of length $L_{WL} = 22$ m, service speed $V_S = 9.5$ knots, total resistance $R_T = 22.5$ kN at $V_S$, hull efficiency $\eta_H = 1.05$, rotary efficiency $\eta_R = 1.00$, shaft efficiency $\eta_S = 0.97$, propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.60$, service margin $M = 0.20$.

**Goal:** Select main engine BHP.

---

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= 9.5 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s per knot} \\
    &= 4.887 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The factor **0.5144 m/s per knot** arises because 1 nautical mile = 1852 m and 1 hour = 3600 s.

---

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Horse Power (EHP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_S}{75} \quad [\text{metric HP}] \\
    &= \dfrac{22{,}500 \text{ N} \times 4.887 \text{ m/s}}{75} \\
    &= \dfrac{109{,}957.5}{75} \\
    &= 1466.1 \text{ HP (metric)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $R_T \cdot V_S$ is power in watts; divide by **75 (since 1 metric HP = 75 kgf·m/s ≈ 735.5 W)**.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Shaft Horse Power (SHP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SHP &= \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S} \\
    &= \dfrac{1466.1}{1.05 \times 1.00 \times 0.97} \\
    &= \dfrac{1466.1}{1.0185} \\
    &= 1439.4 \text{ HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The **mechanical efficiency chain** $\eta_H \eta_R \eta_S$ accounts for energy losses in wake fraction, thrust deduction, bearing friction, and gear mesh.

---

**Step 4 — Compute Brake Horse Power (BHP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BHP &= \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_D} \\
    &= \dfrac{1439.4}{0.60} \\
    &= 2399.0 \text{ HP} \\
    &\approx 2400 \text{ HP} \approx 1789 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $\eta_D$ (propulsive or quasi-propulsive efficiency) is the **biggest single loss**; it captures propeller's own hydrodynamic inefficiency and the wake-thrust deduction interaction.

---

**Step 5 — Apply service margin for fishing duty**

Fishing vessels experience **continuous low-load operation** (trawling at 3–5 knots), **storm conditions**, and **biofouling**. KTU's recommended margin for a working trawler is **20%**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BHP_{MCR} &= BHP \times (1 + M) \\
          &= 2400 \times 1.20 \\
          &= 2880 \text{ HP} \\
          &\approx 2146 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Continuous Service Rating (CSR) of the selected engine must be **≥ 2880 HP**, with MCR typically 10–15% higher.

---

**Step 6 — Engine selection (Analytical Matrix)**

| Engine candidate | CSR (kW) | Mass (kg) | BSFC (g/kWh) | Length × Width × Height (m) | Match? |
|------------------|----------|-----------|--------------|----------------------------|--------|
| CAT C32 ACERT    | 1193     | 2400      | 213          | 2.4 × 1.4 × 1.5            | ✗ (under) |
| **Cummins QSK60-M** | **2165** | **5200** | **198**     | **3.6 × 1.6 × 2.0**        | ✓ (exact) |
| MAN L+V 27/38    | 2700     | 7100      | 192          | 4.1 × 1.6 × 2.2            | ✓ (over) |
| Wärtsilä 6L20    | 1080     | 2800      | 200          | 2.6 × 1.3 × 1.7            | ✗ (under) |

> The **Cummins QSK60-M at 2165 kW CSR** is the closest match — slightly under (3.7% deficit, acceptable for a 20% margin) and is the KTU-preferred textbook example for a 22 m trawler.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Propulsion System Selection

| Criterion (weight) | FPP + Reverse Gear | CPP | Waterjet | Outboard | Voith-Schneider |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----|----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Initial cost (25%)** | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ |
| **Manoeuvrability (25%)** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Fuel efficiency (20%)** | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| **Shallow-water / Trawl safety (15%)** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Maintenance ease (10%)** | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Suitability <12 m (5%)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For trawlers in Kerala coast** (shallow coastal waters, frequent net-handling), the **scoring** tips in favour of **Waterjet** for small vessels and **CPP** for medium trawlers where bollard pull is critical.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fishing Vessel Propulsion System (FPP + Diesel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Marine Diesel Engine] --> B[Flywheel Coupling]
    B --> C[Reverse Reduction Gearbox]
    C --> D[Intermediate Shaft]
    D --> E[Tail Shaft with Bearing]
    E --> F[Fixed Pitch Propeller]
    F --> G[Thrust Generated in Water]
    H[Fuel Tank] --> A
    I[Sea Water Cooling] --> A
    J[Exhaust Manifold] --> A
    K[Governor / ECU] --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — CPP Propulsion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENG[Main Engine] --> RG[Reduction Gearbox]
    RG --> SH[Shaft Line]
    SH --> CPP[Controllable Pitch Propeller Hub]
    CPP --> P1[Blade 1]
    CPP --> P2[Blade 2]
    CPP --> P3[Blade 3]
    CPP --> P4[Blade 4]
    HPU[Hydraulic Pitch Unit] --> CPP
    CTL[Bridge Pitch Lever] --> HPU
    ENG --> CL[Clutch Lockup]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Waterjet Propulsion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Diesel Engine] --> GB[Jet Drive Gearbox]
    GB --> IMP[Impeller]
    IMP --> DUCT[Intake Duct Below Hull]
    DUCT --> IMP
    IMP --> NZL[Reversible Steering Nozzle]
    NZL --> DIS[Discharge to Sea]
    NZL --> RVS[Reverse Bucket]
    RVS --> DIS
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Main Engine Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Mission Profile] --> B[Step 2: Resistance Estimation]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute EHP and SHP]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply Efficiencies to Find BHP]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add Service Margin]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Engineering Filters]
    F --> F1[Weight and Space]
    F --> F2[Fuel Type and BSFC]
    F --> F3[Emission Compliance]
    F --> F4[Maintenance and Spares]
    F1 --> G[Step 7: Select from Catalogue]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Final Engine Selection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `F1`) and labels contain no markdown formatting — satisfying the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the major components of a conventional shaft-line propulsion system for a fishing trawler and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Main Engine** — converts fuel chemical energy into rotational mechanical power at the flywheel.
2. **Clutch / Coupling** — engages/disengages power transmission and protects against torsional shock.
3. **Reverse-Reduction Gearbox** — reduces engine rpm to propeller rpm and provides astern rotation.
4. **Intermediate / Tail Shaft** — transmits torque from gearbox to propeller.
5. **Stern Tube with Bearings** — supports the shaft at the hull penetration with minimum friction.
6. **Fixed-Pitch Propeller** — converts shaft torque into hydrodynamic thrust.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare Fixed-Pitch Propeller (FPP) and Controllable-Pitch Propeller (CPP) for fishing vessel application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FPP | CPP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Pitch | Fixed at design | Variable in operation |
| Reverse thrust | Requires reverse gear / engine reversal | Achievable by feathering blades |
| Bollard pull | Limited (engine stalls at low speed) | High — full thrust at zero ship speed |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (hydraulic hub, control system) |
| Suitability for trawling | Marginal | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Simple | Complex (hub servicing) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer with sub-part structure.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of a **waterjet propulsion system** suitable for a 14 m fishing trawler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of waterjet propulsion for fishing vessels operating in shallow coastal waters, with specific reference to trawl gear safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Waterjet Propulsion System:**

A waterjet system comprises an **intake duct** fitted on the hull bottom (forward of the transom), an **impeller** (mixed-flow or axial), a **gearbox** driven by the main diesel engine, and a **steerable discharge nozzle** at the transom.

1. The engine drives the impeller through a marine gearbox (often with a "get-home" facility).
2. Water enters the intake duct under the forward motion of the vessel (ram effect) augmented by the impeller suction.
3. The impeller accelerates the water radially and axially, converting engine torque into kinetic energy of the water column.
4. The high-velocity jet is discharged through a **steerable nozzle** at the transom; the reaction produces forward thrust.
5. **Reversal** is achieved by a **reverse deflector bucket** that folds down in front of the nozzle, redirecting the jet forward — instantaneous and at any engine speed.
6. **Steering** is by rotating the nozzle (±35°), controlled from the bridge.

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.3.)*

**(b) Advantages and Limitations:**

*Advantages (4 marks):*
- **No exposed propeller** — trawl nets, warps, and cod-end cannot foul the propeller; ideal for bottom trawling.
- **Shallow-water operation** — entire intake is flush with the hull, with minimum drag projection.
- **Excellent manoeuvrability** — vectored thrust + reverse bucket gives tug-like control.
- **Lower underwater radiated noise** — better for fish detection and reduced marine mammal impact.

*Limitations (3 marks):*
- **Poor propulsive efficiency at low speeds** (< 8 knots) — losses in duct, impeller, and nozzle.
- **Cavitation risk** in aerated water from surface trawl hauling.
- **High capital cost** and specialised maintenance.
- **Susceptible to debris ingestion** (plastic, ropes) at the intake.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A 20 m fishing trawler is to operate at a service speed of 10 knots. The estimated total resistance is 30 kN. Assuming $\eta_H = 1.05$, $\eta_R = 1.00$, $\eta_S = 0.97$, and $\eta_D = 0.60$, **calculate the required Brake Horse Power (BHP)**. Add a **20% service margin** and recommend an engine from the catalogue in Section 3.1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **criteria** that govern the selection of a main engine for a fishing vessel, covering at least **six technical and economic factors**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s**
$$V_S = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Horse Power**
$$EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_S}{75} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 5.144}{75} = 2057.6 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft Horse Power**
$$SHP = \dfrac{1466.1}{1.05 \times 1.00 \times 0.97} = \dfrac{2057.6}{1.0185} = 2020.0 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 4 — Brake Horse Power**
$$BHP = \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{2020.0}{0.60} = 3366.7 \text{ HP} \approx 2511 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — With 20% margin**
$$BHP_{MCR} = 3366.7 \times 1.20 = 4040 \text{ HP} \approx 3013 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation**
From the catalogue: **Wärtsilä 6L20** (1080 kW) is too small; **MAN L+V 27/38** (2700 kW) is the nearest fit. Selecting **two engines in twin-shaft** (e.g., 2 × 1500 kW) provides redundancy — a typical KTU-recommended solution for vessels over 18 m.

**(b) Selection Criteria for Main Engine:**

1. **Power Match** — BHP must equal or exceed (EHP / η_P) × (1 + margin).
2. **Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — Lower BSFC means longer endurance and lower operating cost.
3. **Weight-to-Power Ratio (kg/kW)** — Critical for vessel stability and draft.
4. **Physical Dimensions** — Engine room length, height, and width; also alignment with shaft.
5. **Reliability and Service Interval** — Major overhaul period (e.g., 12,000 h vs 24,000 h).
6. **Fuel Availability** — HSD vs LDO vs marine gas oil (MGO); bunker port network.
7. **Emission Compliance** — IMO Tier II / Tier III (especially for vessels > 130 kW from 2024).
8. **Capital Cost and Lifecycle Cost** — Including spares and trained manpower.
9. **Starting and Reversal** — Quick-reversing capability for trawling.
10. **Auxiliary Compatibility** — PTO for hydraulic pumps, alternators, and refrigeration compressors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert knots to m/s** (factor 0.5144) before computing EHP. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 2. Using **wrong efficiency denominator** — confusing $\eta_P$ (total) with $\eta_D$ (propulsive). **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Not applying service margin** — KTU expects at least 10–20% for fishing vessels. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 4. Selecting engine from catalogue **without justification** — must show power + weight + BSFC cross-check. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 5. Confusing **FPP reverse mechanism** — FPP needs reverse gear, CPP does not. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Propulsion System = Engine + Transmission + Propulsor.** Never treat them in isolation.
- **EHP → SHP → BHP** is the power chain (chain of efficiency divisions).
- **FPP** = cheap, simple, common; **CPP** = high manoeuvrability, ideal for trawlers; **Waterjet** = safest for trawl gear; **Outboard** = small artisanal craft; **Voith-Schneider** = exceptional manoeuvrability but rare in fishing.
- **Service margin:** 10% (trollers), 20% (trawlers), 25% (storm-zone operations).
- **BSFC reference:** 190–220 g/kWh; lower is better.
- **Power-to-weight ratio:** aim for ≤ 5 kg/kW for displacement trawlers.
- **Selection criteria (memorise six minimum):** power match, BSFC, weight, dimensions, reliability, cost, emissions, fuel availability.
- **V_S × 0.5144** = m/s. **1 metric HP = 75 kgf·m/s** (use this in EHP formula).
- **Efficiencies** typically: $\eta_H \approx 1.05$, $\eta_R \approx 1.00$, $\eta_S \approx 0.97$, $\eta_D \approx 0.55–0.65$.
- **Twin-shaft** arrangements offer **redundancy** for vessels above 18 m — KTU expects this in long-answer solutions.
- **Emission norms** for fishing vessels: IMO MARPOL Annex VI — Tier II for new builds from 2024 (130–2000 kW range).
- **Kerala-specific consideration:** most fishing vessels operate in **shallow coastal waters (5–40 m depth)** → favour waterjet or CPP with high bollard pull.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Equipment's/Instruments for Fish Finding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Equipment's/Instruments for Fish Finding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SELECTION OF EQUIPMENT'S / INSTRUMENTS FOR FISH FINDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Fish finding equipment* refers to the suite of electro-acoustic, optical, and electronic instruments installed aboard a fishing vessel that detect, locate, quantify, and visualize the presence of fish shoals, seabed topography, and submerged obstacles to enable efficient and sustainable fishing operations.

In the context of KTU Module 3, "selection" is not merely a procurement exercise — it is a **design trade-off decision** that balances detection range, target resolution, vessel size, fishing gear type, target species behaviour, water depth, and economic viability (CAPEX + OPEX).

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat in the Sea"

Imagine a **bat flying in total darkness** using high-frequency squeaks. The bat screams, listens for the echo, and "draws a picture" of the world using the time it takes for the sound to bounce back. A fish-finding instrument works on **exactly the same principle**, except:

| Feature | Bat (in air) | Fish Finder (in water) |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Air | Seawater |
| Signal | Acoustic chirp (~50 kHz) | Acoustic ping (~50–200 kHz) |
| Speed | ~343 m/s | **~1500 m/s** |
| Sensor | Ears | **Piezoelectric transducer** |
| Display | Brain | **LCD/LED screen** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why water matters:** Sound travels **~4.4 times faster in seawater** than in air because water is denser and far less compressible. This is why fish-finders operate at much higher frequencies and over much longer ranges than air sonars.

### 1.2 Classification of Fish-Finding Instruments (KTU Board-Approved Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies fish-finding instruments into **three primary functional families**:

1. **Acoustic (SONAR-based) instruments** — Echo sounder, sonar, net recorder, sonar scope.
2. **Optical instruments** — Underwater cameras, ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), fish-lights.
3. **Electronic / Navigational integrators** — GPS plotters, AIS, echo-GPS integrators, sounders with chart overlays.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, always open by stating the **three families** before drilling into any one — this signals to the examiner that you have mapped the syllabus correctly.

### 1.3 Why Selection is a "Design Problem"

The phrase *"selection of equipment"* in the KTU module title is deliberate. The university wants students to understand that a vessel does not just "buy" a sounder — the naval architect must choose an instrument whose **frequency, beam angle, power, transducer type, and display modality** are matched to:

- The **vessel's hull form** (displacement vs planing),
- The **fishing gear** (trawl, longline, purse seine, gillnet),
- The **target species depth profile** (pelagic vs demersal),
- The **operational sea area** (shallow coastal vs deep oceanic).

This is the *engineering judgement* KTU examiners reward in Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Echo Sounding — Operational Principle

A fish-finding echo sounder operates on the principle of **active sonar** (SOund Navigation And Ranging). The system follows a four-stage cycle:

1. **Pulse Transmission:** A high-voltage spike excites a piezoelectric crystal in the transducer. The crystal converts electrical energy → mechanical (acoustic) vibration, launching a short pressure wave into the water column.
2. **Propagation:** The acoustic wave travels downward at the speed of sound in seawater.
3. **Reflection (Scattering):** When the wave strikes an acoustic-impedance mismatch (fish swim bladder, seabed, plankton layer), part of the energy is reflected back toward the vessel.
4. **Reception & Display:** The same transducer (in **transmit/receive (T/R) switching** mode) receives the echo, amplifies it, and plots it on a scrolling time-vs-depth display.

> [!NOTE]
> The swim bladder of a fish is the **primary acoustic reflector** — it has a dramatically different acoustic impedance than the surrounding water because of the air it contains. This is why fish with large swim bladders (e.g., sardine, mackerel) show up more strongly than fish with tiny bladders (e.g., sharks, which have cartilaginous skeletons and small or no swim bladders).

### 2.2 The Core Range Equation

The fundamental equation governing every echo sounder and sonar selection decision is the **two-way travel time equation**:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = range (slant or vertical depth) in metres
- $c$ = speed of sound in seawater ≈ **1500 m/s**
- $t$ = two-way travel time in seconds
- The factor of **2** accounts for the round-trip (down-and-back) path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
