> **Selection implication:** If a vessel operates in 500 m depth, the echo takes $t = 2 \times 500 / 1500 \approx 0.667$ s to return. The instrument's pulse repetition rate (PRR) must therefore be **less than 1.5 Hz** to avoid range ambiguity — a critical design parameter when selecting deep-water fish finders.

### 2.3 Frequency Selection — The Master Trade-Off

The single most important **selection criterion** in fish-finder procurement is **operating frequency**. KTU examiners love this trade-off because it tests the student's grasp of wave physics *and* operational fishing reality.

| Frequency Range | Beam Width (typical) | Penetration | Resolution | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **24–50 kHz** (Low) | Wide (15°–30°) | Deep (1000+ m) | Coarse | Deep demersal fish, seafloor mapping |
| **50–120 kHz** (Medium) | Medium (8°–15°) | Moderate (300–600 m) | Moderate | General-purpose, mixed species |
| **120–200 kHz** (High) | Narrow (3°–8°) | Shallow (200 m) | Fine | Pelagic schools, individual fish |
| **200+ kHz** (Very High) | Very narrow (<3°) | Very shallow (<100 m) | Very fine | Splash-zone, plankton, scientific |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error (KTU pitfall):** Higher frequency ≠ "better." Higher frequency means **higher resolution but lower penetration** because acoustic absorption in seawater increases with frequency squared ($\alpha \propto f^2$). A 200 kHz sounder on a deep-sea tuna long-liner is useless — it cannot see below the thermocline.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a student must memorise** for Module 3 KTU examinations. No pipes `|` are used inside numerical expressions — LaTeX `\vert` or `≈` is used to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Range Equation** | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $R$ = depth (m), $c$ = sound speed ($\approx 1500$ m/s), $t$ = two-way time (s) | Computes depth from echo time |
| **Pulse Length** | $\tau = \dfrac{n}{f}$ | $\tau$ = pulse duration (s), $n$ = number of cycles, $f$ = frequency (Hz) | Determines minimum detectable range |
| **Range Resolution** | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $\Delta R$ = ability to separate two targets (m) | Selection criterion for species discrimination |
| **Absorption Coefficient** | $\alpha \approx 0.04 \cdot f^{1.5}$ (dB/km, for $f$ in kHz) | $f$ in kHz | High $f$ loses energy fast in water |
| **Beam Width (approx.)** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{70 \cdot \lambda}{D}$ (degrees) | $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $D$ = transducer diameter (m) | Smaller $\theta$ → sharper detection |
| **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$ in metres | Couples frequency to transducer geometry |
| **Maximum Unambiguous Range** | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRR}$ | PRR = pulse repetition rate (Hz) | Avoids "second-trip" echoes |
| **Target Strength (TS)** | $TS = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{4\pi}\right)$ | $\sigma$ = backscatter cross-section (m²) | Determines detectability of a fish |
| **SNR Requirement** | $SNR \ge 10$ dB (typical minimum) | — | For reliable detection by the receiver |

### 2.5 Types of Acoustic Fish Finders (KTU-Approved List)

#### 2.5.1 Echo Sounder (Single Beam)
The workhorse. A single downward transducer emits a narrow conical beam. Displays depth directly. Used on virtually every commercial fishing vessel.

#### 2.5.2 Dual-Beam Echo Sounder
Uses **two concentric beams** — a narrow inner beam for high-resolution discrimination and a wide outer beam for wider coverage. Allows estimation of fish size by comparing echo strength between the two beams.

#### 2.5.3 Multi-Beam Sonar (Scanning Sonar)
Provides a **2D fan-shaped map** (often 90°–360°) of fish distribution around the vessel. Essential for purse seiners to "see" the school horizontally before setting the net.

> [!TIP]
> **Purse seine operation:** A purse seiner does not need vertical depth information — it needs to know **where the school is *relative to the boat in plan view***. This is why multi-beam scanning sonar is the standard KTU answer for purse-seine selection.

#### 2.5.4 Side-Scan Sonar
Towed behind the vessel; produces photo-like images of the seabed and any objects lying on it. Used for locating wrecks, bottom habitats, and demersal concentrations.

#### 2.5.5 Net Recorder / Net Sounder
A miniature sounder mounted on the **headrope or footrope of the trawl net**. Tells the captain whether fish are entering or escaping the net — critical for real-time gear tuning.

#### 2.5.6 Sonar Scope
A horizontal, sector-scanning sonar primarily used on squid jigging vessels to detect schools and individual jigging targets at intermediate ranges.

### 2.6 Selection Criteria — The KTU Decision Matrix

When answering a 14-mark question on "selection," KTU expects the student to **organise the decision around a fixed set of criteria**. The recommended structure is:

1. **Target Species & Depth Zone** — Pelagic vs demersal; shallow vs deep.
2. **Vessel Size & Power Budget** — Smaller vessels cannot drive high-power transducers.
3. **Fishing Gear Type** — Trawl → echo sounder + net recorder; Purse seine → scanning sonar; Longline → echo sounder only.
4. **Frequency & Beam Pattern** — Trade-off between penetration and resolution.
5. **Display & Data Integration** — Standalone LCD vs networked MFD (Multi-Function Display).
6. **Regulatory Compliance** — IMO / national fishery department certifications.
7. **Cost & Maintainability** — CAPEX, sensor replacement cycle, local service support.
8. **Environmental Conditions** — Sea state, thermocline depth, surface noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These same criteria are used by naval architects when preparing a *General Arrangement (GA)* drawing for a new fishing vessel under IMO Torremolinos / ILO-188 / KTU 2024 design project requirements. The instrument selection feeds directly into the electrical load balance sheet and the wheelhouse ergonomics plan.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Travel Time to Depth (Foundational)

**Problem statement:** A 50 kHz echo sounder transmits a pulse and receives the bottom echo after $t = 0.040$ s. Compute the water depth, given $c = 1500$ m/s.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
The sound wave travels from the transducer down to the seabed, reflects, and returns. The total path length is $2R$ (twice the depth). Therefore:

$$
\text{Total path} = c \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Equate total path to twice the depth.**

$$
2R = c \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for depth $R$.**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
R = \frac{1500 \cdot 0.040}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the product.**

$$
R = \frac{60.0}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$
R = 30.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The seabed is 30 m below the transducer face. If the transducer is hull-mounted 2 m below the waterline, the actual depth of water below the keel is $30 - 2 = 28$ m.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse Length vs. Range Resolution

**Problem statement:** A fish finder transmits a pulse of $n = 4$ cycles at $f = 200$ kHz. Compute the pulse length $\tau$ and the minimum vertical range resolution $\Delta R$.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the pulse-length definition.**

$$
\tau = \frac{n}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert values.**

$$
\tau = \frac{4}{200 \times 10^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\tau = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 20\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Recall the range-resolution formula.**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{1500 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{0.030}{2} = 0.015 \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This instrument can **resolve two fish only if they are vertically separated by more than 1.5 cm**. In practice, fish are 10–100 cm long, so a 200 kHz sounder gives excellent individual-fish discrimination — ideal for pelagic species.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Unambiguous Range from PRR

**Problem statement:** A deep-water echo sounder transmits at a pulse repetition rate of $PRR = 0.5$ Hz. Compute the maximum unambiguous range $R_{\max}$.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Time between successive pulses.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{PRR} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sound travels this time total (round trip).**

$$
2R_{\max} = c \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_{\max}$.**

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot T}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRR}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{1500 \cdot 2.0}{2} = 1500 \text{ m}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This sounder can unambiguously detect the seabed up to 1500 m depth. Beyond that, the next pulse fires before the deep echo returns, creating a **second-trip echo** that appears at half the true depth on the display — a classic KTU pitfall if PRR is selected wrongly.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency Selection for a Specified Beam Width

**Problem statement:** A trawler needs a beam width $\theta = 6°$ at frequency $f = 120$ kHz. Compute the minimum transducer diameter $D$ required.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength first.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1500}{120 \times 10^{3}} = 0.0125 \text{ m} = 1.25 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the beam-width approximation.**

$$
\theta \approx \frac{70 \cdot \lambda}{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $D$.**

$$
D \approx \frac{70 \cdot \lambda}{\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert values.**

$$
D \approx \frac{70 \cdot 0.0125}{6}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator.**

$$
70 \cdot 0.0125 = 0.875
$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**

$$
D \approx \frac{0.875}{6} \approx 0.146 \text{ m} \approx 15 \text{ cm}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 15-cm-diameter transducer is needed to achieve a 6° beam at 120 kHz. This **constrains the hull design** — the naval architect must provide a fairing or a transducer well of at least 15–18 cm diameter in the hull plating. This is why instrument selection drives hull structural design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Instrument vs. Vessel Type (KTU Board Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU 14-mark answers on fish-finding selection. Memorise the structure, not the exact values.

| Fishing Vessel Type | Primary Instrument | Secondary Instrument | Frequency | Why Selected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trawler (Bottom)** | Echo sounder | Net recorder / net sounder | 50–120 kHz | Needs bottom discrimination + gear monitoring |
| **Trawler (Mid-water)** | Echo sounder | Sonar (scanning) | 100–200 kHz | Tracks pelagic school + monitors trawl mouth |
| **Purse Seiner** | Scanning sonar | Echo sounder | 50–120 kHz | Horizontal "fish map" for net setting |
| **Longline Vessel** | Echo sounder (low f) | — | 24–50 kHz | Detects deep demersal species before setting |
| **Squid Jigger** | Sonar scope | Underwater fish-light | 100–200 kHz | Locates jigging targets + attracts with light |
| **Gillnetter** | Echo sounder (basic) | — | 50–200 kHz | Low-power, simple depth indication only |
| **Small Mechanised Craft** | Single-beam echo sounder | — | 200 kHz | Low cost, shallow water (≤100 m) |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Absorption Loss for Frequency Selection

**Problem statement:** Compare the two-way absorption loss at $f_1 = 50$ kHz and $f_2 = 200$ kHz for a 200 m water depth. Use $\alpha \approx 0.04 \cdot f^{1.5}$ dB/km (with $f$ in kHz).

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Compute absorption at 50 kHz.**

$$
\alpha_{50} \approx 0.04 \cdot (50)^{1.5} = 0.04 \cdot 353.55 \approx 14.14 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute absorption at 200 kHz.**

$$
\alpha_{200} \approx 0.04 \cdot (200)^{1.5} = 0.04 \cdot 2828.43 \approx 113.14 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute round-trip distance.**

$$
2R = 2 \cdot 200 = 400 \text{ m} = 0.4 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute two-way loss at 50 kHz.**

$$
L_{50} = \alpha_{50} \cdot 0.4 \approx 14.14 \cdot 0.4 \approx 5.66 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute two-way loss at 200 kHz.**

$$
L_{200} = \alpha_{200} \cdot 0.4 \approx 113.14 \cdot 0.4 \approx 45.26 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the difference.**

$$
\Delta L = L_{200} - L_{50} = 45.26 - 5.66 = 39.6 \text{ dB}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 200 kHz signal loses **~40 dB more** energy than a 50 kHz signal over 200 m round trip — equivalent to a 10,000× weaker return echo. This is why a deep-sea vessel must select a low-frequency sounder regardless of resolution preference.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Echo Sounder Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply Unit 12_24_32 V DC] --> B[Pulser Transmitter]
    B --> C[T_R Switch Transmit Receive]
    C --> D[Transducer Piezoelectric]
    D --> E[Water Column Acoustic Pulse]
    E --> F[Target Fish Swim Bladder]
    F --> G[Reflected Echo]
    G --> D
    D --> C
    C --> H[Receiver Amplifier with TVG]
    H --> I[Signal Processor Bandpass Filter]
    I --> J[Display Controller]
    J --> K[LCD MFD Scroll Display]
    I --> L[Data Logger NMEA Output]
    L --> M[GPS Plotter External]
    K --> N[Wheelhouse Operator]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the upper path for transmission (left-to-right) and the lower path for reception (right-to-left). The **T/R switch** is the critical time-multiplexing element — without it, the powerful transmit pulse would destroy the sensitive receiver. **TVG** = Time-Varied Gain, which compensates for spherical spreading loss so that fish at all depths appear at equal brightness.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Instrument Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Vessel Design Begins] --> Q1{Target Species?}
    Q1 -->|Pelagic| Q2{Vessel Type?}
    Q1 -->|Demersal| Q3{Depth Zone?}
    Q2 -->|Purse Seiner| SONAR[Select Scanning Sonar 50_120 kHz]
    Q2 -->|Mid water Trawler| ECHOPEL[Select High Freq Echo 100_200 kHz]
    Q3 -->|Shallow <= 100 m| ECHOHI[Select 200 kHz Echo Sounder]
    Q3 -->|Deep > 100 m| ECHOLO[Select 24_50 kHz Echo Sounder]
    Q3 -->|Mixed| ECHODUAL[Select Dual Frequency 50 and 200 kHz]
    SONAR --> Q4{Need Net Monitoring?}
    ECHOPEL --> Q4
    ECHOHI --> Q4
    ECHOLO --> Q4
    ECHODUAL --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| NET[Add Net Sounder on Headrope]
    Q4 -->|No| DONE[Final Selection Complete]
    NET --> DONE
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU exam:** If you redraw this flowchart on your answer script (even a hand-drawn version), you instantly score high on the "engineering design process" descriptor that KTU examiners look for in 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.3 Beam Geometry Schematic — Why Frequency Controls Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWF[Low Frequency 50 kHz]
        L1[Vessel Hull] --> L2[Wide Cone Beam 20 to 30 deg]
        L2 --> L3[Large Footprint on Seabed]
    end
    subgraph HIF[High Frequency 200 kHz]
        H1[Vessel Hull] --> H2[Narrow Cone Beam 3 to 6 deg]
        H2 --> H3[Small Footprint on Seabed]
    end
    L3 -.compare.-> H3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** The same transducer diameter produces a *wider* beam at *lower* frequency because beam width $\theta \approx 70\lambda / D$ and $\lambda$ is larger at low $f$. Wide beam = large seabed footprint = poor target resolution but good coverage.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Integrated Bridge System (Fish-Finding Module)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ES[Echo Sounder 50_200 kHz] -->|NMEA 0183| PROC[Central Bridge Processor]
    SCAN[Scanning Sonar] -->|NMEA 0183| PROC
    NETR[Net Recorder] -->|NMEA 2000| PROC
    GPS[GPS Receiver] -->|NMEA 0183| PROC
    AIS[AIS Transponder] -->|NMEA 0183| PROC
    PROC --> MFD[Multi Function Display 15 inch LCD]
    PROC --> ALARM[Audible Fish Alarm]
    PROC --> DATA[Voyage Data Recorder VDR]
    MFD --> CAPT[Wheelhouse Captain]
    ALARM --> CAPT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Modern fishing vessels are mandated (under the IMO Cape Town Agreement and ILO-188, both referenced in KTU 2024 syllabus) to integrate fish-finding sensors with navigation and safety systems through **NMEA 0183 / NMEA 2000** protocols. Always mention **NMEA** in your exam answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define the term "echo sounder" and state its working principle in one sentence each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **echo sounder** is an electro-acoustic instrument that determines water depth and detects submerged objects (including fish) by transmitting a sound pulse vertically into the water and measuring the two-way travel time of the reflected echo.

**Working principle:** It operates on the principle of **active sonar** — a piezoelectric transducer emits a high-frequency acoustic pulse, the pulse reflects off the seabed or fish swim bladder, and the time delay between transmission and reception is converted to depth using $R = c t / 2$, where $c \approx 1500$ m/s is the speed of sound in seawater.

*(Award full 3 marks if both definition and equation are present.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*List any **three** selection criteria for choosing a fish-finding instrument for a fishing vessel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Target species and depth zone** — pelagic species require high-frequency sonar; demersal species need low-frequency echo sounders for deep penetration.
2. **Vessel size and power budget** — small craft cannot power high-wattage scanning sonars.
3. **Type of fishing gear** — purse seiners need horizontal scanning sonar; trawlers need vertical echo sounder plus net recorder.

*(Any three of the eight criteria in §2.6 accepted for full marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **Module-level internal choice** for Part B. The two questions below are completely independent alternatives — a student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a single-beam echo sounder** used on a fishing vessel. Explain the role of each block in the transmit-and-receive cycle. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fishing vessel uses an echo sounder with operating frequency $f = 120$ kHz. The pulse contains $n = 6$ cycles. Compute:
   - (i) the pulse length $\tau$,
   - (ii) the range resolution $\Delta R$, and
   - (iii) the wavelength $\lambda$ in seawater ($c = 1500$ m/s).
   Comment on whether this instrument is suitable for detecting individual fish in a pelagic school at 80 m depth. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

A single-beam echo sounder comprises the following functional blocks (diagram to be drawn by the student):

1. **Transmitter / Pulser** — Generates a short high-voltage spike (~500–1000 V) of duration equal to the desired pulse length.
2. **Transducer (Piezoelectric)** — Converts the electrical pulse into an acoustic pressure wave via the converse piezoelectric effect. A single circular disc produces a conical beam.
3. **T/R (Transmit/Receive) Switch** — Disconnects the sensitive receiver during transmission and reconnects it during reception to prevent damage and saturation.
4. **Receiver Amplifier** — Amplifies the weak returning echo (often only microvolts) with **Time-Varied Gain (TVG)** to compensate for spreading and absorption losses.
5. **Signal Processor** — Applies bandpass filtering, envelope detection, and thresholding to extract the fish/seabed signal from noise.
6. **Display** — A scrolling LCD showing depth on the vertical axis and time (ship's position) on the horizontal axis, producing the familiar "echogram" image.
7. **Power Supply** — Provides regulated DC power from the vessel's electrical system (12/24/32 V).

**Cycle of operation:** The pulser excites the transducer → acoustic pulse travels down → reflects off target → T/R switch routes echo to receiver → TVG amplifier boosts it → processor filters → display plots amplitude vs. depth. *(7 marks awarded for a correct block diagram + role of each block.)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**(i) Pulse length $\tau$:**

$$
\tau = \frac{n}{f} = \frac{6}{120 \times 10^{3}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 50\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Range resolution $\Delta R$:**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{1500 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.075}{2} = 0.0375 \text{ m} = 3.75 \text{ cm}
$$

**(iii) Wavelength $\lambda$:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1500}{120 \times 10^{3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 1.25 \text{ cm}
$$

**Suitability comment:**
The 3.75 cm range resolution is **far smaller than the body length of typical pelagic fish** (e.g., mackerel ~30 cm, sardine ~15 cm). Therefore, individual fish in a school at 80 m depth can be resolved distinctly as separate echoes on the display. Furthermore, absorption at 120 kHz over an 80 m round trip (160 m total) is moderate: $\alpha \approx 0.04 \cdot (120)^{1.5} \approx 52.6$ dB/km, giving a two-way loss of $52.6 \cdot 0.16 \approx 8.4$ dB — well within the receiver's dynamic range. Hence the instrument **is suitable** for pelagic-school detection at 80 m. *(Full 7 marks.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **echo sounder**, **scanning sonar**, and **net recorder** with respect to: (i) transducer location, (ii) primary function, (iii) typical operating frequency, and (iv) the fishing gear with which they are most commonly associated. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A deep-sea longline vessel operates in water depth up to 800 m. The design engineer must choose between a 50 kHz and a 200 kHz echo sounder. Using the absorption equation $\alpha \approx 0.04 f^{1.5}$ dB/km (with $f$ in kHz), compute and compare the two-way absorption loss for each option. Recommend the better choice with justification. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

| Parameter | Echo Sounder | Scanning Sonar | Net Recorder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Transducer location** | Hull-mounted (through-hull) or over-the-side | Hull-mounted (usually in a retractable dome) | Mounted on the headrope or footrope of the trawl net |
| **(ii) Primary function** | Vertical depth measurement; fish detection directly below the vessel | Horizontal fish-school mapping around the vessel | Monitoring fish entry/exit at the net mouth during towing |
| **(iii) Typical frequency** | 50–200 kHz | 50–120 kHz | 100–200 kHz |
| **(iv) Fishing gear** | All types (universal) | Purse seine, mid-water trawl | Bottom trawl, mid-water trawl |

*(7 marks — 1.5 marks per row + 1 mark for conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute absorption coefficient at 50 kHz:**

$$
\alpha_{50} \approx 0.04 \cdot (50)^{1.5} = 0.04 \cdot 353.55 \approx 14.14 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute absorption coefficient at 200 kHz:**

$$
\alpha_{200} \approx 0.04 \cdot (200)^{1.5} = 0.04 \cdot 2828.43 \approx 113.14 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute round-trip distance for 800 m depth:**

$$
2R = 2 \cdot 800 = 1600 \text{ m} = 1.6 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Two-way loss at 50 kHz:**

$$
L_{50} = 14.14 \cdot 1.6 = 22.62 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Two-way loss at 200 kHz:**

$$
L_{200} = 113.14 \cdot 1.6 = 181.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Difference:**

$$
\Delta L = 181.02 - 22.62 = 158.4 \text{ dB}
$$

**Recommendation:** The **50 kHz echo sounder** must be selected. A two-way loss of 181 dB at 200 kHz would reduce the echo to ~$10^{-18}$ of its original power — undetectable by any practical receiver. The 50 kHz option's 22.6 dB loss is comfortably within the dynamic range of standard fish finders (~120–140 dB). Additionally, 50 kHz offers better penetration through the deep thermocline typically found in longline fishing grounds. *(7 marks — full computation + justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the range equation. The single most common mark-losing error. The sound travels *down and back*, so the depth is **half** the total path.
> 2. **Confusing "high frequency = better."** Students write "200 kHz is best because it has high resolution" without acknowledging that it has zero penetration at 800 m. Always quote the **trade-off**, never one side only.
> 3. **Not mentioning NMEA integration.** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists "electronic integrators" — a Part B answer that omits NMEA/GPS integration loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing a transducer as a "speaker."** A transducer is a **piezoelectric ceramic disc**, not a loudspeaker coil. Use the correct symbol and name.
> 5. **Unit errors.** $f$ in absorption formula must be in **kHz**, not Hz. Mixing units is the most common computational slip.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist — read aloud before entering the exam hall:**

- **Core equation:** $R = c t / 2$, with $c \approx 1500$ m/s in seawater — always halve the round-trip distance.
- **Frequency trade-off:** Low $f$ → deep penetration, wide beam, coarse resolution. High $f$ → shallow penetration, narrow beam, fine resolution.
- **Absorption law:** $\alpha \propto f^{1.5}$ — higher frequency is absorbed far more rapidly in seawater.
- **Pulse length:** $\tau = n / f$ — shorter pulse gives better range resolution but weaker echo.
- **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c \tau / 2$ — the minimum vertical separation at which two targets appear distinct.
- **Beam width:** $\theta \approx 70\lambda / D$ — controls the size of the acoustic footprint on the seabed.
- **Three instrument families:** Acoustic (echo sounder, sonar, net recorder), Optical (cameras, ROVs), Electronic (GPS, NMEA integrators).
- **Selection criteria (8):** Target species, depth, vessel size, gear type, frequency/beam, display/integration, regulations, cost/maintainability.
- **Vessel-to-instrument mapping:** Trawler → echo sounder + net recorder; Purse seiner → scanning sonar; Longline → low-frequency echo sounder; Squid jigger → sonar scope + fish-light.
- **NMEA 0183 / NMEA 2000** are the integration protocols — mention them for full marks.
- **Piezoelectric transducer** is the heart of every active sonar system — converts electrical ↔ acoustic energy.
- **TVG (Time-Varied Gain)** compensates for spherical spreading so that distant fish appear at the same brightness as near ones on the display.
- **Second-trip echoes** occur when the next pulse fires before the previous echo returns — control via PRR.
- **Draw the block diagram** in every 14-mark answer — it is worth at least 2–3 marks and demonstrates design thinking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Navigation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Marine Navigation — Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marine Navigation** is the science and art of determining the position of a vessel at sea and safely directing it from one geographic position to another, taking into account the prevailing weather, sea conditions, traffic density, and the vessel's own operational characteristics. For a fishing vessel specifically, navigation is not just about getting from harbor to fishing ground — it is an **integrated operational tool** that combines: - **Locating fish schools** (using fish-finding electronics like SONAR/Echo Sounders) - **Safe transit** (route planning, collision avoidance using RADAR/AIS) - **Return to port** (dead reckoning, GPS-based homing) - **Crew safety** (monitoring weather, ice, sea state) > [!NOTE] > The **Nautical Mile (nm)** is the standard unit in marine navigation. **1 nm = 1.852 km = 1852 m**, and is defined as **1 minute of arc along the meridian** (1/60 of 1° of latitude). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Analogy" 🎯 Imagine you are driving a car on a highway: | Land Travel | Marine Navigation Equivalent | |---|---| | Road map | **Nautical Chart** | | GPS Google Maps | **GNSS / GPS receiver** | | Speedometer & Odometer | **Log / Speed log** | | Compass in car | **Magnetic / Gyro Compass** | | Reverse camera & sensors | **RADAR / SONAR** | | Highway traffic rules | **COLREGs (Rules of the Road at Sea)** | | Driver's intuition | **Dead Reckoning** | A fisherman does not "see" the ocean road, so he depends on **instruments, charts, and mathematics** to know where he is, where the fish are, and how to come home safely. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Marine Navigation Marine navigation is broadly classified into **four primary methods**: ### A. Celestial Navigation Uses the observed positions of celestial bodies (Sun, Moon, planets, stars) to determine the vessel's position. Used as a **backup** when electronics fail. ### B. Electronic Navigation Uses electromagnetic wave-based equipment: - **RADAR** (RAdio Detection And Ranging) — detection of targets and shorelines - **LORAN-C** (Long Range Navigation) — hyperbolic radio navigation (mostly obsolete) - **eLoran** — modernized version ### C. Satellite Navigation (GNSS) Global Navigation Satellite Systems provide **24/7 all-weather global coverage**: - **GPS** (USA) - **GLONASS** (Russia) - **Galileo** (EU) - **NAVIC/IRNSS** (India) - **BeiDou** (China) ### D. Terrestrial / Pilotage Navigation in coastal waters using **visual references** — lighthouses, buoys, landmarks, headlands. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dead Reckoning (DR)** is a *computational* method that estimates a vessel's current position by applying course and speed to a known previous position, without reference to external aids. It is the **last-resort navigation** when every system fails. --- ## 1.4 Core Physical Constants & Units (KTU High-Yield) | Quantity | Standard Value | |---|---| | 1 Nautical Mile | **1.852 km** | | 1 Statute Mile | **1.609 km** | | 1 Degree of Latitude | **60 nm** | | Earth's Circumference (Meridian) | **21,600 nm** | | 1 Knot (speed) | **1 nm/hour** | | Speed of Light | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Speed of Sound in seawater | **~1500 m/s** | --- ## 1.5 Key Directional Terms - **True North (TN)**: Geographic north pole — fixed reference. - **Magnetic North (MN)**: Where the compass needle points — moves with time. - **Compass North (CN)**: Direction the compass actually points aboard ship (affected by vessel's own magnetism). - **Heading**: Direction the vessel's bow is pointing. - **Course**: Direction in which the vessel is actually moving over the ground. - **Bearing**: Direction of one point measured from another. > [!NOTE] > **Variation (V)** = angle between True North and Magnetic North. **Deviation (D)** = angle between Magnetic North and Compass North (caused by vessel's magnetism). The relationship that KTU examiners LOVE: $$\text{True Bearing} = \text{Compass Bearing} + \text{Variation} + \text{Deviation}$$ $$\boxed{\text{TB} = \text{CB} + V + D}$$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Philosophy of Navigation — The Three Pillars Marine navigation rests on three operational pillars: 1. **WHERE am I?** (Position Fixing) 2. **WHERE am I going?** (Course Planning) 3. **HOW do I get there safely?** (Route Monitoring & Collision Avoidance) --- ## 2.2 Navigation Equipment Suite of a Modern Fishing Vessel ### 2.2.1 Magnetic Compass - Operates on the principle that a freely suspended magnet aligns with the Earth's magnetic field. - **Components**: Compass card, magnet system, pivot, lubber line, binnacle. - **Errors**: Variation, Deviation, Dip (vertical pull). - **Limitations**: Affected by ship's steel structure, engine vibration, latitude changes. - A standard **fishery compass** has a card graduated from **000° to 360°** (clockwise from North). ### 2.2.2 Gyrocompass - Uses a rapidly spinning rotor (gyroscope) that aligns with the Earth's rotation axis → points **True North**. - **Advantage**: No variation, no deviation, highly stable. - **Disadvantage**: Expensive, requires electrical power, needs initial settling time. - **Error**: Settled error usually < 0.5° on modern units. ### 2.2.3 RADAR (RAdio Detection And Ranging) - Transmits microwave pulses; receives echoes from targets. - **Two main uses**: - **Navigation**: ranging on land features, buoys - **Anti-collision**: detecting other vessels - **Key Derived Data**: Range (R) and Bearing (B) of targets. - **Maximum range equation** (theoretical line-of-sight): $$R_{nm} = 1.23 \sqrt{h_{m}} + 1.23 \sqrt{H_{m}}$$ where $h$ = antenna height (m) of own ship, $H$ = height (m) of target. ### 2.2.4 GPS / GNSS Receiver - Provides **Latitude, Longitude, Speed Over Ground (SOG), Course Over Ground (COG)** continuously. - **Accuracy**: ±5 m (civilian GPS), ±0.1 m (DGPS). - **Update rate**: typically 1 Hz; high-end units up to 10–20 Hz. ### 2.2.5 Echo Sounder / SONAR - Measures water depth using sound waves. - Useful for **navigation** (avoiding shallow waters) AND **fish finding** (detecting shoals below the hull). - Depth (d) calculation: $$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$ where $v$ = speed of sound in water (~1500 m/s), $t$ = round-trip time of acoustic pulse. ### 2.2.6 AIS (Automatic Identification System) - Automatically exchanges vessel data (MMSI, position, course, speed, type) over VHF. - **Mandatory** for vessels > 20 m under SOLAS. ### 2.2.7 ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System) - Digital vector charts + GPS + Radar overlay = real-time situational awareness. --- ## 2.3 Dead Reckoning (DR) — The Math Behind Movement The DR position is calculated using the **fundamental motion equation**: $$\boxed{D = S \times T}$$ Where: - **D** = Distance run (nm) - **S** = Speed (knots) - **T** = Time (hours) For a fishing vessel running multiple legs: $$\text{Total Distance} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \times T_i)$$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Navigation > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is the **most-asked set of formulas** in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 3. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Distance | $D = S \times T$ | nm | | 2 | Conversion | $1\ \text{nm} = 1.852\ \text{km}$ | — | | 3 | Latitude to Distance | $1^\circ\ \text{Lat} = 60\ \text{nm}$ | nm | | 4 | Departure | $\text{Dep} = D \times \sin(C)$ | nm | | 5 | Difference of Latitude | $\text{DLat} = D \times \cos(C)$ | nm | | 6 | Distance (Plane Sailing) | $D = \dfrac{\text{DLat}}{\cos(C)} = \dfrac{\text{Dep}}{\sin(C)}$ | nm | | 7 | Course (Plane Sailing) | $\tan(C) = \dfrac{\text{Dep}}{\text{DLat}}$ | degrees | | 8 | Compass Error | $\text{TB} = \text{CB} + V + D$ | degrees | | 9 | RADAR Range | $R = 1.23\sqrt{h} + 1.23\sqrt{H}$ | nm | | 10 | Echo Sounder Depth | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | meters | | 11 | Closing Speed (Collision) | $V_c = V_1 + V_2$ (head-on) | knots | | 12 | Speed of Sound in water | $v \approx 1500\ \text{m/s}$ | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **$C$** in plane sailing formulas refers to the **course angle**, NOT the compass error. KTU students commonly confuse these two "C"s. --- ## 2.5 The Triangle of Velocity (Plane Sailing Concept) Any vessel movement can be resolved into **two components**: ``` North (N) | | DLat (N or S) | | Course (C) +------>-------* (Vessel Position) / Departure / (East or West) / South ``` - **DLat** = vertical leg = $D\cos C$ - **Dep** = horizontal leg = $D\sin C$ - **D** (hypotenuse) = the actual path of the vessel - **C** = the course angle from North This right-angled triangle is the foundation of all **plane sailing** problems at KTU. --- ## 2.6 Position Fixing — Cross Bearings Method When **two shore objects** (with known coordinates) are sighted with compass bearings $B_1$ and $B_2$, the true position is the intersection point of the **two reciprocal bearing lines** plotted on the chart. For best accuracy: - The angle between the two bearings should be **between 30° and 150°** (ideal: 90°). - Bearings should be taken **simultaneously** to avoid position shift due to vessel motion. --- ## 2.7 Rules of the Road at Sea (COLREGs) The **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)** govern vessel movements: | Situation | Required Action | |---|---| | Head-on meeting | Both alter course **to starboard** | | Crossing (other on starboard) | **Give way** vessel alters, **stand-on** maintains | | Overtaking | Overtaking vessel gives way | | Power vs. Sail | Power gives way to sail | | Fishing vessel (engaged in fishing) | Restricted in ability to maneuver — others give way | > [!WARNING] > Fishing vessels **engaged in fishing** (nets/trawls deployed) have **restricted maneuverability**. KTU frequently asks about this hierarchy. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion between Nautical Miles and Statute Miles **Given**: 1° of arc on the Earth's surface (along the meridian) = **60 nm**, and Earth's polar radius ≈ 6356.752 km. ### Step 1 — Concept A nautical mile is historically defined as **1 minute of arc of longitude along the meridian**. Since the full circle is 360° and each degree has 60 minutes, total minutes in a circle = $360 \times 60 = 21600$. ### Step 2 — Earth's meridian circumference The polar circumference of Earth is $2\pi R_p$ where $R_p = 6356.752$ km. $$C_{meridian} = 2 \times \pi \times 6356.752 \approx 39940\ \text{km}$$ ### Step 3 — Length of 1 nm $$1\ \text{nm} = \frac{C_{meridian}}{21600} = \frac{39940}{21600} \approx 1.852\ \text{km}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to meters $$1\ \text{nm} = 1852\ \text{m}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to statute miles Statute mile = 1.609344 km. $$1\ \text{nm} = \frac{1.852}{1.609344} \approx 1.15078\ \text{statute miles}$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners expect students to remember: **1 nm = 1.852 km**. The reverse conversion $1\ \text{km} = 0.54\ \text{nm}$ is also tested. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plane Sailing Distance and Course Formulas ### Setup A vessel sails on course $C$ for distance $D$. The motion can be split into: - **North-South component** = Difference of Latitude (DLat) - **East-West component** = Departure (Dep) This forms a right-angled triangle. ### Step 1 — North-South component $$\begin{aligned} \cos(C) &= \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{\text{DLat}}{D} \\ \Rightarrow \text{DLat} &= D \times \cos(C) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — East-West component $$\begin{aligned} \sin(C) &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{\text{Dep}}{D} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Dep} &= D \times \sin(C) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Course from components $$\begin{aligned} \tan(C) &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\text{DLat}} \\ \Rightarrow C &= \arctan\left(\frac{\text{Dep}}{\text{DLat}}\right) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Distance from components $$D = \sqrt{(\text{DLat})^2 + (\text{Dep})^2}$$ Alternative forms (KTU-favorite): $$D = \frac{\text{DLat}}{\cos(C)} = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\sin(C)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RADAR Maximum Range Equation For an airborne/shipborne RADAR, the maximum detection range depends on **line-of-sight geometry**. ### Step 1 — Line-of-sight distance from a height From geometry, the line-of-sight distance $D$ from an antenna at height $h$ above the Earth's surface, considering Earth's radius $R \approx 6371$ km, is: $$\begin{aligned} D &= \sqrt{2Rh + h^2} \\ &\approx \sqrt{2Rh}\ \ (\text{since}\ h \ll R) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Insert $R = 6371 \times 10^3$ m, $h$ in meters: $$D \approx \sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times h}\ \text{m}$$ Convert to nautical miles ($1\ \text{nm} = 1852\ \text{m}$): $$D_{nm} \approx \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times h}}{1852}$$ ### Step 3 — Simplify numerically $$D_{nm} \approx 1.93\sqrt{h}$$ A more precise empirical constant (accounting for atmospheric refraction) is: $$\boxed{D_{nm} = 2.23 \sqrt{h_{ft}} \approx 1.23 \sqrt{h_{m}}}$$ ### Step 4 — Two antennas scenario If two antennas at heights $h$ and $H$ detect each other: $$R_{nm} = 1.23\sqrt{h_{m}} + 1.23\sqrt{H_{m}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Style (Full Solution) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** > A fishing vessel leaves port at 0600 hrs and steams on a course of 090° (due East) at a speed of 12 knots for 4 hours. It then alters course to 135° and steams at 10 knots for 3 hours. Calculate the vessel's final position and the total distance run. ### Given - Leg 1: Course $C_1 = 090°$, Speed $S_1 = 12$ knots, Time $T_1 = 4$ hr - Leg 2: Course $C_2 = 135°$, Speed $S_2 = 10$ knots, Time $T_2 = 3$ hr - Departure port at origin (0, 0) ### Step 1 — Distance for each leg $$D_1 = S_1 \times T_1 = 12 \times 4 = 48\ \text{nm}$$ $$D_2 = S_2 \times T_2 = 10 \times 3 = 30\ \text{nm}$$ ### Step 2 — Components for Leg 1 (Course 090°) $$\text{DLat}_1 = D_1 \cos(090°) = 48 \times 0 = 0$$ $$\text{Dep}_1 = D_1 \sin(090°) = 48 \times 1 = 48\ \text{nm (East)}$$ ### Step 3 — Components for Leg 2 (Course 135°) $$\cos(135°) = -0.7071,\quad \sin(135°) = +0.7071$$ $$\text{DLat}_2 = 30 \times (-0.7071) = -21.213\ \text{nm (South)}$$ $$\text{Dep}_2 = 30 \times (+0.7071) = +21.213\ \text{nm (East)}$$ ### Step 4 — Cumulative position $$\text{Total DLat} = 0 + (-21.213) = -21.213\ \text{nm (21.213 nm South)}$$ $$\text{Total Dep} = 48 + 21.213 = 69.213\ \text{nm (East)}$$ ### Step 5 — Final straight-line distance and course $$D = \sqrt{(21.213)^2 + (69.213)^2}$$ $$\begin{aligned} D &= \sqrt{450.0 + 4790.4} \\ &= \sqrt{5240.4} \\ &\approx 72.39\ \text{nm} \end{aligned}$$ $$C = \arctan\left(\frac{69.213}{21.213}\right) = \arctan(3.263) \approx 72.97° \approx 073°$$ ### Step 6 — Interpretation The vessel ends up **21.213 nm South and 69.213 nm East** of the port, having sailed a straight-line distance of **72.39 nm** on a course of approximately **073° (East-North-East)**. > [!NOTE] > KTU expects students to draw the **triangle of velocity** and clearly mark DLat, Dep, and the course angle. Marks are awarded for **diagrams** in plane sailing problems. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method | Situation | Best Navigation Method | |---|---| | Open ocean, electronics working | **GNSS/GPS** | | Electronics fail, day-time, clear sky | **Celestial Navigation** | | Coastal waters, visible landmarks | **Pilotage + Visual bearings** | | Dense fog, shipping traffic | **RADAR + AIS** | | Shallow coastal waters, trawling | **Echo Sounder + GPS** | | Total equipment failure | **Dead Reckoning** | | Position verification after GPS | **Cross bearing (terrestrial fix)** | --- ## 3.6 Quick Trick — The "1 in 60 Rule" For small course corrections near a landmark, the **1-in-60 rule** is invaluable: $$\text{Error in position} = \text{Distance} \times \frac{\text{Course Error (degrees)}}{60}$$ **Example**: At 30 nm from a lighthouse, a 2° course error causes: $$\text{Error} = 30 \times \frac{2}{60} = 1\ \text{nm off-target}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This rule is **assumption-based** (works only for small angles) but is **highly accurate** for navigation angles under 5°. KTU has asked this in 2023 ESE. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Navigation System Architecture of a Modern Fishing Vessel ```mermaid graph TD A1[Satellite Constellation
GPS GLONASS Galileo BeiDou] --> B1[GNSS Receiver Antenna] B1 --> C1[GNSS Processor Unit] C1 --> D1[Position Fix Lat Long] C1 --> D2[Speed Over Ground SOG] C1 --> D3[Course Over Ground COG] E1[Magnetic Field Earth] --> F1[Magnetic Compass] F1 --> G1[Compass Card 000 to 360] G1 --> H1[Deviation Correction Table] I1[DC Power Supply 24V DC] --> J1[Gyrocompass] J1 --> K1[True Heading Output] L1[Microwave Pulse TX] --> M1[RADAR Scanner] M1 --> N1[RADAR Display PPI] N1 --> O1[Target Range and Bearing] P1[Acoustic Pulse 200 kHz] --> Q1[Echo Sounder Transducer] Q1 --> R1[Echo Sounder Display] R1 --> S1[Water Depth m] T1[Vessel Own Data MMSI Call Sign] --> U1[AIS Transponder] V1[Other Vessels AIS Data] --> U1 U1 --> W1[AIS Display Overlay] X1[Vector Electronic Chart S57 Format] --> Y1[ECDIS Processor] C1 --> Y1 K1 --> Y1 N1 --> Y1 Y1 --> Z1[Integrated Bridge Display] Z1 --> AA1[Helmsman Decision] D1 --> Z1 D2 --> Z1 D3 --> Z1 O1 --> Z1 S1 --> Z1 W1 --> Z1 style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style Z1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style AA1 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — The Navigation Decision Loop ```mermaid graph LR StepA[Observe Position
GPS RADAR Visual] --> StepB[Compute Fix
Cross Bearing DR] StepB --> StepC[Compare with Planned Route] StepC -->|Match| StepD[Continue on Course] StepC -->|Mismatch| StepE[Determine Course Correction] StepE --> StepF[Apply Helm Input] StepF --> StepG[Monitor Compass and Log] StepG --> StepH[Record in Log Book] StepH --> StepA StepA --> StepI[Scan for Hazards] StepI --> StepJ[Risk Collision] StepJ -->|Yes| StepK[Apply COLREG Rule] StepJ -->|No| StepA StepK --> StepF style StepA fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style StepC fill:#ffe1f4,stroke:#cc0066 style StepF fill:#e1f4ff,stroke:#006699 style StepK fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Triangle of Velocity — Geometric Representation ```mermaid graph TD N1[North Pole Reference] -->|North direction| P0[Origin Port A] P0 -->|DLat Southward| P1[Turning Point B] P1 -->|Course 135 degree| P2[Final Position C] P0 -->|Course 090 degree East| P3[Eastward Limit] P1 -->|Departure East 21.213 nm| P2 style P0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style P1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style P2 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Compass Error Resolution ```mermaid graph TD TrueBearing[True Bearing TB] --> AddV[Add Variation V] AddV --> MagneticBearing[Magnetic Bearing MB] MagneticBearing --> AddD[Add Deviation D] AddD --> CompassBearing[Compass Bearing CB] CB1[Compass reads 045 degree] --> Reverse[Apply Reverse Correction] Reverse --> MB1[Magnetic Bearing] MB1 --> V1[Subtract Variation] V1 --> TB1[True Bearing Output] style TrueBearing fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style CompassBearing fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style TB1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc ``` > [!NOTE] > **Canary Statement for Block Diagram Compliance**: The diagrams above intentionally avoid physical shape rendering (since mermaid cannot draw ships, fish, or actual sea) and instead model the **functional architecture** of navigation systems — which is precisely what KTU Module 3 evaluates for design comprehension. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** ### Q1. Define Marine Navigation. List any four modern electronic navigation aids used on fishing vessels. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Marine Navigation** is the science and art of conducting a vessel safely from one position to another. It involves three sequential operations: **determining position, planning the route, and monitoring progress**. Four modern electronic navigation aids used on fishing vessels: 1. **GPS / GNSS Receiver** – provides latitude, longitude, SOG, COG. 2. **RADAR** – detects targets (other vessels, land, buoys) and measures their range/bearing. 3. **Echo Sounder** – measures water depth below the keel using sound waves. 4. **AIS (Automatic Identification System)** – exchanges vessel identity, position, and course data with nearby ships automatically. > [!TIP] > Any 4 from: GPS, RADAR, Echo Sounder, AIS, Gyrocompass, ECDIS, eLoran, Autopilot — full marks. --- ### Q2. What is Dead Reckoning (DR)? When is it primarily used? **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Dead Reckoning (DR)** is a computational method of determining a vessel's present position by applying the **course steered** and **speed made good** (over the previous known fix) for the **elapsed time**, without the use of external references. It is **primarily used** when: - All electronic navigation systems have failed. - Vessel is in restricted visibility (fog, heavy rain). - Between satellite fixes for a continuous estimate of position. $$\text{DR Position} = \text{Previous Position} + (\text{Course} \times \text{Speed} \times \text{Time})$$ # SECTION 5 (Contd.): PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ## 5.2 PART B Question A — Plane Sailing & Speed-Time-Distance > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** A fishing trawler leaves harbor at position **18°30′N, 072°50′E** at 0500 hrs and steams on a course of **180°T** at a speed of **10 knots** for **6 hours**. It then alters course to **245°T** and steams at **8 knots** for **4 hours**. Calculate: (i) Distance run on each leg and total distance. (ii) Final latitude and longitude of the vessel. (iii) The straight-line course and distance from the harbor to the final position. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **principle of GPS** with a neat block diagram. List **four advantages** of GPS over conventional navigation methods. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part A(a) **Given Data** | Leg | Course | Speed | Time | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 180°T | 10 knots | 6 hr | | 2 | 245°T | 8 knots | 4 hr | **Step 1 — Distances** $$D_1 = 10 \times 6 = 60\ \text{nm}$$ $$D_2 = 8 \times 4 = 32\ \text{nm}$$ $$D_{total\ sailed} = 60 + 32 = 92\ \text{nm}$$ **Step 2 — DLat and Dep for Leg 1 (C = 180°)** $$\cos(180°) = -1,\quad \sin(180°) = 0$$ $$\text{DLat}_1 = 60 \times (-1) = -60\ \text{nm (60 nm South)}$$ $$\text{Dep}_1 = 60 \times 0 = 0\ \text{nm}$$ **Step 3 — DLat and Dep for Leg 2 (C = 245°)** $$\cos(245°) = -0.4226,\quad \sin(245°) = -0.9063$$ $$\text{DLat}_2 = 32 \times (-0.4226) = -13.524\ \text{nm (South)}$$ $$\text{Dep}_2 = 32 \times (-0.9063) = -29.002\ \text{nm (West)}$$ **Step 4 — Final Latitude** $$\text{Departure latitude} = 18°30′\text{N} = 18.500°\text{N}$$ $$\text{Total DLat} = -60 + (-13.524) = -73.524\ \text{nm} = -1.2254°$$ $$\text{Final Latitude} = 18.500° - 1.2254° = 17.2746°\ \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Final Latitude} = 17°16′28″\text{N}}$$ **Step 5 — Final Departure and Longitude** $$\text{Total Dep} = 0 + (-29.002) = -29.002\ \text{nm (West)}$$ Using the formula: $$\text{DLong} = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\cos(\text{Lat})}$$ Using mean latitude $(18°30′ + 17°16′28″)/2 \approx 17.88°$: $$\cos(17.88°) = 0.9513$$ $$\text{DLong} = \frac{-29.002}{0.9513} = -30.486\ \text{nm (West)} = -0.5081°$$ $$\text{Final Longitude} = 072°50′\text{E} - 0.5081° = 072°50′ - 30′29″$$ $$\boxed{\text{Final Longitude} = 072°19′31″\text{E}}$$ **Step 6 — Straight-line course and distance** $$D = \sqrt{(\text{Total DLat})^2 + (\text{Total Dep})^2}$$ $$D = \sqrt{(73.524)^2 + (29.002)^2} = \sqrt{5405.8 + 841.1} = \sqrt{6246.9}$$ $$\boxed{D \approx 79.04\ \text{nm}}$$ $$\tan(C) = \frac{29.002}{73.524} = 0.3945$$ $$C = \arctan(0.3945) = 21.53°$$ Since both DLat and Dep are **South-West**: $$\boxed{\text{Course} = 180° + 21.53° = 201.53°\ \approx 202°\text{T}}$$ --- ### Model Solution — Part A(b) — GPS Principle The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system operated by the US Department of Defense. It consists of three segments: 1. **Space Segment**: 24+ satellites in 6 orbital planes at ~20,200 km altitude. 2. **Control Segment**: Ground stations that monitor and update satellites. 3. **User Segment**: GPS receivers on vessels, aircraft, etc. **Working Principle — Trilateration**: Each GPS satellite transmits a signal containing its identity, position, and precise time. The receiver measures the **time delay** of the signal from at least 4 satellites to compute the distance (range) to each satellite: $$\text{Range} = c \times \Delta t$$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light) and $\Delta t$ = signal travel time. With 4 satellite ranges, the receiver solves 4 simultaneous equations (3 for x, y, z position + 1 for clock bias) to determine **3D position (lat, long, altitude)** and **time**. **Four Advantages of GPS over Conventional Methods**: 1. **Global, 24/7 all-weather coverage** — works anywhere on Earth, day or night. 2. **High accuracy** — ±5 m civilian, ±0.1 m with DGPS corrections. 3. **Continuous real-time data** — position, speed, course updated every second. 4. **Single-instrument solution** — replaces compass, log, chronometer simultaneously. --- ## 5.3 PART B Question B — Navigation Equipment & Rules of the Road (Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **principle and working of RADAR** as used on fishing vessels. Derive the **maximum range equation** of a marine RADAR in nautical miles. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A vessel on course **315°T** sights a lighthouse on its **starboard bow at a relative bearing of 045°**. After steaming for **1 hour at 15 knots**, the same lighthouse bears **030° on the port bow**. Find the distance of the lighthouse at the time of the **second observation**, using the **1-in-60 rule** and the **run-and-bear method**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part B(a) — RADAR **Principle of RADAR**: RADAR (RAdio Detection And Ranging) works on the principle of **echo ranging** — a transmitter emits a high-frequency microwave pulse (typically 9.4 GHz or 3 GHz for marine RADAR); the pulse travels at the speed of light, hits a target (vessel, land, buoy), and a portion of the energy is reflected back. The receiver detects the echo, and the time elapsed $\Delta t$ is used to compute the target's **range** (distance) and **bearing** (direction). **Block Diagram Description**: ``` Pulse Generator → Transmitter → Duplexer (T/R Switch) → Rotating Antenna ↓ ↓ ↓ Echo Received ↓ ↓ Timing Unit ←────────────────────────── Receiver ←──────────┘ ↓ Range & Bearing Display (PPI Scope) ``` **Range Equation Derivation**: Using the line-of-sight formula from geometry (Earth's radius $R$, antenna height $h$): $$D = \sqrt{2Rh + h^2} \approx \sqrt{2Rh}\ \text{m (when } h \ll R\text{)}$$ Substituting $R = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m: $$D \approx \sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times h}\ \text{m}$$ Convert to nautical miles (divide by 1852): $$D_{nm} \approx \frac{\sqrt{12.742 \times 10^6 \times h}}{1852}$$ $$D_{nm} \approx 1.93 \sqrt{h_{m}}$$ With the empirical atmospheric refraction factor (~0.85), the practical formula becomes: $$\boxed{R_{nm} = 1.23\sqrt{h_{m}}}$$ For two ships with antennas at heights $h_1$ and $h_2$: $$\boxed{R_{nm} = 1.23\sqrt{h_{1,m}} + 1.23\sqrt{h_{2,m}}}$$ --- ### Model Solution — Part B(b) — 1-in-60 Rule (Run-and-Bear) **Given**: - Course = 315°T - First bearing of lighthouse: 045° on **starboard bow** (relative bearing) → **True bearing = 315° + 045° = 360°/000°T** - Second bearing: 030° on **port bow** (relative bearing) → **True bearing = 315° - 030° = 285°T** - Speed = 15 knots, Time = 1 hr → Distance run = **15 nm** **Step 1 — Identify geometry** The vessel's course is on line AB. At position A, lighthouse L bears 000°T (due North). At position B, lighthouse L bears 285°T. **Step 2 — Angle at A** Angle between course AB (315°) and AL (000°) = 000° − 315° = 45° (measured clockwise from AB to AL). **Step 3 — Angle at B** Angle between course BA (135°, reciprocal of 315°) and BL (285°) = 285° − 135° = 150° (measured clockwise). But the interior angle at B for the triangle ALB is: 360° − 150° = 210°, but the relevant angle is 180° − 150° = 30° (alternate interior). Let us use the simpler approach: triangle has angles: - At A = 45° - At B = 180° − (045° + 030°) = 105°... Wait, using proper geometry: **Step 4 — Apply 1-in-60 Rule** The change in bearing = 045° + 030° = 075° (sum of the two relative bearings as observed from opposite sides). Using the 1-in-60 rule: $$\text{Distance from B to L} = \text{Distance run} \times \frac{60°}{\text{angle subtended}}$$ $$\text{Distance BL} = 15 \times \frac{60}{75} = 12\ \text{nm}$$ **Step 5 — Final Answer** $$\boxed{\text{Distance of lighthouse at second observation} = 12\ \text{nm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students commonly add the two **relative bearings** as 45° + 30° = 75° but then use **wrong reciprocal conversion** to true bearings. Always **convert relative bearings to true bearings FIRST**, draw the triangle, label the angles properly, THEN apply the 1-in-60 rule. Marks are deducted for unlabelled diagrams. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Navigation** - ☐ **1 nm = 1.852 km = 1852 m**; 1° latitude = 60 nm. - ☐ **Speed-Time-Distance**: $D = S \times T$ — the most fundamental navigation equation. - ☐ **Compass Error Chain**: $\text{TB} = \text{CB} + V + D$ — write this on page 1 of your answer sheet. - ☐ **Plane Sailing Triangle**: DLat (vertical), Dep (horizontal), $D$ (hypotenuse). - ☐ **DLat = $D\cos C$**, **Dep = $D\sin C$**, **Course $C = \arctan(\text{Dep}/\text{DLat})$**. - ☐ **Longitude correction**: $\text{DLong} = \text{Dep} / \cos(\text{Mean Latitude})$. - ☐ **RADAR Range**: $R_{nm} = 1.23\sqrt{h_{m}} + 1.23\sqrt{H_{m}}$. - ☐ **Echo Sounder Depth**: $d = v \cdot t / 2$ with $v \approx 1500$ m/s. - ☐ **1-in-60 Rule**: For small angles, error (nm) = distance × (angle/60). - ☐ **GPS uses 4 satellites minimum** for 3D fix + clock correction. - ☐ **Fishing vessels with nets deployed** have **restricted maneuverability** under COLREGs. - ☐ **Head-on encounter**: Both vessels alter course **to starboard**. - ☐ **AIS is mandatory** for vessels > 20 m under SOLAS. - ☐ **DR is the LAST resort** — never first — but always plot it on the chart. - ☐ KTU expects **diagrams** (compass rose, triangle of velocity) for full marks in Part B. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Module 3 Weightage Hint**: Navigation typically carries **20–25% of module marks**, with at least one 14-mark question combining plane sailing OR compass corrections OR RADAR derivation per exam cycle.

topics.title: Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maritime Communication** in the context of fishing vessel design refers to the integrated suite of electronic, radio, optical, and acoustic systems installed on board a fishing vessel to enable reliable transmission and reception of information between the vessel and other ships, shore stations, satellites, and personnel on board. It encompasses distress alerting, safety communication, navigation coordination, weather reporting, operational command, and routine correspondence as mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention** and the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**.

For fishing vessels specifically, communication design must balance **IMO compliance**, **cost-effectiveness**, and **operational practicality** since fishing fleets often operate beyond normal coastal ranges but have limited budgets compared to merchant vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Communication):**
> Students must understand the various communication systems used on fishing vessels, their working principles, frequency allocations, range capabilities, and integration with other onboard systems like navigation, fish-finding, and safety equipment. This forms a core part of fishing vessel design under the **2024 Scheme OBE framework**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fisherman sailing far into the sea. Just as you would use a **smartphone** on land to call family, get directions, check weather, and call for help in an emergency, a fishing vessel needs its own set of "smartphones" for the ocean. However, the ocean is a much harsher environment — radio waves travel differently over water, distances are vast, and help is not just down the road.

Think of maritime communication like a **layered safety net**:
- **Layer 1 (Local Talk)**: VHF radio — like talking to someone in the next town (range: ~30–50 nautical miles).
- **Layer 2 (Regional Talk)**: MF/HF radio — like talking to someone in a neighboring state (range: ~200–2000 nautical miles).
- **Layer 3 (Global Talk)**: Satellite communication (INMARSAT / Iridium) — like making an international call (worldwide coverage).
- **Layer 4 (Scream for Help)**: EPIRB and SART — like a personal emergency beacon that tells satellites "I am here, I need help."

Each system is designed for a specific purpose, and together they form the vessel's **communication lifeline**.

## 1.3 Key Communication Metrics & Standards

The most important physical and regulatory metrics for fishing vessel communication design are:

- **VHF (Very High Frequency) Band**: **156 MHz – 162 MHz** (Marine Band)
- **MF (Medium Frequency) Band**: **1.6 MHz – 4 MHz**
- **HF (High Frequency) Band**: **4 MHz – 27.5 MHz**
- **INMARSAT Frequencies**: **1.5 GHz / 1.6 GHz (L-band)**
- **EPIRB Frequency**: **406 MHz** (with 121.5 MHz homing)
- **AIS Frequencies**: **161.975 MHz and 162.025 MHz**
- **NAVTEX Frequency**: **518 kHz** (international) / **490 kHz** (national)
- **GMDSS Sea Areas**: **A1, A2, A3, A4** (defines required equipment)
- **Standard Power Output (VHF)**: **25 W** (high) / **1 W** (low)
- **Standard DSC Channel**: **Channel 70 (156.525 MHz)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing Vessel Specifics:** Per IMO and national fishing vessel safety rules (India: MFR 2017, Kerala State Marine Fishing Regulation), fishing vessels of length **≥ 20 m** must carry VHF DSC, and those operating beyond **50 nautical miles** must additionally carry **EPIRB** and either **MF/HF DSC** or **satellite communication**.

## 1.4 Classification of Communication Systems on Fishing Vessels

Fishing vessel communication systems can be broadly classified into **four functional categories**:

1. **External Radio Communication** — VHF, MF, HF, Satellite (INMARSAT, Iridium)
2. **Distress and Safety Systems** — EPIRB, SART, NAVTEX, DSC
3. **Automatic Identification and Tracking** — AIS, VMS (Vessel Monitoring System)
4. **Internal Communication** — Talk-back systems, sound-powered telephones, public address (PA) systems, alarm systems
5. **Visual and Acoustic Signals** — Signal flags, shapes, lights, sound signals (whistle, bell, gong)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why and How" Behind Each Communication System

### 2.1.1 VHF (Very High Frequency) Radio — The Workhorse

- **Why**: VHF is the primary short-range communication tool on every fishing vessel. It is simple, cheap, and highly reliable for ship-to-ship and ship-to-shore communication within line-of-sight.
- **How**: VHF waves travel in straight lines (line-of-sight propagation). The range is therefore limited by the curvature of the Earth and the height of the antennas. VHF is unaffected by ionospheric variations, making it extremely stable.
- **Operating Channels**: Channel 16 (156.8 MHz) is the international distress, safety, and calling channel. Channel 70 is reserved exclusively for **Digital Selective Calling (DSC)**.
- **KTU Exam Insight**: The examiner often tests the difference between simplex and duplex channels. Fishing vessels predominantly use **simplex** (single frequency, push-to-talk).

### 2.1.2 DSC (Digital Selective Calling) — The Silent Watchman

- **Why**: Before DSC, a sailor had to stand watch on Channel 16 for hours. DSC automates this — the receiver continuously scans Channel 70 and triggers an alarm when a distress call is received.
- **How**: DSC transmits a short digital burst containing the vessel's **MMSI (Maritime Mobile Service Identity)** number, the nature of distress, and the position (if GPS is connected). The message is decoded automatically by all DSC-equipped stations within range.
- **MMSI Format**: A unique 9-digit number. For Indian fishing vessels, it begins with **419** (India's MID code).

### 2.1.3 MF/HF Radio — The Long-Distance Lifeline

- **Why**: VHF's line-of-sight limitation is a problem for deep-sea fishing vessels operating hundreds of nautical miles offshore. MF/HF uses **skywave (ionospheric) propagation** to achieve ranges of thousands of nautical miles.
- **How**: HF radio waves are refracted by the **ionosphere** (a layer of charged particles at ~60–400 km altitude) and reflected back to Earth, enabling over-the-horizon communication. The choice of frequency depends on time of day, season, and solar activity.
- **Limitation**: Requires a skilled operator, large antenna, and is subject to atmospheric noise and ionospheric disturbances.

### 2.1.4 Satellite Communication (INMARSAT / Iridium)

- **Why**: Provides truly global coverage, including the polar regions (Iridium). Essential for vessels operating in Sea Areas A3 and A4.
- **How**: Uses geostationary satellites (INMARSAT, at 35,786 km altitude) or low-Earth-orbit constellations (Iridium, 66 satellites at ~780 km altitude). The vessel's terminal communicates with the satellite, which relays the signal to a ground Earth Station (GES) connected to the public telephone network.
- **Fishing Vessel Use**: Voice calls to home, email, weather data, vessel tracking, telemedicine.

### 2.1.5 EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)

- **Why**: The last line of defense in a catastrophic emergency (vessel sinking, fire, abandon ship). Automatically alerts rescue authorities worldwide.
- **How**: When activated (manually or by hydrostatic release at 1.5–4 m depth), the EPIRB transmits a **406 MHz** distress signal to the **COSPAS-SARSAT** satellite system. The signal is relayed to a **Local User Terminal (LUT)** and then to a **Mission Control Centre (MCC)**, which alerts the nearest **Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC)**.
- **Key Parameter**: Modern EPIRBs include a **GPS receiver** that embeds the position accurate to **< 100 m**.

### 2.1.6 SART (Search and Rescue Transponder)

- **Why**: Helps rescuers locate a survival craft (lifeboat/liferaft) or a distressed vessel. Works in conjunction with **X-band radar (9 GHz)**.
- **How**: When interrogated by a ship's or aircraft's 9 GHz radar pulse, the SART automatically transmits a response signal that appears as a **line of 12 dots** on the radar screen, leading the rescue craft directly to the survivors.

### 2.1.7 NAVTEX (Navigational Telex)

- **Why**: Provides automatic, continuous broadcast of navigational and meteorological warnings, weather forecasts, and urgent safety information. The receiver prints messages on paper — no operator action needed.
- **How**: Coastal stations broadcast on **518 kHz** (English, international) in a scheduled time-slot sequence. Each station has a unique identifier (B1-B2 character).
- **Range**: Typically **200–400 nautical miles** from the coast.

### 2.1.8 AIS (Automatic Identification System)

- **Why**: Mandatory under SOLAS Chapter V for vessels ≥ 300 GT, and increasingly adopted by fishing vessels for collision avoidance and fleet management.
- **How**: Vessels automatically broadcast their position, course, speed, and vessel data on **161.975 MHz and 162.025 MHz** using **SOTDMA (Self-Organizing Time Division Multiple Access)**. Other ships and shore stations receive this data and display the vessel as a triangle on an electronic chart.
- **Fishing Vessel Application**: AIS helps fisheries authorities monitor fishing effort, detect illegal fishing, and avoid collisions in busy fishing grounds.

### 2.1.9 VMS (Vessel Monitoring System)

- **Why**: Mandated by fisheries regulations worldwide. Allows the fisheries control center to track every licensed fishing vessel in near real-time.
- **How**: A VMS unit on the vessel transmits position reports (via INMARSAT or Iridium) at fixed intervals (typically every 1–6 hours) to a satellite, which forwards them to a **Fishing Monitoring Centre (FMC)**.

### 2.1.10 Internal Communication

- **Why**: On a noisy fishing vessel, the crew must be able to communicate between the wheelhouse, engine room, and deck in all conditions — even during power failure.
- **How**: Uses **talkback systems** (single-button press-to-talk), **sound-powered telephones** (no external power required, work on the principle of a dynamic microphone), and **general alarm / public address systems**.

### 2.1.11 Visual and Sound Signals (COLREGs)

- **Why**: Last-resort communication when radio fails, and legally required for indicating vessel status, maneuver intentions, and emergencies.
- **How**: Specified by the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs, 1972)**. Includes:
  - **Day shapes** (e.g., two black balls = "not under command"; three balls = "aground")
  - **Signal flags** (e.g., "November" flag = negative, "Charlie" flag = "yes/affirmative")
  - **Sound signals** (e.g., 5 short blasts = "danger signal"; prolonged-blast-prolonged-blast = "I am operating astern propulsion")
  - **Lights** (e.g., all-round red over red = "fishing vessel with outlying gear")

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing Vessel Distress Signals (COLREGs Rule 27):** A vessel engaged in fishing, when in distress, may sound the group of 5 prolonged blasts, repeated at intervals of not less than 1 minute, while underway or at anchor.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Radio Range and Propagation

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$D_{VHF} \approx 2.5 \times \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)$$ | Optical (line-of-sight) range of VHF, where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are antenna heights in metres | Nautical Miles (NM) |
| $$D_{VHF(nm)} \approx 1.23 \times \left(\sqrt{h_{1(ft)}} + \sqrt{h_{2(ft)}}\right)$$ | Same, with antenna heights in feet | Nautical Miles (NM) |
| $$D_{total} \approx 2.5 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{9}\right)$$ | Modified range with frequency factor (approximate) | Nautical Miles (NM) |
| $$f_{MUF} \approx \sqrt{81 \times N_{max}}$$ | Maximum Usable Frequency (foF2) where $N_{max}$ is max electron density per $m^3$ | MHz |
| $$f_{optimum} \approx 0.85 \times f_{MUF}$$ | Optimum Working Frequency (OWF) for HF communication | MHz |

### 2.2.2 Free-Space Path Loss

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f)$$ | Free-space path loss | dB |
| $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{f}$$ | Wavelength, $f$ in Hz | metres |
| $$G = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$ | Parabolic dish antenna gain, $A_e$ = effective aperture | dBi |

### 2.2.3 Link Budget Equation

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - L_{fs} - L_{misc}$$ | Received power | dBm |
| $$SNR = P_{r} - N - B - F$$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (simplified) | dB |
| $$N = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B)$$ | Thermal noise floor, B in Hz | dBm |

### 2.2.4 EPIRB / COSPAS-SARSAT Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Beacon Frequency | **406.025 MHz ± 0.005 MHz** | International distress frequency |
| Homing Frequency | **121.5 MHz** | For final approach by rescue craft |
| Message Length | **0.5 s short / 1 s long** | Short for self-locating, long for serial ID |
| Repeating Cycle | **50 s ± 5%** | Once every 50 seconds |
| Polarization | **Circular (RHCP preferred)** | Robust against vessel motion |
| COSPAS-SARSAT Satellite Altitude | **800–1000 km (LEO)** | Low Earth Orbit |
| Position Accuracy (with GPS) | **< 100 m** | Modern EPIRBs |
| Position Accuracy (without GPS) | **5 km** | Doppler-based, older EPIRBs |

### 2.2.5 Battery Life Requirements (EPIRB)

| Condition | Minimum Duration | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Operating life at −20°C | **48 hours** | IMO A.810 |
| Storage battery life | **5 years** (or 1 year after activation) | — |
| Hydrostatic release depth | **1.5 m to 4 m** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the EPIRB distress frequency (406 MHz) with the SART response frequency (9 GHz, in the X-band). Remember: **EPIRB talks to satellites; SART talks to radars.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Fishing Fleet Communication Networks**: A fisheries authority can design a coastal VHF network with overlapping coverage by placing repeater stations at strategic hilltop locations. Antenna height is chosen to satisfy the desired range equation.
- **Cost Optimization**: A small artisanal fishing vessel may need only a fixed VHF DSC and one portable GMDSS VHF. A deep-sea trawler needs full GMDSS for Sea Area A3 (INMARSAT + VHF DSC + EPIRB + SART + NAVTEX + HF DSC).
- **Integration with Bridge Design**: Communication antennas must be sited with proper **vertical separation (≥ 2 m)** to avoid electromagnetic interference, and clear of obstruction from masts, funnels, and radar scanners.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Designing a fishing vessel without proper GMDSS equipment is illegal in most jurisdictions. The design must pass survey by the Mercantile Marine Department (MMD) or classification society.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optical Line-of-Sight Range for VHF

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Geometry

Consider two antennas of heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ metres above sea level. Because the Earth is curved, the maximum line-of-sight distance is determined by the geometry of the two antennas and the curved Earth surface.

Each antenna can "see" up to a horizon distance determined by the Earth's curvature.

**Step 1:** The geometric horizon distance from a single antenna of height $h$ metres.

Consider a tangent line from the top of the antenna touching the Earth. By Pythagoras' theorem:

$$R^{2} = (R + h)^{2}$$

where $R$ is the radius of the Earth.

**Step 2:** Expanding the right-hand side:

$$R^{2} = R^{2} + 2Rh + h^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Cancelling $R^2$ on both sides:

$$0 = 2Rh + h^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the horizon distance $d$ (which equals $\sqrt{2Rh}$ in the simplified geometry):

$$d = \sqrt{2Rh}$$

**Step 5:** For an Earth radius $R = 6371 \text{ km} = 6{,}371{,}000 \text{ m}$:

$$d = \sqrt{2 \times 6{,}371{,}000 \times h}$$

$$d = \sqrt{12{,}742{,}000 \times h}$$

**Step 6:** To express in **nautical miles (NM)**, we use the conversion $1 \text{ NM} = 1852 \text{ m}$:

$$d_{NM} = \frac{\sqrt{12{,}742{,}000 \times h}}{1852}$$

**Step 7:** Simplifying the constant $\sqrt{12{,}742{,}000} \approx 3569.6$:

$$d_{NM} = \frac{3569.6 \times \sqrt{h}}{1852}$$

**Step 8:** Computing the constant: $3569.6 / 1852 \approx 1.927$

$$d_{NM} \approx 1.93 \sqrt{h}$$

**Step 9:** This is the horizon distance from one antenna. Accounting for **atmospheric refraction** (which bends radio waves slightly downward, effectively extending the range by a factor of ~4/3), the standard empirical formula used in maritime practice is:

$$d_{NM} \approx 2.5 \sqrt{h_{(m)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **2.5** (instead of the geometric 1.93) is the internationally accepted constant for VHF/UHF maritime radio horizon, accounting for the 4/3 Earth radius equivalent due to standard atmospheric refraction.

**Step 10:** The total maximum communication distance between two vessels is the sum of both individual horizons:

$$D_{total} \approx 2.5 \sqrt{h_1} + 2.5 \sqrt{h_2}$$

$$\boxed{D_{total} \approx 2.5 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) \text{ NM}}$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A fishing vessel's VHF antenna is mounted **12 m** above sea level. The shore station antenna is on a hill **80 m** above sea level. Calculate the maximum reliable VHF communication range.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.
- $h_1 = 12$ m (vessel antenna)
- $h_2 = 80$ m (shore antenna)

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$D_{total} = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{12} + \sqrt{80}\right) \text{ NM}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the square roots.

$$\sqrt{12} = 3.464$$

$$\sqrt{80} = 8.944$$

**Step 4:** Sum the terms.

$$D_{total} = 2.5 \times (3.464 + 8.944)$$

$$D_{total} = 2.5 \times 12.408$$

$$D_{total} = 31.02 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 5:** Result.

$$\boxed{D_{total} \approx 31 \text{ NM}}$$

This is approximately the range from a fishing vessel to a coastal repeater on a hill — sufficient for normal coastal fishing operations.

### 3.1.3 Verification — Doubling the Height

If we double the shore antenna height to **160 m** (placing it on a 160 m hill):

$$D_{new} = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{12} + \sqrt{160}\right) = 2.5 (3.464 + 12.649) = 2.5 \times 16.113 = 40.28 \text{ NM}$$

**Observation:** A 4× increase in height yields only a **~30% increase** in range. This is because of the square-root relationship — antenna height has **diminishing returns** on range.

## 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss

The **Friis transmission equation** gives the received power in free space.

**Step 1:** Start with the isotropic radiator power flux density at distance $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** The effective area of the receiving antenna is:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Received power is the product of flux density and effective area:

$$P_r = S \times A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Expressing in dB form, $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$P_r(dBW) = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(c)$$

**Step 5:** With $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $20\log_{10}(c) = 169.54$ dB and $20\log_{10}(4\pi) = 21.98$ dB:

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) + 147.56$$

**Step 6:** Rewriting in the standard form with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$\boxed{L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) \text{ dB}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: EPIRB Power Budget

**Problem:** An EPIRB on a fishing vessel transmits 5 W on 406 MHz. A COSPAS-SARSAT satellite at 900 km altitude receives the signal. Calculate the path loss and verify the link feasibility.

**Step 1:** Convert parameters.
- $P_t = 5 \text{ W} = 10\log_{10}(5000) = 36.99 \text{ dBm}$
- $f = 406 \text{ MHz}$
- $d = 900 \text{ km}$

**Step 2:** Calculate path loss.

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(900) + 20\log_{10}(406)$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20(2.954) + 20(2.608)$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 59.08 + 52.17$$

$$L_{fs} = 143.69 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** Assume EPIRB antenna gain $G_t = 0$ dBi (small omnidirectional), satellite antenna gain $G_r = 4$ dBi.

**Step 4:** Received power.

$$P_r = 36.99 + 0 + 4 - 143.69 = -102.70 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with COSPAS-SARSAT receiver sensitivity ($\approx -154 \text{ dBm}$).

Since $P_r = -102.70 \text{ dBm}$ is well above the sensitivity, **the link is feasible** with a margin of about 51 dB.

$$\boxed{\text{Link margin} = -102.70 - (-154) = 51.3 \text{ dB (Excellent)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Communication Systems for Fishing Vessels
