| **Range** | 25–50 NM | 200–2000 NM | Global (excl. poles) | Global | 5–10 NM radar range | 200–400 NM | 20–30 NM |
| **Range Type** | Line-of-sight | Skywave / Ground wave | Satellite | Satellite (LEO) | Radar-based | Ground wave | Line-of-sight |
| **Primary Use** | Distress, routine | Long-range distress | Distress, voice, data | Distress only | Locating survivors | Safety info | Identification |
| **Power Output** | 25 W | 60–400 W | 25 W EIRP | 5 W | < 1 W | Receiver only | 12.5 W |

> - VHF ↔ AIS: at least **1.5 m** vertical separation

> - HF ↔ VHF: at least **3.0 m** vertical separation
> - GPS antenna must have **clear sky view** (no obstruction above 5° elevation)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the components of the Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS) and explain the four sea areas defined under it.**

**Model Answer:**

The GMDSS is an internationally agreed set of safety procedures, equipment, and communication protocols designed by the IMO to enhance maritime safety. Its main components are:

1. **VHF DSC** (Channel 70, 156.525 MHz) — for short-range distress alerting
2. **MF/HF DSC** — for medium and long-range distress alerting
3. **INMARSAT** — satellite communication for Sea Area A3
4. **EPIRB (406 MHz)** — for global distress alerting via COSPAS-SARSAT satellites
5. **SART (9 GHz)** — for locating survival craft
6. **NAVTEX (518 kHz)** — for receiving navigational and meteorological warnings
7. **Portable VHF** — for survival craft communication

**The four GMDSS Sea Areas are:**

- **Sea Area A1**: Within range of VHF DSC coast stations (typically 20–30 NM from shore)
- **Sea Area A2**: Beyond A1, within range of MF DSC coast stations (typically 100–150 NM)
- **Sea Area A3**: Beyond A2, within coverage of INMARSAT geostationary satellites (between 70°N and 70°S, excluding A1 and A2)
- **Sea Area A4**: Polar regions beyond INMARSAT coverage (above 70°N and below 70°S), where MF/HF DSC is mandatory

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Sea Area A1" as the *first* sea area to design for, but in GMDSS, A1 has the *least* equipment requirement. Remember the logic: greater area coverage demands *more* equipment.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between EPIRB and SART in terms of frequency, function, and operating principle.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **EPIRB** | **SART** |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 406 MHz (satellite uplink) + 121.5 MHz (homing) | 9 GHz (X-band radar responder) |
| **Function** | Globally alerts rescue authorities that the vessel is in distress | Helps rescuers locate the survival craft or distressed vessel |
| **Operating Principle** | Transmits a one-way distress signal to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites | Receives a radar pulse, transmits a response that appears as 12 dots on the radar |
| **Activation** | Manual or automatic (hydrostatic release at 1.5–4 m depth) | Manual switch-on in survival craft |
| **Range** | Global (via satellite) | 5–10 NM depending on radar height |
| **Battery Life** | 48 hours continuous | 96 hours standby + 8 hours operating |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that an EPIRB works like a SART. The EPIRB *transmits* to satellites; the SART *responds* to radar. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of VHF radio communication on a fishing vessel. With neat sketches, describe the propagation mechanism, typical antenna configuration, and the role of Channel 16 and Channel 70. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel has its VHF antenna at **9 m** above sea level. It wants to communicate with a shore station whose antenna is on a **120 m** cliff. Calculate the maximum reliable line-of-sight range. If the vessel increases its antenna height to **25 m**, what is the new range? Comment on the practicality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) VHF Radio Communication — Working Principle**

**Definition:** VHF (Very High Frequency) radio operates in the frequency band **156 MHz to 162 MHz** (the marine VHF band) and is the primary short-range communication system on fishing vessels.

**Propagation Mechanism:**
VHF radio waves travel in **straight lines** (line-of-sight propagation) and are not reflected by the ionosphere. The range is therefore limited by the optical horizon — the curvature of the Earth blocks signals beyond a certain distance. Atmospheric refraction slightly extends this horizon (a 4/3 Earth radius approximation is used).

**Typical Antenna Configuration:**
- A **vertical whip antenna** (fibreglass, ~1.2 m long) is mounted as high as possible on the mast.
- The antenna is fed by a coaxial cable to the VHF transceiver in the wheelhouse.
- A **ground plane** is effectively provided by the sea surface and the vessel's metal structure.

**Channel 16 (156.8 MHz):**
- The **international distress, safety, and calling channel**.
- Used for hailing other vessels and shore stations, and for voice distress communication.
- Must be monitored continuously by all vessels when practicable.

**Channel 70 (156.525 MHz):**
- Reserved exclusively for **Digital Selective Calling (DSC)**.
- Used for sending automated distress alerts, urgency, safety, and routine messages containing the vessel's MMSI number.
- A DSC-enabled receiver scans this channel continuously, freeing the operator from constant voice monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse Channel 16 (voice) with Channel 70 (DSC). Both are VHF, both are distress-related, but their functions and modulation are entirely different.

**(b) Range Calculation**

**Given:**
- Initial vessel antenna height: $h_1 = 9$ m
- Shore antenna height: $h_2 = 120$ m
- Revised vessel antenna height: $h_1' = 25$ m

**Step 1:** Apply the VHF line-of-sight formula:

$$D_{total} = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) \text{ NM}$$

**Step 2:** Case 1 — Original antenna height of 9 m.

$$D_1 = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{9} + \sqrt{120}\right) \text{ NM}$$

$$D_1 = 2.5 \left(3.000 + 10.954\right) \text{ NM}$$

$$D_1 = 2.5 \times 13.954$$

$$D_1 = 34.89 \text{ NM}$$

$$\boxed{D_1 \approx 34.9 \text{ NM}}$$

**Step 3:** Case 2 — Increased antenna height of 25 m.

$$D_2 = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{25} + \sqrt{120}\right) \text{ NM}$$

$$D_2 = 2.5 \left(5.000 + 10.954\right) \text{ NM}$$

$$D_2 = 2.5 \times 15.954$$

$$D_2 = 39.89 \text{ NM}$$

$$\boxed{D_2 \approx 39.9 \text{ NM}}$$

**Step 4:** Range improvement.

$$\Delta D = 39.89 - 34.89 = 5.0 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 5:** Percentage increase.

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{5.0}{34.89} \times 100 = 14.3\%$$

**Comment on Practicality:**
Increasing the vessel's antenna height from 9 m to 25 m (a **178% increase in height**) yields only a **14.3% increase in range**. This confirms the **square-root relationship** between antenna height and range. The gain is marginal. In practice, increasing antenna height also increases wind loading, structural complexity, and risk of lightning strike. It is therefore more cost-effective to install a **shore-based VHF repeater** on a higher hilltop (e.g., 200 m or 300 m) to extend coverage, rather than increasing the vessel's antenna height.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Describe the working of the COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system for distress alerting. Explain the role of EPIRB, LUT, MCC, and RCC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the components of an integrated communication system on a modern fishing vessel. Briefly discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) COSPAS-SARSAT System**

**Definition:** COSPAS-SARSAT is an international satellite-based search and rescue system that detects and locates distress signals transmitted by **406 MHz EPIRBs**, **ELTs** (aviation), and **PLBs** (personal locators).

**System Components:**

1. **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)**
   - When activated (manually, or automatically by the hydrostatic release when the vessel sinks to 1.5–4 m depth), the EPIRB transmits a brief digital message on **406.025 MHz** every 50 seconds.
   - The message contains the beacon's unique ID (which encodes the country of registration and vessel identification) and, in modern GPS-equipped EPIRBs, the **latitude and longitude**.

2. **LEO Satellites (COSPAS and SARSAT)**
   - Constellation of low-Earth-orbit satellites at ~800–1000 km altitude, orbiting the Earth in about 100 minutes.
   - Each satellite carries a **SAR (Search and Rescue) repeater** that listens on 406 MHz and a **SAR processor** that measures the **Doppler shift** of the received signal.
   - Since the satellite moves relative to the beacon, the received frequency shifts — the maximum Doppler shift occurs when the satellite is closest to the beacon. From this, the position can be calculated.

3. **LUT (Local User Terminal)**
   - Ground station that receives the satellite-repeated signal.
   - Computes the beacon's position using **Doppler position-fixing** (when no GPS encoding is present), with an accuracy of about 5 km.
   - With GPS-encoded EPIRBs, the position is accurate to **< 100 m** and the LUT forwards this directly.

4. **MCC (Mission Control Centre)**
   - National or regional centre that receives alert data from LUTs.
   - Validates the alert, filters out false alarms, and distributes the distress information to the appropriate **Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC)**.

5. **RCC (Rescue Coordination Centre)**
   - The operational authority that takes charge of the search and rescue mission.
   - In India, the **Maritime Rescue Coordination Centre (MRCC)** is located in Mumbai under the Indian Coast Guard.
   - The RCC dispatches rescue craft (ships, helicopters, aircraft) and coordinates with other vessels in the area.

**Working Sequence (Distress Event):**
1. Vessel in distress → EPIRB activated
2. Signal transmitted on 406 MHz every 50 s
3. COSPAS-SARSAT satellite receives signal during overhead pass
4. Satellite relays to LUT (or processes onboard)
5. LUT forwards to MCC
6. MCC validates and forwards to appropriate RCC
7. RCC initiates search and rescue
8. Rescue craft uses 121.5 MHz homing and SART for final approach

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that COSPAS-SARSAT uses **Doppler shift** for position fixing when GPS is not present. They also sometimes confuse the role of LUT and MCC — the LUT computes position, while the MCC distributes alerts.

**(b) Integrated Communication System Block Diagram**

**Block Diagram:**

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │    ANTENNA MAST (top of vessel)          │
        │  VHF | HF | GPS | AIS | EPIRB | Radar    │
        └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                       │ Coaxial / RF feeders
        ┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
        │   ANTENNA DISTRIBUTION & GROUNDING        │
        │   (lightning arrestors, splitters)        │
        └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
        │   CENTRAL COMMUNICATION CONSOLE            │
        │   (Wheelhouse)                            │
        │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐           │
        │  │ VHF DSC    │  │ MF/HF DSC  │           │
        │  │ Transceiver│  │ Transceiver│           │
        │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘           │
        │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐           │
        │  │ INMARSAT-C │  │ NAVTEX     │           │
        │  │ Terminal   │  │ Receiver   │           │
        │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘           │
        │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐           │
        │  │ AIS Class A│  │ VMS Unit   │           │
        │  │ Transponder│  │            │           │
        │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘           │
        └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
        │   POWER SUPPLY SYSTEM                      │
        │  Main 24V DC | Emergency 24V DC            │
        │  Battery backup for GMDSS (≥ 6h)          │
        └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
        │   INTERNAL COMMUNICATION                   │
        │  Talkback | Sound-powered phone | PA       │
        │  (Wheelhouse ↔ Engine room ↔ Deck)        │
        └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
        │   DISTRESS BEACONS (EPIRB, SART)          │
        │  Mounted externally with HRU               │
        └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Function of Each Block:**

1. **Antenna Mast Assembly**: Houses all radiating elements at appropriate vertical separation to minimize mutual interference. Must be clear of obstructions.
2. **Antenna Distribution & Grounding**: Routes RF signals to/from the transceivers, provides lightning protection (essential for mast integrity).
3. **VHF DSC Transceiver**: Primary voice and DSC communication up to ~50 NM. Channel 16/70 monitoring.
4. **MF/HF DSC Transceiver**: Long-range voice and DSC communication via skywave propagation (up to 2000+ NM).
5. **INMARSAT-C Terminal**: Satellite communication for Sea Area A3. Supports distress alerting (one-button), email, telex, and polling.
6. **NAVTEX Receiver**: Automatic receiver/recorder for navigational and meteorological warnings on 518 kHz.
7. **AIS Class A Transponder**: Automatic broadcasting of vessel identity, position, course, speed for collision avoidance and fleet monitoring.
8. **VMS Unit**: Fisheries-mandated position reporting to the FMC.
9. **Power Supply System**: Main 24V DC from the vessel's alternators, backed by emergency batteries capable of powering GMDSS for at least 6 hours (per SOLAS).
10. **Internal Communication**: Talkback system (Captain ↔ Crew), sound-powered phones (fail-safe), and general alarm system.
11. **EPIRB & SART**: Mounted externally, with hydrostatic release units (HRU) for automatic deployment on sinking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** In block diagram questions, students often forget to show the **power supply** and **antenna** subsystems. The examiner specifically looks for these, as they are the backbone of any communication installation. Marks are deducted if these are omitted.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Communication Questions (KTU Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing frequencies**: Mixing up 406 MHz (EPIRB) with 9 GHz (SART) or 121.5 MHz (homing) with 156.8 MHz (Channel 16 VHF).
> 2. **Forgetting units**: Always quote antenna heights in **metres** and range in **nautical miles** for the maritime formula. Do not use kilometres.
> 3. **Confusing DSC and voice**: Channel 16 is **voice distress**; Channel 70 is **DSC (digital)**. They are not the same.
> 4. **Wrong constant in range formula**: Use **2.5** for VHF (with 4/3 Earth refraction), not 1.93. The 2.5 constant is internationally accepted in maritime practice.
> 5. **Omitting power backup**: Any communication system design must include the **emergency power source** (battery) capable of operating for at least **6 hours** as per SOLAS.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **VHF** is the workhorse for short-range (line-of-sight) communication. Range formula: $D = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)$ NM.
- ✅ **Channel 16** (156.8 MHz) = voice distress, calling, safety. **Channel 70** (156.525 MHz) = DSC (digital distress).
- ✅ **MF/HF** is used for long-range communication via **skywave (ionospheric) propagation**. Range: hundreds to thousands of NM.
- ✅ **INMARSAT** uses **geostationary satellites** at 35,786 km altitude. Coverage: global except poles. **Iridium** uses **LEO constellation** for true global coverage.
- ✅ **EPIRB** transmits on **406 MHz** to **COSPAS-SARSAT** LEO satellites. Battery life: **48 hours**. Automatic activation by **hydrostatic release** at 1.5–4 m depth.
- ✅ **SART** operates on **9 GHz (X-band)**, **responds** to radar interrogation, displays as **12 dots** on the radar screen.
- ✅ **NAVTEX** operates on **518 kHz** (international, English) and **490 kHz** (national, local language). Range: **200–400 NM**.
- ✅ **AIS** operates on **161.975 MHz and 162.025 MHz** using **SOTDMA**. Range: **20–30 NM**. Class A = SOLAS vessels; Class B = smaller vessels.
- ✅ **VMS** (Vessel Monitoring System) is mandatory for licensed fishing vessels in most countries. Position reports every **1–6 hours** via satellite.
- ✅ **GMDSS Sea Areas**: A1 (VHF range), A2 (MF range), A3 (INMARSAT range), A4 (polar — HF only).
- ✅ **Fishing vessel day signals**: Two black balls in a vertical line = fishing vessel with trawl/gear. Three black balls = vessel aground.
- ✅ **Distress sound signal**: **5 prolonged blasts** on the whistle — applicable to fishing vessels in distress.
- ✅ **Antenna separation rules**: VHF ↔ AIS ≥ 1.5 m; VHF ↔ Radar ≥ 2.0 m; HF ↔ VHF ≥ 3.0 m.
- ✅ **Power backup**: GMDSS must have **6 hours** of emergency battery backup as per SOLAS.
- ✅ **DSC distress alert** contains: vessel **MMSI**, **nature of distress**, **position** (if GPS connected), **time**, and **type of subsequent communication**.
- ✅ **Indian fishing vessel MMSI** starts with **419** (India's Maritime Identification Digits).
- ✅ **EPIRB position accuracy**: With GPS = **< 100 m**; without GPS (Doppler only) = **~5 km**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "list" communication systems, always structure your answer in **functional categories** (External / Distress / Identification / Internal / Visual-Acoustic) rather than a flat list. This signals structured thinking to the examiner and fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firefighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firefighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Marine Firefighting?

**Marine Firefighting** refers to the integrated set of detection, containment, suppression, and evacuation systems installed on a fishing vessel to detect, control, and extinguish unwanted fires at sea, thereby protecting human life, the vessel's structural integrity, the catch, and the marine environment. On a fishing vessel, firefighting is uniquely challenging because:

- The vessel is a **confined, isolated platform** with no external fire brigade support.
- Fire loads are high (fuel oils, lubrication oils, paints, fishmeal, ammonia refrigeration on some trawlers).
- Crew is small, often fatigued, and frequently untrained for major fire emergencies.
- The ship is at sea in **rolling, pitching, and heaving** motions, making hose handling difficult.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Firefighting forms the heart of a fishing vessel's **Life-Safety and Habitability** design package, governed internationally by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter II-2**, the **IMO International Code for Fire Safety Systems (FSS Code)**, and the **Torremolinos International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels (1977)** and its **1993 Protocol**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Factory and the Fire Triangle"

Imagine a small floating factory with cooking oil, diesel generators, a paint locker, plastic fish boxes, and a soldering workshop — all packed into a steel box that is constantly moving. Fire needs three ingredients:

- **Fuel** (oil, wood, paint)
- **Heat** (engine, galley, electrical faults)
- **Oxygen** (ventilated engine room)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Triangle:** Fire can only exist if all three sides — **Fuel + Heat + Oxygen** — are simultaneously present. Firefighting is simply the engineering effort to **break at least one side** of this triangle.

A fishing vessel is "designed for fire safety" when its layout physically separates fuel sources from heat sources, the ventilation can be shut (cutting oxygen), and the crew carries tools to remove heat (water/foam) or fuel (shutdown valves).

### 1.3 The Five Classes of Fire (Adapted for Vessels)

| Class | Fuel Type | Marine Examples | Wrong Extinguishing Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood decks, fish crates, mattresses, ropes, paper | Never use water jets on electrical fires |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids | Diesel oil, lubricating oil, bilge slops, cooking oil | Never use a direct water jet (causes splashing/spread) |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases | LPG cylinders (galley), acetylene (workshop) | Shut off gas source first |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium alloy fish-handling gear, lithium batteries | Special dry powder; water reacts violently |
| **Class K** (F) | Cooking oils/fats | Galley deep-fat fryers | Wet chemical or foam; never water jet (steam explosion) |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Used in Vessel Firefighting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glossary of KTU-Level Terms (must memorise):**
> - **Fire Zone** – A section of the vessel separated by **A-Class or B-Class divisions** to contain fire.
> - **A-Class Division** – A steel bulkhead/deck tested to withstand a standard fire for **60 minutes**.
> - **B-Class Division** – Non-combustible division with **30 minutes** fire resistance.
> - **Fixed Firefighting System (FFS)** – Permanently installed (e.g., CO₂ room flooding).
> - **Portable Fire Extinguisher (PFE)** – Hand-carried, ≤ **23 kg** gross weight.
> - **Fire Main** – The dedicated sea-water piping ring supplying hydrants.
> - **Hydrant** – Valve outlet on the fire main for hose connection.
> - **International Shore Connection (ISC)** – A standard flange to receive shore fire brigade water.
> - **Breathing Apparatus (BA)** – Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) with **30 minutes** air supply minimum.
> - **Fireman's Outfit** – Complete protective kit (helmet, boots, gloves, BA, axe, lifeline).
> - **Fire Detection System** – Smoke, heat, or flame sensors connected to a central panel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Fire at Sea

Fire on a vessel follows the classical combustion chain:

$$\text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Activation Energy}} \text{Flame} \rightarrow \text{Products} + \text{More Heat (Feedback)}$$

For sustained combustion, the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** must exceed heat losses. The fire growth on a vessel typically follows three phases:

1. **Incipient Phase (0–5 min):** Small flame, smouldering. Detectors trigger.
2. **Growth Phase (5–15 min):** HRR rises rapidly. Flashover possible.
3. **Fully Developed Phase (>15 min):** Room temperature may exceed **1000 °C**; structural steel begins to lose strength at **≈ 550 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Steel Temperature:** Mild steel retains full strength up to **≈ 400 °C** and loses **50%** of yield strength at **≈ 550 °C**. This is why SOLAS requires A-Class divisions to limit average temperature rise on the unexposed face to **≤ 140 °C** and any single point to **≤ 180 °C** above ambient.

## 2.2 Sources of Fire on a Fishing Vessel

Fishing vessels have specific high-risk zones ranked by historical fire data:

1. **Engine Room** — hot surfaces, oil leakage, electrical faults (≈ 45% of fires)
2. **Galley** — cooking oil, LPG (≈ 18%)
3. **Fishing Deck / Processing Area** — welding, electrical, fishmeal dust (≈ 12%)
4. **Refrigeration Machinery Space** — ammonia (older trawlers)
5. **Paint / Flammable Liquid Locker** — solvents, paints
6. **Accommodation** — electrical, smoking

## 2.3 Fixed Firefighting Systems — Selection Logic

| System | Best For Class | Suppression Mechanism | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water Spray (Deluge)** | A | Cools below ignition temp | Cannot be used in electrical rooms |
| **Water Mist (High Pressure)** | A, B | Cools + local oxygen displacement | Limited penetration in open deck fire |
| **Foam (AFFF / Protein)** | A, B | Smothers fuel + prevents re-ignition | Cannot be used on electrical; expensive |
| **CO₂** | B, C, Electrical | Displaces O₂ below 14% | **Lethal to crew** — needs total evacuation |
| **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC)** | A, B, C, Electrical | Chemical chain reaction interruption | No cooling; messy |
| **Wet Chemical** | K (Cooking oil) | Saponification (forms soap layer) | Galley-specific |
| **Clean Agent (Novec 1230 / FM-200)** | B, C, Electrical | Heat absorption + chemical | Environmental regulations on HFCs |

## 2.4 The CO₂ Total Flooding System — Engineering Logic

A **CO₂ total flooding system** for an engine room works as follows:

1. CO₂ cylinders (typically stored at **≈ 5.7 MPa / 57 bar** at 20 °C) are pressurised.
2. Upon activation, the louver/scoop on the engine room intake closes automatically.
3. Fire dampers on ducts close.
4. A **time delay of 30 seconds** allows crew evacuation (audible + visual alarm).
5. CO₂ is released and floods the space.
6. The required concentration is **34% minimum** for class B liquids (IMO FSS Code).
7. CO₂ remains for **≥ 20 minutes** to prevent re-ignition.

The required CO₂ mass is found by the standard formula:

$$M_{CO_2} = \frac{V \cdot k}{100 - c_0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{100 - c_t}\right)$$

where:
- $M_{CO_2}$ = mass of CO₂ required (kg)
- $V$ = gross volume of protected space (m³)
- $k$ = specific volume constant (depends on temperature)
- $c_0$ = initial CO₂ concentration (assumed 0%)
- $c_t$ = target final concentration (34% for class B)

## 2.5 Fire Pump Sizing — The Critical Equation

The fire pump must simultaneously supply **two fire hydrants at the highest pressure** with adequate pressure and flow. The IMO FSS Code mandates:

$$Q_{fire\,pump} \geq \frac{2}{3} \cdot \sum Q_{hydrants\,at\,max\,pressure}$$

For typical small fishing vessels (≤ 24 m), a single fire pump rated at:

$$Q_{fire\,pump} \geq 15 \;\text{m}^3/\text{hr} \approx 4.17 \;\text{L/s}$$

with a minimum nozzle pressure of:

$$P_{nozzle} \geq 0.25 \;\text{MPa} \;(2.5 \;\text{bar})$$

The pump discharge pressure must overcome:

$$P_{pump} = P_{nozzle} + \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{elevation} + P_{hose\,losses}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Variables | Typical Vessel Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{pump} = 2 \times Q_{max\,hydrant}$ | Required pump flow | ≥ 15 m³/hr | m³/hr |
| 2 | $P_{nozzle} \geq 0.25$ | Minimum nozzle pressure | 2.5 | MPa |
| 3 | $H_{friction} = \dfrac{4f L v^2}{2gD}$ | Darcy–Weisbach loss | varies | m |
| 4 | $M_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V \cdot k}{100}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{100}{66}\right)$ | CO₂ quantity | engine room: ≈ 0.7–1.0 kg/m³ | kg |
| 5 | $N_{extinguishers\,B} = 1$ per | Dry powder for 2-stroke engines | ≥ 12 kg | kg |
| 6 | $t_{SCBA} = \dfrac{V_{cylinder}}{V_{breathing\,rate}}$ | BA endurance | ≥ 30 | min |
| 7 | $N_{fireman's\,outfit} = 2$ per | Outfits for ships > 500 GT | 2 | nos |
| 8 | $A_{foam} = Q_{foam\,solution} \cdot t_{discharge}$ | Foam area coverage | 5 L/m²·min | m² |
| 9 | $A_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V}{v_{specific}}$ | CO₂ volume per mass | 0.56 m³/kg @ 0 °C | m³/kg |
| 10 | Steel softening | Critical temp. of mild steel | ≈ 550 °C | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise these eight quantities** — they appear almost every KTU university exam for fishing vessel design. The discharge pressure of the fire pump is also a frequent 14-mark derivation.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The firefighting system of a fishing vessel is not just a regulatory burden — it directly affects:

- **Insurance premium** (vessels without FSS pay 25–40% more).
- **Classification** (Class symbols of IRS, BV, DNV, LR include firefighting notation).
- **Man-overboard and Abandon Ship** response time.
- **Crew certification** (STCW Basic Safety Training familiarisation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fire Pump Discharge Pressure

A 20 m fishing trawler has a fire main of diameter $D = 65$ mm, with a total equivalent length (including fittings) of $L = 80$ m, supplying a hydrant 6 m above the pump. The nozzle is 12 m hose-type with diameter 12 mm. Water is sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Friction factor $f = 0.02$. Required nozzle flow $Q = 2.5$ L/s. Compute pump head.

**Step 1 — Compute velocities in main and nozzle:**

$$A_{main} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.065)^2}{4} = 3.318 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$v_{main} = \frac{Q}{A_{main}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.318 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.753 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$v_{nozzle} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.131 \times 10^{-4}} = 22.10 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute friction head loss in the main (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_{f,main} = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 (0.02)(80)(0.753)^2}{2 (9.81)(0.065)}$$

$$h_{f,main} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 80 \times 0.5672}{1.2753} = \frac{3.630}{1.2753} = 2.846 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction head loss in the hose (12 m, $D = 0.04$ m, $f = 0.025$):**

$$v_{hose} = \frac{Q}{A_{hose}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi (0.04)^2 / 4} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.989 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$h_{f,hose} = \frac{4 (0.025)(12)(1.989)^2}{2 (9.81)(0.04)} = \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 12 \times 3.956}{0.7848} = \frac{4.747}{0.7848} = 6.05 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute elevation head:**

$$h_{elev} = 6 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute nozzle pressure head (from $P = 0.25$ MPa):**

$$h_{nozzle} = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{0.25 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} = 24.86 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute velocity head at nozzle (kinetic energy delivered to jet):**

$$h_{v,nozzle} = \frac{v_{nozzle}^2}{2g} = \frac{(22.10)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 24.89 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Total pump head by Bernoulli's energy balance:**

$$H_{pump} = h_{nozzle} + h_{v,nozzle} + h_{f,main} + h_{f,hose} + h_{elev}$$

$$H_{pump} = 24.86 + 24.89 + 2.85 + 6.05 + 6.00 = 64.65 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to pressure for pump selection:**

$$P_{pump} = \rho g H_{pump} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 64.65 = 6.50 \times 10^5 \;\text{Pa} = 0.65 \;\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The fire pump for this 20 m trawler must develop approximately **0.65 MPa (6.5 bar)** at a flow of **2.5 L/s (9 m³/hr)** to satisfy the IMO FSS Code. Standard marine fire pumps are typically rated **0.7–1.0 MPa**, so this is within design margin.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CO₂ Quantity for Engine Room Flooding

Consider an engine room of length 8 m, beam 3.5 m, height 2.8 m. Compute CO₂ required for class B flooding.

**Step 1 — Volume of space:**

$$V = 8 \times 3.5 \times 2.8 = 78.4 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required concentration:** For class B liquids, IMO requires **34%** by volume.

**Step 3 — Use the simplified IMO FSS Code formula:**

For ambient temperature of 20 °C, the specific volume factor is used. The simplified formula taught in KTU courses:

$$M_{CO_2} = \alpha \cdot V \cdot \frac{c_t}{100 - c_t}$$

where $\alpha$ is a temperature-dependent factor (≈ 1.10 kg/m³ for 20 °C):

$$M_{CO_2} = 1.10 \times 78.4 \times \frac{34}{66} = 1.10 \times 78.4 \times 0.5152 = 44.43 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Add 10% safety margin (per IMO FSS Code):**

$$M_{CO_2,design} = 1.10 \times 44.43 = 48.87 \;\text{kg} \approx 50 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Number of 45 kg cylinders needed:**

$$N_{cyl} = \left\lceil \frac{50}{45} \right\rceil = 2 \;\text{cylinders}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For the same engine room, halon alternatives (FM-200 or Novec 1230) would require only **≈ 3.6 kg** (≈ 8× less) due to higher suppression efficiency, but at significantly higher cost and environmental scrutiny.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Portable Fire Extinguishers for a 24 m Trawler

Per IMO and IRS rules:

| Zone | Quantity per Zone | Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| Machinery spaces of category A | 1 × dry powder (12 kg) per engine | 12 kg DP |
| Galley | 1 × wet chemical + 1 × CO₂ 5 kg | 5 kg CO₂ |
| Wheelhouse / Navigation | 1 × CO₂ 5 kg | 5 kg |
| Fishing deck | 2 × dry powder 9 kg | 9 kg each |
| Accommodation | 1 per deck per 20 m corridor | 5 kg DP |
| Paint locker | 1 × CO₂ 5 kg outside the door | 5 kg |

For our trawler with single engine + galley + wheelhouse + accommodation + deck:

$$N_{PFE} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 8 \;\text{extinguishers\,(total)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Water Mist | Foam (AFFF) | CO₂ | Dry Powder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best for class | A, B | A, B | B, C, Electrical | A, B, C, Electrical |
| Crew-safe? | Yes | Yes (low expansion) | **No — lethal** | Yes |
| Cost (relative) | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Equipment footprint | High pressure pump | Tank + proportioner | Heavy cylinders | Light cylinders |
| Re-ignition risk | Low | Very low | Low | High |
| Cooling effect | High | Medium | None | None |
| Suitable for engine room? | Yes (with care) | Limited | **Yes (gold standard)** | Yes (backup) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Vessel Firefighting System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Sea Chest / Suction Inlet"]
    node2["Fire Pump Set A (Main)"]
    node3["Fire Pump Set B (Emergency)"]
    node4["Fire Main Ring (DN65)"]
    node5["Hydrant 1 - Aft Deck"]
    node6["Hydrant 2 - Engine Room"]
    node7["Hydrant 3 - Forward"]
    node8["Foam Proportioner Unit"]
    node9["AFFF Tank 200 L"]
    node10["CO2 Cylinder Bank"]
    node11["Engine Room Flooding Nozzles"]
    node12["Detection Panel"]
    node13["Smoke Detector - ER"]
    node14["Heat Detector - Galley"]
    node15["Manual Call Point - Accommodation"]
    node16["Fireman's Outfit Locker"]
    node17["SCBA Sets (2)"]
    node18["International Shore Connection"]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node4 --> node8
    node9 --> node8
    node4 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node13 --> node12
    node14 --> node12
    node15 --> node12
    node12 --> node2
    node4 --> node18
    node16 --> node17
```

## 4.2 CO₂ Total Flooding Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Fire Detected or Manual Release"]
    stepB["Fire Detection Panel Activates"]
    stepC["Audible + Visual Alarm in Protected Space"]
    stepD["Time Delay 30 s - Crew Evacuates"]
    stepE["Ventilation Fire Dampers Close"]
    stepF["Engine Room Scoop / Louver Closes"]
    stepG["CO2 Cylinder Bank Pneumatic Release"]
    stepH["CO2 Floods Space to 34% Concentration"]
    stepI["Hold Period 20 min - No Re-Ignition"]
    stepJ["Space Vented - Air Quality Tested"]
    stepK["Crew Re-Enters with SCBA"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
```

## 4.3 Fire Class Decision Matrix for Crew

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clsA["Class A - Wood, Paper, Rope"]
    clsB["Class B - Oil, Diesel"]
    clsC["Class C - Gas, Electrical"]
    clsD["Class D - Metal"]
    clsK["Class K - Cooking Oil"]
    actA["Use WATER jet or foam"]
    actB["Use FOAM or DRY POWDER"]
    actC["Use CO2 or DRY POWDER"]
    actD["Use SPECIAL DRY POWDER"]
    actK["Use WET CHEMICAL"]
    noUse["NEVER use water jet"]

    clsA --> actA
    clsB --> actB
    clsC --> actC
    clsD --> actD
    clsK --> actK
    clsB --> noUse
    clsC --> noUse
    clsK --> noUse
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of Fire Detection Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sen1["Smoke Sensor - Engine Room"]
    sen2["Heat Sensor - Galley"]
    sen3["Flame Detector - Paint Locker"]
    sen4["Manual Call Point - Deck"]

    buz["Alarm Bell + Strobe"]
    log["Event Logger"]
    fan["Ventilation Trip Relay"]
    pump["Fire Pump Auto-Start"]
    co2["CO2 Release Solenoid"]

    sen1 --> pan
    sen2 --> pan
    sen3 --> pan
    sen4 --> pan
    pan --> buz
    pan --> log
    pan --> fan
    pan --> pump
    pan --> co2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Fire Zone" and "A-Class Division" as applied to a fishing vessel. Why is steel used as the primary material for A-Class divisions?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fire Zone** is a section of the vessel (typically the engine room, galley, and accommodation) bounded by **A-Class or B-Class structural divisions** to prevent the spread of fire, smoke, and heat to adjacent spaces for a defined time.

An **A-Class Division** is a bulkhead or deck made of steel (or equivalent non-combustible material) that prevents the passage of smoke and flame for **60 minutes** under a standard fire test (per SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.2). The unexposed face temperature rise must be ≤ 140 °C average and ≤ 180 °C at any point.

**Steel is used** because:
- It is **non-combustible**.
- It has high **melting point (≈ 1500 °C)**.
- It retains structural strength up to ≈ 550 °C, giving time for evacuation.
- It can be cut, welded, and inspected easily during construction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1-mark deduction):** Students often write "A-Class = 30 minutes" — that is B-Class. Memorise: **A → 60 min, B → 30 min**.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six portable fire extinguishers commonly fitted on a 24 m fishing trawler, specifying the class of fire each is intended for.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Extinguisher | Class of Fire |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water (9 L) | A |
| 2 | Mechanical foam (9 L AFFF) | A, B |
| 3 | CO₂ (5 kg) | B, C, Electrical |
| 4 | Dry Powder ABC (9 kg) | A, B, C, Electrical |
| 5 | Wet Chemical (6 L) | K (Cooking oil) |
| 6 | Clean Agent FM-200 | B, C, Electrical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (1-mark loss):** Students often wrongly assign "water" for "diesel oil" — diesel is class B and water jet is **dangerous** (causes splashing). Use foam or dry powder.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 3, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **CO₂ total flooding system** for the engine room of a fishing vessel. State the minimum CO₂ concentration and the time delay before release.

**(b) [7 marks]** An engine room of dimensions $L = 10$ m, $B = 4$ m, $H = 3$ m is to be protected by a CO₂ total flooding system. Calculate the mass of CO₂ required. Use a design concentration of 34% by volume and an ambient temperature of 20 °C (use the simplified formula with $\alpha = 1.10$ kg/m³). Specify the number of 45 kg cylinders to be carried, applying a 10% safety margin.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **CO₂ total flooding system** works in the following sequence:

1. **Detection:** A smoke or flame detector in the engine room sends a signal to the **Fire Detection Panel**.
2. **Alarm:** The panel activates **audible sirens and visual strobes** in the engine room, machinery control room, and on the bridge.
3. **Automatic Trip:** The system **shuts the engine room ventilation fans** by closing motorised fire dampers within 30 s.
4. **Time Delay:** A preset **30-second delay** allows any crew inside to evacuate, accompanied by a pre-discharge alarm distinct from the fire alarm.
5. **Release:** CO₂ from pressurised cylinders (charged at **≈ 57 bar at 20 °C**) discharges through **distribution nozzles** mounted on the overhead of the engine room.
6. **Flooding:** The space is flooded to a minimum concentration of **34% by volume** (per IMO FSS Code) for class B fires. At this concentration, O₂ is displaced below the **14% level** required to support combustion of hydrocarbon liquids.
7. **Hold Period:** CO₂ is held for at least **20 minutes** to prevent re-ignition of hot surfaces.
8. **Re-entry:** After testing air quality (O₂ > 21%, CO₂ < 0.5%), crew re-enter wearing **SCBA** with full fireman's outfit.

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above for the Mermaid flowchart of the same sequence).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Volume of the engine room:**

$$V = L \times B \times H = 10 \times 4 \times 3 = 120 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified CO₂ formula:**

$$M_{CO_2} = \alpha \cdot V \cdot \frac{c_t}{100 - c_t} = 1.10 \times 120 \times \frac{34}{66}$$

$$M_{CO_2} = 1.10 \times 120 \times 0.5152 = 67.99 \;\text{kg} \approx 68 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Apply 10% safety margin:**

$$M_{design} = 1.10 \times 68 = 74.8 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Number of 45 kg cylinders:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{74.8}{45} \right\rceil = 2 \;\text{cylinders\,(90\,kg\,capacity)}$$

**Answer:** 2 cylinders of 45 kg each (total 90 kg, providing 15.2 kg spare).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget the 10% safety margin and quote "2 cylinders" anyway. The correct number is **2** here, but the calculation must explicitly show the 1.10 multiplier, or full 7 marks are not awarded. **Also note:** The factor 34/66 comes from $c_t / (100 - c_t)$ — students who write $34 \times 120 / 100$ lose 2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Fire Triangle** and discuss how each extinguishing medium (water, foam, CO₂, dry powder) breaks one or more sides of the triangle.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 20 m fishing trawler has a fire main of diameter 65 mm and total equivalent length 80 m. A single fire pump must deliver 2.5 L/s to a nozzle 6 m above the pump through 12 m of 40 mm hose. The nozzle pressure must be at least 0.25 MPa. Taking friction factor $f = 0.02$ for the main and $f = 0.025$ for the hose, and assuming sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³), determine the **required fire pump discharge pressure** in MPa.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Refer to Section 3.3 Comparison Matrix above for the breakdown table.

> Detailed narrative:
> 
> - **Water** (spray/mist) — **Removes Heat** by converting sensible heat to latent heat of vaporisation (2.26 MJ/kg). Used against Class A.
> - **Foam (AFFF)** — **Smothers Fuel** by forming an aqueous film over the flammable liquid. Cuts off oxygen and prevents re-ignition. Best for Class B.
> - **CO₂** — **Displaces Oxygen** from the protected space. The concentration is raised above 34%, lowering O₂ to below 14%, which is insufficient for hydrocarbon combustion. Best for Class B, C, electrical.
> - **Dry Powder (ABC)** — **Breaks the Chemical Chain Reaction** of the flame by interfering with free radicals (H⁺, OH⁻). Multi-purpose but no cooling.
> 
> Hence each agent attacks at least one of {Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, Chain Reaction} of the **Fire Tetrahedron** (the modern extension of the Fire Triangle).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Refer to Section 3.1, Derivation 1 above for the full 8-step computation.

**Recap of the final answer (from Derivation 1):**

$$H_{pump} = 64.65 \;\text{m} \implies P_{pump} \approx 0.65 \;\text{MPa} \;(\approx 6.5 \;\text{bar})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (2-mark loss):** Many students forget the **velocity head at the nozzle** $v^2/2g$, which contributes ≈ 25 m (nearly equal to the pressure head). Without it, the answer drops to ≈ 0.40 MPa and is rejected by examiners. **Always include kinetic energy of the jet** in Bernoulli's equation for fire pump problems.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Frequency Mark Loss Zones (based on 2020–2024 KTU answer scripts):**
> 1. Confusing **A-Class (60 min)** with **B-Class (30 min)** — loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting **10% safety margin** in CO₂ calculation — loses 1 mark.
> 3. Not mentioning the **30-second time delay** in CO₂ flooding — loses 1 mark.
> 4. Using water jet for **diesel/bilge** fire in answer — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. Forgetting **velocity head** in fire pump head calculation — loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. Writing **SCBA endurance = 60 min** (it is 30 min for standard 6 L @ 200 bar) — loses 1 mark.
> 7. Citing **"SOLAS Chapter III"** for fire (correct is **Chapter II-2** + **FSS Code**) — loses 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firefighting on Fishing Vessels — One-Page Revision Sheet**
> 
> - **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Heat + Oxygen. **Fire Tetrahedron** adds Chain Reaction.
> - **Five Classes:** A (solid), B (liquid), C (gas), D (metal), K (cooking oil).
> - **SOLAS Chapter II-2 + FSS Code** govern all marine fire safety; **Torremolinos 1993 Protocol** for fishing vessels specifically.
> - **A-Class Division** = 60 min fire test, ≤ 140 °C avg, ≤ 180 °C point; **B-Class** = 30 min.
> - **Steel softens at ≈ 550 °C** — basis of A-Class design temperature.
> - **CO₂ total flooding** needs **34% min** concentration, **30 s delay**, **20 min hold**.
> - **CO₂ mass** = $\alpha \cdot V \cdot c_t / (100 - c_t)$, with **10% safety margin**, using **45 kg cylinders** typically.
> - **Fire pump flow** ≥ 2 × max hydrant flow; for small trawlers **≥ 15 m³/hr** is typical.
> - **Nozzle pressure** ≥ **0.25 MPa (2.5 bar)**.
> - **Pump head** = nozzle pressure head + velocity head + friction loss + elevation.
> - **Always include $v^2/2g$** of the jet in Bernoulli's equation.
> - **SCBA** endurance = **30 min** (6 L cylinder @ 200–300 bar).
> - **Fireman's outfit** includes helmet, boots, gloves, axe, lifeline, **SCBA**, and a waterproof torch.
> - **International Shore Connection** is a **standard 150 mm flange** for shore fire water.
> - **Detection types:** Smoke (ionisation/photoelectric), Heat (fixed temp 57 °C or rate-of-rise), Flame (IR/UV), Manual call point.
> - **Common systems on trawlers:** Water spray, Foam (AFFF), CO₂, Dry powder, Halon-replacements.
> - **Engine room** accounts for **≈ 45%** of all vessel fires → highest priority zone.
> - **Diesel** is Class B → **never use direct water jet** (causes splashing and spread).
> - **LPG / acetylene** is Class C → shut source first, then CO₂ or dry powder.
> - **Foam expansion ratio:** Low (6:1), Medium (60:1), High (1000:1) — used for different scenarios.
> - **Fire drill** must be conducted **weekly**; **abandon ship drill** monthly per SOLAS.

> **End of KTU-Premier Notes — Module 3: Firefighting**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifesaving and Net Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lifesaving and Net Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lifesaving Appliances – Formal Definition
**Lifesaving appliances** are the complete suite of personal, collective, and signaling devices mandated aboard a fishing vessel to ensure the survival, location, and recovery of crew members during emergencies such as man-overboard (MOB), vessel sinking, fire, collision, or abandonment of ship. The design, carriage quantity, and performance standards are governed by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** Chapter III, the **International Life-Saving Appliance (LSA) Code**, and the **Torremolinos International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels** (as adapted by flag states and FAO/ILO/IMO).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For fishing vessels, the Torremolinos Protocol (1977) and its 1993 amendments form the primary regulatory spine, supplemented by the FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety for Fishermen and Fishing Vessels (1995), Part B – Voluntary Guidelines.

### 1.2 Net Monitoring – Formal Definition
**Net monitoring** refers to the integrated suite of hydroacoustic, electromechanical, and telemetry-based sensors deployed on or near the fishing gear (trawl, purse seine, gillnet, longline) to continuously relay real-time information about the net's geometry, depth, water-column position, spread, symmetry, opening height, bottom contact, and catch volume back to the wheelhouse displays, allowing the skipper to optimize gear efficiency and prevent gear damage or loss.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
**Analogy for Lifesaving – The Parachute Analogy:**
Think of a fishing vessel as an aircraft flying low over a turbulent ocean. The **lifesaving appliances are the parachutes, oxygen masks, and locator beacons** that ensure the "flight crew" survives if anything goes wrong. The lifejacket is your personal parachute; the liferaft is the emergency landing pad; the EPIRB is the satellite homing device that tells rescuers exactly where you landed.

**Analogy for Net Monitoring – The Reversing Camera Analogy:**
Net monitoring is to a trawler what a reversing camera and parking sensors are to a modern car. The skipper cannot "see" the net through the murky ocean, just as a driver cannot see through the rear of an SUV. Sonde sensors, door-spread sensors, and symmetry sensors act as the "eyes" — translating invisible underwater geometry into visible wheelhouse screen data, so the skipper can "park" the gear perfectly on fish schools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IMO standard symbols used on **safety placards, lifebuoy markings, and emergency muster lists** are **white-on-green** (survival craft, lifejackets) and **white-on-red** (fire appliances). Always use the standard **ISO 7010 / IMO A.760(18) symbols** in design drawings.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Constant / Standard | Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| SOLAS LSA Code Chapter II / III | — | Standard for lifejackets & liferafts |
| Lifejacket buoyancy | **≥ 150 N** (adult) | Minimum SOLAS requirement |
| Lifebuoy buoyancy | **≥ 14.5 N** when in fresh water | Marked with vessel name & port |
| EPIRB frequency | **406 MHz** | COSPAS-SARSAT global satellite system |
| EPIRB battery life | **≥ 48 hours** transmitting | At −20 °C operating temp |
| SART radar frequency | **9 GHz (X-band)** | 9.3–9.5 GHz sweep response |
| Hydroacoustic net sonde ping rate | **50–200 kHz** | Depth & catch telemetry |
| Drop-out safety factor for lifejackets | **≥ 2.0× design load** | EN ISO 12402 / SOLAS |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Lifesaving Appliances
Lifesaving appliances are bifurcated into **personal** and **collective** categories. The design logic behind each is rooted in three survival priorities, in order: **(1) Stay afloat, (2) Stay warm, (3) Be found.**

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**
- **Personal appliances** keep the individual from drowning and hypothermia.
  - **Lifejackets (PFDs):** Inflatable or inherently buoyant foam (PVC/EPE). Must self-right an unconscious wearer face-up in **< 5 seconds** (SOLAS).
  - **Immersion suits:** Reduce convective heat loss; maintain body core temp in cold seas for **≥ 6 hours** at **5 °C water**.
  - **Safety harnesses & lifelines:** Restrict crew movement in heavy weather.
- **Collective appliances** rescue multiple persons simultaneously.
  - **Liferafts (inflatable, SOLAS A/B packs):** Inflated by CO₂/N₂ bottles; boarding ramp; insulated floor; rainwater collection.
  - **Lifeboats:** Open or enclosed (free-fall capable on vessels > 85 m, though rare for fishing).
  - **Rescue boats:** Rigid/fast-recovery for MOB retrieval.
  - **Buoyant apparatus:** Float-free canisters.
- **Locating & signaling appliances** ensure rescue services find the survivors.
  - **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon):** Floats free, transmits to satellite.
  - **SART (Search and Rescue Transponder):** Active radar reflector responding to X-band radars.
  - **Pyrotechnics:** Hand flares (red, parachute), smoke canisters, line-throwing rockets (**range ≥ 230 m** at 4–8 m height).
  - **VHF DSC (Digital Selective Calling):** Channel 70 distress alerting.
- **Survival accessories:** Thermal protective aids, food rations, water, first-aid kits, fishing kits, sea anchor.

### 2.2 Classification of Net Monitoring Systems
- **Single-function sondes:** Measure only depth (pressure-based) or temperature.
- **Multi-function trawl monitors:** Combine depth, height, geometry, doorspread, symmetry, and catch volume in one wireless unit.
- **Sonar-mounted net sounders:** Echo-sounding transducers mounted on the headline.
- **Catch indicators (PI sensors):** Measure fill level, typically via load-cell or optical/ultrasonic sensor on the codend.
- **Door-spread sensors:** Trawl doors (otter boards) — measure distance between port/starboard doors using hydroacoustic pulses through water.
- **Symmetry sensors:** Measure headline tilt angle.
- **Catch sensors:** Hydroacoustic "fish-count" sensors.
- **Wireless surface units:** Receivers on the hull translate signals into wheelhouse displays (e.g., Scanmar, Marport, Simrad ITI).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lifejacket buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | N (where $\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³) |
| 2 | Safe wearer weight | $m_{w} \le F_b / (g \cdot \text{SF})$ | kg (SF ≥ 2.0) |
| 3 | Liferaft packing capacity | $N_{raft} = \lceil N_{crew} / n_{raft} \rceil$ | persons |
| 4 | EPIRB freeboard stability | $h_{f} \ge 0$ (positively buoyant) | m |
| 5 | Hydrostatic pressure at net depth | $P = P_{atm} + \rho \, g \, z$ | Pa |
| 6 | Sonde depth resolution | $\Delta z = \frac{c}{2 f_s}$ | m (c = 1500 m/s sound in seawater) |
| 7 | Acoustic two-way travel time | $t = \frac{2d}{c}$ | s |
| 8 | Doorspread from acoustic round-trip | $D_{spread} = \frac{c \, t_{door}}{2}$ | m |
| 9 | Trawl wire scope ratio | $\text{Scope} = \frac{L_{wire}}{D}$ | dimensionless (typically 2.5–4.0) |
| 10 | Symmetry tilt (headline angle) | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Delta h}{L_{head}}\right)$ | degrees |
| 11 | SART visibility range | $R_{SART} \approx 8\text{–}15\,$ nm | nautical miles |
| 12 | Survival time in cold water (rule of 50/50) | $T_{50/50} \approx 15 - T_{water}$ (rough) | minutes |
| 13 | Trawl headline height estimation (sim.) | $H = f(D, V_{tow}, W_{head})$ | empirical |
| 14 | Signal attenuation in seawater | $A(d,f) = 0.008 + 0.008\!\cdot\!d + 0.00008\!\cdot\!f^{1.5}$ | dB/m, kHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely applied by naval architects during stability booklets (Free Surface Effect for lifejacket storage), by master fishers for gear-tuning, and by IMO auditors for SOLAS certification of fishing vessels.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Naval architecture:** Determining lifejacket lockers' weight/center-of-gravity impact on intact stability.
- **Fishing efficiency:** Net monitoring reduces **bycatch**, **fuel consumption**, and **gear loss** (industry reports indicate up to **20–30 % fuel saving** with optimal trawl geometry).
- **Maritime safety law:** Compliance with the FAO/ILO/IMO Code Part B and Torremolinos Protocol.
- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Cospas-Sarsat system saves ~3 lives daily worldwide, with EPIRBs being the primary alert device.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Liferafts Required

A fishing vessel of **24 m LOA** with a crew complement of **18 persons** is to be certified under SOLAS / Torremolinos rules. Each inflatable liferaft has a rated capacity of **10 persons**.

**Given:**
- $N_{crew} = 18$
- $n_{raft} = 10$ (capacity per raft)

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**
The total required raft capacity must equal or exceed the crew complement.

$$
N_{raft} \times n_{raft} \ge N_{crew}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
N_{raft} \times 10 \ge 18
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the minimum count:**

$$
N_{raft} \ge \frac{N_{crew}}{n_{raft}} = \frac{18}{10} = 1.8
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ceiling function (whole rafts only):**

$$
N_{raft} = \lceil 1.8 \rceil = 2
$$

**Step 5 — Validate SOLAS redundancy rule:**
SOLAS requires that the total capacity of liferafts on **each side** of the vessel be sufficient to accommodate the entire crew (for vessels where liferafts are stowed in pairs port/starboard).

$$
\text{Each side: } \tfrac{18}{2} = 9 \text{ persons} \Rightarrow 1 \times 10\text{-raft per side suffices.}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Minimum **2 liferafts (10-person each)**, one stowed port and one stbd, with hydrostatic release units (HRU) set to release at **≤ 4 m depth** — standard SOLAS configuration.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydroacoustic Net Depth from Sonde

A net sonde is mounted on a trawl headline at depth $z$. The surface vessel's transducer emits an acoustic pulse and receives the echo in **$t = 0.080$ s**. Compute the depth.

**Given:**
- Speed of sound in seawater, $c = 1500$ m/s (standard KTU value)
- Two-way travel time, $t = 0.080$ s

**Step 1 — Write the two-way travel equation:**

$$
t = \frac{2 z}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for depth:**

$$
z = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
z = \frac{1500 \, \text{m/s} \times 0.080 \, \text{s}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
1500 \times 0.080 = 120 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 2:**

$$
z = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \, \text{m}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The headline is at **60 m below the surface**. Cross-check with hydrostatic formula $P = P_{atm} + \rho g z$ for consistency.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Door Spread from Acoustic Pulse

Two trawl door sensors (port & starboard) exchange acoustic pulses. The round-trip travel time is **$t_{door} = 0.012$ s**. Compute the door spread.

**Given:**
- $c = 1500$ m/s
- $t_{door} = 0.012$ s

**Step 1 — Geometry of acoustic ranging:**

The pulse travels from port door to starboard door and back. The acoustic path length is therefore $2 \times D_{spread}$ — but in real systems it is the chord distance; for symmetrical gear (low tilt) we approximate chord ≈ spread.

**Step 2 — Apply two-way travel formula:**

$$
t_{door} = \frac{2 D_{spread}}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D_{spread}$:**

$$
D_{spread} = \frac{c \cdot t_{door}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
D_{spread} = \frac{1500 \times 0.012}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$
D_{spread} = \frac{18}{2} = 9 \, \text{m}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The trawl doors are spread **9 m apart** — within the optimal range of **8–12 m** for typical demersal trawls of this size, indicating correct warp length and door spread.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Which Net Sensor

| Parameter to Monitor | Recommended Sensor | Typical Brand | Mounting Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net depth | Depth/temperature sonde | Scanmar PI | Trawl headline |
| Doorspread | Door sensor (acoustic) | Scanmar DD | Inner face of each door |
| Symmetry | Symmetry sensor | Marport M3 | Headline (centered) |
| Opening height | Height sensor (pressure) | Simrad PI50 | Top of headline |
| Bottom contact | Bottom contact sensor | Scanmar BC | Ground gear |
| Catch volume | Catch sensor (load) | Marport Catch | Codend |
| Geometry (3D) | Spread + Height + Symmetry combo | Simrad ITI/TrawlEye | Distributed |
| Acoustic net sounder | 200 kHz transducer | WESMAR TC | Headline |
