**Steel construction** in a fishing vessel refers to the engineered assembly of rolled steel plates, sections, and profiles, joined principally by **arc welding**, to form a watertight, structurally redundant, and load-bearing hull. It encompasses the selection of grades, the calculation of **scantlings** (plate thicknesses, section moduli of stiffeners, web and flange dimensions), the layout of primary, secondary, and tertiary members, the detailing of brackets and end connections, and the fabrication sequence governed by classification society rules (e.g., **IRS, DNV, LR, ABS**) and **IACS Unified Requirements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to recall **rule-based scantling formulas** and the **hierarchy of structural members** for a fishing vessel up to ~24 m LOA. Memorizing IACS coefficient tables is the high-yield area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ship as a Skeleton

Think of a steel fishing vessel as a **human body**:
- The **shell plating** is the **skin** that keeps water out.
- The **transverse frames and web frames** are the **ribs** that give shape and resist crushing loads.
- The **keel, bottom plating, and deck plating** form the **spine**, carrying the global **hull-girder bending moment**.
- The **longitudinals and stringers** are the **muscle fibers** that distribute local loads.
- The **brackets and gussets** are the **tendons and ligaments** that tie everything together so forces transfer smoothly.

Without the skeleton, the body collapses. Without the steel frame system, the shell plating would buckle under wave slam and hydrostatic pressure.

## 1.3 Why Steel is Chosen for Fishing Vessels

- **High strength-to-weight ratio** compared with wood or FRP for vessels > 18 m.
- **Ductility** — absorbs impact from grounding, collision, and wave slam without brittle fracture.
- **Weldability** — permits modular prefabrication and rapid repair in coastal shipyards.
- **Predictable fatigue behaviour** under cyclic wave loading.
- **Availability of standardized rolled sections** (L-angles, T-bars, flat bars, bulb plates, HP-sections).

> [!NOTE]
> Standard steel grades per **IACS UR W11 / IS 2062**:
> - **Grade A** — Mild steel, yield strength $f_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Grade B / D / E** — Normal / higher strength, $f_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Grade AH32 / DH32 / EH32** — High tensile, $f_y = 315 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Grade AH36 / DH36 / EH36** — High tensile, $f_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$

## 1.4 Corrosion Allowance

Steel corrodes in seawater at approximately **0.1 mm/year on the underwater hull** and **0.05 mm/year above waterline**. Classification rules therefore prescribe an **additive corrosion allowance $t_k$**:

$$t_k = \begin{cases} 0.5 \text{ mm to } 1.0 \text{ mm} & \text{(boot-top and topside)} \\ 1.0 \text{ mm to } 2.0 \text{ mm} & \text{(bottom and side shell)} \\ 1.5 \text{ mm to } 3.0 \text{ mm} & \text{(bilge, keel, rudder horn)} \end{cases}$$

This $t_k$ is **added** to the rule thickness during new construction and must be **renewed** during every drydock.

## 1.5 Hierarchy of Structural Members (Quick Map)

| Level | Member | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | Keel plate, sheer strake, deck stringer plate | Carry hull-girder bending |
| Secondary | Transverse frames, web frames, side girders, deck transverses | Maintain hull form, support shell |
| Tertiary | Longitudinals, floors, brackets, stiffeners on webs | Distribute local pressure |
| Local | Brackets, collars, lugs, doubler pads | Stress reduction at intersections |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Types of Construction Systems

There are **three principal framing systems** used in fishing vessels:

**(a) Transverse Framing System**
- Frames spaced at typically **s = 500 mm to 700 mm**.
- Frames supported by **side girders** and **web frames** every 2–4 frame spaces.
- Used in the **bottom, side shell, and lower deck** of most small/medium fishing vessels.
- Frames are **continuous** (e.g., L 75×50×6 or T 100×50) welded to the shell.

**(b) Longitudinal Framing System**
- Longitudinals spaced at **s = 300 mm to 500 mm** on the shell.
- Supported by **transverse web frames** every 2.5 m to 3.0 m.
- Reduces plate thickness requirement, used in larger fishing vessels > 30 m.
- Higher labour cost in fabrication.

**(c) Mixed Framing**
- Longitudinally framed bottom and sheer strake, transversely framed topside.
- Common in modern fishing trawlers.

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical 18–24 m fishing trawler, the **transverse system** dominates because of ease of construction in small Kerala coast shipyards (Cochin, Beypore, Mangalore cluster).

## 2.2 Scantling Philosophy — Why "Rule Formulas"?

Classification societies do not permit a designer to do a full **FEA** for every plate and stiffener. Instead, they publish **semi-empirical rule formulas** derived from:
- Beam-on-elastic-foundation (BoEF) plate analysis
- 25-year wave-loading statistics (IACS S21 environment)
- Cumulative fatigue damage (Palmgren–Miner)
- Service experience with sister vessels

A KTU student must memorize the **structure** of these formulas, not raw numbers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below use SI units unless noted. Length $L$ is in **metres**, spacing $s$ in **mm**, thickness $t$ in **mm**, section modulus in **cm³**.

| # | Quantity | Rule Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shell plate thickness | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{230} + t_k$ | $k$ = material factor ($\sqrt{235 / f_y}$); $L$ = rule length |
| 2 | Deck plate thickness | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{200} + t_k$ | Higher factor due to slamming |
| 3 | Bottom plate thickness | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{250} + t_k$ | Lower factor; hydrostatic pressure |
| 4 | Section modulus of frame | $SM = c \cdot s \cdot l^2 \cdot k \;\; [\text{cm}^3]$ | $c$ = coefficient, $l$ = span |
| 5 | Web thickness of frame | $t_w = 0.015 \cdot l \cdot \sqrt{k} + 1.5$ | mm |
| 6 | Flange breadth of frame | $b_f = 0.10 \cdot l \cdot k$ | mm |
| 7 | Bracket minimum thickness | $t_b = 0.75 \cdot t_w$ | mm |
| 8 | Pillar / stanchion area | $A = \dfrac{W \cdot s_f}{\sigma_y}$ | $W$ = load, $s_f$ = safety factor (1.5) |
| 9 | Hull-girder SM | $SM_{min} = f \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7)$ | $f$ = 0.005 for mild steel |
| 10 | Plate bending stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2}$ | $p$ = pressure, $s$ = spacing |

### Material Factor $k$

$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{f_y}}$$

- For Grade A: $k = 1.000$
- For AH32: $k = 0.864$
- For AH36: $k = 0.814$

### Frame Span $l$ and Spacing $s$

- $s$ = frame spacing (transverse = 600 mm; longitudinal = 400 mm typical).
- $l$ = unsupported span of frame between supports (girders / web frames).

### Why the $\sqrt{L}$ in Plate Thickness?

The wave-induced hydrostatic head $p$ scales roughly as $L$ (longer vessels meet larger waves). Plate bending stiffness $D \propto t^3$. Setting $D \cdot \kappa = p$ and noting curvature $\kappa \propto 1 / s^2$ gives:

$$t \propto \sqrt{p} \cdot s \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \propto \sqrt{L} \cdot s$$

This is the deep reason behind the empirical formula.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scantling decisions** dictate **steel weight**, which decides **deadweight**, **fuel economy**, and **stability**.
- The **frame system choice** affects **fish hold capacity** (longitudinals intrude less into the hold than webs).
- A wrongly chosen $t_k$ leads to **premature wastage** — a primary cause of fishing-vessel casualties in tropical waters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Plate Bending Under Lateral Pressure

Consider a rectangular plate of dimensions $s \times s$ (square panel between stiffeners), simply supported on all four edges, subjected to uniform lateral pressure $p$. The maximum bending stress in the centre is given by classical plate theory:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{C \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2}$$

where $C$ is a coefficient depending on the edge-fixity factor. For simply supported plate, $C = 0.5$. For a clamped plate, $C = 0.308$.

The **derivation** proceeds as follows.

**Step 1**: Plate flexural rigidity

$$D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}$$

**Step 2**: Governing differential equation for a laterally loaded plate

$$D \cdot \nabla^4 w = p$$

where $w(x,y)$ is the transverse deflection.

**Step 3**: Navier solution — assume double Fourier series for $w$ satisfying simply supported edges:

$$w = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} w_{mn} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 4**: For a square panel $a = b = s$ under uniform pressure, the dominant term is $m = n = 1$, giving maximum deflection:

$$w_{max} = \dfrac{4 p s^4}{\pi^4 D} \cdot (0.00406) \approx \dfrac{0.00406 \cdot p s^4}{D}$$

**Step 5**: Bending moments at mid-span:

$$M_x = -D \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)$$

**Step 6**: Maximum fibre stress

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 M_{max}}{t^2} = \dfrac{6 \cdot 0.0479 \cdot p s^2}{t^2} \approx \dfrac{0.5 \, p \, s^2}{t^2}$$

> This is the origin of the textbook formula $\sigma = 0.5 \, p \, s^2 / t^2$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Shell Plate Scantling (KTU Pattern)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A 22 m fishing trawler of Grade A steel is to be designed. The transverse frame spacing in the side shell is 600 mm. Calculate the required as-built shell plate thickness. Take corrosion allowance $t_k = 1.0$ mm, rule length $L = 22$ m, $k = 1.0$.

**Solution — Exhaustive Steps:**

**Step 1:** Identify the rule formula from the KTU formula sheet for side shell:

$$t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{230} + t_k$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values — convert spacing to mm explicitly.

$$s = 600 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 22 \text{ m}, \quad k = 1.0, \quad t_k = 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the square root term.

$$\sqrt{L} = \sqrt{22} = 4.6904$$

**Step 4:** Compute the rule thickness component.

$$t_{rule} = \dfrac{600 \times 1.0 \times 4.6904}{230} = \dfrac{2814.24}{230} = 12.236 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Add corrosion allowance.

$$t = 12.236 + 1.0 = 13.236 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Round up to next standard plate thickness (IS 2062 plates come in 1 mm increments for $t < 20$ mm).

$$t_{adopted} = 14 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification using bending-stress check** with sea pressure $p = 25$ kN/m² (typical for mid-side):

$$\sigma = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 600^2}{14^2} = \dfrac{4500}{196} = 22.96 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This is well below allowable $0.6 f_y = 141$ MPa, hence **safe**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Construction Systems

| Parameter | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing | Mixed Framing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frame spacing $s$ | 500–700 mm | 300–500 mm | Variable |
| Plate thickness | Higher | Lower (~15% saving) | Optimized |
| Web frames spacing | 2–4 frame spaces | 2.5–3.0 m | Selected |
| Fabrication cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Inspection access | Easy | Hard in deep tanks | Moderate |
| Best for LOA | < 25 m | > 30 m | 18–35 m |
| Typical fish hold loss | Small | Negligible | Small |

## 3.4 Bracket Sizing — Worked Steps

A web frame has web thickness $t_w = 6$ mm. Calculate the minimum bracket thickness at the frame-stiffener connection.

**Step 1:** Use the KTU rule from formula sheet (Entry 7):

$$t_b = 0.75 \cdot t_w$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$t_b = 0.75 \times 6 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Round up to next standard:

$$t_{b,adopted} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Verify arm length — bracket arm $a$ should satisfy $a \geq 1.5 \cdot t_b$ projection. For $t_b = 5$ mm, $a \geq 7.5$ mm. Practically take $a = 50$ mm to 75 mm for stiffener connection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slenderness check** — bracket arm should not exceed 60 times its thickness in compression to prevent tripping failure: $a / t_b \leq 60$.

## 3.5 Stiffener Buckling — Analytical Derivation

For a stiffener subjected to in-plane compression $\sigma_c$ (e.g., longitudinal in way of hull bending), Euler-type column buckling governs:

$$\sigma_c \leq \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(l / r)^2} \quad \text{where} \quad r = \sqrt{I / A}$$

Converting to a **rule-based slenderness limit**:

$$\dfrac{l}{r} \leq \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{0.6 \, f_y}}$$

For Grade A, $E = 210$ GPa, $f_y = 235$ MPa:

$$\dfrac{l}{r} \leq \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 210000}{0.6 \times 235}} = \sqrt{14678} \approx 121$$

This is why **frame depth-to-thickness** ratios are capped at $d / t_w \leq 50$ to 60 in typical fishing-vessel rules.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hull Frame System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the load-transfer hierarchy in a typical fishing trawler with **transverse framing**. Each block represents a structural group; arrows indicate the direction in which loads (pressure, slamming, hull-girder bending) travel toward the keel — the ultimate "ground" of the floating system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ENVIRONMENTAL LOADS"] --> B["WAVE SLAMMING DECK"]
    A --> C["HYDROSTATIC SIDE PRESSURE"]
    A --> D["WAVE LOAD ON BOTTOM"]

    B --> E["DECK PLATING"]
    C --> F["SIDE SHELL PLATING"]
    D --> G["BOTTOM PLATING"]

    E --> H["DECK TRANSVERSE FRAMES"]
    F --> I["SIDE TRANSVERSE FRAMES"]
    G --> J["BOTTOM TRANSVERSE FRAMES"]

    H --> K["SIDE GIRDERS AND DECK GIRDERS"]
    I --> K
    J --> L["FLOORS AND CENTERLINE KEEL"]

    K --> M["WEB FRAMES AND TRANSVERSE BULKHEADS"]
    L --> M

    M --> N["HULL GIRDER GLOBAL BENDING MEMBER"]
    N --> O["KEEL PLATE AND SHEER STRAKE"]

    O --> P["SUPPORTS FISH HOLD AND SUPERSTRUCTURE LOADS"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Scantling Calculation Workflow

The diagram below traces the **step-by-step rule-based design path** that a naval architect follows for shell plating — the workflow a KTU student must reproduce in the answer paper.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: FIX RULE LENGTH L"] --> S2["STEP 2: CHOOSE FRAMING SYSTEM"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: SELECT FRAME SPACING s"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: PICK STEEL GRADE AND FACTOR k"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: COMPUTE RULE THICKNESS"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: ADD CORROSION ALLOWANCE t_k"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: ROUND UP TO STANDARD PLATE"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8: VERIFY BENDING STRESS"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9: VERIFY BUCKLING SLENDERNESS"]
    S9 --> S10["ADOPT THICKNESS OR ITERATE"]

    style S1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style S5 fill:#ffd966,color:#000000
    style S10 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Bracket and Stiffener Connection — Detail Architecture

The figure is rendered as a **block architecture** rather than a freehand weld symbol, because the connection is more easily understood as a load-flow node.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["SHELL PLATE PANEL"] --> Q2["PLATE BENDS UNDER PRESSURE p"]
    Q2 --> Q3["LOAD TRANSFERRED TO STIFFENER EDGE"]
    Q3 --> Q4["STIFFENER ACTS AS BEAM ON ELASTIC FOUNDATION"]
    Q4 --> Q5["STIFFENER SPAN SUPPORTS BRACKET END"]
    Q5 --> Q6["BRACKET TRANSFERS SHEAR TO PARENT FRAME"]
    Q6 --> Q7["PARENT WEB FRAME CARRIES LOAD TO FLOORS"]
    Q7 --> Q8["FLOORS DELIVER LOAD TO KEEL PLATE"]
    Q8 --> Q9["KEEL PLATE ACTS AS PRIMARY HULL GIRDER FLANGE"]

    style Q1 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
    style Q4 fill:#ffe699,color:#000000
    style Q6 fill:#f4b183,color:#000000
    style Q9 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **mid-ship region** (0.4 L amidships) the bending moment peaks. Rule formulas automatically scale plate thickness upward here, and **double bottom / hopper tanks** may be introduced. For 22 m fishing trawlers, a **single bottom with floors** is typical.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "scantlings" as applied to steel ship construction. List the three main types of framing systems used in fishing vessels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Scantlings refer to the **sizes and dimensions** of all structural members of a ship — plate thicknesses, web and flange dimensions of stiffeners, girders, web frames, and pillars — as derived from classification society rule formulas or direct strength calculations. Scantlings govern the structural strength, stiffness, and weight of the vessel.

**Three framing systems (1 mark):**
1. **Transverse framing system** — frames spaced 500–700 mm perpendicular to the centreline.
2. **Longitudinal framing system** — longitudinals spaced 300–500 mm parallel to the centreline.
3. **Mixed framing system** — combination of both, used for optimum scantlings.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**What is corrosion allowance? State typical values for (a) bottom shell, (b) side shell, and (c) topside plating of a fishing vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Corrosion allowance $t_k$ is the **extra thickness** added to the rule-derived plate thickness during new construction to compensate for the gradual wastage of steel due to corrosion during the vessel's service life. It ensures that the **renewal thickness** at the next dry-dock still meets the rule minimum.

**Typical values (2 marks):**
- (a) **Bottom shell plating**: $t_k = 1.5$ to $2.0$ mm
- (b) **Side shell plating**: $t_k = 1.0$ to $1.5$ mm
- (c) **Topside / deck plating**: $t_k = 0.5$ to $1.0$ mm

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **transverse framing system** of a fishing vessel with a neat sketch. State the typical range of frame spacing, web frame spacing, and the role of side girders.

**(b) (7 marks)** For a 20 m LOA fishing trawler built of Grade A mild steel, the side shell has transverse frames at 600 mm spacing. The mid-span between two side girders is 2.4 m. Calculate (i) the rule plate thickness, and (ii) the section modulus required for the frames. Adopt $k = 1.0$, $t_k = 1.0$ mm, and rule coefficient for side shell $c = 0.045$ for the frame formula $SM = c \cdot s \cdot l^2 \cdot k$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Transverse Framing System (7 marks):**

In the transverse framing system, the **primary load-bearing members** are the **transverse frames** (T-bars, L-angles, or HP-sections) that run **vertically across the hull cross-section**. These frames are spaced at regular intervals of typically **500 mm to 700 mm** along the length of the vessel.

- **Frame spacing $s$**: 500–700 mm, chosen as a sub-multiple of the rule length.
- **Web frame spacing**: every 2 to 4 frame spaces (1.2 m to 2.8 m), provide intermediate support to the frames.
- **Side girders**: horizontal members at the level of the **stringer plate** (sheer strake) and at the **turn of the bilge**, support the ends of the transverse frames and distribute the side pressure load to the **web frames** and **transverse bulkheads**.
- **Floors**: vertical plates at the centreline, fitted at every frame, close the bottom of the frame ring and transmit the load to the keel.

The arrangement creates a **ring of steel** every 600 mm that maintains the hull form and resists local pressure.

**Part (b) — Scantling Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Rule plate thickness of side shell (3.5 marks):**

Using the KTU formula for side shell:

$$t = \dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}}{230} + t_k$$

Substituting $s = 600$ mm, $k = 1.0$, $L = 20$ m, $t_k = 1.0$ mm:

$$t = \dfrac{600 \times 1.0 \times \sqrt{20}}{230} + 1.0$$

$$t = \dfrac{600 \times 4.4721}{230} + 1.0 = \dfrac{2683.3}{230} + 1.0 = 11.666 + 1.0 = 12.67 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **$t = 13$ mm** (next standard).

**(ii) Required section modulus of frame (3.5 marks):**

Using the rule formula for section modulus:

$$SM = c \cdot s \cdot l^2 \cdot k$$

Substituting $c = 0.045$, $s = 600$ mm (note: in some rulebooks, $s$ is in metres for this formula; we use 0.6 m), $l = 2.4$ m, $k = 1.0$:

$$SM = 0.045 \times 0.6 \times (2.4)^2 \times 1.0 = 0.045 \times 0.6 \times 5.76 = 0.1555 \text{ m}^3$$

Converting to cm³: $SM = 155.5$ cm³.

**Recommended standard section:** T-bar **T 200 × 10 / 100 × 6** with $SM \approx 165$ cm³, web thickness 6 mm, flange 100 × 6 mm.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

**(a) (7 marks)** Discuss the materials and grades of steel used in fishing vessel construction. Explain why Grade A is the most common, and the role of high-tensile grades such as AH32.

**(b) (7 marks)** A fishing vessel has a side shell frame of span $l = 2.0$ m supported at both ends. The frame is to be built of Grade A steel. Calculate (i) the required web thickness, and (ii) the required flange breadth. Use the KTU formulas:

$$t_w = 0.015 \cdot l \cdot \sqrt{k} + 1.5, \quad b_f = 0.10 \cdot l \cdot k$$

Adopt $k = 1.0$. Also verify the slenderness limit $d / t_w \leq 50$, taking frame depth $d = 150$ mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Steel Materials and Grades (7 marks):**

Fishing vessels are built from **rolled carbon-manganese steel** conforming to **IS 2062** (India) or **IACS UR W11** (international). The principal grades are:

1. **Grade A** — Ordinary strength, $f_y = 235$ MPa.
   - **Most common** because of low cost, excellent weldability with ordinary electrodes, good ductility, and adequate strength for vessels up to 30 m LOA.
   - Does not require pre-heating for plates up to 20 mm thickness.

2. **Grade B / D / E** — Normal strength with improved notch toughness for low-temperature service. D and E are killed and fine-grain treated.

3. **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** — High-tensile, $f_y = 315$ MPa.
   - Used to **reduce scantlings and steel weight** in larger vessels, increasing deadweight capacity.
   - Allows lighter scantlings at the **amidships** where bending stresses are maximum.
   - Requires controlled welding (low-hydrogen electrodes, pre-heating for thick plates).

4. **AH36 / DH36 / EH36** — High-tensile, $f_y = 355$ MPa.
   - Used in large fishing vessels and trawlers above 30 m.

The **material factor $k$** accounts for the higher yield strength. For Grade A, $k = 1.0$; for AH32, $k = 0.864$, which directly **reduces plate and frame scantlings** by 13.6%.

**Part (b) — Web and Flange Dimensions (7 marks):**

**(i) Web thickness (3.5 marks):**

$$t_w = 0.015 \cdot l \cdot \sqrt{k} + 1.5$$

With $l = 2.0$ m, $k = 1.0$:

$$t_w = 0.015 \times 2000 \times 1.0 + 1.5 = 30 + 1.5 = 31.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Wait — correction by length unit check:** The formula is calibrated for $l$ in **mm in some class rulebooks, m in others**. In the KTU formula sheet the $l$ is the **span in metres**, but $t_w$ comes out in **mm** because the coefficient 0.015 already absorbs the unit conversion. For $l = 2.0$ m:

$$t_w = 0.015 \times 2.0 \times 1.0 + 1.5 = 0.03 + 1.5 = 1.53 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **$t_w = 6$ mm** (next standard for welded T-section). Practically, $t_w$ is never less than 5 mm to avoid burn-through during welding.

**(ii) Flange breadth (3.5 marks):**

$$b_f = 0.10 \cdot l \cdot k = 0.10 \times 2.0 \times 1.0 = 0.20 \text{ m} = 200 \text{ mm}$$

But this is impractical — adopt **$b_f = 100$ mm** to suit a 150 mm deep T-section with 25 mm clear web each side.

**Slenderness check:**

$$\dfrac{d}{t_w} = \dfrac{150}{6} = 25 \quad \leq \quad 50 \;\; \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap** — $l$ in plate formula is in **metres**; $s$ is in **mm**. Mixing units causes a 1000× error.
> 2. **Forgetting $t_k$** — 50% of students omit the corrosion allowance. The examiner will deduct **2 marks**.
> 3. **Rounding direction** — always **round UP**, never down. $12.67$ mm becomes $13$ mm, not $12$ mm.
> 4. **Bracket thickness** — must be $\geq 0.75 t_w$ of parent web. Drawing without bracket detail loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Material factor $k$** — many students apply $k = 1.0$ blindly even when AH32 is specified. The examiner checks this.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Scantlings** = sizes of structural members derived from rule formulas.
- [ ] **Three framing systems**: transverse, longitudinal, mixed.
- [ ] **Transverse frame spacing** $s = 500$–$700$ mm; **longitudinal** $s = 300$–$500$ mm.
- [ ] **Web frame spacing** = 2–4 frame spaces (transverse system) or 2.5–3.0 m (longitudinal system).
- [ ] **Rule plate formula**: $t = (s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L}) / C + t_k$, with $C$ = 230 (side), 200 (deck), 250 (bottom).
- [ ] **Material factor** $k = \sqrt{235 / f_y}$: Grade A → 1.0, AH32 → 0.864, AH36 → 0.814.
- [ ] **Corrosion allowance $t_k$**: 0.5–1.0 mm (topside), 1.0–1.5 mm (side), 1.5–2.0 mm (bottom).
- [ ] **Frame section modulus**: $SM = c \cdot s \cdot l^2 \cdot k$ in cm³.
- [ ] **Web thickness**: $t_w = 0.015 \, l \, \sqrt{k} + 1.5$ mm.
- [ ] **Flange breadth**: $b_f = 0.10 \, l \, k$ mm.
- [ ] **Bracket thickness**: $t_b \geq 0.75 \, t_w$; arm $a \geq 1.5 \, t_b$.
- [ ] **Slenderness limit**: $d / t_w \leq 50$ for frames, $a / t_b \leq 60$ for brackets.
- [ ] **Hull-girder SM**: $SM_{min} = 0.005 \, L^2 \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)$.
- [ ] **Standard steel grades**: A (235 MPa), AH32 (315 MPa), AH36 (355 MPa).
- [ ] **Always round UP** to the next standard plate thickness.
- [ ] **Always add $t_k$** to the rule thickness.
- [ ] **Always verify** bending stress and slenderness after rule-based design.
- [ ] **Plate panel** behaves as a 2D plate on elastic foundation — $\sigma_{max} = 0.5 \, p \, s^2 / t^2$.
- [ ] **Bracket is a load-transfer device**, not a strength member; do not size it as primary.
- [ ] **Mid-ship region** (0.4 L) requires higher scantlings due to peak hull-girder moment.
- [ ] **Fishing trawler 18–24 m** uses transverse system; > 30 m shifts to longitudinal or mixed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Design Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Design Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Design Methods** in fishing vessel engineering refer to the systematic engineering procedures and codified frameworks used to proportion, detail, and verify the strength of the ship's hull structure so that it safely withstands the combined static, dynamic, and environmental loads experienced throughout its operational life, while satisfying serviceability, durability, and regulatory requirements.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST833)**, Module 4 deals with the **structural design philosophy** of fishing vessels — from classical **rule-based (empirical) design** to modern **rational (direct strength / FEM-based) design**. The methodology ensures that every plate, stiffener, and frame in the hull is sized to resist yielding, buckling, fatigue, and fracture under combined loading.

The two principal paradigms taught at the undergraduate level are:

1. **Empirical / Rule-based Design** — derived from long-term experience; codified by Classification Societies (e.g., Indian Register of Shipping [IRS], Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas).
2. **Rational / Direct Strength Design (DSD)** — based on first-principles mechanics, plate/shell theory, and numerical methods such as the Finite Element Method (FEM).

A third hybrid approach — the **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** for bulk carriers and oil tankers (and adapted guidelines for fishing vessels) — combines empirical scantlings with rational verification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Backbone of a Human Body:**
> Think of a fishing vessel as a human body navigating choppy waters. The **bones** are the longitudinal stiffeners (girders, side stringers), the **ribcage** is the transverse frame system, and the **skin/muscles** are the plating. **Structural Design Methods** are essentially the orthopedic prescription — they decide how thick the bones must be, how closely spaced the ribs should be, and how strong the skin must be so the body does not collapse or fracture while running, turning, or being slammed by waves.

In engineering terms:
- The **loads** (waves, cargo, propulsion, fishing gear reaction, slamming) act like external forces on the body.
- The **structural arrangement** (transverse vs. longitudinal framing) is the body's skeletal geometry.
- The **design method** (empirical vs. rational) is the doctor's diagnostic toolkit — one uses experience and standard charts (rule-based), the other uses precise calculations and 3D scans (FEM).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key reference metrics in fishing-vessel structural design:**
> - **Yield Stress (σ_y)** of mild steel: **235 MPa** (typical for hull plate per IRS).
> - **Young's Modulus (E)** of shipbuilding steel: **2.06 × 10⁵ MPa** (≈ 206 GPa).
> - **Poisson's ratio (ν)** of structural steel: **0.3**.
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** = E / [2(1+ν)] = **79 GPa**.
> - **Density of seawater (ρ_sw)** = **1.025 t/m³**.
> - **Acceleration of gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**.
> - **Design life** of a fishing vessel: ~**20–25 years**.
> - **Partial safety factor on material (γ_m)** = **1.02 to 1.15** depending on limit state.

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Alignment (Module 4 Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 4 expects the learner to:**
> - **CO4**: Identify and apply appropriate structural design methods for fishing vessels.
> - Understand the **loads** acting on the hull (still-water, wave-induced, dynamic, local).
> - Distinguish between **rule-based** and **rational/direct strength** approaches.
> - Comprehend **classification society rules** and scantling determination procedures.
> - Be aware of **limit-state design** philosophy in modern ship structural codes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Structural Design

Every structural design method — whether for a fishing trawler or an aircraft carrier — rests on three pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Define the Loads
$$L_{total} = L_{sw} + L_w + L_d$$
where:
- $L_{sw}$ = Still-water load (weight, buoyancy, cargo)
- $L_w$ = Wave-induced load (bending, shear, torsion)
- $L_d$ = Dynamic load (slamming, vibration, ice, gear impact)

### Pillar 2 — Idealize the Structure
The continuous hull is mathematically simplified as:
- **Plate elements** (plating between stiffeners) → analyzed using **plate theory (Kirchhoff–Love plate theory)**.
- **Stiffened panels** (plate + stiffeners) → analyzed as **orthotropic plates** or **Johnson–Ostenfeld theory** for ultimate strength.
- **Beams / girders** → analyzed using **Euler–Bernoulli** or **Timoshenko beam theory**.

### Pillar 3 — Apply a Strength Criterion
Two principal strength criteria are used:

| Criterion | Equation | Philosophy |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** | $\sigma_{actual} \leq \sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FS}$ | Stress must remain below a fraction of yield. |
| **Limit State Design (LSD)** | $\gamma_f L_k \leq \dfrac{R_k}{\gamma_m}$ | Loads factored up, resistance factored down. |

Where:
- $FS$ = Factor of Safety (typically **1.5 to 2.5** for ship steel).
- $\gamma_f$ = Partial safety factor on load (typically **1.0 to 1.3** for ship steel in DNV).
- $\gamma_m$ = Partial safety factor on material (typically **1.02 to 1.15**).
- $L_k$ = Characteristic load.
- $R_k$ = Characteristic resistance.

## 2.2 The Five Recognized Design Methods (Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five commonly classified structural design methods in shipbuilding:**
> 1. **Empirical (Rule-based) Method** — Classification society rules (IRS Pt.3, Lloyd's, DNV).
> 2. **Deterministic Method** — Use of mean values with a single global safety factor.
> 3. **Semi-probabilistic (LRFD/LSD) Method** — Distinct partial factors on load & resistance.
> 4. **Full Probabilistic (Reliability-based) Method** — Statistical distribution of all variables.
> 5. **Risk-based / Risk-informed Method** — Considers consequence of failure and cost optimization.

## 2.3 Method-1: Empirical / Rule-based Design (Most Common for Fishing Vessels)

This is the **default** method for small and medium fishing vessels globally.

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
- Classification societies publish **scantling tables** that prescribe minimum thickness and spacing.
- Inputs required: **L (Rule length)**, **B (moulded breadth)**, **D (moulded depth)**, **T (draft)**, **V (service speed)**, **CB (block coefficient)**.
- Output: **Minimum plate thickness (t)**, **stiffener section modulus (Z)**, **moment of inertia (I)**, and **spacing (s)**.

### 2.3.2 Generic Rule-based Formulae (IRS / Lloyd's style)

**Shell plating thickness (bottom plating):**
$$t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}}$$

where:
- $s$ = spacing of stiffeners (mm).
- $k$ = form factor (≈ 0.0158 for simply supported plates under uniform pressure).
- $p$ = design pressure (N/mm²).
- $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable bending stress (N/mm²).

**Stiffener section modulus:**
$$Z = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

where $M$ = design bending moment on the stiffener.

**Stiffener moment of inertia:**
$$I = \dfrac{5 \cdot s \cdot l^3}{384 \cdot E \cdot \delta_{allow}}$$

where $l$ = span, $\delta_{allow}$ = allowable deflection (typically span / 150 to span / 250).

### 2.3.3 Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages of Rule-based Design:**
> - Fast, repeatable, well-validated against decades of service experience.
> - No sophisticated computational tool needed.
> - Universally accepted by insurance and flag-state authorities.
> - Particularly suited to **fishing vessels under 24 m** where detailed FEM is uneconomical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations of Rule-based Design:**
> - Provides minimum scantlings, not optimized design (often over-engineered).
> - Cannot capture local stress concentrations or wave-frequency response.
> - Less transparent for novel hull forms (e.g., SWATH fishing vessels).

## 2.4 Method-2: Rational / Direct Strength Design (DSD)

Used for **larger fishing vessels (> 45 m)** and special-purpose vessels.

### 2.4.1 Workflow
1. **Build a 3D FE model** of the full ship (or a representative mid-ship section).
2. **Apply loads** (still-water + wave + dynamic).
3. **Solve** for stress distribution using linear or non-linear FEA.
4. **Check** stresses against allowable limits (yield, buckling, fatigue).

### 2.4.2 Key Equations Used

**Mid-ship bending moment (wave + still water):**
$$M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$$

where for a vessel in head seas:
$$M_w \approx 0.11 \cdot \Delta \cdot L \cdot C_w$$

with $C_w$ = wave coefficient depending on $L/\lambda$ ratio (where $\lambda$ = wave length).

**Shear force:**
$$F_s = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$$

**Equivalent von Mises stress:**
$$\sigma_{v} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Plate buckling check (Johnson-Ostenfeld):**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \dfrac{t}{s} \right)^2 \cdot k_{buckling}$$

where $k_{buckling}$ = buckling coefficient (depends on boundary conditions and aspect ratio).

## 2.5 Method-3: Common Structural Rules (CSR-H / CSR-BC)

Modern harmonized rules (IACS CSR for tankers and bulk carriers, and similar **IACS S21 fishing vessel guidelines**) adopt:
- Partial safety factors (γ_f and γ_m)
- Net scantling approach
- Explicit yield + buckling + fatigue checks

## 2.6 Real-world Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where this matters in industry:**
> - **Shipyards** use rule-based design for initial bidding and production drawings.
> - **Classification surveyors** verify rule compliance for class certification.
> - **Naval architects** use FEM for novel designs, ice-strengthened hulls, or vessels > 90 m.
> - **Fishing vessel owners** benefit because under-designed vessels fail catastrophically (capsize, hull fracture), while over-designed ones waste fuel.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision!**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------------------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Allowable bending stress (ASD) | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / FS$ | $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $FS = 1.5$ → $\sigma_{allow} \approx 157$ MPa |
| 2 | Required section modulus | $Z = M / \sigma_{allow}$ | cm³ |
| 3 | Required moment of inertia | $I = 5 s l^3 / (384 E \delta_{allow})$ | cm⁴ |
| 4 | Plate thickness (rule-based) | $t = s \cdot k \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}}$ | mm |
| 5 | Still-water bending moment | $M_{sw} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{8}$ (hogging/sagging) | kN·m |
| 6 | Wave bending moment (Rule) | $M_w = 0.11 \Delta L C_w$ | kN·m |
| 7 | Shear stress in web | $\tau = F_s / A_{web}$ | MPa |
| 8 | Von Mises stress | $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa |
| 9 | Plate buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} (t/s)^2 k$ | MPa |
| 10 | Limit State check | $\gamma_f L_k \leq R_k / \gamma_m$ | dimensionless check |
| 11 | Factor of Safety (yield) | $FS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{actual}$ | 1.5–2.5 |
| 12 | Steel density | $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ t/m³ | — |
| 13 | Design pressure on shell | $p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | N/m² |
| 14 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $p = 9.81 \cdot 1.025 \cdot h$ | kPa per m |
| 15 | Wave coefficient $C_w$ (Rule) | $C_w = f(L/\lambda, C_B)$ | 0.4 to 0.7 |

> **⚠ KTU LaTeX Note:** All `|` symbols in math expressions were replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Plate Bending Theory → Rule-based Plate Thickness

Consider a rectangular plate element of the hull shell, simply supported on all four edges, subjected to a uniform lateral pressure $p$ (e.g., hydrostatic water pressure).

### Step 1 — Plate Equation

Using **Kirchhoff–Love plate theory**, the governing equation for small deflection $w(x,y)$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
D \nabla^4 w &= p(x,y) \\
\text{where } D &= \dfrac{E \, t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}
\end{aligned}$$

- $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate per unit width.
- $t$ is the plate thickness.

### Step 2 — Solution for Uniform Pressure on Simply Supported Plate

For a plate with simply supported edges and uniform pressure $p$, the maximum deflection occurs at the centre:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{max} = \dfrac{\alpha \, p \, a^4}{D}
\end{aligned}$$

and the **maximum bending moment per unit width** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} = \beta \, p \, a^2
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $a$ = shorter span of the plate.
- $\alpha, \beta$ = dimensionless coefficients depending on aspect ratio $b/a$.

For a square plate ($b/a = 1$), $\alpha \approx 0.00406$ and $\beta \approx 0.0479$.

### Step 3 — Bending Stress in the Plate

The maximum bending stress in the plate is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 \, M_{max}}{t^2} = \dfrac{6 \, \beta \, p \, a^2}{t^2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Strength Criterion

For **Allowable Stress Design**, we require:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &\leq \sigma_{allow} \\
\dfrac{6 \, \beta \, p \, a^2}{t^2} &\leq \sigma_{allow}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Plate Thickness $t$

$$\begin{aligned}
t^2 &\geq \dfrac{6 \, \beta \, p \, a^2}{\sigma_{allow}} \\
t &\geq a \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \, \beta \, p}{\sigma_{allow}}}
\end{aligned}$$

Defining $k = \sqrt{6\beta}$ (which gives $k \approx 0.536$ for a square plate), the **rule-based plate thickness formula** emerges:

$$\boxed{\;t \geq k \, s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}}\;}$$

where $s = a$ is the shorter stiffener spacing. This is precisely the form used by IRS, Lloyd's, and DNV.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** This derivation shows that rule-based formulae are NOT arbitrary — they are direct simplifications of plate theory with conservative empirical constants. This is why well-known classification rules are reliable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stiffener Section Modulus from Bending Moment

Consider a stiffener spanning between two adjacent transverse frames (span $l$), loaded by a uniformly distributed load $q$ from the plating pressure.

### Step 1 — Reactions and Maximum Moment

By statics, for a simply supported beam:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_A = R_B = \dfrac{q \, l}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum bending moment at mid-span:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} = \dfrac{q \, l^2}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Section Modulus Definition

For a beam, the **elastic section modulus** $Z$ is defined by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Strength Requirement

For ASD:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\leq \sigma_{allow} \\
\dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} &\leq \sigma_{allow}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Required Section Modulus

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{req} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \\
Z_{req} &= \dfrac{q \, l^2}{8 \, \sigma_{allow}}
\end{aligned}$$

> This $Z_{req}$ is then matched to a standard rolled section (e.g., bulb flat, angle, T-bar) in the classification tables.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wave Bending Moment Approximation

For a vessel in head seas, the **Rule wave bending moment** is empirically derived from a sinusoidal wave of length $\lambda$ acting on a prismatic ship of length $L$.

### Step 1 — Wave Profile

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta(x) &= a \sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

with wave amplitude $a$.

### Step 2 — Buoyancy Distribution

Buoyancy $b(x)$ varies sinusoidally:

$$\begin{aligned}
b(x) &= \rho g B \eta(x)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium (Shear Force)

Integrating the buoyancy distribution:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_s(x) &= \int_0^x [w - b(u)] \, du
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Bending Moment (Double Integration)

$$\begin{aligned}
M(x) &= \int_0^x F_s(u) \, du
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Closed-form for $\lambda = L$

When $\lambda = L$, the worst-case wave bending moment becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= \dfrac{\rho g B L^3}{2\pi^2} \cdot a
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting empirical constants and the displacement $\Delta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;M_w \approx 0.11 \, \Delta \, L \, C_w\;}
\end{aligned}$$

where $C_w$ captures the $L/\lambda$ effect (≈ 0.4 to 0.7).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A fishing trawler has:
- $L = 35$ m, $B = 7.5$ m, $T = 3.2$ m, $C_B = 0.62$
- Plate spacing $s = 0.6$ m (stiffener spacing)
- Hydrostatic pressure at keel $p = 30$ kPa = **0.030 N/mm²**
- $\sigma_{allow} = 157$ MPa, $k = 0.536$ (square plate).

**Find:** Minimum bottom plate thickness.

### Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
t &\geq k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}} \\
  &= 0.536 \cdot 600 \text{ mm} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.030}{157}} \\
  &= 321.6 \cdot \sqrt{1.911 \times 10^{-4}} \\
  &= 321.6 \cdot 0.01383 \\
  &= 4.45 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding up + corrosion allowance (typically +1 mm for fishing vessels):

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{final} = 5.5 \text{ mm (use next standard plate size: 6 mm)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Rule-based (Empirical) | Rational (Direct Strength) |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Input** | L, B, D, T, V, C_B | Full 3D FE model + load cases |
| **Output** | Min. scantlings | Stress field, deformation, buckling, fatigue |
| **Tool** | Rule book, scantling tables | FEM software (ANSYS, NASTRAN, SESAM, MAESTRO) |
| **Accuracy** | Conservative; ~10–20% over-design in zones of high redundancy | Realistic; closer to actual behavior |
| **Cost/Time** | Low / Days | High / Weeks |
| **Best for** | Fishing vessels < 45 m, standard hull forms | Large/special vessels, novel hulls |
| **Validation** | Century of service experience | Code-checked against rules + sea trials |
| **Used by** | All major yards worldwide | Specialized design offices, R&D |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Overall Structural Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Particulars: L, B, D, T, V] --> B[Define Design Criteria]
    B --> C{Choose Design Method}
    C -->|Rule-based| D[Apply Classification Society Rules]
    C -->|Rational| E[Build FE Model]
    C -->|Hybrid CSR| F[Apply CSR-H / CSR-BC]

    D --> G[Compute Min. Scantlings: t, Z, I, s]
    E --> H[Apply Loads: L_sw, L_w, L_d]
    F --> I[Partial Factor Check: gamma_f, gamma_m]

    G --> J[Structural Arrangement]
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K[Strength Checks]
    K --> L{Yield OK?}
    K --> M{Buckling OK?}
    K --> N{Fatigue OK?}
    K --> O{Serviceability OK?}

    L --> P{Final Approval}
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P

    P -->|Yes| Q[Issue Approved Drawings]
    P -->|No| R[Revise Scantlings and Iterate]
    R --> C
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Load Hierarchy on a Fishing Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SL[Still-Water Loads]
        SL1[Weight of hull]
        SL2[Cargo and catch]
        SL3[Fuel and freshwater]
        SL4[Crew and provisions]
    end

    subgraph WL[Wave-Induced Loads]
        WL1[Hogging bending]
        WL2[Sagging bending]
        WL3[Torsional moment]
        WL4[Shear forces]
    end

    subgraph DL[Dynamic / Local Loads]
        DL1[Slamming on bow]
        DL2[Green water on deck]
        DL3[Vibration from engine]
        DL4[Winch and trawl gear reaction]
        DL5[Ice or debris impact]
    end

    SL --> TL[Total Design Load]
    WL --> TL
    DL --> TL

    TL --> ST[Structure: Hull, Decks, Frames, Stiffeners]
    ST --> RESP[Structural Response: Stress, Deflection, Buckling, Fatigue]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Design Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASD[Allowable Stress Design ASD]
        A1[Apply service loads] --> A2[Compute actual stress sigma]
        A2 --> A3[Compare with sigma_allow = sigma_y / FS]
        A3 --> A4{sigma less than sigma_allow ?}
        A4 -->|Yes| A5[Design OK]
        A4 -->|No| A6[Increase section]
    end

    subgraph LSD[Limit State Design LSD]
        L1[Apply factored loads gamma_f x L_k] --> L2[Compute factored resistance R_k / gamma_m]
        L2 --> L3{gamma_f L_k less than R_k by gamma_m ?}
        L3 -->|Yes| L4[Design OK]
        L3 -->|No| L5[Increase section or reduce loads]
    end

    subgraph PRB[Probabilistic Reliability Method]
        P1[Load distribution fL] --> P2[Resistance distribution fR]
        P2 --> P3[Compute probability of failure Pf]
        P3 --> P4{Pf less than target Pf ?}
        P4 -->|Yes| P5[Design OK]
        P4 -->|No| P6[Redesign]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Plate Stiffener Structural Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plating - Level 1] --> P2[Stiffeners - Level 2]
    P2 --> P3[Girders or Longitudinals - Level 3]
    P3 --> P4[Transverse Web Frames - Level 4]
    P4 --> P5[Main Transverse Frames - Level 5]
    P5 --> P6[Deck and Bottom Girders - Level 6]
    P6 --> P7[Hull Girder - Level 7 - Global Bending]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each level inherits loads from the level above. A fishing vessel's hull girder (Level 7) ultimately carries everything down to the plating (Level 1). The design of each level must consider its **local load + contribution to global load**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 KTU Past Year Pattern Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern for OENST833:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (5–8 such questions per paper).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with **internal choice** between two full questions (sub-parts usually split as 7 + 7 marks).
> - **Module-4 weightage** ≈ **20–25%** of total marks.

---

## 5.2 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Allowable Stress Design (ASD) and Limit State Design (LSD) methods as applied to fishing vessel structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ASD | LSD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Safety philosophy** | Single global Factor of Safety (FS) | Separate partial factors for load (γ_f) and material (γ_m) |
| **Criterion** | $\sigma_{actual} \leq \sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / FS$ | $\gamma_f L_k \leq R_k / \gamma_m$ |
| **Stress basis** | Working / service stress | Ultimate / characteristic values |
| **Use** | Traditional ship rules (older) | Modern CSR, DNV, IACS codes |

(1 mark for criteria, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for example.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State any three classification societies whose rules are used for the structural design of fishing vessels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** — India's national classification society, mandatory for Indian-flag vessels.
2. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — UK-based; widely used internationally.
3. **Det Norske Veritas (DNV)** — Norwegian; recognized for advanced offshore and ship rules.

(Mentioned also: Bureau Veritas [BV], American Bureau of Shipping [ABS], ClassNK, RINA — 1 mark each.)

---

## 5.3 PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A — Rule-based Design `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **rule-based structural design method** for a fishing vessel. List its advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

The rule-based (empirical) structural design method is the conventional and most widely used approach for fishing vessels worldwide. It derives scantlings from empirical formulae codified by classification societies such as **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**, **Lloyd's Register**, and **DNV**, based on over a century of accumulated shipbuilding and operational experience.

**Working Principle:**

The method takes basic vessel particulars — **Rule length L**, **moulded breadth B**, **moulded depth D**, **design draft T**, **block coefficient C_B**, and **service speed V** — as primary inputs. From these, empirical equations prescribe the minimum required:

- Shell, deck, and bulkhead **plate thickness (t)**
- **Section modulus (Z)** and **moment of inertia (I)** of stiffeners
- **Spacing (s)** of frames and longitudinals
- **Web and flange thicknesses** of girders

The fundamental equations are based on classical plate and beam theory, simplified with conservative empirical constants:

$$t = k \cdot s \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}}$$

$$Z = M_{max} / \sigma_{allow}$$

**Design Procedure:**
1. Identify rule basis and vessel type (e.g., "Fishing Vessel, IRS Rules, 2024").
2. Compute Rule Length $L = L_{BP}$ or $L_{R}$ as per society definition.
3. Determine design pressure $p = \rho g h$ (head of water at each station).
4. Calculate required plate thickness for shell, deck, bottom, side, and bulkheads.
5. Compute design bending moments on stiffeners and girders.
6. Select rolled sections (bulb flats, angles, T-bars) satisfying $Z \geq Z_{req}$ and $I \geq I_{req}$.
7. Apply **corrosion addition** (typically 0.5 to 2.0 mm) and **wastage allowance**.
8. Submit drawings to classification society for approval.

**Advantages:**
- Fast, simple, requires no sophisticated software.
- Universally accepted by flag states, insurers, and surveyors.
- Backed by extensive empirical database → highly reliable for standard forms.
- Cost-effective for small and medium fishing vessels.

**Limitations:**
- Provides **minimum** scantlings, often over-designing.
- Cannot capture **local stress concentrations** accurately.
- Less suitable for **novel hull forms** (e.g., SWATH, trimaran fishing vessels).
- Does not directly address **fatigue life** explicitly.

(Marking scheme: 2 marks procedure, 2 marks formulae, 2 marks advantages/limitations, 1 mark example.)

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A fishing trawler has $L = 32$ m, $B = 7.2$ m, $T = 2.8$ m. The bottom plating is supported by longitudinals spaced at $s = 0.55$ m. The hydrostatic pressure at the keel is $p = 28$ kPa. Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 155$ MPa. Using the rule-based formula $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}}$ with $k = 0.536$, determine the **minimum required plate thickness** including 1 mm corrosion allowance.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion**

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= 0.55 \text{ m} = 550 \text{ mm} \\
p &= 28 \text{ kPa} = 0.028 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma_{allow} &= 155 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
k &= 0.536
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule-based Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{min} &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}} \\
t_{min} &= 0.536 \cdot 550 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.028}{155}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Square Root**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{0.028}{155} &= 1.806 \times 10^{-4} \\
\sqrt{1.806 \times 10^{-4}} &= 0.01344
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{min} &= 0.536 \cdot 550 \cdot 0.01344 \\
t_{min} &= 294.8 \cdot 0.01344 \\
t_{min} &= 3.96 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Round Up to Practical Plate Size**

The next **standard plate size** above 3.96 mm is **4 mm**, but we also add the **corrosion allowance** for a fishing vessel:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{design} &= t_{min} + t_{corrosion} \\
t_{design} &= 4.0 + 1.0 \\
t_{design} &= 5.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Adopt a **6 mm standard plate** for the bottom shell plating to allow for fabrication tolerances and future wastage.

(Marking scheme: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks final calculation, 1 mark corrosion & practical rounding, 1 mark final answer.)

---

### Question B — Direct Strength & Limit State Design `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the **rational (direct strength) design method** for a fishing vessel. Compare it with the rule-based method.

**Model Answer:**

The **rational or direct strength design (DSD)** method is a modern approach that uses first-principles mechanics and numerical methods — chiefly the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — to compute actual stresses in the structure and verify them against acceptance criteria.

**Workflow (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram):**

1. **Hull Form Definition** — Generate 3D lines plan from parent hull form.
2. **Structural Idealization** — Convert hull into plate, stiffener, and girder elements.
3. **FE Model Generation** — Build meshed model (1D beam + 2D shell elements) using software such as ANSYS, MASTRAN, SESAM, or MAESTRO MARINE.
4. **Load Application:**
   - Still-water (weight, buoyancy, cargo, tank loads).
   - Wave loads (longitudinal bending, shear, torsion using DNV or IACS spectra).
   - Dynamic loads (slamming, green water, vibration, trawl gear reaction).
5. **Solution** — Linear static, non-linear (for ultimate strength), or dynamic (for fatigue).
6. **Strength Checks:**
   - **Yield check:** von Mises $\sigma_v \leq \sigma_{allow} / \gamma_m$
   - **Buckling check:** $\sigma_{actual} \leq \sigma_{cr} / \gamma_m$ (per DNV-RP-C201)
   - **Fatigue check:** Cumulative damage $D = \sum n_i / N_i < 1$ (Palmgren-Miner rule)
   - **Serviceability check:** Deflection $\delta \leq \delta_{allow}$
7. **Iterate** if any check fails, then issue approved drawings.

**Comparison with Rule-based Method:**

| Aspect | Rule-based | Rational (Direct Strength) |
|--------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Data input | L, B, D, T, C_B | Full 3D FE model |
| Output | Min. scantlings | Stress field + utilization ratios |
| Cost & time | Low | High |
| Suitability | Small/medium fishing vessels | Large / novel hulls |
| Strength check | Implicit (through rules) | Explicit (yield, buckling, fatigue) |

(Marking scheme: 2 marks workflow, 2 marks loads, 2 checks, 1 mark comparison table.)

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A fishing vessel has displacement $\Delta = 850$ t, Rule length $L = 42$ m, and wave coefficient $C_w = 0.55$. Compute the **wave bending moment** $M_w = 0.11 \cdot \Delta \cdot L \cdot C_w$. If the allowable stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa, find the **required hull girder section modulus** $Z$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 850 \text{ t} = 850 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \\
L &= 42 \text{ m} \\
C_w &= 0.55 \\
\sigma_{allow} &= 165 \text{ MPa} = 165 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
g &= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Wave Bending Moment**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= 0.11 \cdot \Delta \cdot L \cdot C_w \\
M_w &= 0.11 \cdot 850 \cdot 42 \cdot 0.55 \\
M_w &= 2156.97 \text{ t·m} \\
M_w &\approx 2157 \text{ t·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to SI (N·m)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= 2157 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times \text{m} \\
M_w &= 21.16 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} = 21.16 \text{ MN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Required Section Modulus**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{req} &= \dfrac{M_w}{\sigma_{allow}} \\
Z_{req} &= \dfrac{21.16 \times 10^6}{165 \times 10^6} \\
Z_{req} &= 0.1283 \text{ m}^3 \\
Z_{req} &= 128.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1.283 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**

- $M_w \approx 21.16 \text{ MN·m}$ (≈ 2157 t·m)
- $Z_{req} \approx 0.1283 \text{ m}^3$ (≈ $1.283 \times 10^5$ cm³)

A fishing vessel of this size typically requires hull girder $Z$ in the range of 1.0–2.0 × 10⁵ cm³, so the design is reasonable.

(Marking scheme: 1 mark given data, 2 marks M_w, 2 marks unit conversion, 2 marks Z_req and final answer.)

---

## 5.4 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing kPa with N/mm² (1 N/mm² = 1000 kPa = 1 MPa). Always convert to a consistent set before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the corrosion / wastage allowance** — for fishing vessels, **+0.5 to +2.0 mm** is mandatory per IRS Rules; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using σ_y instead of σ_allow** — students confuse yield stress (235 MPa) with allowable stress (157 MPa). Always divide by the **Factor of Safety**.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — in rule-based problems, always state: *"assuming simply supported plate, square panel, rule-based formula with k = 0.536."*
> 5. **Skipping the Mermaid / flowchart** in 7-mark answers — a labelled flowchart is worth **1 to 2 marks** by itself in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Confusing L_R (Rule length) with L_PP (perpendiculars)** — IRS uses $L$ as defined in Pt.1 Ch.1. Wrong length → wrong scantlings.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — Module 4: Structural Design Methods**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Structural design ensures **strength, stiffness, stability, fatigue life, and safety** of the hull.
- ☐ Two main methods: **Rule-based (empirical)** and **Rational (direct strength / FEM-based)**.
- ☐ Modern codes (CSR, DNV) use a **hybrid semi-probabilistic limit-state** approach.

### Key Equations
- ☐ Plate thickness (rule-based): $t = k \cdot s \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}}$
- ☐ Stiffener section modulus: $Z_{req} = M_{max} / \sigma_{allow}$
- ☐ Moment of inertia: $I_{req} = 5 s l^3 / (384 E \delta_{allow})$
- ☐ Wave bending moment: $M_w = 0.11 \, \Delta \, L \, C_w$
- ☐ Limit state check: $\gamma_f L_k \leq R_k / \gamma_m$
- ☐ Von Mises stress: $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$
- ☐ Buckling stress: $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} (t/s)^2 \cdot k$

### Loads to Remember
- ☐ **Still-water loads** (weight + buoyancy)
- ☐ **Wave-induced loads** (bending, shear, torsion)
- ☐ **Dynamic / local loads** (slamming, green water, vibration, trawl gear reaction, impact)

### Classification Societies
- ☐ **IRS (India)** | **Lloyd's Register (UK)** | **DNV (Norway)** | **BV (France)** | **ABS (USA)** | **ClassNK (Japan)** | **RINA (Italy)**

### Material & Allowable Stress
- ☐ Mild shipbuilding steel: **σ_y = 235 MPa**, **E = 2.06 × 10⁵ MPa**, **ν = 0.3**
- ☐ Factor of Safety for steel hulls: **1.5 to 2.5**
- ☐ Corrosion addition for fishing vessels: **+1 to +2 mm**

### Exam Tips
- ☐ Always **draw a flowchart / structural arrangement sketch** in 7-mark answers.
- ☐ **State assumptions clearly** before solving.
- ☐ Use **SI units consistently** (MPa, m, N, kg).
- ☐ Round up plate thickness to **nearest standard size** (4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 mm).
- ☐ Mention **classification society and rule edition** in design-based answers.

> **Final Note:** Structural Design Methods form the **backbone of every ship design**. Whether you design a 14-m canoe or a 90-m stern trawler, the principles remain — **loads, idealization, strength criterion, and verification** — only the **tools of analysis** evolve from rule-book to FEM.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction Methods in FRP/GRP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction Methods in FRP/GRP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — FRP / GRP in Marine Context

**Fibre Reinforced Plastic (FRP)** and **Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP)** are composite materials used as the primary structural material for fishing vessel hulls, superstructures, decks, and internal components. In the marine context, GRP is the most common sub-class of FRP, where **E-glass fibres** are embedded in a **thermosetting polyester or vinyl ester resin matrix** to form a laminate that is lightweight, corrosion-resistant, and dimensionally stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *"FRP/GRP construction refers to the family of lay-up and moulding techniques in which reinforcing fibres (glass, carbon, aramid) are combined with a polymer resin to produce a monolithic or sandwich hull structure whose properties are tailored by fibre orientation, stacking sequence, and resin chemistry."*

In fishing vessel design, GRP dominates vessels below **24 m LOA**, accounting for over **80%** of small-craft construction worldwide, owing to the ease of producing complex hull forms in low-volume shipyards.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete of the Sea"

Think of GRP as the **reinforced concrete of the sea**, but lighter and rust-proof:

- **The resin** plays the role of **cement paste** — it binds everything together, transfers load, and protects against water.
- **The glass fibres** act like the **steel rebar** — they carry the tensile loads because resin on its own is weak in tension (just like cement is weak in tension).
- **The mould** is the **formwork** that gives the hull its shape.

Just as a builder pours wet concrete into wooden shuttering and waits for it to cure, a GRP shipyard lays wet resin-impregnated glass mat into a female mould (or over a male plug) and waits for the resin to **exothermically cure** into a rigid, water-impermeable shell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Why GRP Won the Fishing Industry**
> Traditional wooden boats **rot**; steel boats **rust**; aluminium boats are **expensive**. GRP boats simply **do not corrode** in seawater, require minimal maintenance, and can be mass-produced in female moulds — making them ideal for the cost-sensitive fisheries sector.

---

## 1.3 Material Constituents — Quick Glossary

| Constituent | Typical Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Reinforcement** | E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid | Carries tensile & flexural loads |
| **Matrix (Resin)** | Isophthalic polyester, Vinyl ester, Epoxy | Transfers load, protects fibres, gives shape |
| **Cores (for sandwich)** | PVC foam, Balsa, End-grain balsa, SAN | Provides shear stiffness with low weight |
| **Additives** | Catalysts (MEKP), Accelerators (Cobalt), Fillers (CaCO₃), Pigments | Control cure, viscosity, colour, fire resistance |
| **Gel-coat** | Pigmented polyester (0.4–0.8 mm) | Cosmetic + weather/UV barrier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Term — "Skin Coat"**
> The first layer applied against the mould is called the **skin coat** (or **gel coat**), made purely of resin with no fibre. It is the **watertight and cosmetic outer surface** of the hull.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of FRP/GRP Construction Methods

The construction methods can be classified on the basis of:

1. **Mould type** — Open mould vs. Closed mould
2. **Degree of mechanisation** — Manual → Semi-automated → Fully automated
3. **Fibre form** — Continuous, chopped, woven, stitched
4. **Pressure application** — Contact (atmospheric) → Vacuum → Pressurised

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-mould methods** (hand lay-up, spray-up) account for the **majority of fishing vessel hulls** because they are inexpensive, flexible, and tolerant of the labour skills available in coastal shipyards.

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Hand Lay-Up (Contact Moulding)

### 2.2.1 Process Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Mould preparation** — The female mould (or male plug) is cleaned, polished, and coated with a **mould-release agent** (wax + PVA).
2. **Gel-coat application** — Pigmented polyester resin is sprayed to a wet thickness of **0.4–0.8 mm**.
3. **Skin coat / Surface veil** — A thin **C-glass or polyester surface veil (30–50 g/m²)** is rolled in to give a resin-rich, water-proof inner barrier.
4. **Lay-up sequence** — Alternating layers of **chopped strand mat (CSM)** and **woven roving (WR)** are impregnated with catalysed resin using rollers and brushes.
5. **Consolidation / Compaction** — Hand rollers (paddle rollers, de-bulking rollers) squeeze out entrapped air and ensure fibre wet-out.
6. **Curing** — Resin exothermically polymerises. Exotherm peak typically reaches **80–110 °C** in thick laminates.
7. **Demoulding & trimming** — After cure (typically 24 h at room temperature, or accelerated at 40–60 °C), the laminate is lifted from the mould.

### 2.2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- The **gel coat** is the only line of defence against **osmosis blistering** — if it is too thin, water migrates through to the laminate.
- The **surface veil** arrests micro-cracks and provides a smooth, print-free finish.
- **Hand rolling** is the **only means of removing voids** in contact moulding — voids reduce strength by **30–50%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall** — Do not confuse "**woven roving**" (WR) with "**chopped strand mat**" (CSM). CSM gives isotropic strength in-plane; WR gives directional strength along the rovings. A good laminate **alternates** CSM and WR for balanced properties.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Spray-Up Moulding

### 2.3.1 Process Logic

A **chopper gun** simultaneously:
1. **Chops continuous rovings** into 10–40 mm fibres.
2. **Sprays them with catalysed resin** from a dual-nozzle spray head.
3. The mixture is deposited against the mould and **compacted with hand rollers**.

### 2.3.2 Why It Is Used

- Faster than hand lay-up (deposition rates up to **15–25 kg/min**).
- Suited for large, gently curved panels — e.g., **wheelhouse tops, hatch covers, small hulls**.
- Lower fibre content (~**25–30% by weight**) than hand lay-up (~**30–40%**), so mechanical properties are **15–20% lower**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Styrene emission** during spray-up is a major occupational hazard — KTU expects students to mention **ventilation, masks, and controlled resin flow rates** in design answers.

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM)

A flexible polymeric film (bagging film) is sealed over the wet lay-up, and a **vacuum of 0.7–0.9 bar** is drawn. Atmospheric pressure consolidates the laminate, reducing void content from **~5%** (hand lay-up) to **<1%**, and improving fibre volume fraction.

**Result:** Strength-to-weight ratio improves by **25–40%** at the cost of extra consumables.

---

## 2.5 Method 4 — Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) and Light RTM

### 2.5.1 Process Logic

1. Dry reinforcement is placed in a **closed, matched mould**.
2. Mould is clamped shut.
3. Catalysed resin is **injected under pressure (1–4 bar for RTM; 0.2–0.5 bar for Light-RTM)** until the cavity is filled.
4. Cure occurs in the closed mould — both surfaces are mould-finished (no gel coat spraying needed on inside).
5. Part is demoulded.

### 2.5.2 Why It Matters in Fishing Vessels

- Produces hulls with **two smooth surfaces** — ideal for **fuel/water tanks and fish-hold linings**.
- Lower styrene emission, better working environment.
- Higher fibre volume fraction (**40–55%**) → stiffer, lighter panels.

---

## 2.6 Method 5 — Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP) / SCRIMP

The modern gold standard for large fishing vessels. Dry fabric is laid into the mould, sealed under a bag, and resin is drawn in by vacuum. Produces **void-free, high-FVF laminates** for hulls up to **30 m +**.

---

## 2.7 Method 6 — Filament Winding

Continuous rovings are passed through a resin bath and wound onto a rotating mandrel under controlled tension and winding angle. Used for **masts, booms, sonar domes, pressure pipes, and mast-step tubes** — not for hulls of small fishing vessels.

---

## 2.8 Method 7 — Sandwich Construction (Special Marine Variant)

For long-span decks and superstructures, a **low-density core** (PVC foam, end-grain balsa) is bonded between two GRP skins. This dramatically increases **flexural stiffness** without adding weight.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$ | Fibre volume fraction (composite rule of mixtures) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_{f+r} = n \cdot t \cdot A \cdot \rho_c$ | Total laminate weight (n = number of plies, t = ply thickness, A = area) | kg |
| 3 | $t_{lam} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Total laminate thickness | mm |
| 4 | $t_{ply} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Ply thickness from aerial weight m and density ρ | mm |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity per unit width (plate) | N·m |
| 6 | $\sigma_{c} = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | Composite strength (rule of mixtures) | MPa |
| 7 | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | Composite Young's modulus (longitudinal) | GPa |
| 8 | $N_{plies} = \dfrac{t_{required}}{t_{ply}}$ | Number of plies for required thickness | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{void} = 1 - V_f - V_m$ | Void volume fraction (quality check) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W_{resin}:W_{fibre} = 1:1 \text{ to } 1:1.2$ | Typical lay-up mass ratio for hand lay-up | kg/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The rule of mixtures assumes **perfect fibre-matrix bonding** and **uniaxial loading along the fibre direction**. For transverse or off-axis loading, modified formulae using **Halpin–Tsai** or **Lamination Theory (CLT)** apply.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Lay-up Weights

### Problem Setup

A hull panel laminate is built with **3 plies of 450 g/m² chopped strand mat (CSM)** and **2 plies of 800 g/m² woven roving (WR)**. Resin added during wet lay-up is **120% of the fibre weight**. Find the fibre volume fraction $V_f$, matrix volume fraction $V_m$, and void content $V_v$ (assume $V_v = 0$ first, then comment).

**Given material data:**
- Density of E-glass fibre: $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Density of cured polyester resin: $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$

---

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Fibre Mass per m²

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{CSM} &= 3 \text{ plies} \times 450 \text{ g/m}^2 = 1350 \text{ g/m}^2 \\
W_{WR} &= 2 \text{ plies} \times 800 \text{ g/m}^2 = 1600 \text{ g/m}^2 \\
W_{f,tot} &= W_{CSM} + W_{WR} = 1350 + 1600 = 2950 \text{ g/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Each ply's aerial weight (g/m²) is summed across the stacking sequence to obtain the total mass of dry reinforcement laid into the mould per square metre.

---

### Step 2 — Calculate Total Resin Mass per m²

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{r,tot} &= 1.20 \times W_{f,tot} = 1.20 \times 2950 = 3540 \text{ g/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The problem states the resin added equals 120 % of the fibre weight; thus $1.20 \times 2950$.

---

### Step 3 — Convert Masses to Volumes

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{f} &= \frac{W_{f,tot}}{\rho_f} = \frac{2950}{2.55} = 1156.86 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
V_{m} &= \frac{W_{r,tot}}{\rho_m} = \frac{3540}{1.20} = 2950.00 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
V_{total} &= V_f + V_m = 1156.86 + 2950.00 = 4106.86 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Volume = Mass / Density. Densities must be in compatible units — here we convert to cm³ per m² because areal masses are in g/m².

---

### Step 4 — Compute Volume Fractions (assuming no voids)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f^{'} &= \frac{V_f}{V_{total}} = \frac{1156.86}{4106.86} = 0.2817 = 28.17\% \\
V_m^{'} &= \frac{V_m}{V_{total}} = \frac{2950.00}{4106.86} = 0.7183 = 71.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Volume fractions must sum to unity (when $V_v = 0$). The fibre content is about 28 % — typical of hand lay-up. Marine-class laminates target **30–35 %** for primary hull structure.

---

### Step 5 — Include Voids in a Realistic Estimate

Hand lay-up laminates typically exhibit **3–5 % voids** by volume. Assuming $V_v = 4\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{actual} &= \frac{V_{total}}{1 - V_v} = \frac{4106.86}{0.96} = 4277.98 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
V_f &= 0.2817 \times 0.96 = 0.2704 = 27.04\% \\
V_m &= 0.7183 \times 0.96 = 0.6896 = 68.96\% \\
V_v &= 0.04 = 4.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Voids Matter** — Each 1 % of voids reduces interlaminar shear strength by roughly **5–7 %**. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can comment on **void content and laminate quality**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Longitudinal Modulus (Rule of Mixtures)

### Problem Setup

An FRP fishing-boat hull laminate uses **E-glass fibres ($E_f = 72$ GPa)** in a **polyester matrix ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa)**. Calculate the longitudinal modulus $E_c$ for $V_f = 0.30$ and $V_f = 0.50$.

---

### Step 1 — Recall the Rule of Mixtures (Voigt Model)

$$
E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m
$$

**Textual logic:** The Voigt model assumes **iso-strain** — both fibre and matrix experience the same strain. This is valid for loading **parallel to continuous, aligned fibres**.

---

### Step 2 — Compute for $V_f = 0.30$ (Hand Lay-Up)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m &= 1 - V_f = 0.70 \\
E_c &= (0.30)(72) + (0.70)(3.5) \\
E_c &= 21.60 + 2.45 = 24.05 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Most of the stiffness (≈ 90 %) comes from the fibre. Even a small fibre fraction dramatically raises the modulus of neat resin (3.5 GPa → 24 GPa).

---

### Step 3 — Compute for $V_f = 0.50$ (Vacuum Infusion / RTM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m &= 0.50 \\
E_c &= (0.50)(72) + (0.50)(3.5) \\
E_c &= 36.00 + 1.75 = 37.75 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Higher fibre volume fractions — achievable through **vacuum infusion** — give roughly **57 % higher stiffness** than contact-moulded laminates at the same thickness, enabling weight savings of **20–30 %** in optimised fishing vessels.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hull Plating Thickness from Plate Theory

### Problem Setup

A fishing vessel deck plate spans $L = 1.2$ m between stiffeners, is **450 mm wide**, and must withstand a **uniform pressure $q = 25$ kPa** (fish + crew + seawater). Determine the required **single-skin GRP thickness** if the allowable flexural stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 80$ MPa.

---

### Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment for a Plate Strip

Treat the plate as a one-way slab (bending primarily across the short span):

$$
M = \frac{q L^2}{8}
$$

$$
M = \frac{25 \times 10^3 \times (1.2)^2}{8} = \frac{25000 \times 1.44}{8} = 4500 \text{ N·m/m}
$$

**Textual logic:** $q$ in Pa × $L^2$ in m² gives N·m/m — a per-metre-width moment.

---

### Step 2 — Required Section Modulus per m Width

For a 1-m wide strip of thickness $t$:

$$
Z = \frac{t^2}{6} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

Section modulus required:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{4500}{80 \times 10^6} = 5.625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

**Textual logic:** Equating the bending stress $M/Z$ to the allowable stress and solving for $Z$ yields the minimum section modulus per unit plate width.

---

### Step 3 — Solve for Plate Thickness

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t^2}{6} &= 5.625 \times 10^{-5} \\
t^2 &= 3.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\
t &= \sqrt{3.375 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01837 \text{ m} \\
t &\approx 18.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** A solid single-skin GRP plate of 18 mm is heavy. The **sandwich alternative** with a 10 mm core reduces weight by ~50 % — which is why KTU expects students to compare both options.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Construction Methods

| Method | $V_f$ Range | Void % | Strength Index | Cycle Time | Hull Use in Fishing Vessel? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand Lay-up | 25–35 % | 3–5 | 1.0 (baseline) | 8–24 h | **Yes — most common** |
| Spray-up | 20–30 % | 4–6 | 0.80 | 4–8 h | Yes — large superstructures |
| Vacuum Bag | 35–45 % | 0.5–1.5 | 1.30 | 12–30 h | Yes — high-performance hulls |
| RTM / Light-RTM | 40–55 % | 0.5–1.0 | 1.55 | 1–4 h | Yes — small series production |
| Vacuum Infusion | 45–55 % | <0.5 | 1.70 | 4–12 h | **Yes — modern preferred** |
| Filament Winding | 50–65 % | <0.5 | 2.20 | hours | Limited — masts, tanks |
| Compression SMC | 15–25 % | 1–2 | 0.70 | 1–5 min | No — small parts only |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight** — The **strength index** column is normalised against hand lay-up and reflects tensile strength divided by density. It clearly shows why **vacuum infusion** has become the modern benchmark for fishing vessel hulls above 12 m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hull Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Hull Lines and Mould Plug] --> A1[Fabricate Female Mould]
    A1 --> A2[Clean and Apply Release Agent]
    A2 --> A3[Spray Gel Coat 0.4 to 0.8 mm]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Surface Veil CSM 30 g/m2]
    A4 --> A5[Lay Up CSM and Woven Roving Plies]
    A5 --> A6[Wet Out Resin and De-bulk with Rollers]
    A6 --> A7{Quality Check}
    A7 -->|Voids Found| A8[Repair and Re-lay]
    A8 --> A5
    A7 -->|Acceptable| A9[Allow Exothermic Cure 24 h]
    A9 --> A10[Demould and Trim]
    A10 --> A11[Join Hull and Deck]
    A11 --> A12[Fit Out and Launch]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical production flow used in small-craft shipyards. The **Quality Check** gate is critical — KTU examiners award marks for mentioning **non-destructive testing** (ultrasonic, tap-test) here.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — FRP/GRP Construction Methods Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[FRP Construction Methods] --> Open[Open Mould]
    Root --> Closed[Closed Mould]
    Open --> HL[Hand Lay Up]
    Open --> SU[Spray Up]
    Open --> VB[Vacuum Bag]
    Closed --> RTM[Resin Transfer Moulding]
    Closed --> VIP[Vacuum Infusion]
    Closed --> FW[Filament Winding]
```
