```mermaid
    Closed --> CM[Compression Moulding]
    HL --> Hull1[Fishing Vessel Hulls less than 12 m]
    SU --> Hull2[Superstructures and Wheelhouses]
    VB --> Hull3[High Performance Racing and Longline Boats]
    RTM --> Hull4[Small Series Trawler Hulls]
    VIP --> Hull5[Modern Trawlers and Seiners 12 to 30 m]
    FW --> Hull6[Masts Booms Sonar Domes]
    CM --> Hull7[Hatch Covers Cleats Small Brackets]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each leaf node maps a method to a specific fishing-vessel application. KTU questions often ask *"Which method is best for a 20 m trawler hull?"* — the answer is **Vacuum Infusion (VIP)**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Hull Plating Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OuterGel[Outer Gel Coat 0.5 mm] --> CSM1[CSM Layer 450 g per m2]
    CSM1 --> WR1[Woven Roving 800 g per m2]
    WR1 --> CSM2[CSM Layer 450 g per m2]
    CSM2 --> Core[PVC Foam Core 15 mm]
    Core --> CSM3[CSM Layer 450 g per m2]
    CSM3 --> WR2[Woven Roving 800 g per m2]
    WR2 --> CSM4[CSM Layer 450 g per m2]
    CSM4 --> InnerGel[Inner Gel Coat 0.5 mm]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **sandwich lay-up sequence** for a marine-grade hull panel. KTU expects students to identify the **outer skin**, **core**, and **inner skin** and explain why this configuration maximises **stiffness-to-weight ratio**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selecting a Construction Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: New Fishing Vessel Order] --> S1{Vessel Length?}
    S1 -->|Under 8 m| S2[Hand Lay Up]
    S1 -->|8 to 15 m| S3{Production Volume?}
    S1 -->|Over 15 m| S4[Vacuum Infusion or RTM]
    S3 -->|One off| S5[Hand Lay Up with Vacuum Bag]
    S3 -->|Small series| S6[Light RTM]
    S2 --> Q1{Stiffness Critical?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| S7[Add Sandwich Core]
    Q1 -->|No| S8[Single Skin]
    S4 --> Q2{Weight Critical?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| S9[Sandwich with PVC Foam]
    Q2 -->|No| S10[Single Skin Infusion]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A practical **decision-support tool** for shipyard engineers. KTU examiners often test this kind of methodology-selection thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]**
**"List any three construction methods used for FRP/GRP fishing vessel hulls and state the relative fibre volume fraction of each."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hand Lay-up (Contact Moulding):** $V_f$ ranges **25–35 %**. The oldest and most common method, suitable for one-off fishing boats.
2. **Vacuum Bag Moulding:** $V_f$ ranges **35–45 %**. Atmospheric pressure consolidates the wet lay-up, reducing voids and raising fibre content.
3. **Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM):** $V_f$ ranges **40–55 %**. A closed-mould process that gives two smooth surfaces and high consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> Higher $V_f$ ⇒ higher strength and stiffness but also higher tooling cost. **3 marks** for three correct method–range pairs.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"What is a gel coat? Why is it applied first in FRP lay-up?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **gel coat** is a **pigmented, thixotropic polyester or vinyl ester resin** applied as the **first layer (0.4–0.8 mm wet)** against the mould surface before any reinforcement is laid. It serves three purposes:

1. **Cosmetic finish** — provides a smooth, glossy, coloured outer surface.
2. **Weather and UV barrier** — protects the structural laminate from sunlight, salt, and abrasion.
3. **Osmosis resistance** — acts as a resin-rich barrier against water ingress into the laminate.

It is applied first because it must bond directly to the mould surface and form the **outer skin** of the finished hull.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 4 Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**"With neat sketches, describe the hand lay-up method of FRP construction for a small fishing vessel hull. Compare its advantages and limitations with the vacuum infusion process."** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Hand Lay-Up Process Description (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step model answer:**

1. **Mould preparation (1 mark):** A female mould (typically GRP itself, supported by a steel/wooden frame) is cleaned, polished with cutting compound, and waxed three times with a mould-release wax followed by a PVA film. The mould surface determines the cosmetic finish of the hull exterior.

2. **Gel coat application (1 mark):** Pigmented polyester gel coat is sprayed to a wet thickness of 0.4–0.8 mm using a gravity-feed or pressure-pot spray gun. The operator allows the gel coat to cure to a *"tacky but not transferable"* state (Barcol hardness ~ 15).

3. **Surface veil and skin coat (1 mark):** A 30 g/m² C-glass or polyester surface veil is rolled in to give a resin-rich, microcrack-resistant inner barrier.

4. **Lay-up of structural plies (2 marks):** Alternating layers of chopped strand mat (CSM) and woven roving (WR) are laid. Each ply is **wetted out** with catalysed resin (1–2 % MEKP by mass) and **consolidated** using paddle rollers. Lay-up proceeds from the keel upward to the sheer, with extra thickness concentrated in high-stress zones (keel, chines, transom, deck edge).

5. **Cure and demould (1 mark):** The laminate cures exothermically over 24 h (or accelerated at 40–60 °C for 4–6 h). The hull is demoulded using wedges and compressed air, then trimmed.

6. **Quality control (1 mark):** Visual and tap-test inspection, supplemented by ultrasonic testing in critical zones.

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Vacuum Infusion (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Vacuum Infusion |
|---|---|---|
| Mould type | Open, single-sided | Open, single-sided + bag |
| $V_f$ (fibre volume fraction) | 25–35 % | 45–55 % |
| Void content | 3–5 % | <0.5 % |
| Strength-to-weight | Baseline (1.0) | ~1.7× higher |
| Cycle time | 24 h | 4–12 h + bag prep |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium (vacuum pump, consumables) |
| Skill level | Moderate | High (leak-free bagging critical) |
| Health/Styrene exposure | Higher (open mould) | Lower (closed bag) |
| Suitability | One-offs, prototypes, repair | Series production, large hulls |

**Key advantages of hand lay-up (1 mark):** Low tooling cost, simple equipment, easy repair, ideal for one-off fishing vessels.
**Key advantages of vacuum infusion (1 mark):** Higher mechanical properties, lighter hulls, lower emissions, better consistency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing **gel coat** with **surface veil** (they are different layers).
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **catalyst (MEKP)** and its typical dosage.
> 3. Stating void content without numerical ranges.
> 4. Drawing a lay-up sequence without **labelling each ply** (CSM vs. WR).
> 5. Omitting the **de-bulking / roller consolidation** step.

---

### Question B — Module 4 Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
**"Explain the vacuum infusion process (VIP) used in modern GRP fishing vessel construction. Derive the fibre volume fraction for a laminate with the following stacking sequence: 2 × 600 g/m² CSM, 1 × 1200 g/m² biaxial fabric, and a 1.2 : 1.0 resin-to-fibre mass ratio. Assume E-glass density 2.55 g/cm³, cured resin density 1.20 g/cm³, and a void content of 1 %."** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Vacuum Infusion Process Explanation (7 Marks)

1. **Mould preparation (1 mark):** Same as hand lay-up — female mould is waxed, PVA applied, gel coat sprayed and cured to tack.

2. **Dry stack lay-up (1 mark):** All reinforcement (CSM, woven roving, biaxials, foam cores for sandwich) is placed **dry** on the gel coat. No resin is added at this stage.

3. **Flow media and consumables (1 mark):** A **flow media** (e.g., knitted PE net) is placed on top to channel resin. **Resin inlet pipes** and **vacuum outlet pipes** are positioned at strategic points — inlets typically at the bottom (keel) and outlets at the top (sheer).

4. **Bagging and sealing (1 mark):** A nylon bagging film is draped over the entire stack and sealed to the mould flange with tacky tape. Vacuum ports are connected.

5. **Vacuum and leak check (1 mark):** Vacuum of 25–28 inHg (0.85–0.95 bar) is drawn and held for 15–30 min to verify a leak rate below ~1 inHg/5 min.

6. **Resin infusion (1 mark):** Catalysed resin is allowed to flow into the cavity under vacuum. The front advances from inlet to outlet, wetting the fibres progressively. Infusion time for a 15 m hull is typically 1–3 h.

7. **Cure and demould (1 mark):** After the part is fully wet out, the vacuum is maintained during cure. The hull is demoulded after 12–24 h, trimmed, and post-cured if needed.

#### Part (b) — Fibre Volume Fraction Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Total fibre aerial mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{CSM} &= 2 \times 600 = 1200 \text{ g/m}^2 \\
W_{biax} &= 1 \times 1200 = 1200 \text{ g/m}^2 \\
W_{f,tot} &= 1200 + 1200 = 2400 \text{ g/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Resin mass (1.2 : 1.0 ratio)**

$$
W_{r,tot} = 1.2 \times 2400 = 2880 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \frac{2400}{2.55} = 941.18 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
V_m &= \frac{2880}{1.20} = 2400.00 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
V_{f+m} &= 3341.18 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for 1 % voids**

$$
V_{total} = \frac{V_{f+m}}{1 - 0.01} = \frac{3341.18}{0.99} = 3374.93 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute final volume fractions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \frac{941.18}{3374.93} = 0.2789 \approx 27.9\% \\
V_m &= \frac{2400}{3374.93} = 0.7111 \approx 71.1\% \\
V_v &= 0.01 = 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 27.9 + 71.1 + 1.0 = 100.0\%$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion** — Even with VIP, the fibre volume fraction is governed by the **resin-to-fibre ratio** set during infusion. To raise $V_f$ above 30 %, the operator must use **drier fabric stacks**, faster flow media, and **drier resin** — typically targeting a **1.0 : 1.0** ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in $V_f$ Calculations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include voids** — voids always exist in real laminates; KTU expects a void-aware calculation.
> 2. **Mixing density units** — g/cm³ must be consistent with cm³ output; m³ output requires kg/m³.
> 3. **Reading the ratio backwards** — *"1.2 : 1.0 resin-to-fibre"* means **resin = 1.2 × fibre**, not the other way.
> 4. **Omitting resin density** — a frequent slip-up that loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FRP/GRP Construction Methods**

- ✅ **FRP** = Fibre Reinforced Plastic (general term); **GRP** = Glass Reinforced Plastic (specific to glass fibres).
- ✅ **E-glass** is the marine-industry standard fibre — cheap, strong, corrosion-proof.
- ✅ **Polyester resin** dominates fishing-vessel construction; **vinyl ester** is used where osmosis resistance is critical.
- ✅ The **gel coat** is the **first layer applied** — 0.4–0.8 mm — and gives cosmetic + waterproof protection.
- ✅ **Hand lay-up** is the most common method for small fishing vessels ($V_f = 25–35\%$, voids 3–5 %).
- ✅ **Vacuum infusion** is the modern benchmark for larger hulls ($V_f = 45–55\%$, voids <0.5 %).
- ✅ **CSM** = isotropic in-plane strength; **WR** = directional strength; alternate them for balanced laminates.
- ✅ **Sandwich construction** (GRP skins + foam/balsa core) gives the best **stiffness-to-weight** ratio for decks and superstructures.
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures** for longitudinal modulus: $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ — valid only for fibres aligned with the load.
- ✅ **Voids** are the laminate's enemy — every 1 % of voids reduces ILSS by ~5–7 %.
- ✅ **Filament winding** is used for masts, booms, sonar domes — not for hull plating.
- ✅ Always mention **non-destructive testing (NDT)** in quality control — ultrasonic, tap-test, acoustic emission.
- ✅ **KTU mark-grabbers:** numerical $V_f$ calculations with void correction, neat labelled lay-up sequence, comparison tables between two methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mnemonic — "HGVBRC"** for the six principal methods: **H**and lay-up, **G**el coat, **V**acuum bag, **B**ag moulding, **R**TM, **C**ompression moulding. A 2024 KTU short-answer favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminum and Ferro-Cement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aluminum and Ferro-Cement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — ALUMINUM AND FERRO-CEMENT IN FISHING VESSEL DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4)**
> This module introduces two **non-conventional hull construction materials** widely adopted for small and medium fishing vessels: **Aluminum alloys** and **Ferro-cement**. Both materials are alternatives to traditional steel and wood, and their selection is dictated by vessel size, operational profile, economics, and the boat-builder's expertise.

---

### 1.1 Aluminum as a Hull Material

**Formal Definition:**
Aluminum, in marine applications, refers to a family of **aluminum-magnesium (Al-Mg, 5xxx series)** and **aluminum-magnesium-silicon (Al-Mg-Si, 6xxx series)** alloys. The most common marine-grade alloys are **AA 5083** (hull plates), **AA 6082** (extruded structural sections), and **AA 6061** (fittings). These alloys are selected for marine service due to their superior **corrosion resistance** in seawater and good **strength-to-weight ratio**.

**Key Properties at a Glance:**

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density | **2.7 t/m³** | ~1/3 the weight of steel (7.85 t/m³) |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **70 GPa** | ~1/3 that of steel (210 GPa) |
| Yield Strength (5083-H116) | **215 MPa** | Comparable to mild steel |
| Melting Point | **~660 °C** | Loses strength rapidly above 200 °C |
| Corrosion Rate in seawater | **< 0.025 mm/year** | Naturally protective oxide layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lightweight Champion with a Built-in Raincoat"**
> Imagine building a boat hull with the same structural logic as a soda can. A soda can is incredibly thin aluminum yet strong enough to resist crushing because of its curved geometry. Marine aluminum works similarly — it is **lightweight** (easy to lift, faster to propel), and the moment it touches oxygen or water, it forms an invisible **aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) layer** that "self-heals" scratches and prevents rust. This is why aluminum boats can sit in seawater for decades without corroding through, unlike steel which needs paint and sacrificial zinc anodes.

**Why Aluminum for Fishing Vessels?**
- **High speed & fuel economy:** Lower displacement → lower powering requirement → smaller engine → reduced operating cost.
- **Payload advantage:** The weight saved on the hull can be converted to fish hold capacity or fuel.
- **Low maintenance:** No annual repainting cycle if alloy selection is correct.
- **Ease of repair:** Standard MIG/TIG welding in any boatyard.

---

### 1.2 Ferro-Cement as a Hull Material

**Formal Definition:**
Ferro-cement (also written *ferrocement*) is a composite material consisting of a **cement mortar matrix** (typically Portland cement + fine sand + water, with optional admixtures) reinforced with closely spaced layers of **wire mesh** (chicken mesh, hexagonal mesh, expanded metal lath) and **skeletal steel bars** (rods or angle-iron frames). The defining feature is the **very high ratio of surface reinforcement to volume of mortar**, producing a thin, strong, crack-resistant shell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Composition (per cubic metre of mortar):**
> - **Cement:** ~600–800 kg
> - **Sand:** ~1.0–1.5 times the cement mass
> - **Water/Cement ratio:** 0.35–0.45 (kept low to minimize shrinkage cracking)
> - **Mesh layers:** 4–8 layers of galvanized or mild-steel chicken wire
> - **Skeletal steel:** 6–10 mm diameter MS rods forming a 3D cage

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Concrete Eggshell"**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Picture a hollow **eggshell**: extremely thin, yet you cannot crush it easily in your hand. Now imagine the eggshell is built from sand-cement mortar (concrete), but inside the shell is a dense network of fine wire mesh, like a fishing net embedded in plaster. Even though the wall is only **15–25 mm thick**, the mesh arrests every micro-crack and distributes loads. A ferro-cement boat hull behaves exactly like that eggshell — thin-walled, lightweight, surprisingly tough, and can be built by a small team using mostly hand tools, without a shipyard.

**Why Ferro-Cement for Fishing Vessels?**
- **Low capital investment:** Materials are locally available in coastal/rural areas.
- **Simplicity of construction:** No welding of pressure vessels, no rolling of plates.
- **Durability in seawater:** Properly cured ferro-cement has a service life of **30+ years**.
- **Damage tolerance:** Small punctures do not propagate catastrophically like in fiberglass or steel plate.
- **Ideal for developing economies:** Suitable for artisanal and small-scale fishermen.

---

### 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Reference — Aluminum vs. Ferro-Cement vs. Steel vs. Wood**
> | Material | Hull Weight (relative) | Skill Required | Initial Cost | Maintenance |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Steel | 1.00 (baseline) | High (welding) | Medium | High (painting, anodes) |
> | Aluminum | **0.35–0.45** | High (specialized welding) | High | Low |
> | Ferro-cement | **0.60–0.80** | Low–Medium (no welding) | Low | Low |
> | Wood | 0.55–0.70 | High (carpentry) | Variable | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aluminum Alloys — Marine Metallurgy

### 2.1.1 Why Pure Aluminum Cannot Be Used

Pure aluminum has a tensile strength of only **~90 MPa**, which is too low for marine structural use. The addition of alloying elements — chiefly **magnesium (Mg), manganese (Mn), silicon (Si), and copper (Cu)** — dramatically increases strength through **solid-solution strengthening** and **work hardening**.

For fishing vessels, the **5xxx series (Al-Mg)** is the industry default for plates because:
1. Magnesium content (3–5%) gives **moderate-to-high strength** without heat treatment.
2. The alloy is **non-heat-treatable**, meaning it cannot lose strength if mistakenly heated during nearby welding — a major practical advantage.
3. It has **excellent corrosion resistance** in marine atmospheres.

### 2.1.2 Temper Designations Explained

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoding "5083-H116"**
> - **5083** = Alloy (Al-Mg-Mn family, ~4.5% Mg)
> - **H1** = Strain-hardened (cold worked)
> - **16** = Specifically stabilized for marine use (resistant to exfoliation / layer corrosion)
> This is the alloy stamped on the back of virtually every aluminum fishing boat plate worldwide.

### 2.1.3 Critical Design Issue — Elastic Buckling

Aluminum's modulus of elasticity (**E = 70 GPa**) is only **1/3 of steel's**. This means an aluminum plate of the same thickness and unsupported span will deflect **3× more** under the same load. Therefore:

- Plate thickness **must be increased**, OR
- Stiffener spacing **must be reduced**, OR
- Section modulus of stiffeners **must be increased**.

The KTU-relevant plate-buckling formula (applicable to both aluminum and steel plates) is given in §2.3 below.

---

## 2.2 Ferro-Cement — Composite Mechanics

### 2.2.1 The "Two-Material Synergy"

Ferro-cement is a **macro-composite**. Load is shared between:
- **Mortar matrix** (carries compressive loads, provides shape)
- **Steel mesh + skeletal rods** (carry tensile loads, arrest cracks)

The **specific surface of reinforcement (Sf)** — defined as the **total surface area of reinforcement per unit volume of composite** — is the most important parameter. For marine ferro-cement, **Sf ≥ 0.6 m²/kg of composite** is the typical minimum.

### 2.2.2 Failure Modes to Understand

| Failure Mode | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Cracking** (through-thickness) | Tensile stress > mortar tensile strength | High Sf, low w/c ratio, proper curing |
| **Delamination** (mesh corrosion) | Chloride ingress, cover < 3 mm | Adequate cover, low permeability mortar |
| **Spalling** | Freeze-thaw, impact | Air-entrained admixtures, quality cover |
| **Steel corrosion** | Carbonation, chlorides | Galvanized mesh, dense mortar, painting |

### 2.2.3 Curing — The Most Critical Step

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Examiners frequently award full marks only when students mention **7-day wet curing** (or 14-day preferred). Without adequate curing, the mortar loses up to **50% of its potential strength**. Curing is not optional; it is the *sine qua non* of ferro-cement quality.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are directly applicable for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Module 4.**

### A. Weight & Displacement Calculations

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | $\Delta$ = displacement (t); $\rho$ = water density (**1.025 t/m³** seawater); $\nabla$ = volume displaced (m³) | Displacement from hull volume |
| 2 | $W_{hull} = \rho_{mat} \cdot V_{hull}$ | $W$ = hull weight (t); $V$ = hull volume (m³) | Hull weight estimation |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{Al}}{W_{St}} \approx 0.35$ | Weight ratio of aluminum to steel hull of equal stiffness | Comparing aluminum vs. steel |
| 4 | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{W_{FC}}{W_{St}} \approx 0.60$–$0.80$ | Weight ratio of ferro-cement to steel | Comparing ferro-cement vs. steel |

### B. Plate Buckling (Critical for Aluminum Design)

| # | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12 (1-\nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | $\sigma_{cr}$ = critical buckling stress (Pa); $E$ = Young's modulus; $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (**0.33** for Al, **0.30** for steel); $t$ = plate thickness (m); $b$ = shorter span (m); $k$ = buckling coefficient (≈4.0 for simply supported long plate) | Plate panel design |
| 6 | $\sigma_{cr,Al} \approx \dfrac{1}{3} \sigma_{cr,St}$ | Same thickness & geometry | Shows Al's buckling disadvantage |
| 7 | $\dfrac{t_{Al}}{t_{St}} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$ | Required thickness ratio to match steel stiffness | Plate-thickness rule of thumb |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** *"To match steel, make aluminum **1.7× thicker**."*

### C. Ferro-Cement Specific Surface & Strength

| # | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | $S_{f} = \dfrac{A_{mesh}}{V_{composite}}$ | $S_f$ (m²/m³ or m²/kg) | Quality check |
| 9 | $\sigma_{t} = 0.4 \sqrt{f'_{c}}$ (MPa) | $f'_c$ = cube compressive strength (MPa) | Splitting tensile strength of mortar |
| 10 | $f'_{c,28} \approx 1.5 \cdot f'_{c,7}$ | Strength gain between 7 and 28 days | Curing benefit |
| 11 | $\text{Min. cover} \geq \max(3\text{ mm}, d_{mesh}/2)$ | $d_{mesh}$ = mesh wire diameter | Corrosion protection |

### D. Cost & Lifecycle (High-Yield for Essays)

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | $\text{LCC} = C_{initial} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{maintenance}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Life-cycle cost comparison |

where $C$ = cost, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = service life (years).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Aluminum Plates Must Be ~1.7× Thicker Than Steel

**Objective:** Show mathematically that to achieve equivalent plate-panel stiffness, an aluminum plate must have thickness $t_{Al} = \sqrt{3} \cdot t_{St}$.

**Given:**
- Steel: $E_{St} = 210$ GPa, Poisson's ratio $\nu_{St} = 0.30$
- Aluminum: $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, Poisson's ratio $\nu_{Al} = 0.33$
- Same panel geometry, same $k$, same $\sigma_{cr}$ required.

**Step 1 — Write the plate-buckling equation for both materials.**

For steel:
$$
\sigma_{cr,St} = \frac{k \pi^{2} E_{St}}{12 \left(1-\nu_{St}^{2}\right)} \left(\frac{t_{St}}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

For aluminum:
$$
\sigma_{cr,Al} = \frac{k \pi^{2} E_{Al}}{12 \left(1-\nu_{Al}^{2}\right)} \left(\frac{t_{Al}}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the critical stresses for equal stiffness.**
$$
\sigma_{cr,St} = \sigma_{cr,Al}
$$

Substituting both equations:
$$
\frac{k \pi^{2} E_{St}}{12 (1-\nu_{St}^{2})} \left(\frac{t_{St}}{b}\right)^{2} = \frac{k \pi^{2} E_{Al}}{12 (1-\nu_{Al}^{2})} \left(\frac{t_{Al}}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms.** $k$, $\pi^{2}$, $12$, and $b$ are identical on both sides, so:
$$
\frac{E_{St}}{(1-\nu_{St}^{2})} t_{St}^{2} = \frac{E_{Al}}{(1-\nu_{Al}^{2})} t_{Al}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

For steel: $1-\nu_{St}^{2} = 1 - 0.30^{2} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$

For aluminum: $1-\nu_{Al}^{2} = 1 - 0.33^{2} = 1 - 0.1089 = 0.8911$

Plugging in:
$$
\frac{210}{0.91} t_{St}^{2} = \frac{70}{0.8911} t_{Al}^{2}
$$

Compute the constants:
$$
230.77 \cdot t_{St}^{2} = 78.55 \cdot t_{Al}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the thickness ratio.**
$$
t_{Al}^{2} = \frac{230.77}{78.55} \cdot t_{St}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{t_{Al}^{2}}{t_{St}^{2}} = 2.938 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{t_{Al}}{t_{St}} = \sqrt{2.938} \approx 1.714
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{Al} \approx 1.71 \cdot t_{St}\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
This is the famous **"factor of √3 ≈ 1.73"** rule taught in every fishing-vessel design class. An aluminum plate must be roughly **70% thicker** than an equivalent steel plate to provide the same buckling resistance. Designers compensate by using **closely spaced stiffeners** so that the unsupported panel span $b$ is small.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Aluminum Fishing Boat Displacement

**Problem:**
A 12 m aluminum fishing trawler has an external volume of **45 m³** including superstructure. Aluminum hull density **2.71 t/m³**; the hull material volume fraction is 55% of external volume (rest is internal space, fuel, fish hold, engine). Calculate (a) hull mass, (b) total mass if outfit represents 35% of hull mass, (c) operating displacement in seawater of density **1.025 t/m³**, (d) draft if waterplane area is **30 m²**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hull material volume:**
$$
V_{hull,mat} = 0.55 \times 45 = 24.75 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Hull mass (lightship — empty):**
$$
W_{hull} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V_{hull,mat} = 2.71 \times 24.75 = 67.07 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3 — Outfit mass:**
$$
W_{outfit} = 0.35 \times 67.07 = 23.47 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4 — Lightship mass (hull + outfit, no payload):**
$$
W_{LS} = 67.07 + 23.47 = 90.54 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 5 — Displaced volume in seawater:**
$$
\nabla = \frac{W_{LS}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{90.54}{1.025} = 88.33 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Mean draft:**
$$
T = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WL}} = \frac{88.33}{30} = 2.94 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{LS} \approx 90.5 \text{ t};\quad T \approx 2.94 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** This type of **2-step problem (mass → displacement → draft)** is a guaranteed 14-mark question every alternate year. Master the formula chain.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Aluminum vs. Ferro-Cement Decision Grid

| Decision Criterion | Winner | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum hull weight | **Aluminum** | 2.71 t/m³ vs. ~2.4–2.6 t/m³ for FC, but FC needs thicker shell → aluminum lighter in practice |
| Lowest initial cost | **Ferro-cement** | Materials cost ~30% of aluminum |
| Ease of repair in remote coastal village | **Ferro-cement** | Cement, sand, mesh, water — no welding |
| High-speed craft (>20 knots) | **Aluminum** | Weight saving translates to speed/fuel |
| Fire resistance | **Ferro-cement** | Aluminum melts at 660 °C |
| Galvanic corrosion risk | **Ferro-cement** (no risk) vs. **Aluminum** (risk with steel fasteners) | Need insulating gaskets for Al |
| Suitable for vessels > 18 m | **Aluminum** | FC becomes too heavy at large sizes |
| Suitable for vessels < 12 m | **Ferro-cement** | FC competitive for small craft |
| Buildable by semi-skilled labor | **Ferro-cement** | Critical for developing economies |
| Impact resistance (collision with debris) | **Aluminum** | FC is brittle; can crack on hard impact |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three advantages of using aluminum alloys for fishing vessel hull construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Light weight** — Density 2.71 t/m³ gives ~65% weight saving over steel, allowing higher payload, smaller engine, and lower fuel consumption.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — Natural oxide layer (Al₂O₃) self-heals, eliminating frequent repainting; suitable for prolonged seawater exposure.
3. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — Alloys like AA 5083-H116 have yield strength of ~215 MPa, comparable to mild steel.
4. *(Optional 4th point)* Ease of fabrication by MIG/TIG welding; non-magnetic (useful for degaussing).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define "specific surface of reinforcement" in ferro-cement and state its recommended minimum value for marine hulls.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Specific surface of reinforcement (Sf)** is defined as the **total surface area of mesh and skeletal steel reinforcement per unit volume of ferro-cement composite** (units: m²/m³ or m²/kg). It is the single most important parameter governing crack resistance and tensile strength.

$$
S_f = \frac{A_{mesh}}{V_{composite}}
$$

**Recommended minimum** for marine hulls: **$S_f \geq 0.6$ m²/kg** (or equivalently 2.0–4.0 m²/m³ depending on reference basis). Higher Sf → finer crack pattern → better water-tightness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **properties, grades, and marine applications** of aluminum alloys used in fishing vessel construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 m fishing boat is built in **AA 5083-H116 aluminum** with hull plate thickness 6 mm and stiffener spacing 450 mm. If the design were changed to **mild steel** with the same unsupported panel dimensions, determine the **equivalent steel plate thickness** that would provide equal buckling resistance. Use $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{St} = 210$ GPa, $\nu_{Al} = 0.33$, $\nu_{St} = 0.30$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Aluminum Alloys — Properties, Grades & Marine Use**

1. **Why Aluminum — Salient Properties:**
   - **Density:** $\rho = 2.71$ t/m³ — about 35% of steel's density.
   - **Young's Modulus:** $E = 70$ GPa — about 1/3 of steel, requiring thicker plates or closer stiffeners.
   - **Yield Strength (5083-H116):** 215 MPa — comparable to mild steel structural grade.
   - **Corrosion Resistance:** Excellent in marine atmosphere due to passive Al₂O₃ film.
   - **Non-magnetic & Non-sparking:** Safer around fuels.
   - **Reflectivity:** High — lower radiant heat absorption.

2. **Common Marine Grades:**
   | Alloy | Composition | Where Used |
   |---|---|---|
   | **AA 5083** | Al-Mg (~4.5% Mg) | Hull plates, decks |
   | **AA 5086** | Al-Mg (~4.0% Mg) | Smaller craft plates |
   | **AA 6061** | Al-Mg-Si | Fittings, masts, extrusions |
   | **AA 6082** | Al-Mg-Si | Heavy extrusions, structural frames |

3. **Marine Applications in Fishing Vessels:**
   - Hull plating for trawlers, seiners, longliners up to 30 m.
   - Superstructures, wheelhouses, mast/derricks.
   - Fish-hold linings (with proper insulation).
   - Buoyancy compartments and tank tops.
   - Small craft (canoes, dinghies) where weight is critical.

4. **Joining Methods:** MIG (Metal Inert Gas) and TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding using **AA 5356 filler wire**; mechanical fastening with stainless steel bolts using insulating **nylon or rubber gaskets** to prevent galvanic corrosion.

---

### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- $t_{Al} = 6$ mm
- $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $\nu_{Al} = 0.33$
- $E_{St} = 210$ GPa, $\nu_{St} = 0.30$
- Equal geometry, equal buckling stress required.

**Step 1 — Write plate-buckling equality:**
$$
\frac{E_{St}}{(1-\nu_{St}^{2})} t_{St}^{2} = \frac{E_{Al}}{(1-\nu_{Al}^{2})} t_{Al}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t_{St}$:**
$$
t_{St} = t_{Al} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E_{Al}}{E_{St}} \cdot \frac{(1-\nu_{St}^{2})}{(1-\nu_{Al}^{2})}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the numbers.**

Compute each term:
$$
\frac{E_{Al}}{E_{St}} = \frac{70}{210} = 0.3333
$$

$$
(1-\nu_{St}^{2}) = 1 - 0.30^{2} = 0.91
$$

$$
(1-\nu_{Al}^{2}) = 1 - 0.33^{2} = 0.8911
$$

$$
\frac{0.91}{0.8911} = 1.021
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$
t_{St} = 6 \cdot \sqrt{0.3333 \times 1.021} = 6 \cdot \sqrt{0.3403} = 6 \cdot 0.5834
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{St} \approx 3.50 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
A **3.5 mm** steel plate provides the same buckling resistance as a **6 mm** aluminum plate — a ratio of **1.71×**, confirming the $\sqrt{3}$ rule. Designers therefore use thicker aluminum plates but gain the benefit of ~65% weight reduction overall because the thickness increase (1.71×) is offset by the density ratio (1/3.0).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the **construction procedure of a ferro-cement fishing boat hull**. List any **four advantages and three disadvantages** of ferro-cement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ferro-cement fishing boat has the following: external volume = 32 m³; hull shell volume fraction = 40%; mortar density = 2.30 t/m³; specific surface of reinforcement $S_f$ = 1.8 m²/kg; mesh wire diameter = 1.2 mm. Estimate the **(i) hull mass** and **(ii) total surface area of mesh per m² of hull surface**. Assume the hull surface area = 80 m². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Ferro-Cement Hull Construction — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Building the Skeleton (Frame):** Weld or tie a 3D framework of **6–10 mm mild steel rods** in the shape of the hull. Spacing between rods typically **100–150 mm**.
2. **Tying the Mesh:** Wrap **4–8 layers of galvanized chicken wire or hexagonal mesh** around the skeleton. Each layer is tied to the rods and to the previous layer using soft iron tie wire.
3. **Plastering (Mortar Application):** Apply the cement-sand mortar **(1:2 to 1:3 mix by mass)** from the **outside first**, working through the mesh into every gap. Plaster from inside next. Total shell thickness target: **15–25 mm**.
4. **Compaction:** Hand-trowel vigorously to expel air voids and ensure mortar penetrates all mesh layers.
5. **Curing:** Cover with wet hessian (burlap) or spray water for **minimum 7 days (preferably 14)**. This is the most critical step.
6. **Finishing:** Apply a **cement-water slurry coat** for water-tightness, then epoxy-based marine paint.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Low capital cost — materials are cheap and locally available.
2. Simple technology — no welding or rolling of plates; semi-skilled labor can build.
3. Damage-tolerant — small punctures do not propagate; easy field repair with fresh mortar.
4. Long service life (30+ years) if properly cured and painted.

**Three Disadvantages:**
1. Heavy compared to aluminum and GRP at larger sizes.
2. Labor-intensive — skilled plasterers required to avoid voids.
3. Vulnerable to cracking if curing is inadequate or impact damage is severe.
4. *(Bonus)* Poor performance under concentrated point loads unless skeletal rods are well-designed.

---

### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- External volume $V_{ext} = 32$ m³
- Hull shell volume fraction = 40% (so $V_{shell} = 0.40 \times 32 = 12.8$ m³)
- Mortar density $\rho_{m} = 2.30$ t/m³
- Hull external surface area $A_{ext} = 80$ m²

**Step 1 — Hull Mass (assuming the shell is the dominant mass):**

If we treat the entire shell as mortar (a simplification that ignores steel mass, valid for a first-order estimate):
$$
m_{hull} = \rho_m \cdot V_{shell} = 2.30 \times 12.8 = 29.44 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{hull} \approx 29.4 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

*(For a more accurate answer, subtract ~10% for the volume occupied by steel mesh and rods, then re-add their mass. In a 14-mark paper, the simplified calculation above is acceptable unless specifically asked to refine.)*

**Step 2 — Total Surface Area of Mesh per m² of Hull:**

Given $S_f$ in terms of area per kg — but we need area per m² of hull surface. Convert using:

The hull shell volume is $V_{shell} = 12.8$ m³. The total surface area of mesh inside the shell:
$$
A_{mesh,total} = S_f \cdot m_{hull} = 1.8 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{kg} \times 29440 \text{ kg} = 52{,}992 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Per square metre of hull surface:
$$
\frac{A_{mesh,total}}{A_{ext}} = \frac{52{,}992}{80} \approx 662 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{mesh} \approx 662 \text{ m}^{2} \text{ of mesh per m}^{2} \text{ of hull surface}}
$$

**Engineering Sanity Check:** This value (~660 m²/m²) is consistent with 5–7 layers of standard chicken mesh (each layer contributing ~100 m²/m² when wrapped). This is a healthy marine-grade value.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up the $\sqrt{3}$ rule:** Many students claim aluminum plates need to be 3× thicker. The correct factor is **√3 ≈ 1.7×** (thicker, not 3× heavier in absolute terms). Always show the derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting curing in ferro-cement answers:** Listing "advantages of ferro-cement" without mentioning the **mandatory 7-day wet curing** costs 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Ignoring Poisson's ratio in buckling problems:** Numerical answers without $\nu$ are technically wrong; examiners deduct 1 mark for sloppy dimensional treatment.
> 4. **Wrong alloy code:** Writing "Al 6063" or "Al 1050" instead of **AA 5083** shows lack of marine-specific knowledge.
> 5. **Confusing density of aluminum:** Common wrong value: 2.71 g/cm³ written without conversion. Use **2.71 t/m³** for ship design calculations.
> 6. **Not mentioning galvanic corrosion:** Aluminum + steel fasteners + seawater = corrosion disaster. Insulating gaskets are mandatory.

---
